content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: dulcis 'sweet' [adj. /] (P1.+)
Derivatives: dulciculus [adj.] 'sweet little' (P1.+), dulcitas 'sweetness' (Acc.+),
dulcedo, -inis 'sweetness' (Lucr.+); dulcifer 'containing sweetness' (PI., Enn.).
Pit. *dulbvi- 'sweet'.
IE cognates: Gr. Y^>ku<; [adj.] 'sweet', Myc. de-re-u-ko perhaps Idleukosl, Gr.
yXeuKog [n.] 'sweet wine' (Arist.; recent); ayXeuKt^ 'sour' (Epich.).
Since Latin /-stem adj. are often formed from w-stems, it is often assumed that dulcis
represents *dulku-i~; yet the disappearance of the second *w is unexpected: *dulquis
would be unproblematic. Greek yXuicCx; is explained as an assimilation from *djkus,
while Lat. could show *djk- > *dolk- > dulcis-, but the explanation for Greek is ad
hoc. Even if it were correct, we would still not have an etymology for Graeco-Latin
*dl(u)ku-. It is likely that we are dealing with a common borrowing from an unknown
source. Since 'milk' is termed a 'sweet' substance in languages, there may be a link
with lac, lactis, Gr. yaXa < *glg-t.
Bibl.: WH I: 379f„ EM 186f., IEW 222.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: dumus 'shrub' [m. o] (Andr.+; sg. only in Ov., usually pi. dumi Cic.+; dusmo [adj.]
Andr. apud Paul. ex. F.)
Derivatives: dumetum 'thicket' (Cic.+).
Pit. *dusmo~.
IE cognates: Olr. dos 'shrub' (< *dus-o?). The appurtenance of Gm. forms in *tus~,
*tus- 'to spread, tear* is uncertain: Norw. tos 'ravel, fuzz', tossa 'to strew', ME
totusen 'to ruffle', MLG tosen 'to tear', OHG zirzuson 'to ruffle', MHG zusack
'brushwood', zuse 'brushwood, frizzle'.
A Latin-OIr. correspondence *dus~, without further connections.
Bibl.: WH I: 381, EM 187, IEW 175ff„ Leumann 1977:205, Schrijver 1991: 246.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: duo, duae, duo 'two' [adj. num.] (VOLat., Naev.+). Forms: nom.m. duo (P1.+),
acc.m. duds, nom.acc.n. duo (also dua in inscr.), gen.m.n. duom (Garigliano Bowl),
duum (Naev.+), dudrum (PI.+), dat.abl.m.n. duobus, nom.f. duae, acc.f. duas, gen.f.
duarum, dat.abl.f. duabus. The form duo is also used case-indifferent, e.g. duo verbis
(Nov.), acc. duo vilicos (PI.), inter nos duo).
Derivatives: duodecim 'twelve' (P1.+), ducentx 'two hundred' (P1.+), duumviri
'board of two men' (CIL, Cic.+).
Pit. nom.m. *duo. It. cognates: U. dur [nom.m.], tuf [acc.f.?], tuva [nom.acc.n.],
tuves, duir [dat.m., n.], tuvere [loc.-abl.f. + -en] 'two'. U. desenduf /acc.pl.f.]
'twelve' < *dekm + *duons; U. dupursus [dat.pl.] 'biped' < *du-pod-.
PIE *du-o-h[ (nom.du.m.) 'two', *du-o-ih! (nom.du.n.f.). IE cognates: Olr. dan, dou,
do; OW dou, MW deu < PC]. *duuo; Hit. tan 'for the second time' < *duoiom,
HLuw. twa/i- 'two', twisu 'twice*, Lyc. kbi- '(an)other'; < *dui-; Skt. dvau I duvau /
dva [nom.acc.d.m.], dve / duve [nom.acc.d.f.n.], YAv. duua [nom.d.m.], duuae-ca
[n.], duiie [acc.d.f.]; Gr. 8uo, 8uco, Arm. erlcu 'two', erkotasan 'twelve', Alb. dy, Lith.
m. du, OCS dbva 'two' < PIE *duo-h, Lith. dvi, OCS dbve < PIE *duo-ih,; Go. twai,
t
OE m. tu, OIc. m. tveir 'two'; ToA m. wu, f. we.
Original *duo has become duo by iambic shortening. A hapax duo is sometimes
alleged for Plautus (Mil. 1384), but the example is not probative (WH I: 382). The
Latin forms other than the nom.m., and all the other Italic forms of the cardinal, must
have been modeled on the nominal and pronominal plural endings. The retention of
-u- shows that the syllabification was *duo rather than *dwo. The noun duumviri goes
back to a syntagm duum virum 'of two men'. U. dupursus has replaced PIE *dui-pod-
by *du-pod- or * duo-pod-. Latin has replaced *dui- by *du- in a number of
transparent possessive compounds containing 'two': dubius, duplex, duplus,
dupondium, ducenti.
Greek 8TJO beside the dual 6v...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: durus 'hard, solid, endurable' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: duriter 'harshly' (Enn.+), duritia 'hardness' (P1.+), duritudo
'insensibility' (Cato), duracinus 'having ^hard beny or fruit* (Cato+), duramen 'hard
growth' (Lucr.+), durare 'to harden; hold out, endure' (P1.+); obdurare 'to be
persistent' (P1.+), obdurescere 'to become hard' (P1.+), perdurare 'to hold out' (P1.+).
Pit. *duro-.
PIE *du(e)h -ro- 'far, long'. IE cognates: Skt. dura- 'far, distant', Av. durdt 'from
2
afar', duire 'far', OP duraiy 'far and wide', duvaistam 'very long, very far'; Gr. 8t]p6<;
'long, too long', Dor. Sdpog, mostly Sripov, Sapov [adv.] 'long' < *dueh ro-, Arm.
2
erkar 'long (in time)' < *dueh ro-.
2
The meaning of durare must have evolved from 'be hard' to 'endure, last'. The
difference in root ablaut between PIE *duh -ro- (Ilr., Latin) and *dueh -ro- (Greek,
2 2
Arm.) is explained by Vine 2002 from the original substantival value of the latter. He
adduces textual evidence from Homer to show that 6r]p6$ was actually a noun 5rip6v
'extent, duration', and this seems convincing.
Bibl.: WH I: 384-386, EM 188f., IEW 219f., Schrijver 1991: 232, Vine 2002:
340-342. ->• dudum
U. dud [adv.] 'for the second time'.
Pit. *du-tjo-m.
IE cognates: Skt. dviiiya- 'second', OAv. daibitiia-, YAv. bitiia- 'second', abbitim
[adv.] 'two times' < Pllr. *dwi-t(i)ja~; To A wat, ToB wate 'second' < *dui-to-.
Pit. *du~tjo- probably replaces PIE *dui-tio~, cf. U. dupursus 'biped' for *dui-pod-.
The suffix was probably adopted from *tri-tio- 'third', shown by U. tertio- and Latin
tertius. A similar formation arose independently in Ilr.
Bibl.: WH I: 376, EM 188, IEW 228ff., Untermann 2000: 193f. Lat. dud
Sbrius 'drunk' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: ebriacus 'intoxicated' (Lab.), ebriolus 'tipsy' (PI.), ebriolatus 'id.'
(Lab.).
Pit. *efrio-.
PIE *hiegwh-ro- or *h,eh gwh-ro- (or *h,(o)gwh-r-o-) 'drinking'. IE cognates: Hit.
1
ekit-'/aku- /?egw-, ?gw-/ 'to drink, drink to, toast', P...

Pit. *epio-.
PIE *hiedh-l(o)~ 'kind of tree'. IE cognates: OPr. addle, Lith. egle 'spruce, fir'; Ru.
el', OCz. jedl 'spruce, fir' [f. i] , OPo. jedl [i.],jedla < BS1. *edli- < PIE h^-li-
'pine'; maybe with root ablaut Gaul, odocos 'elder' (borrowed into Gm. > OHG
attuh, attah 'dwarf-elder, danewort').

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ecce 'look!' [interj.] (PI.+)
Derivatives: eccum, eccos, ecca, eccam, eccas 'here he/she/they (is/are)' (PL, Ter.);
ecqtri 'any?' (P1.+), ecquid 'is it true that? whether' (P1.+), ecquis 'is there anyone
who?' (PL+). V
Pit. *ek H- *ke. It. cognates: Sabellic *ek(e)~ in Pael. ecuc, O. ek(uk) , U. eso
[nom.sg.f.] 'this' < *eko, *eki5 [nom.acc.], *ekso, -a [obi.].
A combination of PIE *h]e- 'he' (see s.v. is, ea, id) extended by means of *k, plus
/
*-ke (see s.v. -ce). Originally, the Italic combination *ek(e) was uninflected; it
became an inflected pronoun in Sabellic. In Latin, it was reinforced with *-ke, and
originally remained uninflected. The gendered forms eccum etc. are explained from
*ecce + *hom etc. by WH, but it cannot be excluded that these are simply inflected
forms of what was originally an uninflected demonstrative; cf. the same process in
iste and ipse. The older etymology of ecce as *ed-ke is unlikely, since Latin and PIE
had no n. pronoun *ed, only *id.
Bibl.: WH I: 390, EM 190f„ IEW 281ff., Schrijver 1991: 33, Untermann 2000: 217f.
—> -ce; is, ea, id

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: bait' (Andr.+), escarius [adj.] 'of food' (P1.+), inescare 'to entice with bait' (Ter.+);
esitdre 'to feed on' (PL+); esus, -us 'the eating' (Novius+); esunre 'to be hungry'
(P1.+), esurio 'a hungry man' (PL), esurTgo 'hunger' (Varro), esurialis 'of famine'
(PL+); media 'starvation, fasting' (P1.+); adesse 'to eat into' (Lucr.+), ambesse 'to
consume' (P1.+), ambestrTx 'gluttoness' (PL), comesse 'to eat up, spend' (P1.+),
comedo 'a glutton' (Lucil.+), exesse 'to eat up' (PL+), exesor 'which eats away'
(Lucr.), obesus 'fat, thick' (Laev. apud Gell.+), peresse 'to eat up' (P1.+), subesse *to
eat away below' (P1.+); vescus 'thin, attenuated' (Lucil.+).
Pit. *ed- 'to eat'. It. cognates: O. edum [inf.] 'to eat'; maybe also U. ezariaf
[acc.pl.] 'certain oblation' < *ed-es-asio-'?
PIE *h]ed-mi, *h,d-enti [pr.] 'to eat', pf. *h,e-hid- IE cognates: Olr. ithid, ith
'eats', ethait 'they eat' < *ed~, sb. estir < *ed-; MWji 'eats' (denominal); Hit. ed~' /
ad-, Pal. ad-, CLuw. ad-/ad- 'to eat', HLuw. ad-, Skt. admi, dtti, adanti 'id.', Gr. £5©
'to eat, devour', inf. e5p.evat (H.), frit. eSojiai, Arm. owtem, OLith. edmi 'to eat', Lith.
esti 'feeds' (of animals), eska 'food', OCS jasti 'to eat', Is .jamb, 3s .jastb < hied-tei,
jadb 'poison' < *hied-o-, Go. itan, OHG ezzan, OIc. eta < *ed-e/o-, ToB matsts- 'to
starve', ToA natsw- < PTo. *ndtsw- < PIE *n-h]d-tu-ie/o-, ToByesti 'food, meal'.
Long e in the present paradigm and in esus is due to Lachmann's law in the sequences
*edt- (> ess-) and *eds-. Lat. ellum < *edlo~, esca < *ed-sk-a. The derivational basis
for edulis is unknown. According to Risch 1954, esurire was formed as an
antonymous formation to *satur-Tre 'to be satisfied', giving rise to the class of
desideratives in -urTre. Like Keller 1982: 88f., I doubt whether the adj. vescus is
related to vescor, since the meaning can only be connected via a complicated chain of
shifts. One may alternatively suggest a compound of ve- (taken from vesanus,
veg...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: egeo 'to need, want' [v. II] (P1.+)
Derivatives: egenus 'lacking, in need of (P1.+), egestas 'extreme poverty' (P1.+);
indigere 'to need, lack' (P1.+), indigem [acc.sg. of *indigis] 'bereft' (Pac. apud Cic.),
indigus 'needy' (Lucr.).
Pit. *egos, -es- 'need, necessity'; *ege- 'to lack'. It, cognates: O. egmo [nom.sg.],
egm[as] [gen.sg.], egmad [abl.sg.], egmazum [gen.pl.] 'case, question' < *eg-ma~.
PIE *h|eg-es- 'lack, need'. IE cognates: OIc. ekla 'lack', OHG eko-rodo 'only';
ToAByak- 'to neglect, be careless about', ToB sb. yaknantar 'they must neglect'.
The noun eges-tas and the adj. egenus < *egesno- betray an old s-stem *egos, -es-.
The compounds *indigis and indigus < * end-ego- contain *endo 'in'. Untermann
suggests that O. *egma- 'case, question' may be derived from 'what one needs',
hence from the root of 'to want, need' attested in egeo. The pair egere : * egos-
matches part of the 'Latin Caland system' as described by Nussbaum 1999a. Either of
these forms may be old. The root etymology and the cognates are not tremendously
convincing, Tocharian 'to neglect' seems to match best. Hit. ak-V akk- 'to die, be
O. eitiuvam 187
killed' is reconstructed as *Ho(k)-ei, -end to a root *h e($- by Kloekhorst
!/3
(2008: 167f.), and must thus be separated. Latin also continues a (probable) root
*h|eg- in aid 'to say'; it is possible to derive the meaning 'case, question' (O.
*eg-ma-) from 'to say', but a connection of 'to say' and 'need' (Latin *egos-) is much
more difficult.
Bibl.: WH I: 394, EM 192, IEW 290, Schrijver 1991: 35, Untermann 2000: 198, LIV
—*• aid
egO 'I' [pron. pers. Is. nom.] (VOLat+; Axdea, vase inscr. (CIL 474) ego; ego P1.+)
Pit. *ego. It. cognates: Ven. ego, Fal. eqo, eko, eco 'I', SPic. ekii 'I' (?).
PIE *heg, *hieg-(o)H, *hieg-H-om 'I'. IE cognates: Hit uk; Skt. aham, OAv.
t
azSm, as-cit, YAv. azdm, OP adam < h[egHom; Gr. eyro, Lesb. ep. Dor. also eyd>v,
Lac. Tarent. eytovr] < *h,eg- + -e/oH; Arm. es; OPr. es, as, Lith. ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: emo, -ere 'to take (Paul, ex F.), buy (P1.+)' [v. Ill; pf. emf, ppp. emptum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: (1) em particle 'here you are', emax 'fond of buying' (Cato+), empticius
'obtained by purchase' (Varro+), emptor 'buyer' (P1.+), empturTre 'to hanker after
buying things' (Varro), emptus, -Us 'the purchasing' (Varro); maybe ellum 'there he
is!' (PI., Ter.) and ellam 'there she is!' (PI.) is from *em-illu/am\ (2) Compounds
based on the meaning 'to take': adimere 'to remove, take away' (Naev.+), comere (pf.
compst) 'to adom, arrange' (P1.+), comptus, -us 'adornment' (Lucr.+), demere, pf.
dempsl 'to remove' (P1.+), demptio 'removal' (Varro), vindemia 'vintage' (P1.+),
vindemitor 'grape-picker' (Naev.), vindemiator 'grape-picker' (Varro+), virgidemia 'a
harvest of rods' (P1.+), dirimere 'to pull apart, separate' (Lucr.+), eximere 'to take
out, omit' (P1.+), eximius 'excepted; exceptional' (P1.+), exemptid 'removal'
(Varro+), exemplum 'example' (P1.+), exemplar, -oris 'typical instance' (Lucr.+),
interimere 'to kill, destroy' (P1.+), perimere 'to destroy' (Cato+), praemium
'payment, reward, booty' (P1.+), praemiosus 'profitable' (Cato+), praemiator 'who
collects rewards' (Naev.), promere, pf. prompsi 'to bring out, forward' (P1.+),
promptus, -us 'the action of maming available' (Lucr.+), promus 'steward' (P1.+),
supprdmus 'assistant steward' (PI.), promptdre 'to be in charge, be steward' (PI.),
promptarius 'that serves for storing' (P1.+), exprdmere 'to bring out, reveal', sumere,
pf. suremi, surempsit (Andr. apud Paul, ex F.) and sumpsT 'to take up, seize' (Andr.+),
sumptid 'the taking' (Cato+), sumptudsus 'expensive, lavish' (P1.+), sumptus, -us
'spending, expense' (PI.+); absumere 'to use up' (P1.+), absumedd 'act of
squandering' (PI.), assumere 'to take, add' (Varro+), consiimere 'to consume,
destroy' (Cato+), resumere 'to pick up again, put on again' (Enn.+); (3) Compounds

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: en 'ever?; behold! * [interj.] (P1.+; in OLat. en only occurs in en umquam 'ever')
PIE *hien? IE cognates: Gr. fjv, f]f|v [inteij.] 'hey!'.
Inteijection which calls the attention. At least a part of the Latin attestations, esp.
from Verg. on, will represent the identical Greek word fjv. According to Leumann, en
derives from *est-ne 'is it not?'. For the OLat. usage with umquam, this seems quite
conceivable; the blunt rejection by WH is unmotivated. But it seems doubtful whether
all syntactic uses in Lat. can be explained either from *estne or from the Greek use of
fjv; hence, it remains possible that Latin inherited a particle *en.
Bibl.: WH 1:403f., EM 196, IEW 314, Leumann"!977: 93, 209.
endo, indu(~), indi- 'in, on, to' [pref., postpos.y prep.] (Duenos inscr. ted endo 'to
you', Lex XII endo 'within, onto, thereupon', OLat. endo, indu [prep.] from Ennius to
Cicero, as an archaism) > *
Derivatives: indoles, -is [f.] 'nature' (P1.+); indotuetur 'watches' (Enn.); induere 'to
put on' (P1.+); indugredior 'ingredior' (Lucr.); indupedire 'to hinder' (Lucr.);
induperator 'imperator' (Enn., Lucr., Juv.), induperare 'imperare' (Enn.); industrius
'diligent' (P1.+); induvolare 'involare' (Enn.); indigena [f.] 'native' (Verg.+);
indigere 'to need, require' (P1.+), *indigis 'bereft of (Pac.), indigus 'needy' (Lucr.);
indiges 'native (deity)' (Verg.+); indipiscd/or 'to overtake, acquire' (P1.+).
Pit. *(e)ndo 'in, into'.
PIE *h](e)n-do 'inside'. IE cognates: Olr. and 'in it', Hit. anda 'into, within', andan
'within, inside', CLuw. anta 'into', HLuw. anta 'within', Lyc. nte 'inside' <
*hindo(m); Gr. evSov 'inside, at home'.
V
Word-final short Vo after a heavy first syllable can only be explained as a retention of
PIE *-o, since iambic shortening does not apply. It must be assumed that the shift of
*-o > *-e (whence indi-gena; cf. 2s.ipv.med. -re < *so) was relatively recent. Sommer
1914 argues that -o was also supported by Greek reminiscences (like evSo...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: enim 'well!; for' [ptcle.] (Naev.+)
Pit. *eno- 'that one', *enim [acc.sg.]. It. cognates: O. fnlm, inim., inim, ini, EIVEIJJ.,
inim, Pael. inim li(:)ni(:)ml, U. ene, eiye, enem 'and' lenemi < *enim; in U. only
connecting two sentences. The first element also in O. e-tanto. With a different suffix
vowel: Pael. inom 'and' < *enom (or spelling error for inim) . U. enu, ennom, enno,
enom, eno '(and) then' < *endom < *eno-dom; U. enuk 'then' < *en-do(d)~k(e); U.
enumek 'then' < ennom-ek; inumek, inumk, inuk '(and) then' < inom + -ek, or
spelling variant for enumek.
PIE *hje-no-. IE cognates: Gr. eiceivoq 'that' < *e-ke-eno~.
IEW derives enim from the stem of Lith. anas 'that', OCS om> 'he'. Yet I reconstruct
this pronoun as *h en-. Since Gr. eiceivoi; seems to contain a stem *en(i/o)~, Beekes
2
(1995: 202) suggests that *h en- was "transformed in *eno~" in Greek. Initial *e~, I
2
presume, would have been taken from the pronoun *he, *hji 'this' (see Lat. is, ea,
t
id). This is possible. Alternatively, PIE *hie might have acquired a suffix *-no~,
directly yielding Pit. *eno~. This is reminiscent of Skt. and [adv.] 'thus', OAv. ana,
YAv. ana [ins.sg.] 'with this' (Fischer-Ritter 1991: 10). Since Sabellic also shows
evidence for a stem *eno~, this may have been the Pit. stem. The isolated Pit. form
*enim may point to the earlier existence of pronominal variants with an additional *-i,
as found e.g. in Hittite asi / uni / ini 'that (one)' < *h]6s+i, *h]6m+i, *h|i+m+i
(Kloekhorst 2008: 220f.). If interpreted as an accusative of direction, *eni-m can be
explained from a semantic shift 'to that' > 'in addition' > 'and'.
Bibl.: WH I: 404f., EM 96, IEW 319ff., Meiser 1986: 110f., Klingenschmitt 1987:
175, Schrijver 1991: 36, Untermann 2000: 225f., 343f. nam, nunc

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ensis 'sword' [m. i] (Cato+; almost only in poetry)
Derivatives: ensiculus 'toy sword' (PI.).
Pit. *ensi- 'sword'.
PIE *(hie)ns-i- 'sword, large knife'?
All the alleged IE cognates are uncertain: the connection with Skt. asi- [m.] 'sword,
slaughtering knife' is doubted by EWAia I: 145; in Palaic hasira- 'dagger', there is no
trace of a nasal. The appurtenance of Gr. fxop 'sword', allegedly an Aeolic reflex of
*ns-r, is also disputed. Morphologically, *ns~(o)r in Greek vs. *ns-i in Latin would be
difficult to account for.
Bibl.: WH I: 406, EM 197, IEW 771, Schrijver 1991: 63.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: prohibent', inebra [nom.pl.n.] 'omnia, quae tardant vel morantur agentem'.
Pit. *n~xafro- 'inhibiting'.
The meaning suggests a derivation from (the same preform as) inhibed 'to hold back,
prevent'. We find once <enubro> and several times <enibr-> (gloss.) and <inebr->
(Paul, ex F.). These point to a stem *enu/ibro~, nom.sg.m. *enu/iber. The forms in
unraised en- are the oldest, while the vowel quality -u- is determined by the following
labial. If cognate with *en-habere, the source form was *n~xqfi'os > *en-hsbros >
nom.sg.m. OLat. en-uber. In the oblique cases, *a in a closed syllable regularly gave
e, hence inebr-. The resulting alternation *enu/iber ; *enebr- was levelled in different
directions.
Bibl.: WH 1:406, EM 197. habed
eo, Ire 'to go' [v. irr.] (Lex XII, P1.+). Forms: pr. ed, is, it, imus, itis, eunt; ptc. tens,
euntis', 3p.pr.ind. obinunt, prodTnunt, redinunt (Enn.); pf. IV: // / ivi (both P1.+), ppp.
Hum.
Derivatives: itus, -us 'the going' (Lucr.+); abire 'to go away' (Naev.+), abitid 'a
departing' (P1.+), abitus, -us 'departure' (P1.+), adire 'to approach' (P1.+), adiiio 'the
act of approaching' (P1.+), aditdre 'to approach often' (Enn.), aditus, -us 'approach,
access' (P1.+), ambire 'to solicit (for); go round' (P1.+), ambitio 'soliciting,
candidature' (P1.+), ambitus, -us 'circuit, cycle' (Varro+), ant(e)ire 'to surpass;
precede' (P1.+), antidire 'to surpass' (PI.), circumire 'to go round' (P1.+), circu(m)itio
'circumlocution, rotation' (Ter.+), circu(m)itus, -us 'cycle' (Varro+), coire 'to come
together, have sexual intercourse' (P1.+), coetus, -iis 'encounter, assembly' (P1.+),
coitio 'meeting, partnership' (Ter.+), exire 'to come out' (Naev.+), exitio 'the going
out' (PL), exitus, -its 'departure, conclusion' (Lucil.+), exitium 'destruction, death'
(P1.+), exitiabilis 'causing death' (P1.+), exitialis 'causing death' (Lucr.), mire 'to go
into; begin' (P1.+), initare 'to enter habitually' (Pac.), initus, -us 'entry' (Luc...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: epulum 'public feast' [n. o\ (Naev.+)
Derivatives: epulae [f.pl.] 'sumptuous meal, banquet' (P1.+), epuldnus 'banqueter'
(Paul, ex F.\ epulari 'to dine sumptuously' (Acc.+), coepulonus 'table-companion' (PI.).
Pit. *ep(V)lo- 'ritual'?
PIE *h]ep-lo-. IE cognates: OHG uoba 'festival' (deverbal to uobenl) .
There is no hard evidence that epulum referred to a religious or sacrificial banquet: all
attestations can mean just 'banquet, large meal for celebrating'. If derived from a
verb, the only good candidate within Latin is apio < *hip- 'to seize': a 'banquet' as an
'opportunity to grab food'. In fact, Nussbaum 1997: 188 has proposed the etymology
*hep-lo-, he sees the same stem reflected in sollemnis. Since the cognate forms (if
!
they are cognate) in Gm. contain a different root vowel, and since it is uncertain that
epulum has anything to do with 'taking' or 'seizing', the connection with *h,ep- 'to
take' remains a root etymology. WH and EM connect epulum with ops 'work', but
this is semantically uncompelling, and it has now formally become impossible in
view of the reconstruction *h3ep- for ops.
Bibl.: WH 1:410, EM 199, Schrijver 1991: 36. apTscor, sollus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: equus 'horse' [m. o] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: equa 'mare' (P1.+), equola 'young mare' (P1.+), eculeus 'foal, pony'
(Varro+); equile 'stable for horses' (Cato+), equinus 'of horses' (Acc.+); eqiaso
'horse groom' (Varro+); equaria 'herd of horses' (Varro), Equirria/Ecurria, -drum
[n.pl.] 'annual horse races' (Varro+); eques, -itis [m.] 'horse rider; knight' (P1.+);
equitare 'to ride a horse' (Lucil.+), equitatus, -us 'cavalry' (Cato+).
Pit. *ekwo- 'horse'. It cognates: Ven. ekvon [acc.sg.] 'horse', ekvopetaris, ekvopetars,
equpetars, ecupetaris, ekupetaris, eppetaris, epetaris [nora.sg.m.] 'of a horse-rider' <
*ekwo-pet-dripresupposing *ekwo-pet-o~ 'horse-rider' or 'charioteer'.
PIE *hie&-u- 'horse'. IE cognates: Gaul, epo-, Olr. ech 'horse'; Hit. *ekku- [c,],
CLuw. assu- or azzu- [c.], HLuw. asu-, Lyc. esb- 'horse' < PAnat. *Hek-u-\ Skt.
cisva- 'horse', Av. aspa-; Myc. i-qo, Gr. mnoq 'horse', uinoxr^ 'horse rider', Arm. es
'horse'; OPr. aswinan 'horse milk', Lith. asvienis 'stallion'; OE eoh, OIc. jor, ToB
yakwe, ToAyuk 'horse'.
The original Latin paradigm must have been *ekos, acc.sg. *ekom, gen.sg. *ekwT; the
stem equ~ was generalized from the oblique cases. The noun eques < *ekw(o)-it~ may
have been formed on the model of pedes 'pedestrian' < *ped-it- 'going on foot'.
Kloekhorst 2008: 237ff. argues that the Anatolian forms point to an original PIE w-stem
r M f
*hjek-u- 'horse', which was thematized to *h]ekuo- in the other branches of IE.
Bibl.: WH I: 412, EM 199f., IEW 301, Lejeune 1974: 334, Pulgram 1976, Leumann
1977: 86, 372, Schrijver 1991: 36.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: er, -is 'hedgehog' [m. r] (P1.+; <irim> Capt. 184; <erem> Nemes. Cyn. 57)
Derivatives: irTcius 'hedgehog; spiked barrier' (Varro+).
Pit. *xer~.
PIE *gher- 'hedgehog'. IE cognates: Gr. XHP 'hedgehog' (only attested in Hesych),
Xoipoc, 'porcupine' < *ghorio-,Alb. derr 'pig, swine'. "
The form erTcius suggests that PI; irim is a mistake of the transmission for *erim. In that
case, the original stem may have been /ir(-i')-!, which can be connected with Greek yf\ p
if Latin earlier had *her < *gher(-). This may reflect a PIE root noun nom.sg, *gher-s to
the root *gher- 'to be excited, be bristly' found in horior and others.

horior, horreo

Derivatives: erctum (ciere) 'inheritance, inherited part' (Cic.+), inercta 'indivisa'
(Paul, ex F.), disertiones 'divisiones patrimoniorum inter consortes' (Paul, ex F. 12)

PIE *h,rk-i- 'to divide'? IE cognates: Hit. ark- / ark- 'to cut off, divide' < PIE

The Latin formation suggests an original present *ercTre. According to Eichner 1982:

suggests original *orc- for Latin, which was analogically changed to ere-. LIV, on the
other hand, reconstructs a preform *h;rk~Je- which would phonetically yield arc-
(thus Schrijver 1991: 72f.); in view of existing arced, it seems unlikely that *arcire
would have been changed to *erc~. Hence, the preform for Latin *ercTre may have
been an /-present *h erk-i~. Yet all of this remains rather uncertain. Petit 2004 argues
{
that Lith. arsyti 'to tear up' < *hiOrk- also belongs here.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: err5, -are 'to roam; waver' [v. I] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: errantia 'misguided condition' (Acc.), errabundus 'wandering'
(Lucr.+), erraticus 'wandering, wild' (Cato+), err alio 'the roaming* (P1.+); error
'doubt; mistake' (P1.+); aberrdre 'to wander away, stray* (P1.+), deerrare 'to wander
off (P1.+).
Pit. *ers-aje~.
PIE *hiers-o/h - 'erring, error'. IE cognates: Arm. eram 'to boil; be restless' <
2
*ersa~; Go. airzeis 'mistaken', airzjan, OHG irren 'to be mistaken; lead into mistake'
< *erz-(i)io~.
Both Latin and Armenian seem to continue a denominative verb *ers-dje- 'to be
restless' to a noun *erso- or *ers-h . Although these words are not discussed in LIV,
r
their phonological shape matches PIE *h]ers- 'to flow' (Skt. ar§ati). If the noun
meant 'flowing to and fro, uncontrolled flowing', it is conceivable that a derived
meaning 'erring, error' arose already in PIE. Nussbaum (2007b) connects the PIE root
*hjer- 'to arrive, reach', and compare the s/c-present *h|r-ske/o- (Skt. /•cchdti, Gr.
EpXopat).
Bibl.: WH I: 416f., EM 201, IEW 336£, Klingenschmitt 1982: 96, Schrijver 1991:
36, LIV *h,ers-.
Sruca 'caterpillar; rocket (kind of herb)' [f. a] (Moretum+; a variant is uruca)
The most likely analysis is er-uca. If the basis is indeed the same is er 'hedgehog', the
caterpillar was denominated for its rugged back, as it often is. The suffix was
probably adopted from a semantically close word, cf. especially verruca 'wart;
hillock'.
Bibl.: WH I: 417, EM 201, Leumann 1977: 340. er

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: erus 'master, lord' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: era 'woman, mistress' (P1.+), erilis 'of a master' (P1.+).
Pit. *eso- 'master'.
PIE *hiesh -o- 'master'. IE cognates: Gaul. Esus, Aesus 'important god of the
2
Gauls'; Hit. isha- 'master, lord' [c.].
The most obvious connection is with the Gaulish deity *Esu/o-\ Lucian indicated that
the e- of the Celtic word equals Latin e, but that does not mean much historically.
IEW connects ems with Gr. euq 'good, noble' < *hjsu-. If Ilr. *asu- 'life' also
belongs to this root, Latin eras might also continue *hes-u- 'the good one' >
r
'master'. Yet the w-stem would have switched to an o-stem in the prehistory of Latin,
which is unusually early. Also, the semantics are not compelling. Hit. isha- would
match perfect semantically, as well as phonetically (if the reconstruction of Hit. is
correct), and this option is preferred by Schrijver. Still, the word remains isolated
within IE.
Bibl.: WH 1:419, EM 201f„ IEW 342, Schrijver 1991: 36.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ervumi 'a kind of cultivated vetch' [n. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: ervilia 'cultivated vetch' (Varro+).
Pit. *erVgw-o-7
IE cognates: Mir. orbaind 'grains'; Gr. SpoPoq, Gr. epepivfloc; 'chick-pea'; OHG
araweiz, OS erwit 'peas' < PGm. *arwait-.
The Latin, Greek and Gm. words clearly belong together, but a common phonological
preform cannot be reconstructed. Since the morphological make-up of these words is
different (Gr. -ivfloq, Gm. *-ait\ presence vs. absence of a medial vowel) and the word
refers to an autochthonous crop, it may represent a loanword from a non-IE
substratum language.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: et 'and what is more, too; and' [cj., adv.] (Lex XII, Andr.+)
Derivatives: etiam 'still, also, even' (Andr.+), etenim 'and indeed* (P1.+), eisi 'even
if (P1.+).
Pit. *eti. It. cognates: U. et, et, Pael. et 'and' < *eti. In U., et connects syntagms, not
sentences.
PIE *hieti 'still, also'. IE cognates: Gaul, eti 'yet, still, equally', etic 'and* <
*eti-kwe; Skt. ati, Av. aid 'beyond, over, to', Gr. sxi 'still, also, further', NPhr. eu-
'and'; Go. ip 'but', id-weit [n.] 'reproach', OIc. idja-grcenn 'green again', OHG
it(i)-wiz [n.], OE ed-wit [n.] 'disgrace' < PGm. *ip(i), OHG it-mdli 'festive, solemn',
MDu. ad/t-mael [n.] 'period of the day', OFr. et-mei [n.] 'half or whole a day', OE
ed-m&le [n.] 'feast' < PGm. *id(i).
Originally used to add something to a previous utterance, or to indicate the sequence
of different actions. Later on, it replaces -que. Possibly, PIE *hieti is an old loc.sg. to
a root noun from *h et~ 'go beyond' (reflected in Skt. at- 'to travel, wander').
}
Bibl.: WH 1:421, EM 202, IEW 344, Schrijver 1991: 37, Untermann 200: 240.

only occurs in compounds front of/-, and even there, it is often assimilated to ef-.)

As a preverb: O. eh-, ee-, U. e-, eh-, ehe-, e-, eh- /e-/ < *ex~. O. ehtrad [prep. + acc.]

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: expretus 'extinguished (vel sim.)' [adj. o/a] (PI. lxitoc.446, Paul, ex F.)
Pit. *eks-preto- or *eks-spreto~.
In the literature, expretus only occurs in Plautus, in the line it magister quasi lucerna /
undo expretus linteo. Equating linteus with the wick of a candle, Klingenschmitt
(1989: 95) translates the line as follows: 'The teacher withdraws like an oil lamp,
extinguished, when the wick is still drenched [scil. with oil]'. Hence, expretus would
mean 'extinguished'. K. assumes a word play with linteum, which can also mean
'hair', which, in the given context, may be drenched with blood from a wound
inflicted on the teacher, K. assumes that a candle was usually 'extinguished' by
means of blowing it out, and hence he suggests that expretus belongs to a root *preh
r
'blow', which LIV and others reconstruct for Gr, TUp-TcpuM-i. This is conceivable. Yet a
derivation from spernere, spretus 'to disdain, scom' cannot be dismissed as easily as
K. does. He doubts that spernere, which originally meant 'to kick, tread', would have
undergone a semantic shift from 'kicked out' to 'blown out'. Yet in view of the
sudden extinction of a flame when blown out, I think that such metaphorical usage
cannot be excluded.
Bibl.: WH I: 431, EM 206, Klingenschmitt 1989 (= 2005: 269-283), LIV *preh?
r
*spherH-? —* spernere

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ex(s)ul, -is 'banished person' [m. /] (Pl,+)
Derivatives: ex(s)ulare 'to be banished' (P1.+), ex(s)ilium 'exile' (P1.+) [only
<exul-> and <exil-> in inscr.].
Pit. *ek(-)s(-)Vl- (or derived from a verb in *ek(-)s(-)Vl~).
Several etymologies are possible. It might be a derivative of a verb *ex-sulere 'to take
out' to the root *selh 'to take', cf. consul and consulere; hence exsul 'the one who is
r
taken out'. It might belong to amb-ulare < *-al- 'to walk', hence 'who walks out'. It
might even belong to *h]elh -, the root of Gr. eXawco 'to drive': ex-ul 'who is driven
2
out' (cf. proelium).
Bibl.: WH I: 432, EM 207, Benedetti 1988: 164-169. -»• ambuld

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: faba 'bean' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: fabulus 'bean' (Pl.+),fabaginus 'of beans' (Cato),fabaiia 'bean-stalks'
(Cato+), fabalis 'of beans' (Varro+), fabarius 'of/for beans' (Cato+), fabatns 'made
of beans' (Varro+).
Pit. *fqfa- 'bean'. It cognates: Fal. haba 'bean' [Velius Longus, 2nd c. AD],
IE cognates: OPr. babo 'bean', Ru. bob; OIc. baun, OHG bona, OE bean 'bean' <
PGm. * baund < * bat-no? Farther removed are Gr. (paKoq 'lentil' and Alb. bathe
'horse-bean' < *b(h)ak-.
No PIE word can be reconstructed. Since the Italic, Slavic and Germanic words are
similar in form and meaning, they are probably independent loanwords from a
European substratum word of the form *bab- (or similar) 'bean'.
Bibl.: WH 1:436, EM 208, IEW 106, Schrijver 1991: 488, Kuiper 1995: 79f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: faber, -bri 'craftsman, artisan* [m. o] (P1.+) *
Derivatives: fabre 'skilfully' (P1.+), infabre 'unskilfully' (Pac.+), fabrtlis 'of a
workman' (Varro+); fabrica 'workshop; craft; plan' (P1.+), fabricare/i 'to fashion,
build; devise' (P1.+), perfabricare 'to fashion completely' (PI.), fabricator 'maker'
(Lucr.+).
Pit *fafro~. It. cognates: Pael .faber [nom.sg.?] is probably a loan from Latin.
PIE *dhHbh
-ro- 'craftsman'? IE cognates: Arm. darbin 'smith' (< *dabr-mo-)\ Lith.
daba 'nature, habit, character', dabar 'now', dabnits 'smart, well-dressed, elegant',
OCS podoba 'ornamentation, decency', Ru. doba (dial.) 'time, measure', OCS dobrb
'good, beautiful", Ru. dobryj 'good'; Go. ga-daban 'to happen', pf. gadob 'it fits',
gadof ist 'it is fitting', OE gedefe 'fitting'.
The noun fabrica suggests the earlier existence of a feminine noun to which an adj.
*fabriko- referred; maybe ars 'art, craft'. According to Leumann 1977: 337,fabrica
was formed after a Greek example, e.g. VOUTIKT] (TE^VTI). The closest cognate is
Armenian darbin; together with faber, it could reflect PIE *dhHbh-. The BS1. words
meaning 'nature' and 'good' (with short vowel in front of b < *bh) and the Gm. words
*dab~, *ddb- could go back to PIE *dhobh-. Their connection with faber is possible,
but semantically not compelling. Beekes (1996: 230) argues that, if all these words
are cognate, they can only be explained from a non-IE root
Bibl.: WH I: 436f., EM 208, IEW 233f., Schrijver 1991: 102, Untermann 2000: 254,
LIV *dheHbh- 'to fit'.
faciO, -ere 'to make' [v. Ill; pf. feci, ppp. factum, sb. faxo, faxim, faxTs, pr.ipv. fac]
(VOLat.+: Duenos inscr. feced tfekedl 'fecit', Praeneste (315BC)/ecit/, Corchiano (4th
c. BC) feced, Lacus Fucinius fecront, Paestum fecere 'fecerunt'; Lex XIH-)
Derivatives: facilis 'easy' (P1-+), facul 'facile' (Pac., Acc., Lucil.), facilitas 'ease'
(Ter.+), facultas 'easiness; ability, skill, chance' (Ter.+), difficilis 'difficult' (P1.+;

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: faex, -cis 'wine-lees, dregs' [f. k] (Cato+)
Derivatives: faecarius 'used for lees' (Cato), faecatus 'made from lees' (Cato),
faeceus 'resembling dregs, foul' (PI.), faecula 'dried lees of wine' (Lucr.+);
defae/icare 'to remove the dregs' (PI.+).
No etymology.
Bibl.: WH 1:444, EM 213.
fSgus 'beech' [f. o] (Varro+)
Derivatives: fdgineus 'beech-' (Cato+), fagutalis 'of the beech-grove' (Varro+).
Pit. *fdgo-.
PIE *bheh g-o- 'oak' I 'beech'. IE cognates: Gaul. *bagos 'beech' (in French place
2
names), Gr. (p-qyoc,, Dor. cpayoc; [f.] 'kind of oak, acorn', OHG buoh 'written
document, book', OHG buohstap 'letter' < *'piece of beech for writing', OIc. bok,
OE boc, bece (< *bdJgon-), OHG buohha 'beech', Go. boka 'letter'. Unrelated: Alb.
bung 'kind of oak' and Slav. *buzt 'elder'.
The adj.fagutdlis presupposes a derivative *Jdgutumtbeech-grove'. The restriction to
western IE languages and the reference to different trees have suggested to some
scholars that this word was not PIE, but a later loanword. In the Balkans, from which
the beech started to spread after 6000 BC, the Gr. word means 'oak', not 'beech'. Yet
'oak' and 'beech' are both 'fruit-bearing trees', so that a semantic shift from 'oak' to
'beech' appears quite conceivable. The word itself may then have been PIE after all.
Bibl.: WH I: 445, EM 213, IEW 107f., Leumann 1977: 276, Schrijver 1991: 142,
Demiraj 1997: 112f., EIEC 58f. fraxinus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: falls, -ere 'to deceive' [v. Ill; pf.fefelli; ppp.falsum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: fallacia 'deceptive behaviour, trick' (Pl+),falldx 'deceitful' (Lucil.+);
falsus 'erroneous, untrue' (Lex XI1+), falsdrius 'a forger' (Cato+); falsidicus 'lying'
(PI.), falsificus 'acting deceitfully' (Pi.), falsiiurius 'swearing falsely' (PI.),
falsiloquus 'speaking deceitfully', falsimonia 'deception' (PL); refellere 'to refute'
(Ter.+).
Pit. *fal-s-e/o- [pr.], *fal-s- [pf.].
PIE *sgwhh (e)l-n- 'to stumble'. IE cognates: Skt. (Br.+) skhalate [3s.pr.med.] 'to
2
stumble, fail', MP skarwidan 'to stumble, stagger'; Gr. cxpc&Aoo 'to bring down',
otpaXXopai 'to fall', aor. eo<paXr]v 'I fell down', Arm. sxalem 'to stumble, fail'.
The pr. may represent either *fal-d~, *fal-n- or *fal-s~. The pf. fe-fellT is a recent
formation *fe-fall- on the basis of the pr., as shown by word-internal -f- and by -ellT
(instead of -ulT< *-Vl-ai, cf.pello -pepuli). The original pf. may have been *fal-s-ai;
thus Meiser 1998 and 2003. The ppp. can have been formed analogically after e.g.
sallo -salsus. The Ilr., Arm. and Gr. forms are very close in meaning and form, and
can be connected if Gr. continues a labiovelar. The correspondence is irregular
inasmuch as it seems to require a PIE voicaless aspirate stop, but this might be due to
an original heavier cluster of consonants in (pre-)PIE, cf. Lubotsky 2006 on PIE
*TsperH- 'to kick with the heel' possibly from *pds-per(H)-. Iffallo belongs here, it
has developed metaphorically from 'stumble' to 'deceive' (the tr. semantics are
explained by the nasal present, as in Greek).
Bibl.: WH I: 447ff„ EM 213f., IEW 489f„ Leumann 1977: 169, Meiser 1998: 211,
2003: 176f., LIV *(s)gybh el-.
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: falx, -cis 'hook, scythe, sickle' [f. t, gen.pl. -ium] (Cato+)
Derivatives :falcula 'pruning-knife, bill-hook' (Cato+).
IE cognates: Olr. delg [m.] 'thorn, pin', W. dala 'sting' < PCI. *delgo- 'pin, needle';
Lith. dilge 'nettle', Lith. dilgus 'stinging, smarting', OIc. dalkr 'pin, spine, dagger,
knife* [m.], OE delg 'clasp', dale 'bracelet'.
The suggested connection with Sicilian Z&YKXT|, ACIVKXE" 'sickle' (thus still IEW) is
too uncertain. EM suspect a loanword origin, which in case of a technical word like
this would not be surprising. Semantically, the comparison with Olr. delg, OIc. dalkr
seems acceptable, but Latin -al- cannot be derived from a root *ctlg~, nor can
word-final k. So at most, falx is a borrowing from a non-Latin IE language of Italy.
Bibl.: WH I: 449f., EM 214, IEW 247.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: famulus 'servant, slave' [m. o] (P1.+; nom.sg. famul Enn., Lucr., gen.pl. famulum
Verg.)
Derivatives: famula 'serving woman, maid' (Cic.+), famulanter 'in the manner of a
servant' (Acc.), famulitas 'servitude' (Pac.+); familia 'household, servants' (Lex
XII+; gen.sg. usually familias), familiaris 'of one's household, private' (PI +),
familiaricus 'of household slaves' (Varro+), familiaritas 'close friendship' (Ter.+).
Pit. *famelos 'slave', *famelia 'household'. It. cognates: Pael. famel, O. fml
[nom.sg.] 'slave' < *famelos, O. famelo [nom.sg.], U. famerias [nom.pl.?]
'household' < *famelia-.
PIE *dhhm-elo- 'fundament'. IE cognates: Gr. -fle^EiXux [n.pl.] 'fundaments' (from
r
metrical lengthening of tfejie^ia) , iSepeXioq 'belonging to the fundaments', tfejioxx;
'expositions, admonitions' (Hsch.), 'justice, law'.
Rix 1994a interprets Pit. *famelo- as a back-formation to *famelia 'household', f. of
an adj. *famelio- 'basic; of the house' which could belong to a pre-form PIE
*dhh -m-elo- 'fundament'. The latter was probably a diminutive to *dhhmo- 'basis'.
1 r
This explanation implies that a semantic shift from 'basis' to 'house, dwelling-place'
took place at some point; compare the meanings of Skt. dhaman- 'law;
dwelling-place'.
Bibl.: WH I: 452f., EM 215, IEW 235ff., Benveniste 1969 II: 102-105, Rix 1994a:
35ff., Untermann 2000: 262-264, LIV *dheh.
r

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fanum 'shrine, temple, consecrated place' [n. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: hanula (Paul, ex F.) 'small temples', fanare 'to dedicate' (Varro+),
fdndticus 'of a temple; enthusiastic' (Ctc.+); profanus 'secular, not religious' (P1.+),
profanare 'to offer in front of a temple; to desecrate' (Cato+).
Pit. *fasno- 'temple'. It. cognates: O. fnsnu [nom.sg.], fusnam, fisnam, Pael.
fesn(am) [acc.sg.], U. fesnafe [acc.pl. + -en] ,fesnere [abl.pl. + -en] 'temple' <
PSab. *fesna-. . j
PIE *dhh]5-no- 'divine, holy; consecrated place'. IE cognates: see s.v.Jeriae.
Fanum represents a derivative in *-no- from the root *dhh]S- 'gift' which is also
reflected in feriae (see there for the farther etymology). According to Schrijver, U.
fesna- may continue an original plural (collective) *dheh|S-n-h to the sg. *dhhs-no-.
2 r
Alternatively, one might assume that Sabellic has adopted the full grade *fes- from
*fes-io- cf. Lat .feriae. The form hanula is probably a dialectal form, with f- > h-.
i
Bibl.: WH I: 453f., EM 215f., IEW 259, Schrijver 1991: 92, Untermann 2000:
281-283, LIV *dheh. -> feralis, feriae

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: far, -rris 'husked wheat, emmer; grain, flour' [n. r] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: farina 'flour' (P1.+) (< *farrina), farinarius 'of flour' (Cato+),
farraceus 'of emmer' (Varro+), farrago, -inis 'mixed crop of inferior grains'
(Varro+),farrariusfarrearius 'for grain' (Cato).
i
Pit. *fars, gen.sg *fars-Vs 'flour'. It. cognates: O. far, U. far [acc.sg.], farer
[gen.sg.] 'flour'; U. farariur [nom.pl.m.] 'of the grain' < *-asio-; U. fasiu, farsio,
fasio [acc.pl.n.j 'of flour' < *farseio~. Maybe Fal. farme(n)tom [acc.n.] <
*fars-mentom, if correctly restored.
IE cognates: Olr. bairgen 'bread, loaf, W. Co. Bret, bara 'bread'; OCS brasbno
'food', ORu. borosbno '(farinaceous) food', SCr. brasno 'flour, food' < *bharsinom;
Latv. bariba 'food'; Go. barizeins 'from barley', OIc. barr 'grain', OE here 'barley'
< PGm. *bar(e/a)z-.
Italic and Slavic can continue a stem *bhars-, which Gm. has remade into an 5-stem
with ablauting suffix. Celtic may have the same root, but the suffix does not contain
*-s-. In view of the vowel *a and the semantic field (grains), it may well be a
loanword *bhars- 'primitive kind of cultivated wheat, emmer, spelt' from a non-IE
language.
Bibl.: WH I: 455f., EM 216, IEW 111, Giacomelli 1963: 244f., Leumann 1977: 180,
184, Meiser 1986: 174, Schrijver 1991: 113f., Sihier 1995: 211, 306, Untermann
2000: 265f,
farcio, -Ire 'to fill completely, stuff [v. IV; farsT,fartum\ (Enn.+)
Derivatives: farcimen 'sausage' (Lab. apud Gell.+); *fars, fartim [?LCC.\, farte [abl.]
'stuffing, mincemeat' (?\.), fartim [adv.] 'tightly, densely' (Lucil.+), farticula 'small
dish of stuffing' (Titin.), fartor 'one who fattens birds for table' (PI.+), fartum
'sausage; stuffing' (Varro+), fartura 'the stuffing' (Varro+); confercire 'to pack
closely together' (Varro+), confertus 'thronging' (Enn.+), effercire 'to cram' (P1.+),
suffarcinare 'to stuff a person by padding out his clothes' (P1.+).
Pit. *fark-je- 'to stuff, *fark-to-, *fark-ti-.
IE cognates: Gr....

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: farnus 'ash-tree (?)' [f. o] (Vitr.)
The meaning of this near-hapax cannot be ascertained. As Schrijver 1991 shows in
detail, it is very difficult to connect farnus with fraxirms by means of a common
preform or a common PIE root. The word cannot be derived from PIE *bhrHg- 'birch'
by means of known phonetic rules and without a number of ad hoc assumptions, and
the meaning is uncertain anyway. It is best to regard the etymology as unknown.
Bibl.: WH I: 458, EM 217, IEW 139f., Schrijver 1991: 106, I86f„ 489, Rasmussen
1993: 182. -»•fraxinus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fas 'what is right by divine law, morally right' [n. (indecl.) 5] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: nefastus 'contrary to law, unfit' (P1.+), nefas 'offence' (Varro+),
nefarius 'wicked, immoral' (Cato+); fastus 'lawful' (Varro+), fasti, -drum [m.pl.]
'days on which business may be done, list of festivals, list of consuls' (Cic.+),
Pit. *faos > *Jas.
PIE *bbeh -os, -es- [n.] 'saying, utterance'.
2
The noun originally survived only in the sequence fas est *'the saying exists' > 'it is
right'. From this, * fas-to- and *fas-io- were derived.
Bibl.: WH I: 105,458, EM 70, 217, IEW 105f., LIV 2.*bheh -. for, fori
2
fascinum/s 'evil spell; penis' [n./m. o] (Paul, ex F., Verg.+)
IE cognates: Gr. pacncavo^, -ov 'sorcerer, slanderer' (Att.), paoKeiv 'to say, slander'
(Hsch.). ' / /
If pacncavoc; and fascinum are indeed related, they would point to a meaning 'curse,
spell' in a loanword from an unknown third language.
Bibl.: WH I: 459, EM 218, IEW 9If., Leumann 1977: 167, Schrijver 1991: 102.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fascis 'bundle, faggot; (symbol of) power' [m. z] (P1.+)
Derivatives: fascia 'ribbon, band' (Varro+), fasciculus 'bundle, bunch' (Cato+),
fascina 'bundle' (Csto), fasciola 'ribbon, band' (Varro).
Pit. *faski- 'bundle'.
IE cognates: Olr. base 'necklace', W. beich 'burden', Bret, bec'h 'load' < PCI.
*b(h)askio-.
An Italo-Celtic correspondence *bhaski(o)- 'bundle', for which no PIE etymology is
available. Greek words such as (paKsXoc; 'bundle' and paoiaoi 'bundles of osiers'
(Hsch.) are probably unrelated, since they show irregular alternations within Greek,
and do not have exactly the same preform *bhaski-(o~).
Bibl.: WH I: 459f„ EM 218, IEW 111, Schrijver 1991: 102f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fastigo, -are 'to make pointed, taper' [v. I] (Caes.+)
Derivatives: fastigium 'sharp point, tip, roof; height, depth' (Varro+).
Pit. *farstTg- / *farsti-ag~.
PIE *bhrs-ti- 'top, point'. IE cognates: OHG burst, borst, OE byrst 'bristle' <
*bhrs-ti-; Skt. bhrsti- 'point', YAv. vouru.barasti- 'name of a part of the earth which
lies in the northwest' are derived from a root *bhfs 'to whet' by EWAia II: 273, 277,
but this does not seem certain.
EM suppose that fastTgare was backformfid from fastTgatus 'pointed', but this would
presuppose a noun *fasfigum, for which there is no evidence. Still, fastigium is older
than fastTgare, which induced Leumann to suggest that the verb was derived from the
noun in -inm. The PIE form *bhrs-ti- would yield pre-Latin *bharsti- by means of
Schrijver's rule that */- > ar in front of CCC. The (nominal) root *bhrs- is found
without suffix in Celtic: Ir. barr, W. bar, Bret, barr 'top' < *bhrs-o-.
Bibl: WH I: 461 f„ EM 218, IEW 109f., Leumann 1977: 294, Schrijver 1991: 490,
Dunkel 2000a: 94f. fastus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fastus, -us 'pride' [m. u] (Cat.+)
Derivatives: fastTdium 'disdain, aversion' (P1.+), fastTdidsus 'critical, squeamish'
(PI.+), fastidfre 'to show aversion to, scorn' (P1.+), fastidiliter 'in the manner of one
who is hard to please' (Varro).
Pit. *fars-tu-7
PIE *bhrs-tu- 'top, point'?
Lat. fastTdium reflects a cp. *fastu-taid-o-, with haplology, to taedet 'to be averse'
(P1.+-). The only reasonable connection of fastus is with *farsti- 'top'; this would
imply a semantic shift from 'top' to 'haughtiness' which is conceivable, but the
w-stem is not attested independently. Also, fastTdium would be a tautology.
Bibl.: WH I: 460ff., EM 218f., IEW 109f., Leumann 1977: 556, Schrijver 1991: 490.
—> fastTgd, taedet

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fateor 'to acknowledge, admit' [v. II; ppp.fassum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: confiterT 'to admit' (Lex XII+), Tnfitids ire 'to deny; disown' (P1.+),
profiterT 'to declare, promise' (P1.+).
Pit. *fat-e~. It. cognates: O. fatfum [inf.] 'to speak'.
PIE *bhh -to- 'spoken' and/or *-bhh -t- 'who speaks'. IE cognates: Gr. -tpaxoi; 'said'.
2 2
At first sight, faterT seems based on *fa-to- 'said', but Leumann avows his reticence
to derive an e-verb directly from a ppp. EM regard confiterT and profiterT-as older than
faterT, and based on nominal compounds of the type *kom-fat~, * pro-fat-. This type of
cp. does occur in Latin in roots in PIE *-H, but the e-type of verbs is rare; it occurs in
lateri. The noun *infitiae may continue *in-fato- 'unsaid'. The ppp. fassus < * fat-to-
must be a recent derivative from *fat~; the old ppp. of fori survives in fatus, with
introduction of the full grade from the present.
Bibl.: WH I: 462£, EM 219, IEW 105f., Leumann 1977: 555, Schrijver 1991: 96,
Untermann 2000: 266f., LIV2.*bheh -. /or,.fas
2

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fatlgo, -are 'to tire out, harass' [v. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: affatim, ad fatim 'amply, sufficiently' (Andr.+), fatTscereZ-scT 'to split
open; become weary' (Pac.+), defe/atTgare 'to exhaust, tire out' (P1.+), defetTscT (ppp.
defessus) 'to suffer exhaustion, become worn out' (PI.+), fessus 'tired, exhausted'
(Varro+),
Pit. *fati- 'exhaustion', *fasso- 'exhausted'.
All based on a noun *fatis 'exhaustion, fill', preserved in affatim. The verb fatigare is
explained by Dunkel 2000a from *fati-h g- to agere. The verb fatiscor was probably
2
built on the cp. defetiscor. In the latter, the reduction to e is irregular, since we expect
*defitiscor from *de-fati-. Probably, the e was taken from defessus. In defessus <
* de-fat-to-, the reduction to e is regular; from this cp. adj., fessus has later been
detached. All of this implies that the oldest system was a noun *fati- and a ptc.
*fasso~, hence, there must have existed a root *fat-. Wachter 2004: 378 interprets PI.
Cist. 654 fossa as 'tired, exhausted', and as the only preserved instance of * fat-to- >
*fassus, which was replaced by fessus (taken from compounds) elsewhere. The
reconstruction of a PIE root *dhH- 'to vanish' by WH is gratuitous. Theoretically, we
would need a form *bhHt- or *dhHt- to explain fat-. Unfortunately, no cogent
etymology is available.
Bibl.: WH I: 463, EM 219f., IEW 239, Leumann 1977: 83, 536, Keller 1992:
260-265, Sihler 1995: 67, Dunkel 2000a: 94.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fatuus 'silly, foolish' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: Fatuus (Varro) an oracular god. :
Pit. *fatowo- 'of speech'. It. cognates: O. fatuveis [gen.sg.], faxofe [voc.sg.] maybe
= Fatuus. r
}
PIE *bhh -tu- 'speech'. ' ;
2
I
Lat. fatuus looks like a derivative from a nominal or verbal stem *fat- or an adj.
*fatu/o~, but neither pre-Latin *fati- 'exhaustion1 nor *fato- 'said' provide an
understandable semantic basis. However, if we connect the fact that Fatuus is said to
be an alternative name for Faunus, and that he predicted the future, and that this god
is attested on an Etruscan mirror as Fatuvs in a clear oracular function (Weiss 2007b),
we may venture a derivation from for 'to say' (Untermann 2000). The name of the
god would then have come to be used pejoratively as 'silly'.
Bibl.: WH I: 464, EM 220, IEW 11 If., Untermann 2000: 268, LIV 2.*bheh -. fas,
2
for
\
Faunus 'mythical king; (pi.) deities of the countryside' [m. o] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: Fauna 'a rustic goddess' (Var.); Favonius 'the west wind, Zephyr' (P1.+).
Pit. *fawe/ono-:; *faw5n(-jo)~. It. cognates: U. fons, fos [nom.sg.m.], foner
[gen.sg.f\foner [nom.pl.] 'merciful' < *fVu(V)ni- (Schrijver).
PIE *bt1h u-n- 'favourable'. IE cognates: Olr. buan 'good, favourable; firm', MW
2
bun 'maiden, sweetheart'.
Formerly, there was no good comparison for Faunus. Since Lat. faved and U. fons
'merciful' are now derived from the PIE root *bhh u- 'to become', it becomes
2
possible to derive Faunus from a similar preform as fons, viz. Pit. *fauVno- <
*bhh u-e/ono- 'favourable'. Theoretically, *bhh eu-no- would also yield Pit. *fauno~,
2 2
but we have no other evidence for an ablaut grade *bhh eu- in Pit., nor would it be
2
expected in view of*bheh,u- in other IE branches.
If Favonius is indeed cognate, it would most likely go back to an earlier noun
*favd, -onis 'who favours', an individualizing form of a PIE n-stem adj. as attested in
Umbrian and Celtic. This also yields a good semantic moti...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: faveo 'to be favourably inclined, to approve' [v. II; pf. favT, ppp. fautum] (Naev.+;
fove CIL I2 573)
Derivatives:faventia 'auspicious behaviour' (Acc.), fautor 'supporter, partisan' (also
favitor) (PI.+),fautrix, -icis 'female supporter' (Ter.+); faustus 'fortunate; favourable'
(PI.+); maybe fovea 'maidservant' (PI.).
Pit. *faw-e-.
PIE *bhh u-eh 'to be favourable to'. IE cognates: Olr. bae 'profit, benefit' <
2 r
*bheh u-io-, Skt. bhus- 'to exert oneself for someone'.
2
Because of the rule *-ouV- > -avV-, faveo might reflect * foiled, but the hapa xfove is
unreliable (see also Vine 2006a: 225f.). Lat. faustus « *foustus < *faves-to~.
Schrijver 1991 rejects the etymologies *bhouH-eie- 'to make someone grow' (for
semantic reasons) and *dhogwh-eie- 'to make warm' (for phonetic reasons). He opts
for PIE *gwhou-eie- 'to worship', derived from PIE *gwhu- 'to heed, worship'.
Lubotsky 1995 points out that the semantics of *gwV hardly compel us to derive
faveo from it, and, furthermore, that it is not necessary to derive -ere from a causative
verb: it can also be a stative suffix. Lubotsky then supports Kortlandt's suggestion
that the original shape of the root of 'be(come)' was actually *bhh u-, from which Olr.
2
bae 'profit, benefit' can be derived as *bheh u -io-. This comes close to faveo in
2
meaning, as do, in Lubotsky's view, some of the Skt. usages of bhu- and bhus-.
V.
Hence, faveo may reflect *b h u-eh.
2 r
Bibl.: WH 1:464ff., 468, EM 220f„ IEW 453, Schrijver 1991:441 f., Lubotsky 1995:
225, Untermann 2000: 302, LIV *dheu- 'run'. Faunus,fid,fui

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: favllla 'ashes' [f. a] (Ter.+)
Pit. *fawV~.
PIE *dhouH-V- 'smoke / smoking'.
WH and EM do not doubt the appurtenance offavilla to foveo 'make warm' < 'burn';
WH reconstruct *fouilla, EM *<fgwh- > fav-. Schrijver 1991 argues that "the obscure
formation" (in -ilia) "and technical meaning of the word" render it likely that it is a
non-native word. In any case, the sequence fav- would conflict with fov- if both were
from *dhog"'h-, and none of the proposed solutions to this problem has been
satisfactory. The suffix is reminiscent of scintilla 'spark', but difficult to explain
historically. Vine 2006a: 241-243 now offers a new etymology: a derivation from
*dhuH- 'to smoke'. If Thurneysen-Havet's Law applied in Vine's formulation (i.e. in
pretonic position with PIE place of the accent), favTUa must reflect a stem form
*dhouH-V- with unaccented first syllable. Of the various possible scenarios to arrive
at such a form, Vine's first proposal is the following: PIE *dhouh -i- 'smoke, soot',
2
ins.sg. *dhouh -ihi 'with smoke, with soot' > deriv. Pit. *pouT-no- 'smoky, sooty' >
2
*pauino- > deriv. *fauin(e)la- '(small) smoky stuff = 'ashes'. Another possible
starting point would be a PIE o-stem *dhouh -o- 'smoking', whence an adj. in
2
*-iH-no- could be derived. In any case, Vine's etymology seems more likely than the
earlier proposals.
Bibl.: WH I: 466, EM 221, IEW 240f., Schrijver 1991: 442f„ LIV *dheg,,1,-,
*dhueh -. —* fove5,fumus
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: favus 'honeycomb' [m. o] (Varro+)
Etymology unknown. One might follow WH and derive favus from PIE *bhuH- /
*bhh u- 'to dwell', in the sense that the honeycomb is (part of) the 'dwelling' of bees.
2
Applying Vine's (2006) formulation of Thurneysen-Havet's Law, favus could then
reflect a preform *bhouh -o-, but final accentuation usually indicates an agent noun,
2
whereas favus would rather require a resultative noun 'building'. Alternatively, one
may think of a formation *bhueh -uo- > *fuauo- 'being' > *fauo- 'building', but this
2
reconstruction is unsupported by other evidence".
Bibl.: WH I: 469, EM 222, Schrijver 1991: 444.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fax, -cis 'torch; a light' [f. k] (P1.+)
Derivatives: facula 'torch' (P1.+); faces (Paul, ex F.; meaning not indicated); facetus
'clever, adept' (P1.+), inficetus 'boorish, humourless' (P1.+), facetia 'cleverness, joke'
(also pi. -ae) (P1.+).
Pit. *fwak!w>-(er).
IE cognates: Lith. zvake 'candle'; Gr. (p6\ji 'light'?
The derivation of facetus from *fake- (whence also faces) was doubted by EM, but
would morphologically be sound. Semantically, one may envisage a shift from
'enlightened' to 'clever'. Whether faces is a remnant of the stem *fak-e~ is uncertain,
since its meaning is unknown. It would fit, though. Schrijver 374f. is unsure whether
*fak- or *fake- must be regarded as the oldest nominal stem in Latin - it is hard to
decide. The appurtenance of Gr. (p<i\j/ is uncertain, since it is a hapax from Hesych.
Schrijver (p. 461) conjectures that it represents a remake of Gr. qmq 'light' on the
model of (Sy 'eye', in which case it would not belong here. Since the Lith. form,
might reflect PIE *ghuok-, this removes the need to reconstruct a root-final labiovelar,
though it is still a possibility. In Latin, *fuo- would have been unrounded to *fua- in
open syllable, i.e. in the oblique case forms. The root structure *Dh-T does not occur
in certain PIE words, nor can *k(w) be regarded as a root extension: this would imply a
suffix ablaut *- after a root *g\i- (homonymous with 'to pour'?), which would
be unique. Hence, if this connection of two isolated words in Latin and Baltic is
correct at all, it probably reflects a later loanword.
Bibl.: WH I: 438,471, EM 209, 222, IEW 495, Leumann 1977: 165, Schrijver 1991:
371, 374f., 461,465.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: purification' (said of luno, Paul, ex F.), Februarius [adj.] '(the month) February; of
February' (Varro, Cic.+), februatio 'ceremony of purification' (Varro), februatus
'concerned with purification' (Varro, Paul. exF.).
Pit. *f(w)esro- or *fetfvro-.
PIE *dhues-ro- 'the smoking' or *dI,egwh-ro- 'the burning'.
Februum, -ua represent a substantivized adj. *februus 'purificatory', which in its turn
presupposes earlier *febro- 'purification'. This would match WH's reconstruction
*dhues-ro- 'smoking' more or less: we would only need to assume a semantic shift
from 'the smoking' to 'the purifying'. The development *dhue- > fe- is generally
accepted, although there are no completely certain instances. If correct, this preform
would be another instance of an e-grade in a substantival ro-formation, cf. Vine 2002.
However, I see no way to exclude root identity of februus with febris < PIE
*dhegwh-ri-. That is, *febro- might have meant 'a burning, offering', whence
*febrowo- 'belonging to an offering, means of purification'.
Bibl.: WH I: 472f„ EM 222f., IEW 268-271, Pisani 1979.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fecundus 'fertile' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Pit. *fek-odno-l
PIE *dbehk-? IE cognates: seefemina.
r
Latin has seven gerunds in -cundus: secundus (< *sekw-omeno-?), facundus, fecundus,
Jracundus, iucundus, verecundus and rubicundus. Apart from secundus (which can
hardly have led to the recognition of -cundus), the only form which may have had an
original *k is fecundus. It is usually derived from *dheh(i-) 'to suck', but this verb is
r
homophonous to *dheh 'to put', and may well in origin be the same verb. Since 'to
r
put' takes a ^-suffix in some Italic, Greek and Phrygian forms (see s.v. Lat.facio),
some of the forms meaning 'to suckle' may also have had this suffix. In view also of
felix 'fortunate', fenum and fenus, it seems that fe(k)~ had developed from 'suckling'
to 'bringing forth, giving birth to' (maybe already in PIE), hence fec-undus
'characterized by producing, suckling' > 'fertile'.
Bibl.: WH I: 473, EM 223, IEW 242, Leumann 1977: 332, Schrijver 1991: 139,
Meiser 1998: 228, LIV *dheh (i)-. —> Jelix,felo,femina,Jenus, fetus, filius
1

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fel, fellis 'bile, gall' [n.] (P1.+)
IE cognates: Gr. xoXog 'gall, wrath', 'bile'; Lith. tulzis, Latv. zulfkjts 'bile' <
ghlh -; OCS zlbcb 'bile' [f.], Ru. zelc', SCr. zuc, gen.sg. zitci; OIc. gall, OHG OS
3
galla 'gall, bile' < PGm. *gallon-.
Etymology uncertain. The nom.sg. must be from *fell, since it did not change to *fol
(Cowgill 1978: 38). *Fell was analogically introduced for the original nom.sg.
(*feldl), and then -11 was simplified to -I. The gen.sg. fellis might reflect *fel-n-os.
But intervocalic -11- can also stem from *-/w-. Alternatively,^/ and its paradigm may
have been analogically influenced by mel, mellis 'honey', cf. Cowgill. Yet the root is
uncertain: the closest cognates which also mean 'bile' have *gh-, but if fel is from
from *ghelH- 'yellow', initial/' instead of *h- must be a dialectal feature. If the root
was it is possible to derive fel from a Latin root for colour terms (flavus) For
the meaning of the Greek forms, see Driessen 2003a: 287ff.
Bibl.: WH I: 473f., EM 223, IEW 429ff., Sihler 1995: 298. flavus, fldrus, helvus
feles, -is 'small carnivora, such,as 'marten', 'wild cat" [f] (P1.+)
IE cognates: W. bele 'marten*<*bhe!ego-?
The connection with W. bele is possible, but feles can go back to other preforms than
*bhel~. The inflection may represent an earlier e-stem, but also an n-stem with
nom.sg. *-en, or maybe a root noun. The e-stem inflection may have been productive
in wild animal names, cf. meles, volpes, verres. No etymology.
Bibl.: WH I; 474, EM 223£, IEW 118ff., Schrijver 1991: 123,375.
felix,-Iris 'fruitful; fortunate' [adj. £] (P1.+)
Derivatives: felicitas 'luck' (Ter.+), infelix 'unlucky, unhappy' (P1.+), infelTcitas
'misfortune' (Ter.+), infeticdre 'to bring bad luck' (P1.+).
Pit. *fel(w)i- 'suckling'.
PIE *dheh[-l(u)
-i-. IE cognates: Olr. deil 'female pig', dela, delech 'having udders,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: felo, -are 'to suck (milk)' [v. I] (Varro+)
Derivatives:^//^ 'to suck (sexually)' (Cat.+),
Pit. *felo~. It cognates: U. feliuf,/?/m [^cc.pl.m.] 'suckling' < *felio~.
PIE *dhehl-o- 'suckling'. IE cognates: seejelix.
r
The derivation of felare and XJ.felio- from a noun *fela- 'breast' is not very plausible
(pace Untermann 2000). Since there is ample evidence for an adj. *dhehI-(o/h2-), it
r
seems more likely that the Latin verb 'to suckle' derives from an adj. 'who suckles',
whereas the Umbrian adj. can simply have replaced the suffix *-Io- by *-lio~.
Bibl.: WH I: 475f., EM 224, IEW 242, Schrijver 1991: 139, 344, Untermann 2000:
27If., LIV *dheh](i)~. —* fecundus,felix, femina, fenus, fetus, filius

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: femur, -inis 'thigh' [n. «] (P1.+; secondary forms: nom.sg, femen Ampellius, femus
Apul., gen.sg. femoris Cic.+)
PIE *dhen-ur?
An isolated r/«-stem, which must be an archaic formation. EWAia I: 773f. follows a
suggestion by Steinbauer 1979, who derives femur from PIE *dhen-ur, -uen-s 'bow'.
Semantically, this is not problematical; cf. also Janda 1998. Yet phonetically, there is
no indication that *nu yielded (n)m in Latin.
Bibl.: WH I: 477, EM 224, Janda 1998.
-fendo, -ere 'to hit, strike' [v. Ill; -fendT, -fensum]: defendere 'to ward off, defend'
(Lex XII+), offendere 'to strike against; encounter' (P1.+), Tnfensus [adj.] 'aggressive,
hostile' (P1.+)
Derivatives: defensare 'to protect' (PI.), defensor 'protector, defender' (Ter.+),
offensio 'obstacle, offence' (Varro+), offensare 'to collide with' (Varro+), ojfensus,
-us 'collision' (Lucr.+).
Pit. Y' (e)nd-.
PIE *gwhen-dh- 'to hit, strike' or ipv.sg. *gwhndhi. IE cognates: Olr. gonaid, •go'm
'wounds, kills', W. gwan 'to thrust, hit', MCo. gwana 'to sting', OBret. goanaff 'to
punish, sting' < *gwan-e/o-\ Hit. kue(n)-zl/ kun- / kuua(n)- 'to kill', Lyc. 3p qahti
'they destroy'; Skt. pr. 3s. hand, 3p. ghnanti 'to kill'; Gr. tfetvoa, Arm. jnem 'to
strike'; Lith. ginti 'to protect, defend', 3s. gina (the acute is unclear), OCS gbnati
'chase, persecute' < BS1. *gwV.
This inherited verb is only preserved in compounds. The adj. infensus presupposes an
earlier verb *in-fendere 'to strike into, attack'. PIE had a root present *gwhen-/*gwn-,
from which -fendo was derived either by suffixation of PIE or the whole
paradigm was derived from an original pr.ipv. sg. *fende < *gwhndhi 'strike!' (thus
LIV).
Bibl.: WH I: 332f., EM 224f., IEW 491ff., Leumann 1977: 166, 603, Sihler 1995:
510, LIV *guhen-.
ienum / faenum 'hay' [n. o] (Cato+)
Derivatives: faenarius 'of hay' (Cato+); faenisex, -cis 'a mower' (CIL, Varro+),

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fer(c)tum 'a kind of sacrificial cake' [n. o] (Cato+)
Pit. *ferkto-
PIE *bherg-to- 'roasted'? IE cognates: Skt. pr. bhrjjati 'to fry' (Middle Indie for
*bhfjyatil), bhrjjana- [n.], bhrastra- 'frying-pan' [n.] (metathesized from
*bharstra-7)\ Khot. brijs-, MP bristan 'to fry, roast'; Lith. birgelas 'kind of beer'.
The cluster ret can be regarded as a case of archaic spelling; but in view of -fertus, it
may also carry restored -c-. Skt. and Baltic may reflect *bhr-g- (but only if the velar
was depalatalized in Baltic, for which there seems to be no context available; and the
Lith. circumflex does not fit), and Latin *bher-g- 'to roast' (Vine 1986). This root
might be an enlarged variant of PIE *bhr- 'to boil, seethe', reflected in ferveo. Yet the
semantic origin offer(c)tum is unknown, and it may also be connected with/ara'o 'to
stuff. In that case, it would represent a full grade *bherg-to- 'stuffed' > 'cake'.
Bibl.: WH I: 486f., EM 230, IEW 137, Leumann 1977: 217, Schrijver 1991: 255,
LIV *bherg-. —»farcid,ferveo7

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: feriae 'religious festival' [f.pl. a\ (P1.+; Paul, ex F.fesiae)
Derivatives: Jeriatus 'keeping holiday' {¥\.+)\ festus [adj.] 'holiday' (PL+), Jestivus
'festal, excellent' (P1.+), fesifvitas 'festivity, charm' (P1.+), profestus 'ordinary,
working' (PI.+).
Pit. *fes-io- 'holiday', *fes-to- 'holiday'. It. cognates: O. fusfais, fiisiais, fisiais
[dat.abl.pl.] '(religious) holidays' < *fesja~.
PIE *dhehs-io-, *dllehs-to- 'divine, holy'. IE cognates: Skt. dhisa 'approximately:
r r
with impetuosity'; Gr. i3e6q 'god', tieocpciToq 'decreed by a god' < *dhhs-o-, Arm. pi.
r
r
dik' 'gods' < *dbehs-.
r
These words seem to be derived from a PIE s-stem *dhehs- 'religious gift' or 'rite'
r
(cf. Av. dah- !da?ah-i 'gift' < Ilr. *(faH-as~), cf. Benveniste 1969 II: 13. Apparently,
the different derivatives with suffixes such as *-o-, *-io-, *-to- came to mean 'divine'
at an early stage.
Bibl.: WH I: 454f., 481, 489, EM 226f., IEW 259, Schrijver 1991: 139, Meiser
1998: 107, Untermann 2000: 281, LIV *dheh. fanum,feralis
r
ferio, -Ire 'to strike' [v. IV; no pf. or ppp.; Paul, ex F. 3p.ferlnunt] (P1.+)
Derivatives: referTre 'to strike in return' (PI.+).
Pit. *fer-je/o~.
PIE *bherH- 'to pierce, strike'. IE cognates: Alb. bie, bjerre 'to fall, get through' <
*ber(-ie)~.
The etymology is based on the assumption that ferio belongs to the root for 'to
pierce', attested with o-grade in forare and several BS1. and Gm. cognates. Since this
was a laryngeal-final root, ferio cannot go back directly to a PIE e-grade, since
*bherH~ie- would yield Latin *feraie- > *fera- (as per Rix 1999, Meiser 1998: 186f.).
Also, since most languages continue the o-grade of *bherH-, the e-grade of Latin and
Albanian is diverging. Possibly, the root-final laryngeal was lost in prevocalic
position, and the resulting form *bher- was recharacterized with *-ie/o-.
Bibl.: WH 1:48If., EM 227, IEW 133ff., Schrijver 1991: 216, LIV *bherH-. ford

Derivatives: fermentare 'to cause fermentation' (Varro+).
Pit. *fer(a)mentom.
PIE *bher(H)-mn [n.j 'ferment'. IE cognates: OE beorma, MLG barme, NHG Burme
'barm'.
Form and meaning of the original root are not clear. Semantically, it is most attractive
to regard fermentum as a derivative of the primitive root *bhr(H)- underlying the
extended root *bhrH-u- or or *bhr-u- 'to boil' reflected in ferveo and defrutum.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fero, ferre 'to carry, take' [v. irr.] (VOLat.+: CIL 560 (Praeneste) asomfero). Forms:
pr. fero, fers, fert, ferimus, fertis, ferunt, ipv.fer.'ferte!, pf. (te)tuli, ppp. latus; OLat.
sb. abstulds, attulas (Nov.) attolat (Pac.).
Derivatives: ferax 'bearing rich crops, productive' (P1.+); ferentarhts 'light-armed
soldier' (P1.+); cp. in -fer, -era, -erum 'bringing, carrying'; afferre 'to bring, deliver'
(P1.+), auferre 'to take away' (Naev.+), circumferre 'to carry round, spread' (P1.+),
conferre 'to bring, bestow, collect' (Naev.+), deferre 'to bring down, transfer' (P1.+),
differre 'to carry away, spread, put off (P1.+), efferre 'to carry out, bring forth' (P1.+),
inferre 'to carry into, bring forward' (P1.+), inferius 'used in offerings' (Cato+),
inferiae [f.pl.] 'offerings made to a dead person's manes' (Lucr.+), offerre 'to put in
someone's path, offer, provide' (PI.+),perferre 'to carry, deliver' (P1.+), praeferre 'to
carry in front of, exhibit' (Aedituus+), proferre 'to bring forth, display, utter'
(Naev.+), referre 'to bring back, withdraw, return; record' (Naev.+), refert 'it makes a
difference, is important' (P1.+), sufferre 'to offer; submit to' (P1.+), transferre 'to
transport, transfer' (P1.+).
Pit. pr. *fere/o-, pf. *(te)tol-, ppp. *tlato-. It cognates: Vol. ferom [inf.], Man.feret
[3p.pr.], ferenter [3p.pr.ps.], U. f<e>rar [3s.sb.ps.?], fertu, fertu [3s.ipv.II], fertuta
[3p.ipv.II], ferest [3s.fut], O. f]errins [3p.sb.] 'to carry, bring'; with prev. am-. U.
aferum, afero [inf.], O. arafret [3p.pr.], U. anferener [gdve., gen.sg.m.] 'to

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: carry', OW beryt [3s.], MBret. berqff 'to flow' < PCI. *bere/o-, Skt. bhdra-, Av.
bar a-, Gr. (pep®, Phryg. aP-ftepET, Arm. berem, Alb. bie 'to bear, carry', Lith. berti,
beriit 'to scatter, OCS bbrati, Is. berg 'to gather, select', Go. bairan, ToB act. par am,
ToA med. pdrtar; see tolld for the cognates of tulT, latus.
Pres .fers < *feres, fert < *feret, fertis < *feretis, inf. ferre < *feresi, ipv.fer < *fere.
The cp. refert contains a case form of res 'case, thing' in the first member, most likely
the abl.sg. re. The suffix -tilis instead of -His in fertilis is striking. Szemerenyi 1989:
38 derives fertilis via dissimilation from *fer-tr-i~, which he compares with Av.
barvdri- 'giving birth', i.e. *bher-tr-iH-. But fertilis is attested so recently (Cic.+) that
this seems a moot possibility. It seems more likely that -tilis was taken from other adj.
in -tilis (built to ppp. in -tus), e.g. fictilis, plectilis, textilis. Also its antonym sterilis
'barren' (P1.+) may have played a role.
Bib!.: WH I: 483ff„ II: 426, EM 227ff„ 567, IEW 128ff., 1060f„ Leumann 1977:
347f., 530, Schrijver 1991: 181, Sihler 1995: 541 f., Meiser 1998: 224, Untermann
2000: 48f., 275-278, Schumacher 2004: 218-223, LIV *bher-, *telh -. -^forceps,
2
forda,fors,fur, tolld

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ferus 'wild, savage, brutal' [adj. o/aj (Carmen Arvale, Naev.+)
Derivatives: fera 'wild animal' (Pac.+), ferinus 'of wild beasts' (Lucr.+), ferdx
'fierce, arrogant' (P1.+), ferdcia 'fierceness, arrogance' (Cato+); efferus 'untamed,
wild' (Lucr.+), perferus 'completely wild' (Varro).
Pit. *%wero-.
PIE gVehi(-)r- 'wild animal' (> *ghuehiro-). IE cognates: Gr. i&qp, -6q, OPr.
[acc.pl.] swirins, Lith. zveris, Latv. zv§rs, OCS zverb, SCr. zvijer [i], Bulg. zvjar 'wild
animal' < BS1. *zue?ri-; ToB serwe 'hunter' (< *ghuer-ud-).
_
T
The retention of -rus (as opposed to vir < *viros) mustibe due to the influence of f.
fera, n.pl. fera. The adj.fer-dx may be (one of) the source(s) of the suffix -ox, if from
*[f.Jero-h kw- 'having a fierce aspect'. This can be disputed, but I see no better source
3
for this suffix. The short -e- offerus can be explained from pretonic shortening in front
of a resonant, as per Schrijver's formulation of Dybo's Law in Latin (1991: 343).
Bibl.: WH I: 487f., EM 230, IEW 493, Leumann 1977: 377, Schrijver 1991: 337,
Sihier 1995: 159, Meiser 1998: 111,. -> serenus, vir

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fervefl 'to be intensely hot, boil' [v. II; pf. ferbui (Hor.+)] (Varro+). Also pr.ferv-ere,
pf. fervl (Naev.+)!.
Derivatives: fervescere 'to become hot' (PI,+),fervefacere 'to make very hot' (P1.+),
fervidus 'hot' (Acc.+), fervor 'heat, ardor' (Varro+); confervefacere 'to make
thoroughly hot' (Lucr.+), defervefacere 'to boil thoroughly' (Cato+), defervescere 'to
come to a full boil; cool off (Cato+), efferv(e)o 'to boil up/over' (Lucr.+),
effervescere 'to boil up or over' (Cato+), infervefacere 'to bring to the boil' (Cato+),
infervere 'to come to the boil' (Cato+), infervescere 'id.' (Cato+), perfervefieri 'to
become very hot' (Varro+), praefervidus 'exceedingly hot' (Acc.+).
Pit. *ferwe/o~.
PIE pr. *bheru-e/o- 'to be hot, boil'. IE cognates: Olr. berbaid, W. berw 'to boil' <
*bher-u-.
Fervere occurs mainly from PI. to Verg., whereas fervere occurs mainly from Verg.
onward (with the exception of infervere). This suggests that fervere was replaced by
fervere in the course of time. Still, it is likely that fervere had been around longer,
since we find infervere in Cato, and since fervescere (whence fervefacere) and
fervidus are most likely to be derived from a verb in -ere. This begs the question of
the original semantic distribution. LIV hesitatingly assumes that fervere goes back to
*forvere from a caus. *bhor-u-eie-; the e-grade would then have been adopted from
fervere. Yet there is no discernable difference in meaning between fervo and ferveo,
and Latin would have tolerated a difference in vocalism if there was one in meaning.
It seems more likely that the original verb was fervere, after all, but that it was
replaced (or, initially: joined) by fervere before the time of Plautus. The rise of
fervere can be ascribed to the stative meaning 'to be hot, be boiling'.
Bibl.: WH I: 487, EM 230, IEW 143ff., Schrijver 1991: 252-256, Meiser 1998: 214,
LIV *bheru-. —> defrutum,fermentum

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: festino, -are 'to make haste, hurry' [v. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: fesfirms 'quick, in a hurry' (Sail.+), praefestinare 'to make great haste'
(PI.+), praefestinatim 'in great haste' (Sis.); confestim 'immediately' (Naev.+).
Pit. *fristi- 'haste'.
PIE *bhristi-. IE cognates: W. brys 'haste; speedy', MBret. bresic, brezec 'swift,
hurried' < *bhris(s)ti-.
The form confestim points to an earlier noun *festi-. Schrijver 1990 reconstructs a
noun *festi- 'hurry' from a root *bMris- which he also finds in Celtic, and compares it
with Skt. bhri-. Vine 1999c assumes that festinus is older than festinare, in spite of its
more recent attestation. In that case, there would have been an ins.sg. *fris-ti 'with
haste' from which *fristi-no- was derived. Even if the actually attested festinus is
regarded as a more recent creation, Vine argues, festinare may have been built on an
earlier adj. *festino-. Vine's explanation is attractive semantically, since the earlier
explanation offestinare as a denominative to an alleged noun *festi-on- 'haste' does
not explain the meaning 'to haste' (one would expect 'to be a haste', which is
nonsense). Italo-Celtic *bhristi~ might be connected with *bhriH- 'to cut' (Skt.
bhrinanti 'they wound', YAv. pairibrinayha 'you have cut', RuCS brijg, briti 'to
shave'). The bare root would be *bhri-, with a suffix *-s-. But the semantic connection
with 'haste' is hardly compelling, so this etymology remains gratuitous.
Bibl.: WH I: 259, 488, EM 231, IEW 143, Leumann 1977: 327, Schrijver 1990,
1995: 410, Vine 1999c. infestus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fetialis 'one of the college of twenty priests of Rome' [m. i] (Varro+)
Pit. *Jeti-ali-.
PIE *dhehti- 'the putting, making'. IE cognates: Skt. -dhiti- [f.] 'id.', Av. ni-dditi-
r
'concealment', Gr. i3em<; [f.] 'setting, position', Lith. detis 'load', OCS blago-deth
'benediction', Go. gadeds 'adoption', missadeps 'crime', OIc. dad, OHG tat 'deed'.
A substantivized adjective meaning 'of the (religious) congregation'. Thus, the
inherited noun *feti- < *dhehti- 'putting, placement' came to mean 'prescription,
r
law', and eventually 'body of priests'.
Bibl.: WH I: 489f., EM 231, Leumann 1977: 344f„ Untermann 2000: 260, LIV
*dhehi- —*•-do, -dere

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fetus 'having recently given birth; fertile' [adj. o/a] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: feta 'a female animal which has just given birth' (P1.+), fetura
'breeding, parturition' (Var.+), fetus, -us [m.] 'parturition, breeding, offspring' (P1.+);
effetus 'that has borne fruit; exhausted' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *feto-.
PIE *dheh,-to- 'having given birth'.
Risch 1984: 189-191 has shown that the earliest meaning attested for fetus is 'having
given birth', 'breeding' (of birds), from which 'fertilized, fertile' was derived by
means of a metaphor mainly applied to the earth and to plants. Only rarely does it
mean 'pregnant'. For the semantics of the root, see fenum. Probably, fetus is an
inner-Italic formation, as is fecundus.
Bibl.: WH I: 490, EM 231, IEW 242, LIV *dhehi(i):. fecundus, felix, felare,
fenus, fetus,filius *
i
fiber, -brT 'beaver' [m. o] (PI.+; also feber Varro+)
Pit. *fifro- i *fefro- 'beaver'.
PIE *bhe-bVu-, *bhe-bhr-o- (or *bhi-bhr-o-) 'brown; brown animal, beaver'. IE
cognates: Gaul, bebru- (in PN); Skt. babhru- 'red-brown; brown horse', Av. bafira-
'beaver', OPr. bebrus, Lith. bibras, bebras, bebriis, bibrus, Latv. b§brs, CS bebn,
bobrb, Ru. bobr, gen.sg. bobra, SCr. dabar, Sin. bobsr, bebdr 'beaver' < BS1.
* bebrus, *bobros; OHG bibar, OS bibar, bever, OE beofor, bebr, bebir < WGm.
*bebru-, OIc. bforr, ONorw. bifr-, OSwe. biur, ODan. bicever < North-Germanic
* bebru-. V
It is not clear whether the few attestations of feber, febr- in glosses and in Varro
represent a linguistically real, older form. In any case, the IE cognates all continue
V-, so that fiber can be interpreted in two ways: either its initial vowel was
raised to -i- in Latin at some stage; or it alone continues *bhibhr-. In view of the
vacillation between the reduplication vowels *e and */ in PIE, the matter cannot be
settled. In Ilr. and BS1., o- and u-stems occur side by side, whereas Gm. has only a
w-stem and Latin an o-stem. If the word is cognate with PIE *bhruH-no- 'brown',...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ficus 'fig-tree; fig' [f. o] (P1.+;J7CMJ, -us Varro+)
Derivatives: ficula 'fig' (PL), jtculneus, ficulnus [adj.] 'of figs' (Cato+), jicedula
'small bird feeding on figs, beccafico' (Lucil.+), ficedulenses, -ium [pi.]
'beccafico-men' (PL),jicetum 'fig-orchard' (Varro+).
IE cognates: Gr. OTKOV, Boeot. TOKOV, Arm. t'owz 'fig'.
Loanword from another language in the Mediterranean. The word may have been
adopted into pre-Latin in the form *i%ko- or *i3iko-.
Bibl.: WH I: 492, EM 232.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fido, -ere 'to trust' [v. Ill; ppp.ftsum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: jidus 'faithful, reliable' (P1.+), infidus 'faithless, treacherous' (P1.+);
jiducia 'guarantee, confidence' (PL +); fides 'trust, guarantee' (P1.+), fide lis 'faithful,
loyal' (Naev.+), fidelitas 'faithfulness' (P1.+), mfidelis 'disloyal' (P1.+); (Dius) Fidius
'a god sworn by in oaths, maybe Jupiter' (P1.+); foedus, -eris [n.] 'treaty, agreement'
{?\+; jidus Enn. apud Varronem), foederains 'bound by treaty' (SCBac. foideratei +),
confoedusti, -drum [m.pl.] 'allies' (Paul, ex F. \fidusta 'trustworthy' (Paul, ex F. );
perfidia 'faithlessness' (PL+), perfidiosus 'treacherous' (PL+); confidere 'to put one's
trust in, be sure' (PL+), confidentia 'self-confidence' (Naev.+), confidentiloquus
'speaking audaciously' (PL), diffidere 'to have no confidence in' (PL+).
Pit. *feipe- [v.], *feipo- [adj.], *fip-e- [f.], *foipo- [m.], *feip-os- [n.] 'trust'. It.
cognates: U. kumpiflatu, kupiflatu, combifiatu [3s.ipv.II], kupifiaia [3s.pr.sb.],
combifiansi [3s.pf.cj.], combifiansiust, combijiansust, combifiansiust [3s.fut.pf.] 'to
announce, communicate', denom. verb to a noun *kombifiom < *kom-b heict -io-. U.
fise, fiso [dat.sg.] probably a deity 'Trust' < *bhidto- and *bhidtu-; V.fisim, fisi,fisei
[acc.sg.m.], fisier, fisie [gen.sg.m.], fisie, fisi, fisei [dat.sg.m.], fisiu, fissiu, fisiu
[abl.sg.m.],yme [loc.sg.m.\fisiem [loc.sg.m. + -en] < *fis-io- 'belonging to Fiso-';
U.jisouie [gen.sg.], fi<s>uvi, fisoui [dat.sg.] , fisouie, fisoui [voc.sg.] 'Fiso-' < adj.
*fisou-io-, deriv. to *fisu-; U. fisouina [abl.sg.f.] 'belonging to Fiso-', deriv. from
jisu- or from jisouio-.
PIE *bheidh-e/o- [pr.] 'to trust', *bheidh-o- [adj.] 'faithful', *bhidh-eh [f.] 'faith'. IE
r
cognates: Gr. rceiflopai 'to be convinced, obey', aor. e7ui3opiv, pf. ji£7tou3a 'trust'; act.
(sec.) jceii3co, aor. eTteioa 'to convince'; Alb. be 'oath', bese 'faith', bindem 'to be
convinced, believe'; OCS beda 'distress, necessity'...
