content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pin' (Cato+). It. cognates: possibly O. fifikus [2s.fiit.pf.] 'to make(?)' < *fi-fig-us-s
(Kortlandt 2007: 153); reluctantly Untermann 2000 for U. fiktu, afikta [3s.ipv.II] '?'.
PIE *dheigw -e/o- or *dhiHgw-e/o-. IE cognates: Lith. diegii 'to plant, sting'; maybe
ToB 3s.sb. tsakam 'bites'.
The forms ftvere and offivebant retain the regular intervocalic reflex of *gw. The g
was later introduced analogically from the perfect. Fibula < *fiwibula < *fiue-c^la.
LIV explains fivo from the thematization of the weak stem *dhiHgw- of a PIE root
present (or rather a root aorist?). The reconstruction of the root-internal laryngeal is
based on Toch. tsak-, which may rather belong to a root in PIE *d-. The acute and the
long vowel of Lith. dygti 'to sprout' can be explained from Winter's Law. For Baltic
and Latin, a root ^dheigw- would suffice.
Bibl.: WH I: 492, 495f„ EM 232, 234, IEW 243f., Sihier 1995: 583, Untermann
2000: 279, LIV *dheiHgw-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: filius 'son' [m. o] (Lex XII, Andr.+)
Derivatives: filia 'daughter' (Andr.+),filiolus Tittle son' (P1.+).
Pit. *filio/a- 'son, daughter'. It. cognates: Ven.filia [nom.sg.] 'daughter', Fal. fileo,
hileo, fio, fi., f. [nom.sg.m.] 'son', filea [nomsg.f.], file(ai) [dat.sg.f.] 'daughter'; U.
fel (abbreviated) probably a loan from Latin.
PIE *dh(e)hji-l- 'suckling, child'. IE cognates: see s y.fehx and femina.
A PIE /-adj. meaning 'suckling' can be reconstructed both from the PIE root *dheh
r
and from its extended variant *db'hi-. The U. suffix *-io- after *dhehl- is matched
r r
in Latin by fil-ius after *dhhi-. Although Latin fil- can reflect *feil-, Faliscan fileai
r
shows Pit. */?/-.
Bibl.: WH I: 496, EM 234, IEW 24If., Schrijver 1991: 242, Giacomelli 1963: 245f„
Lejeune 1974: 335, Meiser 1998: 85, Untermann 2000: 270f„ Wallace 2005, LIV
* dheh i (-i)-. fecundus, felix, felo, Jem ina, fenus, fetus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: filix, -cis 'large fern, bracken' [f. k] (Vitr.+; var.felix)
Derivatives: Jilicula 'small kind of fern' (Cato+).
Pit. *fel-e/ik~.
PIE *bhel-e/ik- 'henbane'. IE cognates: W. bele, bela 'henbane' < MW *beleu [pi.]
< LPBr. *bel- 'henbane'; Gaul, deity *Belenos, Brit. *Belinos in PN; RuCS belem>,
MoRu. belend, Cz. blen, bltn 'henbane' < *belno-, SCr. bun < *b!no~; OHG bilisa,
bilesa, bilsa, MDutch bilse 'henbane' < *belos-\ OE beolone, belene, OS bilene <
*belun-on-, Dan. bylne < *buln~.
It is uncertain which spelling is the oldest, but since filix might be an assimilation
from felix, the latter form might be original (or *felex). The suffix -ik- or -ek- is found
in other plant names (larix 'larch', carex, rumex), and can have been added
secondarily. The stem *fel- has been identified with PIE *bhel- 'henbane' in Celtic,
Germanic and Slavic, which was rejected by WH on semantic grounds. Schrijver
1999: 37f. rightly restores this connection: the stems of henbane show a superficial
resemblance to the feathered leaves of fern, and both plants have well-known
medicinal properties. This often suffices to create formal similarities in languages.
Bibl.: WH 1:497, EM 234, IEW 120, Schrijver 1991: 37f.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: filum 'thread, line; build (of a person)' [n. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: filatim 'thread by thread' (Lucr.).
Pit. *fi(s)lo-.
PIE *gwhiH-(s-)lo- 'sinew, sinew thread'. IE cognates: MW gieu, W. gicrn 'sinew,
nerves', OCo. goiuen, MCo. (lenited) ieyw < *gii- < *gi- < PIE *gwiH-; Skt. jjyd-
'tendon, bow-string', Av.jiid- 'bow-string' <IIr. *JiHaHGr. J3io<; 'bow; bow-string'
< *gwiH-o-; Arm. jil 'sinew, cord' < *gwhiH-sl-; OPr. pettegislo 'back vein', Lith.
gysla, Latv. dzi(k)sla 'vein, sinew' < Proto-Baltic *giHla; OCS zila, Ru. zila, SCr.
zila, Sin. zila 'vein' < PS1. Hila.
The unenlarged root is shown by Ilr. and Greek, but they also have initial *gw- instead
of *gwh-. In BS1. and Celtic, the velar is uncertain, whereas Arm. needs like Latin.
Some languages show the suffix *-sleh - (Baltic, Armenian), some do not (Slavic). In
2
Latin, this is impossible to determine.
Bibl.: WH I: 497f., EM 235, IEW 489, Schrijver 1991: 242, 1995: 286f. funis

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: findo, -ere 'to split, cleave* [v. Ill; pf.fidl, ppp.Jissum] (PL+)
Derivatives: fissum 'cleft, split' (P1.+), jissilis 'split' (P1.+); diffindere 'to divide,
split off (Lex XII+), Tnfindere 'to cleave' (Acc.+).
Pit. *find~e~. It. cognates: maybe Hern, hvidas /fidas/ [2s.pr.sb.] 'to break'.
PIE pr. *bhi-n-d- 'to cleave'. IE cognates: Celtib. bidetud [3s.ipv.] 'to split?',
robiseti [3s.sb.] < PCI. *bid-e/o-, *bid-se-\ Skt. pr. bhinatti, aor. abhedam, pf.
bibheda 'to split', bhinna- 'split', bhid- [f.] 'splitting', pur-bhid- 'breaking the walls',
YAv. asto.bid- 'who breaks a bone'; Gr. (peiSopai 'to spare', Go. beitan, OE bltan,
NHG beifien 'to bite'.
WH and EM suspect that the pf. was *fifidf. Fissilis was derived productively from
the ppp.
Bibl.: WH I: 500f., EM 235, IEW 116f., Untermann 2000: 278, Schumacher 2004:
224, LIV *bheid-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fingo, -ere 'to form, fashion' [v. Ill; pf. finxi, ppp.fictum] (P1.+)
Derivatives; figulus 'potter' (Varro+), fig(i)linae [f.pl.] 'potter's workshop'
(Varro+), figularis 'of a potter' (PL); figura 'form, appearance' (Ter.+), figurare 'to
shape, fashion' (Varro.+), figurator 'who shapes' (Varro); fictor 'id.' (P1.+), fictrix
'deceiver' (LuciL), fictilis 'made of pottery' (Cato+), jictura 'formation' (PL);
affingere 'to add, attach' (Varro+), confingere 'to form; invent, fabricate' (P1.+),
effigia (P1.+) / effigies (Cic.+) 'statue, portrait', ejfingere 'to wipe clean (Cato+), to
shape, reproduce (Cic.+Y; fitilla 'kind of cake offered in sacrifices' (Sen., Plin.).
Pit. *finge/o- [pr.], *fe-fig- [pf.] 'to knead, form', *fixlo- 'who forms', *fy-do-
'cake, offering', *feixo- [m.] 'wall'. It. cognates: Fal. fifiked [3s.pf.], f(if)iqod
[3p.pf.], fita [nom.sg.f. of ppp.] 'to produce'; Presam. (Tortora A2) fefiKeS 'has
made' [3s.pf.], O. feihuss [acc.pl.], feihuis [abl.pl.] 'wall' [m. o] . Uncertain U. fikla,
ficlam, field [acc.sg.], fiklas [gen.sg.] 'certain sacrificial substance' < *fig-kla- <
*cti^-tl-h -rl O. fifikus [2s.fut.pf.] < *fi'fig-us-sl Maybe also U. fiktu, afikta
2
[3s.ipv.II] '?'. .
PIE pr. *dhi-n-gh-, aor. *dh(e)igh-; noun *dheigh-(o-?) 'wall'. IE cognates: Verb: Celtib.
inf. ambi-tinkounei 'to build', Olr. dingid•ding 'to oppress' < PCI. *dinge/o- 'to
press, form'; SkX. deh- 'to smear, to anoint, to plaster' [pr.], YAv. pairi.daezaiia- 'to
embank', uz-dista- 'piled up'; Arm. dizanem, aor. edez 'to pile up'; Lith. ziesti,
ziedziu 'to mould (from clay)', OCS zbdati, zizdg 'to build', Go. digan 'to form
mould', Toch. tsik- 'to form'; Noun: Skt. deht- 'embankment, dam, wall', Av.
uz-daeza- 'id.', pairi.daeza- 'wall', OP dida-, MP diz, MoP diz 'fortress', Bac. XiCp, /
'id.'; Gr. zelyoq [n.], roi/pq [m.] 'wall', OPr. seydis 'id.', Cz. zed', gen.sg. zdi,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: finis 'boundary, limit; territory [pi.]' [m. i (acc.pl. Jineis in inscr., abl.sg. fini PL'+)]
(P1.+; f. in Acc.+)
Derivatives: finitimus 'living near the boundary' (Lucr.+); fimre 'to draw
boundaries, limit' (Varro+), fimtor 'one who marks out boundaries' (PL+); affinis
'bordering on, connected' (P1.+), affinis [m.] 'a relation by marriage; neighbour'
(P1.+), affinitas 'relationship (by marriage)' (P1.+), confinis 'adjacent, akin' (Varro+),
confinium 'common boundary' (P1.+), defimre 'to fix, settle, define' (PI.+), definitio
'definition, marking' (Varro+), infimtus 'indefinite, unlimited' (Varro+), praeJTmre
'to prescribe, determine' (Ter.+); perfines 'perfringas' (Fest.).
Pit. *fini-l *fi/eig'sni-l
The etymology is disputed. WH reconstruct *fig-s-ni- 'driven in, implanted', as
referring to a material boundary marker (a standing stone, a pole). Another possibility
would be *bhiH-ni- to *bhiH- 'to hit, strike', especially in view of perfines 'you must
strike'. Bammesberger 1990 connects PGm. *baina- 'bone, leg' and OIc. beinn
'straight', which may suggest that *baina- originally referred to a pole or an upright
boundary mark. Whereas PGm. *baina- could go back to *bhoiH-n-o-, Lat .finis could
reflect *bhiH-n-i-.
Bibl.: WH I: 502f., EM 236f., IEW 243f., Leumann 1977:343. figd,perfines

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fid, fieri 'to happen, become' [v. IV] (Andr.+). Forms: pr. fid, fis, fit, fimus, fitis,
fiunt, ipv. fi, fite, fito, ps. fitur, fitum est; inf. fiere (Enn.), fieri (elsewhere), sb. fiam,
ipf.fiebam.
Derivatives: defierJ 'to be lacking' (P1.+), Tnfit 'begins' (P1.+), superfieri 'to be
superfluous'. As pr. to -facto: confieri 'to be done, happen' (Ter.+), interfierl 'to be
destroyed' (P1.+).
Pit *fwije/o~. It. cognates: O. fiiet, fiiet [3p.pr.], U. fuia [3s.pr.sb.], fuiest [3s.fut.],
fito [ppp., acc.sg.n.] 'to take place, happen' < *Ju-je/o~. The vowel F can be directly
from *u, or from the />iiis-rule. The U. ppp. fito- may reflect * fit-to-.
PIE *bhh u-ie/o- 'to become'. IE cognates: Gaul, biiete 'you are/must be*, Olr. bimt
2
'I am', biid, biith, -hi 'is', MW bydaf 'I am', by6, byt, OW -bid 'is' (cj.), MBret.
bezaff, bezaf 'I am', bez, MCo. bethafi bythaf T am', beth, byth, OBret. -bid 'is' <
*biie/o- < PCI. *buiie/o~; ?Gr. Att. (pi3op,ai 'to grow, become'.
Originally an active verb with a stem */?-, the inf. of which {fiere) acquired a ps.
ending -i. Used as an intransitive counterpart to facer e 'to make'. To explain the stem
fi-, Schrijver mentions two possible scenarios: 1. Thurneysen's rule *-uiV- > *-uV-, or
2. a development (e.g.) 3s. *bhHu-ie-ti > *bhuieti > *fuiieti > *fult > fit. See also
Schrijver 2003: 77, for Celtic. Kortlandt 2007: 136 now opts for the second
possibility, especially in view of the Celtic cognates. *fi- would develop phonetically
in the 23s. and 12p. pr. forms, and then spread to the other forms of the paradigm. The
inf. fiere shows its recent date by the absence of contraction, and it and fid, fiunt
retain the long vowel in front of hiatus. Maybe U. fuia retains *fu- in front of *-/a-7
Since fieri does not normally have a pf., the form fitum in Andr. must be a
nonce-formation, cf. Meiser 1986.
Bibl.: WH I: 504f. EM 231, 317, IEW 146ff., Leumann 1977: 106, 530, Meiser
}
1986: 53, Schrijver 1991: 322ff., Sihl...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: firmus 'firm, stable, strong, reliable' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: firmitas 'strength, stability' (PI.+), firmiter 'firmly' (P1.+), firmitudd
'firmness' (P1-+); firmare 'to confirm, make strong' (Ter.+), firmdmentum 'support,
prop' (Afran.+); affirmdre 'to add strength, confirm' (P1.+), confirmare 'to
strengthen, encourage' (P1.+), infirmus 'weak' (Ter.+), infirmitas 'weakness' (Ter.+),
mfirmare 'to weaken' (Acc.+), cffirmare 'to make obdurate' (P1.+).
Pit. *fermo-.
PIE *dher-mo- 'holding'. IE cognates: Skt. dhar- 'to hold, keep, bear, support',
dhdrman- [n.] 'support, firm hold, fixed order, law', Av. dar- 'to hold'. Lith. daryti
'to do' < *dhor-, dereti, 3s. dera 'to bargain, bet'.
The i of firmus may go back to *e and be due to raising after a labial, cf. Watkins
1973b: 196. It seems less likely that the raising can be ascribed to a cluster *rg, as
Leumann 1977: 45 suggests: iji the cases which he adduces, the velar is still present,
and Mirqurios and commircium^ also have a preceding iabial; only stircus does not.
WH reject a preform *ferGmo~ because they reject an outcome -rm-.
Bibl.: WH I: 505f., EM 237, IEW 253, LIV 145ff. ^feri,fortis,frenum,fretus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: flagito, -are 'to beset with demands, summon' [v. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: flagitium 'demonstration of disapproval; disgrace' (PI.+), flagitdtor 'one
who makes importunate demands' (P1.+); difllagitare 'to importune on all sides' (PI.).
Pit. *flag-e/o- 'to whip'.
PIE *bhl(e)h )g-e/o-? IE cognates: OIc. blaka, blakra 'to hit back and forth', blak 'a
(2
blow', blekkja (< *blakjan) 'to hit'; Lith. bloksti, blaskiii 'to swing back and forth'.
All built on an iterative flag-itdre to an earlier, unattested verb *flag-ere. The original
meaning was 'to whip somebody as a punishment', whence 'to scold'; cf. WH. This
means that this word family is connected with flagrum 'whip', and that the stem
appears as flag-r- in the latter and flag-V- here. This would match Schrijver's rule
(1991: 191) of a development *CRHCC> CRaCC as opposed to *CRHC> *CRdC. If
correct, this inference would support the likelihood of an early (PIE?) origin of these
words. If *flagere contains a PIE full grade, we may reconstruct *h ; but the situation
2
does not seem certain enough: it might be zero grade, and the cognates in Gm. and Bl.
are not certain.
Bibl.: WH I: 508f., EM 238, IEW 154, Schrijver 1991:185, LIV ?*bhleh g-. flagrum
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: flagro, -are 'to be ablaze, burn' [v. I] (Cic.+)
Derivatives: flagrantia 'blaze, passionate glow' (P1.+); deflagrare 'to bum down'
(Erm.+); flamma 'flame' (Naev.+), flammdrius 'one who dyes garments
flame-coloured' (P1.+), flammescere 'to become fiery' (Lucr.), flammeum
'flame-coloured veil' (Cmc\\.+),flammeus 'flaming, fiery' (Enn,+),Jlammans 'flaming'
(Lucr.+), wflammare 'to set on fire' (Ertn.+); flammifer 'carrying flames' (Enn.+).
Pit. *flagro-, *flagma. It. cognates: O. flagiui, flagiui [dat.sg.] offlagio-, an epithet
of Iuppiter.
PIE *bhlg-ro- 'burning', *bt1lg-mh2- 'flame', IE cognates: see fulgd.
The verb is probably a denom. of an adj. *flagro- 'burning'. Schrijver (1991: 485)
posits a rule PIE *RDC > Latin RaDC, which serves to explain *flagro- < *bhlg-ro-,
among other forms. The noun flamma reflects a noun *flag-ma from a zero grade
*bhlg-m- which is striking next to PIE *bhlog-mo- > Gr. (pXoy|i6<; 'flame'.
Bibl.: WH I: 51 Of., 513, EM 238f., IEW 124f., Schrijver 1991: 477ff., Meiser 1998:
64, Untermann 2000: 289f„ LIV +bhle(g'-. -> fulgo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: flamen, -inis 'certain kind of priest, flamen' [m. /)] (Varro+)
Derivatives; Flaminius 'a Roman gentilicium' (Varro+).
Pit. *fla(d)men-cl *flagsmen~! 'sacrificial act'.
PIE *bhleh (d)~mn 'sacrifice'? *bhlg- s-mn 'burnt offering'? IE cognates: Go. blotan
2
'to honour through sacrifice', OHG bluozan 'to sacrifice', OIc. biota 'to sacrifice' <
*bhleh d-.
2D
The old connection offlamen with Skt. brahman- is highly problematic, and has been
dismissed by Schrijver. As WH surmise, the ending -en points to an archaism,
probably a n. noun 'sacrificial act' which changed its semantics to 'priest'; for a
similar shift, cf. augur 'bird-observer', see s.v. augeo. The only viable comparanda
are found in Gm., but they show root-final (or suffixal) *-d~. In Latin, it is impossible
to decide whether flamen reflects * fid-men, *flad-men, *flag-smen, or yet another
preform. Schrijver reconstructs PIE *bhleh -mn (a n. with grade), but *bhleh -d-mn
2 2
is also possible. A connection with *bhlg- 'to shine, burn' would yield *bhIg-s-mn
'burning, burnt offering' > *flagsmen as a possible preform.
Bibl.: WH I: 512, EM 239, IEW 154, Leumann 1977: 208, Schrijver 1991: 176,
Sihler 1995:198.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: flavus 'yellow, blonde' [adj. o/a] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: flavescere 'to turn yellow' (Cato+). »
Pit. *fldwo-. K
PIE *bhleh -uo- 'yellow'? IE cognates: Mir. bid 'yellow'; OHG bldo, OE blow, OIc.
3
bldr 'blue' < PGm. *bleua-. ' •
Schrijver derives flavus from a root *bhlh - which he also sees reflected in fids and
3
florus. The only way to connect flavus and the Germanic words for 'blue' (if they are
cognate) is to posit a development *bhleh -uo- > *bhleh -uo- (loss of labialization in
3 1/2
*h ) for both Gm. and Latin, as Schrijver does (p. 300). This seems a hazardous
3
assumption, but there is no better alternative, unless one separates flavus from the
Gm. words. In that case, one could posit PIE *bhlhi-uo- > flavus. Of course, it is
uncertain whether flavus is related to florus to start with.
Bibl.: WH I: 513f, EM 239, IEW 160, Leumann 1977: 55, Schrijver 1991: 147,177,
298,301, LIV *bhleh -. -^florus,fids
3
flectC, -ere 'to bend, curve; modify, soften' [v. Ill; pf.flexT, ypp.flexum\ (Naev.+)
Derivatives: flexare 'to bend' (CdXo), flexibilis 'easily bent, flexible' (Lab.+), flexus,
-us 'the bending, turn' (Andr.+), flexuosus 'winding' (Cato+), flexura 'curve;
inflexion' (Varro+); inflectere 'to bend (inwards), turn' (P1.+), inflexus, -us 'winding'
(Naev.+), reflectere 'to bend back, turn around' (Ter.+); flexanimus 'persuasive;
distracted' (Pac.+).
Pit. *flek-t-.
Form and meaning render it possible that flecto contains a suffix which was
added on the model ofplecto 'to plait', maybe also of necto. Possible preforms of the
root are *bhleK-, *dhleK-, *gwhleK-, none of which has obvious IE comparanda.
Bibl.: WH I: 514f., EM 239, Leumann 1977: 539, Sihler 1995: 535. necto,plecto

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: flig5, -ere 'to strike down' [v. Ill; pf.flixT, ppp.yiTctam] (Andr., Acc.)
Derivatives: flictus, -us 'collision' (Pac.+); affligere 'to strike, cause destruction'
(P1.+), afflictare 'to strike repeatedly, vex' (P1.+), confligere 'to collide, argue'
(Lucil.+), conflictare 'to contend; harass' (Ter.+), effligere 'to strike dead' (P1.+),
efflictdre 'id.' (PL), afflictim (Naev.), efflictim 'passionately' (P1.+), profligare 'to
crush, overwhelm' (P1.+).
Pit. *flfg- or *fleig~,
PIE *bhleig-e/o- or *bhHH-g-e/o- 'to hit, crush'. IE cognates: Gr. (p?apco 'to rub,
crush'; Lith. blaizyti 'to tear off, Latv. blaizit 'to squeeze, beat, rub', bliezt 'to beat,
hew' < *bhloig-, CS blizt 'near, close', Ru. blizyj (dial.) 'short-sighted', Ru. blizkij
'near, close' < *bhlig-. Less certain: Go. bliggwan, OHG bliuwan 'to hit' < PGm.
*bliwwcm- < *bhHH-y-7
The meaning of fligere, the Gr., BS1. and Gm. words is close enough to render a
correspondence in form likely, but the preforms cannot be identical. Greek requires a
suffix *gw, Latin and BS1. can have *g, Gm. has no velar but probably needs *-H~.
The long vowel in Latin can be from *iH or *ei, Greek may have secondary
lengthening, BS1. does not need a laryngeal because the acute and the Slavic long
vowel can be explained from Winter's Law. Hence Schrijver's suggestion that the
root was *bhli- with different enlargements in different branches.
Bibl.: WH I: 517, EM 240, IEW 160f., Schrijver 1991: 230f., LIV *bhleig-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: flo, flare 'to blow' [v. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: flabellum 'fan' (Ter.+), flabellifera 'maid holding a fan' (PL),
Jlabellulum 'small fan' (Ter.); flabrum 'gust of wind' (Lucr.+), flamen 'gust, wind'
(Enn.+), flatus, -us 'blowing, blast, breath'; afflare 'to breathe, blow' (Varro+),
afflatus, -us 'breath, breeze' (Varro+), conflare 'to blow on, bring about' (P1.+),
deflare 'to blow away' (Varro+), difflare 'to disperse by blowing' (P1.+), efflare 'to
emit, breathe out' (PL+), inflare 'to fill with air, blow on' (P1.+), perflare 'to blow
through' (Varro+), refldre 'to blow back, blow out again' (Acc.+), sufflare 'to puff
up' (P1.+).
PIE pr. *bhlehi-/*bhlh]- 'to blow'? IE cognates: see fleo.
It is possible to derive sujflamen 'bar used for breaking wheeled vehicles' (Juv.+)
from sufflare. It can then be detached from fulcid. If flare is related to fled, the root
was probably *blh. The ppp. might directly reflect *bhlhto-, as Schrijver posits, but
r r
of course it can be secondary. The pr. is explained by Schrijver (1991: 402) from
*bhIhC-, the zero grade of the PIE root present (accepted by Meiser 1998), because
r
he expects *falje/o- as the outcome of the latter. LIV reconstructs *bhlhi-ie-, but does
not say how *flaje- > fla- arises from this preform. Alternatively, one might separate
'to blow' from 'to cry', since the semantics are sufficiently different, and reconstruct
PIE *bblh - for flare; yet this would leave PGm. *e in OHG blaen, Go. blesan 'to
2
blow' unexplained.
Bibl.: WH I: 517, II: 625, EM 240f., IEW I20ff., Schrijver 1991: 177, 402f., Meiser
1998: 124, LIV *bhlehr. fled

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: flos, -ris 'blossom, flower' [m. J] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: Flora 'the goddess of flowers' (Varro+), floralia, -ium [n.pl.]
'flower-gardens' (Varro+), florere 'to blossom, bloom' (Cato+), florens, -ntis
'prosperous' (P1.+), florescere 'to begin to flower' (Varro+), floridus 'flowery'
(Varro+);florifer 'producing flowers' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *flos{~) 'flower', *flos-a- 'goddess of flowers'. It. cognates: Vest, flusare
[abl.sg.m.] month name, from *flos-dri- 'of Flora'; O. fluusai [dat.sg.] the goddess
'Fl5ra'; O. f<l>uusasiafs [dat.abl.pl.] 'Floralia', feast of Flora < *JIos-asio-.
PIE *bhleh -os 'blossoming' > 'flower'. IE cognates: Olr. blath, W. blawd 'flower,
3
bloom', OCo. blodon, LCo. bledzhan 'flower' < *bldtu- < PIE *bhleh -tu-, MBret.
3
blezu, Bret, bleunv 'flowers' < *blatmV-; OHG bluowen, OE bldwan 'to bloom' <
*blojan~, Go. bloma, NHG Blume < *blo-m- 'flower'.
The derivation of Flora from flos is reminiscent of aurora to earlier *ausos. Oscan
fluusa- shows that the cult of this goddess was known more widely among the Italic
peoples. The ablaut grade of the root in the PIE s-stem cannot be determined with
certainty.
Bibl.: WH I: 518f., EM 241, IEW 160, Sohrijver 1991: 131,1995: 179, Sihler 1995:
310, Untermann 2000: 290-293, Stuber 2002: 76, LIV *bhleh -. fldvus,florus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fluo, -ere 'to flow, run (of waters)' [v. Ill; pf. fluxi, ppp. fluctum (younger flwatm)\
inscr. (CIL 584) conflouont] (P1.+)
Derivatives: fluentum 'a stream' (Lucr.+), flu(u)idus 'flowing, liquid' (Lucr.+);
flu(it)itare/flutdre 'to flow, run' (Lucr.+); flTictus, -us 'wave, billow' fluctuare
'to surge, be in agitation' (PI.+), fluctuosus 'agitated' (P1.+); fliictifragus 'that breaks
the waves' {Lucr.)\ flumen 'river, stream' (Enn.+); fluvius 'river' (Naev.+; fluvia [f.]
Acc. Sis.); flustra, -drum 'calm (of the sea), swell' (Naev.+); affluere 'to flow
towards; be rich' (Laev.+), afluere 'to flow away; abound in' (P1.+), confluere 'to
flow together, assemble' (P1.+), confluvium 'place where streams meet' (Varro),
confluges 'meeting place of rivers' (Andr.; conflages in Paul, ex F. may be a mistake
for * confluges), defluere 'to flow down, away' (Cato+), diffiuere 'to flow away in all
directions, dissolve' (Ter.+), ejfluere 'to flow out, escape' (Cato+), Tnfluere 'to flow
in' (Varro+), perfluere 'to stream' (Ter.+), perfluctuare 'to flood over' (Lucr.+),
profluere 'to flow forth, overflow' (Naev.+), profluvius 'fluctuating' (Caecil.),
profluvium 'a discharge' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *flow-e/o-.
PIE *bhleuH-(e/o-) 'to flow (over)'. IE cognates: Gr. Att. cpXeco 'to abound', (pXuco
'to seethe, boil over'; Lith. bliduti, 3s. blidwia 'to bleat, sob, weep', Latv. bjaut 'to
bleat, bellow', OCS b}bvati, Is. bjujg 'to vomit', Ru. blevdf (vulg.) < PIE *bhleuH-.
Leumann regards the spelling^- as irregular for *flou~, having been introduced from
compounds where *-flou- was in non-initial syllable. Leumann 1977: 279, 436
explains fluentum as a singularized form of a n.pl. fluenta to fluens, -ntis. The verb
form conflouont points to a pr.stem *fluu- < *ftou~, but confluges and the pf. flicd
seem to require a stem *flug-. Hence, Meiser reconstructs *bhleugw-. Yet, as EM have
already pointed out, PIE labiovelars lost their labiality after *-u-. Weiss 1994 shows
that ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: focus 'hearth, fireplace' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: foculus 'small stove' (Cato+).
A connection with Lat.^izc 'torch' and Lith. zvake 'candle' is formally impossible, since
Pre-Lat. *fwakV- would not yield foe-. Any etymology positing PIE *Dh-k(w) would
violate the PIE root structure constraints. Hamp 1992 proposes to explain focus as a
back-formation to foculus, which he explains from *Jweklo- < *ctg"'h-e-tlo- 'hearth'.
Yet this is chronologically difficult: initial > Pit. */'V is expected to yield *fe-,
and could only yield *fo- if the simplification of > f- were dated after *-we- >
*-wo-, which happened in the fifth c. BC. But that is rather late for the change >/-.
Bibl.: WH I: 521, EM 243, IEW 495, Hamp 1992.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fodio, -ere 'to pierce; to dig' [v. Ill; pf.jodi, ppp. fossum\fodiriCato lx] (P1.+)
Derivatives: fodicare 'to stab, prick' (P1.+); fossa 'ditch, trench' (Cato+), fossula
'small trench' (Cato+), fosslcius 'obtained by digging' (Vano+),fossilis 'obtained by
digging' (Varro+); circumfodere 'to dig round' (Cato+), confodere 'to dig up; pierce'
(P1.+), defodere 'to bury, insert in the ground' (P1.+), effodere/ecfodiri 'to dig up,
gouge out' (P1.+), infodere 'to place in the earth' (Cato+), interfodere 'to pierce'
(Lucr.),perfodere 'to make a hole through' perfossor 'burglar' (P1.+).
Pit *fop-i~.
PIE *bhodh-i- 'to poke, dig'. IE cognates: Hit.padda- /padd- 'to dig (the ground)' <
*bbodhh -; OPr. boadis 'stab' [m.], embaddusisi 'they stick', Lith. besii, 3s. beda 'to
2
stick, drive (into), dig', Latv. best 'dig, bury', Lith. badyti, Latv. badit 'to butt, prick';
OCS bosti, Is. bodg, ls.aor. bast 'to stab', SCr. bosti, bodem < BS1. *bed-, *bod-;
ToApatar 'they ploughed' < *bhodh-.
The long -o- in the pf. is probably analogical to pr.fod-, for instance on the model of
venid: vera. Since all languages show verb formations with o-grade (Hit, Latin, BS1.,
Toch.), the original formation may have been iterative or intransitive: *bhodh-(i-). The
Latin i-stem conjugation might be a rest of the PIE suffix, which surfaces as *-ie/o- in
this category in other branches o£IE.
Bibl.: WH I: 521, EM 243, IEW 113f., Leumann \9]1'. 568, Schrijver 1991: 411,
Meiser 1998: 212, LIV *bhedhh -.
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: foedus 'foul, unclean; fearful, repugnant' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: foedare 'to wound, dishonour, make unclean' (P1.+).
Pit *fo(j)ipo- 'afraid5.
PIE (*bhe-)bhoiH-/bhiH- [pf.] or *bhoH-i- [pr.] 'to be afraid'. IE cognates: Skt.
bibhaya 'is afraid', ptc. bibhivams-, YAv. bifiiuua 'afraid'; OPr. biatwei, Lith.
bijoti(s) , Latv. -bides 'to fear, be scared', OCS bojati s<? 'id.' < BS1. *b(o)i(?)-a?-;
OHG bibet (< PGm *bi-bai~) [3s.pr.act.] 'to tremble' (from an old pf.).
If the original meaning was 'awful, frightful, terrible' (thus surmised by WH, and
reiterated by Nussbaum), the adj. can be derived from PIE *bhiH- 'to be afraid'. Since
there is no productive adjectival suffix *-d(h)o-, and since foed- may represent either
*foid- or *fojid~, Nussbaum interprets foedus as an idus- adj. He does not dwell on the
o-ablaut, but since idus-adj. are often derived from stative verbs (pr. in -e-), foedus
may be based on the PIE pf. *(bhe-)bhoiH- (preserved in Skt. and Slavic) 'to be
afraid', or from an o-grade i-present *bhoH-i- (cf. Hamp 1985b).
Bibl.: WH I: 522f., EM 244, IEW 16If., Nussbaum 1999a: 390f, LIV *bheih.
r

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: foeteo 'to stink' [v. II] (P1.+; variants/^/-,
Derivatives: foetidus 'stinking' (P1.+).
Pit *fwoit- / *fwdje/ot- 'smoking, smelly'.
PIE *dhuoh -i- 'to smoke'. IE cognates: Olr. de [f], gen. diad 'smoke', Mir. dethach
2
'id.' < *duiiot- < *dhuh -i-ot- (see s.v. suffio for the phonetics); Hit. tuhhuuai- /
2
tuhhui- [c.] 'smoke' < PIE nom. *cfueh -u-oi-s, acc. *ctuh -u-oi-m, gen.
2 2
*ctuh -u-i-os. *
2
EM suggest that faeteo may be the original form, but also consider a relationship with
foedus 'repulsive'. If we apply the rule that *oi yielded oe after a labial (Poenus)
except in front of i in the next syllable (punicus), then foetidus cannot be old, or
proves *faetidus. But if foete- is more original, we can posit earlier *f(w)oit- or
*f(w)ojVt~, which enables a connection with Olr. de 'smoke'. Tocharian and Hittite
show that the full grade of *duh - was of the type *dueh -, so that we could assume a
2 2
basis *dhuoh -i-t- (> *fivoit-) or *dfluoh -i-e/ot- (> *jw5je/ot-) for the verb foetere. The
2 2
/-stem derivative which forms the basis of Italo-Celtic *dhuoh -i-t-, *d\ih -i-ot- may
2 2
be attested in Hit. tuhhuuai- /tuhhui- 'smoke'.
Bibl.: WH I: 499f„ EM 244, IEW 261-67, Leumann 1977: 65, Schrijver 1995: 292,
Kloekhorst 2008: 895. fimum, suffio, fumus, fuligo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: folium 'leaf [n. o] (P1.+)
Pit. * folio-.
PIE *dholH-io- 'leaf. IE cognates: W. dail 'foliage' < *dhlH-ih -; Gr. aor. eflc&ov,
2
pr. MXlco 'to flourish, grow', tfdXoq [n.] 'sprout', *I3OWK;, -u [n.j (only in gen.pl.
tfaX&flv) , tfaXepoc; 'flourishing', tfaXedo) 'to flourish', -QuhXa 'leaves' (Hsch.); Arm.
dalar 'green, fresh', Alb. dal [Is.], del [23s.] 'to come out'.
Folium has often been compared with Gr. (puHov 'leaf < *bhol-io- (with raising of
*o in Gr. between labial and resonant). Beekes 1991 considers the possibility that OS
blad, OHG blat 'leaf is a f-derivative from the stem *bhl-. Yet there is no verbal stem
from which the noun could have been derived. Michiel Driessen (p.c.) proposes to
connect folium with the root *dhlH- 'to sprout, blossom'. The colour of the laryngeal
might be determined as *h on the strength of -flatepog, but this might be a recent
1
formation to the stem *i3aXv-. Possibly, the o-grade and the suffix offolium are also
preserved in Gr. 'foliage', although this is a hapax from Hsch.
Bibl.: WH I: 523f„ EM 244, IEW 122, 234, Klingenschmitt 1982: 172, Sihier 1995:
43, LIV *dhalh. fidvus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fons, fontis 'spring, well' [m. i] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: Fontanalia, -ium [n.pl.] 'the festival of Pons' (Varro).
Pit. *fonti-. It. cognates: possibly U. funtlere, fondlire [abl.pl. + -en] '?', a certain
locality; maybe *fontelo~.
PIE *dhonh -ti- 'flowing'. IE cognates: Skt. dhan1 'to run, to flow', pr. dhdnva
2
[2s.ipv.act.], OP danu[...] [3s.pr.] 'flows' < IE *cfenhu~; maybe Av. danu- 'river',
r
Oss. don 'water, river' < PIr. *dana~, ToB tsnamo* 'flowing', ToA sb. tsnantar,
ptc.pf. tsno 'to flow' < *tsan- < PIE *cfen~.
Semantically, the connection with words for 'to run, flow' are unproblematic.
Formally, WH suggest a contamination of *fontos and *fentis, whereas EM argue that
the o-grade betrays an old root noun. Since we now reconstruct the root as *dhenh -
2
with a final laryngeal, neither hypothesis is very attractive: the zero grade *dhnh -ti-
2
would yield Lat. *fnatis, whereas an old root noun would yield *dhonh > *fona. One
2
might instead posit a ft'-stem *dhonh, -ti- with loss of the laryngeal due to the de
Saussure effect. For the formation type, cf. mons.
Bibl.: WH I: 525, EM 244f., IEW 249, Untermann 2000: 301, LIV *dhenh - 'to run'.
2
for, far! 'to speak, say' [v. II; occurs mainly in 3s. and 3p.; ppa.fans, gd\e.fandus,
ppp .fatwri] (P1.+)
Derivatives: Jatum 'prophecy, destiny' (P1.+); fama 'news, rumour, public opinion,
reputation, fame' (Naev.+), infamia 'disgrace, bad reputation' (P1.+), infamis 'of ill
repute' (V\.+),famigerabilis 'famous' (Varro), famigeratid 'gossip' (V\.), jamigerator
'a gossip' (PI.),famosus 'infamour, notorious' (P1.+); fibula 'talk, rumour; story, tale'
(Naev.+), fdbulinus 'the god of speech' (Varro), fabularT 'to talk' (PI.+), cdnfabulari
'to converse' (PI.+); facundus 'eloquent' (P1.+), facunditas 'eloquence' (P1.+),
facundia 'the ability to speak eloquently' (Ter.+); affabilis 'easy to talk to' (Ter.+),
effort 'to speak to' (Acc.+), effari 'to utter, say' (Enn.+), infans, -ntis 'unable to
spea...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: forda 'carrying, pregnant (of cows)' [adj. a] (Varro+; also horda)
Derivatives: fordicTdia, -drum [n.pl.] 'festival at which pregnant cows were
sacrificed' (Varro+).
Pit. *for(i)dd-.
PIE *bhori-d- 'giving birth'? IE cognates: Lith. ber(g)zdzias 'barren (of a cow)',
ber(g)zdi 'barren cow', RuCS brezda, brez(d)a 'pregnant', Ru. berezaja (dial.) 'mare
in foal' < *bherdio- 'carrying, pregnant'.
The initial syllable of the BS1. words is circumflex (suggesting *-dh- in the suffix
rather than *-d-), but Latin forda cannot reflect *-rdh- (which would yield *forba).
Thus, Latin and BS1. continue two independent formation on the basis of PIE *bhr-.
Nussbaum 1999a proposes that Latin forda was built on an i-stem *bhori- 'birther,
who gives birth', which was enlarged by *-d- (as often in Greek, and as in Latin
pecu-d- of the same semantic sphere) and finally hypercharacterized by adding fem.
*-a. Pre-Latin *forida > forda.
Bibl.: WH I: 527, EM 228, IEW 128ff., Nussbaum 1999a: 381,406, LIV *bher-. -+ferd

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: forfex, -icis 'tongs, pincers; shears, scissors' [f. (m.) k] (Celsus+; also forpex)
Pit. *forpo- 'shearing', *forpdje/o- 'to shear'. It. cognates: U. furfati, furfant
[3p.pr.], efurfatu [3s.ipv.II + e- 'out'] certain action with 'sheep' as direct object, 'to
shear'?
PIE *bhrdh-o- [adj.], IE cognates: Gr. rceptfto 'to capture, take in', 7iToX.uiopi9o<;
'capturing cities', Ttoptfera 'to pillage'.
Either a noun with a word-internal cluster -if-, which is irregular according to the
standard Latin sound laws, or a compound in -fax 'making'. However, since cp. in
-fax normally do not show syncope (aurifax, opifax, etc.), the latter is less probable.
Also, the recent date offorfex makes a foreign origin more likely. An old connection
is with U.furfa-, which is often translated as 'to shear' (e.g. Meiser 1986: 101). One
might connect Gr. Tteptfo} 'to capture, take in', 7iioX.i7ropi3o<; 'capturing cities' (Janda
2000: 230-240), in which case the U. verb could be denominal to an adj. *bhrdh-o-
'capturing, harvesting, shearing'.
Bibl.: WH 1:526, EM 247, IEW 138, Untermann 2000:302f„ LIV ?*bherdh-. ->forceps

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: foria 'diarrhoea' [f. a] (Varro)
Derivatives: foriolus 'suffering from diarrhoea' (Lab.); conforire 'to defile with
ordure' (Pompon.).
Pit. *foria-7
WH tentatively propose a connection with a PIE root *dher- 'to shit', which is
otherwise only attested in enlarged form in other branches of IE: Lith. derkii 'to make
dirty' < *dher-k-, OIc. drita (dreit), OHG trizan 'cacare' (maybe a Gm. rhyming
formation to *skita- 'to shit'), Ru. dial, dristatb 'to suffer from diarrhoea', Bulg.
driskam, dristb. WH admit themselves that this renders it uncertain that Latin would
continue an unenlarged root form. Since 'to defecate' is often referred to by means of
euphemisms, a derivation from the root *bher- 'to carry', e.g. iterative *bher-bhor-ie-
'to bring (away) repeatedly' seems at least equally likely; or foria might be a
derivative from fordre 'to pierce', as 'piercing' (cf. German Durchfall).
Bibl.: WH I: 527f., EM 247, IEW 256. fero, ford

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: foris 'door' [f. /] (P1.+; rarely sg., usually pi. fores, -ium ('folding doors').)
Derivatives: fords 'to the outside' (Naev.+), foris 'on the outside' (P1.+), foricula
'window-shutter' (Varro+).
Pit. *fwor-(i~) > *for-i~; *for-d-.
PIE *dhu6r-, *dhur- 'door'. IE cognates: Olr. dorus [m.'w] , Dor, Duir [toponyms],
W. dor, Bret, dor < PCI. *duora, fduro- 'door'; Hit. andurza [adv.] 'inside, indoors'
Skt. dvar- [f. (du.pl.)] 'door, gate', YAv. duuar-, OP duvar(a)~ 'gate',
Gr. tfupa 'door', Myc. o-pi-tu-ra-jo bpithuraioii 'door-keeper', Arm. dowr-k' [pi.]
'door', Alb. dere < *d(u)or-om/a, Lith. pi. durys, OCS dvbrb < BS1. *dvir~, *dur-\
Go. daw [n.j, OHG turi, OIc. dyrr < *(tur-(i-), ToB twere 'door' < *ctuoro~.
Lat. fords and foris reflect the acc.pl. and loc.pl. (» ins.pl.) of an a-stem'ybra-:
hence, these developed from 'to the door' and 'at the door', respectively. The
evidence points to a PIE root noun
*d uor-, *d ur- 'door', maybe used as a
plural or dual tantum; Greek, Albanian (possibly) and Latin (and Celtic) show an
additional derivative *dhu(o)r-h -
2
'(set of) door(s)'. The Latin i-stem was probably
derived from *dhuor- (cf. Schrijver 1991:472) and may originally have been used as a
singular, but it usually occurs as a plural from the earliest literary texts onwards.
Sihler assumes that foris is a back-formation to the pi. fores, which cannot be ruled
out. In view of the zero grade which is reflected e.g. in Germanic and Slavic,
however, the original pi. or du. seems to have had *dhur-, which would yield Latin
*Jur-es (the inf. fore < *fuse has lowering in front of r < *z< *s). Also, the sg. foris is
attested in the oldest literature (Plautus).
Bibl.: WH 1:529f., EM 246, IEW 278f., Schrijver 1991:47If, Sihler 1995:148. ->forum

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: forma 'form, contour, appearance; beauty' [f. a] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: formare 'to mould, fashion' (Lucr.+), formamentum 'arrangement'
(Lucr.), formaster, -tri 'kind of pastry' (Titin.), formatura 'the shaping' (Lucr.),
formosus 'beautiful' (Ter.+), formula 'pretty appearance; register, formula, document'
(P1-+); deformis 'misshapen, disfigured' (Lucil.+); deformare 'to design, sketch;
spoil' (P1.+), mformare 'to fashion, sketch' (Varro+).
EM stress the length of o in forma, but there seems to have been a recent lengthening
of short *o in front of -rC- (Leumann 1977: 114). Leumann derives formaster (with
the pejorative suffix -aster) from formus 'hot', but it seems more likely that forma
was its basis. Since formica 'ant' and formidd 'ghost, scarecrow' point to a
dissimilation *m - m > *f - m, forma might go back to *morma. Maybe forma and
Gr. pop<pi] 'form, shape, contour' were borrowed from a third party; or the Greek
word was borrowed into another language, such as Etruscan, and passed thence into
Latin as *morma.
Bibl.: WH I: 530, EM 247, Leumann 1977: 21, 114, 319, Sihier 1995: 76.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: formica 'ant* [f. a] (PI.+)
Derivatives: formicinus 'ant-like, crawling' (PL).
Pit. *mormika-.
PIE *moru-o/i-(?), IE cognates: Olr. moirb, W. myr, Bret, merien 'ant' < *morui-;
Skt. vamrd- 'ant', valmika- [m.] 'ant-hill1; Av. maoiri-, Oss. mcelzyg/mulzug, Pasto
mezay, Sogd. ym'wrc < PIr. *marui(ka)~, Sogd. zm'wr'k, Khwar. zmwrk, MP, MoP
mor < *maruaka~; Gr. puppr^, -T^KCX; 'ant', puppoc;, pup^a^ Poppai;, Sppucai; (with J3-
and zero < *p-); RuCS mravbjb, ORu. morovej, Po. mrowka, SCr. mrdv 'ant' <
*moru-iH~; OIc. maurr < *mour-o~, Far. meyra < *mauron~, Dan. my re, OE myre,
ME mire, MDu. miere, Crimean Go. miera < *meur(j)dn- [f.] 'ant'; ToB warme*,
nom.pl. warmi 'ant' < *upno-.
The word for 'ant' is difficult to reconstruct because of the deviating forms; probably,
taboo distortions took place in many languages. This process can still be observed in
modem dialects, for instance of Dutch and German. Latin /- might go back to *m- via
a dissimilation of *m- — m> *f-m. Meillet (1918) assumes an intermediate stage
*[b] (voiced labial fricative), which he regards as an argument for the view that all
PIE *bh changed to *b before yielding Latin f. In the IE branches, we find the stem
structures *moru-o/i- (Celtic, Iranian, Slavic, Gm.), *mormo/i- (Greek) and *itormo/i-
(Skt., Toch., Greek?). Since the latter is quite similar to PIE *u(o)rmo/i~ 'worm,
insect' (see s.v. vermis), it may have been influenced by it; consequently, for 'ant' the
form *moruo/i- is more likely to be old.
Bibl.: WH I: 531, EM 247f., IEW 749, Leumann 1977: 191.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: formldo, -inis 'fear, alarm, awe; bogy' [f. «] (P1.+)
Derivatives: formidare 'to fear, dread' (Naev.+), formido/ulosus 'alarming;
frightened' (Naev.+).
Pit *morm-Tdo/en-1
PIE *mor-mo- 'fear'? IE cognates: Gr. popp.6, -ouq 'bogey, monster', poppuaoopai
'to frighten', poppopoq, (.wppoq 'fear'; maybe to Gr. poppupw 'to roar and boil'?
Leumann (1977: 341) regards formidtdosus as a rhyming formation to peric-idosus
'dangerous'. The noun formidd can be interpreted as the antonym of cupido 'desire'
and htbido 'lust', which may explain the suffix. Since there are no verb forms
*form-(i-)- indicating 'to fear', Latin form- might go back to *morm-, in which case a
connection with the Greek words is possible. This would imply a noun *mormo-
'fear' as is reconstructed by IEW. There is no PIE etymology for this connection, if it
is correct.
Bibl.: WH I: 532, EM 248, IEW 749, Leumann 1977: 191,367.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: formus 'warm' [adj. o/a] (Paul. exF.)
Derivativesiformidus 'warm' (Cato apud Paul, ex F.).
Pit. *x"ormo-.
PIE *gwtlo/er-mo- 'warm'. IE cognates: Skt, gharma- 'heat, glow', Av. garama-
'wamn; heat', OP garma-pada- 'the fourth month (June-July)' < *gwhor-mo-; Gr.
i3epp6<;, Arm. jerm 'warm' < *gwher-mo-; maybe Alb. zjarm 'heat' < *gwhermo- (cf.
de Vaan 2004b: 82); OPr. gorme 'heat', Latv. garme 'warmth' *gwhor-mo-.
The adj. formidus can be explained as modelled on its antonym frigidus.
Theoretically, formus may reflect *gwhormos or *gvvhpnos; but since no other
language shows a zero grade, *gwbormo- seems more likely. The PIE vacillation in the
root vowel between *gwhermo- and *gwhormo- may be due to the different verbal
formations of the root, of which we find bot *gwher-e/o- 'to heat' and *gwhor-i- 'to be
hot', cf LIV.
Bibl.: WH I: 532f., EM 248, IEW 493ff„ Leumann 1977: 329, Schrijver 1991: 420,
Meiser 1998: 103, Nussbaum 1999a: 405, LIV *gyher-. -t forceps, fortius
a

Derivativesifornax [f.] 'furnace' (Cato+), fornacalia, -drum [n.pl.] 'Baking Festival'
(Varro+);praefumium 'furnace-entrance' (Cato+).
Pit. */orno-.
PIE *gwhr-no- 'heat' or 'oven'. IE cognates: Olr. gorn 'fire' < *gwhor-no-; Skt.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: or *gwher-os- [n.] (de Vaan 2004b: 82), RuCS grbno 'cauldron, pot, oven' [n.], Ru.
gorn 'blacksmith's hearth' [n.] gen.sg. gorna; gorno 'blacksmith's hearth, clay-oven'
;
< *gwW.
The original form must have been forn~, which became furn- in part of the Roman
speech. The raising of -or- in front of a consonant seems to have been an ongoing
process; it might have been dialectal, since it is regular in Sabellic. Since Skt. and
Slav, show zero-grade *gwhr-no-, this is also the most likely reconstruction for fornus.
Of course, in view of formus with a PIE o-grade, it is conceivable that Pit. or Latin
introduced the o-grade into the noun, which would give *gwhor-no- > formis.
Bibl.: WH I: 533f., EM 248, IEW 493ff., Leumann 1977: 48, Sihler 1995: 43,
Meiser 1998: 64, LIV *gyher-. ^ formus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: foro, -are 'to bore through, pierce' [v. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: foramen 'aperture, hole' (Cato+); inforare 'to bore into' (P1.+),
perforare 'to make a hole, pierce' (Varro+).
Pit. *foraje/o~.
PIE *bhorH-ie/o- 'to pierce, strike'? Or a noun *bhorH-h - 'hole'? IE cognates: Lith.
2
barti, 3s. bara 'to scold, accuse, forbid', Latv. barti 'to scold, blame1, OCS brati
Is. borjg 'to fight', Ru. borot' 'to overpower' < *bhorH-, OIc. berja 'to beat, hit',
OHG berjan 'to hit, pound, knead1 < PGni. *barjan~.
Regarded as a denominal verb by WH and IEW. This is conceivable, but the o-grade
presents in other branches of IE suggest a different solution: an iterative verb
*bhorH-(i-) 'to pierce many times, bore'. If the final laryngeal was vocalized in Latin,
we can assume a phonetic development *bhorHie- > *foraje- > ford-.
Bibl.: WH I: 48If., EM 248, IEW 133ff., Schrijver 1991: 216, LIV *bherH-. ferio

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fors, -tis 'chance, luck' [f. /] (P1.+; only nom./ors and abl. forte 'by chance')
Derivatives: forsitan 'perhaps' (Ter.+); fortasse (P1.+), fortassTs 'id.' (PI.+); fortuTtiis
'by chance, random' (PI,+),fortuna 'fortune, favourable outcome' (Naev.+), fortunatus
'fortunate, lucky' (Naev.+), fortundtim 'prosperously' (Enn.), fortunare 'to make
fortunate' (PI.+), Tnfortunatus 'unfortunate' (P1.+), infortunium 'misfortune' (P1.+).
Pit. *forti- [fi] 'luck', *fortu- 'luck'. It. cognates: Pael. forte [gen.sg.?] *forteis,
maybe borrowed from Latin.
PIE *bhr-ti- 'bearing, case'? IE cognates: see s.v.ferd.
The adv. forsitan derives from *fors sit an 'perchance it be that'. The origin of
fortasse seems to be *forte an sit/s, but the phonetics (especially the short final
vowel) are unclear. The noun fortuna and the adj. fortu-Ttus presuppose a w-stem
*fortu- 'chance, luck', whence an adj. *fortuno- 'lucky'. The precise origin of -Ttus in
fortu-Ttus and in its semantic neighbour gratu-itus 'free of charge' is unclear. The
semantic shift from 'load' or 'the carrying' to 'chance, luck' is not obvious, and EM
go so far as to reject the connection between fors and fero. Yet the co-occurrence of
ti- and ta-stems seems old, and there are many ways in which a meaning 'chance,
luck' can originate.
Bibl.: WH I: 534f., EM 249, IEW l28ff., Leumann 1977: 323, Untermann 2000:
304, LIV *bher-. fero

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fortis 'strong, robust' [adj. /'] (Lex XII+; variant horctus,forctus,forctis 'good' Festus
348, Paul, ex F. 84, 102)
Derivatives: fortitudo 'strength, courage' (Ter.+).
Pit. *forkti/o-. It. cognates: O. fortis [comp., nom.acc.sg.n., adv.] 'stronger'; maybe
borrowed from Latin.
PIE *bhorg-to- 'stuffed'? *dh(o)rgh-to- 'strong, robust'? IE cognates: Skt. caus: m
barhayati 'to lay low', pr. *bpnha-, aor. barh- 'to make strong', pari-bfdha- [ppp.]
'firm, strong', dvibdrhas- [adj.] 'with double strength', brhant- 'great, large', YAv. us
.. . barazaiia- 'to make strong / big', barazah- [n.] 'height', barssna [ins.sg.)]
L T F
'elevated place, height', YAv. barszant- 'rising high, high, loud' < b (e)rg -, Olr. bri
'hill', Brigit (woman's name), Arm. barjr 'high', ToA parkar 'long' // Skt. drmha-
'to make firm', pf dadfhand- [ptc.med.] 'holding fast', ptc. drdhd- (< *drzdh&)
'fixed, firm, solid', OAv. dTdarazo [2s.desid.inj.act.] 'to desire to fasten', dsraz-
'fetter', YAv. darzzaiia~ 'to fasten', darazra- 'strong, firm'; Lith. dirzti 'to become
hard'.
The form and meaning are not specific enough to decide on the etymology. In view of
the adj. meaning, one would expect the suffix to have been *-to- rather than *-ti-. The
root may have a structure *bh/dh/gwh + (o)r + K-. Within Latin, farcio 'to stuff seems
the best semantic match which would also satisfy the phonetics: PIE *bhorg-to- would
yield * fordo-. A connection with PIE *bhergh- ' high, elevated' (WH) does not
explain the meaning of fort is. Alternatively, one might connection fortis with the Ilr.
and Baltic forms for 'to make firm, become hard'; but only if these continue PIE *</-,
not if they continue PIE *d- (as LIV suspect). Semantically, this would be
satisfactory.
Bibl.: WH I: 535f., EM 249f., IEW I40f., Untermann 2000: 304f., LIV *bhergh-1
*dhergh-. —> firmus

stadium)' (Erm.+),forensis 'of the forum, public'(Varro+).
Pit. *jworo~ '(room) near the door'. It. cognates: U. furu,/wro [acc.sg.] 'forum'.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: passage', Lith. dvaras [m.] 'estate; court', OCS dvorb 'court', PTo. *twere 'door'.
WH interpret forum as 'fenced area' to the root of fordre, but Pokorny 1959 rejects
this. Forum is generally regarded as a derivative of PIE 'door', and connected with
other IE forms from *ctuor-o-. The required semantic development is 'area at the
doors' > 'entrance room, vestibule' > 'public room' > 'public space'; this is not so
problematic as to overrule the formal correspondences with Lith. dvaras.
Bibl.: WH I: 537f„ EM 250, IEW 278f., Meiser 1986: 116, Schrijver 1991: 471£,
Sihler 1995: 180, Untermann 2000: 305. foris

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fovea 'pit' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: favisae/favissae [f.pl.] 'vaults, subterranean chambers' (Gel.+).
The connection with Gr. yph, H. %EIF] 'serpent's den' must be dismissed. It is
uncertain that fovea m\6favisae belong together, as their etymology is unknown.
Bibl.: WH I: 538, EM 250, IEW 451, Schrijver 1991: 448.
foveO 'to make or keep warm; to relieve' [v. II; pf.fevl, ppp. Jo turn] (P1.H-)
Derivatives: feculum 'device for warming' (PI), foculare 'to revive, cherish'
(Varro+); fementnm 'soothing application, remedy' (HOT.+), femes, -itis [m.] 'chips of
wood' (Verg.+), fetus, -us 'heating' (Plin.).
Pit. *fofeje/o-.
PIE *dhogwh-eie- 'to burn'. IE cognates: Mir. daig, gen.sg. dega 'fire, pain'; Skt.
dahati, caus. dahdyati, YAv. dazaiti 'burns', Gr. i&ppa 'ashes', Alb. djeg 'to bum',
n-dez 'to ignite'; Lith. degti 'to burn', OCS zesti, Is. zegg, Toch. tsak- 'to burn up,
consume by fire', tsak- 'to illuminate'.
Schrijver 1991: 278 concludes that fe~ probably reflects unsyncopated *fowi- <
*fowe~. Thus, feculum < *fowe-cuhim, fementum < *fowe-mentum. Apart from
foculum, all derivatives appear quite late. The pf. Jovi has developed by contraction
from *fowe-wai. Foveo escaped the delabialization of *ou > *au because *gwh had not
changed into *u at that time (Schrijver 199(1, Vine 2006a: 212).
Bibl.: WH I: 466f., EM 250, IEW 240f., Schrijver 1991: 277ff., 448, Meiser 1998:
206, LIV *dhegyh. favillajebris

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fraces, -um 'fragments of olive pulp left after pressing' [f.pl. k] (Cato+; sg. frax
(gloss.), pi. also flaces)
Derivatives: fracidus 'soft, pulpy' (Cato), fracescere 'to become soft' (Cato+),
confracescere 'to putrify, rot' (Varro).
Pit. *frak-.
IE cognates: OPr. dragios 'yeast', Lith. drdges, Latv. dradzi, OCS drozdbje 'dregs'
< BS1. *dro(z)gi(?)a?\ OIc. dregg 'yeast, dregs'. Alb. dra 'dregs, sediment' (maybe to
drege 'scab, sore', Demiraj 1997) may have *drag-, but *drab- also seems possible.
With *-£>-: Mir. drab 'dredgs', Ru. drob 'yeast', OHG pi. trebir 'husks (of grapes)' <
PGm. *drdsna- in OE drosne, OHG truosana, MoDu. droesem 'dregs'.
The spelling fracc- may have been influenced by flacced 'to decline in strength',
flaccus 'drooping, floopy', which are semantically quite close. Flaces may have -/-
from flocces. The Gm., BS1. and Latin words for 'dregs' clearly belong together, but
their vowels do not match, and point to a non-IE *a. The velars do not match either,
but Latin voiceless *k may have arisen in the nom.sg. *praks and thence spread
through the paradigm. It appears that we are dealing with a loanword from an
unknown, non-IE language, or from a lost IE language in which the root *dhregh- 'to
drag' or *d\eh gh- 'to soil' yielded a form *d rag - 'dredges of wine, oil, fat'.
2
Bibl.: WH I: 538f., EM 251, IEW 251, Schrijver 1991: 486.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fragro, -are 'to smell strongly' [v. I] (Cat.+)
Pit. * fragro-.
PIE *bhrh[g-ro- 'smelling'. IE cognates: Mir. brert 'putrid, foul', MW braen, B
brein 'putrid, corrupt'
< PCI. *bragno- < *bbrh,g -no-; Olr. braig(a)id*, •braig 'to
fart, break wind' < *brag-ie- Olr. broimm, W. bram, Co. Bret, bramm 'fart' <
t
*brag-smn; MHG brcehen < *brekjan 'to smell' < *bhrehig-; OHG braccho, MLG,
MDu. bracke 'hound' < *bhrh]g-n-.
Schrijver adopts the etymology preferred by WH and IEW, viz. of an adj. *fragros
from which the verb fragrdre was derived. The postulated *fragros can reflect PIE
*bhrHg-ro-; if one connects the Gm. words cited here, the laryngeal is *hi. Leumann
has proposed a connection with Skt. ava-ghrati 'smells', abhi-jighrant- 'smelling',
but Schrijver shows that this is formally very difficult. In 1995, Schrijver connects the
Celtic words cited. Schumacher is hesitant to build on MHG brcehen, and reconstructs
the root as ^rHg-.
Bibl.: WH I: 540, EM 251, IEW 163, Leumann 1977: 166, Schrijver 1991: 185f.,
1995: 170f., Schumacher 2004: 232.
frater
239
fraga, -Orum 'strawberry' [n.pl. o] (Vergilius+)
Pit .frdgo-.
IE cognates: Alb. (Eastern Geg) drathe, (Tosk) dredhe 'strawberry'.
The meaning of fraga closely matches that of Alb. dredhe, and the two may reflect
*dhrHg-o-. It seems unlikely that the word indicated 'strawberry' in PIE, however,
and it may well be a loanword. An alternative connection of fraga with Gr. pa^,
pay'grape', ptb^ 'grape' is possible if one starts from *srag~; both the phonological
form and the diverging meanings 'strawberry' and 'grape' would then point to a third
(non-IE?) language from which the word was borrowed.
Bibl.: WH I: 540, EM 251, Schrijver 1991: 177, Demiraj 1997: 144.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: frango, -ere 'to break' [v. Ill; pf.fregf, ppp .fraction] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: fragescere 'to become subdued' (Acc.), fragilis 'fragile, crackling'
(Lucr.+), fragmen 'a piece broken off (Sis.+), fragmentum 'fragment' (Lucil.+),
fragor 'the breaking, crash, roar' (Lucr.+), fragdsus 'brittle, rugged' (Lucr.+);
confringere 'to destroy, ruin' (Andr.+), confragosus 'uneven, difficult' (P1.+),
defringere 'to break off (Cato+), diffringere 'to break up' (P1.+), ejfringere 'to break
open' (P1-+), infringere 'to break, crush, deprive of (P1.+), interfringere 'to break
(here and there' (Cato), offringere 'to break up by cross-ploughing' (Varro+),
perfringere 'to break, fracture^ (Lucr.+), praefringere '-to break at the end' (P1.+),
refringere 'to break back, force open' (P1.+), suffringere 'to break the lower part of
(P1.+); compounds in -fragium '.the breaking', -fragus 'who breaks'.
Pit. *frang~ [pr.], *fragto- [ppp.].
PIE *bhr-n-g- [pr.] 'to break', *bhrg-to- [ppp.]. IE cognates: Go. brikan, OHG
brehhan 'to break' < *bhreg/g-.
The long vowel of fractus is due to Lachmann's Law. Schrijver 1991 argues that
Latin and Irish have continued a PIE root present *bhreg-/*bhrg- in different ways,
whereas LIV opts for an old athematic aorist. The latter seems more likely in view of
the meaning. Since the Irish forms which Schrijver connected in 1991 are now
connected with fragrdre, it may be that Gm. continues the old root aorist, and Latin
the nasal presents
Bibl.: WH I: 539, 541, EM 251, IEW 165, Schrijver 1991: 137, 478, 483f., LIV
*bhre(g—• sujfragium

content:

Derivatives: frater cuius 'little brother' (P1.+), frater citlare 'to swell up (of a boy's
breasts at puberty)' (PI.)\fraternus 'of a brother' (Pac.+).
Pit. * frater-. It. cognates: Ven. [f.Jhraterei [dat.sg.], U. frater, frater [nom.pl.], O.
fratrum, U. fratrum, fratru, J'ra/rom [gen.pl.], fratrus [dat.pl.], fratruspe(r) [abl.pl.
+ -per] 'brother'; U. fratreks, fratrexs [nom.sg.m.], fratreci [dat.sg.m.] ,fratreca
[abl.sg.f.] 'belonging to the brotherhood' *frdtr-ikoU. fratrecate [loc.sg.?] 'the
office of a fratrex1 < *frdtrikato-.
PIE *bhreh -tr- 'brother'. IE cognates: Olr. brathair, W. brawd; Skt. bhratar-, Av.
2
OP bratar- 'brother', Gr. (ppa-np [m.] 'member of a brotherhood', atppfjicop 'not
bound by social ties', Phryg. ppaxepe 'brothers', Arm. elbayr 'brother', OPr. brati,
brote, Lith. brolis, Latv. bralis, OCS bratrb, bratb, Go. bropar, OHG bruoder, OIc.
brodir, ToB procer, To A prdcar 'brother' *
For PIE 'brother', Pinault (2007: 276f.) assumes an etymology *bVeh - 'group of
2
males borne by the same mother' > *bhreh -tr 'belonging to the *bhreh '. In his view,
2 2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fraus, -dis 'harm, danger; deceit' [f. d\ (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: fraudare 'to cheat, swindle' (P1.+) (pf. frauddvi and frausus sum),
fraudatio 'cheating' (PI+), defrau/udare 'to cheat' (P1.+); fraudulentus 'dishonest'
(P1.+), fraudulentia 'dishonesty' (PI.); frustra 'in vain' (later frustra) (P1.+), friistrdrT
'to delude; frustrate' (P1.+), frustratio 'deception, trick' (P1.+), frustratus, -us
'deception' (PL), frustrdmen 'id.' (Lucr.), defrustrdri'to foil completely' (PI.).
Pit. *frawV-. It. cognates: U.frosetom est [3s.pf.ps.] 'is not valid (?)' < *jrauss-ito-
< intensive formation on the basis of *fraud-to~? (Meiser 1986: 242).
PIE *dhrou-V-d(b)-? IE cognates: Skt. dhruti- 'deception, error', -dhrut- 'deceiving',
YAv. drduuaiiat 'will deceive', Parth. dr'w- 'to seduce' < *dhr(o)u-.
Lat. frustra is pi. of *frustrum < * fraud-tro-. Szemerenyi 1989: 33ff. and Schrijver
1991: 444, independently of one another, derive fraus from PIE *dhreugh- 'to
deceive', but not in the same way. Szemerenyi posits an abstract *dhreugh-os, which
would have yielded a paradigm *fros, *froris, whence with diss. *frddis, and with
hypercorrect au for urban o finally fraus. These assumptions (*eu > *d, the
dissimilation and the hypercorrection) are ad hoc and render the solution unlikely.
Schrijver postulates that fraus derives from a PIE root as reflected in Skt.
dhruti- 'deception', varima-dhrut- 'deceiving Varuna', Parth. dr'w 'to deceive'. He
then posits *cfrou- V-<fh>- whence *frowVd- and with unrounding of *ow > *frawVd-
> fraud-. Tor frustra, Schrijver reconstructs *ctrou-C- or *ctreu-(V)C-. This solution
is relatively elegant on the phonetic side, but the status of the reconstructed suffix
remains unclear. According to the rule established by Vine 2006a, the first syllable
t
should have been pretonic: *frou-.
Bibl.: WH I: 543, EM 252, 257, IEW 277, Szemerenyi 1989: 33ff., Schrijver 1991:
444, Untermann 2000: 301, LIV 2.*dW.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fraxinus 'ash-tree' [f. o] (Enn.+)
Pit. *frakse/ino-.
PIE *bhrHg-s-e/ino-. IE cognates: Skt. bhurja- 'kind of birch', Oss. bcerz I bcerzce
'birch', OPr. berse, Lith. berzas, Latv. b^rzs, CS breza, Ru. bereza, OIc. bjgrk, OHG
birihha 'birch'.
The quantity of the a is unknown, but Schrijver convincingly argues that short a is
more likely. All existing etymologies start from the assumption that fraxinus is
cognate with the word for 'birch' in other IE languages. Schrijver proposes the
following scenario: a PIE root noun *bherHg-/*bhrHg- 'birch' acquired the meaning
'ash-tree', and became formally influenced by the PIE paradigm of *Heh -s- 'ash'
3
(Lat. ornus). The new 5-stem *bherHg-(o)s, gen.sg. *bhrHg-s-os 'ash' was extended
by means of the suffix *-eno- or *-ino- (cf. almts, farnus, ornus, quemus). The
resulting *bhrHg-s-e/ino- yielded *frak-s-e/ino- by means of Schrijver's vocalization
rule of *CRHDC > *CraCC. If PIE 'birch' is indeed derived from the root *bhreh,g-
'to shine', it probably refers to the white barch of the birch. But the full grade in the
word for 'birch' (in BS1. and Gm.) would have schwebe-ablaut with regard to the
verbal forms Skt. bhraja-, YAv. braza- 'shines'.
Bibl.: WH I: 544, EM 252, IEW 139f., Schrijver 1991: 186ff., LIV *bhreh,g-.
farnus, ornus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fremo, -ere 'to utter a deep dull continuous sound' [v. l\l;fremui,fremitum] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: fremitus, -us 'roar, rumble' (P1.+), fremibundus 'roaring, growling'
(Acc.+); perfremere 'to fill a place with roaring' (Acc.).
Pit. *freme/o~.
PIE *bhrem-e/o- 'to hum, rumble'. IE cognates: W. brefu 'to bleat, roar', Parth.
brm~, MP bram- 'to cry' < Ilr. *brama~, Po. brzmiec 'to hum, sound', OHG pram
[pret.], MHG brimmen 'to roar'.
The reconstruction of the PIE root is not completely certain: especially from the point
of view of Latin morphology, a set root is also conceivable (Meiser 2003).
Bibl.: WH 1: 544f., EM 252f., IEW 142f, Meiser 2003': 125, LIV *b\em-.
frendo, -ere 'to grind one's teeth' [v. Ill; ppp. fresum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: nefrens, -ndis 'not able to chew yet, infant' (Andr.+).
Pit. *frend-.
PIE *g,vhrendth)-e/o-. IE cognates: Lith. gr^sti, Is. grendziu 'to plane, scour' < *-nd~,
OE grindan 'to grind', Go. ^grinds 'ground, crushed', OHG grint, MoDu. grind
'gravel', OIc. grandi 'sandbank', OIc. grunnr, Go. grundu- 'ground, base
The morphology of the adj. ne-frend- 'not grinding its teeth' is odd: one would expect
*in-frend- (Bader 1962: 37). Livingston 2004: 67f. argues that ne-frend- is a recent
reformation of *in-frend-, to avoid semantic ambiguity with *frendere *in 'biting
(on)'. She assumes that nefrens was modeled on deprans 'not eating' (Naev.+). For
PIE, it is impossible to unite the Latin, Baltic and Gm. words under one certain
preform: Gm. must have *-dh-, whereas Baltic need *-d- (Winter's Law). Are these
different dental suffixes to an earlier root *gwflren-?
Bibl.: WH I: 545, EM 253, IEW 459, Schrijver 1991: 186, Sihier 1995: 163f., LIV
*g(y\end-.
fr€num 'horse's bridle or harness' [n. o; nom.acc.pl. usually freni,frenos\ (Acc.+)
Derivatives: infrendre 'to curb, restrain' (Acc.+), effrenatus 'unrestrained' (Acc.+),
refrenare 'to hold back, restrain' (Varro+).
Pit. *freno~.
PIE *dhr-ehno- 'holding'.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: frequens 'occurring at close intervals' [adj. nt] (P1.+)
Derivatives: frequentare 'to populate, occupy, visit' (P1.+), infrequens 'not crowded,
absent' (P1.+); fraxdre 'to go the watchman's rounds' (Paul, ex F.), flaxare 'id.'
(gloss.).
Nussbaum 2007b connects frequens with fraxare. Semantically, this is quite
attractive. Formally, the forms could go back to a root *bhrkw-: frequens < *bhrekw-,
whereas the zero grade *bhrks- would yield *farks- (> *far$a-) by Schrijver's rule
(1991: 495f.). The preform *farks- could then have been leveled to *fraks- by analogy
with *frekw~. Note that a *bhrekw- would have an unusual PIE root structure *Dh-T.
Alternatively, frequens has been connected with farcio (WH, Eichner 1982: 19),
which is semantically less straightforward. See s.v. farcio for further considerations,
Bibl.: WH 1:456, EM 253, IEW 110f., LIV *bhrekV — farcio

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fretum 'sea strait' [n. o] (Naev.+: also fretus, -us i -T)
Derivatives: fretale 'frying-pan' (Apicius)?
Schrijver defends the etymology *bVeto- 'which seethes' to the root of fermentum,
ferveo, but I see little support for it. The meanings 'raging, swelling' (Lucr.) are
clearly derived from 'strait, channel'. Formally, the suffix *-eto- usually takes the
zero-grade of the root only in compounds (cf. Vine 1998a), or when derived from
existing nouns (vicpgxoc;). No good other etymology exists. Note that PIE *Dh-T is not
a canonical root structure, so that we cannot reconstruct a root *bhrt-.
Bibl.: WH 1:546£, EM 253, IEW 132f., Schrijver 1991: 255.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fretus 'relying on' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)
Pit. *freto~. It. cognates: U.frite [abl./loc.sg. or adv.] 'relying on' or 'with the aid of
from *fret(o)-.
PIE *dhr-ehto-. IE cognates: see s.y.firmus.
r
Since the PIE root did not end in a laryngeal, the only way to explain fretus as a ptc.
is to a (stative) verb 'to rely' in *-e-, like EM suggest. This would require *-eh to- in
r
the vein of Nussbaum 1999a: 409, who discusses the type acetum 'vinegar' to acere
'to be sour'. The basic verb might be *dher- 'to hold', unattested as a verb but
continued in Jirmus and fere, or *bher- 'to bear' as in ferre. Semantically, since *(/' er-
is an aoristic root, it seems more likely that this would have served to derive a stative
verb: 'to be holding'.
j
Bibl.: WH I: 505, EM 253f., IEW 253, Untermann 2000: 298, LIV *dher-. fere,
firmusjrenum

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: frigo, -ere 'to roast' [v. Ill]; frixi [not in CL^t.],frictum (PI.+)
It. cognates: maybe U. frehtef [acc.pl.]'?', frehtu '?' (object).
IE cognates: Gr. <ppuyco 'to roast'.
Since the Greek verb is very close in form and meaning, but cannot be matched by
means of sound laws, the verb might be a loanword from a third party, or from Greek
into Latin in an indirect way. Giacomelli 1994: 36 assumes a Greek loanword, of
which ph-» Lat.f- and u » Lat. / show that it does not belong to the oldest layer of
Greek loanwords. MMP bryz 'to roast' < PIr. *bra(i)j~ has secondary ^vowels, cf.
Cheung 2007. The absence of a pf. in CLat. (frixi only attested in grammarians) could
also point to a loanword (M. Weiss, p.c.).
Bibl.: WH I: 548, EM 254, IEW 137, Schrijver 1991: 256, Untermann 2000: 296.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: frigus, -oris 'cold, frost' [n. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: frigere 'to be cold, lack vigour* (Andr.+; pf. frixi), frigefactare 'to make
cold' (PI.), frigescere 'to become cool' (Cato+), frigidus 'cold' (P1.+), frigiddrium
'cold room' (IMC'II.+),frigedd 'cold' (Varro); perfrigefacere 'to make very cold' (PL),
perfngescere 'to become very cold' (Varro+), refrigerare 'to cool down (tr.)'
(Cato+), refrigescere 'to cool down (intr.)' (Cato+).
Pit. *srigos- [n.], *sriged-n-.
PIE *sriHg-o/es- 'frost, bitter cold'. IE cognates: Gr! piyoq 'cold, frost', piyiov
'colder', pf. eppiya 'to be cold', pr. pryeco (Pi.+), piyeSavoc; 'ghastly, terrible'.
In view of the zero grade in the root, which is unusual for s-stems, Schrijver considers
the possibility that Gr. puyoi; and Latin frigus are independent formations. In his view,
frigus might be derived from frigere, a stative verb with regular zero grade. However,
the productive process in Latin usually yields s-stems in -or, -oris from verbs in -ere;
hence frigus seems older than this process. It seems more likely that the root had zero
grade throughout-in PIE, or in the derivative from which Gr. and Lat. took their
reflexes. According to Nussbaum 2004a, Latin frigedd and Gr. piyeSavo^ jointly point
to an apparent n-stem *sriged-(6)n~, from earlier *srige-(e)d-.
Bibl.: WH I: 547, EM 254, IEW 1004, Schrijver 1991: 231, Stiiber 2002: 152, LIV
?*sreiH(g'-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: frio,-are 'to pulverize, crumble' [v. I] (Varro+)
Derivatives: infriare 'to crumble ingredients in or on' (Cato+); Jricare 'to rub, chafe'
(PL+; pf. fricui » fricavi, ppp. fric(a)tum), circumfricdre 'to rub round about'
(Cato), confricare 'to rub, massage' (P1.+), defricate [adv.] 'sharply, keenly' (Naev.),
petfricare 'to rub all over' (Cato+), refricare 'to rub again' (Cato+); frivolus [adj.]
'worthless, insignificant' (Phaedrus+), frivolaria 'a play about trifles' (PL apud
V&nonem),frivuscuhim 'slight quarrel' (Ulp.), refrivus 'shredded' (Fest., Plin.).
Pit. *fri-a-, *fri-k-, *fri-wo~.
PIE *bhriH-o-, *bhriH-uo- 'cut'. IE cognates: Olr. brieid*, •bria 'to hurt, damage' <
*brei-ase/o- [pr.sb.], Skt. pr. bhrindnti, YAv. pairi.brina- 'to cut (around)', broWra-
'blade'; OCS briti, Cz. bfiti (arch.) 'to shave'.
The verb friare can be denominal to qn adj. *frio- < *bhriH-o-, while re~frTvus and
frivolus continue an adj. *bhriH-uo-, The v. fricdre presupposes an adj. *fri-ko~,
which was probably built secondarily to *frio~.
Bibl.: WH I: 549, EM 255, IEW 166f., Schumacher 2004: 235, LIV *bhreiH-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: frons, -ondis 'foliage, leaves' [f. nd] (Enn.+; var.fruns,frund- a few times in Enn.)
Derivatives: frondere 'to have leaves' (Cato+), frondescere 'to become leafy'
(Erm.+),fronddsus 'leafy' (Enn. +); frondifer 'leaf-bearing' (Naev.+).
WH hesitatingly connect Germanic words in *bhrm- for 'brim, bud', whereas EM
regard the etymology as unsure. Solmsen (ZVS 35, 474ff.) compared Ru. dern 'lawn,
grass', Gr. flpova 'herbs, flowers', which might go back to a stem *dhr(o)n-. Yet in
order to connect Lat .frond- to this, a suffix *-d- or *-di- must be added, which makes
the comparison less likely. Of course, the Greek and Latin forms might go back to an
identical Mediterranean substrate word of floral character. Alternatively, one might
think of a stem *bhr-n- 'load, yield, fruit' to the root *bher-, the nom.sg. of which
would have been *bh(e)ron. To this, a suffix *-dh- could have been added (as in glans
'acorn'), yielding *bhr-on-dh-.
Bibl.: WH I: 550f., EM 255, IEW 142.
frOns, -ontis 'forehead, brow; front' [f. nt(i)] (Naev.+; P1.+ also m.; gen.pl.frontium)
No plausible etymology. WH and IEW connect several Celtic and Germanic words in
*bhrVnT- meaning 'side' or 'steep', but their meaning is not specific enough.
Semantically, it is tempting to compare frons with PIE *h;ibhruH- 'eyebrow', but a
putative formation *h bhrouH-nt~ > *frdwont- would have a suffix *-nt- that remains
3
unaccounted for, and might phonetically rather be expected to yield *frunt-. Iffrendo
'to grind one's teeth' is indeed an extension of a root *gwhren-, front- might reflect
*gwhron-t- 'the side where the mouth is, front', vel sim. But this is very speculative, of
course.
Bibl.: WH I: 551, EM 255, IEW 167.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fruor, frul 'to enjoy (the produce of)' [v. Ill; ppp.friictum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: friictus, -us 'enjoyment, revenue; fruit, produce' (Lex XII+), fructuarius
'fruit-bearing, usufructuary' (Varro+), frilctuosus 'fruitful, profitable' (Varro+); fi-ux,
-ugis [f.] 'crop, yield (sg.), fruit(s) (pi)' (Lex XII+; esp. p 1. fruges), frugT [ * dat. sg. of
fritx] 'having merit, good, valuable'; frugaliter 'economically' (P1.+), friigalior
'having more merit, more honest' (P1.+); frumentum 'fruit of plants, com, grain'
(P1.+), frumentarius 'of com' (Cato+), frumentarius [m.] 'a dealer in corn' (PI.+);
frumscere, frumtus sum 'to enjoy' (P1.+); frugifer 'fruitful, productive' (Enn.),
frugiferens 'fruitful' (Lucr.Xfrugiparus 'giving birth to fruits' (Lucr.).
Pit. pr. *friig-je/o-, root noun *frug~. It. cognates: U. frif, fri [acc.pi.] 'fruits' <
*bhriig~; O. fruktatiuf [nom.sg.] 'usufruct' < *frugtdtion- built to *frug-i-td- <
*bhrug-ito-, ppp. of *bhrug-io- 'to use'.
i
PIE pr. *bhruHg-ie/o- 'to use'. IE cognates: Go. brukjan, pret. bruhta, OE brucan,
OHG bruhhan, OS brukan 'to use', Go. bruks, OHG bruhhi, OE bryce 'useful'.
Fruor reflects a development *frug-ie- > *fruje- > *fruwe- > *frue-. The stem *frug~
appears in the root noun frux (from Pit. date, as U, fri(f) shows), and in frumentum <
*frug-men- and friiniscere < *frug-n~. The latter can reflect an earlier nasal present
stem *frug-n-e/o-, which may be compared with Go. us-bruknan 'to break off, even
though -wow-inchoatives are productive in Gothic. The restriction to Gm. and It., and
the pervading zero grade, may cast doubts on a PIE origin; yet there is no decisive
argument against it.
Bibl.: WH I: 551ff., EM 256f., IEW 173, Schrijver 1991: 232f., Untermann 2000:
297-299, LIV ?*bhreuH(g)-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: frustum 'crumb, fragment' [n. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: frustulentus 'full of crumbs' (PL), frustatim 'in little pieces'
(Pompon.+), frustillatim 'id.' (P1.+).
Pit. *frusto-.
PIE *bhrus-to- 'broken'. IE cognates: Olr. bru'id 'to break, smash', Old French
bruiser 'to break' < PCI. *brus-ieOlr. bronnaid 'to injure' < *brus-na-ti, MW breu,
W. brau 'brittle, fragile', MCo. brew, Bret, brev 'broken' < *bruso~, OE briesan,
bryscm 'to break, bruise' < *brqusija~; maybe Alb. bresher 'hail' < *bhreus-n~.
Latin frustum could represent an isolated Latin reflex" of a root *bhrus- 'to break'
found in Celtic and Germanic (and maybe Albanian). It has also been connected with
Lith. druskd 'salt', Latv. druska 'crumb, scrap, bit', Go. drauhsos 'crumbs, lumps',
W. dry 11 'piece, lump' < *d rus-. This is phonetically equally possible, but the
presence of verb forms from *bhrus- in Celtic and Germanic renders a derivation of
frustum from *bhrus- more likely (because of *-to-).
Bibl.: WH I: 553, EM 257, IEW 171, Schrijver 1995: 341, LIV *bhreus-.
frutex, -icis 'shrub, bush; shoot, 'blockhead" [f. k] (PL+)
Pit. *frut~. I
Since the root of OE breowan 'to brew' is now reconstructed as
*bhreuH- ' to sprout,
frutex can only be connected through a shortening from *fruto~, but Schrijver 1991
acknowledges no shortening of long pretonic vowels in front of stops. Also, the
semantic link between 'to boil' and frutex is not obvious. Alternatively, frutex may be
connected with Olr. broth 'awn, hair' (possibly from *bhruto-), but PIE origin is
uncertain; it might be a borrowed word for a plant.
Bibl.: WH I: 554, EM 257, IEW 169, Schrijver 1991: 254.
fucus 'drone, gadfly, hornet' {m. o] (P1.+)
PIE *bhoi-ko-? *bhou-ko-? IE cognates: Olr. bech 'bee' [m.] < *bheko-; OPr. bitte,
Lith. bite, Latv. bite < *bhi-t-, OCS bbcela, btcela 'bee' < *bhi-kel-eh ; OIc. by [n.],
3
MoDu. bij, OHG bini [n.], OHG bia [f.], OE beo 'bee' [f.] < *bVon-, *bhi-ni-. OE
beaw 'gadfly', MoFr. ban 'hornet'...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fugio, -ere 'to run away, flee' [v. III;JugT,fugitum] (Lex XII, Andr.+)
Derivatives: fuga 'the running away, fleeing, escape' (V\.+),fugax 'fugitive, elusive'
(PI.+\fugare 'to cause to flee' (P\.+),fugeI(l)a 'flight' (Cato+),fugitdre 'to run away
from, avoid' (PI.+),fugitor 'who runs away' (PI.),fugitivus 'runaway, fugitive' (P1.+),
fugitivarius 'who recaptures runaway slaves' (Varro+); aufugere 'to run away' (P1.+),
confugere 'to flee for protection' (P1.+), defugere 'to make one's escape' (P1.+),
diffugere 'to scatter' (Lucr.+), effugere 'to flee, escape' (Naev.+), effugium 'means of
escape' (Lucr.+), petfugere 'to take refuge' (Sis.+),perfugium 'refuge, shelter' (P1.+),
profugere 'to run away' (P1.+), refugere 'to turn back and flee, recoil' (Enn.+),
suffugere 'to escape from under' (Lucr.+), trdnsfugere 'to go over to the other side,
desert' (P1.+); lucrifuga 'a spendthrift' (PI.).
Pit. pr. *fug-i~, pf. *foug~, subst. *Jug-(a~).
PIE pr. *bSag-i- 'to flee', pf. *(bhe-)bhoug- (or root aorist *bheug-). IE cognates: Gr.
(peuyco, aor. (puyeiv, pf. 7tecpeuya 'to flee', cpuya-Se 'on the flight', (pu^a [f.] 'flight,
panic', cpuyr^ 'flight, exile'; Av. buj(i)- [f.] 'penance, atonement', bujat 'frees',
•bunjainti 'they free', bujaiiamna-, MP boz- 'to free, release'.
Bibl.: WH I: 556f., EM 258, IEW 152, Schrijver 2003: 66, LIV l.*bheug-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ful 'to be(come)' [v. pf.] ind. fui, fuisti, fuit, fuimus, fuistis, fuerunt; sb. fua, fuat;
ptc.fut.Jiiturus, inf.fut.fore (Lex XII, Andr.+)
Derivatives: future 'saepius fuisse' (Cato apud. Paul, ex F.); for the preverbial cp.,
see s.v. sum.
Pit. aor. *fu-, ipv. *fu-tdd, pf. *fuf- (whence plq.pf. *Jufa- > ipf.), fut. *(fe-)fu-s~. It.
cognates: U. futu, futu [2s.ipv.II], U.fututo [2p.ipv.II], Presam. fufpoS [3p.pf_], O.
fufans [3p.ipf.], fusid [3s.ipf.sb.], fufens [3p.pf.],ywiJ [3s.pf.sb.], O.fust, adfust, U.
fust, fust, fus, aterafust, andersqfust, andirsafusl, amprefu<u>s [3s.fut.pf.] , furent,
fefure, ambrefurent [3p.fut.pf.] 'to be'.
PIE aor. *bh(e)h u- 'to become', pf. *bhe-bhh (o)u-, -ppp. *bhh u-to- (> futurus). IE
2 2 2
cognates: Olr. 3s. boi 'was', 3p, batar, MW bu, MCo. bue OBret. a-bu 'was' < pf.
t
*bu-b(u)-\ Skt. abhut 'has become' [3s.aor.]; Gr. etpuv 'grew, became'; OLith. bit(i)
'he was', Lith. bM 'to be', OCS 23s. bystb 'was, became', OCS byti 'to be'.
Original *fu-7, with shortening of long ii in front of the next vowel. The form fu~ was
generalized quite early, yielding fu-turus and fore < *fu-se. The forms Juas, fuat
represent an old aor.sb. in -a-.
Bibl.: WH I: 557f„ EM 257f, IEW 146ff., Leumann 1977: 524, Kortlandt 1986 (=
2007: 75-79), Schrijver 1991: 240, Sihler 1995: 552, Untermann 2000: 245-252,
Lazzarini-Poccetti 2001, Meiser 2003:201, LIV "bWv. —• fto; probus, superbus; sum
fulcio, -Tre 'to support' [v. IV; pf.fidsi, ppp.fultum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: fulcrum 'head- or back-support of a couch' (Varro+), fulmenta 'prop,
support' (P1-+), fulmentum 'support' (Varro+); confulcire 'to press together' (Lucr.),
praefuldre 'to place as a support, prop up' (P1.+), suffulcire 'to prop from below' (P1.+).
Pit. *dh/bh-(o)l-k-.
Lat. fulcrum < *fulk-(t)lo-, fulmentum < *fulk-men-to~, pf. fulsi < *fulk-s-ai. The
etymology is unclear. It is often assumed that Latin fulk- is cognate with Gr.
<paXKTi<; [m.] 'rib (of a ship)', tpaXayi...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fulica 'a water-bird (the coot?)' [f. a] (Afran.+)
Derivatives:^//*, -icis 'heron' (Cic.).
The suffix -ik~ is found in other bird-nam^s too. Fulica is often compared with OHG
belihha, -o, NHG Belche 'coot' < PGm. *bel-ik- < *bhel-ig- 'having a white spot'; but
Latin Jill- can only reflect *-ol- (unless it is a form from a different dialect, which is
an ad hoc assumption), and the suffixes do not match completely. The basic word is
assumed to be *bhe/olH- 'white' (Lith. balas, OCS belt 'white', Gr. (paloc, 'white'
Hsch.), but there is no guarantee that the Latin words refer to a bird with white
characteristics. I conclude that the origin offulica and fulix remains unknown.
Bibl.: WH I: 559f., EM 259, IEW 118ff.

Pit. *Juli- 'smoke'.
PIE *dhuh -li- 'smoke, dust'. IE cognates: Mir. duil 'desire'; Skt. dhuli-, dhiili [f.]
2
'dust', dhulika 'fog', Lith. dulis, Latv. dulis 'smoke used in bee-culture'.
Latin derivation to an original z-stem *d\iH-Ii- 'dust', which may be derived from an
adj. *dhuh2-Io- 'smoking, dusty'.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fulvus 'brown, sandy, gold-coloured' [adj. o/a] (Enn.+)
Pit. *fe/olawo-r}.
PIE *dhe/olH~uo- 'yellow'?
The theoretical preforms for fulvus were listed by Driessen 2005: V/d^g^/g^ +
*~e/o- + *-lHVu-/*-lHu- + -os. Unless, of course, *-wo- (which is also found in other
colour adjectives) was a recent addition. The often cited connection with Lith. geltas
'yellow' < PIE *ghelh - is not possible, see s.v. helvus. Driessen connects fulvus with
3
EMoDu. deluw, MoDu del 'yellow(ish)', which could, among other forms, go back to
*t/dhelH -uo-. Latin fulvus could, in theory, reflect the same preform *dhelH-uo-. Yet
in view of the isolated position of the Dutch word within Gm., and the presence of
rhyming colour words in -uw in Dutch, it seems a hazardous assumption. More
promising is the connection with Armenian dalowkn 'jaundice', delin 'yellow', delb
'yellow, blond', which could point to a root *dhlH-, *dhelH- for 'yellow'.
Bibl.: WH I: 561, EM 260, IEW 160, Schrijver 1991: 469, Nussbaum 1999a: 386f.,
Driessen 2005.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fumus 'smoke, fumes'[m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: fumare 'to emit smoke or steam' (P1.+), fumosus 'smoky' (Cato+),
fumidus 'smoking, smoky' (Lucr.+); fumigare 'to treat with smoke' (Var.+);
fumificare 'to make smoke; (PI.+),fumificus 'making smoke' (PL+).
Pit. *fumo~.
PIE *d\ih 2-mo- 'smoke, fume'. IE cognates: Skt. dhiimd- 'smoke, fume', Gr. i5ufi6q
'spirit', OPr. dumis 'smoke', Lith. pi. dumai 'smoke', OCS dymt 'smoke'; OHG
toum 'steam'.
In a preform *dhuHmo- we would expect Dybo's shortening (of pretonic shortening
of long vowels in front of resonants in Proto-Italo-Celto-Germanic) to have taken
place. The long vowel of fumus is explained by Schrijver 1991 from (possible)
analogy with the (*)« in fuligo and suffid. The reconstruction of root-final *-h is
2
based on Hit. antirwahhas 'human' and tuhhae- 'to cough'.
Bibl.: WH I: 56If., EM 260, IEW 261, Schrijver 1991: 233, 342, 357, LIV *dhueh -
2
'to smoke' (intr.). —* Jimum, foeteo, Juligo, fur5, suffid

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fundo, -ere 'to pour, let go, emit' [v. HI; pf.fudi, ppp.fusum\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: fusus, -us 'a pouring' (Varro), fusus 'spindle' (Lucr.+), funditare 'to
pour out continuously; shoot at with slings' (P1.+); affundere 'to pour onto' (Varro+),
circumfundere 'to pour round, distribute' (Cato+), confundere 'to pour together, mix,
confuse' (P1.+), confusicius (PL) 'confused', confusim 'confusedly' (Varro), defundere
'to pour out' (Cato+), diffundere 'to spread widely, diffuse' (Cato+), diffunditdre 'to
dissipate' (PL), diffusilis 'diffusive' (Lucr.), effiindere 'to pour out, shed, utter' (P1.+),
infundere 'to pour.in' (PL+), injundibulum 'funnel, hopper' (Cato+), offundere 'to pour,
extend' (P1.+), profundere 'to pour forth' (Naev.+); Jut is, -is 'water-vessel' (Varro),
exfuti'effusT (Paul, ex K).
Pit. *xund-(e/o~). It. cognates: maybe Fal. huti[c]ilom [acc.n.?] '?'; uncertain U.
hondu [3s.ipv.II] 'throw down!'(?).
PIE pr. *ghu-n-d- 'to pour', pf. *(ghe-)*gh(o)u-; noun *ghe/ou-ti-? IE cognates: Hit.
kutt-, kutt- [c.] 'wall' < *g%t-, *g\it-; Skt.juhoti 'pours; sacrifices' < *ghi-g(e)u-,
a-huti- [f.] 'offering', Av. a-zuiti- [f] 'clarified butter, sacrificial fat' < *-ghuti-, Gr.
Xeto < pr. *gheu-e~, aor.intr. e^pTO 'to pour', xpjxo^ 'spilled', nw{yx> [adv.]
'completely'; Go. giutan, ToB. ku- 'to pour'.
See Kiimmel 2004b: 357 on confusicius. Initial f- < PIE *gh- is only attested m fundo,
and might be conditioned by the vowel u (labial assimilation). Germanic also shows
the root extension *-d~. In Latin, the cf-present was remade into a nasal present. The
forms futis and *ex-futus could continue the d-less form of the root, with futis <
Note, however, that the semantics are not perfect (futis is not an abstract)
and that the noun appears only in Varro.
t
Bibl.: WH I: 563f., EM 260f., IEW 447f„ Giacomelli 1963: 248, Meiser 1986: 168,
Untermann 2000: 331, Vine 2004: 373, LIV *gheu-, ?*gheud-.
