content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fundus 'bottom' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: funditus [adv.] 'completely; from the bottom' (PI.+),Jundare 'to lay the
foundations' (P1.+), fundamentum 'foundation, basis' (P1.+); profundus 'very deep,
boundless' (P1.+).
Pit. *fundo-.
PIE *bhudh-n-o- 'bottom', probably < *b\idh-mn-6s, gen.sg. of *bhudh-men-. IE
cognates: Mir. bond 'foot sole', Skt. budhna- 'bottom, foot, root', Av. buna-, Gr.
7n)i3priv 'bottom, depth, root', OIc. botn, OHG bodam, OS bothme [dat.sg.], OE
botem, OE bodan, OFr. bodem, MDu. bodem 'bottom' < PGm. *budme/6n [nom.],
*buttaz [gen.] < ^W-me/on, *bhudh-(m)n-os (thus Kroonen 2006: 21-23).
The Latin form is due to metathesis of PIE *bhudhno- to Italo-Celtic *bhunct o-.
Bibl.: WH I: 564, EM 261, IEW 174, Schrijver 1991: 501.
fungor, -I 'to perform; go through, enjoy; die; function' [v. Ill\ functus sum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: defungi 'to be quit, settle; come to an end; [pf.] die' (Ter.+), perfungT
'to carry through one's part, be done' (Ter.+).
Pit. *fung-e/o~.
PIE pr.
-n-g- 'to be of use, be used'. IE cognates: Olr. bongaid*, •boing 'to
break, harvest', pf. bobaig*, MW difyngaf 'to rush forward' (< *di-bunge-) < PCI.
*bunge/o-; Skt. pr. bhunjate, bhunjate [3p.], -bhuhjant- [ptc.act.], aor. ma bhojam
[ls.inj.act.] act.: 'to benefit, make benefit; atone'; med.: 'to benefit, enjoy, consume',
OAv. buj- [f.] 'atonement, expiation'; Arm. bowcanem 'to feed', aor. bowci.
Bibl.: WH I: 565, EM 262, IEW 153, Schumacher 2004: 238f., LIV 2.*bheug-.

Derivatives: funginus 'like a mushroom' (PI.)-
IE cognates: Gr. oTtoyyoq 'fungus', Arm. sownkfg 'mushroom'.
Probably a loanword from a non-IE language, borrowed independently into Greek,
Latin and Armenian in a form *sphong- vel sim.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: funis 'rope, cable' [m. (f.) i] (Cato+; gen.pl. funium, abl.sg. fune, acc.sg. funem,
abl.sg.ywMi lx Cato)
Derivatives: funiculus 'thin rope, cord' (Cato+); funambulus 'tightrope walker'
(Ter.+); semifunium 'half-length of rope' (Cato).
Pit. *xwoini~.
PIE *gwhoiH-ni- or *gwhoHi-ni- 'rope'. IE cognates: see s.v.filum.
Since the meaning is very close to filum, funis might represent an o-grade derivative
of the root *gwhiH- or *gwbHi- which can be reconstructed for filum.
Bibl.: WH I: 567, EM 262, IEW 272, Schrijver 1991: 242. filum

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: funus, -eris 'funeral rites' [n. r] (Lex XII+; one gloss has ex libris antiquis ... pro
funus fo<i>rms, but whether the addition of <i> is correct is uncertain.)
Derivatives: junestus 'concerned with death or mourning, lamentable' (Acc.+),
funebris 'funerary' (Cic.+).
If the form f onus attested in the gloss is to be restored to *foinos, the noun might be
derived from the PIE root *bheih - 'to be afraid', viz. as *bhoih -nos- [n.] 'fear' >
2 2
'mourning'. But this is not very compelling, of course. If the spelling fonus represents
a real form fonus, it could represent a non-urban monophthongization of *founus >
Lat. funus. One could then reconstruct PIE *cte/ou-nes- 'the passing away' from the
root *dheu- 'to go, pass away'. Watkins 1990 compares Hit. tuhhus-1 'to end',
tuhhu(i)sta 'has ended' < PIE *Teuh or *Tueh (Kloekhorst 2008: 890). Latin funus
r r
would then continue PIE *dheuh -nes- 'closing ceremony'. Other derivatives from this
2
root are uncertain: Watkins connects *duno- 'hill-fort, enclosure', but this may also
contain PIE *d~.
Bibl.: WH I: 568, EM 262, IEW 260f.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fur, -is 'thief [m. r] (P1.+) > *
Derivatives: Jurax 'given to stealing' (P1.+), furinus 'of thieves' (PL), fiirarT 'to
steal' (Cato+), furtificus 'thievish' (PL), furiim 'secretly' (P1.+), furtum 'theft,
deception' (Lex XII+), furiivus 'stolen, stealthy' (P1.+); suffurari 'to steal
unobtrusively' (PL).
Pit. *jor~.
PIE *bhor 'thief [m.]. IE cognates: Gr. (poop 'thief.
It has been assumed that fur was borrowed from Greek. Yet it is questionable whether
Greek cp- would be rendered with Latin f- so early: in view of the many derivatives,
the word seems to have been in the language well before Plautus. Also, PIE *-or turns
into -ur in Latin around 200 BC (cf. cur), so that the vowel of fur is no compelling
argument for a sGreek origin. Finally, the noun furtum can hardly be explained as a
derivative from fur. According to Forssman 1992: 309, furtum originally had a short
vowel and reflects *bhftom, with the zero-grade of the root offur. Lat. furtivus would
have been formed on the basis of *bhrtos > *furtos.
Bibl.: WH I: 569, EM 262f., IEW 128-39, Schrijver 1991: 120, Forssman 1992: 309,
LIV *bher-. ferre

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: furca 'fork or similarly shaped instrument' [f. a\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: furcifer 'one who is punished with the 'fork', scoundrel' (P1.+); furcilla
'wooden pitchfork' (Varro+), furcillare 'to impugn' (PI.\furcillatus 'forked' (Varro).
All etymologies adduced in WH presuppose PIE *gh-, which yields Latin h- in front
of vowels except in fundo. In furca, just as in fundo, we find -u- after the velar stop.
Yet Lith. zergti 'to spread the legs', zirkles 'scissors' presuppose a root *gh(e/o)rg-,
whereas for furca would require old *-u~, since it is inconceivable that the recent
change of *-orc- > -urc- (ca. 200 BC) would have been able to influence the outcome
of the initial stop. One might assume a development *ghorka > *fo/urca in a different
dialect, from which Latin then borrowed the'word; but this is an emergency scenario.
Bibl,: WH I: 569f., EM 263.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: furo, -ere 'to be mad, rave' [v. Ill] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: furia 'fury, rage; Fury' (Enn.+), furiosus 'mad' (Lex XII, Cic.+), furor
'violent madness' (Lucr.+); perfurere 'to rage' (Lucr.+).
Many etymologies have been proposed, but none is clearly the best. In view of the
rule */, *u> e, o in front of *-rV~, a preform in *-us- or *-ur- (or PIE *-uH- >
with subsequent shortening as per Dybo) must be excluded (Parker 1988: 230).
Bibl.: WH I: 570f., EM 263, IEW 268-271, Puhvel 1998, LIV *bherh -.
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: furvus 'dark-coloured, dusky' [adj. o/a] (Varro+)
Pit. *fuswo~.
PIE *dhus-uo- 'dark, black, brown'. IE cognates: Olr. donn 'dun, light brown', MW
dwnn 'dun. dusky', OE dox 'dark-haired, dusky', MoE dusk, OE dosen 'dun,
dingy-brown, dark coloured'.
For furvus, Leumann 1977 assumes *fusouos. More satisfactory is Rix 1981: 199 (=
2001: 287) who posits *ctus-uo- > *Juruo-, with the suffix *-uo- which is also found
in many other colours (flavus,fulvus, helvus, etc.). The root *ctus- is found in several
colour adjectives referring to a brown, dark colour, all with suffixs that are abundant
in colour adjectives. It might be connected with PIE *dhues- 'to fume, breathe; dust',
hence 'dusty, mirky colour',
Bibl.: Sommer 1914: 70, WH I: 572, EM 263, IEW 268-271, Leumann 1977: 50,
206. —> furd, fuscus

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fuscus 'dark-coloured, dusky; (of persons) dark-skinned' [adj. o/a\ (Varro+)
Derivatives: infuscdre 'to darken; contaminate' (P1.+).
Pit. *fusko-.
PIE *d\is-ko- 'dark-coloured'.
The suffix *-ko- was used, among others, to indicate physical properties of people:
cascus, mancus. Hence, EM suppose that fuscus, as opposed to furvus, was originally
used for this purpose.
Bibl.: WH I: 572, EM 263, IEW 268-271. furvus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fustis 'stick, rod' [m. z] (Lex XII+; abl.sg. fustiLex XII, PI.)
Derivatives: jiistitudinus [adj.] 'stick-beating' (Pi.).
Pit. *fusti- / *feusti- f *fousti-.
The most obvious connection would be with Latin -futare. Since the usual outcome of
PIE *-TT- in Latin is -ss- (cf. Hill 2003: 22Iff\ fustis would be an exception. Latin
fustis might reflect *Jut-ti- > *fussi- with subsequent reintroduction of the suffix *-ti~.
Hill 2003 does not discuss the possible connection with futare, and starts from a root
ending in PIE *-d. In view of Dutch and German *busk~, he (p. 236) reconstructs the
root with a full grade *bheHud-, but I am not convinced that long u in the WGm.
words is old: there is a productive ablaut pattern u : u : au : iu in West-Germanic, so
that *biiska- might be explained as a secondary formation *bud-sk-. Hill needs a
dissyllabic base *boud- to explain the rise of -st- from *-dt- in Latin, but if the root
was merely *bhud~, this does not work. Still, I think that the connection with the Gm.
words is possible; see s.v. -futo.
Bibl.: WH I: 573, EM 264, IEW 112, Hill 2003: 229ff., LIV ?*bheud-. -futo
O. futir [nom.sg.], fuutrei, futra [dat.sg.], futreps [gen.sg.?] 'daughter'.
Pit. *fu%ter, *fuxtros.
PIE *dhugh -ter, -tr-os 'daughter', IE cognates: Gaul, duxtir, Celtib. tuater; Hit.
2
ummduttarriiata/i- [c.] a female functionary, HLuw." tuwatra/i-, Lyc. kbatra-
'daughter' < PAnat. *duegtr-, *dugtr- (Kloekhorst 2008: 902ff), Skt. duhitdr-, OAv.
dugadar-, YAv. duySar-, Gr. llhryarrip, -Tpog, Arm. dowstr, OPr. duckti, Lith. dukte,
OCS dbsti, gen.sg. chstere, Ru. doc', gen.sg. doceri:; Go. dauhtar, OIc. dottir, OHG
tohter, ToB tkacer 'daughter'.
The Oscan forms point to ifutr-t < *fuxtr~. Apparently, the PIE laryngeal remained
unvocalized in Sabellic; Schrijver 1991: 105 suggests that this was caused by the
following cluster TC. The absence of h in all Oscan attestations is striking, but not so
problematic as to raise doubts about the e...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gallus 'farmyard cock' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: gallTna 'hen' (P1.+), gallinaceus 'of poulty' (P1.+), galtinarius 'one who
looks after poultry' (Varro+).
IE cognates: W. galw 'to call' < PCI. *galuo-; OCS glast, Ru. golos 'voice' <
*ga/olso-, OCS glagoh 'word' < *ga/ol-ga/ol-, glagolati 'to speak'; OIc. kalla.
Theoretically, the bird could have been denominated 'the Gaulish one' (Gallus), but
there are no indications that chicken were regarded as having come from Gaul. WH
prefer the connection with Gr. KaXatc, -i5o<; 'precious stone; cock', but this does not
have the same velar, and may be connected within Greek with KOHCO. IEWS and
Schrijver's connection with a root *glH- 'to call' seems much better; gallus would
have been the 'caller'. Formally, though, there are difficulties: can gallus represent
*glH-o- > *galos, whence *gal-n-o- > gallus? Since the root represents a sound, and
is attested only in Slavic, Gm. and Italo-Celtic, it might reflect an onomatopoeia
*gal-.
Bibl.: WH I: 580f., EM 266, IEW 350f., Schrijver 1991: 208.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ganeum 'tavern, eating-house' [n. o\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: gdnea 'tavern' (Cic.+), gamo 'pub-crawler' (Naev.+).
Probably a loanword. In form and meaning, one might connect West-Semitic *gann
'garden' (Ugaritic, Aramaic gn, Hebrew gan 'garden'), whence also Gr. yavoq
'pleasure-garden' (on Cyprus), yavsa 'gardens' (Hsch.); for the semantics, compare
French restaurant and German Bier-gar ten.
Bibl.: WH I: 582, EM 267, Masson 1967: 74, Leumann 1977: 361.
gannid, -Ire 'to whimper, snarl' [v. IV] (P1.+)
Derivatives: oggannire 'to speak menacingly, growl' (P1.+), ganmtus, -us
'whimpering, snarling' (Lucr+).
IE cognates: RuCS gugnati 'to whisper, grumble', Ru. gugniti 'to tell, speak', OCS
gggbnivb 'murmuring', Ru. gugnhyj, Bulg. gagniv 'speaking through the nose'.
Most of the other IE forms mentioned by IEW are of the structure gVn-gn- and mean
'to mock',, except for the Slavic forms. In Latin, the onomatopoeic aspect seems
primary ('to growl' rather than 'to mock'). Thus, the Slavic forms are the most likely
comparanda. But Pit. *gangn- (in which *a would be difficult to explain) would not
normally develop to gann-. WH therefore invoke onomatopoeic change in this word,
which is possible.
Bibl.: WH I: 582, EM 267, IEW 352.
garriO, -Ire 'to chatter, jabber' [v. IV] (P1.+)
Derivatives: garrulus 'talkative' (P1.+).
Pit. *garje/o~?
PIE *g(e)h r-ie/o- 'to shout'? IE cognates: Olr. gairid*, •gair.; MW gardu 'to shout'
2
< PCI. *gar-ie/o- < PIE *geh r~, Olr. gdir, W. gawr 'shout' < PCI. *gari- 'shout, call',
2
Olr. gairm 'shouting, calling', W. Bret, garm < PCI. *garsman- 'cry, shout'; Khot.
ysdr- 'to sing', Sogd. z>y, z'r'k, z'r'y 'compassionate; pitiful', Khwar. zry- 'to
announce, reveal, speak about (sorrow)', Oss. Iron zcdyn/zcdd 'to sound', (caus.-iter.)
Iron zaryn/zard, Digoron zarun/zard 'to sing' < PIr. *zaHr- (*zarH-l) 'to bewail the
deceased'; Gr. yfjpix; f. 'voice, speech', Dor. yapuq, -uoq < *geh ru-\ OHG chara
2
'mourning, complaint', Go. ko...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: weep', y6t]<;, -TjToc; [m.] 'sorcerer' (Ion.-Att.); OCS govorb [m.] 'noise, shout, rumour,
murmur' < *gou(H)~; maybe OHG gikewen 'to call', OE ciegan 'shout' (< PGm.
*kaujan).
The length of the a is uncertain. Lat. gains 'jay' is close in form and meaning, which
would point to a (onomatopoeic?) root *ga~. If one assumes a short vowel in gavia, it
can be derived from *gou-i-eh -, and connected with a PIE root *guh -. This remains
2 2
uncertain.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gelus, -us 'cold, frost, ice' [m. u] (Andr.+; gelum [n.] Cato+, marginally gelu [n.])
Derivatives: gelidus 'cold, icy' (Cato+), gelare 'to freeze' (Sen.+); gelicidium 'a
frost' (Cato+); congeidre 'to cause to freeze' (Varro+).
Pit. * gelus, *gel-ou- [m.].
PIE *gel-u- [m.] 'cold, frost, ice'. IE cognates: Go. kalds 'cold', OE calan 'to
freeze', OIc. kala 'to freeze' < PIE *gol-to-; Gr. yeXavSpov 'cold' (Hsch.) is
uncertain. OCS xladb 'coolness, cool breeze', Ru. xolod 'cold' < PS1. *xdldb '(the)
cold' « PIE *goldho-?
For the interpretation of the stem form throughout the Latin period, cf. Nussbaum
1999a: 380. By sound change, *gelu(~) should become *golu(-), but we may assume
that e was restored on the basis of gelidus < *gel-i~, cf. Nussbaum 1999a: 387.
Bibl.: WH I: 585f., EM 268, IEW 366, Leumann 1977: 151, Schrijver 1991: 433,
Sihier 1995: 41. —• glacies

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: geminus 'bom at the same time, twin-born' [adj. o] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: geminare 'to repeat, double' (Ter.+), congeminare 'to double' (P1.+),
congeminatio 'doubling* (PL), geminitudo 'likeness' (Pac.); trigeminus 'threefold'
(P1.+), quadrigeminus 'fourfold' (Naev.+).
Pit. *jemno- 'paired*.
PIE *iemH-no- 'paired, connected'. IE cognates: Olr. emon 'twin' < *emno- <
*iemno-\ Skt. yama- 'twin', yamt- [f.] 'twin sister', OAv. ysma- [m.] 'twin* < Ilr.
*iamHa- < *imHo-; Latv.jumis 'pair*.
Traditionally, geminus is connected with Ilr. *iama- 'twin' because of the meaning;
this is supported by the Olr. cognate, which still shows ie-. If correct, Latin has
analogically introduced g- from elsewhere, probably from gigno 'to give birth to',
genus 'offspring'.
Bibl.: WH I: 586f., EM 269, IEW 505, Steinbauer 1989: 256, Schrijver 1991: 94.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gemma 'bud or eye (in trees); jewel' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: gemmdre 'to come into bud' (Varro+), gemmans 'decorated with gems'
(Lucr.+).
Pit. *gebma- 'bud, sprout'.
PIE *geb-m- 'sprout, bud'. IE cognates: Lith. zembeti 'to germinate, sprout', 3s.
zembi, OCSprozqbngti 'to germinate'.
The meaning 'bud, sprout' is primary. There are two competing traditional
etymologies. WH and IEW prefer a derivation *gembh-na to the root *gembh- 'to
bite', whereas others have suggested a connection with *gem- 'to press' (Lat. gemo).
The second etymology is semantically unconvincing, and leaves the geminate -mm-
unexplained. The first etymology is based on (or at least thought to be strengthened
by) the similar forms of Lith. zembti (colloq.) 'to cut slantwise, sharpen', 3s. zembia,
OCS z§bomi 'to pull out' on the one hand, and Lith. zembeti 'to germinate, sprout',
3s. zembi, OCS prozgbngti 'to germinate' on the other. Yet the accentuation of these
verbs is different, a problem which was seen by IEW, but solved in an unsatisfactory
way, viz. by assuming a lengthened grade present for Lith. zembeti. Since the
meanings 'to cut' and 'to germinate' are difficult to derive from one another, and
since the accentual difference may go back to different root-final consonants, we can
reconstruct *gembh- for the circumflex root 'to cut', and *gemb- for the acute root of
'to germinate' (thus Schrijver, without references). Latin gemma can then reflect a
noun *geb-ma of the deverbal type (as in Jama, Jlammd). Whether Germanic words
can be connected (OIc. keppr 'stick', OE cipp 'beam, ploughshear' < *PGm. *kippa~)
is uncertain.
Bibl.: WH I: 587f., EM 269, IEW 369, Schrijver 1991: 434.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gemo, -ere 'to groan, moan' [v. Ill] (P1.+)
Derivatives: gemitus, -us 'groaning, moaning' (P1.+); ingemere 'to moan' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *gene/o-7
PIE *Gen(H)- 'to call'? IE cognates: Gr. ygyova [pf.] 'to shout so as to make onself
heard', ToA ken- 'to call'.
Lat. gemo has been connected with Gr. yejioi 'to be full' by most scholars, but the
assumed semantic change from 'to grasp; to press' to 'groan, moan' is hard to
imagine. This point is stressed by Vine 2007, who instead proposes to connect Gr.
yeytova and ToA ken- 'to call' < (*ge-)gon-. He posits an original root *Gen(H)- /
*Gon(H)- (with unspecified initial velar stop), which would have acquired -m- for
*-n- in Latin on the model of semantically similar verbs such as Jremere 'to roar',
tremere 'to tremble'.
Bibl.: WH I: 588f., EM 269, IEW 368f., Meiser 1998: 83, Vine 2007.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gena 'cheek, side of the face' [f. a\ (Lex XII+; usually pi. genae)
Derivatives: genuTnus (dens) [m./adj.] 'back tooth, molar' (Cic.+).
Pit. *genu-.
PIE *gen-u- [f.] 'jaw'. IE cognates: Olr. gin [m. u] 'mouth', W. gen 'cheek', pi.
geneu < PCI. *genu~; Skt. hanu- [f.], YAv. +zanauua [du.], Khot. ysanuva 'jaw' < Ilr.
*fh)ami- (with unclear aspirate in Skt.); Gr. yevug, -uo<; [f.] 'jaw', yeveiov (<
*yevgp-iov) 'chin, beard'; Arm. cnawt 'jaw, chin'; Lith. zandas [m.] 'cheek, jaw',
Latv. zuods < *gonH-cto- or *gon-d-o-;Go. kinnus [f.] 'cheek', OIc. kinn [f.] 'slope'
< PGm. *kinnu- (< *genu-/*genuo-)\ ToA sanwem [du.f.] 'jaws' < PIE *genu~ (root
vocalism after ToA kanwem 'knees'?).
Originally the same word *gerw- as 'knee', meaning 'curve'? The adj. genii-inns
'molar' would indeed suggest this. Original * genus has apparently changed to gena
because it was f., and maybe under the influence of mala.
Bibl.: WH I: 589f., EM 269, IEW 381f., Leumann 1977: 327. genu

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gener, -en 'son-in-law' [m. o] (P1.+)
Pit, *g(e)mro-.
PIE *g(e)m-ro- 'son-in-law'. IE cognates: Skt. jamatar- [m.] 'son-in-law', YAv.
zamatar- [m.] 'id.', zamaoiia- 'id.' < Ilr. *jama(Htar)~; YAv. zvmana- [f.] 'reward,
payment, wages', Khot.ysamtha 'payment for use', Sogd. 'wz'm- 'to repay, requite,
condemn', Bactr. 'to be liable' < PIr. *zam- 'to repay, reward'; Gr. yapppo^
'son-in-law' < *gm-ro~, denom. yap&o 'to marry'; Alb. dhender, dhander
'son-in-law, bridegroom'. From a different preform: Lith. zentas [m.] 'son-in-law,
brother-in-law', Latv. znudts 'id.', OCS zqtb 'bridegroom', Ru. zjat' 'son-in-law,
brother-in-law' < *genh -to/i- (Derksen 2008: 543).
3
The m of Ilr. and Greek cannot go back to n, and the Greek form can hardly reflect a
set root. If the root was *gm~, we can explain gener from *gtpros > *gemros >
*genros > gener.
Bibl.: WH I: 590f„ EM 270, IEW 369f., Schrijver 1991: 218, EIEC 332-335, 532f.,
Viredaz 2002.
gEns, -ntis 'race, nation, people' [f. i] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: gentilis Jm.] 'a member of the same gens' (Lex XII+), gentllitas
'members of a gens' (Varro+), gentflieius 'of a gens' (Varro+).
Pit. *genti~.
PIE *genhti- 'child'. IE cognates: Skt. prd-jdti- [f.] 'birth, production', Av.
r
Jrazainti- 'offspring, posterity'; Gr. yeveon; 'birth'; OIc. kind 'kind, race' < *kendi-,
OE gecynd, OHG gikunt 'origin, race' < *kundi~.
The original meaning is 'clan', that is, 'people of the same descent'. Schrijver argues
that Lat. *genti~ is a relatively recent formation, since PIE *gnhti- is continued in
r
Latin by natio. A different approach is chosen by Beekes 1969, and, for instance,
Schaffiier 2001. They argue that the PIE paradigm had ablaut, and that gens reflects
the full grade case forms, whereas natio stems from a zero-grade case form. However,
the large amount of e-grade forms across the IE languages suggests that maybe PIE
already had two stems, *genhti- 'child' and *gnhti- 'offspring'. Since some of the
r r
extan...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: genu, -us 'knee' [n. wj (Andr.+); mainly pi. genua in the oldest sources; also sg.
genus (Lucil.+) and maybe genum (Fronto). Nom.sg. also genu in Verg., Ov. (before
caesura).
Derivatives: geniculum 'small knee; joint' (Varro+), congenuclare 'to fall on one's
knees' (Sis.+).
Pit. *genu.
PIE *gen-u(-hi dual), *gen-u- (obi.) 'both knees'. IE cognates: Olr. glun [n.], W.
pen-(g)lin, Co. penglin, Bret, penn-glin 'knee' < *glun-; Hit. (UZU)genw- / ganu- [n. >
c.] 'knee' < *gen-u- / *gn-eu-m, Skt. janu-, in cp.jnu-, Av. zanu-,fra-snu- 'holding the
knee forward'; Gr. yovo 'knee', yvu^ [adv.] 'with bent knee' (II.), npoxvu 'kneeling',
Arm. cownr < *gonu-, with an /--addition to older a-stem; Alb. gju i gju [m.] 'knee',
Buzuku pi. glunj < PAlb. *glun-\ Go. kniu, OHG chniu, OIc. kne < PGm. *knewa--,
ToA kanwem Jm.du.], ToB keni* [m.du.] 'knees' < PTo. *kenw- < PIE *gomi~.
The PIE paradigm seems to have been nom.acc.sg. *gon-u, gen.sg. *gen-u-s, but
*gn-eu-s also occurred; probably, *-gn-u- was the form in several compounds. The
dual ending *-uhj may also be seen in PCI. *gl-u-n~.
Bibl.: WH I: 592f., EM 273, IEW 380f., Leumann 1977: 441, Klingenschmitt 1992:
121-125, Meiser 1998: 146. gena

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gero, -ere 'to bear, carry' [v. Ill; gessi, gestum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: gestare 'to carry*; (P1.+), gestitdre 'to carry habitually' (P1.+), gestor
'who carries' (P1.+), gestus, -us 'movement, gesture' (Ter.+), gestTre 'to desire
eagerly, be elated' (P1.+); cp. in -ger, -era, -gerum 'bearing' (P1.+); salutigerulus
'employed to carry salutations' (PI.), scutigerulus 'shield-bearer' (PL); gerulifigitlus
'accessory, hodman vel sim.' (PI.); aggerere 'to bring; pile up' (P1.+), agger, -is [m.]
(Lucil.+) 'pile of earth, ramp', congerere 'to bring together, collect' (PI.+), congestus,
-us 'heap, pile' (Lucr.+), digerere 'to carry away, distribute' (Cato+), egerere 'to
carry away, remove; bring forth' (Cato+), ingerere 'to heap on, pour into' (P1.+),
oggerere 'to place in large quantities on/before' (P1.+), suggerere 'to supply, pile up'
(Ter.+), suggestus, -us 'platform' (Cato+).
Pit. *ges-e/o-\
PIE *h g-es- 'to carry'. IE cognates: see s.v. ago.
2
The meaning of gestire presupposes the meaning 'gesture' of gestus (i.e. *'to make
wild gestures'). The compound agger < *ad + -ger cannot contain a PIE root noun,
since the stem *-ges- < PIE *h g-es- is of post-IE date (pace Benedetti 1988: 107f.).
2
The derivation from the root of agd was proposed at an early date (by Osthoff), but
has only gained acceptance since the laryngeal theory has become endorsed, cf.
Schrijver.
Bibl.: WH I: 22, 595, EM 15,273f., Schrijver 1991: 18f., LIV l.*h eg-. ago
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gibber, -is 'hump' [m./n. r] (Lucil.+)
Derivatives: gibber [adj.] 'hump-backed' (Varro+); gibbus 'hump' (Juv.+), gibbus
[adj.] 'bulging' (Cels.).
Pit. *gifri- 'hump', *gifro- 'hump-backed'.
PIE *geibh-? IE cognates: Lith. geibus 'gawky, plump', geibstit, geibti 'to become
weak'; Norw. dial, keiv 'slanted, wrong', keiva 'left hand'.
Lat. gibb- can continue earlier *gTb~, an interchange which we find in many words for
bodily defects. Nussbaum 2004a interprets the facts in the sense that the PIE adj. in
*-ro- yielded an abstract noun in *-ri~. In theory, *gfber and the Baltic forms could
reflect *geibh~, but the meanings are not identical. The distribution of these forms is
very restricted, and the etymology therefore remains very uncertain. The Gm. forms
in *k- also occur with sk-.
Bibl: WH I: 597, EM 274, IEW 354f., Leumann 1977: 182, Sihler 1995: 224.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gigno, -ere 'to create, engender; to be born' [v. Ill; pf. gemtl, ppp. gentium] (P1.+;
OLat. pr. also gend, -ere until Varro)
Derivatives: genitor 'father, creator' (Enn.+), genetrix 'mother' (Enn.+), genetivus
'of birth, acquired at birth' (Varro+), genitalis 'of (pro)creation' (Enn.+), genitabilis
'having the power of creation' (Varro+); egignere 'to bring forth' (Lucr.), progignere
'to bring into being, come into existence' (P1.+), progenies 'offspring, descent'
(Enn.+), progenitor 'ancestor' (Acc.+), regignere 'to bear again' (Lucr.); ingenuus
'free-bom, liberal; native' (PL); genus, -eris 'origin, offspring, race, gender' (Naev.+),
generare 'to beget, father' (PI.+), generalis 'shared by all, general' (Varro+),
generaseere 'to come to birth' (Lucr.), generatim 'by classes' (Varro+), eongenerare
'to bind by kinship; give birth at the same time' (Acc.+); genius 'the male spirit of a
gens' (P1.+); ingenium 'natural disposition, character' (Naev.+), ingeniatus 'endowed
with a special character' (P1.+); germen, -inis 'shoot, sprout' (Lucr.+), germdnus
'having the same father and mother; true' (P1.+); maybe germinascere 'to sprout'
(Cato; uncertain reading, cf. Keller 1992: 302); bigener [adj. o/a] 'hybrid' (Varro,
Paul, ex F.), multigener, -is 'of many different sorts' (P1.+); -gena 'born person' in
indigena 'native, indigenous' (Ov.+), alienigena 'a stranger' (Varro+); -genus in
caprigenus 'sprung from goats' (Pac.+), prTmigenus 'of origination* (Lucr.); -genius
in primigenius 'first of its kind' (CIL 1.60 primogenia, Varro+); -gnus 'born,
originated' in benignus 'kind, generous' (PL+), bignae 'twins' (Paul. exF.), malignus
'ungenerous, grudging' (PI.+), prTvTgnus 'stepson' (CIL 1.583.22+).
It. cognates: O. genetai [dat.sg.] 'daughter' (?) < *genhto-. Second -e- is
r
problematic: because of the general Sabellic syncope of word-internal vowels, one
would expect *genatd- > *genta-,
PIE pr. *gi-gnhe/o- 'to engender', aor. *genh; *genhtor- [...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: by nature', deva-jatu- [adj.] 'having his birth from the gods', jatii-bharman-
'protector by birth', OP datu-vahya- [m.] PN; Pllr. *janH-tu- 'tribe' > Skt.jantu- [m.]
'creature, tribe', OAv. hu-zantu- 'of good lineage, noble', YAv. zctntu- [m.] 'region'.
Gr. yiyvojiai 'to become', aor. eyevexo; yevoq, -eoq [n.] 'race, kind', yevsTi] 'birth'
(H.); yevettop (Ion. Dor.) and yevernp (Arist.) 'begetter', yevereipa [f.] 'mother';
veoyvoc; 'newborn', opoyvioi; 'of the same origin'; Arm. cnawl 'parent' < *genHi-tlo~,
pr. cnanim, aor. cnay 'is born; engenders'; Arm. cin 'birth' < *genH-os-; Go.
niu-kla-hs 'minor' < *niu-kna-7, aina-kla- 'unique' < *-kna-!\ ToAB kan- 'come to
pass [of a wish]; be realized'.
The pr. genere seems secondary, built on the aor. *gen-. The ppp. genitus is relatively
recent, replacing *gnatos on the model of the aor. genui. The truth of this view is
confirmed by the pair prdgigno : progndtus, cf. Schwyzer 1929. The noun progenies
derives from prdgignere, as does progenitor. The noun genitor seems to be inherited,
but it cannot be excluded that it was derived from the ppp. genitus. One argument for
a recent origin is the medial vpwel: in *genator~, the *a would have been syncopated
in most case forms. The form, genetrix shows that we must go back at least to
*genetor- < *genatorbut this may still be built on *genatos. The adj. geneiivus also
preserves medial -e- from the stage *gene-tos of the ppp.; according to Leumann, its
preservation in genetivus may be due to the specific sequence of vowels in this word.
The words in gener- are derived from genus, -eris. The noun germen < * gen-men lies
at the basis of germanus < *germn-ano~, even if the noun is attested significantly later
than the adj. The original form of the noun must have been *genamen < *genhmen-;
r
apparently, the a was syncopated in foursyllabic oblique caseforms. Still, an
inner-Latin formation on the pr.stem gen- cannot be excluded. Of the compounds in
-gena...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gingiva 'flesh around the teeth, the gum' [f. a\ (Cat.+)
Etymology unknown. IEW compares words such as Gr. yoyyuXoq 'round', Lith.
gunga 'hunch, lump' and Germanic worfis meaning 'ball' and 'winding': OIc. kgkkr
'ball', MLG kinke 'bend'. Yet the semantic connection of 'gums' with 'winding' is
not compelling (it is not the most typical aspect of the gums), and alternative analyses
are possible for the compared forms (Lith. may have *u, for instance). For the suffix,
cf. saliva.
Bibl.: WH I: 601, EM 275, IEW 379f., Meiser 1998: 83.
-ginta 'decade' [num. indecl.]: triginta 'thirty' (P1.+), quadraginta 'forty' (P1.+),
quinquaginta 'fifty' (P1.+), sexaginta 'sixty' (Cato+), septuaginta 'seventy' (Varro+),
octoginta 'eighty' (Cic.+), nonagintd 'ninety' (Varro+)
Derivatives: triceni [pi.adj.] 'thirty apiece' (Varro+), tnce(n)simus 'thirtieth' (Cato+);
quadragenarius 'of, containing forty' (Cato+), quadrageni [pl.adj.] 'forty at a time'
(Cato+), quadragesimus 'fortieth' (Varro+), quadrdgie(n)s 'forty times' (Varro+);
quinqudgendrius 'of, containing fifty' (Cato+), qumquagesies 'fifty times' (PL),
quinquagie(n)s 'fifty times' (Varro+); sexagendrius 'of, containing sixty' (Varro+),
sexdgem 'sixty apiece' (PL+), sexage(n)simus 'sixtieth' (Ter.+); septudgeni 'seventy
apiece' (Varro+); octogeni1 eighty apiece' (Lucil.+); nonagesimus 'ninetieth' (Varro+).
Pit. *trigmta 'thirty', *kwadragmta 'forty'.
PIE *tri-/*kwtr-/ etc. + -dkmt-h > *tridkmth *kwdradkmth > *tri?kmth ,
2 2) 2 2
*kwdra"?kmth > *tri?gmth , *kwdra?gmth > *trigmta, *kwadragmta.
2 2 2
The ordinal -c/gesimus reflects < *-gessamo- < *-gen f tamo- < *-gmt-tamo-.
Distributive -em spread analogically from the lower numerals, e.g. septenT and
novem; cf. Leumann 1977: 495. Originally, the numerals in -ginta are compounds of
the lower numerals (in the zero grade) and the nom.acc.pl. of the n. noun *dkmt-
'decad'. In 'thirty' and 'forty', initial *d- of the second member probably became a
glottal st...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: glaber, -bra, -brum 'without hair, smooth, bald' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Pit. *xlapro~.
PIE *ghlh dh-ro- 'smooth'. IE cognates: Lith. glodiis, Latv. gluoss 'smooth', OCS
2
glad-bkb, Ru. glddkij, SCr. gladak 'smooth, even' < PIE *ghleh dh-; OHG glat
2
'smooth, shiny', OIc. gladr 'shiny' < PIE *ghlh dh-.
2
Schrijver regards glaber as the phonetic outcome of the PIE preform, by a
vocalization rule *CRHTC > *CRaTC. Since the usual reflex of *CRHC is *CRdC,
this may imply that the laryngeal was actually ousted before the putative allophonic
prop vowel was phonologized. The acute accent of the BS1. forms points to the
presence of a laryngeal.
Bibl.: WH I: 603, EM 275, IEW 43If., Schrijver 1991: 188, Meiser 1998: 103.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gladius 'sword' [m. o\ (P1.+; n. in Lucil., Var.)
Derivatives: gladiator 'who fights with the sword' (Cato+), gladiatorius 'of
gladiators' (Ter.+). *
IE cognates: W. cleddyf, /Co. clethe, MBret. clezeff 'sword' < *kladimo- (Olr.
claideb is a loanword from W.).
The close connection with Celtic words for 'sword', together with the imperfect
match of initial consonants, and the semantic field of weaponry, suggests that Latin
borrowed a form *gladio- or *kladio- (a hypothetical variant of attested British Celtic
*kladimo- 'sword') from PCI. or from a third language.
Bibl.: WH I: 603, EM 275f., IEW 545ff.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: glans, -andis 'acom' [f. nd] (P1.+)
Derivatives: glandium 'delicate kernel (in meat)' (Naev.); glandifer 'acorn-bearing'
(Lucr.); iugldns 'walnut' (Varro+).
Pit. *gwland(i)- < *gwland~.
PIE *gwlh -n-d(h)-(i)- 'acorn'. IE cognates: Skt. gula- 'acorn, penis, clitoris'
2
(uncertain); Gr. paXavoq 'acorn' < *gvUi -eno-, Arm. kalin, gen. kalnoy 'acom' (but
2
*gwlh -eno~ would give -an- in Arm.); maybe Alb. lend, Tosk lende 'acorn'; OPr. gile
2
'acom, oak', Lith. gile 'acorn', gyli (dial.), Latv. zile < *gwlh -eh,\ RuCS zeludb, Ru.
2
zelud\ SCr. zelud 'acom' < PS1. Helgdb < *gelh -on-d(h)-i-.
2
Beside Latin, only Slavic shows a dental stop in the suffix. In view of mens, mentis <
*mn-ti-, gldns may reflect an old /-stem, which would match Slavic. Yet the endings
of e.g. the abl.sg. do not point in this direction, so it is safer to assume a consonant
stem. The other languages either continue an original n-stem (Arm., Gr.) or a different
formation. Lat iugldns is probably a caique on Greek Atoi; paX,avo<; 'chestnut', with
the gen.sg. *djowes (> Jovis), or with iu- taken from lupiter.
Bibl.: WH I: 604f., 727, EM 276, 326, IEW 472f., Leumann 1977: 134, Schrijver
1991: 223,273, Sihler 1995: 96.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: glarea 'gravel' [f. <5] (Cato+)
Might be derived from the same root as granum via a dissimilation: PIE *grH-ro->
*graros 'pebble, corn' > deriv. *grarejos 'of pebbles' > diss. *glarejos. But this
depends on the original meaning of granum: if this was 'ripened, aged', it is unlikely
that glarea is cognate.
Bibl.: WH I: 605, EM 276, IEW 390f. granum

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gleba 'lump of earth, clod' [f. a] (Cato+; <glaeb-> mainly in Pliny)
Derivatives: glebdrius 'who cuts off lumps' (Varro).
IE cognates: Lith. glibti, 3s. gtibia 'to embrace, clasp, (dial.) take care of, Lith.
glebys 'embrace, armful', Latv. glebt 'to guard, protect' < *glebh-.
If gleba is older than glaeba, one might posit a root noun *gleb- vs. an o-stem
*globo~. The Gm. forms which are often compared (OHG kla/tra) do not mean
'round', and are better ignored. Only the Baltic words are likely to be cognate. In
theory, Latin and Baltic may continue a root *gleb(h>~, *glob(h}-\ yet because of the
restricted distribution, and the existence of deviant vocalism within Baltic, a PIE
origin is not very likely.
Bibl.: WH I: 606, EM 276, IEW 359f., Schrijver 1991: 125. globus
gUs, -ris 'dormouse' [m. r] (P1.+)
Pit. *glis.
' PIE *glh -i- 'husband's sister, aunt' > 'weasel'. IE cognates: Skt. giri- [f.]
2
'sister-in-law', Gr. yaXet], yaXfj (Ar.+) 'weasel, marten' (the word originally indicated
the skin), yalqchin<; 'weasel-eyed', yaliayKtav 'with an arm like a weasel', yaXidevx;
'young weasel' (Crat.), yoliaco 'to be mad' (Hsch.); yaXu; 'yaXaoq' (Hsch.); Arm. tal
'husband's sister'. Skt. giri-, girika- [f.J 'mouse' probably did not exist.
According to Szemerenyi 1995, Lat. glis was borrowed from Gr. yoXtr\ 'weasel,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: marten', with Hellenistic pronunciation as [gali:]. This seems unlikely: it has a
different meaning, and [gali:] does not explain the form of glis. The semantic distance
between 'dormouse' and 'weasel' also mars the comparison with Skt. giri-
'sister-in-law' < *glh -i-, which would formally be attractive. Cuesta Pastor (1996)
2
argues that the Gr. gloss yaXiq 'husband's sister' together with Skt. giri- and Arm. tal,
points to a PIE stem *glh -i- 'sister-in-law', and, since 'aunt' is often used
2
metaphorically for 'weasel', Latin glis can represent the same PIE stem. Oettinger
1998, too, points out that in Romance languages and dialects often 'weasel' and
'aunt' are homonyms, because of the behaviour of the aunt; similarly for Baltic
Nepokupnyj 2002.
Bibl.: WH I: 607, EM 276f., IEW 367, Schrijver 1991: 242f. glds
gllscO, -ere 'to swell, increase' [v. Ill] (P1.+)
Derivatives: congHscere 'to blaze up' (PL), regliscere 'to grow' (PL).
No certain cognates.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: glomus, -eris 'ball-shaped mass' [n. r] (Varro+)
Derivatives: glomerare 'to form into a ball, collect' (Varro+), glomeramen
'aggregation' (Lucr.+); conglomerare 'to concentrate, heap up' (Enn.+).
Pit. *glemos, -es-.
PIE *glem-o/es- [n.]. IE cognates: Olr. glomar 'gag, curb'; Lith. glomoti 'to
embrace', glemzti 'to grab together; rumple', Latv. glemzt 'to eat slowly, talk
nonsense'; OE clam(m) 'tie, fetters', OHG klamma 'trap, gorge'.
Since some Romance forms seem to continue *glem- (Rom. ghem, Venetian gjemo),
it is assumed that the original Latin form was *glemus, which was able to survive in
parts of the Latin speech area^.The change of *glem- to glom- was conditioned by the
preceding velarized I and the non-front vowel in the next syllable (see Schrijver 1991:
468). According to Schrijver, the Romance form *glem- may be a remnant of the
oblique case forms, which must originally have had e-grade in the root: *glem-es-os
etc. There might also have been a PIE o-stem *glom-o-, the o of which was then
introduced into an j-stem *glemos, *glemesos. But since there is no synchronic
evidence for an o-stem, it seems preferable to reconstruct only an j-stem.
Semantically, only the connection with Olr. glomar and Gm. *klam-b- makes
reasonable sense, although Gm. and Celtic fit better together than with Lat. glomus. If
Baltic is cognate, we would have a verbal root *glem~ 'to contain, embrace'. Within
Latin, globus and gleba seem the closest connection to glomus, and they may go back
to (substrate?)'- *gle/ob-. Hence, there may have been a substrate word 'ball'
vacillating between *glem- and *gleb-; or glomus is from * glob-mo-. The words for
'slimy mass', connected by IEW, are much more remote.
Bibl.: WH 1:609, EM 277, IEW 359f., Schrijver 1991:468, Meiser 1998: 83. globus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gloria 'praise, glory' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: gloriari 'to pride oneself, boast' (Ter.+), gloriosus 'glorious, boastful'
(Naev.+).
Pit. *gnosia / *gnoria 'knowledge, fame'.
PIE *gneh -s- 'torecognize, know' or *gne/oh3-ri- 'knowledge'.
3
The etymology as *gndria 'knowledge, fame' to gnarus 'known' and i-gnorare has
been acknowledged by some scholars, and rejected by others. In its favour speak the
semantics of words for 'glory', which in Indo-European societies mostly have to do
with 'spoken praise', 'reputation by hearsay'. Against the assumed etymology speak
the phonetics. A dissimilation *gndr- (> *gror-?) > *glor- is contradicted by gnarus
and ignorare. On the other hand, gnarus kept its gn- on the model of igndrus, so that
the retention of gn-r- in gnarus and ignorare may be due to non-initial (= VOLat.
unstressed) position of the syllables in question. In *gndria, the stress may have
played a part in the dissimilation. It may be assumed that the semantic connection of
gloria to 'to know' was no longer perceived, hence no restoration of gn- took place.
Gloria could be a derivative of Pit. *gnos- 'to know' (see s.v. ignoro) or of PIE
*gne/oh -ri- 'knowledge', cf. Gr. yvcbpi|io<; 'well-known, familiar'.
3
Bibl.: WH I: 609f., EM 277. gnarus, ignoro
glfls, -ris 'husband's sister, brother's wife' [f. r\ (P1.+)
Pit. *glos-.
PIE *gloh -u- 'husband's sister'. IE cognates: Skt. giri- [f.] 'sister-in-law', Gr.
2
yakaq, -to 'husbands's sister, brother's wife', yc⁣ 'yaAaoq' (Hsch.) < PGr.
*yaX-ap-o-; Arm. tal 'husband's sister'; OCS zthva 'id.' < *gIH-uH-.
As Schrijver points out, it is impossible to derive glos directly from a form *glH-V-.
His own tentative solution of a preform *glH-du- with early loss of *H is ad hoc, but
possible. Alternatively, one may observe that none of the branches of IE completely
agree on the stem suffix, and that PIE may have had a root noun *glh -, or a w-adj.
2
*glh -u-. Latin may then reflect *gloh -(u-).
...

Derivatives: gluma 'husk, chaff (Enn.+); deglubere 'to skin, strip' (P1.+); glubere
'to shed its bark' (Cato).
Pit. *gloufe/o- [v.], *glouf(s)ma-.
PIE *gleubh-e/o-' to split', *gle/oubh-(s)mh -. IE cognates: Gr. yXu(pG3 'to carve out',
2
OHG klioban, OE cleofan, OIc. kljufa 'to cleave' < *gleubh-e/o-.
The noun gluma can continue e- or o-grade. It may contain *-„v-, but not necessarily. If
the connection with gliibo is correct, its meaning would be 'skin, hull' of the corn.
The intr. verb glubere can be interpreted as 'to be in a state of peeling, losing its
bark'.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gluten, -inis 'glue, paste' [n. ri\ (Varro, Lucr.+)
Derivatives: glutimim 'glue, paste' (Lucil.+), glutinator 'person who glues papyri'
(Lucil.+); agglutinare 'to glue together, attach' (P1-+), conglutindre 'to stick together'
(P1.+); glittus 'sticky, cohesive' (Cato, Paul, ex F.).
Pit. *gloiten- 'glue'; *glitos?
PIE *glohii-t- 'slime, glue'. IE cognates: Olr. glenoid, -glen; glieid*, ;glia* [sb.J, W.
glynu, MCo. glena, Bret, englenaff 'to stick, glue' < *(en-)gli-na- [pr.], *glei-ase/o-
[sb.]; Gr. yXoiot^ [m.] 'glutinous substance, gum', yXia 'glue', yMvr), yA,fov 'flexible,
strong' (Hsch.), yXvrxov 'gum' (Hsch.); Lith. gleine 'moist clay', glieti 'to putty',
gleima 'slime', Latv. gleimas 'sticky liquid', Lith. gliemezys 'snail' < PIE *glehii-m-;
Ru. glej (dial.) 'clay, loam', SCr. glej (dial.) 'kind of clay' < PS1. *glbjb < PIE *glh i-;
1
RuCS glerrb, Ru. glen' 'moisture, juice', Bulg. glen (dial.) 'waterplant, duckweed',
Sin. glen (dial.) 'mucus, sediment, clay, waterplant' < PIE *gloH]i-n-; Ru. hllna, Cz.
hlina, Bulg. glina 'clay' < PS1. *glina < PIE *gleH,i-n-; OE clafg, MLG klei 'clay' <
PGm. *klaiia~.
Latin presupposes a stem *gloit- with a suffixal *-?- which is otherwise unattested, but
which might be connect with glittus. The latter is basically a hapax; by means of the
littera-rule (see s.v. cello) it might go back to *glitus < *glhii-to-, with laryngeal
metathesis, or < *gleh]i-to~. The n-stem inflection in Latin *gloiten- might be
secondary after unguen 'ointment', or hide an earlier r/M-stem 'glue'. The Hesych
gloss yXixiov is conspicuously similar to glittus, maybe it is based on the Latin form
or was taken from another Italic language.
Bibl.: WH I: 61 If., EM 277f., IEW 356-364, Schumacher 2004: 337f., LIV *gleiH-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: knowledge' (PL, Enn.); gnaruris [adj.] 'having knowledge' (PL); gnarigare 'to
publish' (Andr. apud Paul, ex F.); narrare 'to relate, tell' (Andr.+), narratio 'story'
(Ter.+), denarrare 'to relate in full' (P1.+), enarrare 'to recount' (PI.+), praenarrare
'to explain in advance' (Ter.).
Pit. *gnaro- 'knowing', *gnardje- 'to make knowing, tell'. It. cognates: U. naratu,
naratu [3s.ipv.II] 'to speak, recite' < *gnara~; U. naraklum [nom.acc.sg.] 'oral
statement' < *gnara-tlo-.
PIE *gnh -ro- 'knowing'. IE cognates: Gr. yvdipipoq 'well-known', yvcopi^co 'to
3
make known' (< *gne/ofi3-ri- 'knowledge').
The adj. gnarus kept its g- on the model of ignarus; while narrare lost it, because no
compounded counterpart with -gn- was available. The verb narrare can be explained
from *nardre via the littera-rule. The verb itself must have been Pit., judging by the
U. forms; it is denominal to the adj. *gnaro-. Leumann 1977: 550 interprets
gndr-igare as a rhyming formation to clarigare to clarus.
Bibl.: WH I: 613f., EM 278, IEW 376ff., Leumann 1977: 188, 546, 550, Schrijver
1991:178, Meiser 1998: 77, Untermann 2000: 486f„ LIV *gneh -. nosed
3
268 (g)navus
(g)navus 'busy, diligent, assiduous, active' [adj. o/d] (P1.+; in PI. only adv. nave;
CLat. navus)
Derivatives: Tgndvus 'lazy, indolent' (Pl'.+), Tgnavia 'idleness' (Naev.+), ignavare
'to dispirit' (Acc.).
Pit. *gndwo~.
PIE *gn(e)h -uo- 'knowing'. IE cognates: Ir. gno 'business', W. go-gnaw 'active,
3
persistent', MBret. gnou 'obviously', OBret. bodo-cnous, Mir. gnou 'excellent';
uncertain: OIc. knar 'hardy, vigorous', Crimean Go. knawen 'good'.
If a development *~ow~ > -aw- is acknowledged, gndvus can reflect * grid wo- or
*gnawo- < PIE *gneh -uo- or *gnh -uo- 'knowing'. The semantic shift from
3 3
'knowing' > 'experienced' > 'busy' is unremarkable. It is also possible that *gndwo-
was formed on the model of gnarus.
Bibl.: WH II: 851, EM 432, IEW 387, Schrijver 1991: 298ff., Meiser 1998: 86, LIV
*gneh -. ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gracilis 'slender, slight' [adj. z] (Ter.+; f. gracila in Ter.)
Derivatives: gracilentus 'slender' (Enn.+), gracilens 'id.' (Laev.), gracilitds
'slenderness' (Varro+), gracilitudo 'id.' (Acc.); cracerts, -ntis 'slender' (Enn.).
It. cognates: possibly U. kurylasiu [abl.sg.m.], attr. of 'moon': *k(o)rkelasio-
'waning' (M. Weiss, p.c.).
Leumann holds that gracilentus was created as an antonym to corpulentus. The form
cracens is only preserved in Paul, ex F.\ it may be the original form, which was
r
subsequently dissimilated to grac- in Latin. Lat. *krakilis would reflect PIE *krk- and
be cognate with Skt. kfsa-, Av. kdrdsa- 'lean, meagre', OIc. horr (*hurha-)
'leanness', Lith. karsdti, kdrsti 'to grow old'. Yet PIE *kfk-i- would normally yield
Latin **corci-, and the alleged dissimilation of velars is unwarranted.
Bibl.: WH I: 284, EM 279, IEW 581, Leumann 1977: 284,336.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: graculus 'jackdaw' [m. o] (Varro+)
IE cognates: RuCS, ORu. grakati, Is. gracu 'to caw, croak' < PS1. *grakati; RuCS,
ORu. gbrkati 'to coo', Ru. (dial.) gorkat' < PS1. *gtrkati; OIc. kraka 'crow', krakr
'raven', OE *cracian, cracettan 'to crow (of raven)' < pre-Gm. *grag-.
Lat. graculus could formally correspond with Slavic *gra-k~, but in view of the
obviously onomatopoeic character of gra-, this does not suffice to prove PIE origin.
One might reconstruct a PIE stem *greh - for Lith. groti 'to caw, croak', PS1. *grajati
2
'to caw, croak', but the same objection applies.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gradior, gradl 'to step, walk, proceed' [v. Ill; ppp. .grejjus] (P1.+). Forms of the 4th
cj.: adgredimur (PL), aggreditur (Pac.).
Derivatives: gradibilis 'able to tread' (Pac.); gradus, -us 'step, pace' (P1+);
gradatim [adv.] 'by steps, progressively' (Varrof), praegradare 'to go in front of
(Pac.); grallae [f.pl.] 'stilts' (Varro), grallator 'who walks on stilts' (P1.+); grassdn
'to press on, march, proceed' (P1.+), grassdtor 'vagabond, highway robber' (Cato+);
gressid 'stepping' (Pac.), gressus, -us 'step, walk' (Acc.+); aggredi, -gressus 'to
advance, approach' (P1.+), congredi 'to meet, join battle' (P1.+), congressus, -us
'meeting, encounter' (Lucr.+), degredi 'to depart, go down' (PI.+), digredi 'to go
away' (Ter.+), ingredi 'to enter, begin' (P1.+), indugredi 'to enter' (Lucr.), progredT
'to advance, proceed' (P1.+), regredi 'to go back' (P1.+); aggretus 'departure', egretus
'surge'? (Paul. exF.).
Pit. *grad-(e)i-.
PIE *g(hWh)-(e)i-. IE cognates: Olr. in-greinn* 'to chase', do-greinn* 'to chase,
drive', MW grynnyaw 'to press, thrust' < PCI. *grinde/o- (BrCl. *grindje/o-), Olr. sb.
dcrgre < *greid-se/o~.
All based on a stem *grad-, yielding the noun gradus (whence gradatim,
praegradare), the noun grallae < *grad-(s)la~, and the verb gradl/gradiri. The ppp.
gressus for *grassus < *grad-to- is based on the compounds ag-gressus etc., with
regular -e- in non-initial syllable. The meaning of the verb suggests that it was
initially used mainly with preverbs in the pf., because of the perfective aspect. The
present form -gredior in the cp. instead of *-gridior may also be due to analogy with
gressus. The iterative verb grassari was regularly built on the ppp. * grass o-. The fact
that all fourth-cj. verb forms are made from compounded verbs, conforms to the
pattern of uncompounded third-cj. and compounded fourth-cj. /'-stem verbs as inpario
- experiri. The noun grallae precludes a preform *gra<f -la- (which would yield
{
*grabulae), unless ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: corners of the eyes'; OIc. kramr [adj.] 'damp', Go. qrammipa 'moisture' (if for
*krammipd).
EM suggest that the original noun was *gramma. The meaning and form of Latin and
Slavic are remarkably close; the appurtenance of Gm. is semantically less compelling.
It is impossible to reduce these forms to a common PIE protoform, so if related, they
will represent a common loanword from a third source. In that case, Gr. yXajicov,
-covoq 'blear-eyed' may also be cognate, with liquid dissimilation. The latter stem was
borrowed into Latin as glamae 'rheum in the eye' (Paul, ex F.).

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: grands, -inis 'hail' [f. n] (P1.+)
Derivatives: grandinai 'it hails' (Pac.+).
Pit. * grand- / *gradn-l
PIE *ghreh -d-on, *ghrh -d-n- 'hail'. IE cognates: Skt (RV+) hraduni- 'hail', Sogd.
3 3
zySn 'hail'; Arm. karkowt, gen.datsg. karkti, ins. karktiw 'hail'; OCS gradb, Ru.
grad, SCr. grdd, gen.sg. grada 'hail' < BS1. *gro?d-o~.
Rasmussen 1984 explains the BS1. cognates from nom.sg. *groHd-on, remade into an
o-stem; I would prefer *greh d-6n. Latin would have metathesized e.g. the gen.sg.
}
*grHd-n- > *gradn- into *grand- (cf. undo < *ud-n-); paradigmatic leveling then
yielded a paradigm grand-o, grand-in-. Rasmussen explains Armenian karkut from
*karknit < *gr-grod-i- < *gr-groHd-i- a reduplicated /-adjective. This, then, would
i
point to an originally verbal root *grh d-. Since two plain voiced stops cannot
3
co-occur in one PIE root, and since *-d- is often found as a root enlargement, the root
is likely to have been *grh ~. If the Ilr. words for 'hail' are cognate, they would
3
require a palatal velar (which could have been depalatalized in BS1. and Arm. in front
of putative vocalic */•); but they may also belong to Skt. hrad- 'to resound'.
Bibl.: WH I: 618, EM 281, IEW 406, Rasmussen 1984 = 1999: 152-154, Schrijver
1991:223.

de Vaan Latin Etymology: gr3num 'grain, seed' [n. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: granarium 'granary' (P1.+), granatus, -us 'the production of a crop'
(Cato), grdnea 'pap made from pounded corn' (Cato).
Pit. *grano~.
PIE *grh -no- 'which has ripened, fruit, grain'. IE cognates: Olr. gran [n.], W.
2
grown, Co. gronen, Bret, greun 'grain' < PCI. *grano-; Pashto zanai, zarai 'kernel,
seed', OPr. syrne 'grain', Lith. zirnis [m.], Latv. zirnis [m.] 'pea', OCS zrbno [n.],
Ru. zerno [n.j, SCr. zrno [n.] 'grain' < BSl. *zirn-; Go. kaurn, OHG horn 'grain' <
PGm. *kurna~.
There are two possible root etymologies: either *grH-no- means 'which has ripened',
and belongs to the root 'to be(come) old, ripen' (Skt.jdranti 'they let grow old'), or it
means 'which has been pounded, ground' and belongs to a root 'to rub, ground'. Yet
in the latter case, the root would be without attested finite verb forms, and also, the
semantics would be less evident":grains' and 'seeds' can be ground, but are not by
definition. Hence, I prefer the former solution. j
Bibl.: WH I: 618f., EM 281, IEW 390f., Schrijver 1991: 178, LIV *gerh -.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gratus 'thankful, grateful; pleasant, charming' [adj. o/a] (PI.+)
Derivatives: gratari 'to congratulate' (Pac.+), grates, -ium [f.pl.] 'thanks' (P1.+),
gratia 'favour, goodwill' (P1.+); ingratus 'ungrateful' (PI.+), ingratiis 'against the
wishes of (P1.+), ingratificus 'ungrateful' (Acc.); gratulari 'to give thanks,
congratulate' (Naev.+), congratulart 'to congratulate' (P1.+); gratuitus 'free of
charge' (P1.+-). „'-
Pit. *gwrdto- 'grateful', *gwrdt- 'mercy, grace', *gwrati- 'mercy'; *gwratu- 'grace'?
It. cognates: 0. Srateis, brateis, Pptmjiq, p[pa]nr)ic, Vest, brat., Pael. brat., brais
[gen.sg.], O. Ppcmaji, Pael. bratom [acc.sg.] to brat a- or brad- 'grace, mercy' <
Vra-t-.
PIE *gwrH-to- 'praised (in a song)'. IE cognates: W. barnu, MBret. barn 'to judge'
< PIE *gwer-n-H-; Olr. brath [m.], W. brawd 'judgement', Gaul. Ppaxou 'out of
gratitude' < PIE *gwrH-tu- 'proclamation'; Skt. pr. grnati, g^rilte 'to praise, honour,
welcome', gurta- 'welcome'; gurti- [f.] 'praising, song of praise'; OAv. aibljardtar-
[m.] 'who welcomes'; YAv. auui gsrsnte 'welcomes'; Skt. gir- [f.] 'song of praise,
invocation'; Alb. grab 'to rouse' < *gwrH-sk-; OPr. girtwei 'to praise', Lith. girtas
'praised', girti, Latv. dzirt 'to praise, boast' < PIE *gwrH-; Lit. geras 'good', if < PIE
*gwerH-o-. OCS zrbcb [m.] 'priest', Ru. zrec [m.] < PS1. *zbrcb; OCS zrbti i zreti, Is.
Zbrg, ORu. zereti, zreti, zrbti 'to sacrifice' < PIE *gwrH-; OCS gram,, grano 'verse,
line' < *gworH-no-
Most forms are built on the inherited adj. grdtus and the noun grates. According to
Leumann 1977: 551, gratarl was backformed to gratularT, but I see no compelling
reason to assume this. Leumann also assupies that gratia is secondary from ingratia,
replacing grates. The adj. gratu-Ttus seems to presuppose a w-stem *gratu-, which is
attested in Celtic. For gratularT, an intermediate stage *gratti-tulari is assumed
(Leumann), but this is unnecessary: a derivation grdtus > gratularT is
unproblema...

de Vaan Latin Etymology: grex, -gis 'flock, herd, troop' [m. (f.) g] (P1..+)
;
Derivatives: gregalis 'of a flock' (Varro+), gregatim 'in a flock' (Varro+); egregius
'outstanding, excellent' (Enn.+), congregare 'to bring together' (Lucr.+), segregare
'to separate' (P1.+).
Pit. *gwreg-.
PIE *gwreg- 'group, herd'. IE cognates: Khot. ham-gris-, (caus.) hamgaj- (hamggalj-)
'to gather, assemble', Gr. yapyapa [n.pl.] 'heaps, lots (of people', yspyepa 'heaps'
(Hsch.), yapycripco 'to swarm', Lith. gurguole 'heap'. Probable loanwords from Latin
are Olr. graig [n. /] 'flock of horses', MW gre 'herd' < *gregi~.
The cp. egregius is based on a prepositional phrase *e grege, while segregare was
built on *se grege 'beside the herd*. Lat. grex might contain the unreduplicated
variant of the stem *g(w)rg(w)- found reduplicated in Baltic and Greek. Khot. ggalj- can
be reconstructed as a denominative verb based on the root noun reflected by grex:
*gwrg-je/o- > Pllr. *gjj-ya-» *grjf-aya- > Khot. ggalj-.
Bibl.: WH I: 622, EM 283, IEW 382f., Leumann 1977: 290, 564, Schrijver 1991: 19,
1995:60,69,140.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: grumus 'heap of earth, hillock' [m. o] (Acc.+)
Derivatives: degrumare 'to level off (Enn.+).
Pit. *gromo- 'heap'.
PIE *h gr-om-o-. IE cognates: see s.v. gremium.
2
Lat. grumus could be connected with gremium < *grem- and OCS gramada 'heap,
pile' < *grdm-. -A preform *grdmos may have turned into grumus phonetically: the
change of *om > urn might also found in humanus (see s.v. homo). The words that
retain -dm- either have a following front vowel (abdomen, nomen,Jomes, momentum,
tomentum, omen, vomer, comis) or are due to a contraction of *o+e (pomum,
promus); the only exception is Roma. Thus, the raising of *o in front of m may
require the additional condition of a following back vowel (no exceptions) or
non-front vowel (exception Roma; but being a name, this may have escaped the sound
change). For the relevance of the vowel in the next syllabe for the a vowel change,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: grunda 'roof [f. d] (only in glossaries)
Derivatives: suggrunda 'projecting ledge or sill on a building' (Varro+),
suggrundium 'id.' (Vitr., Plin.).
Pit. *xronda~.
PIE *ghrondh-tb- 'beam, bar, bolt'. IE cognates: Lith. grinda (dial.) 'flooring of a
bridge, (pi.) wooden floor in a barn', Ru. grjada 'ridge, bed (of flowers)', SCr. greda
'garden bed, ridge' < *ghrndh-h -, OIc. grind 'gate made of spars or bars, fence, dock,
2
store-houses' [f.], OE grindel, OS grindil 'bolt', OHG grintil 'bolt, plowbeam' <
*g\endV
Grunda is probably a backformation to suggrunda. As for the IE cognates, the
existence of three different ablaut grades of a hstem is striking. Possibly, Latin
r
-grunda is a collective derived from an o-stem *ghrondh-o-.
Bibl.: WH I: 623f„ EM 283f., IEW 459f.
grundiO, -Ire 'to grunt' [v. IV] (Caecil.+; the more recent variant is grunmre)
IE cognates: Gr. ypu 'certain sound, among others, the grunting of swine', ypuf^o 'to
grunt', ypuAXoq 'piglet'; OE gnm(n)ian, OHG grunzian, NHG grunzen, OE
grunnettan, MoE to grunt < PGm. *grunnatjan-.
The suffix -ire also occurs in other verbs of sound, such as glocire and hinmre.
Probably, an onomatopoeic formation *g(h)ru(n)~ which may or may not be cognate
with the similar words in Greek and Gm.
Bibl.: WH I: 624, EM 284, IEW 406, Leumann 1977:216.
-gru6, -ere 'to rush' [v. Ill] (only in one gloss)
Derivatives: congruere, pf. -HI 'to unite, correspond, agree' (P1-+), congruus
'according' (P1.+), ingruere, pf. -in 'to attack, make an onslaught upon' (PJ.+).
Pit. *xntwe/o~.
PIE *-ghr(e)uhpe/o- 'to rush in'. IE cognates: Gr. expaov 'attacked', ^a-xpTyrjg
'furious' if from *%pap-; Lith. griauti, Is. griduju 'to destroy', griuti, Is. grhfvu 'to
crumble'.
Although -gruo is only attested in cp., there are enough good examples of word-initial
*ghr- > Latin gr- to assume that it would be the regular reflex in the simplex, too.
Bibl.: WH I: 700, EM 284, IEW 460, Sihler 1995: 158, LIV *ghreh u-. ruo
1

Derivatives: gruere 'to crunkle' (Suet., Paul, ex F.).
Pit. *gru-.
PIE *gerh -ou-s, *grh -eu-, *grh -u-os 'crane'; also *g(ejrh -n-. IE cognates: W.
2 2 2 2
Co. Bret, garan 'crane', Gaul, tri-garanos 'with three cranes' < PIE *gerh no-; Gr.
2
yepavot; [f.(m.)] 'crane', maybe Myc. ke-re-na-i dat.pl. /kerenahi/ < *gerh -n-; Gr.
2
y£pT|v (or yepf[v) < *gerh en\ Arm. krownk; Lith. gerve, Latv. dzerve < *gerH-ueh,
r r
Ru. zurdvl' [m.], ORu. zeravlb [m.], SCr. zerdv 'crane' < PIE *gerh -ou-; OIc. tram
2
[m.], OLG crano, OE cran [m.] 'crane' < PGm, *krana(n)~ < *greh -(u)n~?-, OHG
2
chranuh [m.], OE cranoc, cormtc [m.] 'id.' < *kranaka~.
The PIE cognates point to a u-stem *grh -u- 'crane'. Lat. grus can be the phonetic
2

r

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gula 'throat, gullet' [f. a] (P1.+)
Pit. *gula.
IE cognates: Arm. ekowl 'devoured' (pr. klanem secondary?); OCS gl-btati, Ru. glotat'
'to swallow', Ru. glot, glotok 'gulp, mouthfiilF, Cz. hit, maybe < PBS1. *gul-to-.
The traditional etymology *gwl-h - > gula is morphologically unlikely, and will
2
phonetically not work: such a preform would yield *gla, *gala, *gitala or *vala,
maybe *vola; cf. glans, gravis from roots with a labiovelar. A preform *gel- is
impossible too, since this would yield Lat. **gola. Hence, LIV posits a root *guel-,
with a zero grade *gul~. The schwebe-ablaut is conspicuous, as is the absence of
direct evidence for *guel-. Hence, the root may be onomatopoeic, having only the
shape *gul-. In fact, all forms may have arisen in the separate branches. Relatedness
of gula to the stem glut- is possible, but cannot be demonstrated.
Bibl.: WH: 625f., EM 284f., IEW 365, Leumann 1977: 138, Klingenschmitt 1982:
21 If., LIV l.*guel-. glutto

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gumia 'glutton' [f. a] (Lucil.+)
Pit. *gem-e/o- 'to be full, loaded'. It. cognates: U. kumiaf, gomia [acc.pl.f.] <
*gom-ijo- 'pregnant' (of pigs). - ;
IE cognates: Gr. yevto 'he took' (H.), yepto 'to be full (of)', yopoq 'freight, cargo';
Arm. cim, cem 'bridle', cmlem 'to compress'; Latv. gumt 'to seize', SeCS z§ti, Is.
zbmg 'press, squeeze', Ru. zat\ 3s. zmet 'press, squeeze' < PS1. *z$ti < BS1. *gem~,
*gm-ti\ Lith. gamalas, gamulas 'lump, chunk'; RuCS gomola, gomula, ORu. gomola
'lump', Cz. homole 'cone', SCr. gomoja 'pile (of cheese)' < BS1. *gomo/ulo~, -a-\ OE
cumbol 'wound, ulcer', OIc. kumla 'to crush'; Toch. pret. ikama-l, past ptc. lkakama-l
'to carry, wear' < PTo, *kema~.
Lat. gumia is often explained as a borrowing from Umbrian *gomio~, but the meaning
is not the same as 'pregnant'.
Bibl.: WH I: 626, EM 285, Untermann 2000: 310, Meiser 2003: 229, LIV *gem-

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gurdus 'blockhead, dolt' [m. o] (Lab.+)
Pit. *gword-o- 'heavy, stubborn'.
PIE *gwrd-o-. IE cognates: Gr. ppa5u<; 'slow', Lith. gurdits 'id.', Latv. gurds 'tired',
OCS grbdt,, Ru. gordyj 'proud, haughty' < BS1. *gur?dih.
Quintilian calls gurdus of Spanish origin; while this might of course be true, there is
no proof of this. Latin sometimes shows -ur- < *-f- after a labiovelar (Meiser 1998:
63), so that *gHrd-o- 'slow' > 'heavy' could have given gurdus. This would imply
two different adj. in PIE, however: a u-stem and an o-stem.
Bibl.: WH I: 627, EM 285.

de Vaan Latin Etymology: test, taste'; Go. ga-kusts [fi] 'test', OFr. kest, OE cyst [m.] 'choice' < *gus-ti-; Go.
kustus 'test', OE cost 'choice, exellence', OIc. kostr 'selection' < *gus-tu-.
The old them. pr. *geus- was apparently replaced by the intensive gustare, built on

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: guttur, -is 'throat' [n. r] (Naev.+; also m. Naev. to Varro)
The wr-stem is difficult to explain from a known PIE inflectional type: guttur can
hardly be interpreted as a uer/uen-stem, since the base is unknown. The geminate tt is
also problematic: it either belongs to the group of expressive / iterative words
showing this characteristic, or it reflects earlier *gutur. Note that gula, glut- and
gurgulio also refer to the 'throat' and 'swallowing', and also contain g(l)u~. Guttur
may belong to this same family, which has no PIE etymology. IEW connects Hit.
fUZU)kuttar- [(n.)] 'strength, force, power; back of neck, top of shoulders' and MLG
koder, NHG dial. Koderl, Goderl 'double chin, goitre' < *gut- (to OE ceod(a) 'bag',
OHG kiot 'bag'). Yet the connection of the Hittite word is semantically unconvincing.
The Gm. words might be related if the 'throat' was referred to as 'goitre'; but Gm.
might also continue *gudh-.
Bibl.: WH I: 629, EM 286, IEW 393ff., Leumann 1977: 379.
H
habeO 'to have, hold' [v. II; pf. habui, ppp. habitum] (Lex XII, Andr+)
Derivatives: habena 'rein, strap' (Varro+), habilis 'easy to handle' (Enn.+),
habitare 'to live in, dwell' (P1.+), habitatid 'residence' (P1.+), habitudo 'physical
condition' (Ter.+), habiturire 'to be eager to have' (PI.), habitus [adj.] 'in a good
physical condition' (P1.+), habitus, -us 'condition, character, dress' (Lucr.+); abhibere
'to hold at a distance' (PL), adhibere 'to apply, bring into play, consult' (P1.+),
cohibere 'to hold together, contain, restrain' (P1.+), diribere 'to distribute' (Varro+),
exhibere 'to produce, exhibit' (P1.+), inhibere 'to exert; restrain, check' (PL+),
perhibere 'to bestow; regard as, call' (P1.+), posthibere 'to treat as less important'
(Ter.+), praebere 'to put forward, present, provide' (P1.+), praebia, -orum
'prophylactic charms, amulets' (Naev.+), praebitid 'the supplying' (Varro+),
praehibere 'to provide' (PL),*prohibere (also probere Lucr.) 'to keep off, ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fore-arm', YAv. gauua- [m.] 'hand (of daevic beings)', asango.gauua- 'with hands
made of stone', Khot. ggosta 'hand', Wa. gawust 'fist' < PHr. *gabha-\ Lith. (dial.)
gabana [f.], Latv. gabana 'armful (of hay)', Lith. gabenti, 3s. gabena 'to transport,
remove', gabus 'gifted, clever, skilful, (dial.) greedy', gebiti, 3s. geba 'be able, be
capable', gobiis, gobsiis 'greedy'; Belorussian habac, Cz. habati 'to seize', Sin.
gabati 'to be in need, starve, die'.
The pr. habitare is a frequentative of habere. The noun manubiae < *manabiai <
*manu~habiai may well stem from a singular *manu-habies (the form is not discussed
by Schrijver 1991: 382ff.); Leumann posits an intermediate adj. *manu-habo-, which
accords well with Schrijver's findings. Whether manubrium is really derived from
habere is uncertain; but in view of enubro, inebra, and the adj. *en-habro- which
possibly underlies that formation, manubrium may well continue a form
*manu-habro- 'held by hand'. Schrijver 1991 separates the Italo-Celtic forms from
Gm. and most of Baltic, but connects Lith. dial, at-gebau 'I have brought'. Because of
the acute accent in Lith. and the long e in the Oscan pf., Schrijver reconstructs the
root as *gh(e)h,bh-. Yet long e in Oscan can also be explained analogically from other
perfects. See Kortlandt 1992 for a rejection of the appurtenance of PGm. *geban 'to
give'. LIV reconstructs root-final *b, which is possible but less likely since *b was a
very rare phoneme in PIE. The suggestion in LIV that these roots show an
onomatopoeic structure is incomprehensible to me: 'taking' or 'having' is not
normally associated with a specific sound. Italo-Celtic *^ab(h>- and BS1. *gab~,
*ge/ab- point to a PIE root *ghHbh- or a non-IE loanword Ilr. *gabh- could
only be cognate if reflecting *ghHe/obh-.
Bibl.: WH I: 63Of., EM 287f., IEW 407ff„ Leumann 1977: 285, 292, Meiser 1986:
126, Schrijver 1991: 92f„ Sihler 1995: 497, Untermann 2000: 311-316, Schrijver
2003: 68-85, S...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: haedus 'young goat-buck, kid' [m. o] (PI.+; variants edus, Jedus (Varro), aedus,
faedus)
Derivatives: haedillus 'kid' (PI.), haedinus 'of a kid' (Varro+).
Pit. *xaido-.
IE cognates: Go. gaits [m.], OHG geiz, OS get, OIc. geii 'goat' < PGm. *gait-s [nom.].
The restricted distrubtion, together with the impossibility to derive this word from a
known IE root, suggest a loanword *gfaid- 'goat'.
Bibl.: WH I: 632, EM 288, IEW 409f., Schrijver 1991: 269; de Tollenaere 1983.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: haereo 'to adhere, stick' [v. II; pf. haesi, ppp. haesum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: haerescere 'to stick together' (Lucr.), haesitare 'to stick, hesitate'
(Ter.+); adhaerere 'to cling, adhere to' (P1.+), adhaerescere 'to become attached'
(P1.+), adhaesus, -us 'adhesion' (Lucr.), cohaerere 'to stick, adhere, be consistent'
(Ter.+), obhaerescere 'to become stuck' (Lucr.+).
Pit. pr. *xais-e~, aor. *xais-s~.
The pf. and the ppp. show that -r- goes back to *-s~. The connection with Lith. gaisti
'to linger, be slow', which was supported by WH and IEW, is rejected by Fraenkel
1955-1965. Mechanically, one might think of an s-present to a root *gheh -i-, but no
2
credible comparanda are available.
Bibl.: WH I: 632, EM 288, IEW 410.
(h)allus/x 'the great toe' [m.?] (Paul. exF., gloss.)
The original form is unclear (probably hallus or hallux)-, hallux would have a unique
suffix, only hallus has a structure that might be IE. But no etymology is available.
Bibl.: WH I: 633, EM 288.
(h)arundo 279

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: halo, -are 'to emit, be fragrant' [v. I] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: halitdre 'to exhale' (Enn.+), halitus, -us (m.) 'an exhalation, vapour'
(Lucr.+); exhalare 'to exhale' (Pac.+), redhaldre 'to breathe back' (Lucr.).
Pit. *anaslo- 'breath' > *anaslaje- 'to breathe'.
PIE *h enh[-slo- 'a breathing'. IE cognates: Olr. anaid, •ana 'to wait, remain', MW
2
kynhanu to pronounce', MBret. ehanaff 'to dwell' < PCI. *ana~; Skt. pr. aniti
[3s.act.], dnit [3s.ipf.act.] 'to breathe', YAv. antiiapardntiia [gen.du.] 'breathing in,
breathing out', Go. uz-anan 'to breathe out'.
Schrijver 1991: 44f. regards hdlare as an early denominative from a noun *anaslo-
'breath', with regular syncope of the second syllable in front of the long third syllable
(in a foursyllabic word): *anaslaie- > *ansla- > *ald-. The h- was added as an
onomatopoeic element, or it is hypercorrect (initial h- tended to be lost in the historic
period), as in humor next to umor.
Bibl.: WH I: 633, EM 288f., IEW 38ff., Schrijver 1991: 44f., Schumacher 2004:
196, LIV *h enh. —> anhelus, animus
2 r

Only the Gr. words xct|i6<; and %ctPoc; 'curved' are close "in form and meaning, but the

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: hara 'small enclosure for domestic animals, pigsty' [f. a] (P1.+)
The preform *ghr-h - (morphologically difficult) to the root *gher- 'to grab' (cf.
2
cohors) as given by WH, is impossible: this would yield *hora. No etymology.
Bibl.: WH I: 634, EM 289.
har£na 'sand' [f. A] (Cato+; variants asena,'Sabine' fasena Var.)
Derivatives: hdrenatus 'sandy' (Cato+), (h)arenosus 'sandy' (Cato+).
The suffix might reflect a derived adj. in *-es-no- (cf. aenus, terrenus), a derivation
from a verb in -e-, or something else. No etymology.
Bibl.: WH I: 634, EM 289.
(h)arundO, -inis 'reed, cane, rod' [f. A] (P1.+)
Derivatives: (h)arundinetum 'reed-bed' (Cato+).
Pit. *xarund-en-.
One might connect Gm. *hreud- or *hreup- 'reed' and ToB karwa (n.pl.), ToA kru-
'reeds', but this does not lead to a common preform. M. Driessen (p.c.) proposes to
connect harundo with Gaulish *garunda- for 'shallow water-course, river, river bank'
(with regular *nd > nn in Celtic), as reflected in many names in Southern France and
northern Spain: Gasc. Garouno 'water-course', the river Garonne in France,
Provencal garouno 'drainage canal', the Guareca (Garonna 1156 AD) in the Spanish
province Zamora. Since reed thrives excellently in shallow water, it is conceivable
that Lat. harundo derives from the same source as Gaul. *garunda~.
Bibl.: WH I: 634, EM 289, IEW 68.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: haruspex, -icis 'diviner, priest who inspects the organs of sacrificial animals'[m. A:]
(P1.+)
Derivatives: haruspica 'female diviner' (PI.); (h)ariolus 'soothsayer' (Naev.+),
hariola 'female soothsayer' (PL), (h)ariolari 'to prophesy' (P1.+), hariolatio
'prophecy' (Enn.+).
Pit. *xaruspek- 'diviner', *xario-. It. cognates: Fal. harasp[ex] , harisp[ex]
[nom.sg.] 'haruspex'.
PIE *ghrH-u~ 'intestines'. IE cognates: Skt. hira- 'vein', Lith. zarna 'intestine, hose',
OIc. ggrn 'intestines' < *ghorH-nh -
2
There is vacillation between haruspex and (h)arispex, but -u- is earlier and better
attested; also, Hellenistic Greek has borrowed the word as apouoTuiKci. Haru/ispex has
been assumed to be a loanword from Etruscan, in which case the vacillation may be
due to the source language. On the other hand, we find several IE forms from a root
*ghrH- 'intestines' to which haru- can be connected as a w-stem. The dim. hariolus
might be based on a preform *hari- or on *haro-» *hario~.
Bibl.: WH I: 635, EM 289f., IEW 443, Giacomelli 1963: 247, Benedetti 1988:
157ff., Schrijver 1991: 208, Lindner 2002: 230. aruina, specid

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: hasta 'spear, staff [f. a] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: hastatus 'spearman, soldier', hostile '(shaft of a) spear' (P1.+).
Pit. *xasta~. The comparison with U. hosiatu [acc.pl.m.], hostaiir [dat.pl.rn.] '?' is
problematic because of the unknown meaning, and U. o which does not regularly
correspond to Latin a.
IE cognates: Mir. gat 'osier, withe', Mir. gass 'twig, branch"; Go. gazds 'sting',
OHG gart, OIc. gaddr 'goad' < PGm. *gazda-, OHG gerta, OS gerdia < *gazdjd.
Probably not of Indo-European origin, cf. Lubotsky 2004: 329f. Latin ~st~, Mir. -t and
PGm. *zdpoint to a cluster *-sT- in this loanword.
Bibl.: WH I: 636, EM 290, IEW 412fi, Schrijver 1991: 134, Meiser 1998: 119,
Untermann 2000: 336f., Hill 2003: 244.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: haud 'not' [ptcle.] (Andr.+; variants hau before consonant, haul Andr., Naev.)
IE cognates: Olr. gau, go, acc. goi 'falsehood', ME gen, W. gau 'lie, deceit' < *goua~.
According to Leumann 1977: 229, haud was used proclitically and was subject to
word-intemal sandhi: haud aliter but haul temere. Eichner 1995: 66ff, proposes that
VOLat. hauelod (Forum cippus) represents an abl.sg. in -od of an original adj.
*haued~o- 'false, insufficient, lacking'; the latter would be the source of haud. Note
that irregular apocope of an ending *-os or *-om is needed for this explanation to be
correct. Since Latin *-awV- can result from (pretonic) *-owF-, the preform may have
been *ghou-i-dho- 'false', which could be compared with PCI. *gow<5- Tie, deceit' (as
Eichner does). The root could be identified as *gheu- 'to hide', which we find with
different enlargements in Ilr. *g(h)aujh- (Skt. guha [adv.] 'in secret', gUhate, YAv.
guza-, OP apa-gaudaya- 'to hide') and Gr, K£Ui3to 'to hide' if from PIE *gheuc?-. In
view of the uncertainties surrounding the meaning of hauelod and the form of hand,
this remains a speculative etymology.
Bibl.: WH I: 636, EM 290, IEW 414, LIV ?*g(u)heugh-.
haurio, -Ire 'to draw, scoop up' [v. IV; hausi, haustum] (Cato+)
Derivatives: haustrum 'a scoop on a water-wheel' (Lucr.), ham ins, -us 'the drawing,
scooping' (Lucr.+); deorio 'to drain off (Cato), exhaurid 'to draw off, exhaust' (P1.+).
Pit. pr. *aus-je~, pf./aor. *aus-s~.
PIE *h eus-ie/o- 'to scoop'. IE cognates: Gr. auto 'to get a light, light a fire' <
2
*auoa> or *a15ojco (with secondary limitation of 'to scoop' to 'fire'), OIc. ansa 'to
scoop' < *ausanan.
Since initial h- may be hypercorrect, the original form may be *ausje- or *hausje-.
LIV assumes that haurio and Gr. auto have secondary e-grade of the root (possibly
taken from the ^-aorist, Meiser 2003: 121), whereas the aorist *h eus- would be
2
continued by the pf. hausi.
Bibl.: WH I: 637, EM 290, IEW 90, LIV *h us-ie- ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: hebes, -etis 'blunt, weak' [adj.'fj (PI.+, Caecil. acc.sg. h'ebem)
Derivatives: hebere 'to be blunt, be inactive' (Verg!+), hebescere 'to grow blunt,
become feeble' (Lucr.+).
The acc.sg. hebem in Enn. and Caecil. is probably analogical to the nom. hebes,
compare the rise of requiem, requie (Cic.+) to requies, -etis. It seems impossible to
derive the verb from the adj., but also, to derive the adj. from the verb (one would
rather expect *hebidus, for instance). Hence, we must posit a stem *heb~, whence the
verb *heb-e- 'to be blunt' and the adj. *heb-et- 'blunt'. Other t-stem adjectives are
teres 'round' and dives 'rich'. The -e- in -et- (instead of regular -//-) will be due to
vowel assimilation to the first syllable. No etymology.
Bibl.: WH I: 637f., EM 291, Leumann 1977: 285,373.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: hedera 'ivy' [f. a] (Lab.+)
Derivatives: hederaceus 'of ivy' (Cato+).
IEW and Meiser explain hedera from the root *ghed- 'to grab' of prae-hendo.
Whereas IEW assumes a thematized s-stem *hedes-o~, Leumann posits PIE *-er-o~.
The connection with -hendd is suggested by the gloss Paul, ex F. 'quod edera vincit
ad quodcumque se applicaf, but this is not enough evidence. Of course, ivy is a
climbing (or ground-creeping) plant, and one may surmise that its name means 'the
grabbing one', but this is just a guess, especially since the morphology is uncommon:
no s-stem of this root is attested elsewhere in IE. Adjectival *hed-ro- 'grappling' > m.
*heder would be slightly better.
Bibl.: WH I: 638, EM 291, IEW 437f., Leumann 1977: 315, Meiser 1998: 83.
prehendo
282 (h)el(l)uo
(h)el(l)uO 'squanderer, glutton' [m. n] (Ter.+; the oldest texts have hell-)
Derivatives: (h)elludri 'to spend immoderately on eating and other luxuries' (Cic.+).
Pit. *xeIsVwo~. «
In spite of its earlier attestation, hellud is probably a derivative to helluor. This verb
suggests an earlier noun or adj. *helluus or *hellua 'luxury, spendthrift' vel sim. This
would require a preform *xelsVwo-, which yields no promising etymology. Initial h-
seems secure, so WH's etymology from *e-lud 'to bathe abundantly' can be rejected
already for this reason, Knobloch 1973: 63 proposes to connect hellud with U. felsva
'banquet, ceremonial meal', but U. f- normally reflects *bh, *dh or *gwh, not *gb.
Bibl.: WH I: 638f., EM 291.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: helvus 'yellow, dun' [adj. o/a] (Varro+)
Derivatives: helvolus [adj.] 'a variety of wine and grape' (Cato+), helvius 'id.'
(Varro), helvella 'a pot-herb' (Titin.+).
Pit. *xeliwo~.
PIE *ghelh -i-uo~ 'yellow, green'. IE cognates: Skt. hdri-, Av. zairi- 'yellow,
3
greenish' < PIE *ghel(h )i- or *gholh i-, Skt. hiri- 'yellow' (in cp.) < *ghlh i-; Gr.
3 3 3
X^copoq 'pale green, greenish yellow' < *gblh -ro-; Lith. zelvas 'greenish' <
3
*ghelh -uo-, zeiti 'to grow, flourish', Latv. zeh, OCS zelent 'green', Ru. zelenyj <
3
*ghelh -en-; OHG gelo < PGm. *gelwa-, OIc. gulr 'yellow' < PGm. *gula-.
3
Ilr. shows an j-stem adj., which must also be assumed as the basis for the Latin form.
The latter was extended with *-uo- as in some other colour adjectives, Leumann and
i
Sihler reconstruct *^elswo- > *hellwo- > helvus, but the PIE preform is based only
on Lith. gelsvas, with a productive suffix in Lith. Meiser suggests that helvus was
borrowed from a Sabellic dialect, but gives no arguments. Rix (2005: 567) just states
that helvus, if it were an originally Latin word, should have u instead of e in front of
velarized 1. An additional argument seems to be that other colour terms for animals
(rufus, callidus) are also suspect of borrowing.
Bibl.: WH I: 639, EM 291, IEW 429f., Leumann 1977: 141, Cowgill 1978: 42,
Schrijver 1991: 110, 433, Sihler 1995: 41, 181, Meiser 1998: 82, Nussbaum 1999a:
386f., 410. holus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: heres, -edis 'heir' [m. d] (Lex XII+; Naev. acc.sg. herem)
Derivatives: hereditas 'inheritance' (PI.+), heredium 'hereditary estate' (Lex XII
apud Plin., Varro+); exheres, -dis 'disinherited' (P1.+).
Pit. *xered-.
PIE *gheh,ro- 'derelict' + -ed- IE cognates: Gr. >rjpo<; 'orphaned, empty', %ilPa
'widow', ^ptoorai 'who divide the property of somebody who died without sons,
usurpors'.
The form herem is analogical to the fifth declination. See Dunkel 1987 for an
overview of earlier etymologies. If hires is indeed cognate with xr|ptocTai, a
derivation from the root *ghed- 'to take', Lat. -hendo, is impossible. Beekes 1975
suggests a J-stem *gheHr-od-, -ed-m, -d-os, to which Dunkel objects that the meaning
of the suffix remains unclear (in itself not a strong argument) and that J-suffixes are
extremely rare in PIE. This stance may be modified if one accepts Nussbaum's
explanation (2004a) of the Latin type in -edo, Gr. -rj8cov, -E5<BV, as inherited.
Nussbaum explains hered- as a substantivization in *-o/ed- of an adj. *ghehjro-
'derelict' identical to Gr. xfjpoq. Long *-ed- would be due to a suffix conglomerate
*-e~ed~. While one may question the last point, it seems to me that Nussbaum's
explanation is the most likely one. Dunkel himself returns to Prellwitz' explanation of
heres as a compound of (in my reconstruction) *gheh ro- plus the root *hied- 'to eat'.
1
The original meaning would have been 'who eats what has been abandoned'.
Although conceivable in theory, this is not very convincing, since no collocation of
*gheh|ro- and *hjed- is actually attested elsewhere.
Bibl.: WH I: 641f., EM 292, IEW 418f., Beekes 1975: 9f., Leumann 1977: 393,450,
Dunkel 1987, Schrijver 1991: 139, LIV *gheh.;
r
> $
