content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ius, -ris 'broth, sauce' [n. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: iusculum 'broth, soup' (Cato).
Pit. *jowas- or *jus~.
PIE *i(e/o)uH-s- 'broth, soup'. IE cognates: Skt. yiis- 'soup, broth', Khot. yus 'id.',
F
Gr. ^upr) 'leaven, beer-yeast' < *iuHs-meh - (more difficult to connect: Gr. ^copo^
2
[m.] 'sauce, soup'); OPr. juse [fi], Lith. juse 'broth, soup'; CS juxa 'broth', Ru. uxa
'fish-soup', Czjicha 'liquid, sauce, (arch.) soup' < PS1. *juxa < PIE *ie/ouH-s~.
Whereas Skt and Lith. point to *iuH-s-, Slavic requires *ious- < *ieuH-s-. Latin
could have either the full grade or the zero grade. Hence, we seem to be dealing with
an original s-stem. The appurtenance of Gr. (£3 pi) is not completely certain because of
the deviant meaning. The root is often analysed as *ieu- 'to hold tight, fasten', as
attested in Skt. yauti, Lith. jauti, but the semantics are remote, and the root does not
contain a final laryngeal (unless, of course, 'soup' would be analysed as *iu-H-).
Bibl.: WH I: 734, EM 330, IEW 507, Schrijver 1991: 233.
ius, -ris Taw' [n. r] (Lex XII+; in VOLat. inscr. ious)
Derivatives: iustus 'lawful, just' (iouestod 'iusto' Forum inscr.+, iouiste Paul, ex F.),
iustitia 'justice' (Ter.+); iurare 'to take an oath, swear' (PI.+) (3s. iouvesat 'swears',
Duenos inscr.), iurator 'certain official' (P1.+); abiiirare 'to deny knowledge of
(P1.+), adiurare 'to swear' (P1.+), deie/urare 'to swear' (P1.+), eiu/erare 'to reject
under oath', peiie/urare 'to swear falsely' (P1.+), peiiuridsus 'addicted to peijury'
(PL), peiurium 'peijury' (P1.+), peiurus 'perjured' (P1.+); iniuratus 'unsworn' (PL+),
inivria 'unlawful conduct, injustice' (Lex XII+), iniurus 'lawless, unjust' (Naev.+),
iniurius 'unjust' (Naev.+); iur(i)gare 'to quarrel' (PL+), obiur(i)gare 'to reprove'
(PI.+), iurgium 'quarrel, dispute' (P1.+); iudex 'judge' (Lex XII, P1.+), iudicium 'legal
process, trial; decision' (P1.+), iudicare 'to judge, try' (P1.+), iiidicatum 'judgement
debt' (Lex XII+), iii...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: iuvencus 'young bull' [m. o] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: iuvenca 'young cow, heifer' (Varro+).
Pit. *juwnko~. It. cognates: U. iuengar [nom.pl.], iveka, iuenga [acc.pl.] 'young cow'.
PIE *h iu-hn-ko- 'young one, young animal'-, IE cognates: Olr. oac, W. ieuanc
2 t
'youth' < PCI. *iuuanko~, Skt. yuvasa- 'young', Go. jugga-laups 'youth', OHG jung,
OIc. ungr 'young'.
A derivative in *-ko- of the PIE word reflected in iuvenis. The sequence -enc.- instead
of -inc- is unexpected. Iuvencus can be explained as a Sabellism, or as influenced by
iuvenis and iuventus.
Bibl.: WH I: 735, EM 330, IEW 51 Of., Schrijver 19?I: 32If., Meiser 1998: 81, 91,
Untermann 2000: 354. —• iuvenis

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: iuvenis 'young man' [m. /] (PL+)
Derivatives: iuventus 'youth' (P1.+), iuventa 'youth' (Lab.+), iuventas 'youth'
(Lucr.+); iiinior 'younger' (P1.+); iunix, -Jcis 'young cow, heifer' (Persius Flaccus,
hapax), Junius [adj.] 'the month of June' (Enn.+), Iuno 'the goddess Juno' (P1.+).
Pit. * juwen- 'young', *jiinids 'younger'. It. cognates: maybe U. iouies [dat.pl.], iouie
[acc.pl.] '?', party of able-bodied men: *ieu-ie-t
PIE *h iu-hien- 'who possesses vital force' > 'young'; comp. *h iu-hin-ios. IE
2 2
cognates: Skt. yuvan- 'young; young man', YAv. yuuansm [acc.sg.], yunqm [gen.pl.]
'youth', yoista- 'youngest'; yauua [rru] 'youth'; Lith. jdunas 'young', OCS jum> <
PIE *h iou-Hn-o-; Go .jund- [f.] 'youth' < *h iu-h]n-ti-.
2 2
The original n-stem *iuwen- was made into an /-stem. The e instead of i in the second
syllable of iuvenis is probably due to iuventus, where -e- was phonetically retained.
The comparative iunior occurs beside iuuenior, suggesting that it represents a
contraction of the latter. The abstract iuventus is clearly more original than iuventa
and iuventas. Lat. iunix is derived from *h iu-Hn-ih - by Rix 1981: although iuriix is a
2 2
comparatively recent hapax, it is difficult to see how it could have arisen secondarily.
Rix assumes that the Etr. counterpart uni of Lat. fund reflects a borrowing from Latin
*iuni before the suffix *-k- was added. The goddess Iuno will then represent a
different extension of the stem *iun- 'young'. The month name liinius might be a
derivative of f. *ium-, as Rix p. 279 proposes. Fortson 2002 connects the family name
Iunius as from *iou-.
Bibl.: WH I: 735f., EM 331, IEW 510f., Leumann 1977: 83, Rix 1981: 274-279,
Schrijver 1991: 152, 322, Meiser 199£: 155, Untennann 2000: 353. aevum, iugis,
ius, iuvencus

de Vaan Latin Etymology: iuvo, -are 'to help, assist* [v. I; pf. iuvi, ppp. iutum] (Carmen Arvale+, P1.+; CIL sb.
iouent, also pr. iuvere Acc.)
Derivatives: iucundus 'agreeable' (PL+); adiuvare 'to help' (P1.+), deiuvare 'to
refuse help to' (PL); adiiitare 'to help' (P1.+), adiutabilis 'helpful' (P1.+), adiutor
'helper' (P1.+), adiutrix 'female helper' (P1.+).
Pit. *ijow-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: help, care', uz~uidiioi [inf.] 'to protect'.
Pf. iiivi < *iuwa-wl, iutus < *iuwatos. This suggests an earlier stem *iuwa~, as is
preserved in some forms of the verb iuvere < Pit. *i(j)ewa-. Lat. iuvere has replaced
*iovere by analogy with iuvare. Lat. iuvare may be a secondary iterative to (the
predecessor of) iuvere, or it has been backformed from ad-iuvare (cf. pellere -
appellare)\ in the first case, iuvare has its -u- (for *iovare) by analogy with ad-iuvare.
The pf.sb. forms iuverit, iuverint could be replacements of *iuveri(n)t on the model of
fuerit (thus Leumann), or they reflect a Pit. reduplicated ^-present *Hi-HieuH-es- (as
O. didest 'will give'; cf. also monerint < *-es-).
Bibl.: WH I: 736f., EM 331, IEW 77-78, Leumann 1977: 596, Garcia Ramon 1996,
Meiser 1998: 184, 188,206, Rix 1999: 520, LIV *h,euH-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: iuxta 'near by, equally; next to' [adv.; prep.] (P1.+)
Derivatives: iuxtim [adv.] 'in close proximity' (Andr.+).
Pit. *jougVsto-.
PIE *(H)ieug-s- 'yoke'?
The preservation of the cluster -kst- points to syncope from *iugVstdd, abl.sg.f. of
*iugVsto~. One might posit an adj. *ieug-s-to- 'yoked' derived from *ieug-e/os- [n.]
'team of animals, yoke' which is preserved in Lat. iugera. This would have to be a
rather old derivative, from before the generalization of the full-grade suffix *-es-/-os-.
This hypothesis seems more likely than to posit *iug-isto- 'most connected with,
closest' with the PIE superlative suffix *-ist(H)o-, since this suffix is not otherwise
found in Latin, and also, *iug- is not an adjective.
Bibl.: WH I: 737, EM 328, IEW 508-510, Cowgill 1970: 125, LIV *ieug-. -> iungo
L

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: labium 'lip' [n. o] (P1.+; mostly pi. labia)
Derivatives: labia / labea 'lip' (P1.+), labeosus 'thick-lipped' (Lucr.); labrum 'lip,
brim' (Naev.+), labellum 'lip' (P1.+), collabellare 'to make by putting lips together'
(Lab.).
Pit. *labjo~, *labro-.
IE cognates: OE OFr. lippa 'lip' < PGm. *lepjan~, OLFr. lepor, OFr. lepur iip', OHG
lefs < PGm. *lep-e/os~. Less certain: Gr. *lob- in Xopoq [m.] 'lobe, lap, slip', 7ipoXopo^
'crop of birds, Adam's apple', npoXopiov 'the front part of the lobe of the ear'.
Lat. a in labrum might be due to the development *(C)RDC > *(C)RaDC proposed by
Schrijver 1991 (cf. magnus < *mg-no~). Yet nominal cognates of *lb- 'lip' are only
found in Germanic, and *b is a rare PIE phoneme. It is furthermore uncertain that
'lip' can be derived from the verb forms for 'to waver' (It.+Gm. *lab~, LIV *lembH-
'to hang loosely'), as IEW assumes. Hence, *lab- 'lip' may be a borrowing from an
unknown adstrate. t -
Bibl.: WH I: 738, EM 333f. IEW 655-657, Schrijver 1991: 479, Sihler 1995: 146.
}
—»labo, lambo •

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: labo, -are 'to stand unsteadily, waver' [v. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: labascere 'to become uncertain; dissolve' (P1.+), collabascere 'to
waver at the same time' (PL), labefacere 'to make unsteady, weaken' (Ter.+),
labefactare 'to undermine' (P1.+), collabefierT 'to collapse' (Lucr.+); labi, pf. lapsus
'to glide, slip' (PL+); labes, -is 'fall (of earth), landslip; disaster' (P1.+), labdsus
'slippery' (Lucil.), labundus 'gliding' (Acc.), lapsus, -us 'the gliding, falling'
(Andr.+); collabi 'to slip, collapse' (P1.+), delabT1 to slip down, drop' (Varro+), dilabT
'to flow away, perish' (Naev.+), perlabi 'to glide along, skim' (Lucil.+), prolabi 'to
slide forwards' (Acc.+).
PIE *lh2b-eh -, *Ieh b-e/o-, *leh b-eh 'weak'. IE cognates: OCS slabb, Ru. slabyj,
2 2 2 r
SCr. slab 'weak' < PIE *slob-(n)o- (according to Derksen 1996: 83, Latv. slabs,
slabgns 'weak', and Lith. slabnas (Zem.) 'weak' were borrowed from Slavic); OHG
slaf, MoDu. slap.
Schrijver regards labare as the regular reflex of a zero-grade of a root *(s)lh b-. For
2
the assumed cognates of labo, a different solution is proposed by LIV: *slehib-. With
some additional assumptions, this would explain all the cognates, but it cannot
explain the Latin ablaut lab-: lab-. Even if lab- < *lHb- were the oldest form within
Latin, it is difficult to imagine lab-e/o- as a secondary full grade to this root. There is
one other way out: the meanings 'to waver' and 'to slip' are not necessarily
connected. Thus, one might separate labare and connect it to the Baltic and Germanic
words for 'weak'; labi then remains isolated.
Bibl.: WH I: 739, EM 333f., IEW 655-657, Schrijver 1991: 163f„ 179, 378f., LIV
?*slehb-.
I

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lac, -tis 'milk' [n. f] (PI.+; nom.acc.sg. also lade Pl.+, lact Varro, Plin.)
Derivatives: lades, -ium 'the small intestines; chitterlings' (Pl.+), lacteus 'of milk'
(Andr.+), lactarius 'suckling' (Varro+), lactam 'unweaned, sucking; full of milk'
(Andr.+), lactens 'unweaned, sucking; full of milk' (Cato+), lactuca 'lettuce'
(V arro+).
Pit. *(g)lagt-.
PIE *glg-t- 'milk'. IE cognates: Gr. yaka, yaXaKTOcJyXayoq [n.] 'milk' < *glg-(t-),
yaXaiJrivoc, 'sucking milk', Ann. kaxc' (dial.) < *glg-t-s, kat'n 'milk' < acc.sg.
*glg-t-m. The semantics of Hit. kala(n)k- 'to soothe, satisfy' (3s.ipv.act. kalankaddu,
ptc. kalankant-) are too far removed to warrant a connection.
The a of *lakt- can be explained by Schrijver's rule of a development *CRDC >
*CRaDC. The loss of initial *g- is explained by distance dissimilation by Meiser,
whereas e.g. Sihier reconstructs the word with initial in which case Greek and
Armenian would have undergone assimilation to the following velar. Leumann
regards lac as a loanword from Greek *glakt~. To my mind, the last explanation is
veiy unlikely (Greek has a different nom.sg. form), and original *dl- is not supported
by any evidence. Thus, Lat. lact- goes back to *glgt- > *glagt-, and initial *g- has
been lost in Latin through dissimilation.
Bibl.: WH I: 741, EM 335, IEW 400f., Leumann 1977: 187, Schrijver 1991: 479f.,
Sihier 1995: 96, Meiser 1998: 114. -»delicus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lacer 'mutilated' [adj. o/a] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: lacerare 'to tear, torment, ruin' (P1.+), dilacerare 'to tear to pieces'
(P1.+); lacinia 'the edge of a garment' (P1.+); lancinare 'to tear in pieces' (Cat.+).
Pit. *lak(V)-ro- 'torn, ragged', *lank- 'to tear'.
PIE pr. *lh -n-k- 'to tear', adj. *lh k-(V-)ro-. IE cognates: Gr. [aor.] wiefoiKa 'I have
2 2
torn off among the Cyprians (Hsch.; for *a7teXccKa); Xaicig, -i5oq 'rent, rending;
tatters of clothes', XcndC,cfl 'to tear', later] 'rags' among the Cretans (Hsch.), Xaicripa
'rent, piece, fragment' (pap.); Po. lach, Ru. lachon 'rag'?
(DT)lacerare is denominal to lacer, even if lacer is attested much later. The pr.
lancinare looks like a contamination of a pr. *lank- with a nasal stem *lak-e/on-
(which sometimes gave rise to verbs in -inare), from which also lacinia must be
derived. While Meiser 1998 reconstructs *lh k-ero-, Melchert 2007a: 257 suggests
2
that hxvdq was made from an earlier noun *Ih2ki- 'tearing', and that lacer represents a
derived adj. *laki-ro- 'torn'.
Bibl.: WH I: 742f., EM 335, IEW 674, Schrijver 1991: 164f., Meiser 1998:107.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lacio, -ere 'to entice' [v. III(?)] (Paul, ex F. lacit)
Derivatives: (1) allicere 'to entice, attract' (P1.+; pf. -lexi, ppp. -lectum), delicere 'to
lure' (Titin.), delicatus 'luxurious, self-indulgent' (P1.+), deliciae [f.pl.] 'pleasure,
luxuries' (PI.), elicere 'to coax, draw forth' (P1.+; pf. elicui), inlicire (Naev.), illicere
'to entice' (P1.+; pf. illexi), illicium 'lure' (Varro+), pellicere 'to win over, seduce'
(Lex XII+), prdlicere 'to lure ..forward' (P1.+); lactare !to entice' (P1.+), delectare 'to
charm, entice' (P1.+), delectamentum 'instrument of'pleasure' (Ter.+), delectatid
'source of delight' (Ter.+), Hectare 'to worm out (information)' (P1.+), oblectare 'to
delight' (P1.+),prolectare 'to induce to do' (P1.+), sublectdre 'to coax' (PL); lacessere
'to challenge, provoke' (P1.+; pf. -ivi, ppp. -itum); lax 'deceit' (Paul, ex F. ), illex
'who attracts' (P1.+); elecebra 'a means of wheedling something out of a person'
(P1.+), illecebra 'enticement' (P1.+), illecebrdsus 'enticing' (PL), pellecebra 'decoy'
(PL); pellacia 'seductiveness' (Lucr.+); (2) colliciae [f.pl.] 'gutter' (Vitr.+; -qu- Col.),
colliciaris 'designed for making gulleys' (Cato), delicia 'corner beam supporting a
roof (Vitr.+); elix, -cis [m.] 'furrow in a com field for draining off water' (Ov.+);
sublica 'wooden stake or pile' (Naev.+), sublicius 'supported on wooden piles' (esp.
of bridges) (Varro+).
Pit. *lak-i~, aor. *lak-s~, ppp. *lak-to- 'to draw, pull'. It. cognates: maybe Ven.
lag[sto] [3s.pret] '?offered'; O. kellaked as 3s.pf. *ke-le-lak-ed in the analysis of
Untermann 2002:492f.
WH connect *lak- to the root *lakw- of laqueus 'loop, rope'. Whereas *kwt > ct and
*kws > x are unproblematic, the supposed development *kwi > ci (in lacit, deliciae) is
uncertain; one would rather expect -qui-, as in reliquium. Similarly, one would expect
*laquessere and -lequebra. Therefore, we must assume a Pit. stem *lak-. It is possible
to connect this with lacer 'torn, t...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lacruma 'tear' [f. a] (Andr.+)
Variant forms: dacrima (Andr., Paul, ex F.), lacrima (Andr., Naev.). According to EM,
the PL mss. often have -uma; TLL does not give the distribution of -ima and -uma.
Pit. *d(r)(k)akruna-r!
PIE *drk-h (e)kru- 'eye-bitter'. IE cognates: Olr. der, W. deigr 'tear', Hit. ishahru-
2
[n.] 'tear(s), weeping' < *s + *h ehn-'?, Skt. asm-, YAv. asru [pL], Gr. SdKpu, Arm.
2
artasuk' [pL], Lith. asara, OHG zahar, ToB akruna [obl.pl.] 'tear'.
Many investigators regard the word as a loanword from Greek SaKpupcc / 5aicpujia
'tear'. EM give the following arguments for this view: if the form were genetically
related to the Greek form, i.e., Pit. *dakru-mn, one would expect Latin *dacrumen.
The suffix -ma is not productive in Latin, and there are no examples of a suffixation
-u-ma Latin. Lat. -ma only occurs in flamma < * flag-ma, where it is found directly
after the root, as expected from a PIE point of view (Gr. cpXoypog). But Hamp 1972
points out that Greek SaKpvpa / SaKpupa itself is relatively recent (unattested before
the fifth century), and it never becomes the normal word.for 'tear' in Greek (which is
Saicpuov). If the Latin word was inherited, the origin is still problematic: PIE
*drk-h (e)kru- + -mo-? To explain -ma, Hamp 1972: 296 suggests an original n.pl.
2
*dlakruna, in which *n assimilated to u to give *dlakruma. To me, this assimilation
and initial *dl- seem unlikely. A good alternative would be to suppose a distant
dissimilation of *d-n to *d-m, thus *dakmna > *dakruma; compare the reverse in
*temabrae > tenebrae. Obviously, this solution is speculative. For the IE words in
initial *d~, the solution proposed by Kortlandt 1985b is attractive: the more archaic
form *h2ekru- has been replaced by the compound, or perhaps syntagm, *drk-h2kru-
'eye-bitter'. The plural to this word must have been *(drk-)h ekru-n-h2 which is
2 )
reflected in ToA obl.pl. akrunt, ToB obl.pl. akruna and Lat. dacmma.
Bibl.: WH I: 746, E...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lacus, -us 'lake' [m. u] (P1.+)
Derivatives: lacuna 'hollow, pit, pond', lacunar, -aris [n.] 'a panel in a panelled
ceiling' (Cic.+).
Pit. *laku~.
PIE *lok-u- 'lake'. IE cognates: Olr. loch < PIE *lok-u-; Bret, lagen 'small lake';
Gr. Mkkoq 'pond' < *lk-u-o-, OCS loky, gen.sg. lokbve 'puddle, pool, reservoir' [f. «]
, SCr. Vokva < *lok-uH-; OE lagu < *loku-.
Lat. lacunar apparently means 'which contains hollows'. Its meaning renders it
comparable to laqueatus, but this will be a coincidence. The a in lacus can be
understood as the product of unrounding after a velarized [i ], cf. Schrijver 1991: 475
and lanius.
Bibl.: WH I: 747f., EM 337, IEW 653, Schrijver 1991: 475, Meiser 1998: 84.
laqueus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: laedo, -ere 'to injure, damage' [v. Ill; laesi, laesum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: elidere 'to crush, force out' (P1.+), allidere 'to strike against, crush'
(Acc.+), illldere 'to injure by crushing, beat' (Vairo+).
IEW connects some Greek and Baltic words. The connection with Gr. Aicrrpov 'spade,
shovel', however, is very uncertain. The Baltic words (Latv. list, Is. lidu 'to clear
(land)', Lith. lydymas, lyditnas 'clearance') have been connected with Lith. leisti 'to
let' < PIE *lid- by Fraenkel 1955-1965. Hence, no certain cognates remain.
Bibl.: WH I; 749, EM 337, IEW 652.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: laevus 'left' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: laeva 'the left hand; the left' (PI.+).
Pit. Vaiwo-.
PIE *Ieh i-uo- or *lh ei-uo- 'left'. IE cognates: Gr. Xai(p)o<^ 'left', OCS lev*, Ru.
2 2
levyj, SCr. lijevi 'id.', ToB laiwo 'lassitude' (< *-ueh -).
2
The original meaning may have been 'curved', although this can hardly be considered
proven by the quotation from Servius: laevi (sc. boves) quorum cornua ad terram
spectant. Steinbauer (apud Stiiber 2006: 68) proposes to derive *leh iuo- (if this was
2
the original form) from the root *leh - 'to hide' that we find in Lat. lated. The left
2
hand would be called 'the hidden hand', similarly to the 'shaded' hand that can be
reconstructed for scaevus. If the original meaning indeed was 'curved', we may look
for a root *lh2(-i)~ that fits this meaning, but none is immediately convincing. LIV has
a root *leih - 'to stop, stop doing', and also *leh - 'to hide'. The PIE preform of
2 2
laevus is reconstructed as *lh eiuo- by Schrijver 1991 on account of the accentuation
2
of the Slavic forms (thus also Derksen 2008: 275); but for Latin, Greek and Toch.,
*leh i-uo- would also work.
2
Bibl.: WH I: 750f., EM 338, IEW 652, Schrijver 1991: 203. lated, scaevus

IE cognates: Gr. XaXeco 'to talk, chat, prattle', Xakoq 'chattering', A,aXia 'talk'; Lith.
laludti 'to babble', NHG latten.
Onomatopoeic, reduplicated imitation of a baby's sound.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lama 'marshy place, bog' [f. a\ (Enn.+)
IE cognates: Lith. loma 'hollow, valley, plot, lump', Latv. lama 'hollow, pool', SCr.
lam (dial.) 'knee-joint, underground passage', Bulg. lam 'pit, (dial.) quarry'.
In theory, Latv. lama and Latin lama may both go back to *leh -mo-, but the isolated
2
position of lama and the possibility that the Baltic words derive from the root *lem-
'to break' render the connection rather uncertain.
Bibl.: WH I: 753, EM 338, IEW 653f, Schrijver 1991: 142.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lambo, -ere 'to lick' [v. Ill; pf. Iambi?, ppp. lambitum] (Lucil.+)
Derivatives: lamberare 'to beat, defeat' (PL, Paul, ex F.)?
Pit. *lamb~.
PIE *lh -m-P- 'to lick'. IE cognates: Gr. Xa<pt)Ooco 'to devour', lanzo) 'to lick',
2
Wnj/eiv, Xayai; Arm. lap'el 'to lick; Alb. lap 'to lick up water'; Lith. lapenti 'to
absorb greedily' (of swines), Ru. lopat' 'to gobble up', Bulg. lapam 'to eat greedily',
OHG la/fan {luof) 'to lick', leffil 'spoon', OE lapian 'to drink'.
Lamberare occurs in PL in the expression med me ludo lamberds 'you lambera- me at
my own game' Ps. 743. In Lucil 585, lamberat is plq.pf. Unlike for labium, where
one might reconstruct a (non-IE?) root *lb-, the correspondences seem to require a
root *lh b- if it is reconstructed for PIE. In fact, the word is so widespread that a PIE
2
origin seems likely. I posit a PIE form *lh P- (*P being any labial stop). Since the
2
labials do not regularly correspond (*b in Latin and Gm., *p in Alb., BS1. and
probably Greek,/?1 in Arm.), these words probably were onomatopoeic..
Bibl.: WH I: 753f., EM 338, IEW 651, Leumann 1977: 551, Schrijver 1991: 222,
Meiser 1998: 214. labium

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lamenta, -orum 'wailing, groans' [n. o] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: lamenta 'wailing' (Pac.), lamentdri 'to (be)wail, lament' (P1.+),
lamentatio 'wailing' (P1.+), lamentarius 'dealing in lamentation' (PL); latrare 'to
bark, bay (of dogs)' (P1.+), latratus, -us 'barking' (Acc.+), oblatratrix 'female yapper,
shrew' (PL).
Pit. *ldmnto- 'howling', *ldtro- 'barking'.
PIE *leh -mn-to- 'howling, crying', *leh -tro- 'barking'. IE cognates: Skt. rayati
2 2
'barks', YAv. gatfro.raiiant- 'shouting songs' (if from PIE *L); Arm. lam 'to weep,
bewail'; Lith. loti, Is. loju, OCS lajati, Is. lajg 'to bark, scold'; Go. lailoun 'they
scolded'. Uncertain is Gr. Xateiv 'to resound' (Hsch.).
Derived from a verb *la(-je)~ 'to wail'. Since Lat. lam- can reflect *lasm~, Schrijver
argues that lamentum can either be connected with IE reflexes of PIE *leh 'to wail,
r
weep' (as PIE *lh,-s-m-) or with PIE *leh - 'to bark, howl' (as *leh -m- or *lh -s-m-).
2 2 2
Since there is no indication for an *-s- having been part of this formation, I prefer the
connection with the other IE words as *leh -mn-to-. The same root has also yielded
2
Lat. Idtrdre, as a derivative of *latro- 'barking'.
Bibl.: WH I: 754f., EM 339,344, IEW 650f., Schrijver 1991: 142,170f, LIV I.*leh -.
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lamina 'thin sheet of metal' [f. a\ (P1.+; also lammina, lamna)
Pit. *stlamen-l
The only serious etymology offered is a connection with latus 'wide' < *stlatos.
Bibl.: WH I: 755, EM 339, IEW 1018f. — latus?
ISna 'wool' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: lanaris 'woolly' (Varro), landrius 'wool-' (P1.+), laneus 'woollen'
(P1.+); lanitia 'wool (as an article)' (Lab.), lanugo, -inis 'down, first hair' (Pac.+);
lanificium 'the working of wool' (PI.+), laniger 'wool-bearing' (Enn.+).
Pit. *wlana-.
PIE *h ulhi-neh - 'wool' (lit. 'plucked material'). IE cognates: Olr. olann, W.
2 2
gwlan, Hit. hulana-, CLuw. *hulana/i- [c.], Hit. huliia- 'wool'; Skt. urna-, Av.
varana- 'wool', Gr. Xfjvoq [n.] (« *h ulhpneh -, or < *h ulhno-), OPr. wilna 'skirt',
2 2 2 r
Lith. vilna 'wool', OCS vlbna, Ru. volna (dial.) / volna (dial.), SCr. vitna, Go. wulla,
OHG wolla, OIc. ull 'wool'.
The connection of lana with vellus and velld was rejected by Schrijver 1991, but is
retained by Meiser 1998. According to Kloekhorst 20Q8: 357f., the appurtenance of
the Hittite words is uncertain. * f
Bibl.: WH I: 756f., EM 339, IEW 1139, Schrijver 1991: 179-181, Sihler 1995: 103,

langueC 'to be sluggish or faint' [v. II] (Acc.+)
Derivatives: languor 'faintness, exhaustion' (P1.+), languescere 'to grow weak, fall
ill' (Lucr.+), languidus 'faint, exhausted' (Acc.+).
Pit. *(s)Iting-u-.
PIE *sl-n-g-u-_;.'weak, faint'? IE cognates: Skt. slaksnd- 'slippery, meagre, thin' (if
from *slaks-)\ Gr. Xaycri<jj 'to release', Xaycipoq 'slack, emaciated, thin', Xayavov 'thin
cake', Xayvoq 'lascivious, voluptuous', *Xayo<; (*Xa£,) 'slack, thin' in Xayovec; pi. f.
(m.) 'the hollows on the side, the flanks', Xay&q 'hare' (< *Xay(o)-®[uo]-o^ 'with
slack ears'); Xayya^co 'to slacken'; OIc. slab-, OS slac, OE slcec 'weak, floppy', MLG
lak\ ToA slakkar 'sad', ToB slakkare 'darting, tremulous'.
The basis was probably an adj. *lang-u(o)~ 'faint, weak', a nasalized variant of the
root *la...

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lanius 'butcher' [m. o\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: laniarium 'butcher's shop' (Vairo), laniena 'id.' (P1.+), lanienus 'of a
butcher' (Varro), laniare 'to wound savagely, cut up' (Quad.+); lanista 'trainer of
gladiators' (Cic.+).
Pit. *lanio- 'breaker'.
PIE *h lomH-io-. IE cognates: Olr. rolaimethar 'to dare' < *lamie/o- < *lmH-ie-,
3
W. Uafasu 'id.', Co. lauasos 'to be allowed'; Gr. vcoXejia; 'without pause' < *h lem-?;
3
Lith. lemti 'to decide, determine', OCS lomiti, Ru. lomit' 'to break'; OHG lam 'lame'
< *bm(H)-.
Leumann explains the suffix of laniena from dissimilation of *lani-ina, whereas WH
regard it as Etruscan. Since lanista is explained as Etruscan by the Romans, WH
regard the whole word family lani- as Etruscan. This seems somewhat rash to me. A
connection with *lem(H)~ 'to break' is semantically attractive, viz. lanius as 'the
breaker (of bones)', cf. Dutch beenhouwer 'butcher'. A development *lomio- >
*lanio- can be regarded as the same unrounding after I- which Schrijver 1991: 475
posits for lacus 'lake'. Since m generally has a rounding influence on a preceding
vowel (cf. later *em > om), it follows that *lomio- became *lonio- before the
unrounding took place.
Bibl.: WH I: 759f., EM 340, Leumann 1977: 54, Stuber 1998: 135, Schumacher
2004: 446f., LIV *lemH-.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lanx, -cis 'metal dish, tray' [f. k] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: lancula 'plate' (Varro+).
Lanx is connected by WH and IEW with Greek words meaning 'crooked, bent'
(kotpq 'bent, crooked, slanted', ^ixP10^ 'slanted'), and with Gr. XEKO^ [n.], XEKIC;,
XEKOIVT] 'dish, pot, pan'. A meaning 'crooked' is not immediately convincing for
'plate' (one would rather expect 'flat'), and the Latin vocalism is difficult to explain
(though not impossible). EM's explanation of a Mediterranean loanword for the
object 'plate' seems more likely.
Bibl.: WH I: 761, EM 340, IEW 307-309.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lapis, -dis 'stone, pebble' [m. d] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: lapideus 'of stone, stony' (P1.+), lapidarius 'of stone-cutting' (PI-+),
lapiddsus 'stony' (Varro+), lapillus 'small stone' (Varro+); lapicida 'stone-cutter'
(Varro+), lapicidinae [f.pl.] (Cato+), lapidicinae [f.pl.] (Varro+) 'stone-quarries'.
Pit. *la/eped~. It. cognates: U. vapere [loc.sg.], vapefem, uapefe [acc.pl. + -en] ,
uapersus [abl.pl.], uapersusto [abl.pl. + -to] 'stone seat'. Uncertain: SPic. vepeti,
vepetin, vepeten, iepeten, vepetin [loc.sg. + -en] 'monument?' < *-eto~.
IE cognates: Gr. Izms; [n.] 'bare rock, mountain', lenaq, -aboq [f.] 'limpet', Xmadeq
'molluscs which stick to rocks' (Hsch.).
Probably a Mediterranean loanword of the structure *lVpVd~.
Bibl.: WH I: 761, EM 340f., IEW 678, Schrijver 1991: 486, Untermann 2000: 823f.,
838.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: laridum 'bacon' [n. o] (P1.+; Lucil.+ lardum)
The etymology as *lajes-idos (see s.v. largus) would normally yield *laeridus (cf.
Meiser 1998: 88). The Attic adj. Xdpivoq 'fatted, fat' may well be derived from Xotpo^
(II.) 'delicious, sweet', sup. Xapdrcotoi;. Beekes (fithc.) proposes a base *Xa(F)apo<; or
*Xa(f)epoi;, and a possible connection with COTOX-OOCO 'to enjoy' < *lh 2 u-. It seems
likely that Latin laridum is a loan from Greek *larinos with suffix substitution
(assimilation of nasal n to oral d because of r?) or from a Greek dialect form with a
different suffix.
Bibl.: WH I: 764, EM 342, IEW 652. -> largus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: larix, -cis 'larch-tree' [f. k\ (Vitr.+)
Loanword from an unknown language, with the frequent plant suffix *-i/ek-. The
suggestion by WH that the word continued PIE *dr-u- 'tree' in the donor language is
gratuitous.
Bibl.: WH I: 765, EM 342, IEW 214-217, Leumann 1977: 375.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: larua 'evil spirit, demon' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: laruatus 'possessed by evil spirits' (P1.+).
Pit. *Ias-Vwa?
Schrijver assumes that *Cu was realized as Cu(w) after a long vowel in VOLat. and
OLat., and as Cw after a short vowel. If this is correct, larua can reflect earlier
*lar/s-Vw- or *lar/s-w~. A form *las- could be connected with Lar, Laris 'tutelary
god', which is quite attractive semantically.
Bibl.: WH I: 766, EM 342, IEW 654, Schrijver 1991: 294. — Lar

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lasclvus 'playful, unrestrained' [adj. o/a] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: lasctvia 'play, fun, wantonness' (P1.+), lascTvibundus 'frisky' (PL),
lascmre 'to frisk, play' (Lucil.+), lasclvolus 'playful' (Laev.).
Pit. *lasko~.
PIE *lh s-ko- 'desirous'. IE cognates: Olr. lainn 'eager' < *lasni- < *lh s-n-; Gr.
2 2
AiXcriopai 'to desire strongly' < *li-las-ie~, Xnviq 'a Bacchante'; Lith. loksnus
'sensitive' < *leh s-n-, Ru. lasyj 'greedy, eager, affectionate' < *leh s-o-.
2 2
Derivative of a stem *lasko-, which can be regarded as the phonetic reflex of the PIE
preform since Schrijver 1991. A connection with lacio 'to pull' is possible (*lak-sko-,
thus Leumann p. 341), but is semantically less straightforward than with PIE *lb s-
2
'to desire'.
Bibl.: WH I: 1661, EM 342, IEW 654, Leumann 1977: 190, 341, Schrijver 1991:
165f, LIV ?*Ias-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lassus 'tired, weary' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: lassitudd 'tiredness' (P1.+); delassare 'to tire out' (P1.+).
Pit. *lasso~.
PIE *lhid-to- 'tired'. IE cognates: Gr. X^SEIV 'to get/be tired' (Hsch.), Alb. lodhem
'to be tired' (< *led-), Go. leian 'to let' < *leh,d- lats 'slow' < *lhjd-o-.
;
The PIE root is formed with a ^-enlargement to *Ihi- 'to let'. The reflex * lad-to- can
be explained from *IHC- > *taC- or from *RHDC- > *RaDC
Bibl.: WH I: 7671, EM 342, IEW 666, Schrijver 1991: 137, 166, LIV *leh,d-.
lenis, letum
lateO 'to hide, shelter, be hidden' [v. II; pf. latui] (P1.+)
Derivatives: latebra 'hiding-place, lair' (P1.+), latebricola [m.] 'one who skulks in
concealment' (PI.), latebrdsus ' secret, hidden' (P1.+); latibulare 'to lie in hiding'
(Laev.+); latitare 'to be/remain in hiding' (P1.+); deliti/escere 'to go into hiding'
(P1.+), oblitescere 'to become hidden' (Varro+).
Pit. *late-.
PIE *lh -to- 'hidden'. IE cognates: Gr. Xavflavco, Xfji3co, aor. AcuMv 'to be hidden',
2
Mcdpn. 'secretly' < *l(e)h -dh-; T^TODI Irjito 'he forgot' (Hsch.) < *leh -; OCS
2 2
lajati, OCz. lakati, USorb. lakac 'to lie in wait for'.
Latere represents a stative verb in *-e- derived from *latos 'hidden'. Since the -e- of
latebra was short (see Serbat 1975: 62f.), it is best explained by analogy with
tenebrae', if the noun were deverbal, one would expect * latebra.
Bibl.: WH I: 768f., EM 343, IEW 651, Schrijver 1991:166, LIV 3.*Ieh -, laevus
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: latex, -icis 'water, liquid' [m. k] (Acc.+)
If latex was borrowed from Gr. Xara^, -ayoq [f.] 'drop of wine', the suffix could
have been changed to -ex to file the word with the other nouns in -ex. EM rightly
remark that the semantic shift from the specific 'drop of wine' in Greek to a general
'water, liquid' in Latin seems strange. It might be conceivable in the case of a
strongly metaphorical use of 'drop of wine' for 'water'. Note that the use of latex is
mainly poetic.
Bibl.: WH I: 770, EM 343, IEW 654f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: latus 'broad, wide' [adj. o/a] (P1-+)
Derivatives: latitudo 'breadth, broadness' (Cato+); dilatare 'to make wider, spread'
(Varro+); stlatta Targe cargo-boat' (Gel., Paul, ex F.), stlatarius 'conveyed by a
stlatta' (Enn.+).
Pit. *sldto~.
IE cognates: Olr. corrsla* 'to go away'.
WH and Meiser 1998 posit a form *stlH-to- 'spread' to Gr. oieXAto 'to prepare,
dispatch', OCS stblati ' to spread', but this root is now reconstructed without a final
laryngeal (LIV: *stel- 'to place'). Alternatively, one could connect latus with PCI.
*-sla- 'to go away' (< pr. *sla-(ie/o)~) as attested in Olr. consla. In that case, the
spelling stla- in Latin could be interpreted as in the case of lis 'dispute' (see s.v.),
where *sl- yielded I- but was retained as stl- in archaisms. Latin *slato~ would have
developed from 'going apart, spreading' to 'wide'.
Bibl.: WH I: 772, EM 344, IEW 1018f.* Meiser 1998: 112, Schumacher 2004: 587f.
—»latus

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: latus, -eris 'side, flank' [n. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: lateralis 'of the side of the body' (Lucil.+), latusculum 'a side'
(Lucr.+), lateramen 'pottery' (Lucr.).
An original >s-stem to a root *lat~. No good etymology available. Semantically, it is
attractive to connect Olr. sliss, W. ystlys 'side' (< *s(t)lissu~), maybe also W. llys-,
Bret, lez- 'half, somewhat', if Schrijver 1995: 439 is correct in deriving these from
lenited *hlissu-. But while the Celtic forms could go back to *s(t)}t-tu-, such a
preform would give *s(t)oltu- in Latin. Lat. latus might be derived from a preform
*lot-os- (cf. lacus, lanius < *lo-), but this would still be an irregular ablaut form next
to PCI. *stlt~. Another alternative is to derive latus from lateo 'to be hidden', if latus
originally referred to the 'hidden' (invisible) part of a body (cf. the discussion s.v.
laevus). The s-stems were marginally productive still in a more recent period of the
prehistory of Latin, cf. pondus, -eris to pended, and maybe vellus, -eris to vello
(Stuber 2002: 57).
Bibl.: WH I: 772, EM 344, IEW 1018f., Schrijver 1991: 486, Untermann 2000: 827.
—»• later

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: laus, -dis 'praise' [f. d] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: laudare 'to praise' (Naev.+); allaudare 'id.' (PI.), allaudabilis
'praiseworthy' (P1.+), collaudare 'to eulogize' (Naev.+).
Pit. *lau(V)d-.
IE cognates: Go. awiliup 'song of praise', OHG Hod, OE leop [n.] 'song', OIc. Ijod
'strophe, song' < PIE *leu-t-1; Go. liupon 'to sing', awiliudon 'to sing praise', OHG
liudon, OE leopian, OIc. Ijoda 'to sing'.
The only connection put forward is with Gm. *leup-a- 'song' < *leu-t-. If cognate at
all, laus must reflect *lou- cf. Schrijver. Vine 2006a: 238, who assumes *ou >
*au in PIE pretonic position, sketches a possible scenario: PIE *lou-o- 'praising' >
Pit. *lawo- could have given rise to a d-stem *law-ed- 'song of praise' (or
analogically changed a stem *lou-ed- to *lawed-).
Bibl.: WH I: 776, EM 346, IEW 683, Schrijver 1991: 444.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lavo, -are 'to wash, bathe' [v. I; pf. lavT, ppp. lautus, lotus (P1.+), lavatum] (Naev.+);
from OLat. onwards, pr. lavere 'to wash (sth./so.)' beside lavare 'to wash oneself
Derivatives: latrina 'washing-place' (Varro+), lavatid 'washing' (P1.+), lavandaria,
-drum, [n.pl.] 'dirty linen' (Lab. apud Gel.), labrum 'large basin' (Cato+), lavabrum
'bath-tub' (Lucr.), labellum 'bowl, basin' (Cato+); lotium 'urine' (Cato+), lotiolentus
'defiled with urine' (Titin.); abluere (abluT, ablutum) 'to wash off, cleanse' (Pac.+),
abluvium 'inundation' (Lab.+), delubrum 'temple, shrine' (P1.+), diluere 'to dissolve,
dilute' (P1.+), dTluvies 'flood' (Lucr.), diluviare 'to flood' (Lucr.), eluere 'to wash out,
wash clean' (P1.+), eluacrum 'wash-tub' (Cato), elutriare 'to put into a vat or bath'
(Lab.+), eluvies 'the washing away' (Lucil.+), illo/utus 'unwashed, dirty' (P1.+),
illuvies 'dirtiness, filth' (P1.+), interluere 'to flow between; wash during' (P1.+),
malluviae [f.pl.] 'water in which the hands have been washed' (Fest., Paul, ex F.),
pelluviae [f.pl.] 'water in which the feet are washed' (Fest., Paul, ex F.), pollitbrum
'wash-basin' (Andr.+), proluere 'to wash away, wash clean' {?{.+), proluvies 'flood'
(Lucr.+).
Pit. pr. *lowa~, intr.pr. *lowa-e~. It. cognates: U. vutu [3s.ipv.II], anderuomu [anter
+ 3s.ipv.II.ps.] 'to wash' < *louatod, *louamnod.
PIE *(le-)louh - 'to wash' [pr.], *louh -tro- 'wash-basin'. IE cognates: Olr. loathar,
3 3
Bret. laouer 'basin' < PCI. *louatro~, Gr. Xoeco, Xouto 'I wash', H. X,oetp6v, Att.
Xourpov, Dor. Xcoipov 'bath' (Hsch.); Arm. loganam 'to bathe', lowanam 'wash', aor.
logac'e-1 lowac'e-\ OAlb. sb. laa 'to wash', OIc. laudr [n.] 'lye, foam', OE leapor
'soap-foam'.
The pr. lavere shows PIE *louH-C~ > Pit. *lowa- > *lawa-. The same verb is also
contained in the compounds in -/were. Meiser suggests an original reduplicated
*le-louh, which seems semantically attractive. Schrijver 1991: 397 suggests that
r
lavare reflec...

Derivatives: laxare 'to make larger; undo, relax' (Lucr.+), collaxare 'to make loose'
(Lucr.+), dilaxdre 'to stretch apart' (Lucil.), relaxare 'to loosen, relax' (Varro+).

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lego, -ere 'to gather, collect; to read' [v. Ill; pf. legi, ppp. ledum] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: ledor 'reader' (Sis.+); legid 'legion, group of supporters' (Naev.+);
legulus 'a picker' (Cato+); dentilegus 'who collects teeth' (PL), sacrilegus 'who robs
sacred property, sacrilegious person' (PI.+), sortilegus 'soothsayer' (Varro+);
legumen 'leguminous plant, pulse' (Varro+); ablegmina 'partes extorum quae dis
immolabant' (Paul, ex F.)\ colligere 'to gather, collect' (Naev.+), deligere 'to pick
off, pick out' (P1.+), diligere 'to love, hold dear' (P1.+), diligenter 'carefully' (PL+),
diligeniia 'carefulness' (PL+), dilectus, -us 'recruitment, selection' (Varro+), eligere
'to select' (P1.+), electilis 'of special quality' (P1.+), elegans 'careful, delicate' (P1.+),
elegantia 'choosiness, refinement' (P1.+), intellegere 'to understand' (P1.+),
intellegentia 'keenness' (Ter.+), neglegere 'to ignore, neglect' (P1.+), neglegentia
'carelessness' (P1.+), negledus, -us 'neglect' (Ter.+), per/llegere 'to attract; read
over' (Lex XII+), praeliganeus 'made from fruit picked before the crop' (Cato),
relegere 'to pick up again' (Cato+), seligere 'to weed out, select' (Enn.+), sublegere
'to steal away, appoint' (P1.+), translegere 'to read out to so. else' (PL),.
Pit. *leg-e-, aor. *leg-, ppp. *leg-to~, *(-)leg-a-. It. cognates: Pael. lexe, Marr. leexe
llexel [2p.pr.], pelegief [2s.ipv.?] 'to read?', but the endings remain unexplained.
Possibly O. leginum [acc.sg.], leginei [dat.sg.] maybe 'host'.
PIE pr. *leg-e/o- 'to collect', ppp. * leg-to-. IE cognates: Gr. Xsytu, aor. IXefyt 'to
collect, count; speak', Xoyoi; [m.] 'account, word'; Alb. mb-ledh 'to collect'. Possibly,
also Olr. legaid, -lego* 'to melt (tr. and intr.), destroy, extinguish' < *leg-a~, MW
dilein 'to destroy, chase away' < *df-leg-nd-, go-leith 'to fear, avoid' < *uo-leg-tV~;
MW lleith 'wet, moist' < *leg-to~. The evidence for a separate PIE root 'to trickle'
(thus LIV) is extremely weak...

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lemurgs, -um 'evil spirits of the dead' [m.pl. r] (Hor.+)
Derivatives: lemurit (Varro apud Non.) 'lemures', Lemuria, -drum [n.pl.] 'festival to
appease the spirits of the dead' (Ov.).
The long u in Ovid can be explained as metrical lengthening in the four short
syllables of *le-mu-ri-a (Leumann 1977: 115). A connection of lemures with Gr.
Xapupoc; 'voracious', Xapia [f.]-'name of a man-eating monster, of a shark', Xapia
'crevices' (Hsch.) is formally impossible, unless Latin has *lem- and Gr. *lm~. The
further connection with Baltic and Celtic forms made by IEW (Lith. lemoti 'to lick',
W. Uef, Bret, lenv 'voice, cry') is gratuitous. In view of the meaning and the imperfect
formal correspondence, it is more likely that Greek and Latin have both borrowed a
non-IE (e.g. Anatolian/Etruscan) denomination for these spirits.
Bibl.: WH I: 781, EM 351, IEW 675, Schrijver 1991: 218.
l£nis 'soft, mild, easy, calm, moderate' [adj. i] (P1.+)
Derivatives: lemre 'to appease, mitigate' (P1.+), lenitas 'mildness' (Ter.+), lenitudo
'id.' (Pac.+); delenTre 'to soothe down, bewitch' (P1.+), delemmentum 'blandishment,
consolation' (Afran.+), delenificus 'soothing' (P1.H-).
Pit. *leni-.
PIE *lehn(o/i)- 'lazy, slow'. IE cognates: Lith. lenas, Latv. Iqns, l$ns 'lazy', OCS
r
lent, Ru. Ijanoj (dial.) / lenoj (dial.), OCz. leny, SCr. tijen 'lazy, slow' < BS1. *le?nos
i *lenos< PIE *leh(e)no-.
r
The semantic proximity of 'soft, calm' to 'lazy, slow' is close enough to retain the
connection with BS1. *lenos 'lazy'. The i-stem in Latin has apparently replaced an
earlier o-stem (as in hilaris / hilarus, and viridis for *viridus). It is also possible that
lenis was built on an original rc-stem *lehn-, since the BS1. hesitation between acute
r

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lfins, -endis 'nit' [f. d] (Plinius+)
Pit. *dlind-1
PIE *dk(o)n-i-d- 'nit'. IE cognates: Olr. sned [f.], W. nedd, nedden, Co. nedhen
[sing.], Bret, nez, nezenn < PCI. *snida- 'nit'; Gr. KoviSeq 'eggs of lice, nits' [f.pl.],
Arm. anic 'louse' < *Hnid- < *knid-?; Alb. (Geg) thenf< *kon-id-; Lith. glinda, Latv.
gnida, Ru. gnida, SCr. gnjida, Sin. gnida 'nit' < BS1. *gni?da? < *knid- < PIE *knid-;
OE hnitu [£], OHG (h)niz 'nit' < *knid-.
The IE cognates differ in anlaut to such an extent that the reconstruction of one PIE
preform is very difficult. Gr. and Alb. reflect *konid~, while Gm., Slavic, and, with
some special pleading, also Armenian, reflect *knid~. These two forms obviously
seem related. In Baltic, we find a second nasal after *i, and (probably) dissimilation
of the first *n to *l. Like Baltic, Latin also has a postvocalic nasal, but the first nasal
has disappeared. A problematic feature is the vowel e, since PIE *e is often raised to i
in Latin before nC, but *i never appears to be lowered in front of nasals. Finally,
Celtic *snid- is comparable to Latin in that it has a dental obstruent in front of -n-
while Latin has /-, which also has a dental point of articulation. In order to explain the
Italo-Celtic forms from the stem *knid~, one might assume influence from other
nouns for small insects (for lens one may think of PIE *luHs- 'louse' - unattested in
Italic), but, alternatively, there could also be a phonetic reason. For instance, if PIE
had an initial consonant cluster, e.g. *dknid-, this could have been dissimilated in
various ways: Italic *dkni-n-d- > *dklind- > *dlind- > regular *lind-\ Celtic *dknid- >
*dnid- > *snid-. Armenian anic, with irregular an- < *kn~, may then reflect *dknid- >
*dnid- > *h nid- > *anid- (but one would expect *enid~). The noun may be related
}
with PIE *knid- 'to scratch' as reflected in Gr. KVI^CO 'to prick, irritate', OIc. hnita 'to
thrust'.
Bibl.: WH I: 783, EM 351, IEW 436f., 608, LIV *kneid-....

de Vaan Latin Etymology: lens, -tis 'lentil plant, lentil' [f. (m.) /] (Cato+; acc.sg. lentim, abl.sg. lend, nom.pl.
lends)
IE cognates: Gr. Xai3upo<; 'pulse, chickling', OCS l^sta, Ru. Ijac, OHG linsa 'lentil'.
The similarity between Slavic, Gm. and Latin seems too great to be coincidental, but
a common preform cannot be reconstructed. Like other agricultural terms, 'lentil'
may have been borrowed from a non-IE language in Europe.
Bibl.: WHI: 783, EM 351.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lentus 'pliant, flexible; tough; sticky; slow' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives; lentere 'to proceed slowly' (Lucil.).
Pit * lento- 'soft'.
PIE * lento- 'soft, smooth, pliant'. IE cognates: Lith. lenta 'board, plank' < *lent~;
Ru. lut (dial.) 'bast, bark of a linden', Ru. luta (dial.) 'linden', Cz. hit (dial.) 'bast',
Po. tyt (dial.) 'twig, switch' < PS1. *lgtb < *lont-\ OHG lindi, ODan. lind 'soft, mild',
OIc. linnr 'snake' < PGm. *lenpa-\ OIc. lind f. 'linden, linden-wood', OE lind(e) ,
OHG linta, lintea, linda (because of the soft wood of this tree).
Bibl.: WH I: 784, EM 35If., IEW 677.
lepOs, -Oris 'charm, grace' [m. r] (PI.+)
Derivatives: lepidus 'charming' (P1.+), illepidus 'lacking grace' (P1.+), perlepide
'very charmingly' (PI.); maybe lapit 'causes pain or grief (Pacuv. and Paul, ex F.).
Pit. *lep-i-po- 'peeled; charming'?; *lop-i- *to peel'?
PIE *lep-os- [n.] and/or *le/op-i- 'peel, rind'. IE cognates: Gr. XCTCO 'to peel', XETCTOC;
'peeled, unveiled; thin, delicate', Xsmq, Xoitic, 'rind, peel', lomoc, [m.] 'scale, rind',
Xmpoq 'scaly, coarse'; Alb. lape 'rag, leaf; Lith. lapas 'leaf, Latv. lapa 'leaf, Ru.
lepest 'petal'; Lith. lepiis 'weak, soft'.
These words disappear from usage after Cicero. Whether lepos was the derivational
basis for lepidus, or vice versa, is uncertain. Stiiber 2002: 67 opts for the second
solution because of the meanings; The rare form lapit could only belong here if from
*lopit (cf. lanius maybe from *lomio~). The semantics; could then be interpreted as
'peels off, unveils' > 'affects, damages'. The semantic connection with the Gr. words
is not compelling, but the change in meaning of Gr. Xe7n:6<; from 'peeled' to 'refined'
renders the change of Lat. lepos from 'what has been peeled' to 'refinement'
conceivable.
Bibl.: WH I: 785, EM 352, IEW 678, Stiiber 2002: 67, LIV *lep-.

Pit. *leto-.
PIE *lehto- [n.]. IE cognates: OCS letb 'it is possible, allowed' [f.], Ru. let' (arch.)
r
'it is possible, allowed' < *lehti-; OCS leto 'summer, year', Ru. leto 'summer, (pi.)
r
age, years' < PIE *leht6m; OIc. lad, OE Iced 'land', Go. unleds 'poor' (Schaffner
r
2001:241).
May be derived from a ppp. *lehto- 'let, let go', substantivized as *lehto- 'letting
r r
%
go' > 'death'. It would then be identical in origin to Slavic *leto- 'summer, year'
(from 'going').
Bibl.: WH I: 787, EM 352, IEW 666, Neri 2007: 32, LIV *leh. lassus, lenis
r
Igvir/laevir 'husband's brother' [m. o (r)] (Paul, ex F.+)
Only attested in glosses, with vacillating spelling lae-1 le-. The latter would allow
for an interpretation as levir, with monophthongization of ae.
Pit. *daiwer.
PIE *deh i-uer- 'husband's brother'. IE cognates: Skt. devar-, Pashto levar, Gr.
2
5ar}p, Arm. taygr, Lith. dieveris / dieveris, Latv. dieveris, Ru. dever', SCr. djever <
BS1. *da?iuer~; OHG zeihhur, OE tdcor 'husband's brother' < PGm. *taikura^7).
Lat. levir is often regarded as a loanword from a non-urban Latin dialect, because of
initial I- and e for *ai. But /- for d- is also found in lingua and lacrima, so there is no
compelling reason to regard this as non-urban. Final -vir instead of -ver is probably
due to influence of Lat. vir 'man'. In theory, the PIE noun *deh i-uer- 'husband's
2
brother' could be a derivative of *deh i- 'to distribute', but there is no obvious
2
semantic connection.
Bibl.: WH I: 787f„ EM 352, IEW 179, Leumann 1977: 68, 155, Schrijver 1991: 269.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: levis 'light in weight, slight, weak' [adj. z] (P1.+)
Derivatives: levitds 'lightness' (Lucr.+), levare 'to relieve, lessen, lift up' (P1.+);
sublevdre 'to alleviate, assist' (Varro+); levifidus 'untrustworthy' (PI), levipes
'light-footed' (Var.+), levisomnus 'sleeping lightly' (Lucr.).
Pit. *leyu- » *lexwf-.
PIE *hlegwh-u- > *hjlegh-u- 'light'. IE cognates: Olr. laigiu 'lighter, poorer', MW
(
llei 'less', Skt. raghu- 'fleet, fast', YAv. rsuui- [adj.f.] 'agile, fast, quick', sup.
rsnjistaGr. ekayvc, 'small, little', Alb. lehte, Lith. lengvas 'light', OCS Ibgbkb, Ru.
legkij, Go. leihts, OHG liht, OIc. lettr, ToB lahkjse 'light' < PIE *h,lengwh-to-; Gr.
ckcuppoc, 'light, dexterous, fast, little', OHG lungar 'fast, cheerful' < *h]lngwh-ro-.
There appear to be two PIE forms of the root, *h]l(e)ngwh- and *h,legh-; Latin
continues the latter one. PCI. has *lag-u- (cf. Schrijver 1995: 302-305), which is
difficult to explain from either of these PIE forms, but seems closest to Latin levis.
The shift from *gwh to *gh in (Italo-)Celtic can be due to delabialization in front of
*-u~. The nasalless root would then seem oldest, and the *-n- found in most languages
must be secondary; EIEC explained it from influence of the PIE verb *hilengwh- 'to
move fast, easily' (Skt. ramhate 'hastens', Olr. -ling 'to leap'). Like most w-stem
adjectives, levis was remade into an i-stem in Latin.
Bibl.: WH I: 788, EM 353, IEW 660f„ Sihler 1995: 163, Meiser 1998: 120.

lex, legis law' [Ig] (P1.+)
Derivatives: legare 'to send as an envoy, bequeath' (Lex XII+), legdtus 'envoy,
deputy' (P1.+); legitimus 'legal, legitimate' (Varro+); collega [m.] 'colleague, fellow'
(PI+), collegium 'guild, board' (P1-+); ablegare 'to send away on a mission' (P1.+),
allegare 'to send as a representative, employ' (P1.+), allegatus, -us 'instigation'
(P1.+), delegare 'to appoint, assign a task to', relegare 'to banish' (Cic.+); legerupa
[m.] 'law-breaker' (PL), legerupio 'law-breaking' (PL).
Pit. *leg~. It. cognates: Marr. lixs [nom.sg.], O. ligud [abl.sg.], ligis [abl.pl.] 'law' <
*Ieg-; maybe 0. legu '?' if flaws' (Weiss 1993: 22f.); maybe O. llganakdikel
[dat.sg.] name of a goddess; O. ligatus [nom.pl.], ligatuis [dat.pl.] 'envoy', probably
a loanword from Latin.
PIE *leg- 'collection'?
The verb legare and its compounds all have a meaning which involves a 'task,
assignment', and can therefore be interpreted as derivatives of lex Taw'. The Pit. root
noun *leg- 'law' can be interpreted as a 'collection' of rules. Whether the root noun
existed already in PIE is uncertain for lack of precise cognates.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: liber, -brl 'bark of a tree; book' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: libellus 'document, pamphlet' (PI.+), libellio 'bookseller' (Varro+);
maybe Paul, ex F. 73 delibratum, hoc est decorticatum.
Pit. *lufro-.
PIE *lubh~ro- 'leaf, rind'. IE cognates: Olr. luib, Mir. luibh f. 'herb', lub-gort
'garden', Alb. labe 'rind, cork' (< *loub-), OPr. lubbo 'bast, plank, shelf, Lith. Iuba,
Latv. Iuba 'plank, board', Ru. lob 'forehead, brow', gen.sg. Iba, Cz. leb 'skull' < BS1.
*lub-, Lith. luobas 'bast', Latv. luobas 'peel', Ru. lub 'bast' < BS1. *loub-o-; Go.
htbja-leis 'who knows poisons', OIc. lyf[f.] 'medicinal herbs', OE lybb [n.] 'poison,
magic' < *lubh-; Go. laufs [m.], /mf/[n.] 'leaf, foliage', OE leaf, OHG Ioub [n.] < PIE
*loubh-.
Since several IE languages show forms in *lubh- meaning 'bast, leaf, liber has been
reconstructed as *lubro~, and compared with the vacillation in Latin between lubet
and libet, clupeus and clipeus. For liber, however, no form in -u- is attested. Still, for
want of a better alternative, we may surmise that liber is cognate with *lubh- and goes
back to a PIE word or a European word "leaf, rind'.
Bibl.: WH I: 790, EM 354, IEW 690f., Schrijver 1991: 23, LIV ?*leup-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: woman' (substantivized adj.), loifirtato [gen.sg.] 'freedom'; Pael. loufir [nom.sg.]
'free man'; O. luvfreis [gen.sg.] epithet of Iuppiter; maybe U. vufiune, uofwne
[dat.sg.] a deity, often explained as *leuct-i-6no- 'he of the people'.
PIE *hileudh-ero- IE cognates: Gr. eXevflepoq 'free'; Lith. lidudis 'lower people',
CS Ru. ljud 'people', OCS Ijudbje, Ru. Ijiidi [pi.] 'men, people' < IE *h,leuct-o-, -i-,
OHG Hut, pi. liuti, OE leod 'people'.
The spellings loebesum and loebertatem adduced by Varro and Paul, ex F. confirm
the diphthong -oi- found in Faliscan, and show a phonetic change in Latino-Faliscan
of *loufi- > *loifl~. This can be explained as a dissimilation of u to i in front of the
bilabial fricative. Leumann explains libertus as a backformation to libertas on the
model of honestus vs. honestas. The BS1. and Gm. noun *h/leuct- 'people' derives
from a verb *hjleuct- 'to grow'; the deity Liber shows that *h leuct - originally meant
}
'to grow' in Latin too. The derived adj. *hleucf-ero- originally indicated one
t
'belonging to the people', hence 'free' in Latin liber and Gr. eXetn9epo<;. The change
of the pi. liberi to 'children' is explained by Benveniste (1969 I: 324) from legal
terminology, in which the legitimate 'children' of a free couple were denominated as
'free ones'.
Bibl.: WH I: 791-793, EM 355, IEW 684f., Giacomelli 1963: 236, 249f., Benveniste
1969 I: 321-325, Lejeune 1974: 336, Leumann 1977: 61, 335, Meiser 1998: 87,
Untermann 2000: 438f„ 862f., LIV *h,leudb-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: libet 'it is pleasing' [v. II (impers.), pf. libuit / li/ubitum est] (P1.+; the older variant is
lubet)
Derivatives: libere 'to be willing' (PI.), lu/ibens 'pleased, willing' (Andr.+; lub-
mainly in inscr. from outside Rome), lu/ibentia 'pleasure' (P1-+), Libentina 'title of
Venus' (Varro+), lu/ibido [f.] 'desire, lust' (P1.+), libidinitas 'lustfulness' (Lab.);
prolubium 'desire' (Naev.+); allubescit 'is pleasing' (P1.+); Lat. -libet 'any,
whichever' in quilibet 'whoever/whatever you please', etc.
Pit. *luf-e- 'to desire'. It. cognates: O. loufir 'or' [pr.sb.impers.] < *loubh-e/i-r.
PIE *lubh-eh 'to please'. IE cognates: Skt. lobhaya- 'to make crazy', pf. lulobha 'is
r
in disorder', MoP ahiftan 'to be tangled, be hit down, be in love' < Ilr. *l(a)ubh-\ OCS
ljufrb 'sweet, pleasant', Ru. Ijuboj 'any, either' < *leubh-o-, CS ljuby [f.], gen.sg.
ljubovb 'love' < *leubh-uH, OCS ljubo, Ru. Ubo 'or'; OCS ljubiti, Ru. ljubit' 'to love'
< *leubh-; Go. liufs, OHG liob [adj.] 'dear, sweet'.
Original *u has been unrounded between velarized I and b. The suffix of the derived
noun probably was *-edon (from the v. *lub-e-) but it was influenced by cupido. For
the semantics of O. loufir and Lat. -libet 'any', cf. OCS ljubo 'or'. The original
meaning of O. loufir may have been 'is wanted, one wants'.
Bibl.: WH I: 793, EM 367, IEW 683f., Leumann 1977: 367, Meiser 1998: 80,
Untermann 2000: 437, LIV *Ieubh-.
Ebo, -are 'to pour a libation' [v. I] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: libdmentum 'sacrifiacial offering, taste' (Varro+), libum 'sacrificial
cake' (Enn.+); delibare 'to remove, take a small piece from' (Enn.+), delibiitus
'thickly smeared, imbued' (P1.+).
Pit. *leifo- / *loifo- 'sacrificial cake', *lifii~ 'greasy'.
PIE *h Ie/oibh-o- [n.], *h libh -u- [adj.]. IE cognates: Gr. aXeupco 'to anoint', aXeicpap
2 2
'unguent'.

sacrificial cake' to libum. It is uncertain, then, whether the nou...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: libra 'pound (measure of weight) (Lex XII, P1.+); pair of scales (Varro+)' [f. a] (Lex
XII+) :
Derivatives: librare 'to make level, balance' (Cato+), librdriits [adj.] 'of a pound's
weight' (Cato+), libella 'small silver coin; plumb-line' (P1.+); selibra 'half a libra'
(Cato+), simbella 'a coin worth half a libella' (Varro); collibrare 'to measure' (Cato),
deliberare 'to consider carefully, consult' (P1.+); libripens, -ndis 'one who holds the
balance' (Lex XII+).
Pit. Veipra- 'pound'.
Deliberare must be due to influence of liberare 'to free' on *de-librare 'to weigh'.
The weight selibra was probably derived from libra on the model of semodius 'half a
raodius' < *semi-modius. Libella < *librala. Simbella < *sembella < *sembella <
*semibella, with haplology < *semilibella. The Greek coin Mxpa 'name of a Sicilian
coin' was probably borrowed from an Italic language at the stage containing In
theory, an etymon with the PIE suffix *-dhro- is possible, but the root is unidentified.
Of course, a coin may well be a loanword.
Bibl.: WH I: 336,795f., EM 168, 356, Sihler 1995: 139, Untermann 2000: 828.
licet, Dc5re 'it is permitted' [v. II; licitum es/] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: licere 'to fetch (a price)' (P1.+), liceri, licitus sum 'to make a bid' (P1.+),
licitarT 'to make a bid, compete' (P1.+), iicentia 'freedom, licence' (PL+), licentiatus,
-us 'permission' (Lab.); pollicen 'to promise; offer' (P1.+), pollicit ari 'to promise
(assiduously)' (PI+), pollicitdtio 'promise' (PI.+); ilicet 'you may go; that's the end!'
(P1.+), scilicet 'obviously, to be sure' (P1.+), videlicet 'evidently, of course' (P1.+).
Pit. *lik-e- 'to be available, have the value of. It. cognates: O. likltud, licitud
[3s.ipv.II] 'it is permitted' (borrowed from or calqued on Latin); maybe O. XeiKsix
[3s.pr.?] < *lik~e-ti.
The verb licitarT is the frequentative to licet. The form Tlicet < Tre licet contains the
infinitive plus licet. The original meaning of *like- seems to have been...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: licium 'thread, cord' [n. o] (Lex XII, Lucil.+)
Derivatives: bilix, -Tcis 'having a double thread' (Verg.), trilix, •Tcis 'having a triple,
thread' (Verg.+).
Eichner 1995: 68f. interprets the form loidquiod on the Forum cippus as a mistaken
rendering of *loiquiod = *loikwiod abl.sg. of *loik"'io- 'thread'. No IE etymology.

Bibl.: WH 1:798, EM 357, IEW 307-309. oblTquus
liSn, -enis 'spleen' [m. n] (P1.+; nom.sg. lien (Prise.); nom.sg. lien, -enis (PI.) with
iambic shortening)
Derivatives: liendsus 'affected by a disorder of the spleen' (P1.+).
Pit. *li%en-.
PIE *splgh-en, -en- 'spleen'. IE cognates: Olr. selg, MBret. felch, Skt. plihdn-, Av.
spsrazan-, Khot, spuljei 'spleen' < Ilr. *spjfan-, Gr. onXr\v 'spleen', onXnyyva
'intestines', Arm. p'aycaln spleen', Lith. bluznis, Latv. liesa 'id.' < *lielsa, OCS
slezena.
Long -e- is claimed by grammarians (Servius, Priscian) and said to be attested in the
writer Serenus (4th century AD), but the Plautine occurrences of //en(-) can be read
either as two shorts or as a long syllable with synizesis (M. Weiss, p.c.). In Italic, PIE
*sp!gh- was apparently replaced by *(s)lig\ maybe for reasons of taboo; but the
model for introducing is unknown.
Bibl.: Soomer 1914:366, WH I: 799, EM 357f., IEW 987.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lignum 'wood' [n. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: ligneus 'wooden' (P1.+), ligneolus 'id.' (Lucil.+), lignari 'to collect
firewood' (P1.+).
Pit. *legno-.
PIE-*leg(h)-no-.
1 limbus 341
If derived from *leg- 'to collect', lignum must originally have indicated 'wood
collected for firemaking', from the root *leg- 'to collect'. The full grade might have
been adopted from the present. The phonetic change of *-egn- [eyn] > -ign- is regular.
Ritter 2004 prefers to derive lignum from *legh- 'to lie', as 'stray wood'. Both
etymologies seem equally possible.
Bibl.: WH I: 799, EM 358, IEW 658, Sihier 1995:208, Ritter 2004, LIV *leg-. lego

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ligo, -are 'to fasten, bind' [v. I] (Cat.+)
Derivatives: alligare 'to tie, fetter' (P1.+), colligare 'to tie up' (Naev.+), deligare
'id.' (P1-+), obligare 'to tie up, assign, oblige' (P1.+), praeligare 'to tie round, fasten'
(P1.+), religare 'to make fast, tie out of the way' (Andr.+), subligare 'to fasten, gird
up' (Cato+), subligaculum 'sort of loin-cloth' (Varro+); ITctor 'attendant to a
magistrate' (P1.+); religid 'taboo, impediment, sanction; religious practice' (P1.+),
religidsus 'scrupulous, devote, religious' (P1.+).
Pit. *-lig-d'.
PIE *lig- 'to tie'? IE cognates: Alb. lidh 'to tie',
The appurtenance of ITctor and of religid to this verb is uncertain. The fact that the
simplex ligare does not occur before Catullus, all the earlier verbs being compounds,
suggests a basis *lig-e/o- or *leg-e/o~. Semantically, the root *Iig- 'to tie' is a more
likely source. Rather than being a denominal verb, *-lig-d- seems to be of the factitive
type {capere - occupare). Apart from Alb. lidh, which can be explained in various
ways, there are no certain cognates.
Bibl.: WH I: 800, EM 357f.; 569, IEW 668, LIV *leig-.

metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tilium 'lily' [n. o] (Varro+)
Probably a borrowing from an (eastern) Mediterranean language, compare in the first
place Gr. Xeipiov 'lily, narciss'. A similar designation is Coptic hreri, hleli 'lily'.
Bibl.: WHI: 801, EM 358.
lima '(carpenter's) file' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: liyndre 'to rub smooth, polish' (Andr.+).
The etymology.accepted by WH and IEW connects lima with levis 'smooth' and
OHG slim 'mud, slime', slimen 'to polish, rub smooth', from a root *slei~.
Alternatively, it could belong to lino < *h li-n-, hence *h le/oi-mh - (Seldeslachts
2 2 2
2001: 91). But a 'file' itself is not 'smooth', at most it 'makes smooth', which does
not fit the morphology of lima < *-ma~.
Bibl.: WH I: 80If., EM 358fi, IEW 662-664.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: limbus 'ornamental border, fringe' [m. o] (Var.+; Varro also lembus)
Derivatives: limbuldrius 'concerned with making fringes' (PL).
PIE *lemb-o-. IE cognates: Skt. rambate (RV), lambate 'to hang down limply'; OE
(ge)limpan 'to happen, succeed', Eng. limp [adj.] 'limp', MHG lampen, slampen 'to
hang down limply'; ToB lyama, ToA lyam 'sat' [v. pret.].
In view of the phoneme *b, the very specific meaning of limbus and its absence from
the oldest literature, the etymology remains uncertain.
Bibl.: WH I: 802f., EM 359, IEW 655-657, Schrijver 1991: 179, LIV *lembH-.
Umen, -inis 'beam in a doorframe, threshold, lintel' [n. n] (P1.+)
Derivatives: eliminare 'to go outdours, let out' (Enn.+); limes, -itis [m.] 'boundary
strip of land, boundary; road' (Varro+), limitdre 'to define, line off (Varro+),
Umitaris 'of a boundary' (Varro+).
Pit. *lim-en- [n.] 'crossbar', *lim-et- 'boundary'. It cognates: O. liimitu[m [acc.sg.
or gen.pl.] 'boundary path, limit', probably borrowed from Latin.
Derived from (the base of) the adj. limus 'transverse, oblique', possibly *lim-en-
'crossbar'. Limes can be from *lim(o)-it- 'going transverse' (thus WH), but more
likely seems a derivational analysis as *limo- 'oblique' » *lim-et- 'the oblique one,
boundary'.
Bibl.: WH I: 803f., EM 359, IEW 307-309. limus 2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: limus 1 'mud, slime' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: Umax, -acis 'slug, snail' [f. (m.)] (P1.+).
Pit. *(s)le/oimo~.
PIE *loimo- 'loam' or *sle/oh i-mo- 'slime, slimy'. IE cognates: OS lemo, OE lam,
1
OHG leim 'loam' < PGm. *laima/on~; OPr. slayx, Lith. sliekas, Latv. slieka
'earth-worm' < *sloiH-k-; Latv. slienas, OCS sliny [nom.pl.], Ru. dial, slina, SCr.
slina 'saliva' < *slehii-n-h \; Ru. slimdk (dial.) 'snail, slug' < *slehji-m~; OIc. OE
2
MHG slim 'slime'. Gr. Xelpa£, 'snail' (Hsch.) is probably (borrowed from) Latin.
Lat. limus could belong to PGm. *laima/on- 'loam' < PIE *loimo- (to PIE *h li- 'to
2
smear') or to BS1. *sloiH-l*sleHi~, PGm. *slirna- 'slime, saliva'.
Bibl.: WH I: 802-805, EM 359, IEW 662-664, Schrijver 1991: 243, Sihler 1995:214.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: limus 2 'transverse, oblique' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: limulus 'oblique' (PI); sublimen [adv.] 'in a lofty position' (Naev.+),
sublimis 'high, elevated' (Naev.+; also sublimits Enn.+), subhmare 'to raise, send up'
(Enn.+).
Pit. *(s)limo-1
Latin continues *limo- 'transverse' and a compound *sub-lim-i- 'transverse from
below upward' with the original meaning 'upward' of sub. The adv. sublimen
originally occurred mainly in sublimen auferre 'to carry away aloft' vel sim., and
probably represents the acc.sg. *sublimem of the adj. None of the cognates adduced
for limus in IEW is convincing by its semantics, nor does the vowel always fit: Gr.
leipcov 'meadow', Xi|ITJV 'harbour', Thess. 'market', M|IVT) 'lake'; OIc. limr (u) [f.],
OE lim [n.] 'member, twig', OIc. limi [m.] 'bundle of twigs, broom'.
Bibl.: WH I: 805, II: 618f., EM 359,661, IEW 307-309. timen, lituus

Derivatives: delingere 'to lick up, off (P1.+), sublinguld 'under-dish-licker' (PI.);
ligurrire 'to lick, sponge' (P1.+).
Pit. *ling(-e/o)~.
PIE *li-n-gh- [pr.J 'to lick'. IE cognates: Olr. ligid*, •lig* MW llyu, MBret. leat 'to
lick' < PCI. *lige/o-; Skt. redhi/ledhi [3s,pr.], ppa. ririhvams-, Gr. X£i%a>, Arm.
lizanem, Lith. liezti, OCS lizati, Is. lizg 'to lick' < BS1. *leiz-, OE liccian, OHG
leckon < *ligh-n~. >

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lingua 'tongue' [f. d] (Lex XII+; dingua Mar. Victorinus VI 26.2)
Derivatives: li(n)gula 'short sword, tongue-shaped object' (Naev.+), lingulaca (P1.+)
'talkative person'; bilinguis 'of or with two tongues' (P1.+), elinguis 'speechless'
(Pac.+), elinguare 'to tear the tongue out of (PL).
Pit. *dnx(u)wd-. It. cognates: O. fangvam [acc.sg.], fancua [nom.pl.] 'tongue' <
*f3nxua- < *ct-.
PIE *dngh-uh 'tongue'. IE cognates: Olr. tengae, MW tafawt 'tongue' < *tng-, Skt.
2
jihva- 'id.', Av; hizuua-, Arm. lezow, OPr. insuwis, Lith. liezuvis, OCS jqzykb, Go.
tuggo, OHG zunga, OIc. hmga < *dng-, ToA kantu, ToB kantwo 'tongue' «
*tankwo.
The I- of lingua has been adopted from lingo 'to lick', not from Sabellic, which has f-.
The form dingua is poorly attested, but would match the evidence of the other IE
branches. Sabellic/- presupposes *dh-, which must be due to association with another
word in *dh- (but which?), cf. van der Staaij 1995: 23. The absence of the
development *gw > b in Oscan suggests that 'tongue' had the structure *-guH-V- in at
least some forms of the paradigm, cf. Weiss 2007a: 368. Kortlandt 1997b: 160
assumes original proterodynamic inflection in PIE; but this does not exclude the rise
of, say, a gen.sg. *dngh-uh -os at a later, pre-Italic stage. We find tabooistic or simply
2
associative (with 'to lick') changes of the phonological form of 'tongue' in many
Indo-European languages.
Bibl.: WH I: 806f„ EM 360, IEW 223, Sihler 1995: 39, 151, Untermann 2000: 264.
linO, -ere 'to smear, seal, rub' [v. Ill; pf. levi, ppp. litum] (Cato+)
Derivatives: circumlinere 'to smear round, decorate' (Cato+), elinere 'to smear all
over' (Lucil.+), oblinere 'to smear over* close5 (P1.+).
Pit. *lina- 'to smear'.
PIE *h li-n- H- [pr.], *h li-to- [ppp.]. IE cognates: Olr. lenaid, •len 'to stay, glue,
2 2
follow', W. lfynu 'to besmear' < PCI. *lina-; Hit. halina- 'clay(?)' < *h liH-no-; Gr.
2
aXiveiv 'to smear' (Hsch.).
Schrijver (hesitantly) reconstruct...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: linquii, -ere 'to leave' [v. Ill; pf. liqui, ppp. -lieturn] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: delinquere 'to be lacking, err' (PI.+), deliquio 'failure; eclipse' (P1.+),
delictum 'misdeed, offence' (P1.+), delicuus 'lacking' (P1.+), derelinquere 'to
abandon' (Varro+), relinquere 'to leave' (P1.+), reliquiae [f.pl.] 'remnants, remains'
(P1.+), reliquus 'left, remaining' (P1.+).
Pit. *linkw-(e/o-), *(le-)loikw-, *lik"to- 'to leave'; *-lik?-e/ow-o- 'remaining'.
PIE pr. *li-n-kw-, pf. *(le-)loikw-, ppp. *likwto~. IE cognates: Olr. leicid*, •leici* 'to
let (go), leave' < PCI. *linkwe/o-\ Skt. rinakti, pf. rireca, YAv. pr. irinaxti 'to leave';
Gr. Xipjiavo), Xsinto [pr.] 'to let, leave'; Arm. Ik'anem 'id.', OPr. po-linka 'stays',
Latv. likt, Is. lieku 'to leave, put', Go. leifvan 'to lend'.
The verb forms are inherited from PIE. The older form of reliquus was *relihtwos, as
in delicuus. Similarly, reliquiae < *relikuwia. These, then, reflect *-likw-e/ow~o~.
Bibl.: WH I: 808f„ EM 361, IEW 669f., Leumann 1977: 136, Meiser 1998: 98, LIV
*leiky-
Unum 'flax plant, linseed; linen' [n. o] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: linea 'string, cord; line' (P1.+), lineare 'to make straight' (PI+); linteum
'piece of linen cloth, towel, sail' (P1.+), linteolum 'piece of linen' (P1.+), tinted
'weaver of linen' (PI.).
Pit. *lino- [n.].
IE cognates: Gr. Xivov 'linen', OPr. lynno 'flax', Lith. Unas 'flax (plant)', [pi.] linai
'flax (fibres)', Latv. lini 'flax' [pi.], CS Ibnt, Ru. tin, gen.sg. I'na 'id.' < BS1. * linos.
The noun linea can be understood as a substantivization of the adj. linens 'made of
flax or linen', which itself is not attested before Verg. The alternative adj. *linteus is
somewhat surprising, and suggests an unattested noun *linto- 'linen' as its basis. The
variation between *lino- and *linto- in Latin, to which *lino- from Greek and BS1.
can be compared, makes it impossible to reconstruct a common PIE protoform.
Probably, it was a loanword meaning 'flax'.
Bibl.: WH I: ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lippus 'having watery or inflamed eyes' [adj. o/a] (PI.+)
Derivatives: lippire 'to have watery or inflamed eyes' (P1.+), lippitudo
'imflammation or watering of the eyes' (P1.+).
Pit. *l(e)ipo~?
PIE *l(e)ip-o- 'sticky, fat'? IE cognates: Gr. Xmo<; [n.] 'fat', Xmapoq 'fat, greased'.
One of the many adjectives of physical defect with geminated stop. Might belong to
PIE *leip- 'to be sticky, be fat', but the semantics are not compelling.
Bibl.: WH I: 811, EM 362, IEW 670f., LIV l.*leip-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: liqueo 'to be clear, evident' [v. II; pf. HcuT/liquT] (PI.+)
Derivatives: liquor 'a liquid; fluidity' (P1.+), liquidus 'liquid; evident' (Naev.+),
liquidiusculus 'milder' (PI.), liquescere 'to become liquid, melt' (Naev.+); UquT 'to
become liquid, dissolve' (P1.+); colliquescere 'to melt, liquefy' (Varro+), eliquescere
'to become liquid' (Varro+), liquefacere 'to melt' (Lucr.+), colliquefacere 'id.'
(Varro+), deliqudre 'to make clear, strain' (P1.+), eliquare 'to purify by straining'
(Varro+), liquare 'to make liquid or clear' (Hor.+). ;
Pit. *(w)leikw-e/o- 'to make/become liquid', *(w)likw-e- 'to be liquid'.
PIE *uleikw- 'to make moist1? IE cognates: Olr. jliuch 'moist', W. gwlith [m.]
'dew'; ToA [inf.] lyiklsi 'to wash', ToB [pr.] laikanatrd 'washes', liksan- 'washes',
[pf.] laikate 'washed'.
The verbs in -are are compounded factitives to the stative e-verb. The set liqueo —
liquor - liquidus is of a productive type; beside lTquT< *uleikw-, lique- is probably the
old stative. The old nasal present changed to -lingo, see s.v. pollingo.
Bibl.: WH I: 244f., 812, II: 618, EM 131f., 362, 661, Leumann 1977: 117, LIV
*uleiky-. —• lixa, pollingo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lira 'furrow' [f. a] (Columella+)
Derivatives: delirus 'insane' (Lucr.+), delirdre 'to be mad; deviate from the balks (in
ploughing' (P1.+), deliritas 'insanity' (Lab.), delTramentum 'nonsense' (P1.+).
Pit. *loisa~. It. cognates: possibly O. luisarifs [abl.pl.f.] month-name ('in which the
furrows are drawn'?).
PIE *lois-h - 'furrow, track'. IE cognates: OPr. lyso 'field bed', OCS lexa 'field bed,
2
furrow'; OHG leisa 'track'.
Lat. de~lirus probably developed from *de lirad (Ire) '(to go) off the track'.
Bibl.: WH I: 338, 812f., EM 362f., IEW 671, LIV *leis-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lis, -tis 'lawsuit; dispute' [f.] (Lex XII+; arch. Cic. et aliter stlis, inscr. stl.ivd. =
stlitibus iudicandis)
Derivatives: litigare 'to go to law, dispute' (P1.+), Utigium 'quarrel, lawsuit' (PL+).
Pit. *slT-ti~ 'accusation, dispute'.
PIE *sliH-ti-? IE cognates: Olr. liid*, -li* 'to accuse', MW Uiw 'proof, accusation' <
PCI. *liie/o- < *sliH-e/o-, Olr. lithech 'accused person' < *liti-ko-.
Joseph 1986 connects stlis with Olr. Utlfech and the verb liid 'to accuse'. He reports
(p. 122) a suggestion made by Jochem Schindler during a presentation in 1980, to the
effect that Lat. slTs, as found in one inscription from the 2nd c. BC, might be older than
stlis. Initial stl- could be the contemporary phonetic rendering of the rare sequence si-,
which only survived in archaisms, but had otherwise changed to initial /-. If this is
correct, we could reconstruct an Italo-Celtic root *sliH- 'to dispute, accuse'. Olr.
lithech would contain the same nominal ti-stem as Latin. Gusmani 2002 connects Lat.
lis with PGm. *sleitan 'to split', OHG hari-sliz 'splitting the army, desertion', which
would seem to derive from a form in a final *-d~. He is hesitant about connecting the
Celtic forms. Yet we also find PGm. *sleipa- or *slipa- 'bad' in Go. sleips or sleideis
'pernicious, fierce, dangerous', gasleipjan 'to damage', so that, if at all related, we
may posit PGm. *sliH- with different dental enlargements.
Bibl.: WH I: 813, EM 363, Leumann 1977: 189, Schumacher 2004: 452.
litO, -are 'to obtain or give favourable omens' [v. I] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: litatio 'obtaining favourable omens' (PI+).
Denominative to a noun *lita, which was probably borrowed from Gr. Xixf) 'request'
< *Xua, cf. Aacoopai, Hxop.ai 'to pray, beg'.
Bibl.: WHI: 814, EM 363.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lltus, -oris 'sea-shore, coast' [n. r] (P1.+)
Pit. *lei/itos [n.].
PIE *leit-os- 'going'? IE cognates: Av. irWiia- 'to die', ppa. irirWus- 'dead', Go.
ga-leipan 'to go', ToB lit a, ToA lit [pret.], ToB laitam, ToA letas [sb.] 'to go away'.
A connection with *leiH- 'to pour', as is hesitantly accepted by WH, does not make
sense semantically. Somewhat better would be a connection with *lih2- 'to stop,
disappear' (Skt. -ifyate 'dissolves', Gr. Xlva^ai 'I avoid', OE linnan 'to stop'), but the
morphology would remain difficult. The root *leit- 'to go (away)' seems to be a better
candidate: *leit-os- 'the going away' > 'end, side'.
Bibl.: WH I: 815, EM 364, IEW 664f., LIV 1.*leit-.
t
ljtuus 'curved trumpet, curved staff fm. o] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: liticen 'trumpeter' (Cato+).
Etymology unknown. If the meaning 'curved' is original, one could connectd Go.
lipus, OIc. lidr 'member, part, lid' (thus IEW) and posit *litu- 'curve(d)'.
Bibl.: WH I: 8l5f., EM 364, IEW 307-309. -»limus 2, obllquus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: fividus 'of a dull or greyish-blue colour; envious' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: livescere 'to become dull blue' (Lucr.+), Iivor 'bluish discoloration,
envy' (P1.+).
Pit. *(s)le/oiwo- or *(s)liwo-.
PIE *(s)l(e/o)iH-uo- 'plum-coloured, blueish'. IE cognates: Olr. //, W. Iliw 'colour'
< *(s)HH-u~; Ru. sliva 'plum', SCr. sljiva 'id.' < *sliH-ueh, OHG sleha, OE slab,
r
MoDu. (dial.) sleeuw 'sloe' < PGm. *slaix(u)a/o-.
Since the other IE languages point to a derivative in *-uo-, the Latin word family
must be based on livere (only attested after Cic.) or on lividus, which replaced *liuos.
The original ablaut grade of Latin ITv- cannot be determined.
Bibl.: WH I: 816, EM 364, IEW 965, Schrijver 1991: 231. — Hindus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lixa 'water, lye' [f. a] (Nonius)
Derivatives: lixivus '(probably) liquid, flowing' (Cato+); elixus 'boiled' (P1.+),
prdlixus 'having extensive growth, extended, long' (Ter.+), prolixitudo 'great length'
(Pac.); maybe lixula 'kind of cheese pancake' (Varro). r
Pit. *wI(e)ikt"so- 'fluid, flowing'.
PIE *ul(e)ikw-s-.
The noun lixa is probably a f. of the adj. *lixus 'fluid' which is contained in the other
derivatives. This *lixus probably represents an adj. in *-so- to the root of liqued. In
prdlixus, the original meaning was 'flowing forth, continuing', in elixus it must have
been 'boiling out'.
Bibl.: WH I: 8f6f., II: 370, EM 364, 538, LIV *uleiky-. liqueo
\

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: longus 'long' [adj. o/a] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: longe [adv.] 'far, for a long while' (Naev.+); longinquus, longincus
'long; remote, distant' (P1.+), longinquitas 'distance, duration' (Ter.+), longiscere 'to
become long' (Enn.), longitudo 'length' (Cato+), longule [adv.] 'at a distance' (P1.+);
perlongus 'very long' (P1.+).
Pit. *(d)longo
PIE *dlongh-o-. IE cognates: PIE *doIugh- in Hit. *taluki- I talugai- [adj.] 'long';
Hit. zaluknu'to lengthen' < *dlu^-n(e)u~; PIE *dlhjgh-o- in Skt. dirghaOAv.
daraga-, OCS dfagb, SCr. dug, Lith. ilgas, Alb. gjate 'long' (+ *-ta-); Gr. boXixoc; (<
*dolhi-gh-o-?), evSeX^xn? 'lasting long, uninterrupted' < *delhgh-; PIE *dlongh-o-
r t
in Go. laggs, OHG lang, OIc. langr, MP drang 'long'. Gr. Xoyyj] 'spear' would
theoretically go back to *dlongh-o- (if dl- > /- in Greek), but it is rejected by Beekes
(fthc.).
The adj. longinquus was probably formed on the basis of longe (thus EM) as an
antonym to propinquus. A major question is whether PIE *d(o)Iugh- (Hit.), *doligh-,
*delgh- (Gr.), *dlongh- (Lat., Gm.) and *dlh;gh- (Ilr., BSL, Alb.) are different variants
of what was originally a compound petrified in the meaning 'long' (Kloekhorst 2008:
819f. compares cases such as MoE high and dry, safe and sound). The first member

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: could be *de/ol(h compare Cz. del (poet.) [f. i] 'length' < PS1. *dblb, OCS
L
prodbljg 'I prolong', Ru. dlit'sja, Cz. dliti 'to last', Ru. dlina 'length'; the second
member containing *-gh-.
Bibl.: WH I: 820, EM 366, IEW 197.
loquor, loquT 'to talk, speak' [v. Ill; ppp. locutum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: loquitari 'to talk constantly' (PI.+), loqudx 'talkative' (P1.+), loquaculus
'gossiping' (Lucr.), loquela 'speech, utterance' (P1.+); alloquT 'to speak to, invoke'
(Naev.+), colloqui 'to speak to, with' (P1.+), eloqui 'to utter, tell' (PL+), eloquentia
'eloquence' (Ter.+), interloqui 'to speak between' (Ter.+), obloqui1,to interrupt' (PI.+),
praeloquT1 to speak first' (P1.+), prdloquT lto speak forth, announce' (PL+), proloquium
'axiom, statement' (Varro+), transloqui'to go through the whole tale' (PI.).
Pit. *(i)lokn-e/o-.
PIE *tlokw- 'to talk'. IE cognates: Olr. ad-tluchedar 'gives thanks', do-tluchedar
( lucrum 349
'demands'; Ru. folk 'meaning'.
The ppp. locutus must be analogical after e.g. soluT: solutus.
Bibl.: WH I: 821, EM 366, IEW 1088, Schrijver 1991: 476, Sihier 1995: 210, 624.
I5ra 'drink made from watered grapeskins' [f. a] (Cato+; variant Idrea Gell.)
A derivation from lavo as *lowera, as proposed by WH, is unlikely for phonetic
reasons, since *Jowera would normally yield Lat. *lura. For a form Idra to emerge, a
sequence *low- would have to be restored, which is unlikely in view of the Pit.
unrounding in lavo. Morphologically, *louH-ro- is not very convincing. Semantically,
lora could be many things; there is no obvious connection to 'washing'.
Bibl.: WHI: 821, EM 366.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lorum 'leather strap, thong' [n. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: loreus 'made of strips or thongs' (P1.+), lorica 'corselet or cuirass' (P1.+),
loricare 'to cover with a cuirass' (Varrcrf); loripes, -dis 'having deformed feet' (P1.+).
IE cognates: Gr. euXqpa, Dor. afiXripa [pi.], Hsch. apXipa 'reins' (< *d-pXr]po-?),
Arm. lar 'cord'.
Beekes (fthc.) regards the alternation e-/a- within Greek as inexplicable; he therefore
suspects Pre-Greek origin. If euXipa reflects *T|ulT|pa (which would not fit the
hexameter), Proto-Greek may have had *aulera. Theoretically, this might reflect a
PIE form *h e-h ul-er-, but the suffixation is strange. Thus, we may indeed be dealing
2 2
with a loanword, but the ablaut Gr. e vs. Lat. 6 does look Indo-European. Maybe the
giving language was a now extinct IE language? For lorica, a separate loanword
etymology is often proposed, viz. from Gr. i3(bpa£ 'cuirass', Ion. i3a)pr£, or both could
be from an unknown Mediterranean language. Yet WH reject this solution, arguing
that lorica was originally made from leather, and hence belongs to lorum.
Bibl.: WH I: 822, EM 366f., IEW 1140-1144, Schrijver 1991: 122f.

Pit. *(s)lo ufre/o-ko-.
PIE *s!e/oubh-ro- 'gliding'. IE cognates: Go. sliupan 'to creep, slide' (withp < *pp

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lucrum 'material gain, profit' [n. o] (P1.+; lucrifacere 'to make a profit' P1.+)
Derivatives: lucrifer 'bringing gain' (PL), lucrificabilis 'profitable' (PL), lucrifuga
[m.] 'spendthrift' (PI.), lucripeta [adj.m.] 'avaricious'.
Pit. *luklo- > dissim. *lukro~.
PIE *Ih u-tlo- 'seizure, gain'. IE cognates: Gr. cmoXauco 'to enjoy', Xeia, Dor. Xdia
2
(< *Xaf4a) 'booty'; Go. laun [n.] 'reward' < *leh u-no-.
2
Short *lu- would be regular from pretonic *lh u- according to the rules of Schrijver
2
1991: 248f. The PIE basis *l(e)h u- found in lucrum, as well as in Gr. 'to enjoy', Go.
2
'reward', could formally be connected with Hit. lahu-llahu- 'to pour, cast' <
*l(o)h u-, lahhu- 'container' < *leh u-; but the semantics are difficult. Alternatively,
2 ?
the non-Anatolian forms in *l(e/o)h u- could be compared with Hit. lahh- 'military
2
campaign, journey', CLuw. lahhi(ia)* 'to travel, campaign' < *16h -/*lehr, Gr.
2
Xa(p)6<; 'men, army', Olr. laech 'warrior' < *leh u-. The original meaning of *Ieh -
2 2
could have been 'to fight', that of *leh u- 'to acquire by fighting*.
2
Bibl.: WH I: 826, EM 367f., IEW 655, Schrijver 1991:240f.
