content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: luctor, -3rl 'to wrestle, struggle' [v. I] (P1.+; active luctare in OLat., dep. luctari in
CLat.)
Derivatives: luctator 'wrestler' (P1.+); deluctdri 'to fight it out with' (PI.).
Pit. *lukto-.
PIE *lug-to- 'bent'. IE cognates: Olr. fo-loing 'supports', injoing 'connects', MW
ellwng- 'to set free' < PCI. *-lunge/o- 'to place', Gr. Auyoq [f.] 'withy, twigs', Go.
ga-lukan 'to shut', us-lukan 'to open'.
Frequentative verb based on a ppp. *luktos 'bent'. If the etymology is correct, it is
striking that there is no (visible) reflex of Lachmann's lengthening in luctor nor in
luxus. Since Celtic shows a nasal present, the short reflex may have been restored
from this present; but this is speculative, since no trace of such a present exists in
Latin.
Bibl: WH I: 826f., EM 368, IEW 685f„ Schumacher 2004: 460f., LIV 2.*leu(g)-.
luxus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lucus 'sacred grove, wood' [m. o] (P1.+, CIL 1.366 loucom)
Derivatives: Wear, -aris [m.] 'sacred grove' (CIL 1.401, 1.1730); collucare 'to thin
out (trees)' (Cato+); LucTna 'cognomen of Iuno, invoked by women when giving
birth' (P1.+).
Pit. *louko~ 'sacred grove'. It. cognates: Ven. louki [gen.sg.] 'sacred grove', O.
luvkei [loc.sg.], U. vukum-en, vuku [acc.sg.], vukes [gen.sg.], vuku-kum,
uocu-com [abl.sg. + -com], vuke [loc.sg.] '(sacred) enclosure'.
PIE *louk-o- [m.] 'light place'. IE cognates: Skt. loka- [m.] 'free space, world*, Lith.
laukas 'field, land', Latv. lauks 'field, clearing in the woods', OHG loh 'clearing'.
For the derivation of Lucina from lucus 'sacred grove', cf. Leumann 1960.
Bibl.: WH I: 828, EM 368, IEW 687-690, Lejeune 1974: 336, Untermann 2000:
439f., LIV *leuk- luna, lux
ludO, -ere 'to play, sport' [v. Ill; pf. lusi, ppp. lusum] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: ludus (arch, inscr. loed-, loid-) 'sport, play, jest' (Naev.+); ludibrium
'plaything, ridiculous thing' (P1-+), ludibundus 'merry' (P1.+), ludicer [adj. old] 'of the

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: stage, of sport' (P1.+), ludicre [adv.] 'playfully' (Enn.+); ludificdri 'to make an object
of sport, play with' (P1.+), ludificator 'who plays with' (PI.), ludificatus, -us 'the
teasing' (Pi.), ludificabilis 'suitable to play with' (PI.); lusid 'play, sport' (Varro+),
lusitare 'to amuse oneself (P1.+), lusor 'player' (P1.+); alludidre 'to play with' (P1.+),
alludere 'to play, jest' (Ter.+), collusor 'fellow gambler' (Lucil.+), collusim 'in
collusion' (P1-+), deludere 'to deceive' (P1.+), deliidificare 'to make a complete fool
of (PL), eludere 'to deceive, dodge' (Ter.+), eludificarJ 'to fool completely' (PI.),
ittudere 'to make game of (Ter.+), obludiare 'to play the fool' (PL).
Pit. *loid-e/o- 'to play', *loido- 'play'.
PIE *Ioid- 'to let go frequently, play'. IE cognates: Mir. laidid 'impels', Gr.
/UvSecrflm 'to contend' (Hsch.), Xi&i 'plays' (Hsch.), Alb. lind 'gives birth', lindet 'is
born' < *li-n-d-; OLith. leidmi 'I let', Lith. leisti, Is. leidziu 'to let' < pr. *leid~; Lith.
laidyii 'to throw', Latv. laist 'let, publish, set in motion' < *loid-.
The o-grade found in Irish, Baltic and Latin could point to an original causative
present, but the suffix of Latin does not fit. We seem instead to be dealing with an
o-grade present of frequentative meaning. The noun ludus is probably deverbal, since
no equivalents are found elsewhere,
Bibl: WH I: 829, EM 368f, IEW 666, LIV *Ieid-.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lugeo 'to moum, grieve' [v. II; pf. luxi, ppp. factum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: lugubris 'mourning, sad' (P1.+).
Pit. *!oug-eje- [v.], *loug-os-ri~!
PIE *loug-eie- 'to swallow, gasp'. IE cognates: Olr. loingid 'to eat' < *(s)hi-n-g-,
Gr. ^uyyctvcbpevov 'sobbing' (Hsch.), Xu^co 'to have the hiccup, sob' < *(s)Iug-ie-,
XnyjiOi; 'the hiccup(s)'.
WH suggest that lugubris more probably reflects *liigos-ri- (with an s-stem *lugos-
belonging to the e-present lugere) than *luge-bli- (with regular dissimilation to -in-
after the /-containing stem). It is disputed to whicli root lugere belongs. It is often
connected with PIE *lug- 'to break', and indeed a semantic connection between
'breaking' and 'mourning' can often be found in etymology, due to the intensity of
the acts of mourning. Lat. lugere would fit the morphology of the causative, but
semantically a stative (*lug-eh , in which case the zero grade of the root is striking)
r
or a frequentative (*(le-)loug-ie~) of'to break' are more likely. Koiligan 2005 objects
that there is no evidence for the assumed shift in meaning from 'break' to 'mourn' in
Latin, and also, that lugeo is normally used as an active, transitive verb. He therefore
proposes the* alternative connection with PIE *(s)leug- 'to swallow', which would
have developed via 'to gasp' to 'wail, moum'. The causative *loug-eie- could be
understood as,'to bemourn (someone)'. This solution seems more straightforward to
me than the connection with 'to break'.
Bibl.: WH I: 830f., EM 369, IEW 686, Koiligan 2005, LIV *(s)leug-.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lumbncus 'earthworm, intestinal worm' [m. o] (PL+)
IE cognates: see s.v. lumbus.
The meaning 'earthworm' is more frequent and more ancient in the texts than
'intestinal worm', The only OLat. place where lumbncus is translated as 'intestinal
worm' by OLD refers to the form of the worm: Ad tormina et si alvus consistet et si
taeniae et lumbrici molesti erunt 'For gripes, for loose bowels, for tapeworms and
stomach-worms, if troublesome' (Cato, Agr. 126). The word lumbrici will have been
used metaphorically to indicate the form of the worm plaguing the patient; compare
taenia 'ribbon, string; (here:) intestinal worm'. The suffix recalls umbilicus (also
situated in the lower belly) and formica 'ant' (also an insect). WH assume that lumbr-
reflects *lonctr~, and derive the word from a root *lndh-, see s.v, lumbus. But since
the PIE voiced aspirates normally yield stops after a nasal in all Italic languages, thus
bleeding the specifically Latin change *-ctr- > -br- (cf. Meiser 1986: 75f.), *lonctr-
would yield Latin *lundr~. As Latin does not possess a productive suffix -ricus, the
origin of lumbricus remains unclear.
Bibl.: WH I: 831, EM 369, IEW 960f.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lumbus 'hips, loins'[m. o] (P1.+; usually pi.)
Derivatives: lumbifragium 'loin-wreckage' (PL).
Pit. *londwo- 'loins'.
PIE *londh-u-o-. IE cognates: HLuw. la-tara/i- ilandri(ya)~l 'to expand (a country)',
Skt. randhra- 'loin (of animals); weak spot on the body; cavity' < *londh-ro-; OCS
l§dvij% [f.pl.] 'loins, insides, kidneys', Ru. Ijadveja (arch.) 'thigh ' < Venct -u-; OIc.
lend, OHG lentin 'kidneys', OE lendenu 'loins' < *londh-io-; Olr. land 'free space',
MW Hon 'area', Co. Ian, Bret, lann lieath' < *lndh-h -; OPr. acc.sg. lindan 'valley';
2
Ru. Ijadd, Cz. lada, lado 'fallow land' < *lenc?-o-; Go. OIc. OS OE land, OHG lant
'land' < *londh-o-; Lith. lendit 'to creep, sneak (into)'.
Goto 1985 has argued that Skt. randhra- can be connected with lumbus and with Gm.
*landa- 'land', under the assumption of a PIE verb *lenct-e- 'to sink, go down' (Lith.
lendu 'to creep into') which was adopted as such by LIV. Oettinger 2007 has made
the comparison more explicit and has clarified the semantics and the morphology.
Adding HLuw. latara/i- 'to expand (a country)' to the dossier, he posits an original
meaning 'to enter, penetrate' for PIE *lendh-. This would be continued (in a specific
usage) in Lith. lendu, whereas 'land' in BS1., CI. and Gm. can be understood as 'the
area which is entered'. The HLuw. verb 'to expand', a denominative to *landra- <
*lonctto- 'free space', could be understood as 'to make into one's (own) land'. The
Skt. meaning 'weak spot on the body' and 'loin' would have developed in hunter's
jargon for the spot where a hunted animal was the most vulnerable, viz. below the
ribs. This, then, would explain Gm. and Latin 'loins', SI. 'thigh'. The wo-stem of
Latin could be built on the w-stem *lendh-u- still visible in Slavic. The development
*-ndw- > -mb- would be parallel to initial *dw- > Lat. b-.
Bibl.: WH I: 832, EM 369, IEW 675, Meiser 1998: 120, LIV *lendh-. lumbricus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: luna 'moon' [f. a] (P1.+; Praeneste losna)
Derivatives: lunula 'crescent-shaped ornament' (PL).
Pit. *louksnd-.
PIE *le/ouk-s-nh - 'shiny, bright, gleaming'. IE cognates: Olr. dia luain 'Monday',
2
Av. raoxsna- [adj./n.] Tight', Gr. Xuxvoq [m.] 'lamp', OPr. lauxnos [pi.] 'stars', MHG
liehsen 'bright'.
The base *le/uk-s- is also found in lumen and lustrum (see s.v. lux). It might be derived
from the 5-stem *leuk-os~. The word *le/ouk-s-no- was probably used as an epithet for
the moon in PIE.
Bibl.: WH I: 833, EM 373, IEW 687-690, Meiser 1998: 62, LIV *Ieuk-. lucus, lux
lunter / linter, -tris 'trough, vat, tank; small boat' [f. (m.) r] (Cato+)
No etymology.
Bibl.: WH I: 809f., EM 370.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: luo, -ere 'to suffer, make amends for' [v. Ill; pf. hu (lit! Varro)] (Acc.+)
Derivatives: luella 'expiation' (Lucr.), lues, -is 'plague, affliction' (Cic.+); reluere
'to redeem' (Caecil.).
Pit. *luwe/o-.
PIE aor. *l(e)uH- 'to cut loose', pf. *louH-? IE cognates: Olr. as-loi* 'to flee' < PCI.
*eks-luue/o-\ Skt. lundti 'cuts off, aluna- 'not shaved'; Gr. Auto 'to loosen, liberate',
aor. midd. K>|ir)V, X.U(V)TO; OE le [m.] 'sickle'; ToB lyuwa, ToA lyu 'sent'. If Lith.
liauti 'to stop', PS1. *leviti 'to diminish, weaken' are cognate, the root was *lhiu-.
The thematic pr. of Italo-Celtic and Greek is probably secondary to the root aorist
(LIV). The Latin form liti might preserve a trace of earlier *loit-w-.
Bibl.: WH I: 338, EM 168, IEW 681 f., Schrijver 1991: 246, Meiser 1998: 206,
Schumacher 2004:463, LIV *leuH-. —*• lustrum, soluo

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lupus 'wolf [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: lupa 'whore' (P1.+), 'she-wolf (Prop.+), lupanar 'brothel' (P1.+),
luparT 'to prostitute oneself (Lucil.+), luptnus 'of a wolf (P1.+), luptnum 'the lupin
(plant)' (Cato+), lupTnarius 'of lupins' (Cato), lupillum/s 'lupin-seed' (PI.).
Pit. *!ukwo-7 *lupo-l : J
PIE *ulkwo- 'wolf. IE cognates: Skt. vfka-, YAv. vdhrka- [m.], MP gurg, Gr. XVKOC;
[m.], Lith. vilkas, Latv. vilks, ORu. vblkb [m.] < *ujkwo-, Go. wulfs [m.], ToB walkwe
[m.] 'wolf.
The earlier derivation of lupus from PIE *uIkwo- 'wolf requires two ad hoc
assumptions: metathesis to *lukwo- in Italic (as is often assumed for Gr. fajicot; 'wolf),
and borrowing by Latin of the Sabellic outcome *lupo~. This is conceivable.
Alternatively, one could derive lupus from PIE *ulp- / Hup- 'marten' (whence,
among others, Av. urupi-, Lat. volpes 'fox'); cf. de Vaan 2000: 289. This would
amount to a semantic shift from 'marten' to 'fox' to 'wolf, the latter one maybe by
tabooistic replacement of an earlier word for 'wolf. The disadvantage of this
approach is that the stem *ulp- is already continued in Lat. volpes, so that an
additional reflex in lupus would require further special pleading.
Bibl.: WH I: 835-837, EM 370, IEW 1178f., Leumann 1977: 101, Sihler 1995: 161.
—• volpes

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: luridus 'ghastly yellow, colour of bruises' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: luror 'sickly yellow colour' (Lucr.+); elurescere 'to become pale'
(Varro apudNon.); lutum 'yellow dye, colour' (Verg.+), luteus 'pink, pale-red' (P1.+).
Pit. *(s)!ous-? *(s)loiro-1 *(s)loito-r>
The exact source and etymology are very uncertain. Schrijver 1995: 332 suggests that
luridus belongs to MW lieu 'light*, MW lloer 'moon' < *le/ous-, while Nussbaum
1997: 199f. connects it with Lat. lividus (see s.v. for IE cognates). In the latter case,
Lat. lu- can reflect *(s)loiH~. For the meaning 'pink' (not 'yellow') of luteus, see
Edgeworth 1985.
Bibl.: WH I: 837f., 841, EM 37If. lividus

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: luscus 'blind in one eye' [adj. o/a] (PI.+)
Derivatives: luscidsus 'suffering from night-blindness' (Var.+), luscitiosus 'suffering
from night-blindness' (PI.+), lusciniola 'nightingale' (P1.+), luscinia 'id.' (Hor.+).
Pit. *luk-sko~ 'with partial sight, visually handicapped'.
PIE *luk-(e/o-) 'to see'.
The derivatives suggest the previous existence of a v. *luscTre 'to be night-blind'. The
nightingale might be explained with haplology from *lusci-cania 'singing in the
night' or 'blind singer', but this is speculative. Alternatively, it may be based on an
n-stem *lusk-e/on- 'blind one'. WH hesitantly explain luscus from *nuk-sko-, which
they connect with Lith. niukti 'to make a dull sound'; Latin would have changed *n- >
1- under the influence of lux. Yet the semantics of the Baltic forms do not match very
well. Lat. nuscitiosus is a variant of luscitiosus, only attested in Paul, ex F. Fruyt
(1986: 162) suggests that Lat. luscus could reflect *luk-sko- derived from the root
*leuk- 'light'. In this respect, compare the cognate PCI. verb *luk-e/o- 'to see', as in
MW adolwyn 'to beseech', gorllwyn 'to expect, observe' (Schumacher 2004: 459f.);
also MW llygat, MCo. lagas [m.], MBret. lagat 'eye' < *luk-ati- 'seer' (Schrijver
1995: 166). This explanation seems more attractive to me.
Bibl.: WH I: 838, EM 371, IEW 768, Leumann 1977: 556. -> liix
Ifistrum 'ceremony of purification; five-year period' [n. o] (Cato+)
Derivatives: lustrare 'to purify ceremonially, move round, spread light' (Andr.+);
circumlustrdre 'to pace round* (Lucr.+); armilustrium 'ceremony of purifying the
arms' (Varro+), tubilustrium 'festival at which the sacred trumpets were purified'
(Varro+).
Pit. *lustro- 'expiation'.
According to WH and IEW, lustrum 'purification' is based on an earlier meaning
'illumination' < *l(e/o)uk-s-tro~, to Lat. lux. Yet there is no good evidence for
'enlightening' in the meaning of the lustrum. One might connect it with lavo 'to
wash' — but still...

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lutra 'otter' [f a] (Varro, Plin.+)
Pit. *udra~.
PIE *ud-ro- [adj.] 'of the water' > 'water animal, otter' [m.]. IE cognates: Skt. udra-,
YAv. udra- 'otter', Gr. uSpoq, i36pct 'water-snake', OPr. wudro, Lith. udra, Ru. vydra
'otter' < PS1. Hydra < BS1. *u?dra?, OHG ottar, OIc. otr [m.] 'otter'.
The change of PIE *dr to Latin tr is regular. At some stage, initial /- was added,
maybe taken from the verb lavd 'to wash'. But since an otter is a carnivore, the /- may
also have been taken from lupus 'wolf. Otters are known for their playfulness, so that
one might also consider influence from ludere 'to play', viz. at the stage *udrd. The f.
gender in BS1. and Latin (and partly in Greek) is probably due to the generalization of
the fem. of the adjective.
Bibl.: WH I: 840, EM 372, IEW 78-81.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lutum 'mud, dirt, clay' [n. o].(Pl.+)
Derivatives: lutare 'to cover, with mud' (Cato+), lutamentum 'coating of mud'
(Cato), luteus 'worthless, dirty' (P1.+), lutitare 'to d,rag in the mud' (PI.+), lutosus
'very muddy' (Enn.+); lustrum / lustra [n.pl.] 'muddy place, den of vice' (P1.+),
lustro 'a frequenter of brothels' (Naev.+), lustrari 'to haunt brothels' (P1.+).
Pit. *luto~, * lustro-.
PIE *l(H)u-to- 'dirt', *l(H)u-(s)tro- 'dirty place'. IE cognates: Olr. loth 'dirt' < *lutd;
Gr. Aw3pov 'gore, clotted blood', Xupa 'dirty water, moral filth', AUJITI 'maltreatment,
damage'; Alb. lum 'slime, mud'; Lith. liutynas 'loam pit'.
Bibl.: WH I: 839f., EM 371, IEW 681, Schrijver 1991: 241, LIV *leu-. polluo
lux, -cis 'light', [f. k] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: lucere 'to emit light, dawn; to ignite, cause to shine' (P1.+), lucidus
'bright, shining' (Lucr.+), lucescere 'to begin to shine, dawn' (P1.+); lucubrare 'to
work by lamplight' (Varro+), lucubratid 'work done by lamplight' (Cato+);
luculentus 'excellent, splendid' (P1.+), luculentitas 'splendour' (Caecil.+),
luculentaster [m.] 'a kind of confection' (Titin.); lumen, -inis [n.] 'light, daylight'
(Naev,+), luminare [n.] 'lamp' (Cato); lucerna 'oil-lamp' (P1.+); Lucius praenomen
(Sis.+); allucere 'to light; be lit' (P1.+), diliicere 'to be clear1 (Varro+), dilucescere 'to
dawn' (Lucr.+), dilucidus 'clear, lucid' (Ter.+), diluculum 'daybreak' (P1.+), illucere
'to shine (on)' (P1.+), illucescere 'to begin to shine' (PI.+), per lucere 'to transmit
light' (P1.+), perliicidus 'transparent, clear' (PI.+), relucere 'to shine out' (Varro+),

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lucifer 'light-bringing; the morning star [m.]' (Acc.+), lucifugus 'avoiding the light of

r

'dawn, next morning' < *l(e)uk-ot~; Skt. rocate 'to shine', rue- [f.] 'light, splendour,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lustre, appearance', Av. raocah- [n.] Tight', YAv. raocaiia- 'to make shine', raoca-
'to shine', raoxsna- [adj./n.] 'light'; raocana- [adj.] 'shining, light', OP raucah- [n.]
'day'; Gr. XeuKO<; 'clear, light', ^swoto 'to see'; OPr. luckis 'torch, log', RuCS lucb
'ray, light, shining', Cz. louc 'torch' < *louki- OCS luciti 'to happen', Go. liuhap
t
'light'; Toch. luk- 'to shine'.
Lat. lux is a root noun, also attested in vestiges in Skt. The transitive meaning of
lucere only occurs in Plautus, but may be old. Stative lucere has an unexpected full
grade in the root which may have been adopted from causative *louk-eie-, or from the
root noun (Nussbaum 1994: 168). Lucubrdre is a denominative of a noun *lukubro-
'lamplight' < *lukV-3ro-. Lat. lumen < *lousmen < *le/ouk-s-mn. Lucerna seems to
have the suffix of lanterna, but its short -u- is unexplained.
Bibl.: WH I: 823-833, 839, II: 333, EM 368, 372-374, IEW 687-690, Nussbaum
1994, Meiser 2003: 141, LIV *leuk-. liiciis, luna

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: luxus 'dislocated, sprained' [m. o/a] (Cato+)
Derivatives: luxare 'to sprain, dislocate', luxari 'to live riotously' (P1.+),
luxuria/luxuries 'indulgence, immoderate growth, unruly behaviour' (P1.+),
luxuriosus 'given to luxury, excessive' (Cato+), luxus, -us 'extravagant living,
opulence' (Ter.+).
Pit. *lukso~.
PIE *lug-so- 'bent'. IE cognates: see luctor.
If the etymology is correct, the absence of Lachmann's lengthening must be
explained; see s.v. luctor. From an original meaning * lug-so- 'bent', we can explain
the semantic shift to 'sprained' on the one hand, and to luxus [m.] 'the bending' >
Tack of restraining, exaggeration' on the other. The noun luxuriates is probably
based on an adj. *luxuros.
Bibl.: WH I: 841, EM 374, IEW 685f., LIV 2.*]eu(g)-. luctor

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: macer, -a, -um 'thin, lean' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: macellus 'rather thin' (Lucil.+), macere 'to be thin' (PL), macescere 'to
become thin, shrivel' (P1.+), macies 'thinness' (Lucr.+), macilentus 'thin' (PL+),
macor 'thinness' (Pac.), macritudo 'thinness' (PL);permacer 'very lean' (Cato+).
Pit. *makro- 'long, lean'.
PIE *mh k-ro- 'long'. IE cognates: Olr. do-formaig 'to add, increase', MW magu,
2
MCo. maga, MBret. maezur, maguajfr'to raise, feed' < PCI. *make/o- 'to nourish' (<
*'to make long'?), OW MCo. MBret map 'son' < *makuo-, Olr. macc < *makkuo-\
Hit. maklant- 'thin, meagre' < *m(e)h k-lo-nt-, Av. masah- 'size', Gr. jiaKpoq 'long',
2
pfjjco^ [n.] 'length', OHG magar, OIc. magr 'meagre'.
Bibl.: WH II: 2, EM 375, IEW 699, Schrijver 1991: 167, Meiser 1998: 107,
Schumacher 2004:466-468. —• mactus
macerO, -are 'to make wet, soak; to worry' [v. I] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: maceria 'wall of brick or stone' (P1.+; also maceries Varro+),
macerescere 'to become soaked' (Cato).
Pit. *makero- 'soaked / kneaded'.
PIE *meh g-? IE cognates: Gr. jiacooo, Att. jaatTco 'to knead' (for *pa^co on the
2
basis of the aor. ejia^a) , aor.ps. payfjvai, Gr. pay pa [n.] 'kneaded mass, smear';
Arm. macanim 'to thicken, stick together'; OCS mazati 'to anoint', CS mazb
'unction' [f.]; Mole, maka 'to smear', OHG mahhon, OS makon 'to make'.
The attested words presuppose an adj. *makero- 'soaked' or 'kneaded'. The verb
macer are can be interpreted as 'to soaken, soften by soakening', and maceria as 'wall
(of kneaded material, clay)'. This meaning would fit PIE *mh g- 'to knead' well, but
2
Latin -k- does not correspond.
Bibl.: WH II: 2-4, EM 375, IEW 698, Schrijver 1991: 1;42,384, LIV *mag-.
i"

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mactus 'honoured; smitten' [adj. o/a] (Cato+) ;
Derivatives: made [indecl.] 'honoured, blessed, bravo' (Cato+); mactare 'to afflict,
honour, sacrifice, kill' (P1-+), mactabilis 'able to kill' (Lucr.), mactdtus, -us
'sacrificial slaying' (Lucr.); magmentum 'part of a sacrificial animal' (Varro+),
magmentarium 'shrine for the reception of the magmentum' (Varro+).
Pit. *rnakto- 'honoured, blessed', *mak-mn(-to)- 'offering, sacrifice'.
PIE *mh k-(e/o-) 'to make long'.
2
Driessen (p.c.) proposes a connection with PCI. *make/o- 'to raise, nourish' from 'to
make long' (see's.v. macer). Latin 'honoured' (e.g. made esto 'be honoured') could
be understood as. 'made long, made bigger'. This seems slightly more attractive than a
derivation from *mag- as in magnus 'big', since one might expect Lachmann's
lengthening to yield *mag-tos > *mactus. The noun magmentum could also have been
derived from a verbal stem *mak(e/o)- 'to honour'. Risch 1979 explains made esto
from a contamination of mactus esto and *macte fertd\ the denominal verb mactare
he understands as *'to pronounce the mac/e-formula'. Szemerenyi 1989: 29f. regards
made as the oldest form in the expression macte uirtute esto which he translates as
'be (provided) with power (and) virility'. Macte would be the abl.sg. of an i-stem
*mactis < *magh-tis, while mactus would be the thematic variant *maghto-. This is
far-fetched, if only because there is no evidence for the meaning 'power'.
Bibl.: WH II: 4f., 10, EM 376, IEW 708f. macer

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: macula 'stain, spot' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: maculdsus 'stained, spotted' (PI.+).
Pit. *smatlo~.
PIE *smhtlo- 'wiping'? IE cognates: Gr. opaco 'to cleanse, wipe clean', apfjpa
r
'ointment' < * s m e h r M«
The connection with the Greek verb is possible if *smHtlo- would yield Latin
*(s)matlo- > *maku!o~. For the likelihood of the first step in this development, see
Schrijver 1991: 171. Semantically, an etymology of 'stain' as 'smearing' is not
compelling, but it is conceivable. LIV adopts Chantraine's suggestion that the Gr.
forms with -a- are secondary, and the root may have been *smeh.
r
Bibl.: WH II: 5f., EM 376, IEW 966f., LIV ?*smeh.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: madeo 'to be wet' [v. II; pf. madia] (P1.+)
Derivatives: madidus 'sodden, soaked, drunk' (Naev.+), madulsa 'state of
drunkenness' (P1.+); madefacere 'to make wet, soak' (Cato+), madefactdre 'to soak'
(PI.),permadefacere 'to soak thoroughly' (PI.); commadere 'to become tender' (Cato).
Pit. *mad-e~.
PIE *mh d-ehi- 'to be wet'. IE cognates: Skt. mddati 'to rejoice, get intoxicated',
2
OAv. mada- 'intoxication', YAv. maSaite 'gets intoxicated', MoP mast 'intoxicated';
Gr. paScuD 'to be moist', paSapoq 'watery'.
The Plautine invention madulsa is said to have been formed invoke mulsus 'honeyed',
f. mulsa (potio). I agree with Schrijver that the connection of mad- 'wet' with Olr.
maidid 'breaks, bursts' is unconvincing. The Ilr. and Gr. words can be connected
under the assumption that Ilr. mad- reflects PIE *meh d- (cf. Lubotsky 1981) and Gr.
2
and Latin mad- are the regular outcome of *mhjd-.
Bibl.: WH II: 6-8, EM 377, IEW 694f., Schrijver 1991: 167, 171, LIV *mad-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: maereo 'to be sad, mourn' [v. II] (P1.+)
Derivatives: maeror 'grief (P1.+), maestus 'sad, mournful' (P1.+), maestiter 'sadly*
(PL), maestitia 'sadness' (P1.+), maestitudo 'id.' (PI.+), maestare 'to make sad' (Acc.+).
Pit. *mais-.
PIE *meh is-?
2
It seems likely that *mais- 'sad, grief is connected with the adj. miser, but no
acceptable PIE pedigree has been found. It is possible to reconstruct *mais- <
*meh i-s- and miser < *mh i-s-ro- if one accepts Schrijver's hesitant conclusion
z 2
(1991: 248) that pretonic *HI yields short i in Latin. Yet there is no evident candidate
for a PIE root *mh (-i)-. In theory, it might be an /-present to the root *mh - 't0
2 2
beckon, give a sign', reconstructed by LIV for CS mangti 'to beckon', Gr. |xqvij«,
Dor. pavtKB 'to indicate, make known*, and for OCS namajati 'to nod, beckon', Lith.
moti 'to beckon' (possibly also in PGm. *mojan- 'to tire oneself, OHG muoari).
Bibl.: WH II: 8f., EM 377. ^ miser

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: magnus 'great, big, large' [adj. o/a] (Lex XII, Andr.+)
Derivatives: (1) magnitas 'size' (Acc.), magnitudo 'size, extent' (Varro+),
magnu/ificus 'splendid, proud' (P1.+), magnificare 'to prize' (PL+), magnificentia
'boastfulness, majesty' (Ter.+), magnanimus 'brave, generous' (PL+), magnidicus
1
'boastful' (PL); permagmis 'very large' (Ter.+); (2) magis 'more' (Lex XII+),
demagis 'furthermore' (Lucil.+); maior [maiior PL], -oris 'greater, older' (Lex XII+),
maiestds 'dignity, majesty' (Andr.+), maiusculus 'somewhat older' (P1.+); maxu/imus
'greatest, biggest' (P1.+), maximitas 'hugeness' (Lucr.); magister 'commander,
teacher' (P1.+), magistra 'female teacher' (P1.+), magisterium 'instruction, control'
(P1.+), magistrates, -us '(the office of a) magistrate' (P1.+); (3) Mains [adj.] 'name of
the fifth month, May' (Varro+).
Pit. *magno/d- 'great', *magjds-, -jo/es-, -is 'greater', *magisamo- 'greatest'. It.
cognates: Ven. magetlon 'offering' (?); maisteratorbos [dat.pl.] 'to the
* magis terator s' (cf. Marinetti 2004: 395-399); O. mais 'more', U. mestru [nom.sg.f.]
'bigger' < *ma(g)is-tero-\ O. maimas 'biggest' < *ma-i-mo
PIE *mg-no- 'great', *mg-i(V)s- 'greater'. IE cognates: Olr. maige 'large' <
*magio~, magdae 'vast', mal 'prince' < *mag-lo-\ Olr. moigid or mogaid 'to add',
MW moi 'to give birth to a foal' < *mog-\ Hit. mekk-, mekki- i mekkai- 'much, many,
numerous', CLuw. maia- 'much, many' (?) < nom.acc.sg.n. *megh -; Hit. makkess-'
2
'to become numerous', maknu-a 'to multiply' < *mgh -; Skt. mahi- 'large', mahant-
2
'great', Av. maziiah- 'bigger, more', mazista- 'biggest', OAv. mazoi [dat.sg.], mazbis
[ins.pl.], YAv. mazhnt- 'big', Gr. psyaq, Arm. mec, Alb. madh large', Go. mikils, OE
micel, OIc. mikill 'large, great, many', ToB maka, ToA mak 'many'.
For maximus < *magisVmo~, see Cowgill 1970: 125. The reflex magnus < *mgno- is
explained phonetically by Schrijver by his rule *RDC- > RaDC-. The zero-grade
suffix -is of magis is quite ar...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mala 'cheeks, jaws' [f. a] (P1.+; usually pi.)
Derivatives: maxilla ' lower part of the face, jaws' (Cic.+).
Pit. *smaksla-.
PIE *smk-slo/h - 'beard, chin'. IE cognates: Olr. smech 'chin', Hit. zamakur,
2
zamankur 'beard' < *smok-ur (with secondary nasalization, and z- for *.?-); Hit.
samankuruant- [adj.] 'bearded'; Skt. smasru- [n.] 'beard', Arm. mawrow-k' 'beard',
Alb. mjekre 'chin, beard' [f.], Lith. smakras, smakra 'chin', Latv. smakrs 'chin'<
*smok-ro-
Mala reflects *smaks(V)la, whereas maxilla can be from *smakslela, or has the
productive suffix -ilia. Pit. *smaksla- is explained from *smksla- by Schrijver 1991:
496, with anaptyctic a in front of three contiguous consonants. The a cannot reflect *o
with the unrounding of *mo- > ma-, since this unrounding only took place in open
syllable or in front of rC. Leumann 1977 adopts Thurneysen's alternative etymology
as *mand-sla 'chewing parts' to mandere 'to chew'. To its advantage, one may
adduce the fact that *-slo- is usually deverbal; to its disadvantage, that maxilla would
have secondary -xilla on the example of ala - axilla (also a body part).
Bibl.: WH II: 15, EM 379, IEW 968, Leumann 1977: 208, Schrijver 1991: 490,496.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: malleus 'hammer, mallet' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: malleolus 'fire-dart, mallet-shoot' (Sis.+), mallo 'kind of tumour on the
knees (with animals)' (Chiron.+); marculus 'small hammer' (Lucil.+).
Pit. *molalo- 'hammer', *molklo- 'hammer'.
PIE *molH-lo-, *molH-tlo- 'crushing instrument'. IE cognates: OCS mlatb, Ru.
molot, Cz. mlat 'hammer' < *molH-to-.
Malleus could be derived from the root for 'to grind'; for the semantics, cf. Ru. molot
'hammer' to PIE *mlH-. Schrijver 1991 shows that malleus must be an independent
formation of Latin: an adj. in -ens built to a form *malalo- < *mola-lo- (unrounding
of *o after m-) < *molH-lo~. In foursyllabic *malaleos, syncope yielded malleus.
Schrijver also argues that marculus must be a diminutive of a noun in *-ko~. He
assumes a dissimilation I > r from *malakelo- > *malkelo- > *markelo-. The original
form may have been *molH-ko~. Or, if the dim. was formed more recently,
*malalo'kelo- > *mallokelo- > *malkelo~. Yet these explanations are all rather
cumbersome. Seldeslachts-Swiggers 1995 propose PIE *molhtlo- > *moltlo- >
r
*molklo- > *morklo- > marculus. The dissimilation of the first *l to r would have to
precede the unrounding *mo- > *ma~, and thus be rather early. Of course, *molklo-
could have acquired ma- analogically from *malalo- as above. In view of the parallel
formation of *molhtlo- in BS1., this explanation for marculus seems preferable.
r
Bibl.: WH II: 16, 37, EM 380, IEW 716-719, Schrijver 1991: 455-457, LIV *melh -.
2
—• mold

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: malus 'unpleasant, bad' [adj. o/a] (Duenos inscr., Andr.+)
Derivatives: malignus 'mean, unkind' (P1.+), malignitas 'meanness' (P1.+), malitia
'wicked character, vice' (P1.+), malefacere 'to do wrong, harm' (P1.+), malevolus
'ill-disposed' (P1.+), other cps. in male-.
Pit. *malo-. It. cognates: O. mallom, malom [acc.sg.], mallud, malud [abl.sg.] 'bad,
evil' < O. *maluo- or *malio~, or a borrowing from Latin.
PIE *mol-o- 'bad'? IE cognates: Olr. mell 'destruction', Gr. ^lelsot; 'idle; unhappy',
maybe pXaotpti^co 'to slander'; Arm. mel 'sin', Lith. melas 'lie', Latv. maids 'mistake'.
The etymology given here is proposed by EM. It is semantically fine, but since it
concerns a collection of largely isolated words in different IE branches, it remains
uncertain.
Bibl.: WH II: 19f., EM 380, IEW 724, Schrijver 1991: 457, Untermann 2000:445.
m&lus 'pole, mast' [m. o] (P1.+)
Pit. *masdo-.
PIE *mh sdo- 'pole, mast'? IE cognates: Mir. maide 'stick' < *mazdio-; Olr. matdn
2
'club, stick?'; OHG mast 'pole, mast', OE mcest.
If cognate with the Gm. and Irish forms, mains must reflect *mados, with *d > I.
Schrijver 1991 regards a connection with mas, maris 'man' as attrative, in which case
'man' must be a metaphor for 'penis'. The original meaning would be 'pole, stick'.
Although conceivable, I regard this as uncertain. If rejected, no morphological
analysis of the Italo-Celtic-Germanic element *masd- remains. It may be a non-IE
loanword from the technical vocabulary.
Bibl.: WH II: 19, EM 381, IEW 701 f., Schrijver 1991:167. — mas

Derivatives: mammia 'breast' (PI.), mammeatus 'full-breasted' (PI.), mammosus
'large-breasted' (Lab.+), mammula 'nipple' (Varro+), mamilla 'nipple, spout' (Varro+).

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mancus 'maimed, crippled (at the hand)' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Pit. *manko-11
If cognate with rhanus 'hand', mancus must contain the athematic stem *man- 'hand'.
This etymology would imply a semantic shift *man-ko- 'handy' > 'handicapped,
having a defect of the hand'.
Bibl.: WH II: 23, EM 382, IEW 740f. manus,pecco
mandO, -ere 'to chew, bite' [v. Ill; pf. mandi„ ppp. mdnsum] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: mando 'glutton' (Lucil.+); manducus 'masked figure with champing
jaws' (P1.+), manducare 'to chew, eat' (Lucil.+), manducd 'glutton' (Pompon.+),
commanducare 'to chew up' (Lucil.+); masucius 'voracious' (Paul, ex F.).
Pit. *mand-n- 'to stir > chew'.
PIE *mt-n(e)-h - [pr.] 'to stir, whirl'. IE cognates: Skt. manthanti [3pi.act.], aor.
2
dmanthis-, pr.ps. mathya- 'to whirl round, rub, rotate stick to produce fire, shake',
maihita- 'rubbed out', manthd- [m.] 'stirred drink', Oss. yzmcmtyn / (ce)zmcentun 'to
shake, stir around'; Lith. mqsti, 3s. mencia 'to mix', OCS mqsti. Is. mqtg, Ru. mjasti
(obs.) 'to trouble, disturb' < BS1. *ment-. ,
The adj. masiicius (vowel length unproven) can be interpreted as *mdnsucius, remade
from mand-ucus on the basis of the ppp. mansum and/or the frequentative mdnsdre
(Nonius). WH, IEW and LIV assume that mando belongs to the root of Skt. math- 'to
rob, snatch away', Toch. mdnt- 'to hurt, separate' < PIE *menth -. Meiser justifies the
2
semantic difference by positing a shift from 'to tear away' —»• 'grab (loot)' —> 'shred'
—*• 'eat greedily' —»• 'chew', assuming that 'shred' can still be read in some of the
older attestations in Latin. I do not share this view: even in a passage like Andr. 39
cum socios nostros mandisset impius Ciclops, mandisset simply means 'had eaten',
literally 'had chewed'. We cannot go beyound 'to chew' for the synchronic state of
affairs in Latin. It seems more likely that mando belongs to the root *menth - 'to stir,
2
whirl', with a phonetic development as inpando (see s.v.).
Bibl.: WH II: 24, EM 3...

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: maneo 'to remain in the same place' [v. II; pf. mansi, ppp. mansum] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: mantare 'to remain, wait' (P1.+), ommentare 'to tarry' (Andr.);
permanere 'to remain, continue to be' (Ter.+), remanere 'to remain, be left' (Acc.+);
mansid 'staying, lodging' (Ter.+).
Pit. *m(o)n-e~.
PIE *mn-eh 'to remain'. IE cognates: Skt. dmaman [3s.ipf./aor.act.] 'has waited',
r
mamandhi [2s.ipv.act.] 'wait!', pari .. . mamanydt [3s.opt.act.] 'if he would miss /
neglect', YAv. upa.mqnaiia- 'to wait', OP manaya- 'to expect'; Gr. pevoo 'to remain,
stay', pipvto 'id.*, povr) 'staying, detention', Arm. mnam 'stay, expect'.
The ppp. mdnsus (whence mansT) is a secondary formation; the original form was
*mantos, as visible from mantare. The meaning 'remain' shows that manere probably
continues a PIE stative in *-eh, but the morphology of the root is disputed. LIV
r
assumes zero-grade *mn~, but Schrijver objects that we would expect an outcome
*mene~. He proposes that mane- represents *mone- with unrounding of *o in open
syllable after m\ the o-grade would stem from the perfect *me-mon-. For this pf.,
however, there is no certain evidence in the IE languages. LIV furthermore objects
that moneo 'to admonish' did not undergo unrounding, but Schrijver (1991: 472)
obviates this drawback by assuming that o-vocalism was restored in moneo but not in
mane-. Obviously, this is ad hoc, but so is a vocalization *mn-e- > mane-. Sihler
assumes that the a in maneo is a replacement for *mened on the model of habed,
iaced, placed, etc., but I see no motive for this replacement: Latin also has teneo,
mered, sedeo with e-vocalism. There is one other conceivable solution, viz. that
maneo does reflect *mn-e-, and that the regular vocalization of this sequence was
*mone- in Pit., at a stage preceding the Latin unrounding of *mo- > *ma- in open
syllables. In particular, initial m- could have played a rounding role in this process.
Bibl.: WH II: 26, EM 383, IEW 729, Schrijver...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: manus, -us 'hand' [f. u] (Lex XII, Andr.+)
Derivatives: manicae [f.pl.] 'handcuffs, sleeve' (P1.+), manicula 'small hand' (P1.+),
manualis 'held in the hand' (Sis.+), manudri 'to steal' (Lab. apud Gel!.), manuarius
'thief (Lab. apud Gell.), manuleus 'a long sleeve' (PL+), manuleatus 'having long
sleeves' (P1.+), manulearius 'maker of sleeved garments' (PL); commimts 'at close
quarters, close at hand' (Enn.+), eminus 'at long range' (Sis.+); mandare 'to hand
over, commit, command' (P1.+), commendare 'to entrust, recommend' (P1.+),
praemandare 'to recommend beforehand' (P1.+); manceps, -cipis 'contractor, agent'
(P1.+), mancu/ipium 'confiscation, ownership, slave' (Lex XII+), mancu/ipare 'to
transfer, sell' (P1.+), emancipare 'to release, make subservient' (P1.+); manu/ip(u)lus
'handful, bundle, unit' (P1.+), manu/ip(u)laris [m.] 'common soldier', manip(u)latim
'in companies' (P1.+); malluviae [f.pl.] 'water in which the hands have been washed'
(Fest., Paul, ex F.)\ mantelum 'hand-towel, napkin' (Lucil.+), mantelium (Varro+)
'hand-towel, napkin'; manubiae [f.pl.] 'prize-money, gain' (Naev.+), manubiarius
'concerned with booty' (PL); manubrium 'handle, haft' (P1.+); manupretium 'payment
for workmanship' (P1.+), manu/ifestus 'caught in the act, evident' (Lex XII+),
manifestdrius 'caught in the act' (PL+); maniimittere 'to free' (Varro+).
Pit. *man-u- 'hand'. It. cognates: O. manim [acc.sg.] 'hand', U. manuve [loc.sg. +
-en] , mani, manh[abl.sg.], manf [acc.pl.], SPic. manus [abl.pl.] 'hand' < *manu-\ O.
manafum [ls.pf.?] 'to give', aamanaffed, aamanafed, amanafed [3s.pf.] 'to

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: commission', emanafed [3s.pf.] 'id.' < *man-fe-f- 'to put at hands' (+ pre v. a-, e-) <
-; U. mantrahklu, mantraklu, mandraclo [acc.sg ] '?'.
PIE *mon-u- 'hand'. IE cognates: Olr. muin 'protection, patronage', Hit. maniiahh-'
'to distribute, entrust' < *mn-ieh -, OHG munt, OIc. tmind 'hand', OIc. mundr 'the
2
sum which the bridegroom has to pay for his bride' < *mn-to-.
All Latin forms contain the stem *manu- 'hand'. Schrijver argues that mancus
'maimed' proves a stem *man~, but this is uncertain. The nouns manicae and
*manulus (in manuleus) are regular derivations, and in comminus and eminus, we
have *-manus. The verb mandare corresponds with Oscan *manfef- from *manu- +
'to put' = 'to put at hand, commit'. Manceps belongs to capio 'take', and
manip(u)lus might be from *manu-plo- < *-plho- 'a hand-ful' (see pled). For
r
malluviae, see lavo, for manubiae etc. see habeo. Lat. mantelum probably reflects
*man-tergslo~. It is disputed whether the Pit. stem was *man- or *manu-. The only
form which seems to be impossible from *manu- is the U. acc.pl. manf, which
Schrijver argues to be a consonant stem: *man-ps > *man-Vns > *manVf > manf.
Weiss (1993: 46) explains it as analogical to ped-, with which it cooccurred in the set
phrase manibus pedibusque 'with all one's strength'. Schrijver compares Olr. muin
'protection' < *moni- and Hit. maniiahh- 'to hand over', and reconstructs *mon- for
Latin, with *mo- > ma- in open syllable. This seems possible, although the semantic
connection with Irish is not very compelling. The closest semantic and formal match
is with OIc. mund, OE mund, OHG munt 'hand' < *mn-to~. The morphology is
strange: is Pit. *monu- an original adjective?
Bibl.: WH II: 23-25, 29, 32, 34f., EM 381-386, IEW 740fi, Schrijver 1991: 458,
Untermann 2000: 448-451. —> suesco, tergeo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: manus 'good' [adj. o/a] (Carmen Saliare+; maybe Duenos inscr. acc.sg. manom).
Variant: manis.
Derivatives: manT (PL, Lucil.), mane (P1.+) 'early in the day, morning', Manes, -ium
[m.pl.] 'the spirits of the dead' (Lucr.+), Mania 'the mother of the Lares; (pi.) bogies'
(Varro+); immanis 'savage, brutal (P1.+), enormous (Varro+)'.
Pit. *mano-.
PIE *meh -no- 'good'. IE cognates: Olr. maith, W. mad, Bret, mat 'good' < *mh ti~.
2 r
A Latino-Celtic correspondence with regular ablaut, but the root etymology is
uncertain. LIV has a root *meh - 'to give a sign, wink', which might have developed
2
into 'be favourable'. But this is very speculative.
Bibl.: WH II: 25-28, EM 383f., IEW 693, Schrijver 1991: 143. maturus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: marceo 'to be withered, droop' [v. II] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: marcescere 'to wither' (Varro+); permarcere 'to be very weak'
(Enn.+); murcidus 'lazy' (Pompon.), muricidus 'faint-hearted' (P1.+).
Pit. *mark-e- [v.], *morko- [adj.].
PIE *mrk-eh 'to be soaked, be weak', *mork-o- 'weak'. IE cognates: Hit.
r
markiie/a-a 'to disapprove of, refuse' < *mrk-ie/o-\ Skt. pr. pra-mrcyati, caus.
marcayati, aor. mrksTsta sto damage, hurt', mrkta- 'hurt', marka- [m.] 'destruction,
death', mfc- 'the injuring, injure', OAv. msranc- / mwang- [pr.], maraxsaite
[3s.aor.sb.med.] 'to destroy', mardka- 'ruin', YAv. mahrka- [m.] 'death', ahv-maraxs
[nom.sg.] 'destroying life', Oss. marg 'poison'; Lith. mirkti 'to become weak,
soaked', merkti 'to soak', Ukr. morokva (dial.) 'quagmire, swamp', MHG meren 'to
dip bread into water or wine' < *meryen < *merk-. Maybe here Mir. bren, W. braen,
Bret, brein 'rotten, foul' < *mrekno~, *mrakn(i)o~; but these may also contain *b r-,
and belong to OHG braccho 'hound', Lat. fragro.
If marceo belongs here it must reflect PIE *mork- with unrounding of *mo- > *ma-,
or PIE *mrk- with vocalization to *mark- in front of another consonant, or as a
secondary full grade (thus Rix 1996: 61). Since murcidus and muricidus (maybe a
corruption of murcidus) are semantically very close to marceo (see Meiser 1998: 84),
and since they probably reflect *mork-, it is preferable to assume *mrk- for the verb.
Bibl.: WH II: 36f., EM 386f., IEW 739f„ Schrijver 1991: 458f„ 1995: 171f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mare 'sea; sea-water1 [n. r] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: mannus 'of the sea' (P1.+), semimarinus 'half belonging to the sea'
(Lucr.), transmarinus 'of overseas' (Varro+), maritimus 'of the sea, nautical' (P1.+),
mariscus 'rush' (Plin.+), marisca 'large and inferior kind of fig' (Cato+).
Pit. *mari- 'sea, lake', *marisko- [adj.].
PIE *mor-i- [n.] 'sea, lake'. IE cognates: Olr. muir [m.], W. mor 'sea' < *mori, Oss.
mal 'stagnant water', OPr. maty, Lith. mares < *moria, OCS morje, Ru. more [n.], morja
[nom.pl.] < *morio~, Go. mari-s aihws, OIc. marr 'sea', OHG mari, men 'sea, lake'.
Mare has resulted from unrounding of *mo- in open syllable. The adj. mariscus is
considerd to be obscure, but may well be a derivative of mare. Note that *mor-i-
referred to any large body of water, e.g. a lake. Lat. mari-iimus may have been
modelled on fini-timus and ex-timus. If the isolated Ossetic form is a loanword from
Gothic, the etymon *mori is restricted to the European languages.
Bibl.: WH II: 38-40, EM 387, IEW 748, Schrijver 1991:459.
margO, -inis 'retaining wall, border, margin' [m. n] (Varro+)
Pit. *marg-en-.
PIE *mrg-n- 'border'. IE cpgnates: Olr. mruig 'territory, area', W. bro 'country,
land', Gaul. PN Brogi-maros,^ethnonym Allobroges 'Foreigners' < PCI. *mrog-i~;
Hit. mark- / mark- 'to divide, separate' < *m(o)rg-;'-MoP marz 'region' < *morg-\
Go. marka 'area, border'. The different vocalization in Celtic is unexplained.
Schrijver explains margo and marceo from a change *mo- > *ma- in front of r +
velar, but see s.v. marceo. Since margo continues an n-stem, we may also explain
mar- as the regular vocalization of a zero grade in *mrg-n-.
Bibl.: WH II: 39f., EM 387, IEW 738, Schrijver 1991: 459.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mantus 'married, marital; husband' [adj.; m.] (P1.+)
Derivatives: maritare 'to mate, provide with a husband or wife' (Varro+),
commarftus 'fellow husband' (PI.).
Pit. *mareito-/ *manto- 'having a young woman'.
PIE *mor-(e)i- or *mor-ih - 'young woman'. IE cognates: W. morwyn, OCo. moroin
2
'girl, maiden' < PCI. *moreina~, MW merch 'daughter' < *mer-k-; Skt. marya-
'young man', YAv. mairiia- [m.] 'scoundrel, villain', Bac. papTjyo 'servant', Gr.
psTpcd; [m./f.] 'girl, boy' < *mer-io-\ OPr. mergo, -u, -a 'maiden', Lith. marti 'girl,
bride (without children)' < *mor-t-iH-, Lith. merga 'girl' < *mergh-h --
2
Derivation with possessive *-to- from a stem *morei-/*mori- > *mari- 'young
woman'; Latin shows unrounding of *mo- to *ma- in open syllable. The o-grade can
be reconstructed for Latin based on the fact that Celtic and Lith. also have *mor-. No
zero grade is attested for the root of this stem.
Bibl.: WH II: 40f, EM 387, IEW 738f., Thieme 1963: 238-241, Leumann 1977:
334, Schrijver 1991: 459f., 1995: 248.
Mars 'the god Mars; martial prowess, warfare' [m. /] (VOLat.+; Lapis Satricanus
mamartei [dat.sg.], CIL 49 (Tusculum) maurte [dat.sg.])
Derivatives: Marspiter 'Father Mars' [qnly nom.] (Varro+), Mavors, -tis 'the god
Mars, warfare' (P1.+); Marmar, Marmor 'Mars' (Carmen Arvale); Mdrtius 'of Mars,
of March, martial' (CIL 1.1513, Varro+); Mamers, -tis 'the Oscan form of Mars'
(Varro+).
It. cognates: O. papeptEi, mam(e)rt(ei) [dat.sg.] 'Mars', pajiepxivo [nom.sg.f.],
papeprivoup [gen.pl.m.] 'of Mars' (suffix *-mo-), papepxioi [dat.sg.m.],
mamerttiais, mamertiais [abl.pi.f.] 'of Mars' (*-«-); U. marte, marti, marti
[dat.sg.] 'Mars', marties, martier [gen.sg.m.], marti, martie [datsg.m.], martie
[voc.sg.m.], Mars, martses [abl.pi.f.] 'of Mars' (*-io-).
The forms Marmar and Marmor of the Carmen Arvale cannot be trusted, and are
better left out of consideration. WH assume Mavors to be the oldest form, but the
Lapis Satricanus now shows that mamart...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mas, maris 'male, masculine; a male' [adj.; m. i] (P1.+; gen.pl. marium Cic.)
Derivatives: masculus [adj.] 'male' (Varro+), masculus [m.] 'a male' (PI.+),
masculTnus 'of the male sex' (Varro+); semimas 'half-male' (Varro+).
Pit. *mas- / *mas-.
PIE *meh -(5)s / *mh -es, *mh -es-m, *mh -s-os?
2 2 2 2
The ablaut mas vs. maris is explained by Schrijver from an ablauting paradigm
*meh -(o)s, *mh -(e)s-. Adams 1985b: 246 has suggested that mas- in masturbor 'to
2 2
masturbate' contains the same word, the original meaning having been 'penis'. This
would have been used metaphorically for 'male, man'. According to Adams, mdlus
'pole' could belong here too. Watkins 1995: 535f. and Katz 1998a: 21 If. have
suggested that masturbor contains a PIE element *mos(t)- 'marrow'; but it seems
preferable to restrict the development *mo- > ma- to open syllables (cf. Schrijver
1991: 474 and the discussions of marced and margo above).
Bibl.: WH II: 46f., EM 388, Schrijver 1991: 167f. ^ malus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mateola 'wooden hammer' [f. a] (Cato)
Pit. *matejci-.
PIE *mot-(i-?) 'club, hoe'. IE cognates: Skt. matyd- 'club, harrow, roller', mati-kar
'to harrow, level the earth', OCS motyka, Ru. motyga 'hoe', OHG medela 'plough'.
Diminutive of a noun *matea 'hoe' vel sim., which could be from pre-Italic *mot~.
This noun might be derived from PIE *met- 'to measure' > 'throw / reap', which is
attested in the verb meto. Since *mot- denotes an instrument to work the land in all
languages, this would imply that the verb already had this connotation in PIE.
Bibl.: WH II: 49, EM 389, IEW 700, Schrijver 1991:460. meto

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mater, matris 'mother' [f. r\ (Andr.+)
Derivatives: maternus 'maternal, motherly' (Enn.+), matercula Tittle mother' (PI.+),
matrdna 'married woman, wife' (P1.+), matronalis 'of a married woman' (Lab.+),
matrimdnium 'marriage' (P1.+), mdtrdlia, -drum [n.pl.] 'the festival of Mater Matuta'
(Varro+), matrescere 'to become like one's mother' (Pac.), matrix 'female animal
kept for breeding' (Varro+), matertera 'mother's sister' (P1.+); materia /materies [f.]
'material, substance, wood' (PI.+), materiarius [m.] 'timber-merchant' (P1.+),
materinus 'rough, unused' (Cato).
Pit. * mater, *matr-. It. cognates: Fal. mate [nom.sg.], O. maatreis, U. matres,
matrer [gen.sg.], SPic. matereih [dat.sg.?] 'mother'.
PIE *meh -tr- 'mother'. IE cognates: Skt. matdr-, A v. matar-, MoP madar, Gr.
2
pfjrrip, Arm. mayr 'mother', Alb. moter 'sister', Lith, mote, OCS mati, OHG muoter,
Olr. maithir, ToB macer, ToA pacar 'mother'. »
The noun matertera contains the comparative suffix *-tero-: 'who is like a mother',
'the aunt on mother's side'. The PIE root etymology is pncertain. 'Mother' might be a
derivative in *-ter- to the nursery form *ma(ma) 'mum' (cf. Dor. pa 'mother'), but
phonologically it was apparently realized as *meh -, yielding a long vowel.
2
Bibl.: WH II: 49-52, EM 389f., IEW 700f., Giacomelli 1963: 251, Schrijver 1991:
384, Beekes 1995: 188, Untermann 2000: 441.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: maturus 'ripe, full-grown' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: maturare 'to make haste, speed, ripen', maturescere 'to mature, ripen'
(Varro+); immaturus 'unripe, not mature' (P1.+), praematurus 'very early, prompt';
Matuta 'the goddess of Dawn' (Lucr.+), matutmus 'of early morning' (Acc.+).
Pit. *matu- 'ripeness'. It. cognates: maybe O. maatufs [dat.pl.] name of a god.
PIE *meh -tu- 'ripeness'. ^
2
Both *maturo- 'ripe' and *matuto- 'of early morning' presuppose a noun *matu-
'ripeness, early moment'. The root is probably the same as in manus 'good'. Long -u-
can be analogical after the adj. of the type acutus, from a full-grade suffix *-te/ou-
(less likely), or from an ins.sg. *meh tuhj 'with ripeness' (whence *meh -tuh t-, and
r 2 r
thematization).
Bibl.: WH II: 53fi, EM 391, IEW 693, Leumann 1977: 316, Untermann 2000: 442.
—> manus
me 'me' [pron. pers. Is. oblique] (VOLat.+): acc.abl. med, me (med Duenos inscr.,
Garigliano Bowl, Tibur base, PI., Enn.), dat. mihei, mihi, mi, gen.sg. mei.
Derivatives: poss. adj. meus 'my' (VOLat.+: Garigliano Bowl meois [abl.pl.m.]).
Pit. acc. *me (stressed), *me (unstressed), dat. *me/ei, abl. *med, gen. *me/oi; poss.
*mejo~. It. cognates: Ven. mego, mego, meu [acc.]; Fal. med, met [acc.], [me]d
[?abl.]; PalU. miom [acc.sg.], U. mehe [datsg.] 'I, me' < acc. *me-om, dat. *me-hei.
PIE acc. *h]me,*hime, dat. *h meghio, abl. *himed, gen.loc. *himoi 'me'; *h mo-
1 1
[adj.] 'my'. IE cognates: Skt. mam [acc.], maya [ins.], mahyam [dat.], mat [abl.], mama
[gen.], mdyi [loc.], ma [acc.], me [gen.dat.encl.]; Gr. EPE [acc.], JIB [acc.encl.], poi [dat.];
OCS mq. 'me'; Go. mik 'me', NHG mich [acc.], Go. meina [gen.], mis [dat.].
The -d in the abl. can be inherited from PIE. In the acc., it can be the abl. form which
was used as the acc. The long vowel will stem from the stressed acc.sg. *me > *me.
The dat.sg. goes back to *me/ci as shown by Umbrian mehe. The gen.sg. met is really
the gen. of the poss. meus 'my' < *meio-,...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: medeor 'to heal, cure' [v. II] (Cato+)
Derivatives: medicus 'doctor' (P1.+), medicare 'to cure, heal', medicarT 'to cure'
(P1.+), medicTna 'surgery, remedy, healing' (P1.+), medicinus 'of healing' (Varro+),
medicamentum 'medicament, drug' (P1.+); remedium 'remedy' (Cato+).
Pit *med-e~.
PIE *med- 'to measure'. IE cognates: Olr. midithir 'to measure, judge', MW medu
'to think, possess, distribute', MCo. medhes 'to say' < PCI. *mede/o- Olr. mess
I
'judgement' < *med~tu-, air-med 'measure'; YAv. vT-maSaiianta 'they must
measure', vT-mad- 'healer, physician'; Gr. peSto 'to rule', peSopm 'to care for, think
of, |if|8o|iai 'to consider'; Go. mi tan, miton 'to measure, consider', OE metan, NHG
messen.
The meaning of medeor is based on a semantic shift from 'measure' to 'distribute a
cure, heal'. For a PIE stative verb (as assumed by LIV) or a frequentative, the e-grade
in med- would be surprising. If the intermediate phase was 'to judge' (cf. meditor),
medeor and its e-grade may have been grafted on the noun *medo(s)~ 'judgement,
law' (> modus) seen in U. mers, mers 'law'. We can then regard it as a stative verb
*med-e- 'to be a judge' > 'to be a healer, to heal' which was formed within Italic.
Bibl.: WH II: 54f., EM 392, IEW 705f., Schumacher 2004: 478ff., LIV l.*med-.
meditor, modus
meditor, -arT 'to think about constantly, contemplate, exercise' [v. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: permeditatus 'well rehearsed' (P1.+).
Probably derived from the ppp. *meditos belonging to medeor 'to be a judge, give a
judgement' (see s.v. medeor). Hence, *med-itaje/o- meant 'to judge constantly,
contemplate'.
Bibl.: WH II: 55f., EM 392f., IEW 705f., Leumann 1977: 548, LIV l.*med-.
medeor, modus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: medius 'central, middle' [adj. o/a] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: mediast(r)Tnus 'servant employed on general duties' (Cato+); dimidius
'half, divided medially' (P1.+), dimidiatus 'halved' (P1.+); medioxu/imus 'middle'
(P1.+); meridies 'midday, noon' (P1.+), merididnus 'of midday, southern' (Varro+).
Pit. *mepio~. It. cognates: O. mefiai [loc.sg.f.], mefi[u [nom.sg.f.], SPic. mefiin
[loc.sg. + -eri\ 'middle'; maybe SPic. mefistrui [dat.sg.m/n.]'?' if from *mef-istero~.
PIE *medhi-o- ' middle'. IE cognates: Gaul. Medio-lanum, -matrici, Olr. mid-
(*medhu-) 'mid-', Mir. mide 'middle', Olr. i-mmedon 'in medio', Skt. mddhya-, OAv.
maidiia-, YAv. maidiia- 'middle', Gr. peaoq, (ieoooq [adj.] 'middle', OIc. midr, OHG
mitti [adj.] 'located in the middle'.
Lat. dimidivs < *dis-medius. Wachter 2004: 311 explains medioxumits as * medio- +
*ksomo- 'earth' < *dhghomo-, but there is no evidence that PIE *ctcould yield ks in
Latin (texo is not a good example, see s.v.). The adj. is more easily understood as a
derivative in -xumus to mediocris, or to in medio (thus Leumann). Since occurs
as a recognizable suffix in some adverbs of place (Skt. adhi 'on', Gr. -i3i), it might be
hidden in medius. A meaning 'middle' for *me can be supported by Gr. \itza 'with',
Go. mip < *me-t~.
Bibl.: WH II: 57f., EM 393, IEW 706f., Leumann 1977: 498, Schrijver 1991: 133,
Beekes 1995: 220, Sihler 1995: 441, Meiser 1998: 91, Untermann 2000: 464f.
>
medulla 'marrow, pith, interior'iff. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: medullitus [adv.] 'inwardly, from the marrow' (P1.+).
Pit. *(s)meru-lo-l ' •
PIE *smer-u- 'marrow'. IE cognates: Olr. smiur (u), W. mer, Bret, mel 'marrow' <
PCI. *smeru-, OIc. smjgr [n.], OE smeoro, OHG smero 'fat' < *smerua~.
Semantically, the connection with *smer-u- is the most attractive one, but it requires a
change r > d which is the opposite of what one might expect. It could be interpreted
as a hypercorrection in view of *d > r in meridies (where it is a dissimilation) an...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: meio, -ere 'to urinate' [v. Ill; pf. mi(n)xi, ppp. mifnjctum] (C. Titius orat.+)
Derivatives: permeiere 'to urinate all over' (Lucil.+); circummingere 'to piss round'
(Petr.).
Pit. *meix(j)e~, *(-)ming-e/o~.
PIE pr. *h meigh-(i)e-, pr. *h mi-n-gh- 'to urinate'. IE cognates: Skt. ava mehanti
3 3
[3p,act.] 'they piss', amiham [ls.aor.], mehayati [caus.], mihe [inf.], Av. maeza-, Gr.
6[xsixco, Lith. myzti, Latv. mizt, SCr. mizati 'to urinate', Sin. m(a)zeti 'to flow, trickle,
drip', OE migan 'to urinate'.
WH regards -mingere as a secondary present form, based on the pf. minxi\ others, e.g.
LIV, regard mingo as old.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mel, mellis 'honey' [n. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: melculum 'sweetheart' (PI.), mella 'hydromel' (P1.+), mellarius [m.]
'bee-keeper' (Varro+), mellarium 'apiary' (Varro), mettitus 'honey-sweet' (Varro+),
mellina 'sweetness' (PI.)', mellilla 'sweetheart' (PL); mulsus 'mixed with honey'
(PI.+), mulsum 'drink made from honey and wine' (P1.+).
Pit. *melL
PIE *mel-i(-t) [nom.acc.] 'honey'. IE cognates: Olr. mil, W. Co. Bret, mel 'honey';
Hit. militt- / malitt- [n.], CLuw. mallit- 'honey', Pal. malitanna- 'having honey',
HLuw. malidima/i- 'honey-sweet' < *melit, *ml-it-os, Gr. (ieXi, -TO<; [n.] 'honey',
P-EXIOCCC ,Att. -xxa 'bee'; Arm. melr, gen.sg. melow 'honey', meiow, gen.sg. melowi
'bee'; Alb. mjal, mjalte 'honey' < *mel(i)tom; Go. milip [n.] 'honey', OE meledeaw
'nectar', OS mili-dou 'mildew', OE milisc 'honey-sweet'.
The origin of mel, mellis is disputed. Leumann 1977: 213 regards mell- as secondary
on the model offel,fellis 'bile'. Sihler posits a paradigm nom. *melid, gen. *melites >
*meld, *melites » *meld, *meldes > *mell, melles. It is uncertain whether *i in
*melid would be syncopated, but one may salvage Sihler's solution by assuming that
*melles regularly developed in the gen.sg., and *mell was then introduced into the
nom.acc.sg. Meiser reconstructs an «-stem *mel-n- in order to arrive at mell--, since
there is no supporting evidence for an «-suffix, this would imply that Latin
secondarily made a new oblique stem *mel-n- to the nom.acc. *mel < *meli. Latin
mulsus probably has -sus for *-tus on the model of salsus 'salted', but it may also be
original *m(o)l-t/d-to- 'provided with honey'.
Bibl.: WH II: 61f., 123, EM 394, IEW 723f., Sihler 1995: 230, Meiser 1998: 114.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: melior, -oris 'better' [adj. r] (Naev.+; acc.sg. meliosem (Varro); n. melius)
Derivatives: melius [adv.] 'better' (PL+), meliusculus 'somewhat better' (PL+).
PIE *mel-ios, -ios-. IE cognates: Gr. paXct 'very, quite', fifiAAov 'more, rather' (for
*jj.eXXov?), jiaXiaia 'mostly, quite especially'.
The PIE etymology is weak, since Gr. paXa shows no trace of an e-grade, and the root
is not attested in other derivatives. Words for 'good' can have many origins, and are
frequently renewed. Alternatively, one might connect (ieXco 'be anxious, care for',
Hit. mdl 'mind', CLuw. mdl- 'thought, idea' < *mol- (Kloekhorst 2008: 545f.).
Bibl.: WH II: 63, EM 394f., IEW 720 mulier, multus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: membrum 'body part, limb, member' [n. o] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: membratim 'limb by limb' (Varro+), membrana 'membrane, skin'
(Varro+).
Pit. *memsro-.
PIE *mems-ro- 'body-part, meaty part'. IE cognates: Olr. mir 'portion' [n.] <
*mems-ro-; Skt. mamsa- 'meat', mdms-pacani- [adj.f.] 'used for cooking meat', mas
[acc.sg.n.] 'meat'; Gr. pfjpa [pL] 'body-parts', pipcx; [n.] 'thigh' < *mes-ro-\ Arm.
v
mis, Alb. mish, OPr. mensa, menso, Lith. mesa, mensa Zem., Latv. miesa, OCS m§so,
Ru. mjaso 'meat' < *mems-om; CS mqzdra 'inner side of a rind', Ru. mezdrd 'inner
side (of hide)', OPo. miqzdra, mi^zdra 'membrane' < *mems-ro~; Go. mimz 'meat',
ToB misa [pi.] 'meat'.
Most IE forms for 'meat' can go back to a n. *memso-, but Skt. shows traces of an
athematic form *mes- without internal nasal. It is unclear how -m- entered this root:
was the original form a reduplicated *me-ms-l Slavic, Irish and Latin continue
*mems-ro- with the derived meaning 'body part, membrane'. According to Vine
2002: 333, the original PIE form may have been a collective *mems-reh , from which
2
*memsrom was back-formed. Whether Gr. pfjpa also continues this noun seems to be
disputed; as for the meaning, it would perfectly fit If PIE had *mes- in alternation
with *mems~, Greek may have used *mes- as the basis to derive *mes-ro- 'body part'.
Bibl.: WH II: 641, EM 395, IEW 725, Schrijver 1995: 452, Meiser 1998: 119. -»
mensa

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: memim 'to remember' [v. pf.] (Naev.+; ipv. memento)
Derivatives: commeminisse 'to remember' (P1.+), comminisci [pf. commentus sum]
'to think up, invent, feign' (PI.+), recomminisci 'to recollect' (PI.), remiriisci 'id.'
(Ter.+), commentum 'scheme, device' (P1-+), commentari 'to think about, prepare'
(P1.+), commentarius/m 'notebook, record' (Varro+), recommentari 'to recollect'
(PI.); memor, -oris 'mindful' (P1.+), memorare 'to say, tell' (PI.+), memoratus, -us
'account' (P1.+), memordbilis 'remarkable' (P1.+), commemorare 'to recall' (P1.+),
commemorabilis 'remarkable' (P1.+), commemoramentum 'reminder' (Caecil.+),
commemordtio 'id.' (Ter.+), immemor 'forgetful' (PI.+), immemorabilis 'not fit to be
repeated; unable to recollect' (P1.+); memoria 'memory, remembrance' (P1-+),
memoriter 'accurately' (P1.+). Lat. minisci, and mentus (Paul, ex F.) are not
trustworthy.
Pit. pf. *me-mon-, *me-mn-, ppa. *me-mn-os-, ppp. *mn-to~, pr. *men-i(e)-;. It.
cognates: maybe Ven. metlon 'offering' if from *men-tlo-; maybe O. memnim
[acc.sg.] '?' < *me-m(e)n-i(H)o~.
PIE pf. *me-mon~, *me-mn~, pr. *men-i- or *mn-ie~, ppp. *mn-to~. IE cognates: Olr.
muinithir* 'to intend', do-muinethar* 'to think, believe' < PCI. *manie/o-; Skt.
mdnyate [pr.] 'to, think, believe, consider', amata [3s.aor.med.], -mamnate
[3d.pf.med.], mata-. 'thought, regarded as'; Av. mainiia- 'to invent, think1; Gr.
paivopai 'to rage', pf. pepova, pi. pe|iapev 'to intend to', Gr. -pa-roc; 'thought'; Lith.
mihti 'to recall', mineti, 3s. mini 'to mention', OCS mbneti 'to think', Is. mbnjg, 3s.
mbnit < *mn-eh; Go. man 'I think, I believe'.
r
The pf. ipv. reflects *me-mn-tod. The inchoative -mimsci suggests an earlier present
formation *men-i- or *mn-ie~, which is confirmed by other IE languages (cf. Schrijver
2003 for the /-present). The noun commentum can be interpreted as a substantivized
ppp. The older etymologies (WH, EM) derive memor from the root *(s)mer- 'to
remind'. More recent works (...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mendum 'physical blemish or fault; error' [n. o] (Varro+)
Derivatives: menda 'fault; blemish' (Lucil.+), menddx 'untruthful, lying' (P1.+),
mendacium 'a lie' (P1.+), mendaciloquus 'lying' (PI.); mendicus 'beggarly, destitute'
(P1.+), mendicus [m.] 'beggar' (P1.+), mendicimonium 'beggary' (Lab.), mendicitas
'destitution' (P1.+), mendicare 'to beg, be a begger' (P1.+), mendicabulum 'a beggar's
instrument' (P1.+), mendiculus 'beggarly' (PI.).
Pit. *m(e)ndo-/a-.
IE cognates: Olr. mennar 'blemish' (< *mend-rd~), mind 'sign, mark' (< *mndu?),
W. mann 'place', manngeni 'birthmark'; Hit. mant- 'something harming', Lyd. metli-
(something negative), Lyc. mete- 'harm' < *mond- (?).
Probably, menda is based on the old plural of mendum, even if menda is attested
somewhat earlier. If Olr. mind is cognate, we have an Italo-Celtic correspondence
*m(e)ncfh)-o-. If the original meaning was 'sign, mark', one might tentatively derive
the noun from PIE *mn- 'to think', as *mn-d- or as *mn-cth 'to set the mind, be
r
attentive'. Obviously, these are just theoretical possibilities.
Bibl.: WH II: 68f., EM 396, IEW 729f., Rieken 1999:42f., Kloekhorst 2008: 555.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mens, mentis 'mind' [f. i] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: amens 'insane, frantic' (P1.+), amentia 'madness, frenzy' (Ter.+),
demens 'mad' (P1.+), dementia 'madness' (P1.+), dementire 'to lose one's mind'
(Lucr,+), mentid 'mention' (Andr.+); mentiri 'to lie' (P1.+), ementiri 'to falsify,
invent' (PI.+).
Pit. *mgti-.
PIE *mn-ti- [f.] 'thought, mind'. IE cognates: Skt. mati- [f.] 'thought, mind', Av.
"maiti-, Lith. mintis 'thought, idea', OCS pamqtb 'memory, monument' < *mn-ti~;
Go. ana-minds 'suspicion', Go. ga-minpi [n.] 'memory', OHG gi-munt 'recollection,
memory'.
By its fourth conjugation, mentire still shows its origin as a denominative to *mnti-.
The meaning 'to lie' derives from a semantic change 'to have second thoughts, be
inventive' > 'conjure up, lie'.
Bibl.: WH II: 68-70, EM 396f., IEW 726-728, LIV 1. *men-. memini, moneo
m£nsa 'table (for sacred offerings, for meals)' [f. a\ (Naev.+)
Derivatives: mensula 'small table' (P1-+).
Pit. *menssa- 'measured'. It. cognates: U. mefa, mefa [acc.sg.], mefa [abl.sg.], mtfe
[loc.sg.] 'a certain sacrificial object, maybe cake'.
The Latin noun is probably the feminine of the ppp. mensus 'measured' to metior,
which was formed by analogy with pensus 'weighed' < *pend-to~. If U. mefa < Pit.
*menssa- (Meiser 1986: 77) is cognate with mensa, its phonological form would
show that the analogical participle 'measured' had already been formed in Pit. (cf.
Meiser 1986: 164). In Latin, the meaning then shifted from the offering itself to the
object on which the offerings were placed.
Bibl.: WH II: 70, EM 397, IEW 703f., Untermann 2000:463f. ™»metior

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mentum 'chin' [n. o] (P1.+)
Pit. *mnto~.
PIE *mn-to- >chin, mouth'. IE cognates: W. mant 'mouth, jaw, beak', Hit. meni-
[n.], mena- 'face, cheek' < *men-ihi, *men-eh , Go, munps [m.], OIc. munnr, OE
2
mud, OHG mund 'mouth'; OHG mindel, OIc. mel [n.] 'mouth-bit of a bridle'.
Possibly cognate with the verbs e-, promined, which points to a verbal root *men~ 'to
rise up, protrude'. An original verbal adj. *mn-to~, used with different nouns (e.g.
*genu- [n.] 'jaw, cheek'), would explain the gender difference between m. in Gm. and
n. in Italo-Celtic. Hit. meni- might continue an old dual, cf. Rieken 1999: 56f.
Bibl.: WH H: 72f., EM 398, IEW 968, LIV ?3.*men-. ^ minae, mons

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: me5, -are 'to proceed, traverse' [v. I] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: meatus, -us 'movement, course' (Lucr.+); commeare 'to go regularly,
travel' (P1.+), commedtus, -us 'passage, supplies' (P1.+), praetermedre 'to move past'
(Lucr.+), remeare 'to return, recede' (P1.+); commetare 'to go constantly' (P1.+);
semita [f.]' side-path, track, pavement' (P1.+), semitatim 'by side-roads' (Titin.+),
frames, -itis [m.] 'footpath, track, course' (P1.+).
Pit. *mejo~.
PIE *h mei-o- 'to (ex)change'. IE cognates: MW tre-myn- 'to go past'; Skt. vi
2
mayante 'they alternate', apa mayetat 'he should loan', mdyas- 'refreshment,
enjoyment', YAv. fra-mita- 'changed', maiiah- [n.] 'satisfaction, pleasure'; Gr.
dpeipco (< *h mei-gw-) 'to exchange, change'; Lith. mit 'to exchange'; Lith. mamas
2
'exchange'; OCS mena 'exchange, change'; OCS minpti, Ru. mirrnt' 'to pass', OCS
mimo 'by, past', Cz. mijeti, Po. mijac 'to pass'.
The verb -meare might be denominal to a noun 'movement, passage'; a deverbal verb
of the type occupare is less likely, since -meare is intransitive. Commetare can be
from *kom~mej-e-tafrequentative to meare. The nouns tramit- < *trans-mit- 'going
across' and semita < *se-mit- 'going aside' may show a r-suffix added to the
compounded root noun. The etymology is based on the assumption of a semantic shift
'to change' > 'change places' > 'go past' (*mi-n- in BS1., Celtic), 'change places' >
'go on, proceed' (Latin). The Latin verb would derive from a noun *h mei-o-
2
'change, movement', which is not attested outside Latin. LIV reconstructs this root
without initial laryngeal, but then Gr. apeipoo has to be separated.
Bibl.: WH I: 254, II: 73, EM 135, 398, IEW 710, Leumann 1977: 548, Schrijver
1991: 20, Lindner 2002: 234f„ Schumacher 2004: 486, LIV 2.*mei-. -> moenia,
mumis

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: merda 'dung, excrement' [f. a] (Hor.+)
Pit. *(s)merd-a-.
PIE *smerd-h - 'stench'. IE cognates: Lith. smirditi, 3s. smirdi i smirda 'to stink' <
2
*smrd~, smardas, Latv. smards 'smell, odour' < *smord-o~, Ru. smorod (dial.), Ukr.
smorid, gen. smorodu 'stink' < *smrd-o-s.
Latin presupposes a semantic development of a collective *smerd-h - 'what stinks' to
2
'excrement'.
Bibl.: WH II: 74f., EM 399, IEW 970, LIV *smerd-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mereo 'to earn, gain (act.); to deserve (dep.)' [v. II; pf.act. meruT, pf.dep. meritus
sum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: merenda 'light afternoon meal' (P1.+), meretrix 'courtesan' (P1.+),
meretricula [f.] 'courtesan' (P1.+), meretrTcius 'of a courtesan' (PI+), meritum
'service, reward' (PI.+), meritare 'to earn, draw pay' (Cato apud Paul, ex F., Cic.+);
commerere 'to commit; merit fully' (P1-+), demerere 'to earn, oblige' (P1.+), emerere
'to serve out, complete' (P1.+), immerito 'unjustly' (P1.+), immerens 'undeserving'
(PI.+), promerere 'to merit, deserve' (P1.+); Morta 'Fate' (Andr.).
Pit. *mer-e- 'to earn', *mor-to- 'earned'.
PIE *(s)mr-ehi- 'to earn'? IE cognates: Olr., Mir. mart [o], mairt [i] 'death fate',
MW marth 'untimely death', OW marth 'wonder', Co. marth, Bret, marzh 'miracle'
< *mf-sto -sti-; Gr. pEipopai 'receive one's share', pf. Aeol. eppope 'participate' <
I
*se-smor-e Ion. ei'papxai < *se-smr-tai, pepoq [n.] 'part, share', popoq [m.] 'fate,
i
violent death'.
Schrijver (2004: 293) considers it likely that the verb is denominal because it shows
e-grade *(s)mer- instead of zero-grade (as in the statives) or o-grade (causatives). Yet
denominal verbs of the second conjugation usually are intransitive, and in most cases
their derivational basis can still be seen. Thus, merere remains problematic. One
might envisage an original denominal *mor-eie~ 'acquire a share' with introduction of
the e-grade from a noun such as *mer-os- (Gr. pepoq), but this remains a mere
hypothesis. Livingston (2004: 9) regards mereor as an original stative verb, because
of the equation with Gr. peipopai. Livingston plausibly interprets Andronicus' form
Morta as the f. of *mf-to- 'received as one's share'; this would be a close match to the
Celtic words *mf-sto/i- discussed by Schrijver 2004. A derivation from 1. *(s)mer-
'to remember' seems unlikely for semantic reasons.
Bibl.: WH II: 75f., EM 399, IEW 969f., Schrijver 2004, LIV 2.*smer-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mergae 'reaping-board' [f.pl. a\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: merges, -itis 'sheaf of com (Verg.+).
Merges can be understood as 'what one can take with the mergae'. Leumann 1977:
372 has turned the meanings around (merges 'Heugabel', merga 'Garbe'), but this is
not supported by the texts. Many scholars doubt the connection with Gr. apepyco 'to
pluck (flowers), squeeze olives', but the two are quite similar. It is a different
question whether these two forms stem from PIE *h mer/lg- 'to wipe' (Skt. marj-,
2/3
Av. marz-, Gr. opopyvupi 'to wipe' maybe Gr. apsXyco 'to milk'), since the semantic
connection is not straightforward. Lat. mergae and Gr. apepyo) may continue a
separate PIE root *h merg.-, or they might go back to a non-IE loanword of
2
agricultural terminology.
Bibl.: WH II: 76, EM 399, IEW 738, Schrijver 1991: 20.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mergo, -ere 'to plunge, immerse' [v. Ill; pf. mersi, ppp. mersum] (Cic.+)
Derivatives: mergus 'certain sea-bird' (Lucil.+); mertdre 'to submerge, overwhelm'
(Acc.+), mersare 'to dip, submerge' (Lucr.+); emergere 'to come out of, emerge'
(Ter.+), immergere 'to dip, plunge into' (P1.+), submergere 'to cause to sink'
(Lucr.+). ,
Pit. *mezge/Q~.
PIE *mesg-e/o- 'to sink, wash'. IE cognates: Skt. majjanti [3p.pr,] 'to sink, plunge
under'; Lith. mazgoti, Latv. mazgat 'to wash' < *mozg-.
Probably, mergus is deverbal to mergo: the bird which 'dives into' the water.
Bibl.: WH II: 76f., EM 399, IEW 745f., Meiser 1998: 119, LIV *mesg-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: merula 'blackbird' [f. d] (Naev,+)
Derivatives: meruleus 'coloured like a blackbird' (PI.).
Pit. *mesVla- 'blackbird'.
IE cognates: W. mwyalch 'blackbird', Bret, moualc'h < PBrit. *miialx <
* me/is al-(s)kd\ Olr. stmolach, EMoIr. smolach, Molr. smol 'blackbird', Molr. smaol
'thrush' < Olr. *smoelach, gen. smolchae, < *(s)moilax borrowed from Old British
*moialx; OHG amsla, ama/i/usla, OE osle 'blackbird' < WGm. *amsloti-,
*ama/e/uslon-. Maybe OHG meisa, OS mesa, OE mase < PGm. *maison- 'tit' is also
cognate.
The British and Latin forms go back to *mesVI-, the Gm. forms to *amsl-, *amsVJ-.
Schrijver 1997a argues that this points to a loanword from a non-IE substratum
language in Europe.
Bibl.: WH B: 111, EM 400, IEW 35f., Lloyd-Springer 1988: 211, Schrijver 1997a:
307-311.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: merus 'pure' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: meracuhis 'undiluted' (P1.+); merobibus [adj.] 'that drinks unmixed
wine' (PI.); submerus 'nearly undiluted' (PI.).
Pit. *mero-.
PIE *merH-o- 'remaining, pure'. IE cognates: Hit. marri [adv.] 'just so,
gratuitously'.
The word has been compared with Gr. pappmpco 'to flash, sparkle', but even if 'pure'
can sometimes be paraphrased as 'clear' (thus EM), there is no compelling reason to
derive 'pure' from 'shining'. Puhvel connects Hit. marri, which he translates as 'just
like that, at random'; this seems a better solution than Kloekhorst's (2008: 557) 'with
a glimpse', who identifies marri as the hapax marra/i- '(sun)light'. If marri 'just so'
goes back to a n. *morHi, it could be related to Lat. merus if from *merH~oThe
original meaning of the root may have been 'remaining, core, pure'. This, then,
enables a connection with Lat. mora 'delay' < *morH-h 'stiffening, remaining'.
r
Bibl.: WH II: 78, EM 400, IEW 734, Schrijver 1991: 20, Puhvel 2006. mora

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: merx, -cis 'commodity, goods, merchandise' [f. fc] (P1.+; nom.sg. mers PI.)
Derivatives: mercarJ 'to buy, trade' (P1.+), mercatus, -us 'market' (P1.+), mercator
'merchant' (P1.+), mercdtorius 'mercantile' (PI.), mercatura 'trade' (P1.+);
mercimomum 'merchandise, enterprise' (P1.+); commers, -rcis 'friendly intercourse'
(PL), commercari 'to buy' (P1.+), commercium 'trade, relationship, sexual
intercourse' (P1.+), praemercari 'to buy in advance' (PL+); merces, -edis 'payment'
(P1.+), mercen(n)arius [adj./m.] 'working for pay; hired worker' (P1.+), mercedimerus
'working for hire' (Lucil.); Mercurius 'the god Mercury' (Andr.+); Mercurialis 'of
M/mercury' (Cato+).
Pit. *merk- 'trade, exchange'. It. cognates: Fal. mercui [dat.] 'deity Merk-', O.
mirikui [dat.sg.] probably a deity like Mercurius < *merk-o/u-; O. amirikum
[acc.sg.] 'commerce' < a- or ad- + *merk~, O. amiricatud [ppp., abl.sg.] 'sold' vel
sim., denom. verb. According to Untermann, O. mirk- was borrowed from Latin.
All derived from a stem *merk- also found in Faliscan and Oscan. The god Mercurius
was probably the god of exchange. According to WH, the god's name was borrowed
from Etruscan; in principle, the same is possible for the stem *merk- altogether.
Bibl.: WH II: 74, 78f., EM 400, IEW 739, Untermann 2000: 85f, 479.
-met '!' [ptcle. of emphasis] (P1.+: egomet 'I', nosmet 'we', vdsmet 'you', semet
'themselves', ipsemet 'he himself)
Pit. *-metil

PIE *-me-ti 'with'?
Uncertain. One might think of PIE *me 'with' plus an added *-ti (as in Latin aut), or
of PIE *sme, as in Skt. sma, sma 'just, really', smat 'together', Av. mat 'with', Gr.
(IF[V (Dor. Aeol. juav), pev 'certainly, true'.

content:

Derivatives: metari 'to measure off, lay out' (Caes.+).
Pit. *meta~.
PIE *mehto- 'measure, measurement'.
r
Metari is a denominal verb to meta: 'use turn-posts to mark off the land'. WH and
IEW surmise a connection with PIE *meith - 'to exchange, remove', but this is
2
semantically unconvincing: the principal meaning of meta is 'cone* or 'post'. Also,
the required reconstruct *meitH-o- with its lengthened grade is morphologically
unattractive. If the 'cone' or 'post' was used as a mark for measuring distance or
height from the beginning, meta could be derived from the root *meh 'to measure'.
r

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: meto, -ere 'to reap, harvest' [v. Ill; pf. messuT (rare), ppp. messum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: mess is, -is [f.; acc. messem, abl. messe] 'reaping, crop' (P1.+), messio
'harvesting' (Varro), messor 'reaper' (P1.+); demetere 'to mow, pick, reap' (Cato+).
Pit. *met-e- 'to reap'.
PIE *met-e- 'to measure'. IE cognates: W. medi 'to mow, harvest', MBret. midijf1 to
harvest' < PCI. *met-e/o-\ Lith. mesti, Is. metii 'to throw', Lith. metas 'year, time',
mdtas 'measure'; CS mesti 'to throw, sweep' (lsg. metg) , Ru. mesti 'to sweep'.
Unrelated: Hit. dm-, hane/iss-z' 'to wipe', CLuw. am(ma)ssa- / am(ma)ssi(ia)~ 'to
wipe' < *h (o)mhs-, hamesha- 'spring, time of harvest' < *h mehsh o-, Gr. apr)
2 r 2 r 2
'shovel', apaco 'to mow, cut' (< *h mh eh2-ie/o-), &pT]To<; [m.] 'harvest'; OE mawan,
2 r
OHG maen 'to mow', MHG mat, OE meed 'reaping'.
According to Sommer 1914: 568, messrn replaced *messi on the model of its antonym
seruT 'I sowed'. The root etymology is disputed. Schrijver hesitantly posits a root *h m-
2
with different suffixes: *h m-et- in meto and PCI. *met~, *h m-eh in Greek apato and
2 2 r
Germanic. Yet the Anatolian words for 'to wipe' show that the root of that verb and
Greek dpcua was triconsonantal *h mh. LIV derives *met- 'to throw' from 'measure'
2 r
(via 'to aim'), but separates 'to reap'; to my mind, a semantic shift from 'measure,
measure off to 'reap, harvest' is conceivable, so that we need only one root *met-.
Bibl.: WH II: 82f., EM 401, IEW 703, Schrijver 1991: 20, Schumacher 2004: 483,
Kloekhorst 2008: 182f., 279f., 285f., LIV ?1 *met-, ?2. *met-. mateola

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: metus, -us 'fear, alarm' [m. u] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: metuere (metui, metutum) 'to be afraid of, fear' (P1.+), praemetuere 'to
dread in advance' (Lucr.+); metuflcidosus 'apprehensive, awful' (P1.+).
Pit. *met-u~.
Lat. metuculosus was formed on the basis of perlculosus 'dangerous', which is
situated in the same semantic sphere. Klingenschmitt (2004: 24If.) connects metus
with Olr. moth [m.J 'astonishment' < *moto~, denom. mothaigedar 'to be astonished'.
While semantically attractive, this explanation does not address the phonetic aspects of
the etymology. Schrijver (1991: 467-470) posits a rule according to which *e yields
Lat. o after m, w in front of CV, if C is not r, U and V is not a front vowel. Metus would
then be an exception, since all forms in the paradigm would have VOLat. *-tu- or
*-tou~. Schrijver's rule is contested by Meiser 1998: 82, who regards several forms in
mo- as analogical. In his view, metus can be regular from Pit. * metus.
Bibl.: WH II: 83f., EM 402, Leumann 1977: 341, Meiser 1998: 82.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mica 'grain, crumb' [f. <5] (Cato+)
Pit. *meik-a~.
PIE *meik-h - 'blinking'.
2
Nyman 1987 shows that mica originally referred to a 'grain of salt', and subsequently
to any 'glittering crystal(line) particle'. These semantics lead him to derive mica from
the same root as micare 'to quiver, dart, flash', viz. as the 'glittering' particle. This
explanation is formally more attractive that the traditional connection with Gr.
(o)|iTKpoc; 'small'. Semantically, I see no objections.
Bibl.: WH II: 85, EM 402, IEW 966f. mico

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mico, -are 'to quiver, dart, flash' [v. I; pf. micui\ (Enn.+)
Derivatives: dimicare 'to fight, contend' (Sis.+), emicare 'to dash out, jump forth'
(PL+), promicare 'to sprout, shoot forth' (Naev.+).
Pit. *mikaje~.
PIE *mikH-(e)ie- 'to blink'. IE cognates: Olr. de-meccim 'despise', W. ed-mygaf'l
admire'; Ru. mikat' (dial.) 'to stuff (a bag)', Cz. mikati 'to move abruptly', USorb.
mikac 'to blink' < PIE *meik-, CS mbCbta 'vision, apparition', Ru. mecta 'dream,
I
day-dream' < *mik-.
The pf. micuT suggests a stem *mikV-, hence a laryngeal-final root. The basic meaning
of Latin is 'to dash, spring forth, move fast back and forth'. In Slavic, the same
meaning co-occurs with 'to blink' and 'to see'. In Celtic, only the latter is attested.
Hence, the PIE meaning may have been 'to blink'.
Bibl.: WH I: 353, II: 86, EM 402, IEW 712f, Meiser 2003: 138, LIV ?*mei(k)h -.

migrO, -are 'to change residence, move' [v. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: admigrare 'to go and live with' (PL), commigrare 'to migrate' (P1.+),
demigrare 'to go away, depart' (P1.+), emigrdre 'to move out' (PL+), immigrare 'to
go and take up residence' (P1.+), remigrare 'to move back to one's home' (P1.+).
Pit. *migro~.
PIE *h mi-gw-ro- 'changing'. IE cognates: Gr. dpeiPoo 'to change', &poij36<^

'changing', apoipf) 'change'.
Probably a denominal verb to *migro~, which can be a ro-adj. to the same stem

Pit. *meljo-T
PIE *melh -i [n.]? IE cognates: Gr. peXivri [f.] 'millet', Lith. malnos f. pi. 'kind of
2
millet'.
If from *meliom with i-mutation (of the type nihil, similis), milium can be cognate
with Gr. (ieXivrj. The Latin, Gr. and Lith. words could be independent derivatives of
*melh - 'to grind' (e-grade in Gr. and Lat., o-grade in Lith.), thus 'the grain which
