content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: belonging to the thousands' (Varro+), milie(n)s [adv.] 'a thousand times' (PL+).
Pit. *smtxesti.
PIE *sm-ih -ghes-l-ih 'having one thousand'. IE cognates: Skt. sahasra-, Av.
2 2
hazagra- < Ilr. *sajhasra- < PIE *sip-g'leslo- 'having one thousand'; Gr. x&toi, Ion.
X£&ioi, Aeol. xeUioi 'thousand' < PGr *khehliio- < PIE *ghesli(H)o- 'of a thousand'.
Double -//- in mille can be interpreted as indicating palatal /, not necessarily double
*-//-. The mostly accepted etymology as *sm-ih -ghes-l-ih2 'one thousand' is based on
2
the comparison with the Ilr. forms for 'thousand' which reflect *sm-ghes-lo-, and with
the Greek form. Adjectival *ghes-lo- 'heap' was substantivised to f. gheslih -, and
2
'one' consequently acquired the f. form. Meiser 1998 posits a phonetic development
from *sm-ih -ghes-l-ih > Pit. *smixesti > *miheli > *mihile > mille. He attributes
2 2
final short *-i in Pit. to loss of the final laryngeal in *-ih , but this development is not
2
certainly attested (its reconstruction for the o-stems is uncertain). Alternatively, Pit.
may have analogically changed the inflectional category of 'thousand' to a n. /-stem;
the model may have been centum 'hundred', Sihler explains n. -e as a back-formation
to the pi. milia (cf. mare - maria).
Bibl.: WH II: 88f„ EM 403, IEW 446, Leumann 1977: 141, 491, Coleman 1992:
407, Sihler 1995: 424f„ Meiser 1998: 174. hostus, miles, sem-

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: eminere 'to stick out, protrude' [pf. eminui\ (P1.+), eminulus 'projecting' (Lucil.+),
minere 'to lean forward, project' (Lucr.).
Pit. *(eks-)men-e- 'to stick out', *men-a~ 'part which sticks out' > 'threat'.
PIE *m(e)n-eh.
r
In theory, these forms could reflect *min- or a stem *men- which changed to -min- in
non-initial syllable, and was thence imported into minae and minari. The fact that
e-minere can be connected with mentum and mons makes the second solution more
attractive. Thus, the root *men- 'to stick out, protrude' is preserved in stative
e-min-ere. We may assume minae < *men-a- meaning first 'protruding part' and then
'threat', and from mind- was derived minari'. The verb served as the basis for minax
and for the frequentative mini tare. Lucr. minere is a recent back-formation to
eminere. The e-grade in *men-e- might be from *mn-eh (unless *mnV- yielded Latin
r
*monV- or *manV~, see s.v. maneo).

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: younger', mbnjbsi [f.], mbnje [n.], Ru. men'sij [m.] 'smaller, lesser, younger' <
*mi~n-i(e/o)s-jo~, Go. mins 'less', minniza 'smaller, lesser' < *minuiza-\ ToB marwe
'small, young'.
Lat, minimus, minister (< *minos-tero~) are derived from *minos, -os- by means of
productive processes. Lat. miscellus < *minuscellus, dim. to minusculus. The meaning
'miscellaneous' seems to have come about due to the semantic influence of miscere.
For the PIE etymology, I follow Meiser's argument that minor cannot be based on an
old w-stem. The Gm. and BSI. adj. meaning 'less' are built on a form *minu-is-, which
also looks quite secondary. Hence, the original comp. *meihj-i6s- was apparently
influenced by the nasal present *mii}u-(ie-) in Italic, yielding *mi~n-os, -es-, -os-
' smaller'.
Bibl.: WH II: 91-93, 95, EM 404f„ IEW 711, Leumann 1959: 47-49 (for miscellus),
Meiser 1998: 154, Untermann 2000: 471,477f., LIV l.*meiH-. nimis

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mlrus 'remarkable, astonishing' [adj. o/a] (Andr.+; usually predicative minim est)
Derivatives: mirari 'to be amazed' (PI.+), miraculus [adj.] 'freakish' (P1.+),
miraculum 'wonder, marvel' (Cato+), rriirabilis 'marvellous' (P1.+); admirari 'to be
surprised, admire' (P1.+), demirari 'to wonder, be utterly astonished' (P1.-H); permirus
'very remarkable' (P1.+); mirificus 'amazing' (Ter.+); rriirio 'an ugly mask' (Acc.
apud Varronem).
Pit. *smeiro~.
PIE *smei-ro- [n.] 'laughter'. IE cognates: Skt. a-smera- 'without smiling', OE
smcer(e) 'lips', gal-sm&r(e) 'inclined to laughter' < PGm. *smairjaOE sm&ran 'to
mock'; Skt. smayate 'smiles', Gr. <pilo-p|U8i8Ti<; 'who likes to laugh', Latv. smiet 'to
laugh', OCS smijati sg, ToB smiyam 'smiles'.
These words are often connected with PIE *smei- 'to laugh', but EM and Sihler are
sceptical about the semantic justification. Indeed, it is difficult to arrive from an adj.
PIE *smei-ro- 'laughing' or 'ridiculous' at 'remarkable'. Vine 2002: 334 solves this
problem by observing that the oldest form was probably minim, from which the adj.
mirus was back-formed. Thus, we may be dealing with an original collective noun
*smei-ro- 'laughter, smiling'.
Bibl.: WH II: 94f., EM 406, IEW 967f„ Sihler 1995: 214, Meiser 1998: 112.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mix'; Skt. micchamana- [ptc.med.] 'vivid', meksayati 'to mingle, stir', mimiksit-
'desiring for mixing [with milk] (of Soma)', misrd- 'mixed, blended'; Gr. peiyvuni 'to
mingle, mix', pioyco 'id.', aor.ps. iiiyfjvai, Lith. misras 'mixed, blended', maisyti, 3s.
maiso 'to mix', OCS mesiti 'to mingle, mix' < *moik-; OE miscicm, OHG miscen 'to
mix'.
The reason why this verb takes the 2nd cj. is unclear. It is also unclear whether
Praenestine misc is a remnant of a simple thematic stem *misce/o-. The adv.
promiscam in PI. seems to point to an adj. *prdmiscus, hence also a simple thematic
stem. Promiscuus is regularly derived from (pro)miscere. Mixtum may reflect
*mixitum (with syncope before long-vowel endings). The pf. miscuT has an unusual
«-pf. after a heavy stem, which Meiser 2003 attributes to euphonic reasons, miscuT
replacing an earlier s-pf. *misxT < *miksk-s~. But it seems more likely that the pf. to
this pr. is recent altogether.

Derivatives: mitescere 'to become soft, grow mild' (Pac,+); commitigare 'to soften'
(Ter.). i

PIE *m(e)h)i-ti- 'soft'. IE cognates: W. mwydion 'soft parts' < *meit- < *mehii-ti-,
Olr. min 'soft' < *miHni- < *mh i-ni-, W. mwyn 'tender, mild', Bret, moan 'thin,
1
fine' < PCI. *meino/a/i- < *mehii-n-; Skt. mdyas- 'refreshment, enjoyment', YAv.
maiiah- [n.] 'satisfaction, pleasure' < *meiH-os-, OPr. mijls, Lith. mielas, Latv. mT(s
'nice, sweet, dear', OCS milt 'pitiable', Ru. milyj 'sweet, dear' < *m(e)iH-lo-.
Possibly, Pit. *miti- and the other nominal forms in IE languages are derived from a
(verbal) derivative *mh]-i- to *mh 'to measure'.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: moenia, -ium 'defensive walls' [n.pl. /] (Naev.+; sg. moene in Naev.)
Derivatives: moemre, munTre 'to fortify, safeguard' (Lex XII+; moen- in PI.),
mimTtio 'defence work, fortification' (Sis.+); admoemre 'to besiege' (P1.+),
circummoe/unire 'to surround with a wall' (P1.+), commoeiunire 'to surround with
fortifications' (PI.+).
Pit. *moini- 'protective wall'.
PIE *(H)moi-ni- 'construction'? IE cognates: Olr. do-dimen* 'to fix, tie' < PCI.
*mi-nu~, Skt. pr. minotu [3s.ipv.act.], aor. amesta [3s.med.], pf. vz mimdya, ta-ptc. mitd-
'to fix, establish, build', YAv. bsrszi-mita- 'highly built'; Latv. miet 'to drive piles'.
The retention of oe in moenia (as opposed to the usual change to it) is ascribed to fear
of polysemy with regard to munia 'achievements'. A connection with mumts 'charge,
duty' < *moin-os- cannot be rejected with certainty: a wall has defensive duties.
Bibl.: WH II: 100f., EM 409f„ IEW 709, Meiser 1998: 87, Schumacher 2004: 484,
LIV 1. *mei-. —»munus, murus
>
mol£s, -is 'large mass, heap' [f. /] (Acc.+)
Derivatives: mdtiri 'to labour, build up, strive' (P1.+), molitus, -us 'strenuous effort'
(PL), molimen 'force, effort' (Lucr.), molimentum 'effort, labour' (Sis.+); amoliri 'to
remove, obliterate' (P1.+), admoliri 'to exert oneself, lay violent hands on' (P1.+),
commoliri 'to construct, set in motion' (Caecil.+), demoliri 'to throw off, demolish'
(Naev.+), emoliri 'to carry through'; molestus 'troublesome, tiresome' (PL+),
molestia 'distress, annoyance' (P1.+).
Pit. *molo- 'labour, effort', *mol-i-je- 'to labour', *mel-e/os- [n.] 'trouble'.
PIE *mel-e/os- [n.], *moI(-o)- .
There is a slight possibility that molestus reflects *mdlesto- with pretonic shortening
of *~VH- in front of a resonant. More likely is an original j-stem *melos, -es- 'trouble,
obstacle' > *molos, from which mo- was introducted into *melesto- (thus Schrijver
1991: 469f.). The absence of unrounding to *malesto- confirms this analysis. This

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mollis 'soft, gentle' [adj. i] (P1.+)
Derivatives: mollitia 'softness, luxury' (PL+), molliculus 'soft, tender' (PL+),
mollTre 'to make soft' (Ter.+), mollitudo 'softness' (Pac.+), mollescere 'to become
soft' (Lucr.+); molluscus 'having a thin shell' (P1.+).
Pit. *moldu-(i-).
PIE *mld-u- 'soft'. IE cognates: W. blydd 'soft', Skt. mfdu- 'delicate, soft', Gr.
*PM5uc; 'powerless' (in pXaSetq Hsch.) The appurtenance of the Gr. forms is uncertain.
Mollis < *molwi- < *mollwi- < *moldui- < *mjdu-i-. The u-stem adj. were remade
into /-stems in Latin. Lat. molluscus and molluscum 'kind of fungus that grows on
maple-trees* (Plin.) shows the older w-stem: *mldu-sko- > *moldusko- > molluscus.
Bibl.: WH II: 103f., EM 41 Of., IEW 716-719, Schrijver 1991: 20f., Meiser 1998: 64,
LIV *meld-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: molo, -ere 'to grind in a mill' [v. Ill; pf. molui, ppp. molitum] (P1-+)
Derivatives: mola 'millstone; cake of ground barley and salt' (Naev.+), moldrius 'of
a mill' (Cato+), moletrina 'milling-place' (Cato), motile 'component of a mill' (Cato),
molitum [n.] 'flour' (PL); immoldre 'to sacrifice, sprinkle with flour' (Naev.+).
Pit. sg. *mela-, pi. *mal-enti. It. cognates: U. kumaltu, kumultu, comoltu
[3s.ipv.II], kumates, kumate, comatir [ppp., abl.pl.n.] < *kom-m(o)lH-e/o~,
*kom-mlH-to- 'to crush'. Maybe U, maletu [acc.sg.m.], attribute to 'salt'.
PIE *melh -/*mlh - [pr.] 'to crush, grind', *molh -h - [f.] 'mill(stone)'. IE cognates:
2 2 2 2

malan < *moIH-. Skt. mpyati, mrnati 'to crush, grind' < *ml/r-n-H-.
Jmmolare is a denominative to mola. In the athematic paradigm of the PIE verb,
*melhti gave Pit. *melati, whence with rounding in front of velar / > VOLat.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: monlle 'necklace, collar' [n. /] (Afran.+)
Pit. *monjo- 'neck'.
PIE *mon-i- 'neck'. IE cognates: OI5. rnuin [f.] 'upper part of the back between
shoulders and neck', MW mwn, OBret. man 'neck' < PCI. *moni~, Olr. muinel, MW
mwnwgyi 'neck' < *moni-kIo~; Skt. manyd- [f.] 'neck', mani- [ra.] 'ornament carried
around the neck', YAv. zarsnu-maini- 'with a golden neck-ornament' (an epithet of a
vulture), pairi-maniia- 'neck-protection', manaodri- [f.] 'neck'; OCS monisto
'necklace'; OHG mana, OE manu 'mane' < *mon~; OIc. men, OE mene 'necklace' <
*mon-i~.
Since there existed a productive pattern of deriving -/i-adj. with a preceding long
vowel from short-vowel i- and w-stems (Leumann 1977: 350), monTle can be derived
directly from a preform *moni- 'neck'. In that case, the absence of unrounding to
*ma- is unexpected. If the PIE i-stem was thematized to Pit. *monie/o~, the first
syllable was closed, and *mo- would be retained.
Bibl.: WH II: 108, EM 412, IEW 747f., Schrijver 1991:473.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mons, -tis 'mountain' [m. i] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: montanus 'of the mountains, mountainous' (Lucil.+), montivagus
'mountain-ranging' (Lucr.+); promuntu/orium 'headland, promontory, spur' (Pac.+).
Pit. *monti
PIE *mon-ti- 'protrusion, height'. IE cognates: W. mynydd, OCo. menit, OBret.
monid 'mountain' < *mon-io-; Av. maiti- 'mountain, height' < *mn-ti-\ OIc. monir
'ridge of a roof < *moni~, mcena 'to tower'.
The meaning of promuntu/orium makes it attractive to derive it from mons, but the
morphology is unclear: *prd-mont-orium? The suffix -orium is usually derived from
nouns in -tor which belong to verbal stems (Leumann 1977: 301), hence one might
adopt an alternative etymology as *prd-moni-tor 'wamer' (to moneo), and regard a
promontory as a clear 'signpost' in the landscape. This would require syncope of
medial *-e- in *promonetoriom. In mons, the o-grade is unexpected; the Latin noun
looks like a cross of *mn-ti- and *mon-i(o)-.
Bibl.: WH II: 108f., EM 413, 538, IEW 726, LIV ?3.*men-. mentum, minae

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mora 'delay, lapse of time' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: morare/i [act. Naev.Enn.Pac.J 'to delay, hold back, remain' (Naev.+);
commorari 'to detain, remain' (P1.+), demordri 'to keep waiting, linger' (P1.+),
remorari 'to wait; hold up' (P1.+), remora 'hindrance, delay' (P1.+).
Pit. *morH-a- 'delay'.
PIE *morh -. IE cognates: Olr. maraid, •mair 'lasts, remains' < PCI. *mare/o- <
2
*mrH-e-\ Olr. mer 'senseless' < *merH-o- (appurtenance uncertain); Skt. amurchat
(AV) [3s.ipf.], murta- [ppp.] 'to congeal, become solid' < *mrH~sk-, Gr. p<opo<;
'stupid, foolish'.
Morari could be a denominal verb to mora, or it could represent an old iterative
*morH-eie- as proposed by Schumacher. Incidentally, this would point to root-final
*-h - If *mo- regularly became *ma- in open syllable, mora cannot regularly reflect
2
*mor- in open syllable. The vowel o might have been restored in the iterative verb.
One might with Schrijver 1991 posit *morH~, with the laryngeal closing the syllable
in Pit. — but see section 4.2, note 2, of the introduction.
Bibl.: WH II: 110, EM 413, IEW 969f., Schrijver 1991: 110, Schumacher 2004:476.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: morbus 'disease, illness' [m. o] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: morbidus 'sick' (Varro+), morbdsus 'sickly' (Cato+); remorbescere 'to
fall ill again' (Enn. apud Paul. exF.).
Pit *mor-/o-?
PIE *mor-bho-?
Morbus cannot continue an adj. in *-icfo, such as acerbus 'sour', since *moripo-
would normally yield *moridus. Thus, the suffix is more likely to be PIE *-bho-,
which surfaces especially in "adjectives specifying something's appearance"
(Nussbaum 1999a: 393, e.g. albus, sorbum). Morbus could be 'looking like death' to
the root *mr- of mors and moriri. Morphologically, however, a PIE formation
*m(o)r-bho- is somewhat strange. An alternative basis would be *mor~, *mor~, the
(non-IE?) root of 'bramble' (Gr. popov 'black mulberry', W. merwydden).
Bibl.: WH II: 110f., EM 414, IEW 735-737, Leumann 1977: 330.

Derivatives: mordicus 'with ithe teeth, by biting''(Naev.+), morsus, -us 'bite,
wounding' (Cato+), mordax 'prone to bite, biting, sharp' (P1.+), morsiuncula 'a little
bite' (P1.+); admordere 'to bite, extract money from' (P1.+), praemordere 'to bite
at/from the end' (P1.+), remordere 'to bite back, gnaw' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *mord~eje~.
PIE pr. *h mord-(e)ie- 'to crush, bite', pf. *h me-h mord-, ptc. *h mrd-to-. IE
2 2 2 2
cognates: Skt. sam-marditoh [inf.] 'to crush', mfdita- 'smashed, crushed', OAv.
morartdat (< *mf-n-d-) [3s.pr.inj.act.] 'to ruin, crush', OP vimardatiy [pr.], viyamarda
[ipfi] 'to crush'; Gr. &p£p5co 'to deprive of, apeipco (Pi.) 'to bereave', OE smeortan
'to hurt'. ;
An iterative present (biting is typically a repeated action) with PIE o-grade.
Apparently, the simple thematic present was ousted in Latin. Final *-d might be a root
enlargement if Gr. dpeipw is old (but it might be secondary after the aor. f|p£poa).
Bibl.: WH II: 11 If., EM 414, IEW 735-737, Leumann 1977: 604, LIV *h merd-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: morior, morl 'to die' [v. Ill; ppp. mortuum] (Lex XII+; also (e)morTri PI. Enn. Ter.)
Derivatives: commori 'to die together with' (P1.+), demon 'to die' (P1.+), emori 'to
perish, die out' (P1.+), intermori 'to perish, die off, praemort 'to die beforehand'
(Varro+), moribundus 'dying, decaying' (P1.+); mors, -tis [f. /] 'death' (Naev.+),
mortalis [adj.; m.] 'mortal, perishable; human being' (Naev.+), immortalis 'immortal,
eternal' (PI.+), immortalitas 'immortality, being a god' (P1.+), morticinus 'that has
died a natural death' (P1.+), mortifer 'deadly' (Enn.+); mortuus 'dead, done with'
(Naev.+), mortualia 'mourning garments, funeral dirges' (Naev.+), emortualis 'of
one's death' (PI).
Pit. *morje- 'to die', *morto- 'dead', *mortwo- 'dead', *morti- 'death'. It. cognates:
Ven. murtuvoi [dat.sg.] 'dead'.
PIE *mr-ie/o- 'to die', *mr-to- 'dead', *mr-uo- 'dead', *mr-ti- 'death'. IE cognates:
Olr. marb, W. marw 'dead' < *mfuo~; Hit. mer-V 'to disappear, vanish' <
*mer-/*mr-, marnu-1 'to make disappear'; Skt. pr. mriyate (< *mr-ie~), aor. mar-Imf-,
pf. mamara, caus. mardyati 'to kill', martave [inf.] 'to die', mrtd- 'died, dead',
a-mfta~ (< *g-mrto-) 'immortal'; YAv. miriia- 'to die', marsta- 'died', amssa-
'immortal'; Gr. (Hsch.) epopxev [3s.ipf.act.] 'died', &pPpoTO<; 'immortal'; Arm.
merani- 'to die'; Go. maurpr 'murder'; Lith. mirti 'to die', OCS mreti 'to die',
mbrtvb, Ru. mertvyj, SCr. mrtav 'dead'.
Morticmus might be based on an earlier adj. *mortiko~. The change of PIE *mftuo- to
*mftuuo- (conditioned by t?) was Pit. judging by Ven. murtuvo- The formation of
*mrtiio- 'dead' for PIE *mfto~ (as in Slavic) may be due to a contamination with
*mruo~ as attested in Celtic. Lat. (im)mortalis might be based on earlier *morto-
'dead' rather than on mors 'death'.
Bibl.: WH II: 112f., EM 414f., IEW 735, Lejeune 1974: 337, Sihier 1995: 536,
Meiser 1998: 93, Livingston 2004: 10, LIV *mer-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: short period, event' (Ter.+), momen [n.] 'movement' (Lucr.+), mobilis 'quick in
movement, movable' (P1.+), mobilitds 'quickness of movement' (Lucr.+); admovere

'?' < *kom-mow~e/i-to- 'moved'.

*mouh / *muh, mum(m)iia/e-2' <; *mumai- < *mh]U-oi-; Skt. pr. -mivanti-

[ptc.actf.] 'pushing down', ps. -mTvyamdna- [ptc.] (< PIE *mihu-), ° muta- (<
r
*muhto- < *miuhi-to-) in kama-muta- 'impelled by love', Av. a-muiiamna-
r
'motionless', YAv. auua.miuua- 'to clear away', Khot. mvlr- 'to move'; Lith. maudyti
'to bathe', Latv. maut 'submerge, swim', maudat 'bathe' < *mouH-, OCS myti 'to
wash' < *muH~; ToB miw-, ToA mew- 'to shake'.
Lat. motus < *mowe-to-, molus [m.] < *mowe-tu~, mobilis < *mowe-bli~, and
momentum < *mowe-mnto- have restored *-owe- (as far as -e- was syncopated by
rule) after the change of *-owV- > -u- seen in rursus, nundinae. In movere, mo- must
be more recent than the change of *-ow- > *-aw-. Hence, we posit *meuHwhich
could be the PIE root pr. or aorist. The suffix -e- could have been adopted from the
old caus.pr. *maweje- < PIE *mouH-eie (cf. Vine 2006a), or from other stative
presents in *-e-je~.

*mieuh[-. —• sud

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mox 'soon' [adv.] (P1.+)
Pit. *moks(u)A
PIE *moK(-)s(-) 'soon'. IE cognates: Olr. mo [adv.], mos- [prev.] 'soon', MW moch
'soon'; Skt. maksi 'quickly', OAv. mosuca 'and soon', YAv. mosu 'soon'.
It is unclear whether the PIE word is an old loc.pl. in *-su, or whether it contains a
PIE w-stem. The root, too, is uncertain. In theory, Lat. mox and the Ilr. forms could be
derived from *mog-s(~), o-grade of the root *mg- 'great' of magnus\ the semantic
development would have been '(in) much, many' > 'quick, soon'.
Bibl.: WH II: 117, EM 417, IEW 747.
mucrO 'point (of a sword, etc.)' [m. «] (Enn.+)
Pit. *m(e/o)uk-ro-7
PIE *(h )muk-ro-. IE cognates: Gr. apuooco 'to scratch, tear', ajxuxn 'rent, wound',
2
apuypa 'rending'; apuKaXai 'arrow-tips', apua^eo^ai 'to scratch the flesh with one's
nails' (Hsch.); Lith. musti, 3s. musa 'to beat', Latv. mustavas [nom.pl.] 'warping
beam'.
The vowel length of mu- is disputed: the syllable is long in OLat. poetry, which seems
to point to mu \ cro. The appurtenance of Lith. miisti is uncertain, since the meaning is
not 'to scratch'. Greek and Latin might go back to PIE (in which case Greek has some
secondaiy consonant variation from an original stem *amuk~), or be borrowings from
an unknown language. If mu- has a long vowel, the Latin form would reflect
*me/ouk-ro-.
Bibl.: WH II: 117, EM 417, IEW 745, Schrijver 1991: 21.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mucus 'mucus, snot' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: mucere 'to be mouldy' (Cato), mucidus 'snotty, mouldy' (PI.+);
emungere (emunxT, emunctum) 'to wipe one's nose; trick' (P1-+); mugil(isj [m.]
'sea-fish, grey mullet' (Varro+).
Pit *mung- 'to wipe'.
PIE *(s)mu-n-k- 'to brush off, *(s)me/ouk-o- 'snot'. IE cognates: Skt. pr. muncami,
mucyate, aor. amugdhvam [2p.med.ath.], dmucat [3s.act.them.J 'to release, set free',
YAv. fra-muxti- [f.] 'binding loose (shoes)', paiti.smuxta- [adj.] 'wearing shoes', Gr.
puccopai 'to blow one's nose', [ruterr|p, -f^poq [m.] 'nostril', jiu^a 'slime, mucus',
pu^tov [m.] 'kind of mullet'; Lith. maukti 'to pull, peel, skin', mukti, 3s. miinka 'to
walk away', smukti 'to glide (off)', Po. smukac 'to brush', Ru. (dial.) smykat' 'to
milk'; Cz. mknouti 'to move', SCr. maknuti 'move' < PS1. *rm>kngti.
It is not certain that mungo and mucus belong to the same root. Mungo can be
compared with the nasal presents in Skt. and BS1. and with the meaning of Gr.
puooopai. Lat. mucus would have to be an old derivative of the same root; but it can
also belong to IE words for 'moist', 'moor': Mir. mocht 'weak' < *muk-to-\ OIc.
mjukr, Latv. mukls 'marshy'.
Bibl.: WH I: 402f., EM 417,421, IEW 744f., LIV *meuk-.
mfigiO, -Ire 'to low, moo, bellow' [v. IV] (Varro+)
Derivatives: muginan 'to roar, hum and haw' (Lucil.+), mugitus, -us 'lowing,
roaring' (Varro+).
Pit. *mug~. It. cognates: maybe U. mugatu [3s.ipv.II], muieto fust [3s.fut.pf.ps.] '?':
mute- < *mug-je-l
PIE *mflg-. IE cognates: Hit mugae-zl 'to invoke, entreat', denom. to *muga-
'invocation of the gods through noise' < *moug-o-, Glsmtikar- / mukn- 'noisy
instrument, rattle' < *meug-r, *mugnos; Gr. puKaopai 'to low, bellow', pu^to 'to
mutter, moan', Lith. miikiu, mukti 'bellow', Ru. mycat', Ukr. mukaty, MHG miihen
'to low, bellow', OHG muckazzen 'to talk slowly'.
Onomatopoeic form, imitation of a cow's lowing.
Bibl.: WH II: 119f„ EM 417f., IEW 75If., Untermann 2000: 480f. ^ mutus

Derivatives: immulgere 'to milk (into)' (Andr.+); promulgare 'to make widely

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: known' (Cic.+); mulctra/ -um [f./n.] 'milking-pail' (Verg.+).
Pit. *molgeje~.
PIE *h mlg-eie- 'to milk'. IE cognates: Olr. mligid* 'to milk' < PCI. *mlige/o- <
2
*h mlg-; Gr. apiXyoci 'to milk', Alb. mjel, Lith. melzu < *h melg-, OCS mltztp <
2 2
*h mlg-, OHG melchan, OE melcan, ToA malkant (ptc.).
2
Originally a PIE iterative pr. 'to milk'; in view of the Olr. cognate, mulgeo most
likely continues a zero grade of the root. The verb prdmulgare can be interpreted as a
factitive to mulgere: 'to milk forth' > 'make known'. Lat. mulctra was not based on
the pr., but seems to continue an earlier *m(e/o)lg-tro- (cf. Serbat 1975: 313).
Bibl.: WH II: 121, 370, EM 418, 538, IEW 722f., Schrijver 1991: 21, Schumacher
2004: 486, LIV *h melg-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mulleus 'red-coloured (said of shoes)' [adj. o/a] (Cato+; mullos [acc.pl.] Vopiscus)
Pit. *molwo~.
PIE *ml-uo-? IE cognates: Lith. muhas 'reddish, yellowish'.
Driessen 2005: 45 holds that the original form may have been mullus (possibly
retained by Vopiscus) which was remade into mulleus on the model of calceus 'shoe',
with which it always occurs. Driessen rejects the connection with adj. in *-wo-
rn eaning 'black' (e.g. Gr. peXac;, Latv. mqlns 'black') since Pit. *meiano- would not
yield Lat. mullus; he proposes *ml-uo- instead.
Bibl.: WH II: 122f., EM 419, IEW 720f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: multus 'numerous, many' [adj. o/a] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: multesimus 'infinitesimal' (Lucr.), molta (CIL), multa 'penalty' (P1.+),
multare (CIL moltare) 'to fine' (Naev.+), multitare 'to fine habitually' (Cato),
multitudo 'abundance, (large) number' (Varro+).
Pit. *molto-. It. cognates: O. molto [nom.sg.], moltam [acc.sg.], moltas [gen.sg. or
acc.pl.], U. mutu, muta [nom.sg.], muta [acc.sg.], motar [gen.sg.] 'a fine'; O.
multasikad [abl.sg.fi], moltas(ikud) [abl.sg.n.] 'cashed as a fine', derivative in
*-iko- of a form *moltdsio- 'fine-like'; O. moltaum [inf.] 'to fine'.
PIE *m(o)l-to- 'good, big'? IE cognates: Gr. pala 'very, quite', paXXov 'more,
rather' (for *peM.ov?), palioxa 'mostly, quite especially'; Latv. milns 'very many'.
The appurtenance of multa here is not certain, but can be justified: a fine is a
'quantity' one has to pay. Multus may formally be explained from *ml-to~ or *mol-to-
as 'good' or 'strong' vel sim., but the function of *-to- remains unclear. As argued
s.v. melior, the IE root etymology is not very strong.
Bibl.: WH II: 123-125, EM 419f., IEW 720, Untermann 2000: 482-484. — melior

Derivatives: mula 'she-mule' (P1.+), mulio 'mule-driver' (P1.+), muscella 'young
she-mule' (CIL 4.2016).
Pit. *musklo- / *mukslo~.
IE cognates: Gr. puiAog 'lascivious; pack-mule', pu^oi; 'Phocaean name of a
breeder-ass' (Hsch.) < *pUKoXog, Alb. mushk, ORu. mtskb, RuCS mesk 'mule'.
Probably a loanword which entered Europe from Asia Minor in the form *musk- or
* muks-.
Bibl.: WH II: 125f, EM 420, Leumann 1977: 207.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mundus 'clean, elegant' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: munditia 'cleanliness, elegance' (P1.+), mundulus 'elegant' (PL);
immundus 'unclean, squalid' (P1.+), immunditia 'dirtiness' (P1.+), permundus 'very
clean' (Varro).
Pit. *mudno-7
PIE *mud-no- 'happy'? IE cognates: Skt. mud- [f.] 'joy, delight', moda- [m.] joy,
delight', modate 'to be happy', Lith. mudriis, Latv. mudrs 'cheerful'?
Could be derived from the PIE root *meud~ 'to rejoice', although the semantics are
not compelling.
Bibl.: WH II: 126f„ EM 420, IEW 741-743.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: munus, -eris 'function, task, duty' [n. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: munusculum 'small gift' (Pl +), munerare 'to bestow' (P1.+), muneralis
'relating to the giving of presents' (P1.+), munerigerulus 'bearer of presents' (PI.);
moe/itnia, -drum 'duties, functions' (P1.+); communis 'common, general' (Naev.+;
OLat. comoin-\ moe/unis 'obliged, indebted' (P1.+), communitus [adv.] 'jointly'
(Varro), communicare 'to share, communicate' (P1.+); municeps, -ipis 'native, citizin
of a municipium' (Lucil.+), municipium 'community, municipality' (CIL+), miinificus
'dutiful, generous' (PI.+), munificare 'to enrich' (Lucr.); immunis 'not paying a share,
exempt' (P1.+).
Pit. *moini~, *moi-nos- [n.] 'duty, obligation, task'. It. cognates: O. muimku,
muinik. [nom.sg.f.], muinik^m [acc.sg.f.], mumikad [abl.sg.f.], muuu[kum]
[nom.acc.sg.n.], muimkei [loc.sg.n.] 'common' < *mqin-iko-; U. muneklu [acc.sg.n.]
'contribution' < *moin-T-tlo~.
PIE *h2moi-no- [m. / adj.], *h moi-ni- 'exchange'? IE cognates: MW tramwy,
2
tremynu, MCo. tremena, MBret. tremen 'to cross, pass' < PCI. *tras-mi-nV~; Olr.
moin 'value, treasure', W. mwyn 'value', Skt. mem- 'revenge', YAv. maeini- [f.]
'punishment, castigation', OP yau-maini- 'power of revenge', Lith. mamas
'exchange', OCS mena 'exchange, substitution', Go. gamains 'common', OHG
gimeins 'id.' < PIE *moino/h .
r
All words are^based on two stems, *munos- and *muni- 'function, obligation'. A
municeps is one who 'takes an obligation', communis 'who partakes in the duties'.
The i-stem in the adj. could in theory have been created in Italic after adjectives such
as inermis 'unarmed'; on the other hand, Gm. shows the same compound in Go.
gamains. Munis can be a back-formation to communis. Probably Lat. munus is based

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: murmur, -is 'low, continuous noise' [n. r] (Pac.+)
Derivatives: murmurare 'to rumble, mutter, grumble' (P1.+), commurmurare 'to
mutter, murmur' (Varro+), murmurillare 'to mutter faintly' (PI.), murmurillum 'a
faint mutter' (PL).
Pit. *mo/ur-mo/ur-a-.
PIE *mr-mr-. IE cognates: Skt. murtmra- [m.] 'crackling fire', -a [f.] name of a
river, marmara- 'roaring', Gr. poppy poo 'roar and boil', Arm. mrmr-am, -im (<
* murmur-), Lith. murmeti, murm(J)enti 'to grumble, murmur', Cz. mrmrati, SCr.
mrmrati, Bulg. marmorja 'id.' < PS1. *mtrm(-b)rati, Ru. mormulit' (dial.) 'grumble,
chatter', SCr. mrmljiti 'to mumble, grumble' < PS1. *im>rm-l~; OHG murmulon 'id.'.
Since murmur is attested later than murmur are, and since we find reduplicated verbs
everywhere in IE, murmur may well be a deverbal derivative. Latin murmur- is
regarded as a reflex of earlier *mormor- < PIE *mrmr- or *mormor- (the change of
*mor- > mur- being irregular), but it may also have onomatopoeic *-«-.
Bibl.: WH II: 130f., EM 423, IEW 748f., Meiser 1998: 63f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: murus 'defensive wall' [m. o] (P1.+; moerus /moirus CIL, Enn., Acc.)
Derivatives: muralis 'of a wall; turreted' (Lucr.+), pomerium /pomoerium 'strip of
land round the walls, town boundary' (Varro+).
Pit. *moi-ro~.
PIE *(H)moi-ro- 'building'.
Pomerium < *posmoirio- < *posi-moirio- 'what is behind the wall'. It did not
undergo the expected change of *oi to r in non-initial syllable; hence, it may be an
archaism.
Bibl.: WH II: 13If., 334, EM 423f., IEW 709, Meiser 1998: 71, 87, LIV l.*mei-.
moenia
mus, mQris 'mouse' [m. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: miiscipulum 'mousetrap' (Lucil.+), musculus 'mussel, small fish, small
rodent; muscle' (P1.+), murinus 'of a mouse' (Varro+), miiscerdae [f.pl.] 'mouse
droppings' (Plin.); possibly murex, -icis 'shellfish yielding purple dye; its shell'
(Lucil.+); mustela / mustella 'weasel; certain fish (prop, burbot)' (PL+), mustelinus
'of a weasel' (Ter.+), mustricula 'a shoemaker's last' (Afran. apud Paul, ex F.).
Pit. *mus 'mouse', *mus-tra- 'mouse-like animal'.
PIE *muHs 'mouse'. IE cognates: Skt. mis- 'mouse, rat', YAv. mus- [f.] 'name of a
Pairika', Gr. puq 'mouse', OHG mus; OCS mysb, Sin. mis 'mouse' < PS1. *mysb; ToB
mascitse* 'mouse, rat' < PTo. *mast3tse < *m(w)asti- <*muHs-ti-.
Since musculus also means 'mussel', it is conceivable that murex belongs here. It may
also be a loanword, cognate with Gr. 'mussel'. Lat. musculus 'mussel' can be
derived from 'mouse' (cf. musculus 'muscle', named after the form of a muscle), but
may also be cognate with Gr. pua^ and Lat. murex. The noun mustela is discussed
elaborately by Schaffner 2006a, who holds that the original form of the noun was
mustella from *mus~tre-la- 'small mouse-like animal'. He analyzes it as a diminutive
of an earlier noun *mus-tra- 'mouse-like animal' which can be compared with OHG
fledare-mustra 'bat' < PGm. *-mustromustro 'bat' < *mustran-. The preform
*miistro- is also found in Lat. mustricula < *mustro-ke-la- according to Schaffner,
which would ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: musca 'fly' [f. a] (P1.+)
Pit. *musko/a-.
PIE *mu-s-. IE cognates: Gr. pma < *puqja 'fly'; Arm. mown, Gen. mnoy
'mosquito', Alb. myze, mize; OPr. muso, Lith. muse, muso, Latv. mttsa, mtisa 'fly';
OCS muxa, Ru. muxa, Cz. moucha 'fly' <*mou$a, OCS mbsica 'mosquito, locust',
Ru. (dial.) msica 'midge, gnats, small insects'; OIc. my [n.] < *muja- 'mosquito', OE
mycg, OS muggia, OHG mucka.
Quite possibly an onomatopoeic form *mu for the humming sound which flies and
mosquitoes make. The s-extension is found in Latin, BS1., Greek and Gm.
Bibl.: WH II: 133, EM 424, IEW 752.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mutilus 'mutilated, truncated' [adj. o/a] (Varro+)
Derivatives: mutilare 'to mutilate, cut off (Ter.+), admutildre 'to cut loose, fleece'
(PL)-
IE cognates: Ir. mut, Gaelic mutach 'short' < *mutt
No etymology.
Bibl.: WH II: 136, EM 425, IEW 753.
matO / muttO 'penis'[m. n] (Lucil.+)
Derivatives: muto/unium 'penis' (Lucil.+), mii/oetinus 'phallic' (Lucil.).
Pit. *muto-.
PIE *mHu-to- 'strong one'. IE cognates: Olr. moth 'male organ'; Hit. mitua- [c.] an
awe-inspiring quality (loanword from Luw.), CLuw. miiua- 'to oveipower (vel sim.)'
(3sg.pr.act. mu-u-ua-i) (< *mouH-?) HLuw. muwa- 'to dominate(?), to attack(?)',
;
*^2muwita- [c.] 'seed'(?), nimuwinza- [c.] 'child', Lyc. muwete- 'descendance?'; Hit.
muri- 'bunch'; Gr. pupiog [adj.] 'countless, immense' < *muH-ri-.
Since the penis is often referred to with euphemisms, one may derive muto from the
adj. mutus. On the other hand, one may also look for an external etymology. The
closest comparandum of muto seems to be Olr. moth. Applying the rules for pretonic
absence of lengthening in Italo-Celtic sequences of the type *CHu- (Kortlandt 1980a,
Schrijver 1991: 248, 534), we might explain moth < *mHu-to- while muto would be
based on a form *mHu-to-. Weiss 1996a: 207-209 connects muto with Hit. muri-
'bunch' and muwa- 'awe-inspiring quality', positing a PIE root *muh -
1/3
'reproductive power'. In view of the meaning of CLuw. miiua- 'to overpower (vel
sim.)' and Gr. pupio<; 'countless' (connected by Weiss), the original meaning of the
root may have been 'to be powerful / abundant'; the sexual connotation would then be
specifically Italo-Celtic, or even older if HLuw. muwita- means 'seed' and belongs
here.
Bibl.: WH II: 138, EM 426.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mutus 'inarticulate, dumb' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: obmutescere 'to become dumb, silent' (Ter.+); muttire 'to mutter'
(P1.+), muttitio 'muttering' (PI.).
Pit. *muto-.
PIE *mu-to-. IE cognates: Skt. miika- 'dumb', muni- [m.] 'ascetic, hermit', Gr.
PUKOQ, JIIJTIC;, JAUTTCN;, p\>8oc;, puvapog, Hsch. jxuv86<; 'dumb, speechless', Arm. mownj
'dumb'.
Mutus must be understood as 'he who cannot say anything but mu . If we take muttire
as a direct derivative of *muto- (with expressive gemination), both meanings 'to say
mu' and 'to be dumb' are represented. The root *mu is found with various extensions
in the IE languages. Within Latin, we also find it in mugid.
Bibl.: WH II: 139f., EM 426fi, IEW 75If. ^ mugio

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: mutuus 'on loan, reciprocal' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: mutuare/i 'to borrow' (Cato+), miituitdri 'to try to borrow' (PI.),
rnutuiter 'reciprocally' (Varro); rrmtare 'to exchange, replace' (P1.+), mutdtio
'exchange, change' (P1.+), mutabilitas 'liability to change' (Lucr.+), commutare 'to
change, alter' (P1.+), commutabilis 'variable' (Varro+), commutdtio 'change, reversal'
(Acc.+), commutdtus, -us 'change' (Lucr.), commoetdculum 'kind of rod used during
a ceremony' (Paul, ex F.), demutare 'to transform, deviate' (P1.+), immutdre 'to alter,
modify' (P1.+), immutdbilis 'liable to be changed' (PI.),permutare 'to exchange, sell'
(P1.+), transmit tare 'to change into' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *moito-.

Both mutuus and mutare presuppose a nominal stem *muto~ '(object of) change' [m.].
Several etymologies are possible for Pit. *moitoIt may be *moith -o- from the root

*mith - 'to exchange', or *h moi-to- from a different root for 'to exchange'. LIV
2 2
favours the former option, but the meaning of miito (the certain Latin reflex of
*mith -) is much further removed from mutare than munus < *h mi- is.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: naris 'nose; pi. nostrils, nose' [f. /; pi. nares, -ium] (P1.+)
Derivatives: nasus/m jm./n.] 'nose' (Naev.+), nasutus 'having a long nose; witty'
(Lucil.+), Naso 'Roman cognomen' (Ov.+).
Pit. *nas~ [f.], *nasi-, *naso- 'nose'.
PIE *Hnehs-, *Hnh -es-, *Hnh -s- 'nose'. IE cognates: Skt. nas- [f.] 'nose' (nasa
r 2 2
[nom.du.], nasos [gen.du.], nasi ° [loc.sg.]) , ndsa- [f.] 'nose', nasika- [f.] 'nose',
uriinasd- 'with a broad nose', pavinasa- 'with a nose like a rim', YAv. ndtjha
[nom.acc.du.?], naqhanat [abl.sg.] 'nose' (secondary «-stem) , OP naham [acc.sg.]
'nose'; Lith. nosis, Latv. nass < *(H)neh2S-, RuCS nosi>, Ru. nos' < *nh es-; OHG
r
nasa, OE nasu 'nose', OE ndse [f.] 'promontory', OFr. nosi 'moustache'; MoDu.
neiis < *nus-i~.
Latin has two derivatives of the (pre-)PIt. stem *nas- an /-stem and an o-stem. It
t
appears that the PIE word was often used in the dual ('the nostrils'), and the dual of a
f. root noun in PIE would have ended in nom.acc. *-h[e > Pit. *-e. When the dual
(lost in Latin) was reinterpreted as a plural, *ndse may have been remade into *nases
(with the regular pi. ending of C-stems) or *nasoi (as an o-stem). Other scenarios may
be envisaged. The non-rhotacized -s- is difficult to explain, other than possibly being
a remnant from pre-rhotacism times, from a different social layer. The PIE root
cannot be identified with that of anhelus and animus (as proposed by Fritz 1996),
which I reconstruct as *h nh 'to breathe, blow'.
2 r
Bibl.: WH II: 143-146, EM 429, IEW 755, Kortlandt 1985a: 119, Schrijver 1991:
143.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nassa 'fish-trap made of wickerwork' [f. a\ (P1.+)
Pit. *nasso~.
PIE *nHd-to- 'tied, knotted'. IE cognates: Olr. nascaid*, MBret. naska 'to bind' <
PCI. *nad-sk~, Olr. nassae 'bound' < *nad-to~, Go. nati, OHG nezzi, OE, OIc. net
'net' < *nad-i~.
If the root etymology given here is correct, Skt. nahya- 'to tie together' must be
explained differently. However, it is disputed which forms belong together. If Lat.
nodus is cognate, it must reflect *noHd-o-, but Schrijver explains it from *nod-o- and
t
connects OIc. nist 'brooch, pin', OHG nestilo 'string', which are easier to derive from
a root *nd-. Even PGm. *nat-i- 'net' would be easier from PIE *nod-i- than from
*nHd-i-, morphologically. If PGm. *nati- belongs to nassa, we have a
Germano-Italo-Celtic root *nad- 'to bind, plait', which may go back to PIE *nHd-.
Bibl.: WH II: 144f., EM 430, IEW 758f„ Schrijver 1991: 125, 481, Schumacher
2004: 489f., LIV *Hnedh-. nodus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: natinor, -ari 'to be busy' [v. I] (Cato apud Fest.)
Derivatives: natinatio dicebatur negotiatio et natinatores ex eo seditiosa negotia
gerentes (ibidem).
Pit. *gnati-.
PIE *gnhti- 'birth, production'.
r
A possible origin from *nauatinor was rejected by WH, hesitantly retained by
Leumann, and adopted by Flobert 1975? 94. Vine 1999c, inspired by these accounts,
gives an alternative which seems preferable. He starts from a //-stem PIE *gnh -ti-
3
'knowledge' or *gnhti- 'production' (the latter one suggested by Michael Weiss) >
r
Pit. *gnati~, from which an adj. *gnatfno- 'energetic, busy' was derived (maybe still
attested in the gloss natina 'discordia'). From this adj. the verb natTnarT would then
derive. For the basic root, *gnh seems semantically more straightforward.
r

Derivatives: mostly pi. nates, -ium.
Pit. *(g/s)nati-.
PIE *nHt-? IE cognates: Gr. vfirrov [n.] 'back; wide surface'.
The connection between natis and Gr. vcoxov, two isolated words, is certainly possible
from the semantic side, but remains formally uncertain. Schrijver reconstructs a root
noun *n(e)h t- from which Latin would have derived an /-stem *nh t-i- and Greek an
3 3
o-stem *ne/oh t-o-. Another possibility is a root *nh t- (Greek *noh t-o-) or *nh -
3 2 2 2
(Latin *nh -ti- and Greek *noh -to-). But the etymology remains very uncertain.
2 2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ne- 'not, un-' [pref] (Foruminscr.+)
Derivatives: non 'not' (Naev.+; PI. noenum, Lucil. noenu); nec, negue 'not, and not,
not either' (Lex XII, Andr.+), negare 'to deny, refuse' (Naev.+), negitare 'to deny
repeatedly' (P1.+).
Pit. *ne 'not', *ne-kwe 'and not'. It. cognates: O. nep, U. nep 'not' maybe < *ne-kwe.
The Sab. continuants of *ne and *ne are not easy to distinguish (see s.v. ne).
PIE *ne 'not'. IE cognates: Olr. na, nach, MWe. nac 'not' < *ne-kwe, Skt. na 'not',
naca 'and not', Lith. ne, Latv. ne, OCS ne 'not', Go. ni 'not' < *ne, nih '(strongly)
not' < *ne-Kve.
Neque < *ne-kwe 'and not', non <*pe-oinom 'not one'. In the latter form, apocope of -urn
is irregular (maybe due to unstressed use of the word), and -o- can only be explained
from a contraction *ne-oino- > *noino- > non. The form nec has two functions: 1)
apocopated form of neque 'and not', 2) simple 'not' (esp. VOLat.). They may be
etymologically identical, but the second nec could also reflect *ne-ke (cf. the suffix *-ke
in hie); in that case, negare and neglegere can be directly explained from this nec. If they
were formed from negue, they would postdate the apocope of neque > nec.
Bibl.: WH II: 152, 174f, EM 432f„ 439, IEW 756-758, Leumann 1977: 67,
Untermann 2000:494. —»in-, ne, ni
\
-ne 'then? or, whether' [ptcle.] ( Andr.+)
Derivatives: Is sometimes apocopated to -«: ain, satin, viden, audin.
Pit. *-ne.
PIE *-ne. IE cognates: Skt. na 'as, like', A\.ya$-na 'namely', Gr. Thess. ove, xove,
tave, OLith. ne 'as', Lith. ne, negi, negu 'than' (after comp.), nei 'as', Latv. ne 'than';
OCS, SCr. neze 'than' < *ne-g(w)V
May ultimately be the same word as PIE *ne 'not'. The scepticism towards this view
uttered in WH and EM is excessive.
Bibl.: WH II: 150, EM 434, IEW 319-231, Beekes 1995: 222.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: necto, -ere 'to weave, twine together' [v. Ill; pf. nex(u)i, ppp. nexum] (Lex XII, P1.+)
Derivatives: nexilis 'plaited, intertwined' (Varro+), nexus, -us 'binding, bond'
(Lucr.+), nexere (also -are) 'to bind, plait' (Andr.+); adnectere 'to tie on, attach'
(Varro+), conectere 'to join, relate' (P1.+), conexus, -us 'connection' (Lucr.+),
innectere 'to fasten' (Varro+), obnectere 'to entwine' (Acc.+).
WH follow the etymology first proposed by Osthoff in 1890, viz. that neciere is a
remake of earlier *nedere under influence oipectere. The root would be that of nodus
'knot', and may be connected with Skt. nahyanti 'to bind together', sam-naddha-
'bound together' (RV+), upandh- [f.] 'shoe, sandal', YAv. naska- 'collection of
texts', naSa- 'headgear' < Ilr. *Hnadh-. This remains a remote possibility since there
is no evidence for in Latin. An alternatively etymology would be to connect
necto with the root *h n]£- 'to reach' (see s.v. nanciscor), for which LIV reconstructs
2
a root aorist *h nek-.
2
Bibl.: WH II: 155f„ EM 435, IEW 758f. Jlecto,plecto

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nefrones, nefrundines 'kidneys, testicles' [m.pl. n(d)] (Paul. exF.)
Derivatives: nefrundines, nebrundines (Lanuvlnl), nefrones (in Praeneste); Paul, ex
F. nefrendes is probably due to confusion with nefrens 'toothless'.
Pit. * neuron-.
PIE *negwh-ro- 'kidney'. IE cognates: Gr. vetppoi 'kidneys', OHG nioro, OIc. nyra,
OS we. niure 'kidney' < PGm. * neuron-.
The Latin evidence points to o-vocalism: *nefron(d)~. This can be connected with a
PIE stem *negwh-ro- 'kidney', extended by an n-stem (as in Gm.), to which Latin then
added a suffix *-d(h)-. Judging by febris 'fever' < *dhegwh-ri-, the cluster *-gwV > Pit-
*-x'V- developed into PLat. *-fr~. The Latin result -fr- (instead of *-br-) is attributed
by Leumann to reanalysis: ne- was regarded as the negation ne- 'not', and *fr- was
treated as if in anlaut, where the development to fr- is regular.
Bibl.: WH II: 156, EM 436, IEW 319, Leumann 1977: 166.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nem us, -oris 'wood, forest' [n. r] (Enn.+)
Pit. *nem-os-.
PIE *nem-o/es- [n.] 'what is distributed, sacrifice'. IE cognates: Olr. nemed
'sanctuary, holy place', MBret. neved 'sacellum', W. Bret, nant 'vallis', Skt. namas-
[n.] 'worship, honour', Av. namah- 'homage; interest on a loan', Gr. vepo<; 'forest',
Gal. VEJJTJTOV 'holy place', Alb. neme 'curse, imprecation', ORu. (Novg.) namt
'interest*.
The meaning 'forest, (holy) clearance' is shared by Greek, Celtic and Italic. It
originates from ' sacrifice % > 'the place of the sacrifice'. In Ilr., the s-stem means
'worship'. LIV assumes tw6 different roots, *nem-1 'to distribute' and *nem-2 'to
bend', but the meanings are distributed complemeritarily across the IE languages: Ilr.
and Toch. have 'to bend', the European languages 'to distribute' or 'to take'. Since
the j-stem is attested in all languages and presupposes the verbal meaning 'distribute',
there can be little doubt that PIE had only one root *nem-.
Bibl.: WH II: 158f., EM 437, IEW 764, Benveniste 1931: 79ff, EWAia II: 16,
Sylvain Patri 2001, Stiiber 2002: 132f., LIV l.*nen>. -> numerus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: granddaughter', YAv. napti [f.] 'granddaughter' < Ilr. *(H)napt-iH-, Alb. mbese
'niece', OLith. nepte 'granddaughter', RuCS nestera 'niece', OHG nift(a) 'niece';
(3) adj.: Skt. naptriya- 'belonging to the grandson', OAv. naptiia- 'belonging to the
offspring?', YAv. nauua.naptiia- [n.] 'the kinsmen of the nine degrees of kinship of a
family'; Gr. dve\j/i6<; 'cousin, son of sister', CS ORu. netii 'nephew', SCr. necak
'sister's son', netjak 'sister's son'.
The meaning 'cousin' does not appear in Latin before 150 AD, and is therefore
secondary (Beekes 1976). The f. neptis was not remade into an -rfc-stem or an ia-stem
like other f. nouns. The nom.sg. is reconstructed as asigmatic *nep-ot by Beekes
1995: 178. The only indication for the initial laryngeal is Gr. aveyioi;.
Bibl.: WH II: 161, EM 437f., IEW 764, Schrijver 1991: 21, 150-153, 363-366,
Meiser 1998: 142.
Neptunus 'Neptune; the sea' [m. o] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: Neptunalia, -drum [n.pl.] 'the festival of Neptune' (Varro+).
Pit. *neptuno-?
PIE *nebh-tu- 'moisture'? IE cognates: YAv. napta- [adj.] 'moist', aifii.naptTm
[absol.] 'moistening'.
Rix 1981: 123 (= 2001: 291) supports the derivation from an abstract *nebh-tu-
'moisturing' with a suffix *-hjn- for indicating 'rulers': hence 'Lord of moisturing' >
of irrigation, of wells, of rivers. Yet the existence of this suffix is not established.
Alternatively, one might posit a de-instrumental derivative *nebhtuhi-n- 'who is with
moisture' in the vein of Nussbaum 1996. The Etruscan form Netiuns would be
borrowed from Umbrian because of the dental fricative, which might be rendering a
hypothetical U. cognate *Nehtuns.
Bibl.: WH II: 162f., EM 438, IEW 315fi, LIV l.*nebV -> nimbus
Ner5 'Nero' [m. n] (Liv.+)
Derivatives: Nerid/Neria/Nerienis 'Roman goddess, wife of Mars' (PI.+), neriosus
'strong, resistant' (gloss.); Suet, nero 'lingua Sabina fortis ac strenuus', Lydus (de
mensibus): vepiKT) - avSpia, vepcovaq - av5petoui; ... oi Zaj3ivoi; Suet,...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nervus 'sinew, muscle, nerve' [m. o] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: nerviae [f.pl.] 'strings of a musical instrument' (Varro+), nervia, -drum
[n.pl.] 'sinews or tendons' (Varro+), Nervolaria title of a lost play ascribed to Plautus
(Varro+).
Pit. *(s)neuro-.
PIE *snehur [nom.acc.], *snhuen- [obi.] [n.] 'sinew'. IE cognates: Skt. snavan
r r
[n.] 'sinew', YAv. snauuars.bazura- [adj.] 'having arms like sinews', Gr. veupov [n.],
veupa [f.] 'string, sinew'; Arm. neard 'sinew'; ToB snaura [pi.] 'sinews'. Hit.
ishunauuar is a ghost word, see Kloekhorst 2008: 396.
Nervus probably stems from *neuros by metathesis. A similar development can be
seen in parvus < *pauro-. Pit. *(s)neuro- is a thematization of PIE *(s)nehjur.
Bibl.: WH II: 165, EM 439, JEW 977, Schrijver 1991: 269, LIV *sneh. natrix,
r
neo ; j
O. nessimas [nom.pl.f.], nessimass [acc.pl.f.], nesimum [gen.pl.n.], nesimois
[abl.pl.n.], U. nesimei [loc.sg.n.] 'next'.
Pit. *ness- 'next'
PIE *nesd-is- 'nearer'? IE cognates: Olr. nessa [comp.], OW nes, MCo. nes, MBret.
nes 'nearer', Bret, nes 'near' < PCI. *nessa- (« *nedda- Jasanoff 1988-90: 185);
t
Gaul. neDDamon [gen.pl.], Olr. nessam 'nearest, next', W. nessaf, OCo. nesheuin,
Co. nessa, Bret, nessaff 'nearer' < PCI. *nessamo- (< *nezd-isamo-7 Jasanoff
1988-90: 172); Skt. nediyas- 'closer, very close', nedistha- 'closest, next', Av. asna-
[adj.] 'close' (< *nzd-no-), YAv. nazdiio [adv.] 'closer', nazdista- 'closest, next', OP
asna- [adj.] 'close'.
Since the Ilr. forms in *n(e)zd- are the closest in form and meaning, it seems
preferable to derive nessimo- from the same root. Cowgill 1970: 131, 138
reconstructs Pit. *ness-isamo- with haplology > *nessamo-. Yet this does not explain
i
suffixal -i- in Oscan, which should continue a long vowel. Van der Staaij therefore
reconstructs *-io-mo- or *-iH-mo~, but refrains from a further explanation.
Bibl.: IEW 758f., Meiser 1986: 242, van der Staaij 1995: 106, Untermann 2000:
493.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nex, necis 'violent death, murder' [f. k] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: necdre 'to kill' (P1.+), denicalis 'reserved for the purification of the
family of a deceased person' (Cic.+), eni/ecare (ppp. enectus) 'to kill' (P1.+),
internecare 'to exterminate' (P1.+), intemecid 'extermination' (Lucil.+), pernicies
'destruction, ruin' (P1.+).
Pit. *nek~. It. cognates: maybe U. minctu [3s.ipv.II] '?' (prayer for hurting the
opponent).
PIE *nek-s [f.] 'death'. IE cognates: Skt. ndsyati [3s.act.] 'to perish, disappear', aor.
nesat [3s.inj.act.], pf. nanasa [3s.act.], ppp. nasta-, OAv. anqsat [3s.aor.act.], nqsat
[3s.aor.inj.act.] 'to disappear', Av. nasiia- 'to stray from', nasta- 'having missed', Av.
nas- 'distress, misfortune', nasu- 'corpse', Gr. veicpoq [m.] 'body, dead person', VEKUC;
'corpse'; Toch. nak- 'to ruin'.
The meaning of nex presupposes a semantic development from 'disappear' to 'die' at
an earlier stage. I regard nequalia 'detrimenta' (Fest.) as a likely derivative of qualis.
This removes the Latin evidence for a PIE stem *nek-u-. The ppp. e-nectus could
imply that necare arose in compound verbs, esp. e-necare, and substitutes an earlier
verb *necere. But necare could also be denominal in origin. For pernicies, see
permities.
Bibl: WH I: 339, II: 153-155, EM 439f., IEW 762, Sihler 1995: 532, Untermann
2000: 497f., LIV *nelc-, noceo, permities

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: m 'not, that not, that, unless' [ptcle.; cj.] (VOLat.+: Duenos inscr., Garigliano Bowl,
SCBac. nei)
Derivatives: nfve, neive 'if not' (P1.+), nisi 'unless, but that, except' (Naev.+).
Pit. *nei. It. cognates: O. nei 'except' < *ne + T; O. neip, neip, nei, U. neip, neip
'not' maybe < *ne-T-kwid 'nothing'.
PIE *nei. IE cognates: OAv. nae- 'not' in Av. nae-cis 'none', OP nary, nai- 'not',
Lith. nei1 nor, than', OCS ni, Ru. ni 'and not, nor', Go. nei 'not', OHG ni.
A combination of ne 'not' and a deictic particle *-i.
Bibl.: WH II: 166, EM 433, IEW 756-758, Beekes 1995: 222, Untermann 2000:489.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nldor 'strong smell, fumes'[m. r] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: mdoricupius 'who loves the smell of cooking' (PI.+).
Pit. *knei/id-os~.
PIE *knei(H)d-os- 'itch'? IE cognates: Gr. Att. icvioa, Horn KVICTT] 'smell, fiime', Gr.
KVIST] 'nettle' < *knfd, KVIT,CO 'scratch, irritate (skin)' < *knid-ie~, Latv. kniest 'to
itch', Lith. kniedenti 'to clinch', OIc. hniss (< *knid-toycooking odour', OE hmtan
'to sting', OIc. hntta 'strong odour', OIc. knit a 'wound'.
Under the assumption that Gr. KVTO- continues *knTd-s~, and that a 'strong smell' is
derived from a verb 'to itch, sting', nidor could belong here. The vowels are difficult
to reconcile: Lat. and Gm. can have *iH, *eiH or *ei, Baltic can have *ei(H'), but
Greek has *iH in *knid- and *knid~ in KVI£G).
Bibl.: WH II: 166, EM 441, IEW 562f., Meiser 1998: 112, LIV *kneid-. -> lens

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nidus 'nest' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: mdamenta, -drum [n.pl.] 'nesting materials' (PI.), mdulari 'to build a
nest' (Varro apud Non.+).
Pit. *nizdo~.
PIE *nisdo- 'nest'. IE cognates: MIr. net, W. nyth 'nest, dwelling', Skt. nidd- [m.]
'nest, lair', Arm. nist 'residence, settlement', Lith. lizdas, Latv. ligzds 'nest', OCS
gnezdo, Ru. gnezdo, SCr. gnijezdo 'nest', OHG nest 'nest'.
A PIE compound consisting of *ni 'down' and the root *sd- 'to sit'.
Bibl.: WH II: 167, EM 441, IEW 884-887, Meiser 1998:29, LIV *sed-. sedeo, sidd

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nimbus 'rain-cloud, shower' [m. o] (Pac.+)
Derivatives: nimbatus 'stormy' or 'clouded, shrouded in mists' (of a girl) (PL).
Pit. *nefos-?
PIE *nebh-os [n.], gen. -es-os 'cloud'. IE cognates: Hit. nepis [n. > c.], CLuw.
tappas- [n.], HLuw. tipas-*[n.] 'sky, heaven', Skt.^nabhah [n.] 'cloud, sky', Av.
nabah- 'sky', Gr. vstpoq [n.] 'cloud', OCS nebo, Lith. debesis 'sky'; MP namb, MoP
namb 'dew, haze', nem 'moist'? i
Nimbus might be connected to nebula 'cloud' and imber 'shower of rain' < *n(e)bh-.
One might explain the -m- in nimbus by assuming that nimbus continues *nebh-os-
but was reshaped under the influence of imber, which has a similar meaning to
nimbus. This is possible, but gratuitous.
Bibl.: WH II: 168f, EM 441, IEW 315f., Meiser 1998: 81.imber, nebula

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nimis 'too much' [adv.] (Andr.+)
Derivatives:]nimium/s [adj.] 'excessive, too much' (Naev.+).
Pit. *ne *meijis (or *ne *mis) 'not too little, quite a lot'.
PIE *m(e)ihis- 'less'. IE cognates: see s.v. minor,
r
Nimius must be a recent adjectivization of nimis. In the account of Meiser 1998, nimis
< * nimis (iambic shortening) continues *ne meis 'not too little', with adverbial
*meiH-is of the adj. *meih 'little, few' (cf, magis, satis). Since e.g. magis 'more'
r
shows the zero grade of the root, one may also reconstruct *miH-is.
Bibl.: WH II: 169, EM 441, IEW 711, Meiser 1998: 154, LIV l.*meiH-. minor, ne

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ningit, -ere 'it snows' [v. Ill] (Acc.+; nivit 'it snows' Pac.)
Derivatives: nix, nivis [f.] 'snow' (PL+), ninguis [f.] 'snow' (Lucr.+), niveus 'of
snow, snow-white' (Naev.+).
Pit. *sneiwe/o- 'to snow' [pr.], *sningw- 'to snow', *sn(e)iw- 'snow' [m.].
PIE pr. *sneigwh-e/o- 'to snow', *sn(e)igwh- [m.] 'snow'. IE cognates: Olr. snigid,
•snig* 'to snow* < *snigwe/o- or *snigie/o-, Olr. snechtae, MW nyf[ra.] 'snow', Skt.
aor. asnihat [3s.act.], caus. snehdyat (RV) [3s.act.] 'to lie down; lay down', YAv.
snaezana- [adj.] 'slavering', snaeza- 'to snow' (Ilr. *snaijh-ia~), Sogd. snys-, Gr.
vei(pei 'it is snowing', vupct [acc.sg.], Lith. sninga 'it snows', jsnaigas 'snow', Latv.
sniegs, OCS snegb, Go. snaiws, OHG sneo 'snow'.
Lat. nix < nom. *sniks, gen. nivis < gen. *snixwos. The nasal present is probably
secondary.
Bibl.: WH II: 169fi, EM 442, IEW 974, Meiser 1998: 104, 125, Sihier 1995: 163;
Schumacher 2004: 597f„ LIV *sneigyh-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nltela 'kind of rodent' [f. a] (Plin.+)
Derivatives: nitedula 'kind of dormouse' (Cic.+).
A connection with mtor 'to lean, exert' is unlikely for semantic reasons. Nftor does
not mean 'to climb', as WH argue in order to support this etymology. It is formally
more attractive to look for a base *mte-, but I see no good candidate. Maybe nltela
can be connected with nidor 'smell' and its IE cognates meaning 'to scratch'. This
makes non-IE origin possible.
Bibl.: WH II: 170, EM 442.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: niteo 'to be radiant, shine' [v. II; pf. nituT\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: nitor [m.] 'brightness, elegance' (P1.+), nitidus 'bright, elegant' (P1.+),
nitidare 'to make bright' (Enn.+), nitidiusculus 'a little bit more shiny' (PL), nitiditas
'elegance' (Acc.), nitescere 'to become bright' (Enn.+); enitere 'to shine forth' (Acc.+).
The Latin words are easiest understood as building on a ppp. *nitos 'made bright,
shining', but there is no known IE verbal root which fits the form and meaning. If
renideo is connected, the root would be *neit-, *noit-, *nit~. The appurtenance of Skt.
nila- 'dark-coloured' < *nei(H)-? is very uncertain.
Bibl.: WH II: 171, EM 442, IEW 760. renideo
mtor, -T 'to lean on, support oneself; strive, strain' [v. Ill; ppp. nixus, msvs; Paul, ex
F. gnitor, gnixum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: nisus, -us 'advance, pressure' (Pac.+), nixarT 'to support oneself
(Lucr.+), nixus, -us 'straining, travail' (Lucr.+); admit 'to exert oneself, lean' (P1.+),
coniti 'to strain, strive' (P1.+), eniti 'to struggle out, give birth' (P1.+), enixim
'strenuously' (Sis.), obriitl 'to thrust, make a stand' (Enn.+), obnixe 'strenuously'
(PL+), submxus 'propped up, assisted' (PL+).
Pit. *knif-et- [pr.], *knif-s~.
IE cognates: see s.v. coniveo.
The original meaning could be 'lean on' but also 'exert pressure'. Since the original
meaning is unclear, the connection with Skt. yat- 'to be/put in place, line up' (thus
LIV) remains gratuitous. Even for 'lean on', it would not be compelling. Furthermore,
note that *ni does not occur as a preverb in Latin. Lat. nixus (possibly for *nictus, cf.
Leumann 1977: 615) is difficult to explain secondarily, and points to a velar-final
I
root. Thus, the connection with comved 'to be tightly closed' put forward by WH and
IEW still seems preferable; in that case, gn- in Paul, ex F. could be etymologically
justified. Nitor is explained as a back-formation to nfsus by Leumann 1977: 188, but
there is no good model for such replacem...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: no, nare 'to swim' [v. I; pf. navT\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: natare 'to swim, float' (P1.+), natator 'swimmer' (Varro+); enare 'to
swim out, forth' (P1.+), trdnsndre 'to swim, sail across' (Enn.+).
Pit. *(s)naje/o- 'to swim', *(s)nato- 'bathed'. It. cognates: ? U. snata, snatu
[acc.pl.n.], snates, snate [abl.pl.n.] 'cleansed', asnata, asnatu [acc.pl.n.], asnates,
asnate [abl.pl.n.] 'uncleansed'?
PIE pr. *(s)neh -(ie/o~) 'to swim, float', ppp. *(s)nh -to-. IE cognates: Olr. maid,
2 2
•sna* 'to swim' < PCI. *sna(-ie/o)~, Skt. pr. snatas [3d.act.], snati- [ptc.act.f.], caus.
snapdya-, ta-ptc. snata- 'to bathe, wash', YAv.- us...snaiia- 'to wash out', fra-snaiia-
'to wash clean\fra-snata- 'washed', usnaiti- [f.] 'washing off, Gr. vrj^w, Dor. vctxco
'to swim', Gr. veto, ipf. eweov, aor. veuaai 'to swim' < *snd-; ToB nask- 'to bathe'.
Natare was built to a ppp. *natos. Whether its preform was *nh to- or *snh -to- is
2 2
hard to say (cf. Schrijver 1991: 171). The PIE present .was probably a root present, to
which Italic added *-je/o~. The meaning and therefore the appurtenance of the U.
forms is uncertain; they refer to vessels used in connection with sacrifices, maybe
with ointments.
Bibl.: WH II: 146f., 172, EM 442f., IEW 971f., Schrijver 1991: 168f., 403, Sihler
1995: 529, Meiser 1998: 112, Untermann 2000: 687f., Schumacher 2004: 596, LIV
*(s)neh -. —»• navis
2

Pit. *nokeje- 'to cause death'.
PIE *nok-eie- 'to make disappear, cause death'. IE cognates: Skt. nasdya- 'to make

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nodus 'knot, node' [m. o] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: nodare 'to furnish with joints, tie in a knot' (Cato+); enodare 'to prune
surplus nodes from, unravel' (Cato+), inenoddbilis 'that cannot be disentangled'
(Acc.+), internodium 'intemode' (Varro+).
PIE *nodo- / *noHdo- / *neh do- 'knot'. IE cognates: OIc. not 'net'; see s.v. nassa.
3
The root etymology is uncertain; apart from the possible PIE preforms, an analysis as
a loanword is also possible. See s.v. nassa.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nil men 'name' [n. n\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: nominare 'to name, designate' (P1.+), nominitare 'to call' (Lucr.+),
nominalis 'of nouns' (Varro), nominatim 'by name, specifically' (P1.+), nominates,
-us 'noun' (Varro), nominatTvus 'nominative* (Varro+); cognomen 'surname,
sobriquet' (P1.+), cogndmentum 'surname, cognomen' (P1.+), cognominis [adj.]
'having the same name' (P1.+), cogndminare 'to give a (sur)name' (Varro+),
ignominia 'disqualification, disgrace' (Lucil.+), praendmen 'personal name'
(Varro+), praenomindre 'to name with the prenomen' (Varro), pronomen 'pronoun'
(Varro+); nuncupare 'to declare, pronounce, appoint' (Lex XII+).
Pit. *nom-n-. It. cognates: O. numneis [gen.sg.], num(nud) [abl.sg.], U. numem,
nome [nom.acc.sg.], nomner [gen.sg.], nomne [dat.sg.], nomne [abl.sg.], nomneper [+
-per] 'name' [n.].
PIE *h neh3-mn, *h nh -men- [n.] 'name'. IE cognates: Olr. ainm (pi. anmann) ,
3 3 3
OW anu 'name', Hit. laman- /lamn- [n.], HLuw. alaman- [n.], Lyc. alaman- 'name',
Skt. naman-, Av. naman- [n.], nama, nama [adv.] 'named, by name', Gr. ovopa
'name', v(Dvup(v)o<; 'without a name*, Phryg. onoman, Arm. anown, Alb. emer (<
*emen)\ ToA HOW, ToB nem, OPr. emmens, OCS im§ [n.] < BS1. *in?men-, Go. namo,
OE nama, OIc. nafn.
The g in agnomen and cognomen was introduced on the model of aco-gndsco 'to
know, recognize'. The verb nuncupare must be denominal to a noun *nomiceps
'taking a name, declaring' > *nomicupare > *nomcupare > *nomcupare > nuncupare.
The reconstruction of the root is disputed. Originally, PIE *(H)nom- was posited, thus
still by Sihler. Yet this does not explain Latin and Germanic unless by an unmotivated
o-grade. Thus, the root structure was *HnH- Many scholars now seem to agree on the
identity of the second laryngeal as *h , and this had led to an identification with the
3
verbal root attested in Hit. hanna- i hann- 'to judge, sue' (< *h ne-h noh -) and Gr.
3 3 3
Svojicu 'to blame, repudiate*. LIV reconstructs initial *h,...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nonnus 'monk, nun' [m. f. o, a] (Ecclesiastical Lat.)
PIE *nVnV. IE cognates: W, nain 'grandmother', Skt. nana, MoP nana 'mother',
Gr. vawac; [m./f.] 'uncle, aunt', vewoq 'uncle', Alb. nene 'mother', Ru. njanja 'child
maid' (dial, also 'older sister'), Bulg. neni 'the elder'.
Nursery word meaning 'daddy, nanny1, reduplication of dental nasal plus (mostly) /a/.
Bibl.: WH II: 175, EM 444, IEW 754. anus, mamma
nos 'we' [pron. pers. lp.] (Naev.+; nom.acc. nos, dat.abl. nobis; gen. nostrum, nostrv,
nostrorum [m.], nostrarum [f.])
Derivatives: noster 'our' (Andr.+), nostras, -tis 'native, of our country' (Cato+),
nostrdtim 'in our manner' (Varro).
Pit. *nds 'we, us', *nofei 'us', *nos-tero- 'our'.
PIE acc. *nos, gen. *no/es, adj.poss. *ns-tero~? IE cognates: Olr. sni 'we' (<
*snes? *snoi?), -nn 'us' < *(s)nos?, nathar 'ours' < *ndstrom, ar n- < *nstrom; Hit.
anz- 'us' [acc. an-za-as (OS), an-za-a-as, gen.sg. an-ze-el (OS)], Luw. anza 'we, us',
HLuw. anz- 'we, us' < PIE *ns- V, enclitic Hit. =nnas '(to) us, our', HLuw. 'us' <
*-nos; Skt. nas [acc.], nas [gen.dat.] 'us', OAv. na [act.] < *nas, n§ [gen.dat.] < *nas,
YAv. no [acc.gen.dat.] < *nds; Av. ahma 'us' < *ns-me\ Gr. nom. rjjigTq, acc. Tjpaq,
Ion. f)peaq, Dor. nom. ajieQ acc. ape, Aeol. nom. appeq, acc. &ppe < PGr. *aope <
PIE *ns-me\ Alb. na < *nds; OCS nom.pl. my, ny, gen.pl. nasb, Go. uns < *ns-, ToA
nas.
The original nom. *uei(s) was replaced by the stressed acc. form *nds, apparently
already in Pit. The gen. nostrum is originally the gen.pl. of noster, after Plautus it was
replaced by nostri. The opposition between stressed *nos and enclitic *nos may date
back to PIE.
Bibl.: WH II: 175f., EM 444f., IEW 758, Leumann 1977: 463f„ Schrijver 1991: 122,
Sihler 1995: 381, Beekes 1995: 208f., Meiser 1998:158.
\

PI., Caecil. also gn-)

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: novacula 'razor' [f. a] (Cic.+)
Pit. *(ks)nowdtlo~.
PIE *ks-n(e)-u- [pr.] 'to scrape'. IE cognates: Skt. ava k$naumi [pr.], ksmivana-
[ptc.med.] 'to whet, sharpen', °ksnut- [adj.] '-sharpened', ksndtra- [n.] 'whetstone';
ksura- 'razor, sharp knife'; OAv. xsnaus [3s.aor.act.], xsnmtuisa [2s.pr./aor.opt.med.]
'to hear', YAv. hu-xsnuta- 'well-sharpened', OP a-xsnauvaiy/a-xsnumiy 'I hear',
a-xsniidiy 'hear!*.
Instrument noun *sneuaklo- < *ksneuaklo~ based on a verb stem *(ks)neud~, which is
itself derived from a PIE stem *ksneu- 'to scrape (so as to make smooth), sharpen'.
The retention of -ov- proves PIE *-euV- rather than *-ouV~, which would have yielded
*-aw- (at least, in pretonic position). Vine (2006: 214-216) posits an original deverbal
adj. *ksneu-o- '(scraped) smooth' whence a factitive 'to make smooth' or
denominative present *ksneu-efi2-ie/o- would have been formed in Italic. In view of
the meaning, Ilr. and Latin *ks-n-u- is likely to be an original nasal present to *kseu-
'to scrape, make smooth' (see s.v. saucius) rather than a ^-extension to the stem
*ks-en- 'to comb, card'. Both probably go back to a single PIE root *kes- 'to scratch,
*
dig, order' and/or *kes- 'to cut off (Lat. castrdre, cared). The latter two roots may in
origin be identical.
Bibl.: WH II: 178, EM 446, IEW 585f., Schrijver 1991: 449, Meiser 1998: 112, LIV
*ksneu~, *kseu-. —> saucius

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: novem 'nine' [num. indecl.].(Naev.+) '
Derivatives: november, -6ns;. [adj.] 'the ninth month' (Cato+), novenarius 'ninefold'
(Varro+), novem [pl.adj.] 'nine each, nine at a time^ (Varro+), novie(n)s [adv.] 'nine
times' (Varro+); nonus 'ninth' (Cato+), Nonae [f.pL] 'the ninth day before the Ides'
(Enn.+), ndnalis 'of the Nones' (Varro), nonagintd 'ninety' (Varro+), ndnagesimus
'ninetieth' (Varro+), nonussis 'the sum of nine asses' (Varro+); nundinae [f.pl.]
'market day (held every ninth day)' (Lex XII+), mmdinalis 'of market-days' (P1.+),
internundinum (Lucil.+) 'period of nine days'.
Pit. *new# 'nine'.
PIE *hineun 'nine', *h]neun-o- 'ninth'. IE cognates: Olr. noi n-, W. Co. now, Skt.
nch'a, Av. nduua, Gr. ewea, Arm. inn, Alb. nende, Lith. devyni, OCS devgtb, Go.
OHG niun, Toch. nu 'nine'.
Nitndino- goes.back to *noweno-dino- 'of the ninth day', with *di-n- 'day' (cf. dies).
Internundinum must be explained as an univerbation of inter nundinas 'between
market-days' (Risch 1985: 333). The existence of a sg. nundinum 'the period from one
market day to the next' (SCBac.+; nound- SCBac.) has been refuted by Risch 1985,
who shows that all old attestations of trinum nundinum (in laws, in Cicero) 'period
comprising three market-days' must be understood as gen. of pi. nundinae. In norms
'ninth', *-owe- was restored after regular syncope of *noweno- > *nouno- had taken
place; the contraction product of this later sequence *-owe- was o, not u. Nonagintd is
either based on *nonginta, or was formed secondarily to the ordinal nonus.
Bibl.: WH II: 179f., 188, EM 446f„ IEW 3l8f., Schrijver 1991: 17, 275, 278-281,
Meiser 1998: 172, van der Staaij 1995: 111, Coleman 1992: 396,412f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: novus 'new' [adj. o/a] (F1.+)
Derivatives: denuo [adv.] 'anew, once more' (P1.+); novellas 'young, tender'
(Varro+), novalis 'left unploughed, fallow' (Varro+), novitas 'novelty' (Lucr.+),
novare 'to make as something new, renew' (Lucr.+), noverca 'step-mother' (P1.+),
novicius 'newly imported, recently discovered* (P1.+); renovare 'to restore, renew'
(Acc.+); novensides 'title of an obscure set of deities* (Varro+).
Pit. *nowo-, *nowjo-. It. cognates: U. nuvis [nom.sg.], nuvime [acc.sg. + -en]
'new* < *nouio~.
PIE *neuo- 'new, young', *neuio- 'new*. IE cognates: Hit. neua- 'new, fresh',
neuahh'to renew', CLuw. naua/i- 'new', Skt. nava-, YAv. nauua- 'new, young',
Gr. veoq, Myc. ne-wo, Cypr. VEpo-OTaxo^ 'new, young'; OCS nov-b, Ru. novyj, ToA
nu, ToB nuwe 'new, young' < *neuo-; Gaul. Nevio-, Novio-, Olr. nue, W. newydd,
OBret. nouuid, neuued, Bret, neuez, Skt. navya-, Lith. naujas, Go. niujis 'new' <
*neuio-; Gr. veato 'to work fallow land', veavlcu; 'youth, young man' (< *neua-n-),
vsap6<; 'young, fresh', vrjpcx; 'fresh' < *neua~r-; Arm. nor 'new* < *neu-ro~.
The adv. denuo goes back to abl. *de *nowo 'a-new' > *denuwo. Whereas Latin
continues PIE *neuo- Sabellic has reflexes of *neuio~. The noun noverca cannot be
t
directly linked with the Armenian or Greek r-derivatives, since *neur- would yield
Latin *nur- (or *nerv~), and so would *neuVr-. Hence, noverca is probably a recent
formation made to novus; the suffix is unclear. PIE *neuo- may originally be derived
from the word *nu 'now*.
Bibl.: WH II: 180f., EM 447f., IEW 769, Schrijver 1991: 449, Meiser 1998: 68,
Untermann 2000: 505f., Beekes 2003: 165. ^ nunc, nuper

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nox 'night' [f. t] (abl.sg. node, gen.pl. noctium) (Naev.+)
Derivatives: nox 'by night' (Lex XII+); noctii 'at night' (Naev.+), noctua 'night bird,
little owl' (P1.+), noctuinus 'of the little owl' (PI.), nocturnus 'of the night' (Naev.+);
pernoctare 'to spend the night' (PI.+); aequinoctium 'equinox' (Cato+),
aequinoctialis 'of the equinox' (Varro+); noctuvigilus 'wakeful' (PI.), nocticolor
'night-coloured' (Laev. apud Gell.), noctiluca 'that shines by night' (Laev.+),
noctipuga 'the female genitals' (Lucil.), noctivagus 'roaming by night' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *noklw)-t-.
PIE *nogwh-t-s [nom.sg.], *negwht-s [gen.sg.] 'night'. IE cognates: Olr. innocht
'tonight', Hit. neku-' 'tobecome night' (< *neglvh-ti), nekuz [adv.] 'inthe evening' (=
gen.sg.); Skt. ndkt- 'night' (nom.sg. nak, acc.sg. naktam) , Av. upa.naxturusu
[adj.loc.pl.] 'bordering on the night' for "naxtsrd-?, Wax! nayd 'night*, Gr. VU^ (VUKT-)
'night' (< *nokwts), vuKicop [adv.] 'at night', vuKtepot; 'nightly', voKiepiq, -1601; [f.]
'bat', voKTEptvo^ 'nightly'; -vu%- e.g. b/-vo%-oq, ^v-vux-ioi; 'nightly, in the night',
vuxtoq 'nightly', vxy/eb(o 'to wake through the night'; Lith. naktis 'night' (gen.pl-
nakl-q), OCS nostb 'night'; Go. nahts 'night'.
The form nox 'at night' is regarded as the remainder of an old gen.sg. *nokw-t-es
(Leumann 1977: 431), but phonetically more likely is a gen.sg. *nokw-t-s, as Meiser
assumes. Pinault 2006a explains nox 'at night' from an old loc.sg. *nokwi > *nok,
which was remade into nox by analogy wih dins 'by day'. He also assumes that noctu
nudus
417
and noctiirnus presuppose a loc.sg. *nokwt-eu, which was made at an earlier stage on
the example of loc.sg. *dieu 'by day*. Noctua is derived from nox 'night' or noctu 'at
night'. Nocturnus follows diurnus 'daily', vespernus 'of the evening', and probably
reflects *noctu-rino- (or it has acquired -rnus directly at a more recent stage). Verbal
forms are only attested in Anat., the word for 'night' must be...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: niibes, -is 'cloud' [f. (m.) /'] (Andr.+; nubs [nom.sg.] in Andr.)
Derivatives: nubilus 'cloudy, hazy, dark' (P1.+), nubildre/i 'to become cloudy,
overcast' (Cato+), innubilus 'cloudless' (Lucr.), nubilarium 'shelter from the rain'
(Varro+); obniibere 'to veil, cover' (usually the head, but also other objects) (Varro+).
Pit. *(s)noup-(i~).
PIE *(s)ne/oudh-, IE cognates: W. nudd 'haze'; YAv. snaoSa- 'clouds', snaoddnt-
'weeping' (vel sim.), Bal. nod '(rain-)cloud'.
If nubs, -is is the old inflection, this may be a root noun or /-stem. The only direct
cognate is W. nudd, which would imply an etymology *(s)ne/oudh- In Latin,
obniibere suggests that the stem nub- meant a 'cover'; note that 'clouds' are often
denominated as 'cover'. This renders a connection possible with the word nubere 'to
marry' (said of the woman), see there. An alternative analysis is as *sn-eu- + *-ct~, to
be compared with Skt. pra-snauti 'drips, drops',pra-snuta- 'releasing mother's milk',
see s.v. rwtrix.
Bibl.: WH II: 183, EM448f., IEW 978, Schrijver 1991: 375.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nubo, -ere 'to get married' [v. Ill; pf. nupsi, ppp. nuptum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: nuptiae [f.pl.] 'marriage, wedding' (P1.+), nuptialis 'of a marriage or
wedding' (P1.+), nupta 'bride' (P1.+), nuptula 'young bride' (Varro); conubium
'intermarriage;, marriage' (Lucr.+), innuba 'unmarried' (Varro+).
Pit. *{s)nu/ouf-e/o- i *(s)nu/oup-e/o~.
Long o in conubium is supposed to prove original *sn-. A connection with Gr. vuptpT]
'bride' must be dismissed. Nubere has been compared with RuCS snubiti 'to court,
love' (e.g. in LIV), but EM object that this verb is used for the man seeking a bride,
not for a woman marrying. Hence, this connection becomes less certain. EM favour
an interpretation of nubere as 'to take the veil', especially in view of obniibere 'to
cover' (see s.v. nubes). Semantically this is attractive, although it cannot be proven.
Morphologically, however, an etymology involving root-final *-dh is difficult, since
the ppp. is nuptum rather than *nussum (cf. iubeo - hissus). Thus, nuptum would have
to be a fairly recent formation on the basis of the present stem.
Bibl.: WH I: 268, II: 183f., EM 449, IEW 977f„ Leumann 1977: 190, LIV *sneubV

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nfldus 'naked' [adj. o/d] (P1.+)
Derivatives: nuddre 'to make naked, strip' (Enn.+), denuddre 'to lay bare, strip'
(Pac.+).
Pit. *nowo/epo- 'naked1.
PIE *nogws [nom.], *negws [gen.] 'naked'; » LatGm. *nogwodho-. IE cognates:
Olr. nocht, W. noeth (< *nog"'-to-), Hit. nekumanza, Skt. nagnaYAv, mayna-, Khot.
bunaa- (< PIr. *bagnaka-) 'naked', Gr. yupvoc; (< *gogwno- < * nog'no-), Arm. merk
(< *megwro- < *negw-ro-\ OPr. nognan 'leather', Lith. nuogas 'naked', OCS nagb
'naked' (< PIE *nogw-o-); Go. naqaps, OE nacod, MoDu. naakt (< *nogwot/dh-o-),
OIc. nakinn, OFr. naken (< *nogwno-?).
The adj. can be explained via a phonetic development *nogwopo- > *nowodo- >
*noodo- > *nodo- > nudus (Schrijver 1991). The Pit. form is based on a PIE root adj.
which acquired different extensions in different IE languages (cf. Beekes 1994).
Bibl: WH II: 185, EM 449f., IEW 769, Schrijver 1991: 274-281, Beekes 1994: 91-94.
uOgae 'worthless things, nonsense' [f.pl. a\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: niigdn 'to speak without seriousness, tell stories' (PI.+), niigdtor 'one
who trifles, romancer' (P1.+), nugax 'incompetent, farcical' (P1.+), nugatorius [adj.]
'futile, romancing' (P1.+); nugigerulus 'who peddles trumpery' (PL).
Pit. *knug~.
No PIE etymology. Since woFds for 'trifle' are sometimes formed from words for
'nut' or 'seed', a connection with Lat. nux is quite likely. In view of the difference in
vowel length (u vs. u) and in the velar (g vs. k) between nugae and nux, this might be
a foreign loanword in Latin.
Bibl.: WH II: 185, EM 450. nux

metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: num 'I suppose, whether' [ptcle.] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: nunc 'now' (Naev.+), nunciam 'here and now' (PL+); nuper [adv.]
'recently' (PL+), nuperus 'fresh' (P1.+).
Pit. *num 'now', *nu 'now'.
PIE *nu 'now', *nu-n(-6/oi) . IE cognates: Hit. nu [ptcle.] 'and, but', Pal. nu, nu,
Hit. ki-nun 'now', CLuw. ndnun 'now', Skt. nunam 'now, indeed', OAv. nur§m,
YAv. nuram, nurqm, OP nuram 'now', MP nun 'now', Gr. vu, vuv, vuv 'now', Lith.
nun, nunai 'now, today', OCS nyne, CS ntne, Ru. nyne 'now, today', none (dial.)
'now, today', Go. nu, OHG nu, late MHG nun 'now'.
Like some other languages, Latin preserves traces of the PIE adv. *nu (in niiper) and
of extended *nu-n(V) (in nunc < *num-ce). In (*)num, final -m might reflect PIE *-n
(for which hardly any good evidence is available in Latin), or a remake of earlier
*nun on the analogy of nam and -nem. For nuper, two etymologies have been
proposed: an original adj. *newo-paro- 'bringing forth new things', or an adv. *mi
'now' + -per. Phonetically, it is impossible to decide; but semantically, the latter is
definitely more likely, since nuper does not have an active meaning (thus WH). Also,
if the adj. was older (nuperus), one would expect an adv. *nuperum. Probably, PIE
*nu turned to *nu in stressed position in many individual languages.
Bibl.: WH II: 186-188, 190, EM 450, 452, IEW 770, Leumann 1977: 401. -ce,
novus, -per ,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: numerus 'number, rank, category1 [m. o] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: numerare 'to reckon, count1 (P1.+), Numeria 'goddess of delivery and
counting' (Varro); adnumerare 'to tell out, reckon5 (P1.+), denumerare 'to pay in full'
(PI.+), dinumerare 'to calculate' (P1.+), enumerare 'to count up, enumerate' (P1-+),
pernumerdre 'id.' (P1-+), renumerare 'to pay back, report' (P1.+); innumerus
'countless' (Lucr.+), inmimeralis 'id.' (Lucr.+), innumerdbilis 'id.' (Lucr,+).
Pit. *nomeso~.
PIE *nem-e/os- [n.] 'apportioning, sacrifice', *nom-o-. IE cognates: Hit. lammar /
lamn- [n.] 'moment', HLuw. lam(i)ni 'at the time' < *nom~r, *n(o)m-n-os; Gr. vepto
'to dispense, distribute', vojifj [f.] 'pasture', vopo<; [m.] 'pasture, habitation,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: province', vopo<; [m.] 'custom, usage, law, composition'.
Probably a thematized form of the PIE s-stem *nem-os seen in nemus. The o-grade in
numerus must be due to analogical replacement of the e-grade of the root by the
o-grade on the model of *nom~o- 'rank, number', Lat. *nemos, -esos » *nomos,
-esos (cf. pondus, -eris for the same development). Subsequently, the gen.sg.
*nomes-os must have been reinterpreted as the new nom.acc.sg. *nomeso-s, yielding
a thematic paradigm.
Bibl.: WH II: 186f., EM 450f„ IEW 763fi, Meiser 1998: 83, LIV I.*nem-. nemus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nflntius 'messenger; message' [m. o] (Naev.+; grammarians nountius, CIL nontius)
Derivatives: niintidre 'to bring word of, deliver a message' (P1.+), nuniium
'message' (Varro+); enuntidre 'to make known, express' (P1.+), internuntius
'messenger' (PI.+), inierniintia 'female messenger' (P1.+), obnuntidre 'to announce'
(Ter.+), praeniintius [adj./m.] 'heralding; herald' (Lucr.+), rermntius 'reporter' (PI.),
renuntiare 'to report, announce, renounce'.
Probably *nountio-, but the further etymology is unclear. It has been derived from the
root *neuH- 'to cry' (Skt. novate), unattested in Latin, but also from novus 'new'. But
in the latter case, a reconstruction *no(wo)wentio~ does not make sense
morphologically.
Bibl.: WH II: I88f„ EM 451fi, IEW 767, Schrijver 1991: 275.
-nuo, -ere 'to nod' [v. Ill; pf. -niii, ppp. -nutum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: numen 'motion of the head, nod; divinity' (Acc.+); abnuere 'to refuse,
deny, forbid' (P1-+), abnuere 'id.' (Enn.), adnuere 'to nod, permit' (P1.+), innuere 'to
nod, beckon' (P1.+); nutdre 'to nod with the head, bend forward, sway' (P1.+), niitus,
-us 'nod' (Lucr.+), abnutare 'to disapprove' (P1.+), adnutare 'to nod, assent'
(Naev.+), renutare 'to refuse' (Lucr.).
Pit. *nowe/o-.
PIE *neu-e/o- 'to nod1. IE cognates: Olr. as-noi* 'to promise' (< *eks-nou~), ad noi*
'to entrust', MW dineu, MBret. dinou 'to pour forth, flow' < PCI. *neue/o-; Gr. veuco
[pr.] 'to nod, beckon1 (< *neu-s- or < *veco with introduction of veu- from the aorist).
Less clearly: Skt. -nauti [3s.act.pr.], caus. dti-navayet [3s.opt.act.] 'to move', Sogd.
nw- 'to go slowly', n'w- 'to shake', MoP navidan/nav- 'to shake, tremble; move (esp.
when rising from a place)', Lith. niausti 'to bend'.
Numen and nutus can have *ne/ou- and -nud can be from *-neuo. The thematic
present is also found outside Italic, while the Lat. noun and the ppp. were derived
from the pr. ,
Bibl.: WH H: 189, EM 452, IEW 767, Meiser 2003: 235, Schumacher 2004: 491,
LI...
