content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nurus, -us 'daughter-in-law' [f. u] (Lex Reg., Ter.+)
Pit. *snuso-» *snusit~.
PIE *snuso- [f.] 'daughter-in-law'. IE cognates: Skt. snusa-, Sogd. swnsh, Oss.
nostce, MoP suna, sun(h)ar 'daughter-in-law', Gr. vuoq [f.] 'daughter-in-law'; Arm.
now, gen.sg. nowoy [f.], SeCS sm>xa, Ru. snoxa, SCr. snaha < BS1. *snus-eh2~, OE
snoru [f.], OFr. snore 'daughter-in-law'.
The noun has shifted from o-stem to w-stem inflection under the influence of socrus,
-iis 'mother-in-law'. Possibly, PIE *snit-s-o- is a derivative of the same root *sneu-
'to drip, nurse' from which niitrix is often derived.
Bibl.: WH II: 190, EM 452, IEW 978, LIV *sneu- (?). nutrix
nutrix 'child's nurse' [f. k] (P1.+)
Derivatives: nutrire 'to suckle, nourish' (Cat.+); nutricula 'child's nurse' (Lucil.+),
nutricdre 'to suckle, nourish' (P1-+), nutricatus, -us 'nurturing' (P1.+), rmtricatio
'suckling, rearing' (Varro+), nutricius 'foster-father, guardian' (Var.+).
Pit. *(s)noutri.
PIE *sneu-tr-ih2- 'female nurser'. IE cognates: Skt. pra-snauti [3s.act.] 'to drip,
release liquids', prd-snuta- 'releasing mother's milk'. Appurtenance of Gr. vato 'to
flow, stream' (< *vajr-jco) is difficult and requires special pleading.
The verb nutrire was derived from *noutri before this acquired the suffix *-k-.
Afterwards, from *nutrik- the verb nutricdre was derived (thus Steinbauer 1989). It
has been proposed that the PIE root *sneu- is cognate to *sneh - 'to bathe, swim',
2
pointing to an analysis *sn-u- and *sn-h -
r
Bibl.: WH I: 190f., EM 453, IEW 97If., Leumann 1977: 376, Steinbauer 1989: 76f.,
Schrijver 1991: 152f., LIV *sneu-. —> nubes, nurus

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: nux, -cis 'nut' [f. £] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: nuceus 'of a nut-tree' (Cato), nuc(u)leus 'kernel, stone (of fruit)' (P1.+);
nucifrangibulum 'nutcracker' (PL).
Pit. *knuk~.
IE cognates: Olr. cnu [f.], gen. cnao, end, acc. cnoi, MW cneu 'nuts', LCo. knyfan 'a
nut', MBret. cnou, Bret, kraon 'nuts' < PCI. nom. *kntis, obi. *knuu- (< *knu-H-l);
OIc. hnot, OE hnutu 'nut' < *knu-d~.
Lat. nuc(u)leus presupposes a diminutive *nucuhis/-a. As argued s.v. nugae, the
irregular alternations within Latin between nugae and nux already suggest a non-IE
loanword. To this we may add the different suffixes in Celtic (*-//-?) and Germanic
(*-d~) and the sequence *knu-, which does not look very Indo-European (only with a
full grade *kneu~, but this is not attested). In combination with the restriction to
Italo-Celtic and Gm., and the semantic field of trees and fruits, we may safely assume
non-IE origin.
Bibl.: WH II: 19lf., EM 453, IEW 558f., Leumann 1977: 287, Strunk 1993,
Schrijver 1995: 329f. —> niigae
o

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ob 'towards, in front of; with regard to, because of [prep.] (Lex XII+); variants: ob in
isolation and as a preverb in front of vowels, s-, t-, h- and all voiced consonants
except g- (variants ops-, opt- especially in inscriptions); oc- in front of c-; of- in front
o f f; og- in front of g-; o- in front of m- (only in omit to), more usually ohm- or om-\
op- (in front of p-).
Derivatives: optu/imus 'best' (Naev.+), optu/imds, -tis [adj.] 'aristocratic' (Enn.+).
Pit. *op(i). Lejeune 1974 assumes Ven. op < *opi, but this is uncertain. It. cognates:
Ven. op 'because of, for', 0. up, op + abl. 'at'. Fal. opidque 'ob idque'. Maybe in U.
ustentu, ostendu [3s.ipv.II] (meaning uncertain).
PIE *hiopi 'at, by'. IE cognates: Lith. ap(i)- 'around' < *h|6pi; Skt. dpi, Av. aipi,
OP apiy 'also; by, in', Gr. em 'on, at', Arm. ew 'also, and' < *hiepi.
The variant *obs- only occurs in a few forms, and seems to be relatively recent and
short-lived. Older are oscen, ostendo, ostento and derivatives. Original *opw- gave
op-, as preserved in oportet and operid. Cowgill 1970: 116f., 147, Leumann 1977:
317f. and Sihler 1995 regard optumus as a sup. to ob, meaning 'uppermost'. Since ob
does not mean 'up, above' in Latin but 'towards', one may also conjecture a meaning
'foremost'. WH explain optumus as a sup. *opi-tVmo- 'most powerful', but this is
much less likely. Lat. ob and Lith. ap(i)- can be taken to reflect *h|Op(i) 'at, by' (the
Italic forms might be better explained from a monosyllable *h[Op), an ablaut variant
of *h]epi as seen in Ilr., Gr. and Arm. Kloekhorst 2008: 194 argues on semantic
grounds that Hit. appa, CLuw. appa 'back, again' probably reflect *h op-, and go
2
together with Gr. 67tt-ci9ev '(from) behind', Myc. o-pi, oye 'afterwards', o\j/i(-) (Aeol.
and in cp.).
Bibl.: EM 454, Lejeune 1974: 337, IEW 323ff., Leumann 1977: 157f., Hamp 1981a,
Schrijver 1991: 50f., Sihler 1995: 203f., 368, 440, Meiser 1998: 153, Untermann
2000: 799f., 812f., 8l6f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: obliquus 'slanting, transverse' [adj. o/a] (Cato+)
The etymology is unknown. Closest in form and meaning are limus 'transverse' and
sublimis 'transverse from below upward1, and the latter would be morphologically
similar to obliquus. Yet a root *li- with different suffixes *-mo- and *-kwo- does not
immediately make sense, and has no clear connections outside Italic.
Bibl.: WH II: 194f., EM 455, IEW 307-309. limus 2
obHvTscor, -I 'to forget' [v. Ill; pf. oblitus sum] (Andr.+; pr. also oblTscT PI., Acc.)
Derivatives: oblTvium 'forgetfulness' (Lucr.+), oblTvius 'lost in oblivion' (Varro),
oblfviosus 'forgetful' (P1.+), oblivid 'forgetting, oblivion' (Varro+).
Schrijver follows Sommer 1914: 602 in assuming an original verb *ob-lTvere 'to be
smoothe' < *ob-leiwe- < *-leh i-u-eh , a stative verb derived from the adj. *lehii-u-i-
1 r
'smooth' which yielded Lat. levis. Yet a semantic shift from 'to be smooth' to 'to
forget' is not very convincing. Keller 1992:254 connects ob-lTtits with Gr. Xivapai 'to
dodge', Gm. *linnan 'to stop', Skt. -liyate 'dissolved' < PIE *lih - 'to stop /
2
disappear'; but the weaknesses of her theory have been addressed by Seldeslachts
2001: 89. The latter returns to the adj. levis, and proposes to regard oblrviscor 'to start
being erased' as an inchoative deponent to a denominative verb *oblTvire 'to make
smooth, erase' < *ob-levi-je/o-. The ptc. oblitus would stem from *ob-litus, the ptc. of
ob-linere 'to smear, stop', taking its -/- from the pf. ob-livl of that same verb. The
second part of the hypothesis is weak: why would the ptc. of oblinere have been
applied to obliviscorl
Bibl.: WH II: 195, EM 455, IEW 66If., Schrijver 1991: 284, Meiser 1998: 92.
levis

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: obscaenus 'unpropitious, ill-omened; indecent' [adj. o/a] (P1.+; the second vowel
vacillates between ae and e)
Pit *skai-no- 'left, unpropitious'.
PIE *skeh -i-no- 'shaded; left'. IE cognates: see s.v. scaevus.
2
Since e can be a phonetic development for ae in second syllable (*ai > *e > T)
whereas ae cannot be explained if the original vowel was e, obscaenus must be the
original form. EM propose to compare scaevus 'left, bad', and analyse *ob-skai-no-
'coming from the left side'. This seems worthy of consideration: semantically it
would fit perfectly, and, formally, a Pit. formation *skaino- would be quite close to
Balto-Slavic *skeh i-n(-i)- 'shadow'.
2
Bibl.: WH I: 131, EM 456, IEW 628, LIV *skeH(i)-. scaevus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: obscurus 'dark, gloomy' [adj. o/a] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: obscurare 'to obscure (heavenly bodies or sources of light), darken'
(P1.+), obscuridicus 'speaking obscurely' (Acc.), obscuritas 'darkness, obscurity'
(Varro+).
Pit. *skoi-ro-.
PIE *skoh -i-ro- 'dark, shady'? IE cognates: Gr. onpov [n.] (description of a white
2
parasol or canopy); Go. skeirs, OIc. skirr, NHG schier 'clear, bright' < *skiro~.
The original meaning seems to be 'darkened, covered'. It is disputed whether we must
analyse the word as ob-scurus or obs-curus, since oc- is productive in Latin in front of
c-, but oscen shows *obs~. If ob-scurus, Schrijver supports the derivation of *skuro-
from a preform *skuHro- 'roof, cover'; but this is semantically questionable, since the
compound would then mean '(what is) toward/in front of the cover', which is not
what obscurus means, or a pleonastic 'against-cover', which is a rare type of
formation. If obs-ciirus, Schrijver (p. 462) cites Hamp's etymology (1982-1983a: 99)
*obs-kwois-o- 'obstructing one's sight', the phonetic weakness of which Schrijver
points out himself. Among the possible phonetic preforms of non-initial -urus are PIE
*-uHro-, *-e/o(H)uro- and (maybe) *-o(H)iro-. Also, a word in *sk- seems to me
more probable than a word in *k-, since sk- could then have been restored after a
phonetic change to *osc~. If the etymology of scaevus < *skeh iuo- and obscaenus <
2
*op-skeh ino- 'shaded, left' is accepted, one might posit an ablaut variant *skoiro- <
2
*skoh i-ro- 'dark, shaded'. Semantically this would be fine; morphologically, there is
2
no exact cognate in the other IE languages.
Bibl.: WH II: 196, EM 456, IEW 951-953, Schrijver 1991: 246f., LIV *(s)keuh.
r

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: obturo, -are 'to block, stop up' [v. I] (PI.+)
Derivatives: returare 'to unplug' (Varro).
The stem -turd- seems to presuppose a Pit. nominal form *tiiro~, *tVu(Y)ro-, or
*toiro-. WH and IEW connect -turd with PIE *tuh 'to swell', which is semantically
r
possible (*turo- 'swelling' > 'plug'), but since obturare can also be interpreted as 'to
seal, protect', it can also be connected with tutus 'safe' < *tuH~. But obviously, any
plug-like object with the required phonological form could be the input for -turare, so
that all remains uncertain.
Bibl.: WH II: 197, EM 456, IEW 1080-1085. -> turged
>

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: occa 'harrow' [f. a] (LLat. texts and glosses)
Derivatives: occdre 'to harrow, break up the ground' (P1.+), occdtor 'harrower'
(P1.+), occillare 'to break in pieces' (PI.).
Pit. *oketd-l
PIE *h ok-et- [f.] 'harrow'. IE cognates: W. Bret, oged 'harrow', Gr. o£;tva 'an
2
agricultural implement with iron teeth, drawn by oxen' (Hsch.); OPr. aketes, Lith.
akScios, dial, ekicios [nom.pl.f. a], Latv. ece(k)sas 'harrow', Ru. oset' 'granary, rack
for drying grain' [f.], Pl.jesiec (dial.) 'grain sieve' < BS1. *es-et-i~, OHG egida, OE
egede < PGm.. *axepo-.
Since the verb occdre and other derivatives are attested much earlier and better than
the noun, TLL'-suggests that the noun was back-formed to the verb. But since the verb
is of the first class, it would still seem to be formed on the basis of a noun. The Latin
cluster cc may derive from secondary *tk, which led Hirt (1916-17: 230) to posit a
development PIE *oketa > pre-Latin *okita > *otika > syncope *otka > occa. In view
of the closely matching Celtic, Germanic and BS1. forms which have the same
meaning, this idea is appealing; but the assumed metathesis is not supported by other
evidence. Alternatively, occa may be a borrowing from a hypothetical (Italic) dialect
in which *kt developed into kk. In that case, we can posit *oketa- > *okta- > occa.
Bibl.: WH II: 197f, EM 456f„ IEW 18-22, Hirt 1916-17: 230, Schrijver 1991: 52.
—» aceo, acu-, ocris

Derivatives: occultare 'to conceal' (PI.+).
Pit. *kele/o-.
PIE *kel-e/o- 'to hide'. IE cognates: Olr. ceilid*, ceil, OW kelu, MBret. keles 'to
hide' < PCI. *fcel-e/o-, OE helan 'to hide'; Go. huljan 'to shroud' < *kl-ie-.
Present from ob + *kele~. The spelling -quol- in SCBac. is hypercorrect for -cub,
Bibl.: WH II: 198, EM 111, IEW 553f., Schumacher 2004: 394f., LIV *tcel-. ->

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ocior 'faster, swifter' [adj.comp. r\ (Andr.+)
Derivatives: sup. ocissimus (Ter.+) / oxime (Paul, ex F.).
Pit. *ok-.
PIE *hiOhik-u- 'quick', *h ohilc-ios- 'quicker'. IE cognates: OW diauc, MW diawg,
1
Co. dioc, OBret. diochi, Bret, diek 'lazy' < PCI. *dt-akV- 'lazy', lit 'un-swift'; Skt.
asu- 'quick', comp. tisTyas-, sup. asistha-, Av. asu- 'quick, fast', comp. asiiah-, sup.
asista-, Gr. cokuc; 'fast, swift', doKiotoq 'fastest'; Ru.jdstreb, ORu.jastrjabbjastrebh,
Po.jastrzqb 'hawk', SCr.jastrijeb 'kite' <PS1. *jastr$bb.
/ /
The etymology *h]k- rests on the identification with PIE *hieku(-o)- 'horse'. If this is
abandoned, *h k- is also possible.
3

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ocris 'rugged mountain' [m. /] ( Andr.+)
Derivatives: mediocris 'of medium size, moderate' (P1.+), mediocriter 'moderately;
on a large scale' (P1.+).
Pit. *okri- [m.] 'hilltop'. It. cognates: U. ukar, ocar [nom.sg.], ocrem, ocre [acc.sg.],
ocrer [gen.sg.], ukre, ocre [dat.sg.], ocre, ocrem [loc.sg.], ukripe, ukriper, ocriper,
ocreper [abl.sg.]; Marr. ocres [gen.sg.]; SPic. okrei [loc.sg.?] 'castle, upper town'.
PIE *h ok-r-i- 'sharp edge'. IE cognates: Mir. ochair 'edge', W. ochr; Skt.
2
catur-asri- [adj.] 'four-angled' (RV); dsri- [f.] 'sharp edge, angle' (Br.+); Gr. oKpiq
[m.] 'point, sharp edge', ctKpi^ [f.] 'summit'.
Ocris is found as an independent word only in a few instances; it was apparently
ousted by collis. It has been preserved in the compound mediocris, orig. 'situated at
the middle, half-way of the top'. Probably an /-derivative of the adj. PIE *h ek-ro-
2
' sharp'.
Bibl.: WH II: 199, EM 457, IEW 18-22, Leumann 1977: 403, Schrijver 1991: 51,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: octo 'eight' [num. indecl.] (P1.+)
Derivatives: octavus 'eighth' (Cato+); octoginta 'eighty' (Cic.+), octdgeni [adj.pl.]
'eighty each' (Lucil.+), october [adj.] 'the eighth month' (Cato+), octoni [adj.pl.]
'eight each' (P1.+), octdnarius 'containing eight, eight long' (Varro+), octingenti
'eight hundred' (P1.+), octingenarius 'consisting of 800 each' (Varro).
Pit *okto 'eight', *oktdwo- 'eighth'.
PIE *h (e)ktehj'eight', *h kt(e)h -uo- 'eighth'. IE cognates: Olr. ocht «-, W. wyth,
3 3 3
Bret, eiz '8', Olr. ochtmad '8th'; Skt. asta, astdu [nom.acc.] '8', astama- '8th', asiti-
'80', YAv. asta '8', astama- '8th', astaiti- '80', Gr. OKTW '8', oySopo^, oyfioo; '8th',
dySoi^KovTa '80', OPhryg. otuvoi vetei 'in the eighth year7, Alb. tete '8'; Lith. astuoni
'8', Go, ahiau; ToB okt, ToA okat48'.
The ordinal seems to show a change of *-ouos > *-auos, which is reinterpreted by
Schrijver 1991: 300 as a delabialization of PIE *-eh -uo- (with labialized laryngeal) to
3
*-eh2-uo- due to the following *-u~.
Bibl.: WH II: 199f„ EM 457f„ IEW 775, Schrijver 1991: 49, 300, Coleman 1992:
396, 412, Beekes 1995: 213-216, Sihler 1995: 414, 432, Meiser 1998: 56, 86, 175,
Beekes 2003: 184.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: oculus 'eye' [m. o] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: ocellus 'little eye' (PL+), ocellatum 'a stone with small spots' (Varro+),
oculatus 'having sight' (P1.+), oculitus [adv.] 'as dearly as one's eyes', oculissimus
'dearest apple of my eye' (PL), oculeus 'made of eyes' (P1.+); unoculus 'that has one
eye' (P1.+); exoculare 'to deprive of eyes' (P1.+).
PIL *ok(e)lo
PIE *h e/okw-(e)lo- 'eye'. IE cognates: Skt. aksnas [gen.sg.], aks-f [nom.acc.du.]
3
'eye', ctn-dks- 'without eyes, blind' < *h e/okw-s-, YAv. asi [nom.acc.du.n.] 'eye', Gr.
3
<3vy [f.], gen. dMtog 'eye, face', oaae [nom.acc.du.n,] 'eyes' < *h kw-i-, OPr. ackis
3
'eyes' [nom.p.], ackins [acc.pl.], Lith. akls, Latv. acs 'eye', OCS oko [nom.sg.], oci
[nom.acc.du.n.] 'eye' < *h ekw-.
3
Since most IE languages have a root noun or an i-stem 'eye', the Latin formation
probably does not go back to>PIE. Oculus could be derived from a PIE root noun
(*h e/okw-), from a PIE thematic noun (probably *h- okw-o-) or from an earlier verb
3 3
'to see'.
Bibl.: WH II: 200-202, EM 458, IEW 775-777, Schrijver 1991: 49, Sihler 1995: 120,
LIV *h eky-.
3
Sdl 'to hate' [v. pf. only, ppp. osum\ (Naev.+)
Derivatives: odibilis 'odious' (Acc.), odium 'hatred, boredom' (P1.+), odiosus
'offensive, boring' (PL+), odiossicus 'disagreeable' (PL).
Pit. *od- 'to hate', *odio- 'hatred'.
PIE pf. *h^e-h (o)d- 'to hate', *h e/od-io- 'hatred'. IE cognates: Gr. aor.
3 3
65lJo(o)aoT5ai 'to be angry, grumble', pr. OTJSTJETCU 'contends' (Hsch.) (pr. *O5TJOPAI
from a noun *o8-u- 'angriness'?), Arm. ateam 'to hate' < *h (o)d-, OIc. etja 'hetzen,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: odor 'smell, odour' [m. r] (PL+; nom.sg. odds PL)
Derivatives: odorari 'to smell, scent' (PL+), odorus 'having a smell' (Varro+),
odorisequus 'following the scent' (Andr. [uncertain]); olere [pf. oluT\ 'to smell'
(PL+), olere [pf. oluT] 'to smell' (PL, Afran., Pompon.), olor 'smell' (Varro: littera
commutate dicitur odor olor), ol(e)facere 'to smell, hear about' (P1.+), odefacere
'olefacere' (Paul, ex F.), ol(e)factare 'to smell at, sniff (P1.+), ol(e)factus, -us 'th
smelling' (P1-+); inolens 'odourless' (Lucr.), obolere 'to smell, stink' (P1.+), perolere
'to emit a penetrating smell' (Lucil.+), praeolere 'to send out an odour ahead' (P1.+),
subolere 'to smell faintly' (P1.+).
Pit. *ode/o- 'to smell', *odds 'odour'.
PIE pr. *h ed-/*h d- 'to smell'. IE cognates: Gr. o^ra (Ion. Att.) 'to smell', plqpf.
3 3
65cb5et, Gr. 65jit], oapf) 'odour, scent', Arm. hot 'smell' < *h ed-, Lith. uosti, 3s.
3
uodzia, Latv. uost 'to smell, sniff, OCz.jadati 'to investigate, explore'.
Some forms show the change of intervocalic *d > /, the exact conditions of which are
unclear. In this word, it seems that d was preserved in front of o, but changed to I in
front of e. In view offulgere /fulgere, fervere /fervere, it seems likely that olere is
older than olere (cf. Leumann).
Bibl.: WH II: 203, 206, EM 459F, IEW 772f., Leumann 1977: 544, Schrijver 1991:
50, Meiser 1998: 100, LIV l.*h ed-.
3
-oles, -is 'feeding, nursling' [f. /]: indoles, -is f. (P1.+) 'nature, character'; proles
(Lucr.+) 'offspring', suboles (P1.+) 'young growth of a tree; offspring; generation'
Derivatives: prdletarius 'belonging to the lowest class of citizens' (Pl.-h, Lex XII in
Gell.).
This noun only occurs as the second member of compounds. It reflects *al-i- or *al-e-
'upbringing; offspring, descendant' to alere 'to feed'. The vowel was weakened in
non-initial syllable to o, but not further to u. Leumann 1977: 553 assumes that these
nouns were built to a stative verb *alere 'to grow up' (a...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: olor 'swan' [m. r] (Verg.+)
Pit. *elor.
IE cognates: Olr. elu, Mir. elae [f.] < PCI. *eljal, W. alarch, pi. eleirch 'swan' <
*alarko- « *elar-sko- < *el-r-sko-; Ru. lebed' [m.], Cz. labut', SCr. labud 'swan' <
*h elbh-ond-i- (?); OIc. elptr, glpt [f.] OHG albiz, elbiz, OE celbitu, ielfetu [f.] 'swan'
2 s
< *albit-.
The preform was probably *elor (with regular e > o before velarised I), but *oldr
cannot be completely ruled out, since the evidence for the development of *olV- is
scarce: the loanword Ulixes has u-, the verb volvd < *uoluo has o, but in a special
phonetic surrounding. The nom.sg. may have regularly been *eldr < *efy, after which
an -or-stem was created. Together with Olr. elu and W. alarch, olor goes back to an
Italo-Celtic word for 'swan'. The closest connection in form and meaning is with Ru.
lebed', OHG albiz 'swan', which seem to go back to a root *h elbh- (as in albus
2
'white'), but with difficulties, since the Slav, accentuation does not match. Maybe
*-bh- in Slav, and Gm. is a suffix (as more often in animals and colours), and the root
was *h el-, or, if non-IE, * VI-. If it were *h el-, it would be impossible to connect
2 z
Italo-Celtic *el- 'swan'. If a common word *Vl- for 'swan' is accepted, it must be a
foreign loanword into the IE languages. The appurtenance of Gr. eXsa [f.J kind of
singing-bird, perhaps reed-warbler* is very uncertain.
Bibl.: WH II: 207, EM 461, IEW 302-304, Schrijver 1991: 37, 1995: 76, EIEC 538.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: omen, -inis 'omen, augury' [n. n] (P1.+; older osmen according to Varro)
Derivatives: ominari 'to know or tell from omens, predict' (P1.+), ominator 'one
who makes prognostications' (PI.).
Pit. *okws-hm- 'sighting, omen'?
PIE *h eFVs- 'to see' [pr.].
3
It is uncertain whether Varro's osmen ever really existed, or was invented by folk
etymology. Benveniste 1962: lOf. connects Hit. ha-z' /h- 'to believe, trust' < *h eH.
2/3
Oettinger 1979: 361 reconstructs *h e/oh -s-mn 'trust'. Yet semantically I am not
2 3
convinced by this etymology. Others have reconstructed *h ekw-s-mn 'a sighting', the
3
meaning of which seems closer to 'omen'. Note Gr. oppa, -atoq 'eye' < *6rcjj,a <
*h ekw-mn; Latin would have added an *s, as it often does in front of the suffix *-mn.
3
The only drawback is that the suffix -men nearly exclusively occurs after Latin verb
stems or verb stems of an earlier period. For *h ekw- 'to see', we have no evidence in
3
Latin that a verb existed; for PIE, LIV reconstructs a reduplicated perfect and an
^-present (whence Av. aiftii-dxsaiia- 'to watch', Gr. fut. oyopai 'to see'). Thus, omen
could be formed from a PIE s-present. Meier-Brugger 1992 derives omen from
*hi/20g-smen 'speech, what was predicted' to aid < *h eg-. This is semantically
1/2
attractive, but no other evidence for an o-grade of this verb exists in Latin, and one
can hardly assume a swen-derivative to have been built on the PIE perfect *He-Hog-.
Bibl.: WH II: 208, EM 461, Meier-Brugge 1992, LIV ?*h eh -. -»• oculus
2 3

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: omnis 'the whole of, all, every' [adj. z] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: omriino [adv.] 'in every respect, entirely' (PI.+), omnipotens 'almighty'
(P1-+).
Pit. *op-ni-l
PIE *hi e/op-ni- 'working'.
/3
Omnino is a petrified case-form of *omn-ino- 'wholly'. The absence of the change
*omn- > *umn- (a likely but not completely certain change) can be explained if mn
arose from a different sequence, such as *oPn-. WH propose *op-ni- with the stem
op- 'wealth', the stem of which is regarded as verbal PIE *h ep- 'to make' by LIV. In
3
fact, a verbal adj. in *-«/- can be reconstructed for other Latin words too (cf. lenis,
segnis). The verbal root might be *h]Op- 'to take' (opto) or *h ep- 'to work' >
3
'possess'. The former seems slightly more likely for semantic reasons.
Bibl.: WH II: 209f., EM 461, IEW 780, Leumann 1977: 347, Sihler 1995: 43, LIV
l.*h ep-. —> ops, opus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: onus, -eris 'burden, load' [n. r) (P1.+)
Derivatives: onerare 'to load with goods' (P1.+), onerarius 'for carrying loads'
(Naev.+); exonerare 'to discharge, unload' (P1.+); onustus 'loaded, laden' (Naev.+).
Pit *ones- [n.].
PIE *h en-os, -es- [n.] 'load'. IE cognates: Hit. aniie/a-n 'to work; to carry out, to
3
produce, to treat', Pal. aniie/a- 'to work', CLuw. anni- 'to carry out' < *h n-ie/o-; Skt.
3
anas- 'heavy cart', anadvah- [m.] 'ox, draught animal' < *anas-vah- 'pulling a cart'.
The adj. onustus is a to-derivative of the s-stem: *onos-to~. If the Hittite verb belongs
here (semantically this is not compelling), the root must rather be *h]enH-, according
to Melchert 1994: 85. This is adopted by Janda 1999 and Stiiber 2002: 88, who
reconstruct *h]enli3-. The reconstruction with final -h is based on a comparison with
3
Gr. Evooixflow, which I find unconvincing. Kloekhorst (2008: 179ff.) argues that
Melchert's scenario is problematic within Anatolian, and instead proposes to
regularly derive an(n)iia- from PIE *h n-ie/o-.
3
Bibl.: WH II: 210, EM 462, IEW 32If., Lubotsky 1990: 132, Schrijver 1991: 50.
op&cus 'sheltered, shady' [adj. o/a] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: opacare 'to make shady; darken' (Pac.+).
Opticus has since long been compared with Skt. apaka- 'turned away from' and OCS
opaky 'the other way round, behind one's back', Ru. opak(o) (dial.) 'back', but these
must be reconstructed as PIE *h epo-h kw- 'turned away from', which would yield
2 3
Latin *apocus. Klingenschmitt 2004: 249 proposes a metathesis from *apokos to
*opakos, but this seems unlikely; see the discussion of undecim s.v. decern. It seems
more promising to compare the root *(hi)ueh - 'to abandon, leave empty' found in
2
vdnus, vastus, vacuus, maybe also in vagina 'sheath': *op~wako- 'situated towards the
empty side' > *opako-. Obviously, this is just a theorical possibility.
Bibl.: WH 210, EM 703, IEW 53-55, Leumann 1977: 340, Schrijver 1991: 146, 308,
Klingenschmitt 2...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: opimus 'which is taken as trophy, glorious; rich, sumptuous' [adj. o/a] (Lex Reg.,
P1.+)
Derivatives: opimitas 'prosperity' (PI.).
Pit. *opi [ins.sg.]?
The older etymological connection with pinguis, Skt. pfvan- 'fat' must be dismissed.
Form and meaning suggest that opimus was derived from Lat. ops 'power, resources'
which almost certainly continues a Pit. /-stem *op-i-. We must assume suffixation of
adjectival *-mo- together with vowel lengthening of *i to *F; this recalls the process
leading to adj. in *-iV:noi~, -/V:to/~, which Nussbaum explains as derivatives from
ins.sg. forms in *-h!. Since Lat. has no productive adjectival suffix other than
~(t)(i)mus in the superlatives, it might be just this process by which the language
created *opi 'with power, with riches' » *opT-mo- 'abundant'.
Bibl.: WH II: 211f., EM 462, IEW 793f., Leumann 1977: 319. — ops
opinor, -5rT 'to think, believe, suppose' [v. I] (Naev.+; also opinare in PI. to Caecil.)
Derivatives: opinatus, -us 'supposition' (Lucr.+), adopTndri 'to conjecture' (Lucr.),
opinio 'opinion, imagination' (P1.+).
Steinbauer 1989 proposes a derivative *op-eina- 'to tiy to achieve' to a noun *eino-
'purpose', but this noun is as yet illusive. Schrijver stresses that the semantic link
between 'to believe' and 'to choose' is weak, and so is therefore the traditional
etymological connection with the root of optare. Vine 1999a argues that it is difficult
to reconstruct a stem *opidn-/*opion- from which opin-d- could have been derived, as
others had proposed. He argues that the Praenestine spelling opeinor in a
third-century inscription proves that the word contains Pit. *-ei- and not even *-f-,
which reduces the chances of finding a PIE etymology.
Bibi.: WH II: 212f., EM 462, IEW 781, Steinbauer 1989: 188f., Schrijver 1991: 52,
Vine 1999a, LIV ?2.*h ep-.
3

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: oportet 'it is proper, right' [v. II; pf. oportuit] (Naev.+)
Pit. *op-wort-(e~).
PIE *ue-u(o)rt- [pf.] 'to turn'.
The original meaning is thought to be 'it befits, it comes to one', and the form is
derived from *op 'towards' plus a form of the root *uert- 'to turn'. WH assume an
original causative *uort-eieyet a transitive form does not fit the meaning. Therefore,
*op-uort-e- (> *oport-e~, with a sound change dated to Pit. by Meiser 1986: 185) may
contain the perf. stem *ue-u(o)rt-, which is assumed to be attested in CIL aduortit
'advertit'. Thus, 'it has turned toward someone' > 'it befits, it is proper'. The
e-conjugation would be a secondary addition in connection with the stative meaning
of *oport~.
Bibl.: WH II: 214, EM 463, IEW 323-325, Leumann 1977: 553, Schrijver 1991:
472, LIV *uert-. verto

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: oppidum '(fortified) town; barriers' [n. o] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: oppido 'utterly, entirely' (P1.+).
Pit. *op-pe/od-o- 'obstructing'. It. cognates: U. perum, peru, perso [acc.sg.],
pefume, persome [acc.sg, + -en] 'ground' or 'place' < *pedom.
PIE *ped-o- [n.] 'stepped' > 'place, step'. IE cognates: Hit. peda- [n.] 'place',
HLuw. LOCUS-to- [n.] 'place', Skt. pada- [n.] 'footstep, piece of ground', OAv.
pada-, YA\.paSa- [n.] 'footstep', OPpati-padam kar- 'to retreat', Gr. neSov 'ground,
earth', Arm. het 'footprint', OPr. pedan 'ploughshare', Lith. peda, (dial.) pSdas
'footprint', Latv. pqda 'foot-sole, footstep' < *ped~o-; OIc.fet 'step'.
U. *pedom 'ground' can be interpreted as *pedo- [n.], nominalization of *ped-o-
'stepped, trodden', or *ped-o- [n.], with accent retraction of the adj. *ped-o-\ thus
Nussbaum, who assumes that the derivational basis was the verb *ped- 'to step' rather
than the noun *ped- 'foot'. Latin oppido might be explained as *'to the ground' >
'utterly' (Nussbaum). For the noun oppidum, the meanings 'barriers' (in a circus) and
'fort' are both attested in Naev, (the former one in a quotation from Varro). In
connection with 'foot', a meaning 'barriers' is easier to understand than
'fortification', so chances are that WH are right in assuming original *op-pedo- 'what
is in front of the feet, obstructing the way'.
Bibl.: WH II: 214f„ EM 463, IEW 790-792, Untermann 2000: 524f., LIV *ped-.
ob,pes

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ops, opis 'power, ability, resources' [f. i] (Andr.+; abl.sg. opidCIL 1.364, opi Varro)
Derivatives: Opis/Ops, gen. Opis 'a Roman goddess' (PI.+), cop(i)s, copis 'rich,
wealthy' (P1.+), copia 'abundance' (PI.+); inops 'poor, powerless' (P1.+), inopiosus
'destitute' (P1-+); opulentus 'wealthy, rich' (PL+), opulentitas 'richness' (PI.+),
opulenter 'richly' (P1.+); officium 'service, duty' (P1.+), officidsus 'dutiful, attentive'
(Afran.+); opihdareA 'to help, bring relief (Andr.+), opitulus 'cult-title of Jupiter'
(Paul, ex F.) opiparus 'sumptuous, rich' (P1.+), opifex 'craftsman, inventor' (P1.+),
t
opificium 'the performance of constructive work' (Varro+), opificina 'performance'
(PI.). ' <
Pit. *opi~. , ' ,
PIE *h e/op-(i-) 'ability, force'. IE cognates: Hit. happina- 'rich', happinant- 'rich
3
(person)' < *h ep-en-o-; Skt. dpnas- 'possession, property' [n.], YAv. afnay'hant-
3
'rich in property' < *h 3 ep-nos-, Gr. OPJTVT] [f.] 'food, corn' < *h 3 ep-n-h 2 -?
Lat. officium < *opi-fak-io~. The existence of different derivatives in the IE branches,
and also of an s-stem (see s.v. opus), may point to a PIE root noun *op~. Livingston
(2004: 73-82) explains opulentus and the other adj. in -ulentus as dissimilated from
*~owent-o~, on the model of the semantic proximity of adj. in -uus to those in -ulus,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: opto, -are 'to desire, pray for' [v. I] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: optio 'choice, option; junior officer' (P1.+), optidnatus, -us 'the rank of
optw (Cato+), optabilis 'desirable' (Andr.+); adoptare 'to associate, adopt' (P1.+),
adoptatTcius 'adopted' (PI.+), exoptare 'to long for' (P1.+), exoptabilis 'desirable'
(P1.+),praeoptare 'to prefer' (P1.+).
Pit. *opeje- 'to choose, grab'. It. cognates: U. upetu [3s.ipv.II], upetuta [3p.ipv.II],
opeter [ppp., gen.sg.n.] 'to select'; O. ufteis [gen.sg.], uhftis [nom.pl.] '?' < *op-ti-
'help/wish'?
PIE *h!op-eie- 'to choose, grab'. IE cognates: Hit. epp-7' 1 app- 'to take, grab' (< PIE
root pr. *hiep-/*h,p-), Hit. pai-' / pi- 'to give', CLuw., HLuw. piia-, Lyc. pije- 'to
give', Skt. apa, YAv. apa 'has reached' (< IE pf. *h!e-h,(o)p-); maybe Alb. (j)ep
'gives', Is. (j)ap.
Vine 1999a: 520f. explains U. upetu from a PIE iter. *op-eie~; to the resulting pr.
*ope-, Latin could have made a ppp. *opto~, from which optare was derived as a
frequentative. The connection with opinor 'to suspect' is difficult for semantic
reasons; EM regard it with scepsis. A connection with ap- as in apid, apTscor 'to take'
< *hip- would be possible if the latter indeed started from the meaning 'to reach, get'.
U. upetu might then point to a caus. or iter. *op-eie~ > *ope- 'make a choice'. A root
*hip- is more attractive than *h p-, since there is no connotation of 'work' in opto and
3
upetu. LIV and Meiser 2003 assume that a verb form opet lopetf is contained in the
Duenos inscr. sequence noisiopetoitesiai, which is merely a guess. The original form
of the hapax praedotiont in Festus, glosses with 'praeoptant', is too uncertain to be
taken into consideration; Vine 1999a: 522f. suggests that the original form may have
been *praeponont 'they prefer'.
Bibl.: WH II: 217, EM 464, IEW 781, Schrijver 1991: 52, Untermann 2000: 210,
786, Meiser 2003: 65, LIV ?2.*h ep-. —» apiscor, opinor
3

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: opus, -eris 'work' [n. r] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: opera [f.] 'effort, activity' (Naev.+), opella 'little effort' (Lucr.+),
operarius 'labourer' (P1.+), operdria 'woman who hires out her services' (PL),
operatus 'busy, occupied' (Lucil.+), operari 'to be at work' (Plin.+).
Pit. *opes- [n.] 'work'. It. cognates: SPic. opesauom [inf.], U. osatu [3s.ipv.IX],
Pael. upsaseter [3s/p.ipf.sb.ps.], O. upsannum, up]sannu [gdve.acc.sg.m.n.],
upsannam, upsan(am) [gdve.acc.sg.f.], oncavos [gdve.acc.pl.n.]; O. upsed, upsed,
SPic. opsut, o]psuq [3s.pf.], O. uupsens, upsens, ODTICEVC;, Vest, osens [3p.pf.], U.
opset(a est) , oseto (est) [3s.pf.ps.f.], O. upsatuh sent [3p.pf.ps.n.?] 'to erect': pr.,
ppp. *op-es-a~, denom. to *opes- 'work', pf. O.U. *ops~, SPic. *ops~.
PIE *h3ep-os, -es- [n.] 'work'. IE cognates: Skt apas- [n,] 'work, action', apas-
[adj.] 'active, skilful, working', Av. huuapah- 'doing good work, masterly', OAv.
hauuaparjha- [n.] 'creativity', MoP xiib 'good'; OE efnen 'to perform' (< PGm.
*abrtjan), OHG uoben 'to start to work, practice, worship'.
Postgate 1899 has shown that operatus must be a derived adj. 'full of opera, absorbed
in' to opera, whereas operari was back-formed to operatus more recently. This means
that PSab. *opesa- 'to erect' was an independent creation of Sabellic.
BibL: WH II: 217f„ EM 465f., IEW 780, Schrijver 1991: 50, Untermann 2000:
801-803, LIV l.*h ep-. omnis, ops
3

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: orbis 'disc, flat round object, circle' [m. i] (Cato+; abl.sg. orbT)
Derivatives: orbiculus 'small disc' (Cato+), orbiculatus name of a pear (Varro);
orbita 'track made by a wheel; path of a heavenly body' (Var.+), orbitus 'circular'
(Varro); orbile 'rim' (Varro).
Pit. *orfi- 'wheel', *orfi/eta- 'wheel-like, disc'. It. cognates: U. urfeta [acc.sg.] '?'
(an object which the priest holds in his hands).
PIE *h orbh-i- 'turning thing'. IE cognates: Hit. harp-tta(ri> / harp-zi 'to change
3
allegiance, join' < *h erbh-to, iG[S)harpa/i- [c.] 'pile, heap'; Gr. apcpuxvov 'disk (in
}
Lydian) (Weiss 2006: 261); ToAB yerpe 'disc / surface / image' (vel sim. = Skt.
bimba) < *h erbho-.
3
The etymology of orbis is much disputed, see Untermann 2000. Klingenschmitt 1980:
214ff. reconstructs *h (o)r-dhhj-i-, which he interprets as 'where the spokes (of a
2
wheel) are attached* = 'felly' to Skt. ara- 'spoke of a wheel'. While formally
possible, this explanation lacks formal matches in other IE languages, and is therefore
less attractive. Driessen 2001 posits *h drbH-(i-) for Latin, with shortening by
}
OsthofFs law. But it may also be *h orbk-i-, if the Tocharian long vowel is secondary.
}
Driessen rejects the connection with urbs. This solution is developed by Weiss 2006.
He assumes that orbis derives from PIE *h erbh- 'to turn' (*h erbh- cannot be
3 2
completely excluded), a meaning he arrives at by comparing the Hit. verb harp- 'to
change allegiance, join'. Latin orbita and U. urfeta can go back to the same Pit.
preform *orji/eta~, with vowel restoration in the second syllable in Umbrian. Weiss
2006: 252f., 261 analyzes this as a fem. of an original f-stem *orfi-t- 'wheel-like
(one)', which in its turn was derived from an i-stem noun *orbh-i- 'turning thing,
wheel' from which orbis can be. directly derived.
Bibl.: WH II: 219, EM 466,:qntermann 2000: 805, Klingenschmitt 1980: 214ff.,
Driessen 2001: 53f., 57f., Weiss 2006. -» urbs •
if

PIE *h orb -o- 'turning, what/who is turned over' > 'inheritance / heir'. IE cognates:

Hit. harp-"*"* , harp-1'' 'to change allegiance, join' < *h erb -to [med.], harpa/i- 'pile,

mound', Gr. 6p<pavo<; 'orphaned', optpopoxai 'orphan's guardian' (Hsch.), (Sptpcooev
'made into an oiphan' (Hsch.), Arm. orb, -oy 'orphan', OCS rabt, ORu. rob-b, Cz.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rob 'servant, slave' [m.] < *orbo-, OIc. arfr 'heir' < *arbaz, OIc. arfi, OS we. arve
'heir' < *arban~, Go. arbi, OHG arbi, OS erbi [n.] 'inheritance' < *arbija- Go.
t
arbija, OHG arpeo, OE irfa 'heir' < *arbijan- 'having the inheritance'.
According to Weiss, the root *h rbh- 'to turn' here had the specific meaning 'to
3
change membership from one social class to another'. Armenian and Greek can have
*h rbh-, *h orbh- or *Horbh-, the other languages *h orbh- or *Horbh-.
3 3 3
Bibl.: WH II: 219f., EM 466, IEW 781f., Schrijver 1991: 52, Weiss 2006.
Srdior, -Irl 'to lay the warp (of a web); begin to speak or write; begin' [v. Ill; pf.
orsus sum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: exordior 'to lay the warp; begin' (P1.+), exordium 'the warp set up on a
loom before the web is started; the beginning' (Enn.+), redordior 'to unweave'
(Plin.), pnmordium 'beginnings, source' (Pac.+) {ordia prima Lucr.), orsa, -drum
'undertakings; words' (Verg.+); orsus, -us 'web; beginning' (Cic.+).
Pit. *ard-i- 'row, arrangement'. ,
PIE *h or-d-?
2
Long initial o- must be secondary (see s.v. ordo). The original meaning seems 'to be
put in a certain order' (medium tantum), especially as a weaving term, of the threads
which are laid in a pattern. The verb can be interpreted as a denominative to a stem
*ordi-, or as a derived present *ord-ie~. Lat. exordium must be derived from exordiri,
cf. Leumann 1977: 294, unless there was an original stem *ord~, from which
exordium and ordo were independently derived. The forms orsa and orsus are recent
derivatives from the ppp. For further etymology, see ordo.
Bibl.: WH I: 221, EM 467, IEW 55-61, Leumann 1977: 294. ordo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ordfi 'row, line, rank; series, pattern, routine' [m. n] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: ordinare 'to place in rows, arrange' (Cic.+), ordinarius 'regular,
normal' (Cato apud Fest., Liv.+); ornare 'to prepare, equip, adorn' (PI.+), ornatus
'well-equipped' (Ter.+), ornatus, -us 'equipment, adornment' (P1.+), orndmentum
'equipment, ornament' (Andr.+); adornare 'to prepare (tr./intr.); adorn' (P1.+),
exdrnare 'to equip, prepare; adorn' (P1-+), suborndre 'to supply, equip' (Cic.+).
Pit. *ord-n- 'row, order'.
PIE *h or-d-?
2
The lenght of the initial o- is probably secondary, as in other words with *VrC-; cf.
Leumann 1977: 114. The original denom. to ordo was ornare < *ord-n-are <
*orde/on-a- (cf. type nominare)-, this has retained the specialized meaning of 'to
adom'. The more recently attested ordinare was made as a denominative to ordo
when the link between the latter and ornare had become opaque; it accordingly has
the more general meaning 'to put into order'. Semantically, a connection with PIE
*h er- as in Lat. artus 'joint, limb' is attractive; thus also IEW. This would imply a
2
reconstruction *h or-d- with a J-extension which is not otherwise attested for this
2 s
root. The o-grade might then stem from the original perfect. Pokorny considers a
caus. *ord-eio~, but this should yield *ordere.
Bibl.: WH II: 222-223, EM 467-469, IEW 55-61, Leumann 1977: 114, Sihler 1995:
76 —»ordior
orior, -TrT 'to appear above the horizon, rise' [v. IV; pr. oritur, ppp. ortum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: origo, -inis [f.] 'beginning, source' (Cato+), aborigines, -urn [pi.] 'race
of pre-Roman people' (Cato+); aboriri 'to pass away, be aborted' (Varro+), abortio
'miscarriage' (P1.+), abortus, -iis 'failure to fertilize, miscarriage' (Lucr.+), abortare
'to cast its young' (Varro), aboriscere 'to fade away' (Lucr.+), adoriri 'to attack,
assail' (Naev.+), cooriri 'to spring forth, be born' (P1.+), exoriri 'to appear, arise'
(P1.+), exortus, -us 'rising, emergence' (Pac.+), obo...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ornus 'kind of ash-tree* [f. o] (Verg.+)
Pit. *osVno.-. , ' ;
PIE *Hh -o/es-. IE cognates: Olr. (h)uinnius 'ash' < *onnistu-, MW coll. onrt, sing.
3
omen, OCo. onnen, Bret. coll. onn, ounn 'ash-tree' < *osnd/-os; OPr. woasis (<
*osi-s), Lith. iiosis [m.], Latv. uosis [m.], Ru. jasenCz.jasan, SCr. jasen 'ash-tree'
< BS1. *o?s-en- (> Slav.), *o?s-i-o- (> Bait.) < PIE *Heh -s-; Arm. hac'i 'ash-tree',
3
Alb. ah 'beech' [m.], OIc. askr, OHG asc 'ash-tree' < *Hh -o/es-ko-, Gr. o£ua
3
'beech'« *osk-.
The vowel difference between BSI. and the other languages is understandable if BSt.
has the full grade of a root *Hh -, followed by *-s~, whereas the other languages have
3
the zero grade followed by *-e/os~. This would imply that the original noun was an
s-stem. Latin has suffixed *-Vno- (as in other tree names, ci.fraxintts) whereas Celtic
added *~no-.
Bibl.: WH II: 223, WH 469, IEW 782, Schrijver 1991: 77.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: flro, -are 'to pray to, beseech' [v. I] (Lex XII, Andr.+)
Derivatives: oraculum 'divine utterance, oracle' (P1-+), oratid 'speech, utterance'
(Naev.+), orator 'envoy, public speaker' (Naev.+), ordtrix 'female suppliant' (P1.+),
oratus, -us 'request' (P1.+); addrdre 'to plead with, address, pray to' (Lex XII+),
exdrdre 'to persuade' (P1.+), exordbula, -drum 'means of winning over' (P1.+),
exorator 'succesful suppliant' (Ter.), perorare 'to plead, argue' (Lex XII+), inbratus
'not pleaded' (Enn.+), inexorabilis 'relentless' (Ter.+).
Pit. *as- 'mouth'. It. cognates: maybe O. urust [3s.fut.pf.] 'to prosecute (orally)' <
*or-us-t.
The chronology of the attestations shows that 'to plead, speak openly' is the original
meaning of orare; see also Gavoille 2001 on the difference between orare and dicere
in OLat. A denominative verb to os is deemed unlikely by WH and EM, but I fail to
see why: English has a verb 'to mouth', and -are is the productive suffix also after
root nouns; thus also Untermann 2000: 809. The alternative etymology, which is
supported by LIV, seems very unlikely to me: a connection with Skt. a-aryanti 'they
acknowledge' and Ru. orat' 'to shout', since nothing suggests a meaning 'to shout'
for the Latin verb, nor does it seem onomatopoeic. If drare is from *osa-, it would
have to be separated from O. urust. Rix (1993: 331-335) tries to derive both from a
root *h er- 'to speak solemnly', O. from a reduplicated perfect and Latin from a root
2
noun *h or-; this solution is adopted by LIV. It is possible within Italic, but the
2
outer-Italic evidence for a root *h er- 'to speak solemnly' is very weak.
2
Bibl.: WH II: 224, EM 469, IEW 781, Untermann 2000: 809, LIV ?3 *h er-. os
2
os, Oris 'mouth' [n. r] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: oreae [f.pl.] 'mouthpiece, bit' (Naev.+), osculum 'kiss' (P1.+), dsculari
lto kiss' (P1.+), osculentia 'kissing' (PL), deosculdri 'to kiss warmly' (P1.+), Ostia
'port at the mouth of the Tiber' (Enn.+), ostium 'doo...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: os, ossis 'bone' [n. AS] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: ossum 'bone' (Acc.+), ossua, -uum [n.pl.] 'bones' (Pac.+), osseus 'of
bone' (P1.+), ossiculdtim 'bone by bone' (Lucil.); ossifraga 'kind of vulture' (Lucr.+),
ex os 'boneless' (Lucr.+), exossdre 'to remove the bones from' (P1.+).
Pit. nom.acc. *ost, gen. *ostos. It. cognates: Ven. ostinobos [dat.pl.] 'bones' <
*ostino- « *ost-n~.
PIE *h o/est- [n.] 'bone'. IE cognates: MW ascurn, ascwrn, OCo. ascorn 'bone' <
3
*ast-k-, MW assen [fi], OCo. asen, Mir. asna 'rib' < *astVn- (appurtenance
uncertain, see below); Hit, hastai / hasti- [n.] 'bone(s), strength', CLuw. has- 'bone' <
+h esth -oi, -i-; Skt. asthi- [n.] 'bone' (ins.pl. asthabhis, gen.sg. asthnas, gen.pl.
3 1
asnam < *asthnam), asthanvant- 'having bones', OAv. astsntdt- [fi] 'materialness',
YAv. ast- 'bone, body with bones' (gen.sg. as to, astasca, nom.pl. asti, gen.pl. astqm,
instr.pl. azdabis-ca) , Av. astuuant- 'having a body with bones', Sogd. 'stk- 'bone' <
Ilr. *ast-H/n~; Gr. OOTEOV [n.] 'bone', Arm. oskr < *ost-u~; OAlb. ashte [n.] 'bone* <
*ost~.
According to the theories of Steinbauer and Schrijver, the nom.sg. *osta < *HostH
was replaced by *ost to avoid homonymy with the nom.acc.pl. *osta, and the new
form *ost was furnished with an extra *~s. Pit. *osts, *ostos would then have yielded
• *oss, *ostos, which was remodelled to *oss, *ossos. Yet the introduction of an ending
*-s into a nom.acc.sg. neuter is hardly credible: a n. paradigm *ost, gen. *ostos would
have been fine, and the ptc .ferens is not a good comparandum, since it stands within
a paradigm with m. and f. Thus, I wonder whether oss directly reflects the nom.sg.
*ost of the PIE root noun; the sequence -ss- would then have been introduced into the
other case-forms. The stem ossum may be a secondary o-stem *osso-, or it hides a
w-stem *ossu-, which in any case we need for Pac. ossuum. The u-stem can be due to
analogy with artua 'limbs' or maybe cornua 'horns'. Armenia...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ovis 'sheep' [f. (m.) i] (P1.+)
Derivatives: ovile [n.J 'sheepfold' (Cato+), ovillus [adj.] 'of sheep' (Cato+), oviaria
'flock of sheep' (Varro); suovetaurTlia, -ium [n.pl.] 'purificatory sacrifice, consisting
of a boar, a ram and a bull' (Cato+); aububulcus 'pastor bovum' (CGL V 346, 39).
Pit. *owi-. It. cognates: U. uvem, uve [acc.sg.], uvikum abl.sg. (+ -kum) , uvef, oui
[acc.pi.] 'sheep'.
PIE *h eu-i- 'sheep'. IE cognates: Olr. oi, Hit. or CLuw. haui-, Lyc. x^wa- 'sheep';
3
Skt, avi- [m./f.]'sheep, ram', gen.sg. avyas, Skt. avikd- [f] 'little sheep', avya- 'from
sheep', Gr. OK;, Opic; 'sheep', gen.sg. oioc,, Gr. oieog 'from sheep'; Arm. hoviw
'shepherd'; Lith. avis, Latv. avs 'sheep'; OCS ovbca, Ru. ovcd <PS1. *oui-k-ja-; Go.
awistr, OHG ewist 'sheepfold' < *oui-stH-; ToB awi [nom.pl.f.] 'ewe'.
Ovis can reflect Pit. *owis under the assumption put forward by Vine 2006a that
stressed *oV- did not undergo unrounding to *aw-. The appurtenance of aububulcus
is extremely uncertain according to Schrijver (1991: 439), but Vine (2006: 233)
adopts the view that the original gloss may have run aubulcus 'pastor ouium', in
which case au- could reflect pretonic *oui- 'sheep' under Vine's formulation of
Thumeysen-Havet's Law; see also Lindner 2002: 218f. Some scholars assume initial
*h o-, ignoring or explaining away Armenian ho-. See Pinault 1997: 191 ff. for
2
arguments against *h e- and in favour of *h e- (Toch. *a~). The only alternative to
3 2
*h e- would be a static paradigm *h oui- / *h eui~, while assuming that Arm. and
3 2 2
Anat. have the o- vocalism of the former variant, and the h- of the latter; this seems
less plausible.
Bibl.: WH II: 229, EM 471f., IEW 784, Schrijver 1991: 50, 439, 449-454, Sihler
1995: 178, Meiser 1998: 56, Untermann 2000: 818, Kloekhorst 2006b: 92f. -> opilid

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ovo, -are 'to celebrate a minor triumph, rejoice' [v, I] (P1.+)
Pit. *owa-.
The Gr. verb EM{^CL> 'to cry for joy' is probably denominal to cries such as d5ct (H.),
Etiai (-at) (Ar.), evav (E.). Yet the Latin word cannot be a borrowing, since it requires
the Pit. change of *eu > *ou.
Bibl.: WH II: 229f^EM 472, IEW 347, Schrijver 1991: 37,449.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ovum 'egg' [n. o] (Enn.+)
Pit. *dw(j)om.
PIE *h ouiom 'egg'. IE cognates: OW ui, MW wy [m.], OCo. try, Co. oy, Bret, uy, vi
2
'egg' < PCI. *auio-\ Av. aim (acc.sg.), Khot. ahaa-, MP xdyag, Khwar. y'k < PIr.
*auia(-ka)~; Gr. cpov (Ion.-Att.), (bov (Hell.), (B'iov (Sappho); Arm. jow, gen. jowoy <
*idio-« *6io-; Po. jajo; jaje (obs.), SCr.jaje 'egg' < PS1. *aje, OCS ajce, Ru.jajco,
Cz. vejce 'egg' < PS1. *ajbce; Alb. ve, voe; Crimean Go. ada, OIc. egg, OHG ei, OE
<% 'egg' < PGm *ajjaz~.
Schindler 1969 reconstructs *5-h uiom, with a preposition *o. Yet apart from Ilr. *a
2
'toward', such a preposition is unknown, and its existence in PIE is doubtful.
Moreover, the meaning 'which is near the bird' is not very convincing for an 'egg': it
is actually 'in' the bird, or, when it is breeding, 'under' it. I prefer the explanation of
ovum as a vrddhi-derivative of 'bird', hence 'which belongs to a bird'.
Morphologically, this would imply lengthening of an o-grade of 'bird', for which -
admittedly - there is no evidence: *h eu-i- 'bird' > *h ou-i-o- 'egg'. On the other
2 2
hand, it is not unlikely that an earlier ablaut *e/o/zero in 'bird' would have
disappeared, leaving only e/zero.
Bibl.: WH II: 230, EM 472, IEW 783f„ Schindler 1969, Schrijver 1991: 30, 126,
300. —»avis
palam

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: paene 'almost, practically' [adv.| (P1.+)
Derivatives: paenitere (pf. -wz)'to cause dissatisfaction, cause to regret' (P1.+),
paenitudd 'regret' (Pac.),pae/enuria 'shortage, want' (Ter.+).
The basic meaning of the stem *paen- seems to be 'missing, lacking'. IEW connects
paene with Skt. piyati 'scorns', which would fit if we posit *ph -i-; but the root is
2
reconstructed as *phi- in LIV, which does not explain Latin -ae-. Also, the
r
semantics do not match well. Neri (2007: 78f.) takes up a suggestion by Vine and
proposes *p(e)-ai-ni- 'not entirely' < ""from whom has been taken away' or *'who
takes away', from a preverb *pe 'away' and a verb *h ai 'to give, take'. A PIE
}
phoneme sequene *hiai- is in my view not possible, however, and the existence of a
PIE preverb *peus uncertain (see s.v. palor).
Bibl.: WH II: 234, EM 474, IEW 792f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: palam 'openly, publicly' [adv.; prep. + abl.] (P1.+)
Derivatives:propalam 'openly, evident' (P1.+).
Pit. *palaml
PIE *plh -em-i?
2
The use of palam as a prep, postdates that of clam. Schrijver gives three possible
etymologies: (1) an analogical ending -am was taken from clam; (2) a locative
*plh-em-i 'in the flat (hand)' to palma; this might have yielded *palem, however; or
i
a loc. *plh -em > *p(a)lam; (3) an adverbialised acc.sg. of a ^-stem of the same root:
2
*plh -eh2~m > *palam > palam. Since palma and its cognates provide evidence for a
2
PIE w-stem, hypothesis (2) is slightly more likely.
Bibl.: WH II: 237, EM 475, IEW 805-807, Schrijver 1991:209f. clam,palma, planus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: palatum 'roof of the mouth; dome, vault' [n. o] (Varro+)
Derivatives: Paldtium 'the Palatine' (Naev.+), PalatTnus 'Palatine' (Varro+),
Paldtua 'tutelary goddess of the Palatine' (Varro), Palatualis 'of Palatua' (Varro+).
Pit. *palato~.
PIE *pIh -o/u- 'flat, wide'?
2
Since the 'palate' can be referred to as a 'flattened' or 'vaulted' part, and since hills
are also often referred to as 'flat' or 'vaulted' (if their form so suggests), a derivation
of Paldtium from palatum is quite conceivable. Palatum could be an adj, in *~ato~ to
a stem *pal-(V~) 'flat, broad', e.g. *plh -o- or *plh -u-, cf. Hit. palhi- 'broad' <
2 2
*plh ~i-.
2
Bibl.: WH II: 237, EM 475f. planus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: palea 'chaff, husk' [f. d] (Cato+)
Derivatives: uncertain are palea 'the wattles of a cock' (Varro+), palear, -aris [n.]
'dewlap' (Varro+).
Pit. *paleja- [f.].
PIE *p(e)lh 'chaff. IE cognates: Skt. palava- 'chaff, husk' < *pelH-ou(-), OPr.
r
pelwo, Lith. pelus, Latv. pqlus [pi.] 'chaff, OCS plevy [nom.pI.f-], Ru, polova, SCr.
pljeva 'chaff1 < PIE *pelH-u-(eh ); Gr. 7cdW.ro 'to swing, sway' < *plhi-n-, aor. 7I&XTO
2
(av-, KaT-ejtaXio), redupl. ap-jt87iaXcbv, noXoq [m.] '(shaken) lot'.
Palea could be cognate with the stem *pelH-u- 'chaff found in Skt. and BS1. Since
'chaff are the parts of the corn which are separated by threshing them off, these
stems might result nouns derived from PIE *pelh 'to swing'.
r

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: palla 'mantle' [f. a] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: pallula 'little mantle' (P1.+), pallium 'mantle, garment' (Naev.+),
palliatus 'wearing a pallium' (P1.+), pallidum 'small pallium' (P1.+), palliolatim
'in/with a pallium' (P1.+).
No etymology. A PIE preform could for instance be *pHl-n/d/s/u-, or a secondary full
grade a of a root *pelC- as in pellis. But it may well be a loanword.
Bibl.: WH II: 238f., EM 476, IEW 803f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: palleo 'to be pale' [v. II; pi.pallu7\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: pallescere 'to grow pale, fade' (Acc.+), pallidus 'pale, dim' (P1.+),
pallor 'paleness' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *pahvo- 'pale, grey'.
IE cognates: Lith. palvas 'light yellow, straw-coloured', RuCS plavb 'white', Ru.
polovyj 'pale yellow, sandy', SCr. pldv 'pale blue, with light hair' < BS1. *poluo~,
Olc./glr, OHG falo 'faded' < PGm. *fahva~.
Nussbaum 1997 assumes a suffix *-uo- on account of the BS1. and Gm. cognates; he
explicitly separates *pallo- denoting a pale colour from the adj. *polio- (Lat. pullus)
denoting a dark one. BS1. and Gm. suggest a preform *poluo-, but this would not
explain Latin -a-. One might posit *ph el-, but that would be ad hoc. Alternatively,
2
we are dealing with a loanword *paluo- 'pale, grey' into the European languages.
Bibl.: WH II: 239f., EM 476, IEW 804f., Nussbaum 1997: 191. -»pullus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: palma 'front part of the hand; palm-tree' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: palmula 'palm; an oar' (Varro+), palmus 'width of a palm' (Cato+),
pabnaris [adj.] 'the width of a palm' (Varro+), palmarium 'master-stroke' (Ter.),
palmipedalis 'measuring a palm and a foot' (Varro+).
Pit. *palamd-.
PIE *plh -em-h - 'fiat, wide'. IE cognates: Olr. lam, W. Haw < PCI. *<plama-, Gr.
2 2
roXajiT) 'palm of the hand', OHG folma 'hand', OE folm 'flat hand'.
The analysis of the root as *plh - and the vocalization in Latin and Greek, which does
2
not fit the regular outcome of *plHC- in these languages, suggest an m-stem with full
grade of the suffix in Lat. and Gr. Others have assumed that palma was borrowed
from Greek, but this does not explain Lat. palmus, nor can Gr. -aXa- be explained
from *CIHC~. Admittedly, the fact that four branches show a h -stem but seem to
2
have different ablaut of the primary suffix (*-m- in Celtic and Germ., *-em- in Lat.
and Gr.), and especially the separation of Celtic and Italic, are embarrassing.
Bibl.: WH II: 240f„ EM 476f., IEW 805-807, Schrijver 1991: 210, Sihler 1995: 96,
111, Nussbaum 1997: 186. —*• palam

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: palor, -ari 'to wander, stray' [v. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: dispaldri 'to stray off (Sis.+), dispdlescere 'to be spread about' (PL).
Pit. *paIaje/o-.
The stem paid- 'to wander' is explained from *pand-slo- by WH, but Weiss 1993:
53-56 rightly objects that it is somewhat complicated to arrive from an instrument
noun 'spreading, spreader' at a verb 'to wander'; and, furthermore, that one would
expect to find other traces of the alleged noun *palo~. Weiss proposes the alternative
etymology *pe-h lk - 'to wander off; this would semantically be perfect, and links up
2 2
with the existence of amb-ulare. However, the existence of a PIE preverb *pe is
uncertain: the only independent witness for this form would be Hit. pe 'away, thither',
which rather reflects *(h )poi, cf. Kloekhorst 2008: 660.
}
Bibl.: WH II: 241, EM 477, LIV *h elh -.
2 2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: palpo, -are 'to stroke, soothe' [v. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: palpus 'front or palm of the hand' (P1.+), palpitare 'to beat, pulsate'
(Cic.+), palpatio 'caress' (PL), palpator 'cajoler' (PL), palpebra 'eyelid; pi.
eyelashes' (Caecil.+); suppalparT'to wheedle' (PL).
No etymology. PIE origin is not easy for a sequence palp-. To explain the second
vowel of palpebra and palpitare, Serbat 1975: 107 posits a verb *palpere 'to move
repeatedly'. If correct, we could reconstruct a noun *palpo- 'front of the hand' and a
verb *palp-e/o- 'to move' at an earlier stage.
Bibl.: WH II: 241 f., EM 477, IEW 798-801

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: palus, paludis 'fen, swamp' [f. d] (P1.+)
Derivatives: paluster [adj.] 'marshy, fenny' (Sis.+).
Pit. *paliid-.
IE cognates: Skt. palvala- [n.] 'pond, pool'; OPr.pelky 'marsh', Lith. pelke 'marsh,
Zem. puddle', Latv.pelce 'puddle' < *pelHk-iH-h .
z
If the Baltic words for 'swamp' are cognate, Latin palus could be connected with
pall- 'grey, pale'. However, Schrijver rightly objects that the semantic connection
between 'grey' and 'swamp' is uncompelling.
Bibl.: WH II: 243, EM 478, IEW 798-801, Schrijver 1991: 21 Of.
pandS, -ere 'to spread out, extend' [v. Ill; ppp. possum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: pandicularf 'to contort one's face in a grimace' (P1.+), possus
'extended, free; dry, wrinkled' (Naev.+), passim [adv.] 'here and there,
indiscriminately' (P1.+), passum 'raisin-wine' (P1.+), passus, -us 'step, pace' (P1.+),
pandus 'arched, bowed' (Enn.+), Panda 'a Roman goddess' (Varro+); dispandere 'to
open out' [PI. dispennite, ppp. dispessum] (P1.+), expandere 'to spread out'
(Caecil.+), praepandere 'to spread in front, reveal' (Laev.+), repandus 'flattened
back' (Lucil.+), repandirostrus 'having a flattened snout' (Pac.).
Pit. *pand-n-, *pat-. It. cognates: O. patanai [dat.sg.] '?', name of a deity; O.
patensins [3p.ipf.sb.] 'to open' < *pt-n-s~; U. padellar [gen.sg.] name of a deity,
'Patella'.
PIE *pt-n(e)-h - [pr.] 'to spread'. IE cognates: Gr. jritvripi 'to spread out, open', aor.
2
67t£xao(o)a.
I assume that the PIE nasal pr. yielded (pre-)PIt. *pnd-n~, with vocalization to
*pandn- according to the observations in Schrijver 1991: 486-504. The adj. pandus
seems to be a novel creation on the basis of pandere. A dim. *pandiculum 'little
bending > grimace' is behind pandiculari. Outside the present, *pt- acquired the
secondary full grade *pat~, which is attested in the stative pateo (see s.v.) and the ppp.
passus < * patio-, and which was reintroduced in PI. dispennite < *dis-pat-n-, and 0.
patensins (Kortlandt 1999: 248).

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pango, -ere 'to insert firmly, fix' [v. Ill; pf. pepigi, ppp. pactum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: (1) compingere 'to shut up, fix, build' (P1.+), depangere 'to drive down'
(Varro+), impingere 'to fix on, strike against' (P1.+), suppingere 'to attach below'
(P1.+), antepagmentum 'the facing of a door- or window-frame' (Cato+); (2)
compdges, -is [f.] 'framework, joint' (Pac.+), propages [fi] 'which continues' (Pac.+),
propagmen 'prolongation' (Enn.), propagare 'to reproduce, prolong' (Cato+),
propagdtid 'reproduction' (Cato+), propdgo, -inis 'offspring, space for planting'
(Cato+), repdgula, -drum [n.pl.] 'door-bars' (PI.+); (3) pagus 'country district or
community' (P1.+), paganicus 'of the village people' (Varro), paganalia [pi.] 'the
village festival of the pagf (Varro); (4) pagina 'column or page of writing' (Cic.+);
(5) pdla 'long-handed spade' (P1.+), palus 'wooden post, stake, peg' (P1.+), tripalis
'having three stakes' (Varro), paxillus 'wooden pin' (Varro+).
Pit. *pang~, *pagto- 'to attach'; *pdg-o- 'district', *pag-e- 'joint', *pag-e/on-
'space, column', *pag-slo- 'spade, pole'. It. cognates: O. prupukid [abl.sg.] '?' <
*pro-pak-iom 'previous agreement'?
PEE *ph -n-g- [pr.] 'to attach', *ph g-to- [ppp-], *peh g-os [n.]. IE cognates: Skt.

pajra- 'solid, firm', pajas- [n.] 'surface, face', YAv. pazarfhant- [adj.] (meaning
unknown), Khot. paysa- 'surface', Gr. mjyvupi 'to attach, to join', Gr. eu-7nyyf]<;
'well-built'.
The nominal forms -pages, pagus, pagina, -pagin- are derived from a full grade
*pag-, whereas the verb forms reflect *pag-. The nouns palus and pdla are derived
from an inst. noun *pag~slo~.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pannus 'piece of cloth, rag' [m. o] (P1.+)
IE cognates: Gr. jrryvo^ [n.], jrr)vr} [f.] 'woven cloth' (Hsch.) < *pan-, Go. fana [m.]
'cloth, towel', OHG fano 'cloth', NHG Fahne, OE fana 'banner' < *fan-on.
If the Gr. and Gm. words listed are related, they probably represent loanwords from
an unknown source. The vacillation between a and a, and Latin -a- and -tin-, cannot
be explained from a common PIE preform.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: panus 'spool with thread; abcess; main stalk of a panicle' [m. o] (Lucil+; Afran.+;
Plin.)
Derivatives: pani/ucula 'the feathery head of certain reeds and grasses, tuft; kind of
wart' (Plin.+).
In the meaning 'spool with thread', panus may well be a loanword from Doric Greek
*7cavo<;, cf Att. nrjvr], 7ifjvo<; 'yarn'. In the meaning 'abcess', panus is compared with
Ru. puk 'bunch, bundel', OCS pgciti s§ 'be inflated', Ru. pucit' 'become swollen' <
*ponHk-; a root *pnHk- is reconstructed from which Schrijver phonetically derives
panus. This is conceivable, but it is just as likely that the word for 'spool' was
metaphorically used for 'abcess', and certainly for 'tuft' of reed (panicula); cf. the
range of meanings of German Klofi.
Bibl.: WH II: 248, EM 480, IEW 789, Schrijver 1991: 491.
papiliG 'moth, butterfly' [m. n] (Ov.+)
Pit. *pV(l)pVl
IE cognates: OPr. penpalo 'quail', OPr. pepelis, [pi.] pippalins 'bird', Lith. piepala,
Latv. paipala, Ru. perepel, Cz. prepel, krepel 'quail', OIc. fifrildi, OE fifealde, OHG
fifaltra, MHG ftfalter 'butterfly' < ¥Gm.*fifaldron-.
Pa-pilio can reflect reduplication of a root *pl- 'to fly, flutter', which has also served
to build the word for 'quail' in BS1. and 'butterfly' in Gm. It seems unlikely that this
root *pl- is a very early variant of PIE roots such as *pleu- 'to swim, wander', *pleh -
3
'to swim, float', *pelh 'to swing*.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: par, paris 'equal, matching' [adj. i] (P1.+; sup. parisuma Elog.Scip.)
Derivatives: parilis 'equal, similar' (Lucil.+); compdr 'fellow, similar, equal' (P1.+),
comparare 'to align, match, evaluate' (P1.+), dispar 'unequal, different' (Acc.+),
disparare 'to be different* (P1.+), disparilis 'different' (Varro+), disparilitas
'difference' (Varro+), impar 'unequal' (Cato+); aequiperare 'to compare, become
equal' (PI.+), aequiperabilis 'comparable' (P1.+).
Pit. *parVs? *pas-i-l It. cognates: U. pars (est) [nom.sg.] '(it is) prescribed' <
* pares or *paros.
The origin of a in par is disputed: WH regard it as an ancient lengthened grade,
whereas Leumann and e.g. Untermann explain it from *parVs > *pars > *parr. In
view of U. pars, the latter seems more attractive, but it is strange that a similar
lengthening cannot be observed e.g. in ter 'thrice' < *ters. Sab. *parVs may stem
from PIE *prH-V~, but no good etymology is available. WH connect *perh - 'to sell',
2
but 'selling' does not mean 'matching'. Alternatively, Latin may be separated from U.
pars and go back to *pas- *pas- < PIE *pHs-; see pared for a possible candidate.
f

from; save up' (P1-+), impercere 'to spare' (PI.), reparcere 'to be sparing with' (P1.+);
compescere 'to confine, restrain' (P1.+), dispescere 'to divide' (Cato+).
Pit. *pe-ark-e/o- [pr.].
PIE *h er(kkeA>) 'to hold'.

Pit. *pas-e~. >
PIE *peh -s- [pr.] 'to watch, see'?. IE cognates: Arm. hqyim 'to see', Alb. aor.pashe

'I saw'. '
Within Latin, it is possible to connect par: parere would then be a stative verb *pare-
or *pase- 'to be visible', and maybe *pdr-i- 'visible, obvious' > 'seeming' > par
'equal' (cf. German gleich). If the meaning of the root *pHr/s- was 'to see', the
obvious connection is with PIE *pHs- as in Albanian aor. pashe 'I saw', of which we
would then be able to prove *h . For par, paris, an ablaut *peh s-/*ph s- would have
2 2 2
to be assumed. The meaning of this preform would be so close to *peh -s- 'to protect,
2
observe', an s-present to *peh - 'to protect', that one may consider their original
2
identity. 1

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pario, -ere 'to give birth to, bear' [v. Ill; pf. peperi, ppp. partum] (VOLat.+:
Garigliano neipari [2s.ipv.act. IpariI or iparif] 'do not appropriate', Lex XII, Andr.+)
Derivatives: (1) parens, -ntis [m./f.] 'parent, father or mother' (PI.+), parentare 'to
perform the rites at the tombs of the dead' (Varro+), parenticida [m.] 'parent-killer'
(PI.), parentatio 'celebration of the family dead' (Cato-t-); partus, -us 'the giving birth,
birth, progeny' (P1.+), partio 'the giving birth' (P1.+), partitudo 'the giving birth'
(P1.+), partitra 'the giving birth' (Var*o), parturTre 'to be in labour, give birth' (P1.+);
(2) pertculum 'trial, danger' (P1.+), pericularl 'to try out, risk' (Cato), pericliiari 'to
try out, risk; be in danger' (P1-+), perTculdsus 'dangerous' (Cato+); peritus
'experienced, expert' (P1.+), imperitus 'unexperienced' (P1.+); comperm 'to find out,
discover' (Ter.+), experiri (pf. expertus sum) 'to put to the test, attempt' (Naev.+)
7
experientia 'trial' (Varro+), experTmentum 'test, trial' (Varro+), opperlrT 'to wait,
await' (P1.+), reperire 'to recover, discover' (P1.+) [pf. repperi, ppp. repertum].
Pit. *per(e)i-, *par-(e)i~, aor.ptc. *par-(e)nt- 'to bear; find, experience'; *per-ei-tfo-
'experience'. It. cognates: Fal. pe:para[i] [ls.pf.] 'I have procured'; U. amparitu
[3s.ipv.IL], amparihmu [3s.ipv.II.ps.] 'to erect?' <an- 'on' + *pare/T/-ie~.
PIE pr. *perh -i- 'to provide', *prh -ei-, aor. *p(e)rh -, pf. *pe-porh -e. IE cognates:
3 3 3 3
see s.v. paro.
(1) Schrijver 1991 explains par- from antevocalic *prH-, which is more attractive
than the older assumption of an umlaut *pera- > *para-. The old root aor. might be
conserved in parens. The pr. parturTre is one of the two oldest presents in -urTre, and
may have been formed on the model of esurTre (Risch 1954). (2) The semantic
connection between parid 'to bear, produce' and -pertre/T 'to find, experience' is
explained by Schrijver 2003: 79 in the sense that 'to discover' ...

PIE *prh -o- 'providing'. IE cognates: Olr. ernaid*, -em* 'to grant' « PCI.
3
*<parna- (-e- from the sb.), Olr. rath [n.], MW rat 'mercy' < *<prato-\ Skt. ppiati
[3s.act.], aor. piirdhi [2s.ipv.act.], pf. -pupuryds [2s.opt.act.] 'to give, grant',
sadd-prna- [adj.] 'granting continually' < *prH-; Gr. aor. friopov 'provided, gave',
pf.med. TceJipcimn 'it has been fated'.
Mostly explained as a denominal verb to a noun *paro- < *prH-o/h - (cf. puer-pera),
2
but Rix has proposed that it continues *prHie- > *paraie~. This is accepted by LIV.
This will not work with our phonetic rules: *prHie- is expected to yield *parie- cf.
i
Schrijver 1991: 293 (but *prio, *prTre according to Schrijver 2003: 78). The verb
imperare is different in its semantics and because of its -e-\ this it has in common
with properare, Panagl-Lindner 1995: 173 interpret properare as an old compound
*pro-pardre 'to bring to the fore' > 'incite', an a-intensive to par ere. They explain
properus as a back-formation to this verb. Similarly, imperare would stem from
*in-parere 'to bring in > command'. Opiparus must be a more recent formation to
paro. j

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: parra 'kind of bird (of ill omen)' [f. a] (P1.+)
Pit. *parVsa-. It. cognates: U. parfam, parfa [acc.sg.], parfa [abl.sg.] 'certain bird'
< *paresa.
PIE *sprH-e/os-? IE cognates: Co. frau, Bret, frao 'crow' < *spraua- < *spreh u-7;
r
Gr. cTiopyiloq 'sparrow', OPr. spurglis 'sparrow' (< *sprg-), MHG sperke, Go.
sparwa, OE spearwa, OHG sparo < *spor(H)u-en~, OIc. spprr < *spor(H)uo~.
Pit. *parVsa- may reflect earlier *pHr-Vs- or *prHV-s~. This might be an j-less
variant of the forms *sprH- for (mainly) 'sparrow' in other European languages; but
since none of the other forms matches exactly, the etymology remains uncertain. Like
other bird-names, these could be loanwords from a non-IE language.
Bibl.: WH II: 257, EM 484f., IEW 991, Meiser 1986: 174, Schrijver 1991: 21 If.,
Meiser 1998: 116, Untermann 2000: 513.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pars, -tis 'part, piece' [f. /] (Lex XII, Andr.+)
Derivatives: partim [adv.] 'partly' (Andr.+), partJre/i 'to share, divide up' (Andr.+),
particula 'small part' (Varro+), particulatim 'bit by bit', particulo 'a coheir'
(Pompon.+); expers 'having no share, free' (P1.+); particeps 'participant' (Andr.+),
participdre 'to share' (PI.+), participium 'participle' (Varro+), participalis
'participial' (Varro+); dispertTre 'to separate, divide up' (P1.+), impertTre 'to give a
share, present with' (P1.+), bipertTtus 'bipartite' (Varro+), tripertitus 'tripartite'
(Varro+);portio 'degree, portion' (P1.+).
Pit. *parti- 'part'.
Schrijver 1991 explains pars as a recent (e.g. Pit.) but pre-Latin formation based on
the present stem par- 'to bring forth', just like the ppp. partus and the noun partus
[m.] (see s.v. pario). The original meaning of *par~ti- would have been 'lot, portion,
fate', whence 'part, piece'. The latter shift separated the noun from the verb pario,
which is why (in S.'s view) *parti- was not replaced by *partid like many other
*fz-stems in Latin. All agree that portio must have a secondary origin. In its oldest
attestations, it is only found in the abl.sg. pro portidne 'proportionally'. It would have
arisen either from *prd ratidne 'per part' > *prortione > with dissimilation portidne,
or from *pro par(ti)tione. Since partitio is not attested before Cicero, whereas ratio is
fully present from Plautus onwards, the former etymology seems more likely.
Bibl.: WH II: 257-259, EM 485, 524, IEW 817, Leumann 1977: 366, Schrijver
1991: 195-197. pario

'(shep)herd, shepherd's boy'; Finnish paimen 'shepherd' (loanword from Baltic) <

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: passer 'small bird' [m. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: passerculus 'little sparrow' (Pl.+VjDosserH-ms 'of sparrows' (Pompon.).
Pit *pattro-.
PIE *p(e)t-tro- 'who flies, bird'. IE cognates: see s.v.penna.
Schrijver proposes *pttro- 'bird' > *pattro- > nom.sg. *passros, gen.sg. *passrT >
* passes, *pazrT > *passer, *parf. This paradigm would have been skewed, yielding
two nouns: passer 'sparrow' and parus 'tit'. It must be remarked that the expected
meaning of a form *jot-tro- would rather be 'instrument for flying, wing' vel sim.
Also, accipiter may contain *pet-ro- 'wing', although this is uncertain. Hence, the
etymology remains uncertain.
Bibl.: WH II: 261, EM 486, Schrijver 1991: 212,495, LIV 1/2. *peth -. accipiter,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: patior, pati 'to undergo, experience' [v. Ill; ppp. passutn] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: patientia 'endurance, tolerance' (PI.+), passid 'passion' (Varro+);
perpeti 'to undergo to the full, put up with' (P1.+).
Pit. *pat-i~.
It has been suggested that patior belongs to Gr. jrqpa 'suffering' < *pehmn, which
r
would point to a basis *pht- > *pat-. Yet patior cannot be a denominative to a ppp.
r
*phto- or an i-stem *phti~, so the morphological part of the analysis remains
r r
unclear. Alternatively, one might consider a derivation from the root *pet- 'to fly,
fall', e.g. *p(e)t-i- 'to befall', intr. 'it befalls me' > 'I experience'.
Bibl.: WH II: 264, EM 488, IEW 792f., Schrijver 1991: 93, LIV *pethr- ^peto

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: paucus 'few, small in number' [adj. o/a] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: pauciens 'rarely' (Titin.+), pauculus [adj.] 'a small number' (PI.),
paullus (> paulus) Tittle, small' (Ter.+), paulum [n./adv.] 'a little bit / to a small
extent, for a short while' (P1.+), pauldtim 'little by little' (P1.+), paul(l)isper 'for a
brief while' (Pl.-H), paullulus 'little, small' (P1.+), pauttulum [n./adv.] 'a little; to a
small extent' (P1.+), pauxilhts 'little, small' (Naev.+), pauxillulus 'tiny' (Naev.+),
pauxilldtim 'by slow degrees' (P1.+), pauxillisper 'bit by bit' (PL); perpaitci [adj.pl.]
'extremely few' (Ter.+), perpauxillum 'an excessively tiny amount' (PI.);
pauciloquium 'the fact of saying little' (PI.).
Pit. *pauko- 'few', *paurelo- 'a little bit'.
PIE *peh u~. IE cognates: see s.v. parvus.
2
The coocurrence of paucus, paullus and pauxillus seems to suggest that paullus
developed from *pauk-slo- and pauxillus from *paukslelo~, cf. ala < *aksla 'wing'
next to axilla. This is accepted by WH. However, the suffix *-slo- is otherwise only
used for instrument nouns, and only after consonant stems; both are reasons to reject a
preform *pauk-slo- derived from *pau-ko~. Thurneysen (1907: 177) therefore
suggests that pauxillus would be an analogical form made to paullus, and paullus
itself a /o-derivative *pau-re-lo- of parvus < *pauro
Bibl.: WH II: 265f„ EM 489, IEW 842f., Leumann 1977: 208, Schrijver 1991: 269,
Sihler 1995: 222. —• parvus, pauper, puer

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pauper 'poor' [adj. r) (P1.+)
Derivatives: pauperies 'poverty' (Lex XII+), pauperium 'id.' (Caecil.), paupertas
'id.' (P1-+), pauperculus 'poor' (P1.+), paupertinus 'poverty-stricken' (Varro+),
pauperare 'to impoverish, despoil' (P1.+);perpauper 'very poor' (Afran.+).
Pit. *pau(o)-pa/oro~.
PIE *peh u-(o-)p(o)rh -o- 'providing little'. IE cognates: see s.v. parvus.
2 3
Originally a thematic adj., which probably switched to the third declension by
analogy with its antonym dives. The PIE form may have contained final *-prh -o- >
3
*-paro- or *porh -o- > *-poro~.
3
Bibl.: WH II: 267f., EM 490, IEW 842f., Schrijver 1991: 269. parvus, paucus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: paved 'to be frightened' [v. II; pf. pavT\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: pavitare 'to dread' (Ter.+), pavor 'sudden fear, terror' (Andr.+;
Naev.Pac. -as), pavidus 'frightened' (P1.+); expavidus 'terrified' (Laev.+),
perpavefacere 'to make very frightened' (PL).
Pit. *paw-e- 'to be frightened'.
PIE *pou-ehi-''to fear', IE cognates: Olr. omun, MW ouyn, MCo. own, Bret, oun
'fear' < PCI. *ofno- < *pouno- (McCone 1992b), Olr. uath 'fear' < *pou~to-, W. uthr
'terrible' < *pou-tro~.
WH and EM regard pavere as the stative counterpart 'to be struck' > 'to fear' of
pavire 'to hit, strike'. This is possible, but of course one may look for more
straightforward semantic cognates. Schrijver prefers a connection with Celtic nominal
forms in *pou- meaning 'fear'. With the Pit. change of *ow > *aw, this would yield
Lat. pav- - at least, in pretonic position. The pf. pavi is not attested before Ovid, and
may have been taken over from pavio.
BibL: WH II: 266, EM 489, IEW 827, Schrijver 1991:446. ->pavio, pudeo
pavio, -Ire 'to thump, pound, strike' [v. IV] (Cato+)
Derivatives: pavimentum 'pavement' (Cato+), pavicula 'rammer' (Cato+); depuvire
'to beat thoroughly' (Naev.+).
Pit. *pawje/o-. It. cognates: O. pavmentum borrowed from Latin.
PIE *ph u-ie/o- 'to hit'. IE cognates: Gr. Ttalco 'to strike, hit' < *ph u-ie/o-, Lith.
2 2
pjauti, 3s. pjduna 'to cut', Latv. p]au\ 'to mow, harvest' < *pehu- (or *pieh -u~).
t 2
Maybe ToB 3p. pyakar 'they struck down', Gr. Jiiaiw 'to hit', ToABputk- 'to divide'.
LIV follows Hackstein 1993: 161 in reconstructing a pr. *pi(e)h -u-ie/o- which would
z
have lost the first *i in Pit. due to dissimilation (LIV) or simple phonetic loss
(Hackstein). Root-initial *pi- is reconstructed on the strength of Gr. and Toch. forms.
Yet Lat. putdre also shows simple pu-. This cooccurrence recalls Lat. movere and the
(PIE?) developments of the pr. *mih u~, which can be explained from *mih]U-C- >
}
*miuh C- > *muHC~. A similar development may account for ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: p3x, pads 'peace' [f. k] (PL+)
Derivatives: pacere 'to come to an agreement (Lex XII+), pactum 'agreement,
means' (Lex XII+), pactio 'agreement, settlement' (P1.+), pacare '?' (Duenos
inscr.+?), 'to impose a settlement' (Cic.+), pacatus 'peaceful, settled' (P1.+),
paciscere 'to arrange by negotiation, betroth', pf. pactum (Naev+), compacisci 'to
make an agreement' (PL), compa/ectum 'agreement' (P1.+), depecisci 'to come to
terms, agree' (Ter.+),pacificari 'to be reconciled' (PL);pacio 'pactio' (Fest.).
Pit. *pak- 'agreement, peace', *pak-e/o- 'to make an agreement', *pakto- 'agreed',
*pakri- 'id.'. It. cognates: U. pac a [postpos. + gen.] 'because of; U. pase [abl.sg.]
'peace' f. < *pak~; U. pacer [nom.sg.], Marr. pacrsi [nom.sg. + -si] , Mars, pacre
[nom.sg.n.?], Pael. pacrid [abl.sg.], U. pacrer, Pael. pacris, Marr. pacris [nom.pl.m.]
'merciful, auspicious' < *pakri~.
PIE *peh k- 'agreement', *ph k-(e/o-) 'to make an agreement'. IE cognates: Skt.
2 2
pasa- [m.] 'snare, noose', Khot. pasa- 'fastening, cord', Go. jahan, OS fangan 'to
catch' < *pankOHG fuogen, OS Jogian 'to join' < *pak~.
The verb pacare, maybe already attested in the Duenos inscr. (but this is uncertain),
must be derived from the noun pax. The pr. pacere is explained as thematization of an
earlier root aorist by Meiser 2003. We must exclude Hit. pask- /pask- 'to stick in,
plant, set up' < *PosK- (Kloekhorst 2008: 651) from the IE cognates.
Bibl.: WH II: 23If., EM 473, IEW 787f., Schrijver 1991: 97, Untermann 2000:
508-510, Meiser 2003: 184, LIV ^pe^-- pangd
-pe [ptc. encl.]
Derivatives: nempe 'of course, to be sure' (PL+), quippe 'for, indeed' (PL+),
quispiam 'some, someone' (Naev.+), uspiam 'somewhere, anywhere' (P1.+).
Pit. *-pe.
PIE *pe. IE cognates: CLuw. HLuw.pa-i-ppa [encl. advers.], Lith. kaTp 'how?'.
Lat. -pe can be connected with Lith. -p, and will reflect a discourse particle; -piam
'any at all' < *-pe iam. Maybe PIE *kwid-pe is reflected in Lat. quippe and HLuv...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pecto, -ere 'to comb (hair)' [v. Ill; pf.pexi, ppp.pexum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: pecten, -inis [m.] 'comb (for the hair)' (P1.+), pectunculus 'small
scallop' (Varro+); depectere 'to comb out' (Ter.+), oppectere 'to apply a comb to'
(PI.); impexus 'uncombed' (Pac.4r). ;
Pit. *petke/o- 'to comb', *petken- 'comb'. It. cognatesi U. petenata '?'.
PIE *p(e)t£- < *pe-pk- [v.], *p(e)tk-en- [m.]. IE cognates: Gr. TOKO 'to comb, shear',
Lith. pesti 'to pluck', Gr. TZEktco 'to comb, shear', OHG fehtan 'to fight'; Gr. kte1<;,
KTEVOC; 'comb < *TOCTSV-; Skt.pdksman- [n.], Av. pasna- 'eyelash(es)' < *pek-s-.
Lat. has a pr. *pekte/o- and a noun pecten, with unknown quantity of the final vowel
in the nom.sg. (Sommer 1914: 366) because it is only attested twice in verse, both
times before a consonant or consonant cluster. Traditionally, the pr. is reconstructed
with a suffix *-t-e/o-, but this is very rare in IE. The alternative reconstruction by
Pinault 2006b is therefore quite attractive. He proposes a reduplicated verb form
*pe-pk- to have1 dissimilated to *petk~, whence was derived the n-stem *petk-n- /
*ptk-en- 'comb'.-A reduplicated present fits the repetitive meaning of 'to comb,
pluck' very well.
Bibl.: WH II: 269f., EM 491, IEW 797, Sihler 1995: 296-298, 535, Untermann
2000: 549, Meiser 2003: 114, Pinault 2006b: 136-140, LIV l.*pek-.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pectus, -oris 'breast, chest' [n. r] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: pectordlis 'of the breast' (Varro+).
Pit. *pektos~.
IE cognates: Olr. ucht 'breast, chest' [n., m. u] < *puktu-.
Olr. ucht < *puktu- provides a likely cognate, if it reflects earlier *pektu~. The earlier
connection with Toch. passam must be given up, since this belongs to PIE *psten-
'breast'. Skt. paksa- 'wing' is quite removed semantically, and does not explain the suffix.
Bibl.: WH II: 270, EM 491, IEW 792.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pecu (pecu) 'flock, herd' [n. u] (Naev.; pl.pecua; sg. in quot. only in abl.)
Derivatives: pecuarius 'of cattle or sheep' (P1.+),pecuinus 'of sheep, cattle' (Cato+),
pecus, -udis 'any animal of a farm' (P1.*0, pecus, -oris [n.] 'farm animals, livestock'
(Andr.+), peculium 'money or property in possession' (P1.+), peculiaris 'personal,
private' (P1.+), peculiosus 'well provided with peculium' (PI.), peculiare 'to provide
with peculium' (PI.), peculatus, -us 'embezzlement of public money or property'
(P1.+), depeculatus, -us 'the act of defrauding' (P1.+), peciinia 'property, money' (Lex
XII+), pecunidsus 'well provided with money' (Varro+).
Pit. *peku-, *pekos-. It. cognates: U.pequo [acc.pl.j 'cattle' < *pekud.
PIE *pe£-u- [n.] 'cattle', *pek-os, -es- [n.] 'what is being fleeced' > 'cattle/wool'.
IE cognates: Skt. pasu- [m.] 'cattle, animal', pasu- [n.] 'id.', pasumant- 'equipped
