content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ago' (PI.+), pridie [adv.] 'the day before, in the time preceding' (P1.+); (2) primus
'furthest in front, foremost' (Naev.+), adprimus 'the very first' (Andr.+), primanus
'belonging to the first legion' (Cato+), primarius 'leading, excelling' (PI.+),prTmatus,
-iis 'supremacy' (Varro+), primitus [adv.] 'at first' (Lucil.+), primulus 'first'
(Naev.+), primdris [adj.] 'first member or part, nearest' (Cato+), princeps, -cipis 'fist,
earliest, leading' (PI.+), principium 'start, origin' (PI.+), apprimus 'very first, most
excellent' (Andr.+); (3) prTscus 'ancient, of old' (Enn.+), prTstinus 'antique, ancient,
previous' (P1.+).
Pit. comp. *prf-jos- 'earlier, former', n. *pri-is, sup. *pri-isamo- 'first'. It. cognates:
Pael. pri- 'in front of, maybe U. pri-, pri- in prinuvatu- < *pri-; Pael. prismu
[nom.sg.f.] fem. praenomen; Pael.pritrome [acc.sg. + -en] 'further' < *prT-tero~.
PIE *pr(e)i 'before'. IE cognates: Gr. Jtpiv 'before, previously'; OPr. pre; 'at, with,
to', Lith. prie,prie- 'at, with, to', OCS pri 'at, with, by' < PIE *prei.
All seem to be built on an adv. *pri 'before', which is attested by Paul, ex F. as pri
'prae\ It might also be phonologically *pri. The PIE ancestry of pri is uncertain: it
can hardly continue *prh i (zero grade of *preh i > prae) since this would yield Lat.
2 2
*pari. It might be connected with BS1. *prei 'at, with' The sup. contains the suffix
*-isamo~, cf. Pael. prismu; it is used in prmceps < *primo~kap~. The sup, prior
reflects *pri-ids, and its zero grade *prT-is seems to have been used as pris- in pridem
< *pris-dem 'long ago' and in the adj. *prTs-ko- > prisons, and pris-tinus. The suffix
-tinus also appears e.g. in diutinus 'daily' and crastinus 'of tomorrow', where it seems
more original; "see s.v. diu. ; i
Bibl.: WH II: 361-363, EM 534fi, IEW 810-816, Cowgill 1970: 125, Leumann 1977:
317, 321, Sihier 1995: 360, 428, Meiser 1998: 174, Untermann 2000: 577, 579.

de Vaan Latin Etymology: prlvus 'peculiar, individual' [adj. o/a] (PI.+)
Derivatives: privare 'to deprive of, rob' (P1.+), privatas 'private, of a citizen' (Lex
XII+), privatim 'privately, separately' (P1.+); privigna 'step-daughter' (Titin.+).
Pit. *prei-wo- 'separate, individual'. It. cognates: U. prever [abl.pl.m.], preve [adv.]
'one at a time, separately'; O. preiuatud [abl.sg.] 'the defendant', probably a loan
from Latin.
PIE *prei-uo-.
Probably a derivative in *-uo- from the stem *prei- 'in front of, before' seen in prior
etc. Thus, a semantic shift from 'being in front' to 'being separate'.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: straightaway' (PI.+),protinam [adv.] 'directly' (Naev.+).
Pit. *prd, *pro(~) 'before'. It. cognates: O. pru, pr., pr. [prep. + abl.], O. pru-, pru-,
U. pru-, pro- [prev.] 'for, before' < *pro pro; U. promom, pru mum, prumu [adv.]
t
'at first' < *pro-mom 'primum'; O.pruter [adv.] 'before' < *pro-ter.
PIE *pro 'in front of, before', *proH (?); *pro-mo- 'further, up front'. IE cognates:
Olr. ro" [pref.] (perfectivizing); Skt. p%a [adv.] 'before, forward', Av. fra- 'for,
before', Khot. ha-; Gr. rcpo 'before, forwards, forth', Lith. pra- 'by, through', OCS
pro- 'through', Go. fra- [pref.] < PIE *pro~; Skt. jorator [adv.] 'early, in the morning,
the next day', Gr. jipcoT 'early, in the morning', Latv. pruojam 'away, off, Lith. pro-
'pre-, fore-, between', pro 'through, along, past', OCS pra- 'through', OHG fruo
[adj.] 'early'; Gr. npopoq 'protagonist, commander', OIc.framr, OE fram 'excellent'.
The form prod- is recent and was probably built after re-C- : red-V- 'back'. Short
pro- occurs in some forms as a variant of pro-, but also in some older compounds,
such as procul, probus, proprius, etc. Most of the Sabellic forms also go back to
*pro~. Leumann regards *prd as the most original form, which was shortened to *pro
already in PIE. Schrijver 1991, however, follows Beekes in assuming an ins.sg.
*pro-hj (or *pr-oh ?) beside *pro; Untermann 2000: 582 also assumes an ins.sg.
}
Alternatively, one might suggest that *proH arose when *pro- stood before roots in
*HC~.
Bibl.: WH II: 364fi, EM 536, IEW 810-816, Leumann 1977: 560f., Schrijver 1991:
81, Beekes 1995: 221, Sihler 1995: 427,439, Schaffher 1996: 155, Untermann 2000:
581 f., 586, 590. —> per, por, pr ope, re-

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: probus 'excellent, good' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: (1) probitas 'honesty, integrity' (Pac.+); approbus 'excellent'
(Caecil.+), improbus 'of poor quality, improper, greedy' (Lex XII+); probdre 'to
approve of, commend, prove' (Naev.+), probdtid 'approval, proof (Varro+),
probdbilis 'commendable, plausible' (Afran.+); approbdre 'to commend, endorse'
(PI.+), comprobdre 'to confirm, justify' (PI.+), improbare 'to reject, repudiate'; (2)
superbus 'proud, haughty' (Naev.+), superbia 'pride, disdain' (P1.+).
Pit. *pro-f(w)-o- 'favourable'. It. cognates: Pael. pros [nom.sg.m.] 'righteous', U.
prufe [adv.] 'correct', O. amprufid [adv.] 'incorrect' < *pro-bhu-o-; O. prufatted,
prufated, irpcofaxeS [3s.pf.], prufattens [3p.pf.] 'to approve of, denom. verb to
*profo~, in its meaning influenced by Lat. probare.
PIE *pro-*bh(h )u-o-. IE cognates: Skt. pra-bhu- [adj.] 'excelling, excellent'; Gr.
2
imepcpeu [adv.] 'excessively' (Att.), xmep(pur]q 'excessive', urreptpiaXoq 'arrogant' (if
< *-phuwalos with de Lamberterie), tupeap 'mistletoe' (< *h iu~bheh u-r or
r 2 2
*h iu-b eu-r (?) 'ever-growing').
2
The Sab. forms show that there was a Pit. form *profo~; hence, we must start from a
thematized form in *-bhu-o- < *-bhHu-o~, with loss of the PIE laryngeal in the
compound. In or after Pit., *w was lost after a labial obstruent (cf. aperio, operio).
Skt. has a u-stem which might be an internal formation of Skt. Superbus is not found
in Sab., which may be coincidental; but since superbus functions more or less as an
antonym to probus, superbus might be an inner-Latin formation. De Lamberterie
1994: 334 has suggested that Gr. wiepfpeu 'excessively' represent the same formation
as superbus.
Bibl.: WH II: 366, EM 537, IEW 146-150, Meiser 1998: 121, Untermann 2000:
583-585, LIV *bhueh -. fui
2

de Vaan Latin Etymology: procerus 'tall, lofty' [adj. o/a] (Enn.+)
Pit. *k(r)ero~. :
PIE *krehro- 'growing'. IE cognates: see s.v. cresco.
r
Schrijver regards 'the usual connection with cresco as very uncertain, since it is not
compelling that 'tall' derives from 'growing upward'. I think this is too pessimistic.
Rieken 2003: 45 reconstructs a PIE adj. *kirh o- 'growing' which she recognizes in
}
HLuw, zi+ra/i-la-mi-i 'fruitful, thriving' < *zira- 'growth' < *kiro~. She analyzes
procerus as orig. *'having a high growth' and sincerus *'of one growth, unmixed,
real'. This is possible, but the meaning 'fruitful, thriving' of HLuw. is less clearly
connected with 'to grow, increase' than procerus. Hence, I still regard a dissimilation
of *prd-kreros > procirus quite possible.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: procul 'some distance away, (far) away, apart' [adv.] (P1.+)
Pit. *pro-k(w)o- 'at the front'?
PIE *pro 'in front of.
Lat. procul might be a derivative of PIE *pro-ko- 'at the front', hence *prokelo~.
Schaffner 1996 compares PGm. *nixuula~ 'down, directed downwards' < *ni-kue-lo-
and Skt. visva- 'all, whole' < *ui-k#o-, and posits *proculo- < *prokuolo- < PIE
*pro-kue-lo- 'somewhat to the front'. The only problem is the zero ending of procul:
n. *prokulom should yield *proculum. Schaffner 1996: 163 suggests that *-um may
have dropped as in nihil and donee (this does not seem likely, since it only happens in
frequent forms) or by analogy with simul and facul < n. *-//. In that case, a preform
*prokelom « *proko- would also be possible. Thus, this aspect of the etymology
remains open.
Bibl.: WH II: 368, EM 537, IEW 810-816, Schaffner 1996: 162-171. —• pro, re-

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: proelium 'battle' [n. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: proelidre 'to engage in battle' (Lucil.+), proeliaris 'of battles' (PI.+).
Klingenschmitt 1980 posits a sound law *oweli > *owili > *oili > -oeli-, for which
only the PN Cloelius, Coelius and Boelius are adduced as evidence. Proelium would
continue a form *pro-gwe/ol-iowith cognate forms such as MW ryvel, W. rhyfel
'war' < PCI. *ro-bel-, bel 'hits, strikes', erdifel 'smites' (< *ari-di-bel~), Lith. gelti,
3s. gelia 'ache, sting, bite', and OHG quelan 'to be in pain', OE ewelan 'to die'. Yet
in the relative chronology, the Pit. lenition of *gw to *vv precedes the Latin
development of *-owe/oCV- > *-ouCV- > -uC-, so that one would expect *pro-gwelio-
to yield *prulium or, like motus, *prolium. Klingenschmitts proposal may be salvaged
if the root sequence *wel- was restored at a later stage, but no cognate forms are
extant in Latin that would seem to conserve such a root. If we are to explain proe-
from an earlier disyllabic sequence, the syllable boundary must have been present
until quite recently. An alternative candidate would be a preform *pro~xel-io-, but
there is no PIE root *ghel(H)- that comes to mind as a likely root.
Bibl.: WH II: 369, EM 538, Klingenschmitt 1980: 221, Meiser 1998: 87, Brachet
1999, Schumacher 2004: 218.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: prope 'near, near by, close' [adv.] (P1.+)
Derivatives: propior 'nearer, closer' (PI.+); proximus 'nearest, next' (Lex XII+);
propter [adv; prep. + acc, + abl.] 'near, close by; because of, for the sake of
(Naev.+); propinquus 'near, neighbouring' (P1.+), propinqudre 'to approach, be near'
(Lucr.+), propinquitas 'nearness, kinship' (P1.+); propitius 'well-disposed' (Naev.+),
propitiare 'to win over, soothe' (P1.+),propitiabilis 'able to be propitiated' (Enn.).
PIE *propro 'on and on, ever further*. IE cognates: Skt. pra-pra [adv.] 'on and on',
Gr. Ttpo-Tipo 'before; on and on*.
The etymology of these forms has been solved by Dunkel 1980, who convincingly
argues that prope reflects an iterative preverb *propro 'very much in front, near',
with dissimilatory loss of the second *r. Propior is its regular comparative, and
proximus < *propsamo- may be explained as a replacement of the unusual sequence
*-psimos by *-ksimos (British Celtic shows a sound change *ps > *ks in W. uchel
'high' < *oupselo-, but an Italo-Celtic date for this change would place the loss of the
second *r in *propro very early, which is unlikely). The adv. propter < *prope-ter
cannot be separated from prope semantically. Dunkel's explanation of propitius <
*prope-tio- also seems superior to previous explanations of this adj. The suffix of
propinquus is not completely clear: was it built as an antonym to relinquere 'to leave,
abandon'?
Bibl.: WH II: 371-373, EM 538f, IEW 810-816, Dunkel 1979, 1980. pro, re-
proprius 'one's own, peculiar, specific' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: propritim 'particularly' (Lucr.), propriassit 'proprium fecerit' (Fest. 229M).
Pit. *prijo- 'own, dear', *prijaje/o- 'to make one's own'.
PIE *priH-o- 'own, dear'. IE cognates: MW rhydd 'free', Skt. priya-, Av. friia-
'dear, own', Skt. prestha-, Av.fraesta- 'dearest', Gd.freis, OHG fri 'free' < PGm.
*frija- 'free, dear, own'; Skt. pr. prinanti [3p.act.], apriyamana- [neg.ptc.med.], aor.
presat [3s.sb...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: prosperus 'prosperous, successful' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: prosperare 'to succeed, further' (P1.+).
Pit. *pro-sparo-.
PIE *sph,-ro- 'thriving'. IE cognates: Hit. ispai- / ispi- 'to get full, be satiated' <
*sphoi- / *sphi-; Skt. sphird- 'fat', sphaya- 'to become fat, increase', OCS speti 'to
r r
succeed', Ru. spet' 'to ripen' < PIE *speh, OE spowan 'to prosper'.
r
A combination of pro plus PIE *sphi-ro- 'thriving', or a ro-derivative of a PIE
compound verb *pro-speh 'to thrive'.
r
Bibl.: WH II: 375fi, EM 540, IEW 983fi, Schrijver 1991: 93, LIV *spheh. ^ spes

prOtervus 'bold, violent; impudent' [adj. o/a] (P1.+; var. proptervus (PL, Pac.) is
older)
Derivatives:protervitas 'boldness* (Xer.+).
Pit. *propetes-wo-.
PIE *pet-os, -es- [n.] 'impetus, haste'. IE cognates: Gr. -K£TT]<; 'flying'; see s.v.
peto.
The older etymologies with *pet-r/n- 'wing' are semantically unsatisfactory. Rix
1981: 118 (= 2001: 286) proposes a preform *propetes-uo- with regular change of *su
to rv. The preform would be a contamination of *pro-petes 'inclined, hasty' (Gr.
7tpo7C£TT|^ 'falling down, precipitate') and *petes-uo- 'provided with impetus'. This
seems at least better than other solutions.
BibL: WH II: 377, EM 541, IEW 825f., LIV 2.*peth -. peto
2
pruTna 'hoar-frost, rime' [f. a] (Pac.f)
Pit. *prus-wo- 'freezing'.
PIE *prus-uo- 'sprinkling, drop'. IE cognates: Skt. prusva /prusvd 'drop of dew,
cool drop' (cf. Gerow 1973).
Hamp 1973 discusses the etymology at length, and concludes that the most likely
development is the following: *prusmna > *prurwina > *pruwTna (dissimilation of r)
> *prufna > pruina. Skt. prusvd may preserve the nominal basis, derived from the
verb which is still attested in Germanic (see prurio); from this nominal basis, an adj.
in *-Tno- was derived in Latin.
BibL: WH II: 378f., EM 541, IEW 846, Hamp 1973, Leumann 1977: 328, LIV
*preus-. —> prurio

Derivatives: prima 'glowing charcoal...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: puer 'boy' [m. o] (Andr.+; also puerus PL, Caecil.)
Derivatives: puera 'girl' (Andr.+), puerculus 'little son' (P1.+), puerTlis 'of a
boy/child' (P1.+), puerTlitas 'childhood' (Varro+), pueritia 'boyhood' (Ter.+), puella
'girl, young woman' (P1.+), pueltula 'girl' (Ter.+), puellascere 'to become girlish'
(Varro), puellitarT 'to act like a girl' (Lab.), puellus 'young boy' (P1.+); repuerascere
'to become a boy again' (PL lx).
Pit. *puwero-.
PIE *ph u~ero- 'smaller'.
2
Lat. puella < *puere-la 'little girl'. Since PIE *ph u-ero- would possibly yield
2
pre-Lat. * powero-, puer may have been built more recently to the Pit. root *pau-
t
*pu- 'small, young'. Alternatively, puer was built to a stem 'masculine / pubic'
possibly found in pubes.
Bibl.: WH II: 382, EM 543, IEW 842f. paucus,pubes

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pulcher 'beautiful' [adj. o/a] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: pulchralia [n.pl.] 'delicacies' (Cato), pulchritudo 'beauty' (P1.+),
pulchritas 'beauty' (Caecil.+); perpulcher 'very beautiful' (Ter.+).
Maybe earlier polcher, according to Prise, and CIL. The spelling -ch- is Hellenizing,
hence we can posit a stem *polkro- or *pelkro-. WH and IEW assume that this
belongs to PIE *perk- 'variegated' with a dissimilation of *perk-ro- to *pelk-ro- but
t
one would rather expect a dissimilation of the second *r to *perko- in such a case. No
etymology.
Bibl.: WH II: 384, EM 543f., IEW 820f, Sihler 1995: 142.
pQlex, -icis 'flea' [m. k] (Andr.+)
Pit. *pusI-7
PIE ""plus- 'flea'? IE cognates: Skt. plusi-, Sogd. 'ps'h < PIr. *frsa-, Yidgha friyo <
PIr. *fhtsi-ka- Gr. yvUtt (< *psul-), Arm. low (< PIE *plus ); Alb. pleshi [m.]; Lith.
i
blusa, RuCS bl-bcha, Cz. blecha\ OHG floh < *plauxall 'flea'.
The many different forms of 'flea' seem to belong together, but it is impossible to
reconstruct one PIE preform. Taboo deformation and folk etymology often interfere
with the names of insects. Latin pul- might reflect *pusl-, with metathesis from
*plus~.
Bibl.: WH II: 385, EM 544, IEW 102.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pullus 'drab-coloured, sombre' [adj. o/a] ( Andr.+)
Pit. *polwo~.
PIE *p(o)l-u-o- 'gray'. IE cognates: Skt.parusa- 'grey, grey-brown', YAv. pottrusa-
'grey (of hair)' < *pe/ol(H)-u-so-, Gr. nohoq 'grey, grizzled' (< *7IOXI-(F)6-).
Has been derived from the same root as palled (itself not completely clear), but the
semantics of 'pale' and 'sombre' are not necessarily connected, and the sequence
pull- is difficult to combine with pall-. IEW states that (expected) *pol- < *pj- was
changed into pul- under the influence of p-, but this is an ad hoc assumption which is
furthermore contradicted by polluceo and pollud (to mention only the examples of
*poll- plus a back vowel). Nussbaum 1997 proposes to connect pullus with PIE
*pe/ol-(i/u-) 'gray, dark colour' found in other languages; this seems the best solution
proposed so far. Similarly, Driessen 2005: 46f."
Bibl.: WH II: 386, EM 544, IEW 804f., Nussbaum 1997: 191. —• palled
2 t

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pulmo 'lungs'[m. ri] (P1.+) *
Derivatives: pulmoneus 'consisting of the lung' (P1.+).
Pit. *plu-mon>
PIE *plu-mon-? [adj.] 'floating > lung'. IE cognates: Skt. klomart- (< *ploman- <
*pleu-mon-) 'the right lung; [pi.] the lungs', Gr. jiXeupaw 'lung', Lith. plauciai [pi.],
OCSpljusta [pi] 'lungs' < BS1. *plou-tio~, *pleu-tio- [n.].
Two etymologies are mostly found in the handbooks. It has been suggested that
pulmo goes back with metathesis to *plumo, and was borrowed from Gr. HXeupcov.
This has the disadvantage that we expect a long vowel *plitmo, and furthermore, that
the word for 'lung' is not often borrowed (which does not exclude the possibility, of
course). The other option is that pulmo continues an inherited adj. *pleu-mon 'the
floating one* (since lungs float on water); we would then still expect an outcome
*plumo, maybe with metathesis *pulm6. Alternatively, one could posit *plu-mon with
the zero grade of *pleu-\ still, a metathesis to *pulmon would have to be assumed.
Bibl.: WH II: 386, EM 545, IEW 837f., Leumann 1977: 101, 371, Biville 1990 I:
353, LIV *pleu-. pluit

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pulpa 'fleshy parts of a body, best part of the meat' [f. a] (Cato+)
Derivatives: pulpdmenium 'small piece of meat' (Aridr.+), pulmentum 'small portion
of meat or fish to start a meal' (P1.+), pulmentarium 'condiment of a pulmentum'
(Cato+).
Pit. *pelp-a- 'meat', *pelp-m(e)n- 'meat'. It. cognates: U. pelmner [gen.sg.] n.
'meat' < *pelp~men~.
U. shows that the vowel in the first syllable was *e, hence pulpa < *pelpa- and
pulmentum < *pelp-mento-.
Bibl.: WH II: 3861, EM 545, Untermann 2000: 528.
puis, -Itis 'kind of porridge' [f. f] (Cato+)
Derivatives:pultiphagus 'eating porridge' (PI.).
Pit. *po!t-i-.
IE cognates: Gr. 7IOX.TOC; 'porridge'.
Since pult- can reflect *polt~, puis is suspect of being a loan from Gr. JTOXTOI;
'porridge'. Yet Gr. o-stems are usually borrowed into Latin as o-stems, so there must
be more to puis. EM consider an Etruscan mediary, which cannot be ascertained.
Since nokxoq itself does not have an IE etymology, both words may go back to a basis
*polt~, Latin to an /-stem *polti~. The root *pol(H)~ 'flour' can then be compared with
pollen < *pol(H)-n~. Putative *pol(H)- 'flour' might be a loanword from a
Mediterranean language, or belong to an as yet unknown PIE root.
Bibl.: WH II: 387fi, EM 545, IEW 802. pollen

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pulvis, -eris 'dust' [m. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: pulvisculus 'dust' (P1.+), pulverare 'to be dusty; pulverize' (P1.+),
pulverulentus 'dusty' (Varro+), pulvTnus 'cushion, pillow' (P1.+), pulvinar [n.]
'support for a beached ship; cushioned couch' (P1.+), pulvlnaris 'of a cushion'
(Varro+); dispulverare 'to pulverize' (Naev.).
Pit. *pe/olaw(-isy 'dust'.
PIE *pe/olH-u- 'chaff. IE cognates: see spalea.
Pulvis may reflect *pe/ol(V}w-is~, with a rare is-stem which may have been adopted
from cinis, -eris 'ashes'. WH and IEW derive pulvis from PIE *pelH-u-(h2) 'chaff
seen in Skt. palava- 'chaff, husk', OPr. pelwo, Lith. pelus 'chaff, Ru. polova (see s.v.
palea), but Schrijver 1991 objects that the semantic connection of 'dust' with 'chaff
is uncompelling. In my view, it is also difficult to connect 'flour' and 'chaff, since
they are each other's opposite when processing grain. Of course, via a primary
meaning 'to grind' or 'fine dust', they may be connected. Thus, it remains possible to
derive pulvis from *pe/olH-u- (> Pit. *pe/olaw-), but not from *polHu- > PIE *polu-
(Saussure's effect), since *polw- would yield Lat. *poll~. The noun pulvmus can stem
from *poluis-no-: a pillow used to be filled with straw or chaff.
Bibl.: WH II: 388, EM 545, IEW 802, Leumann 1977: 321, Schrijver 1991: 256f.
palea

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pumex, -icis 'pumice' [m. k] (P1.+)
Derivatives: pumiceus 'of pumice' (P1.+), pumicare 'to rub smooth with pumice'
(Lucil.+).
Pit. *poim-Vk~.
PIE *(s)poHi-m- 'foam'.
EM argue that the close similarity of pumice with a sponge explains why *(s)poim-
'foam' was used for 'pumice'. But since spuma does not mean 'sponge', one might more
safely argue that pumice looks like petrified foam, and that this explains the metaphor.
Bibl.: WH II: 388f., EM 545, IEW 1001. ^ spuma

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pumilus 'dwarf, pygmy' [adj. o/a] (Mart.+)
Derivatives:pumilio 'dwarf, midget' (Lucr.+; CILpoum-).
IE cognates: Gr. 7ruy^iaTog 'as large as a fist, dwarf-like; pi. pygmies' (from 7r\)yjif]
'fist'); nuypaMcov PN, maybe originally a non-IE loanword.
According to EM, pumilid was the primary form, from which pumilus was
back-formed. For pumilid, the morphology suggests a borrowing from the Greek PN
nuypaXicov, but this was rejected by WH, who doubt the change of *-ugm- > ~itmIf
direct borrowing from the Greek PN is rejected (note that pumilid is not a PN), one
might save the borrowing hypothesis by supposing that the Romans borrowed m>Yp,f|
'fist' as *pume and derived pumilio 'who is only of the size of a fist' from it, or that
they borrowed an (unattested) Greek noun *jruy|iiXoc,. For Latin gm > mm, ci. flamma.
Quite differently, Adams 1985b: 244 interprets pumilio as 'little hairy one' and
connects it with pubes 'pubic hair, body hair' which might reflect *peum-.
Morphologically, this interpretation would be easier.
Bibl: WH II: 389, EM 545. pubes .

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pungo 'to pierce, sting' [v. Ill; pf. pupugi, ppp. piincium] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: punctdriola 'skirmish' (Cato apud Fest.), expungere 'to prick
thoroughly; cancel a debt' (P1.+); piigio 'dagger, poniard' (Cic.+); pugil, -is [m.]
'boxer' (Ter.+), pugilice 'like a boxer' (PI.), pdgilatus, -us 'boxing' (PL+),
pugilatorius 'used in boxing' (PI.); pugnus 'fist' (P1.+), pugillus 'handful' (Cato+),
pugillaris 'writing-tablet (small enough to hold in the hand' (Lab.+); pugna 'fight'
(Naev.+), pugnare 'to fight, combat' (P1.+), piignaculum 'bulwark' (PI.), pugneus
'made of fists' (PL), depiigndre 'to fight, do battle' (P1.+), depugndtio 'method of
fighting a battle' (Cato), expugndre 'to capture, storm' (P1.+), oppugnare 'to attack,
oppose' (P1.+), propugnare 'to fight in defence of (Varro+), prdpugnaculum
'bulwark, defence' (P1.+), repugndre 'to fight back, defend' (Pac.+).
Pit. *pung- 'to. pierce, sting', *puk/g-n(o)~ 'fist'.
PIE *pu-n(e)-g/k-. IE cognates: Gr. TTU£; 'with the fist', iruypT] [f.] 'fist, fist-fight',
Ttuycbv, -ovog [m;] 'certain measure, distance from the elbow to the first finger-joint';
sXE-7iei>Kf]{; 'with a point', TteuKq [f.] 'pine-tree'.
The nasal present can go back to a root *pug- or to *puk~, with voicing of the velar
stop between nasals. In the latter case, pugil and pugio would have secondarily
adopted this g; yet in view of the difference in meaning, *pug- seems to have been
present separately from pungo, and with the meaning 'fist'. The noun pugillus
presupposes *pug-en-lo- and hence an original n-stem; Lat. pugnus and pugna (length
of ii is automatic in front of -gn-) may then be derived from the w-stem. But they may
also reflect *puk/g-no~. The meaning of pugnaculum suggests that it is an abbreviated
form of prdpugnaculum, rather than an immediate derivative of pugnare (Serbat
1975: 194).
Bibl.: WH II: 383f., EM 543-546, IEW 828, Meiser 2003: 189, LIV *peu(g}-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pur(i)go, -are 'to clean, clear' [v. I] (P1.+; purigare in PL, Varro)
Derivatives: purgamentum 'means of cleansing; that which is cleaned away,
rubbish' (Varro+), purgatio 'ridding, clearing' (Cato+); expurgare 'to free from dirt
or blame, purify' (P1.+), expurgatio 'cleansing, excuse' (P1.+), perpurgare 'to clean
thoroughly, purge' (P1.+; PL perpurigare).
Pit. *pur, *pun- [n.] 'fire'. It. cognates: U. pir, pir [nom.acc.sg.], pure [abl.sg.],
pureto [abl.sg. + -to] n. 'fire' < *pur, abl. *pitred.
PIE *peh -ur [nom.acc.], ph -uen-s [gen.] 'fire'. IE cognates: Hit. pahhur i pahhuen-
2 2
[n.] 'fire, embers, fever', CLuw.pahur- [n.] 'fire'(?) < *peh -ur, *ph -uen-; Gr. jrup,
2 2
gen. 7rupog, Arm. howr, Go-fon, gen.funins, OIc. funi [m.] OIc. furr, fyrr, fyri, OHG
OS OFr.Jiur, OHG also fuir 'fire'; ToApor, ToB puwdr, pwar 'fire'.
Since Lat. purigare does not show a medial long vowel which one would expect from
*puro-ago~, it will contain the word for 'fire' *pur- (rather than *puro- 'clean') and
have meant 'to lead the fire about' (so as to purify); thus Dunkel 2000a: 94. The noun
'fire' is also attested in U. pir, pur-. According to Janda 2000: 44-46, PIE *peh ur
2
'fire' might refer to the 'striking' of sparks used to light a fire.
Bibl.: WH II: 390fi, EM 546fi, IEW 828, Untermann 2000: 557fi, LIV *pieh -.
2
pavio, purus

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pflrus 'clean, pure' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: impurus 'dirty, foul' (P1.+), impuritia 'impurity' (PL+), impuratus
'filthy' (PI.+).
Pit. *puro- 'pure'.
PIE *ph u-ro- 'clear, cleansed'. IE cognates: Olr. ur, W. ir 'green, fresh' < *puro-
2
'clean', Skt. pavate [3s.med.] 'to become clean', punati [3s.act.] 'to clean', YAv.
puitika- 'serving for purification', OHG fouwen, fewen 'to sieve, to sift'; Lith. puras,
puraT 'winter corn', Latv. purs 'grain measure', RuCS pyro 'spelt', Ru. pyrej
'couch-grass', Gr. mipog, Dor. oTrijpoq 'wheat' [m.] < *puHro- 'wheat' (< *'clean'?).
/
Latin and Celtic show a stem *puro- 'clean' which is cognate with Ilr. *puH- 'to
clean*. Janda 2000: 39-43 connects this with the Gr. and BS1. words for 'wheat'. He
argues that wheat was referred to as 'pure' or 'cleansed' because the grains could be
separated from the hulls more easily than in the case of other, older kinds of grain. He
connects Lat. pavio and posits an original root *peh - 'to strike', with ^-extension
2
*peh u~. The meaning 'to clean' would have developed through cleaning by means of
2
striking. Thus, we would have *peh u- 'to strike' > 'to clean', *puH-ro- 'clean' > Gr,
2
BS1. 'wheat'.
According to Weiss 1996b, the hapax salaputium in Catullus may also belong
here. He explains it as a loanword from O. *salaputim 'purification of salt' <
*sal-put-io~, derived in Oscan from a cp. *sal-put- 'purifier of salt' < *-puH-t- 'who
purifies'. In the Catullus passage, 'purification of salt' would be a metaphor for
'refinement of wit'.
Bibl.: WH II: 390f., EM 546f„ IEW 827, Schrijver 1991: 247, Untermann 2000:
557f, LIV l.*peuH-. —>pavio, plus, pur (i)go

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pus, puris 'pus' [n. r] (Lucil.+)
Derivatives: purulentus 'with much pus' (Cato+); suppurare 'to fester under the
surface' (Cato+); putere 'to rot, stink' (P1.+),putidus 'rotting, rotten' (PI.+), putescere
'to begin to rot' (Cato+), exputescere 'to rot away' (P1.+), putor 'rottenness' (Cato+);
puter, -tris [adj.] 'rotten, foul," crumbly' (Andr.+), putrere 'to be in a state of decay'
(Pac.+), putrescere 'to decay, disintegrate' (Varro+), putrefacere 'to cause to rot'
(Varro+),putridus 'rotten' (Caecil.+).
Pit. *pus- 'pus', *puto- 'rotten', *putri- 'rotten'.
PIE *puH-o/es- [n.] 'pus', *puH-to-, *puH-tro- 'rotten'. IE cognates: Olr. othar
'sickness, sick man' < *putro-\ Skt. puvas- [n.] 'pus' < *puH-os, puyati 'to rot, stink',
puti- 'foul, stinking', YAv. puiia- 'to decay', puiti- [f.] 'becoming foul, decay'; Gr.
JTOOV [n.], TT6O<; [n.] 'pus', icuoq [m.] 'animal milk', TCUIDOPAI 'to rot, decay'; Lith.
puvisis 'piece of mouldered wood, (pi.) rotten stuff, Latv. puvesis 'pus' <
*puH-es-io-, Lith. puti 'to rot, decay'; Olc.fiiinn [adj.] 'rotten'.
*
Nom.acc.sg. pus may be due to generalization of *puH-s- from the gen.sg., or to a
phonetic development *puos > *puus > piis in the nom.sg. The forms putere etc. are
probably based on a ppp. *puto- to the root *puH~. For puter and its possible Olr.
cognate othar, Schrijver 1991 is hesitant to choose between *pHu-tro/i- and
*puH-tro/i-. In either case, the short vowel resulting in Italo-Celtic is surprising.
Bibl.: WH II: 391 f., EM 547, IEW 848f, Leumann 1977: 380. Schrijver 1991: 234f,
339,341,534, Stiiber 2002: 136f, LIV 2.*peuH-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: pustula 'blister, pustule, bubble' [f. d] (Tibullus+; variantspusula,pussula)
Derivatives: praepiitium 'foreskin of the penis' (Varro+).
IE cognates: PIE *pus-: Skt. pusyati 'thrive, flourish', Lith. pusli 'blister, bladder',
Ru. puxlyj 'chubby', pitxmit' 'to swell', Norw. f&ysa 'swell'; PIE *put-\ Lith. piisti,
3s.pr.punta, pret. puto 'to swell', Lith. pusti, 3s. piicia 'to blow' (with analogical long
vowel).
Might be related to PIE words continuing *put- 'to blow' or *pus- 'to blossom,
swell', but nothing specific can be said about the relationship, which in any case is
uncertain because the semantics are npt compelling. The appurtenance of praeputium
*'what is at front of the *put(o)-f (Weiss 1996b: 355) here is uncertain, but a
denomination of 'penis' as 'which swells' is conceivable.
Bibl.: WH II: 392, EM 547, IEW 847f.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: puto, -fire 'to prune (trees), scour (wool); make up (accounts), think, reckon' [v. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: putus 'pure, genuine' (P1.+); putator 'who trims trees or bushes'
(Varro+), putatid 'pruning, opinion' (Varro+), putamen 'outer cover, shell' (P1.+);
amputare 'to cut off, prune away' (Lucil.+), computare 'to calculate, reckon' (PI-+),
deputare 'to complete the pruning; regard as, assign' (P1.+), disputare 'to argue,
debate' (PL+), exputare 'to cut off; examine thoroughly' (P1.+), interputare 'to prune
here and there' (Cato+), perputare 'to give a clear outline of (PI.), supputare 'to
prune below, reckon' (Cato+).
Pit. *puto- 'cut off, clean'.
PIE *pHu-to-.
The adj. putus must be the primary word of this family, putare the verbal derivative.
Its original meaning clearly was 'to cut off unwanted branches, prune a tree', whence
'to ponder over, reckon'. On the hapaxperputare, see Kiimmel 2004b: 353. The short
vowel in putus is ascribed to original oxytonesis by Schrijver 1991. For the
reconstruction of the root, see s.v. pavio. If Lith. pjauti goes back to *peut~, the root
must be *ph,u-; but LIV posits *pih u- on account of Gr. 7rca{a> 'to thrust', ToB
2
pyakar 'they struck down'. Latin putus does not allow to decide between *h and *h .
( 2
Bibl.: WH II: 393f, EM 548, IEW 827, Schrijver 1991: 241, LIV *pieh -. pavio
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: putus 'boy' [m. o] (Verg. - a conjecture)
Derivatives: putillus 'very young, tiny' (PL); pullus 'foal, chick, young of an animal'
(P1.+), pullitra 'young chicken' (Varro), pullarius 'of chickens' (P1.+); pusus 'boy'
(Varro+),piisa 'girl' (Varro+),pusillus 'very small, tiny' (Naev.+).

[nom.sg.], pukelef [gen.sg.?], Mars, puclefs, Pael. puclois [dat.pl.], SPic. puqloh
[dat.sg.?] 'son' < *pu-tlo~.
quaero

PIE *put-lo- 'son'. IE cognates: Skt. putrd- 'son, child, young of an animal*, Av.
pu&ra- 'son'; Latv. putns 'bird'; OCS pttica 'bird', Ru. potka 'bird', Cz. piak 'bird',
all< BS1. *put- 'bird'.
Lat. pullus < *put-slo~, as shown by the dim. pusillus < *pussillo- < *putslo-lo-. Thus,
whereas Sabellic continues PIE *putlo~, Latin has inserted an -s-. The dim. putillus
was productively built to putus. The latter seems the most basic form *puto-, but it is
attested only in Empirical Latin in a conjectural form. The form pusus might be a
back-formation to pusillus. Romance continues a VLat. preform *puttus with the
characteristic geminate consonant of expressive words. A connection of the stem
*put- with puer is difficult to establish.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: quatio, -ere 'to shake, toss, hurry along' [v. Ill; ppp. quassum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: quassus, -us 'shaking' (Pac.+), quassare 'to shake repeatedly, damage'
(Naev.+), conquassare 'to shake violently' (Cato+); concutere 'to agitate, strike'
(P1.+), discutere 'to shatter, disperse' (Lucil.+), excutere 'to shake out, throw off
(P1.+), percutere 'to strike heavily, kill' (P1.+).
Pit. *kwat-i~.
PIE *(s)kuot-i-? IE cognates: Lith. kiisti, 3s. kunta 'to recover', kutiti, 3s. kiita 'to
thrive, prosper' < *kui~; OS scuddian, OHG scutten 'to shake' < *skut-, OIc. hossa
'to throw' < *kut-s~.
Together with the Gm. words for 'to shake', and maybe Lith *kut~ 'to recover', quatio
may point to a root *ku(o)t-, with awkward schwebe-ablaut. It might be a non-IE
word in origin. The appurtenance Gr. TtaocKfl 'to sprinkle' is possible from the
semantic side, but since complicated and unwarranted analogies are necessary to
explain Greek -a-, Latin and Greek would almost certainly point to non-IE *kuat- 'to
shake, strew'.
Bibl.: WH II: 399f„ EM 552f, IEW 632, Schrijver 1991: 260, 462f„ Meiser 2003:
115, LIV *(s)kuehit-. —> qualus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: queo, quire 'to be able' [v. II/IV; pf. qurvT; forms pr. qued, quis, quit, quimus, quids,
queunt, ipf. quibam, fut. qulbd, ps. quiiur] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: nequeo, nequTs, nequit etc. 'to be unable' (PI.+; Andr. nequinont), pte.
nequiens, -euntis.
IE cognates: see s.v. eo.
The alternation between Is. -ed, 3p. -eimt and the f-conjugation in most other forms
points to the verb eo, ire 'to go' as a basic element of these forms. It is generally
accepted that the verb was derived from a contracted form of 3s. *neque It or *neque
Itur 'it does no go', 'it is impossible' > 'he is unable' > nequitlnequitur. By removing
the negative element ne-, reanalysis led to a new verb queo, quire 'to be able'.
BibL: WH II: 402, EM 555, Leumann 1977: 521, Meiser 1998: 223, LIV *h]ei-. ed

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: oak', OIc, jjgrr [m.] 'tree, man' < PGm. *ferxu-\ OHG forha, MHG vorhe, OE furh,
OIc. fura [f.] 'fir-tree' < *furyd-\ OIc. Jyri [n.] 'fir forest', NHG Fohre, MoDu.
vuur-hout < *furx-in~. The word for 'fir-tree' is unrelated according to Kluge-Seebold
1999, but in my view the similarity in form is too close to keep them apart. Still, the
root ablaut is strange.
The Latin word shows the regular Italo-Celtic assimilation of *p - kw - > *kw - kw-.
The oldest attestations are of a w-stem, but since the cognate Gm. forms can also be
explained from a stem in *~xwa-, since m. and f. u- and o-stems tend to influence each
other in nom. and acc.sg, and since o-stem tree names tend to be feminine, it cannot
be excluded that quercus continues a Pit. o-stem. IEW and other handbooks also
connect Go. fairhjus 'world', OHG fer(a)h, OE feorh 'life, soul' < *perkw-o~, but
Schaffner 2001 convincingly rejects this connection. His alternative etymology of the
latter words as belonging to the root *perk- 'to plough' (see Lat pored) is attractive.
Gothic fairguni [n.] 'mountain' must also be separated; Schaffher 2001: 193 connects
Av. pauruuata- 'mountains', Gr. JiElpap 'end, border', Hit. pi-e-ru-ni [dat.sg.] 'stone'
< PIE *per-ur, -un-, which is semantically much more straightforward than a
connection with 'oak'. Finally, also unrelated to quercus are probably the BS1. words
for the 'god of thunder': OLith. perkunas, Lith. perkunas 'thunder', perkunija f.
'thunderstorm', Latv. p^rkuons 'thunder, god of thunder', ORu. Perurrb 'god of
thunder', Ru. perun 'thunder, lightning'. This means that only Latin and Gm. contain
certain reflexes of a stem *perkw-u/o- 'oak'.
Bibl.: WH II: 402f., EM 555, IEW 822f., Schaffher 2001: 190-194.

Derivatives: querela 'complaint, lament' (P1.+), querimdnia 'complaint, protest'
(P1.+); conqueri 'to utter a complaint, bewail' (P1-+).
Pit. *k"'es-e/o- / *kwes-e/o-.

TI6-T3I 'where'; Lith. kds 'who', OCS kb-to 'who', ceso [gen.sg.] 'whose'; Go. has
'who'.
The paradigm of the relat. and indef. pronoun in Latin is a mixture of reflexes of
*kwo- and *kwi-; for the forms of the latter (in the paradigm of Latin qui, these are
quern, ques, quibus, abl.sg. qui), see s.v. quis. The acc.sgm. *knom has been preserved
as quom > cum in the conjunction. The gen.sg. cuiius must be from *kwosio + *-s.
The dat.sg. *kwoiiei is probably analogical to the gen.sg. Sabellic retains the older PIE
form. The adv. quasi and quoque (< *quo-que) show shortening of the first long
vowel in front of the originally enclitic -si and -que, and then shortening of the final
long vowel through iambic shortening. Lat. qualis < *kweh -li-, quantus < *kwanto- <

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: qulnque 'five' [num. indecl.] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: quindecim 'fifteen' (Lex XII+), quinquaginta 'fifty' (P1.+),
quinquagenarius 'containing fifty of anything' (Cato+), quinquagesies 'fifty times'
(PL), quingenti 'five hundred' (P1.+); quinquatrus, -uum [f.pL] 'festival of Minerva,
lasting five days in March' (P1.+), quinquertid 'one who competes in the pentathlon'
(Andr.); quini 'five at a time' (P1.+), quindrius 'containing five each; coin worth five
asses' (Varro+), quinavicenarius 'relating to twenty-five' (PI.), quincunx, -ncis 'a
pattern of five, five-twelfths' (Varro+); qum(c)tus 'fifth' (P1.+), quintanus 'of the
fifth' (V arro+), Quintilis 'name of the fifth month' (Varro+).
Pit. *kwenkwe 'five', *kn'(e)nkwto- 'fifth'. It. cognates: O. pumperias, pumperias
[nom.pl.] , pumperiais [dat.abl.pl.], U. pumperias [nom.pl.] in O. probably 'fifth
day', in U. unclear meaning, < *pompe '5' + *-(e)ro''+ io- (er probably from
*peteria- « *peturia- '4'); O. pomtis 'five times' < *pompe-tis, suffix *-is (Lat. -ies)
» *-tis by analogy with sept-, oct- cent-. U. puntes [nom.pl.], puntis [dat.abl.pl.]'?'
< *pomp-ti- 'group of five'?
PIE *penkwe 'five', *pnkw-(t)o- 'fifth'. IE cognates: Olr. coic, W.pymp, Skt. pdnca
'five', pancama- 'fifth', paktha- PN, pankti- [f.] 'set of five', YAv. partca 'five',
puxSa- 'fifth', Gr. nevxe, Thess, Lesb. nepne 'five', 7cep7iT0c; 'fifth', Arm. hing 'five';
Alb. pese, OCS p?tb, Ru.pjat' 'five', OCS pfth, Ru. pjatyj 'fifth', Lith. penki 'five',
penktas 'fifth', Go.^m/'five', OHG fimfto 'fifth', ToA pan, ToB pis ' five'.
Pit. *kwenkwe yielded *kwinkwe by regular sound change. Subsequently, the *k in the
ordinal *kwinkto- was spirantized and the preceding vowel phonetically lengthened:
*kwmxto~. Finally, the spirant was lost, yielding quintus. From the ordinal, i was
analogically introduced into the cardinal *quinque » qulnque. Lat. quinT <
*kwenkw-(s)no~, quincunx < *quinqu- + uncia. The -a- in quinquaginta for
*qui...

content:

quibus)
Derivatives: qui [adv.] 'in what way, how? in that way' (Naev.+), quia 'because'
(P1.+), quisquis, quidquid 'whoever, whatever' (Andr.+), nequiquam 'in vain, without
cause' (P1.+); A number of compound indefinite pronouns is discussed s.v. their
second member: -cumque, -dam, ~dem, -nam, -pe, -piam, -quam, -que, -vfs; for aliquis
'someone', see s.v. alius.
Pit. interrog.: m.f.sg. nom. *kwis, acc. *kwim, ins. *kwT, n.sg. nom.acc. *kwid; nom.pl.
m.f. *kwes, acc. *kwins, n. nom.acc. *kwT(» *kwia), dat.abl. *kwifos; indef.: *kwiskwis
'whoever', *kwidkwid 'whatever'. It. cognates: Presam. VETIK; [nom.sg.], vemet;
[gen.sg.?] 'nobody', O. pis, pis, Vol. pis, U. pisi, U. pisi [nom.sg.m.f.], O. pid, Pael.

[nom.sg.n.] 'who? what?', 'every'; U. piri < +-F; U. pere, per si, persei, perse, pirsi,
pirse 'if < *pid-v, O. pidum, pidum [nom.acc.sg.n.], pieisum [gen.sg.m.] 'someone,
something' < pis + -um; U. pis her 'whoever wants'; O. pis.pis [nom.sg. m.f.], SPic.
pimpfti [acc.sg.m.f.], O. poizeipid [loc.sg.n.] 'every' or 'whichever'; Marr. nipis

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rabio, -ere 'to be frenzied, rave' [v. Ill] (Caecil.+)
Derivatives: rabies 'savageness, passion' (Ter.+), rabidus 'raging, mad' (Lucr.+),
rabidsus 'rabid, mad' (P1.+).
Pit. *rab- i *raf-.
Schrijver 1991 rejects the connection with Skt. rabh- 'to grab' and proposes to
connect ToA rapurne 'desire' and maybe Gr. Epajj.cn 'to desire, love' instead. Yet this
etymology is not ven/ convincing from the semantic side. LIV connects Parth. rf- 'to
attack', MoP rav- 'to go'. Cheung 2007 derives the latter from a root *rabh- 'to be in
violent commotion', which may be seen also in Khot. ariih- 'to move, shake' (+ *d~),
barah- (brah-) 'to soar up' and in other Iranian languages. In theory, Iranian could
reflect *(H)reb(h'1- while Latin would have a secondary full grade *rab- « *e/a/orb-
Since we are dealing with two isolated verbal stems, however, this
etymology remains uncertain. MHG reben 'to move' is isolated within Germanic, and
may belong to *reiban 'to rub'.
Bibl.: WH II: 413, EM 562, IEW 852, Schrijver 1991: 305f., LIV l.*rebh-.

content:

IE cognates: Gr. paf,, paY6<; 'grape', po^, poyoq 'id.', Alb. rrush 'resin'.
Probably a loanword from a Mediterranean language.
Bibl.: WH II: 414, EM 562, Schrijver 1991: 306.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: radius 'ray of light; spoke' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: radiosus 'radiant' (P1.+), radiatus 'radiant; with spokes' (Varro+),
radiare 'to shine' (Lucr.+). «
It is unlikely that radius belongs to radix 'root', as WH and IEW suggest: the
meaning is completely different. Initial rad- may reflect a root *(H)rHcfh)~, for which
there is no clear candidate. The element *- might be a root enlargement.
Bibl.: WH II: 414f., EM 562, IEW 1167, Schrijver 1991: 182.
radix, -Icis 'root' [f. k] (P1.+)
Derivatives: rddicitus [adv.] 'by the roots, completely' (P1.+), radicula 'a little root'
(Laev.+), eradicare 'to tear by the roots, exterminate' (P1.+).
Pit. *wrad-i-.
PIE *ur(e)h -d-iH- 'root'. IE cognates: W. gwraidd 'roots', OCo. grueiten, Bret.
2
gwrizierm 'root' < *uradio- < *urh 2 d-io-, Gr. PA5I^, -IKO<; 'branch, twig', Go. Ivaurts,
OE wyrt, MHG wurz < *urh d-i-, OIc. rot < *ureh d-; Alb. rreze, Geg rraze 'root'.
2 2
In view of the ablaut, it seems likely that PIE *-d- was a suffix, but this cannot be
ascertained.
Bibl.: WH II: 415, EM 562f., IEW 1167, Schrijver 1991: 182f., 1995: 173-175,
Sihler 1995: 179, Vine 1999b. ramus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rado, -ere 'to scrape, scratch, shave' [v. Ill; pf. rdsi, ppp. rasum] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: rostrum 'drag-hoe' (P1.+), rastellus 'wooden rake' (Varro+), ramentum
'shaving' (P1.+), ramenta 'shaving' (P1.+), rasus, -us 'shaving, scraping' (Varro),
rallum 'a scraper' (Plin.); abradere 'to rub away, knock off (Ter.+), corradere 'to
rake together, amass' (P1.+), deradere 'to scrape, shave off (Cato+), erddere 'to
scrape away, erase' (Varro+), irradere 'to scrape (onto)' (Cato); ralhis 'sheer, fine'.
Pit. *rasd-e/o~.
IE cognates: W. rhathu, Bret, rahein 'to scrape, shave' < PCI. *rasd-e/o- 'to scrape,
scratch'.
Lat. ramentum < *rad-mrallum < *rdd-lo~. The adj. ralius is used with tunica, and
it may belong here if it meant 'smoothely woven, polished' < *rad-lo-\ but note that
this is morphologically a strange formation. EM derive rallus from rarus, but then
one would rather expect *rarulus. Lat. rostrum has often been explained from
*rad-tro-, but Serbat 1975: 316 points out that the length of a is unproven, and that
rostrum could go back to *rad-tro- < *rHd-tro~. Since this word family is only found
in Italo-Celtic, a PIE origin is uncertain. If *rasd- is a secondary full grade for *rsd~,
one might consider an original root present *r(e)sd- (thus Meiser 2003); but not
*Hrsdwhich would give Lat. * Vrsd-.
Bibl.: WH II: 415, EM 563, IEW 854, Leumann 1977: 307, Schrijver 1991: 309f„
Meiser 2003: 120, Schumacher 2004: 528, LIV ?*rasd-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: raia 'a sea-fish, ray' [f. a] (Plin.)
Pit. *ragja- or *rajja~.
IE cognates: MDu. rogghe, rochghe, MoDu. rog, MLG rugge 'ray' < PGm. *rugg-,
OE reohhe (once in a gloss), ME rezge, reyhhe 'ray' < *rexj(e (or *raxxja-7).
Raia must be interpreted as Irajja!. Whether the English words for 'ray' can go back
to *raxx~ is disputed; if they do, we have an interchange a/u within Germanic. In the
absence of any further cognates and in the semantic field of animals, this could point
to a loanword from a substrate language. The similarity between the Gm. words and
raia is striking, and since Latin Ijjl can reflect *gj, we might posit *raK~, *ruK- 'ray'.
Bibl.: WH II: 415, EM 563.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ramus 'branch, twig' [m. o] (Cato+)
Derivatives: ramdsus 'with branches, branching' (Lucr.+), ramulus 'little branch'
(Cato+); rames, -itis [f.] » ramex, -icis 'the lungs; varicocele' (PI.+).
Pit. *wra(d)mo-7
PIE *ur(e)h -(d-)mo-? IE cognates: see s.v. radix.
2
Possibly from *rad-mo- < *wrad-mo~, if cognate with radix 'root'. The semantics can
be justified by referring to Gr. pa5i^, -IKOC ;'branch, twig', showing that *ur(e)h d-
2
could mean either 'branch' or 'root'. Gr. pa5apvo<; [m.] 'branch, twig' is probably not
cognate, cf. Schrijver. Of course, if the PIE root was merely *urh -, ramus can also go
2
back to *ur(e)h -mo-. The noun rames seems to be older in its f-stem appearance (PI.)
2
than as a &-stem ramex. According to EM, the lungs were called ramites 'branches'
due to the similarity of their form with the branches of a tree. I
Bibl.: WH II: 416, EM 564, IEW 1167;Schrijver 1991: 182, Sihler 1995: 209. radix

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rana 'frog' [f. a] (Varro+)
Pit. *ra-na-?
Onomatopoeic form, probably from a sound "ra". In theory, the root could be
?*reh (i)- 'to roar' as reconstructed by LIV for BSl. Since Gm. contains forms for 'to
2
roar' with a final velar (MHG ruohen, ruohelen, rueheleri), a preform *rak-sna- is
also conceivable, although the Gm. suffix can easily be secondary; and Latin rag/7
'brays' (of a foal), found in one gloss, is hardly a trustworthy comparandum.
Bibl.: WH II: 416, Efa 564, IEW 859f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ranceo 'to be rotten' [v-. II] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: rancidus 'rotten' (Lucr.+).
No etymology.
Bibl.: WH II: 417, EM 564.
rapiO, -ere 'to seize, take away' [v. Ill; pf. rapui, ppp. raptum] (Naev.; surrepsit
[s-fut.] PI.)
Derivatives: rapidus 'strong-flowing, quick' (P1.+), rapax 'greedy, rapacious,
predator' (P1.+), raptio 'carrying off (Ter.+), raptor 'robber, abductor' (P1.+),
raptare 'to carry away by force, drag off (P1.+), rapister, -tri 'robber' (Lucil.),
raptim 'in a rush' (Lucr.+), rapinator 'pillager' (Varro+); abripere 'to snatch away,
kidnap' (P1.+), arripere 'to grasp, get hold of (P1-+), corripere 'to grab, hurry off
(P1.+), deripere 'to tear off, pull down' (P1.+), dfripere 'to tear to pieces, run after'
(P1.+), eru/ipere 'to seize away' (P1-+), proripere 'to snatch forth, rush forth' (PI+),
surm/ipere, surpere 'to steal' (P1.+), subrepilcius 'stolen' (P1.+); rapsdre 'to hurry
along' (Caes.+).
Pit. *rap-i- 'to seize'.
PIE *hirp-i- [pr.] 'to seize'. IE cognates: Gr. sp&CTOpai 'to devour, snatch away' <
*rep-ie/o~, aor. 3. pi. av-ripeyavro, Alb. ijep 'tear of, away, rob', Lith. ap-ripti 'to
take, catch' (< *(H)reh,p-).
If to a PIE root *h]rep-, rapid could reflect the zero grade *h]rp- > *erp~, then with a
secondary zero grade *rap- (to full grade *rep-). Differently Schrijver 1991, who
posits a separate root *hirhip- for rapid and Lith. rdpti; yet both roots would have an
identical meaning.
Bibl.: WH II: 417, EM 564, IEW 865, Schrijver 1991: 17, 306, Rasmussen 1993:
193, Meiser 2003: 250, LIV *(h,)rep-. repens, utor

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: r5pum 'turnip' [n. o] (Cato+)
Derivatives: rapicius 'of turnips' (Cato+), rapa 'turnip' (Varro+), rapina
'turnip-crop, turnip-field' (Cato+), rapula 'small turnip' (Titin.+).
Pit. *rdpo~.
IE cognates: Gr. pcarug, pcwpuq [fi] 'turnip', paqxivoq, patpavr) 'radish', Lith. rope
'turnip', RuCS repa, OHG ruoba, ruoppa 'turnip'.
This cannot be a regular PIE word: Latin, Baltic and OHG ruoba require *aH Slavic
t
has iel, and Greek single -a-. There is no prothetic vowel in Greek, and the vacillation
between rc and (p also suggests a loanword.
Bibl.: WH II: 418, EM 564, IEW 852, Schrijver 1991: 310.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rams 'of loose structure, sparse, rare' [adj. o/a\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: raro 'seldom' (P1.+), rare 'seldom; sparsely, loosely' (PI.; Col.),
rdrenter 'seldom' (Andr.+), raritudo 'looseness, openness' (Lucr.+), rarescere 'to
thin out, open out' (Lucr.+), rarefacere 'to make less solid' (Lucr.+).
Antonym of densus. EM state that Lat. rdrenter was formed on the example of
frequenter. Rants has been connected with PIE *h2erH- 'to disappear, perish' by EM,
but this is not obvious semantically. Others connect Gr. epfjpog 'lonely'. Schrijver is
critical of all proposals: "The connection of rete and rdrus must be given up on
formal grounds, and that of rete and epfjpoi; on semantic grounds."
Bibl.: WH II: 418, EM 564, IEW 332fi, Beekes 1969: 36, Schrijver 1991: 17, 31 Of.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ratis 'collection of wooden beams, raft; boat, ship' [f. /] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: ratTtus 'bearing the figure of a raft' (Lucil.+).
Pit. *rati~.
PIE *h]rhti- [fi] 'rowing'?
r
Uncertain. Ratis has been connected with Lat. retae 'trees in the bed of a stream'
and/or with OHG ruota, OIc. roSa 'rod, pole', OE rod, but neither is semantically
I
1
ravus 515
compelling. If cognate, this etymology would imply *Hrh ti- > Lat. rat is. Since the
}
PIE root for 'to row' is reconstructed as *hirh]- (see s.v. remits), ratis might be
interpreted as *Hrh-ti- 'the rowing' > 'raft, boat'; note that rafts are normally
l
propelled and steered by means of a pole.
Bibl.: WH II: 420, EM 565, IEW 866, Schrijver 1991: 306f„ LIV 2.*h,reh. remus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: raudus, -eris 'rough piece, lump; bronze coin' [n. r] (Lucil.+; var. rodits, rudus,
roudus Paul, ex F.)
Derivatives: raudusculum 'bronze coin' (Varrof).
IE cognates: Skt. lohd- [m.] 'reddish metal', MP roy 'copper, brass' < PIE
*h|roudh-o-, OCS ruda, Ru. ruda, Scr. ruda 'ore' < PIE *h]roudh-h -, OIc. rauSi 'red
2
iron ore'.
Because of -au- and intervocalic -d-, raudus cannot be an inherited word from PIE
*h]r(e/o)udh-os 'red'; we would expect Lat. *rt1bus. Raudus is probably a loanword
from another IE language, in which *-cf- became The other IE languages show
that the colour 'red' was used to refer to reddish ore, whether copper or iron or
another metal.
Bibl: WH II: 420f., EM 565, IEW 872f., Schrijver 1991: 265. robur, ruber, rufus

raviare 'to be hoarse' (P1.+), rausurus 'about to become hoajse' (Lucil.).
Pit. *rawo~.
PIE *h rouH-o- 'roaring'. IE cognates: Skt. ruvati [pr.], rauti [pr.], rava- [pr.] 'to
3

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: roar', aor. drdvit\ rava- [m.] 'roaring, howling, noise', YAv. uruuato [gen.sg.]
'roaring', Khwar. rw- 'to grunt', Gr. wpuopai 'to howl, roar, complain', OCS rjuti 'to
howl'.
If raucus reflects *rawVko-, all forms can go back to Pit. *rawo- or *rowo~. Schrijver
dismisses raviare (or ravirel) from the evidence since it occurs only once in PI. in a
corrupt passage. For *rawo- or *rowo~, he considers two possible etymologies: (1) to
a root *Hrehj- 'to Ishout, sound' (Skt. rayati 'barks', OIc. ramr 'hoarse') as
*Hrhj-uo-, or to a root *Hreu- 'to roar' (Skt. rauti, ruvati 'to roar', OE reon 'to wail')
as *Hrou-o-. The latter form yields a better connection with forms outside Italic, and a
better explanation for -a-. Vine 2006a: 237 therefore reconstructs *h rouH-o-
3
'roaring' > *rawo~, whence the abstract *rawi- would be derived. Raucus could then
reflect *rawi-ko~.
Bibl.: WH II: 420f., EM 565, IEW 867f, Leumann 1977: 340, Schrijver 1991: 257f.,
446, LIV *h reuH-.
3

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: re-, red- 'back, reverse' [pref.] (red- in front of vowels and h-)
Derivatives: retro [adv, prep.] 'backwards, to the rear' (Ter.+); reciprocus 'moving
backwards and forwards, moving in the opposite direction' (Acc.+), reciprocare 'to
move a thing backwards and forwards' (Enn.+), reciprocicornis 'with the horns
turned around' (Lab.).
Pit. *wre. It. cognates: U. re- 'again' in restatu, revestu.
PIE *ure 'back'. IE cognates: Alb. rre- [prev.] 'back'; Ru. rak, gen.sg. raka, SCr.
rak, Sin. rak < PS1. *rakb 'crayfish, lobster' < *ttre-h]kwo- 'looking backwards'
(Klingenschmitt 2004).
The only acceptable etymology has recently been proposed by Klingenschmitt 2004,
who reconstructs *ure 'back', The form red- is analogical to prod- beside pro-, and
from metanalysis in verbs such as reddo < *re~di-do. Dunkel 1979 explains reciprocus
as a nominalization of *re-kwe *pro-kwe 'both backwards and forwards', and connects
it with other Skt. and Gr. instances of two semantically opposed preverbs coordinated
by *-kwe 'and'. The first labiovelar was apparently delabialised in front of the
following p and *kn; if Klingenschmitt's etymology of re as *ure is correct, there
would even have been a fourth labial consonant in the word *ure-kwe *pro-kwe. The
resulting *(u)reke-prokwo- did not yield *recupricus because its two elements were for
a long time recognised as two separate words, so that they developed as *reke-proko-
until after vowel weakening (Cowgill apud Dunkel 1979: 189).
Bibl.: WH II: 422, 424, EM 565f, Leumann 1977: 340, 559f„ Dunkel 1979,
Klingenschmitt 2004: 247-252. procul

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: recens, -ntis 'of recent origin, new, fresh' [adj. nt\ (PI.+)
Pit. *wre-kent(i)-.
PIE *ken-t- 'young, new'. IE cognates: Mir. cana, cano 'wolf cub'; Gaul.
Cintu-gnatus, Olr. cet- 'first', MW cynt, MCo. kens, Bret, kent 'earlier' < PCI.
*kento/u-; Skt. kanyd-, A v. kaine, kairiin- 'girl' < kaniHn- < PIE *ken-i-Hn- (or
*keniH-n-7), Skt. kamna- 'young', Gr. Kaivoq 'new, unheard of < *kn-io~; ORu. kont
[m.] 'start', OCS is-koni 'from the start' < *kon-; maybe Go. hindumists 'last', OHG
hintana, hintar 'behind', OE hindema 'last' < *ken-t-.
Probably a compound of re- 'again, back' and *kent(i)~ 'young, first'.
Bibl.: WH II: 423, EM 566, IEW 563f.
redimiO, -Ire 'to encircle with a garland, surround' [v. IV] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: redimiculum 'a band to the back of a woman's head-dress' (P1.+).
Pit *do/em-o/i- 'binding, band'.
PIE *do/emh -o/i-. IE cognates: see s.v. domare.
2
The isolated position of this word makes it difficult to establish the etymology. It has
been connected with Skt ^am- < PIE *iem-, in which case we must analyse the word
as *red-im~, and suppose a zero-grade form *im-i- 'binding, garland', or reduction of
*-iem- in non-initial syllable to -im-. But the original meaning of yam- seems to have
been 'to reach, obtain', which makes a connection more difficult. EM note that
redimiculum is attested earlier than redimio, and suggest that it was based on
amiculum 'cloak', prefixed with red- and with analogical lengthening of the vowel in
front of -culum. Yet the use of red- in nominal cp. is secondary to verbal compounds
(see s.v. re-), and lengthening to -i- in this position is not trivial. Also, the difference
in dates of attestation is not decisive (thus also Serbat 1975: 185). Alternatively, Latin
-/- in redimio may reflect any short vowel in open syllable, which makes a connection
with domare 'to tame' possible. If re-dimire is a denominative to a noun *dom-o/i- or
maybe *dem-o/i- 'binding, band', it can be explained through re...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rgmus 'oar' [m. o] ( Andr.+)
Derivatives: remex, -igis 'oarsman, rower' (P1.+), remigium 'outfit of oars; rowing'
(P1.+); biremis 'having oars arranged in pairs' (Sis.+), triresmos [acc.pl.f.] (CIL 1.25,
Columna Rostrata), triremis (Caes.+) 'having three oars', [quinqueresmjos 'ship with
oars arranged in five' (CIL 1.25), septerfesmom 'ship with oars arranged in seven'
(CIL 1.25).
Pit. *re(s)mo- 'oar*.
PIE *hireh(s)mo- 'oar'. IE cognates: Olr. raid, •ra 'to row' < *rd-ie- <
r
*hiroh ie/o-; Skt. aritdr- 'rower, ferryman', aritra- [n.] 'oar' < *hierh]-ter-, -tro-, Gr.
r
eperr)^ [m.] 'rower', epeoato 'to row', ep&rpov 'oar', Lith. irti 'to row', irklas 'oar';
OIc. roa, OE rowan, MHG ruejen 'to row', OHG ruodar, OE rodor 'rudder' <
*h]rohi-.
The forms in -resmo- from the Columna Rostrata, an inscription from 260 BC which
was probably renewed in the first century AD (Diehl 1964: 36) seems to show that
remus reflects *resmo-. On the other hand, the same inscription has primes 'first' and
not *prismos. This is one of the reasons for assuming that the whole inscription is
archaizing (cf. Leumann 1977: 7), in which case -resmo- is uncertain evidence. If the
s is old, we may reconstruct *h reh smo- 'oar' (thus Schrijver 1991: 19). This has the
1 r
disadvantage of schwebe-ablaut when compared to Skt. aritar-, but it would fit the
place of the o-ablaut in Germanic and Celtic, which may well be an iterative o-grade.
If the s of CIL 1.25 -resmo- is hypercorrect, remus may reflect *h reh mo-. Since the
1 r
other IE languages show instrument nouns in *-tio- or *-tro- (maybe remade to
*-tmo- in Greek), I have a slight preference for *-smo- in Latin, since this is more
typically an instrumental suffix. Lat. remex, remigium contain (the root of) ago 'to
do'.
Bibl.: WH II: 428, EM 569, IEW 338, Leumann 1977: 7, 209, Schumacher 2004:
529, LIV 2.*h,reh. ratis
r

PIE *h r-en, -en- 'kidney'? *sren- 'loins'? IE cognates: Olr. am [f. n\ 'kidney,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: gland', W. arenn [f.J 'kidney, testicle' < PCI. *aron~; Hit.
'lung(s), midriff [nom.sg.c. hahris, nom.sg.n. hahri, hahhari, dat.-loc.sg. hahrisni] ;
ToB arance 'heart' (both the physical heart and the seat-of the emotions), ToA arinc
' id.' < PTo. * arance < PIE * A^A^ri- (EIEC 329). :
Mastrelli 1979 connects renes with OPr. straunay, Lith. strinos [f.pl.] 'loins', dial,
also srinos, Latv. siriena 'loins' < Proto-Baltic *srin-7, Av. rdna- 'thigh' < *srana-?
< *sren- 'hip, loins' (IEW 1002). The semantic shift from 'loins' to 'kidneys' is quite
conceivable. Less certain seems the connection with Gr. pa^u; 'spine', since there is
evidence for a PGr. anlaut cluster *ur-. Alternatively, one could connect the Celtic
words for 'kidney'. The etymology as unreduplicated *h r-en- next to PCI. *aron-
2
from reduplicated *h e-h r-en- is possible in theory, but the difference in
2 2
reduplication remains unexplained.
Bibl.: WH II: 4?8f., EM 569f, Driessen 2003b: 358.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: reor, rerl 'to think, believe' [v. II; ppp. ratus 'constitutional, determined'] (P1.+)
Derivatives: ratio 'calculation, account, reason' (P1.+), ratiuncula 'small account'
(P1.+), irritus 'not ratified, unrealized' (P1.+).
Pit. *re-(je/o~) 'to think', *rato- 'thought, counted'.
PIE *Hreh [aor.?], *Hrhto- [ppp.]. IE cognates: Go. rapjo 'number, account',
r r
ga-rapancc 'counted', OS rethia, OHG radja, redea 'accountability'.
The original meaning 'to count' was preserved in ratus 'counted' > 'legal', esp. in
expressions with pars: pro rata parte 'ifl proportion' (Cic.+), rata pars 'a fixed
proportion'. The noun ratio was derived from ratus, either directly (cf. the model
natus : natio) or via an earlier //-abstract *ratis 'reckoning'. The PIE root *Hreh
r
might also be the basis of PIE *(H)reh,dh- ' to bring in order, arrange'. Schrijver
ascribes the root to PIE *h r- 'to fix'; another candidate would be *hireh 'to ask,
2 r
investigate' (Hit. ar(iie/a)-zl 'to consult an oracle' < *hirhie/o-), for which LIV
r
reconstructs a root aorist.
Bibl.: WH II: 419, 429, EM 570, IEW 59, Leumann 1977: 366, Schrijver 1991: 140,
307, LIV 2.*reh]-.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: repens, -ntis 'sudden, unexpected' [adj. nt] (Turp.+)
Derivatives: repente 'suddenly, at once' (P1.+), derepente 'suddenly' (P1.+),
repentTnus 'sudden, without warning' (P1.+).
Pit. *rep-nt~.
PIE *h,rep-nt- [aor.ptc.] 'seizing'.
WH, IEW and Leumann assume *urep~ to the root *uerp~ 'to twist', positing 'with a
sudden move' as the original semantics. This has the disadvantage of schwebe-ablaut
in the root, so that an alternative solution would be welcome. EM consider the
preverb re- plus a root *pen-t- to pendo, but the semantics are unclear, and the root
enlargement *-t- is ad hoc. I am more sympathetic towards a connection with rapid, at
least as far as the semantics are concerned; for a reconstruction, see s.v. rapid.
Bibl.: WH II: 429, EM 570, IEW 1156, Leumann 1977: 190. rapid
r£po, -ere 'to crawl, creep' [v. Ill; pf. repsT, ppp. reptum] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: reptare 'to crawl, move furtively'; adrepere 'to creep up to' (Varro+),
conrepere 'to move stealthily' (PI.+), derepere 'to crawl down' (Varro+), erepere 'to
creep out' (P1.+), obrepere 'to creep up, sneak in' (P1.+), obreptare 'to creep up'
(P1.+).
Pit. *rep-e/o~.
PIE *hjrehip- or *h]rep- 'to creep'. IE cognates: Lith. replioti 'to creep', roploti,
Latv. rapat, rapt 'to creep'; OHG rebo, reba, repa, MHG rebe 'offshoot, bud', MLG
winrave 'vine'.
Bibl.: WH II: 430, EM 570f., IEW 865, Schrijver 1991: 140, Meiser 2003: 120, LIV
?*reh,p-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: res, ret 'property, goods, thing, affair' [f. e] (Lex XII+; acc.sg. rem)
Derivatives: recula 'small amount' (P1.+).
Pit. *re-. It. cognates: U. ri [dat.sg.], ri [abl.sg.], reper [abl.sg. + -per] 'thing,
interest'.
PIE *Hreh]-i- 'wealth, goods' (nom.sg. *rehi-i-s, acc.sg. *rehi-i-m, dat.sg. *rehi-ei)
r
. IE cognates: Skt. rayi- [m. (rarely f.)] 'property, goods' (rayim [acc.sg.], rayah
[gen.abl.sg.]), Av. raii-i- 'wealth' (OAv. raiio [gen.abl.sg.], YAv. raem [acc.sg.]).
The nom.sg. *rehiis would have become Lat. *reis > *ris, the acc.sg. *rehi-m >
r
*rim. The dat.sg. *rehiiei > *reiei > rei is regarded as the regular reflex by Meiser
1998; one might also adduce the gen.sg. *rehi-i-os (if with full grade root) > *rejos
» *rej-T. The new stem *rej~ would yield an acc. *rejem > *rem, and this in turn
may have prompted a new nom.sg. res. The root may be the same as the root
*(H)reh of the Ilr. aor. *ra~s- 'to bestow'.
r
Bibl.: WH II: 430fi, EM 571, IEW 860, Schrijver 1991: 140, 379, Sihier 1995: 341,
Meiser 1998: 148, Untermann 2000: 635, LIV l.*reh. -»• reus
r

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: restis 'rope, cord' [f. /] (P1.+)
Derivatives: restio 'dealer in rope' (P1.+), resticula 'small cord' (Cato+).
Pit. *reskti- 'rope'.
PIE *Hresg-ti- 'rope, cord'. IE cognates: Skt. rajju- [f.] 'rope, string', Sogd. ryzy
(Irayzif) '(woollen?) cloth' < Ilr. *Hrazgu- / *Hrazju-, Lith. rezgu, rezgiu 'to braid,
bind', OLith. rekstis 'basket'.
Bibl.: WH II: 431, EM 571f., IEW 874, LIV *resg-.
rete / retis 'net' [n./f„ m. i] (P1.+)
Derivatives: reticulum 'small net' (Varro), reticulatus 'covered with a net, like a net'
(Varro+). . ,
Pit. *reti~. '• •
/
Maybe original m. * retis, pl.n. *retia; thus Niedermann apud EM 572. The sg. rete
would then be a back-formation. Schrijver argues that the proposed connection with
rams must be given up on formal grounds. If BS1. forms such as Lith. retas 'rare,
thin, slow', retis 'sieve', resti, Is. rentii 'to become rare', Latv. rgns 'standing apart',
OCS redbkb 'rare' (< BS1. *reto~, *ret~, *re?d-) are cognate, we would posit a root
*ret~, not *reh t- (Slav, has *-d~). But in view of the isolated position and the
}
exclusively nominal meanings, rete/-is might wel be a loanword. Rosen 1995
suggests borrowing from a Semitic source, cf. Biblical Hebrew reset 'net (for fishing
and bird-catching)', Ugaritic rdt '(probably) id.'.
Bibl.: WH II: ^31, EM 572, IEW 332f., Schrijver 1991: 17f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: reus 'party in a lawsuit; defendant' [m. o] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: rea [fi] 'female defendant'.
Pit. *rei-wo- 'of wealth' » 'of a case'?
PIE *Hreh]-i-unt- 'possessing wealth'?
WH supports the ancient connection with res 'case' and posits *re-i-o- 'connected
with a court case', whereas EM term the semantic connection with res weak. I do not
share their pessimism. For a suffix *-o- to have this derived meaning, the derivation
must be very old, which makes the etymology *re-i-o- somewhat less attractive. But
one may also posit a suffix *-uo~, whence *rehii-uo- > *reiwo- > *rewo- > reus, with
the same phonotactics as in deus 'god' < *deiwos. Since *-uo- does not appear to
function as a productive denominal suffix in Latin, *reh i-uo- might be a remake of
}
*rehji-uent- as attested in Skt. rayi-vant- and Av. raeuuant- 'rich'; its meaning would
have been renewed within Latin.
Bibl.: WH II: 432, EM 572, IEW 860, Schrijver 1995: 186, LIV l.*reh. res
r
•

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: r5x, r6gis 'king' [m. g] (VOLat.+: Forum cippus recei 'regi', re[x] 'rex')
Derivatives: reguhts 'petty king' (Varro+), regalis 'of a king' (Naev.+), regius 'of a
king, by a king' (Andr.+), regia 'royal residence, capital' (Acc.+), reglna 'queen'
(Andr.+), regillus 'of the queen' (PI. Epid. 223 acc.sg.f. regillam); regificus 'royal,
sumptuous' (Enn.+); regnum 'kingship, reign, kingdom' (Naev.+), regnare 'to rule as
a king' (P1.+), regnator 'who rules as a king' (Naev.+).
Pit. *reg- 'king'. It. cognates: maybe Fal. rex [nom.sg.] (uncertain reading).
PIE *h reg-s, *h reg-m [m.] 'ruler, king'. IE cognates: Olr. ri (gen.sg. rig) [m.]
3 3
'king', rige 'kingdom' (= Skt. rajya- [n.] 'rule, reign'); Skt. raj- 'king' (nom.sg. rat),
OAv. razar- / razan- [n.] 'rule, direction' (YAv. only razar-); YAv. bdrazi-raz-
'reigning in the height'.
Nowicki 2002: 337ff. argues that regma is based on an adi. *reg-Tno- 'regal, meant
for the king'. The adj. regillus would be a derivative of reglna.
Bibl.: WH II: 432, EM 572, IEW 854-857, Schrijver 1991: 121, Meiser 1998: 57,
LIV *h reg-.
3

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rldeo 'to laugh' [v. II; pf. risi, ppp. risum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: ridibundus 'in a state of laughter' (P1.+), risio 'laughing' (PI.), rlsits, -us
'laughter' (P1.+), ridiculus 'funny, absurd' (P1.+), ridicularius 'concerned with jokes'
(P1.+), risitare 'to laugh repeatedly at' (Laev.); adridere 'to smile at' (P1.+), derTdere
'to laugh at' (P1.+), derxdiculum 'a ridiculous thing' (PI.+), irrxdere 'to make fun of
(PI.+), irridiculum 'laughing-stock' (P1.+), conrxdere 'to laugh together' (P1.+),
subrxdere 'to smile' (Varro+).
The old connection with Skt. vrxdate 'is shy' is semantically gratuitous, and this word
has no good PIE etymology, cf. EWAia HI: 482f.
Bibl: WH II: 433f., EM 573, IEW 1158, Leumann 1977: 190.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rigeo 'to be stiff [v. II] (Cic.+)
Derivatives: rigor 'stiffness, cold' (Lucr.+), rigidus 'stiff, inflexible' (Enn.+).
Pit. *rig-e~.
PIE *Hrig-eh 'to be stretched, be stiff. IE cognates: Olr. rigid, -rig* 'to stretch,
r
rule', OBret. diren 'to lead' < *rige/o- 'to stretch', Olr. do-rig* 'to lay bare', MW
gwarwy 'to play', MBret. aeren 'to tie' < PCI. *rig-e/o- 'to tie', Olr. ringid* 'to
torture' < *ringe/o-; OHG reihhon, reihhen, OE rofc(e)an 'to reach, achieve' <
*roig-eie~.
IEW derives riged from regd 'to stretch, rule', in which case 'stretch' would have
been the earlier meaning, and the vowel rig- would have developed in non-initial
syllable. This seems unlikely, especially since rigidus is attested earlier than rigeo and
rigor. WH derived rigeo from *(s)rig-, the root that also yields frigus 'cold': the
original meaning would have been 'stiff from the cold'. But since its root is
reconstructed as *sriHg-, this would leave short rig- 'stiff unexplained. LIV connects
two verbs *reig- 'to stretch, bind', which are probably just one PIE verb, as
Schumacher argues. If the original meaning was'to stretch', we can posit a stative
verb *rig-e- 'to be stretched, be stiff.
Bibl.: WH II: 434, EM 573, IEW 854-857, Schumacher 2004: 543-548, LIV *reig-,
*rei(g-.
n
>

metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rigo 'to irrigate, make wet' [v. I] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: irrigare 'to make wet, irrigate' (P1.+), jirriguus 'flooded, drenched'
(P1.+), irrigivus 'well-watered' (Cato), irrigdtid 'irrigation' (Varro+).
Pit. *rig-a- 'to lead toward'. It. cognates: maybe O. regaturei [dat.sg.] m. epithet of
Iuppiter, *rega-tor- ?
PIE *Hrig- 'to stretch'. IE cognates: see s.v. rigeo.
Quite clearly, the form *in-rigare is older than rigare. Hence, it is conceivable that
the verbal root was that of rego 'to lead*: *in-rigare 'to lead water onto (the fields)'
(thus Panagl 1991). The a-conjugation can be denominal to a noun *in-re/ogo-
'drainage channel' (vel sim.), but maybe it was built by a productive process to regere
like occupdre tol capere. Connecting Alb. rrjedh, one might posit a different root
*reg- 'to flow', as LIV does; but this does not change the problem of Latin -/-.
Finally, it is conceivable that ir-rigare belongs to the root *rig- 'to stretch' reflected
in rigeo. Semantically this is quite satisfactory; it would then be a fairly old
(frequentative) derivative *in-rig-d- 'to lead into'. This etymology yields the most
straightforward explanation of -i-.
Bibl.: WH II: 435, EM 573f, IEW 857, Sihler 1995: 211, Untermann 2000: 631,
LIV ?*reg-. —• rego, rigeo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rima 'narrow cleft, crack' [f. a] (Cato+)
Derivatives: rimarefi 'to examine, scrutinize' (Pac.+).
Several etymologies are theoretically possible. To the root *h]reik- 'to tear, break'
(see s.v. rixa), a preform *h reik-sm-hr 'breach, fissure' would yield rima; but also
}
*reiPmd-, *reidmaand roots in *wr- would lead to the same Latin form. For
*wreid~, cf. Gm. *wrTtanan 'to write'. Thus, the etymology remains unclear.
Bibl.: WH II: 435, EM 574, IEW 857-859. rixa
t
ringor, -I 'to show the teeth' [v. Ill; ppp. rictumJ (Ter.+)
Derivatives: rictus, -us 'the open mouth' (Pompon.+).
Pit. *wreng-e/o~.
PIE *ureng- 'to turn, curve'. IE cognates: OE wrencan 'to turn', OHG bi-renken 'to
sprain' < *urong-eie~.
If ringor is from *rengor, it can be connected with Gm. forms continuing a root
*ur(e)ng~. The ppp. rictus must then have secondary -/-. The meaning would be
derived from 'to curve one's lips, draw up the nose' = showing the teeth.
Bibl.: WH II: 436, EM 574, IEW 1154, LIV *ureng-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rltus, -us 'religious observances, rites'[m. u] (P1.+)
Derivatives: rite 'with the proper rites, duly' (P1.+).
Pit. *reitu~, *reiti- 'rite'.
PIE *h r-(e)i- 'to count, observe carefully'. IE cognates: Olr. renaid*, -ren 'to sell,
2
exchange' < PCI. *rina- < *h ri-n-H-; Olr. rim, W. rhif 'number' (maybe borrowed
2
from Gm. according to Schrijver 1991); Gr. apnSjioq 'number, payment', vrjprax;
'countless'; Old Norwegian rim [n.] 'account', OHG rim [m.] 'row, number'.
According to EM, final -e of rite is always short, which would point to a stem *riti- or
*rTt~. Since root nouns are rare in Latin (except in compounds), the former seems
more likely. It has been suggested that the root is laryngeal-final, but it is impossible
to explain the Greek forms in in that case. Germanic could have *h rei-m~. A root
2
*h ri- is not recognized by LIV, but in view of the meaning, we could analyse it as the
2
root *h er- 'to join, arrange' with a suffix *-/-, which came to mean 'to count'. Thus,
2
ritu- may be cognate with artus 'limb, member'.
Bibl.: WH II: 437, EM 574, IEW 60, Schrijver 1991: 22, Rasmussen 1989: 102,
Schumacher 2004: 55If., LIV I.*h er-. artus
2

content:

inscr.)
Derivatives: rivaiis 'who shares the use of a stream; rival' (Naev.+), rivulus 'small
stream' (Varro+); derivdre 'to divert, pass on' (Ter.+).
Pit. *rlwo~ 'stream'.
PIE *h riH-uo- 'whirling'. IE cognates: Mir. nan 'river, sea' < *reino-; Skt. pr.
3
rfyate 'to flow (producing whirlpools), whirl', rindti 'to make flow, make run', rit-
'flowing, dripping', riti- [fi] 'stream, motion', YAv. aifii.iritim ah- 'to defile with
dirt'; auul.Ariti 'crapped on', airiti- [fi] 'crapping on'; Gr. optvra, Lesb. opiwct) 'to
whirl, stir', Ru. rejat' 'to stream fast, flow' < PIE *h roiB~, Ru. rinutCz. Hnouti se
3
'to stream, flow' < PIE *h r(e)iH-\ OE rid 'stream, brook', OHG rinnan 'to drip'.
3
Steinbauer (1989: 35) interprets the sequence riuois at the end of the second line in
the Duenos inscription as Vi'vis* = 'with streams'. If this is correct (Eichner 1993a:
211, 214 slightly differently translates 'Duftstrome'), long T was original and does not
go back to a Pit. diphthong.
Bibl.: WH II: 437fi, EM 574, IEW 326-332, Schrijver 1991: 24, Meiser 1998: 4,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: robur, -oris 'oak-tree; strength' [n. r] (Cato+; nom.acc.sg. robus Cato, robor Varro)
Derivatives: robus tus 'made of oak; strong' (Cato+), robor are 'to give strength to'
(Varro+); robosem [acc.sg.] (Paul, ex F.).
Pit. *roupos [n.] 'red substance'.
PIE *h!reudh-os, -es- [n.]. IE cognates: Gr. epEutfoq 'redness'.
The inner part of the oak was apparently referred to as 'red'. The vowel o must be
interpreted as a non-Roman dialectal trait, showing that the word was borrowed from
the surrounding dialect area. The form robosem shows an animate s-stem in -os-; or is
it a pseudo-archaism?
Bibl.: WH II: 439, EM 575, IEW 872f., Stiiber 2002: 140f. robus, ruber
rObus 'kind of red' [adj. o/a] (Juv.+)
Derivatives: robus 'kind of wheat' (Col.), robigd 'rust, rust-like coating' (P1.+),
robigindsus 'rusty' (PI.+), Robigus 'deity supposed to avert rust from crops' (Varro),
Robigalia [n.pl.] 'festival in honour of Robigus' (Varro+).
Pit. *roupo- 'red'.
PIE *h]re/oudh-o- 'red'. IE cognates: see s.v. rufus.
A dialectal form (with o instead of u) of the adj. found in rufus.
Bibl.: WH II: 438fi, EM 575, IEW 872fi, Sihier 1995: 141. raudus, rufus
rOdo, -ere 'to gnaw, erode' [v. Ill; pf. rosi, ppp. rosum] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: rostrum 'snout, beak; speakers' platform in Rome' (P1.+), rostratus
'having a beak, beak-shaped' (Enn.+); abrqdere 'to gnaw off (Varro+), obrddere 'to
gnaw at, chew' (PI.), praerddere 'to bite away the end' (P1.+).
Pit. *(w)rdde/o-.
PIE *Hreh d-e/o- or *ureh d-e/o- 'to gnaw, furrow'. IE cognates: Hit. ardu- 'to saw'
3 3
< *Hrh d-u-?, ardal(a)- [n.] 'a saw' (< *-duol~); Skt. radati [pr.], rardda [3s.pf.] 'to
3
dig, furrow, open the way', radita- 'gnawed, bitten', YAv. rddaiti 'to prepare (the
way)', Khot. varrad- (< *ava-rad-a-) 'to scratch'; or OE wrotan 'to dig, rummage',
OIc. rota 'to stir, upset', OHG mozen 'to rummage', OE wrot, MHG ruozel, ruezel
'trunk'.
The root etymology is uncertain. Schrijver connects Skt. radati, which may ref...

rOs, roris 'dew' [m. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: roscidus 'wet with dew, dewy' (Varro+), rorulentus 'wet with dew'
(Cato+), rorare 'to shed moisture, drip' (Varro+); rorifer 'bringing light rain or dew'
(Lucr.+). .
Pit. *rds.
PIE *hiros, *h]ros- 'dew, juice'. IE cognates: Skt. rasa- [m.] 'juice, liquid', YAv.
rarjha- [f.] 'name of a mythical stream', Sogd. r'k 'vein, blood vessel' (< PIr.
*raha-ka~), Lith. rasa, OCS rosa 'dew'; Hit. drsI ars- 'to flow', arsanu-a 'to let
flow', CLuw. arssiia- 'to flow' < *hi(e)rs-, Skt. drsati 'to stream, flow'.
If the word for 'dew' is indeed derived from 'to stream', we must assume
schwebe-ablaut. The resemblance in form and meaning is too great to reject this
etymology.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rota 'wheel' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: rotula 'small wheel' (P1.+), rotundus 'round' (Cato+).
Pit. *rot-a- 'wheel', *re/ot-e/o- 'to run, roll'.
PIE *Hrot-o/h2- [adj.] 'revolving' > 'wheel'; *Hret-e/o- 'to run, roll'. IE cognates:
Olr. reithid*, •reith, OW OBret. redec, MCo. resek 'to run, flow' < PCI. *rete/o~,
Lith. rotas 'wheel, circle, ring, (pi.) cart', Latv. rats 'wheel, (pi.) cart', OHG rad
'wheel' [n.]; Skt. rat ha-, YAv. rada- [m.] 'chariot' < *(H)rot-h -o-.
2
The noun rota can be regarded as the f. of the original adj. 'revolving'. Lat. rotundus
may have been derived directly from the pr. *rete/o- (with replacement of *ret- by
*rot- under the influence of rota) or from an o-grade pf. *(re-)rot- 'to roll', which
disappeared from the language afterwards.
Bibl.: WH II: 443f., EM 577f., IEW 866, Leumann 077: 331, Sihler 1995: 625,
Meiser 1998: 228, Schumacher 2004: 538-541, LIV *ret-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ruber, -bra, -brum 'red' [adj. o/a] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: rubere 'to be red' (Lucr.+), rubicundus 'ruddy, reddish' (P1.+), rubidus
'reddish'? (uncertain meaning) (P1.+), rubor 'redness' (Acc.+), rubrica 'red ochre,
red clay' (P1.+), rubricdsus 'rich in red clay' (Cato+); russus 'red, red-haired'
(Enn.+), russescere 'to turn red' (Enn.); rutilus 'of a warm or glowing red colour,
ruddy' (P1.+), rutildre 'to glow with a red colour' (Acc.+).
Pit. *rupro- 'red', *rufto- 'red'. It. cognates: U. rufru [acc.pl.m.], rufra [acc.pl.f.],
rufrer [gen.sg.m.] 'red'.
PIE *h,rudh-ro- [adj.] 'red'. IE cognates: Skt. rudhira- 'red, bloody', Gr. epoiSpo^,
RuCS rodrt, ToB ratre 'red'; Olr. rondaid*, roind* 'to paint red' < PCI. *runde/o-.
Hill 2003: 224f. explains russus from *rucf-to~ to rubeo, because thematization of an
5-stem is expected to yield *nlsus < *reudh-s-o-. The verbal use of the root *rud1- in
Italo-Celtic is also apparent from PCI. *ru-n-d-e/o- 'to paint red'. There is no good
explanation for rutilus.
Bibl.: WH II: 444f., 455, EM 578, IEW 872f., Leumann 1977: 171, Schrijver 1991:
18, Untermann 2000: 637f., Schumacher 2004: 553. —»robur, robus, rufus

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rudis 'unwrought, not cultivated, crude' [adj. i] (Varro+)
Derivatives: rudus, -eris [n.] 'broken stones, rubble' (Lucil.+), rudectus 'full of
small stones' (Cato+).
There are two competing etymologies. The connection with OHG grioz 'sand, gravel'
and Lith. grudas 'corn' would presuppose the disappearance of initial in Latin,
which is questionable. Most handbooks prefer a derivation from the word for 'red'.
This has been argued most extensively by Risch 1979, who suggests that rudis might
continue the PIE /-stem adj. *h]rudh-i- taken from compounds. Yet there is not a
shimmer of a meaning Ted' in rudis or in rudus 'rubble', so that the supposed shift
from 'crude (meat)' > 'crude' rests in the air.
Bibl.: WH II: 447f., EM 579, IEW 868-871, Leumann 1977: 73, Risch 1979: 713,
Sihier 1995: 158. —* raudits, ruber, rufus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rddo, -ere 'to roar, bellow' [v. Ill; pf. rudivT] (Varro+)
Pit. *roud-/*rud~.
PIE *h r(e)ud-H- [pr.] 'to weep, roar'. IE cognates: Skt. rudanti [3p.act.], rodisi
3
[2s.act.] 'to weep, bewail', OAv. raosta [aor.], uruddiiata [pr.] 'laments'; OLith.
rausti 'weeps', Lith. rauda 'lamentation', raudoti 'to weep, lament', OE reotan 'to
complain, weep'.
The reconstruction of the (suffix?) *-H- is based on Skt. rodi$i. In view of Skt. ruvati
'roar', Gr. 6puo|iai 'to howl, roar', Lat. rumor < *h ru-, final *-d- may have been a
3
suffix, and the initial laryngeal may be *h -.
3
Bibl.: WH II: 447, EM 579, IEW 867fi, LIV *reudH-. ravus, rumor

de Vaan Latin Etymology: rufus 'red, tawny, red-haired' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: rufulus 'reddish' (P1.+).
Pit. *roupo- 'red'. It. cognates: U. rofu [acc.pl.m.], rofa [acc.pl.fi] 'red'.
PIE *h,roudh-o- 'red'. IE cognates: Olr. ruad, OW rud, MW rhudd, Co. rud, OBret.
rud, Bret, ruz 'red' < PCI. *roudo~, Lith. raudas, Latv. raiids 'reddish brown, bay,
reddish', Ru. rudyj (dial.) 'blood-red', rudoj (dial.) 'red, red-haired, dirty', Go. roups
Ted'.
A borrowing from a Sabellic language with Pit. *-p- > -/-.
