content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: simpu(v)ium 'earthenware ladle used in religious ceremonies' [n. o] (Acta Arvalia,
Varro+)
Derivatives: simpulum 'earthenware ladle' (Plin.+).
IE cognates: Gr. CITTUT] (-ua) 'box for keeping flour and bread'.
Leumann suggests that simpulum is a remodelling of earlier simpu(v)ium, which
seems likely. If so, the p in simp- cannot be explained from anaptyxis in *sem-lo-, and
the etymological connection with the root *semH- 'to scoop' (thus Meiser 1998) must
be dismissed. The form and meaning of Gr. OUTUT] are close enough to suggest
borrowing from Greek, or from a Semitic language, from which Greek also took the
word. Adoption into the Latin vocabulary would account for the introduction of the
nasal.
Bibl.: WH II: 540f., EM 627, Leumann 1977: 136, Meiser 1998: 81. sentma

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sincerus 'sound, whole, pure' [adj. o/a] (P1.+) j
Derivatives: sinceris 'pure' (Varro+).
Pit. *sm-kair/so- 'whole, intact'. t
PIE *keh i-r/so- 'whole'? IE cognates: see s.v. caelum.
2
Has often been connected with procerus, but the semantics hardly allow for this
possibility (cf. Hill 2006: 194). The /-stem variant sinceris is argued to be old by
Hiltbrunner 1958: 114f. Formally, sincerus cannot be explained from a dissimilation
of r's, as is the case with procerus. Hiltbrunner 1958: 146-54 returns to an old
suggestion by Corssen from 1868 to connect caeri-mdnia 'purity', and posits an
earlier adj. *caerus 'whole, intact'. Indeed, this seems semantically very attractive,
and formally, it is without problems. The adj. could be Pit. *kairo- or *kaiso-; in
either case, we1 are reminded of *keh i-lo- as attested in PGm. *xaila- and OCS ceh
2
'whole', and maybe in Lat. caelum.
Bibl.: WH II: 54If., EM 627, Hiltbrunner 1958:106-154. caerimonia, caelum, sem-

Derivatives: singuldrius 'separate' (P1.+), singularis 'of a particular person;
singular' (Varro+), singilldtim 'one by one' (P1.+).
Pit. *sem-g-(lo~).
PIE *s(e)m-gh- 'single*.
The older etymology as *sem-gno- to the root *genh 'to be born' must be dismissed.
r
Meiser suggests that singuli contains a distributive particle *-gh-, and was later
suffixed with *-lo~.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: singultus 'sobbing, hiccough' [m. u] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: singultare 'to catch the breath, gasp' (Verg.+).
Pit. *sengw~ol-to-l
PIE *sengw(h)- .
Leumann suggests an original verb *sing-ulare with the same suffix found in other
verbs of sound, e.g. sibilare, and the same kind of derivative as in tumultus. Lat.
singultus cannot represent a ppp. *singulitus under the normal rules of syncope (cf.
Schrijver 1991: 273). Thus, it may have been built directly on the model of other
words in -ultus. The root sing- has been compared with PIE *senglh- 'to sing' (which
shares with sobbing the fact that a sound is produced; but 'sobbing* is seldom referred
to as 'singing'...); but one might also compare PIE *sengw- 'to fall down, sink':
coughing and sobbing are characterized by violent movements of the throat and often
the entire head.
Bibl.: WH II: 542, EM 628, Leumann 1977: 354.

development. If -is- represents the PIE comparative suffix to which an extra
*-tero~ was added, the root can be *sin(H)- or *sen(H)-. WH connect the root *senh -

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sino, -ere 'to leave alone, let be, allow' [v. Ill; pf. si(v)i, ppp. situm] (Lex XII+; pf.sb.
siris, sirit etc. Lex XII, Pl.-Pac.)
Derivatives: situs 'placed, built, deposited' (P1.+); desinere 'to leave off, cease' (Naev,+).
Pit. *sine/o- 'to put in place', *sito- 'placed'.
PIE *tkei- / *tki- 'to build'. IE cognates: Skt. pr. kseti [3s.act.], ksiydnti [3p.act.],
k?ayat [3s.sb.act.] 'to live, stay', OAv. saeiti 'he lives', siieinti 'they are on
somebody's side', Myc. ki-ti-je-si/ktijensii 'they live', Gr. KTI^BIV, icriooai 'to found,
build', (EU)KTI|ievo<; 'well to live'.
Note the difference in meaning between the adj. situs 'placed' (which matches that of
pono < *po-sino) and the meaning of the verb and its ppp. 'to leave, let'. The pf.sb.
sir-T- is explain by Meiser 2003 from *sei-s-T~, whereas Leumann 1977 regards it as a
contracted form of the pf.sb. siveris etc. The root reconstruction is disputed. LIV
hesitates between a root *sh!i- 'to let go', for which the IE evidence is slim (the Ilr.
forms rather mean 'to bind', and can be reconstructed as *sh i-), and *tki- 'to build',
2
with a semantic shift from 'to let someone dwell' > 'leave in peace' > 'leave, let' or
'to let dwell' > 'put in place', and *po-sine- 'to offload, put' > ponere 'put'. In view
of situs, which clearly preserves an older meaning than sind, we may combine these
two paths: *tkei- 'to build', factitive *tki-n- 'to have so. build sth.' > 'to put in place'
(situs, pono) > 'to let, allow' (sino). Thus, I prefer the derivation from PIE *tkei- 'to
build, dwell'; this can be regarded as an /-present to the root *tek- 'to make'.
Bibl.: WH II: 545, EM 628f., IEW 889-891, Leumann 1977: 600, Sihler 1995: 534,
Meiser 1998: 184, 2003: 124, LIV l.*seh,(i>, *tek-, *tkei-. pono, sileo

Derivatives: Tnsinuare 'to work oneself into favour, make one's way in' (PI.+).
Pit. *sinu-.
a
PIE *sinu-? IE cognates: Alb. gji, -ri 'breast' < PAlb. *gjin- < *sin~.
Probably to be connected with Alb. gji 'breast'; if so, Skt. sanu- 'back' cannot be
cognate, since this does not contain *-/-. No further etymology.
Bibl.: WH II: 546, EM 629.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sisto, -ere 'to cause to stand, set up' [v. Ill; pf. stefi, ppp. statum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: assistere 'to stand by, attend, bring to a halt' (P1.+), consistere 'to stop
moving, take a position, arise' (P1.+), desistere 'to leave off, cease' (P1.+), insistere
'to stand on, proceed with' (P1.+), resistere 'to pause, offer resistance' (P1.+),
subsistere 'to stand firm, stop short' (P1.+).
Pit. *s(t)i-sta/o- [pr.], *stato- [ppp.]. It. cognates: U. sestu [ls.pr.], sestu [3s.ipv.II],
seste [23s.pr. or 3s.pr.ps.], O. status [ppp nom.pl.m.], Vol. statom [nom.acc.sg.n.] 'to
place, put, found', maybe 'determine'; Vol. sistiatiens [3p.pf.] 'they have established'
(to *statu-je~, Beckwith 2005: 155), U. restatu [3s.ipv.II], restef, reste [ptc.pr.
nom.sg.m.] 'to place, put' < *sista- derived from *sista/o-.
PIE *sti-sth -(e/o-) 'to put up, make to stand'. IE cognates: Celtib. sis tat 'erects (?)',
2
Olr. sissidir*, -sissedar 'to stand strong' < PCI. *sista/o-, Olr. sessam 'standing',
MW gwaessaf 'support' < *sistamu- [m.]; Skt. pr. tisthati, YAv. histaiti 'to stand',
OP aistata (< *a-hista-) [3s.ipf.]; Gr. IOTTJJUI, Dor. loxctpi 'to (make to) stand'.
Whether the Italic present was thematic or athematic is uncertain. As Schrijver
observes, there is no compelling argument against an original athematic verb.
Bibl.: WH II: 597f, EM 651-654, IEW 1004-1010, Schrijver 1991: 412, Untermann
2000: 672-680, Meiser 2003: 189f„ Schumacher 2004: 57If, LIV *steh -. -^sto
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: so- 'this, that' [pron.] (Enn.: acc.sg.m. sum, acc.sg.f. sam, acc.pl.m. sos)
Pit. *so- i *sa-. It. cognates: O. eks-, ex-, U. es(s)- , Marr. es- 'this' < *ekso-/a-
'this' < *e-ke/o-so (see s.v. -ce); SPic. praistakla-sa [nom.acc.pl.n.].
PIE *so [nom.sg.m.], *seh2 [nom.sg.f.], *tod [nom.acc.sg.n.] 'this, that'. IE cognates:
Celtib. somui [dat.sg.m.], somei [loc.sg.], Olr. so, -so, -siu 'this, that' (< PIE *so), Skt.
sa-lta-, Av. ha, ha-/ta- 'this, he', Gr. o [m.], oq [m.], fj [f.] 'this, he, she', oblique and n.
T-; Lith. tds, ta, OCS tb, ta 'this, that'; Go. sa [m.], so [f.] 'this, he, she', obi. and n.p-;
ToB se [m.], sa [f.] 'this, the'.
PIE *s-/t- 'this, that' originally had *s- only in the nom.sg.m.f. In Pit. or maybe
already in Italo-Celtic, *s- spread to all case-forms. Of this paradigm, Ennius retains
three acc. forms, and OLat. has a few reduplicated forms, for which see s.v. ipse. The
cj. sT< *sei probably was a loc.sg. of the same paradigm. In Sabellic, *so/sa- is found
inflected in the pronoun *ekso/a-. As an uninfected element, *so is found in Lat. ipse.
The variant *to~, originally of the oblique cases, is found in Lat. iste and in the adv.
tarn, topper, turn. It is likely, then, that *to- also had an inflected paradigm in Pit.
Bibl.: WH II: 550, EM 630, IEW 978f„ Leumann 1977: 470, Sihler 1995: 395,
Beekes 1995: 202-204, Schrijver 1997b: 15, Meiser 1998: 164, Untermann 2000:
216-218. —*• -ce, ipse, iste, si, tarn, topper, turn
sObrius 'sober, not intoxicated' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: sobrietds 'sobriety' (Sis.+).
Pit. *se-obri-(o-) 'away from drunkenness'?
PIE *h,ogwh-r-i- 'drinking'.
The adj. probably continues the negating prefix se- plus (the preform of) ebrius
'drunk'. The 5 would seem to point to a preform *so- or *swe- (cf. Meiser 1986:
247f.), but such a preform is uncertain: the other instances of si- can be explained
from Pit. *se(d)~. Weiss 1994 points to the relationship terra : extorris, and suggests
that w...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: socer, -erl 'father-in-law' [m. o] (Naev.+; nom.sg. socerus PI., dat.sg. socrlNaev,)
Derivatives: socrus, -us [f.] 'mother-in-law' (Enn,+; Naev .Acc. m. 'father-in-law').
Pit. *swekuro- 'father-in-law', *swekru- 'mother-in-law'.
PIE *suekuro- [m.] 'father-in-law', *suekruh - (< *suekurh ) [fi] 'mother-in-law'.
2 2
IE cognates: Skt. svasura-, YAv. xvasura~, Gr. eicupoq, Lith. sesuras (< *sesuras by
assimilation), OHG swehur [m.] 'father-in-law', NHG Schwager [m.] 'brother-in-law'
(< *suekurd- '^belonging to, the father-in-law'); Skt: svasru-, Sogd. 'ywsh , OCS
svekry [fi], OHG swigar [fi] 'mother-in-law'. "
The transition of the original w-stem socrus to a tiom.sg. in -us is explained by
Leumann from iambic shortening, whereas Schrijver posits laryngeal loss in oblique
case forms followed by analogical spread to the nom.sg. The similar change in neptis
< *nept-iH- suggests that Schrijver's solution is correct, since neptis cannot be
explained from iambic shortening.
Bibl.: WH II: 550fi, EM 631, IEW 1043fi, Schrijver 1991:259, Meiser 1998: 146.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: socius 'companion, comrade' [m. o] (VOLat.+: Garigliano Bowl sokiois [abl.pl.]);
gen.pl. socium, in verse and fixed formulas.
Derivatives: l-§ocia 'female partner' (PL+), sociennus 'partner' (PI.), societas
'partnership, fellowship' (Enn.+); consociare 'to bring into alliance, associate' (PI.+),
dissociare 'to separate' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *sokjo~. It. cognates: Fal. soc[iai] [dat.sg.fi], sociai [voc.pl.fi] 'girlfriend,
concubine'.
PIE *sokw-(h )-i-o- 'companion'. IE cognates: Skt. sdkhay- [m.] (sakha [nom.sg.],
2
sakhayam [acc.sg.], sakhye [dat.sg.]), Av. haxi- (YAv. haxa [nom.sg.], hose [dat.sg.])
'friend, companion' < PIE *sokwh -oi-; OIc. seggr 'man, warrior'; OE secg 'man,
2
fellow, warrior' < PGm. *sayja- < *sokw-io
The suffix of the hapax sociennus is unique; it is mostly explained as an Etruscan
suffix which was added to the Latin base (Leumann 1977: 321). Whereas Ilr. contains
an i-stem derived from a collective *sokw-h - 'comrades', there is no certainty that
2
Germ, and Latin are also derived from the same /? -stem, since we are unable to
2
discern a trace of the laryngeal. It is equally possible that socius and Gm. *sayja- are
o-stem derivatives from a noun *sokw-i- 'comrades' or an adj. *sokw-i- 'following'.
Bibl.: WH II: 551, EM 631, IEW 896f., Giacomelti 1963: 255f.:, Schrijver 1991:
249,259, Sihler 1995: 334, Meiser 1998: 98, LIV l.^sek11-. sequor

Derivatives: sodalitas 'association, group' (PI.+), sodalicium 'fraternity, partnership'
(Lucil.+).
Pit. *swepali~.
PIE *sue-dhh 'custom, to get accustomed'. IE cognates: Skt. svadhd- [f.] 'custom,
r
peculiar nature' < *sue-dheh (or *sue-dhheh?), Gr. etfoc, [n.] 'custom, usage' <
r r r
(diss.) *hei3o(; < *f£i3o<;; Go. sidus [m.] 'custom' (< *s(u)e-dhh]-u-?).
An Italic derivative in *-ali- of a stem * sweet - < PIE *sue-dhh 'custom', a syntagm
r
which is also reflected in Skt. and Gr. With a first member *sue or *sueh it is also
h
found in suesco (see s.v.). An alternative etymology would be *swe-d-ali- to Gr.
iSioc;, Dor. p5ioq, Arg. MSioq 'own, private' < PGr. *pfte8io- < *swedio-.
Bibl.: WH II: 552, EM 63If., IEW 882-884, Sihler 1995: 131, Stiiber 2002: 155f.

sfil, sOlis 'sun' [m. J] (Lex XII, Naev.+)
Derivatives: solarium 'part which is exposed to the sun, sundial' (P1.+); solstitium
'solstice' (Cato+), solstitialis 'of the summer solstice' (P1.+).
Pit. *saur>
PIE *seh u-l [nom.acc.], *sh u-en- [obi.] 'sun' [n.]. IE cognates: W. haul 'sun'; Olr.
2 2
siiil [f.] 'eye' > *sul-i~, Skt. svdr- (suvar-) [n.] 'sun' (nom.sg. svar (< *siiHf < *sHur
< *sh ul), gen.sg. suras, dat.sg. sure) , OAv, huuars [n.], gen.sg. xvmg < *huuank;
2
YAv. huuars, gen.sg. hii / huro; Skt. surya- [m.] 'sun, deity of the sun', Gr. ep.
TJEXIO^ ,Dor. Aeol. Arc. deXio<;, Dor. (trag.) also 6Xioq 'sun' < PGr. <
*seh -uel-io-; Lith. saule, Latv. saule, OCS sltntce [n.], Ru. solnce < BS1.
2
*sa?ul-l*s?ul-; Go. sauil [n.], OIc. OE sol [f.] < *seh -uel-o~, Go. sunno, OE sunne
2
'sun'.
The IE languages point to an ablauting n. //n-stem meaning 'sun', and a derivative in
-I- or -io- to the /-stem. Since Latin does not show a trace of a derivative suffix, it is
plausible that sol continues the old n. nom.acc.sg., which later became m. (because of
the gender of mensisl). It is uncertain, ...

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: soleo 'to be used to, be liable, cohabit with' [v. II; pf. soluf (Enn., Cato) / solitus sum]
(PI.+)
Derivatives: assolere 'to usually accompany' (P1-+), insolens 'unaccustomed,
unfamiliar* (Ter.+), obsolescere 'to be forgotten about, become tarnished' (Varro+),
obsoletus 'worn-out' (Cic.).
Pit. *sol-e- 'to occupy'.
PIE *sol-eh 'to occupy, inhabit'. IE cognates: see s.v. solum.
r
The etymology is disputed. The meaning is closest to suesco and sodalis, so that WH
and IEW prefer *suedt-e- > *sode-, which then underwent the irregular development
of intervocalic *d to /. However, *swe- > so- would be blocked by a following front
vowel such as e\ hence this etymology must be rejected. LIV, adopting a much earlier
proposal, posits a PIE iterative *sol-eie- 'to be accustomed', cognate with Go. saljan,
and maybe with Latin solum 'ground'. Stiiber 2002: 147 assumes that the root
originally meant 'to come to a standstill', whence frequentative 'to keep returning to'
> 'dwell at' > 'have the custom to'. This is quite a complicated chain of shifts. The
nominal derivatives (OCS selo 'land, village', OHG sal 'home, room') rather point to
an original meaning 'to occupy, inhabit', from which soled can easily be understood
as 'to occupy habitually'; or, if the Latin verb was derived from solum 'ground', it
could mean 'to be inhabiting' > 'be accustomed to'.
Bibl.: WH II: 555, EM 456, 632, IEW 882-884, LIV 2.*sel-. solum, suesco

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: solidus 'solid, thorough' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Pit. *solipo~. i *
PIE *soIh -i-dhh]-o- 'entire'. JQ2 cognates: see s.v. salvus.
2
The difference in semantics renders a derivation ifrom soled 'to be accustomed'
unlikely, even though -idus adjectives often co-occur with statives in -ere. It seems
more likely that solidus derives from the same root *slh - 'whole' as salvus and sollus.
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: solium 'high-backed chair, throne; bath-tub; sarcophagus' [n. o] (PI.+)
Derivatives: soliar, -aris [n.] 'covering for a throne' (Varro+).
Pit. *sod-jo-\
PIE *sod-io-. [n.] 'seat'. IE cognates: Olr. suide [n.] 'seat* < *sodio-.
Probably from*sodio- 'seat' with the change of intervocalic *d> -/-. WH's rejection
of this etymology is not credible: a 'bath-tub' (Cato+) may originally have been a
simple seat in a bathing-house, and the meaning 'sarcophagus' (lst.c. AD+) may be
metaphorical from 'throne'. It is possible but uncertain that ob-sidium, sub-sidium
also contain * sodium; alternatively, they have been grafted on sed-eo.
Bibl.: WH U: 554, EM 633, IEW 884-887, Leumann 1977: 295, LIV *sed-. sedeo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sollus 'unbroken, complete' [adj. o/a] (Lucil, Fest.)
Derivatives: sollistimum 'satisfying the rites' (Cic.+), sollemnis 'performed in
accordance with the prescriptions, solemn, customary' (Cato+), sollemnitus [adv.]
'solemnly' (Andr.); sollers 'clever, skilled' (Cato+), sollicurius 'wholly curious' (Liv.
apud Fest.), solliferreitm 'kind of javelin made wholly of iron' (Liv.+); sollicitus
'restless, troubled, anxious' (P1.+), sollicitdre 'to disturb, worry, tempt' (PI.+),
sollicitatio 'vexation, temptation' (Ter.+), sollicitudo 'anxiety, concern' (P1.+); solox
[adj.] 'coarse, raw' (Lucil.+).
Pit. *solno- 'whole'. It. cognate!: O. sullus [nom.pI.rn.], sullum, sulum, solu
[gen.pl.rn.], sJulias [acc.pl.f.], Pael. solois [dat.pl.rn.], O. sjullad [abl.sg.f.], O. suluh
[adv.] 'each, all, whole'; O. sull[a]m[n.]als [abl.pl.f.] 'connected with the yearly cult
feast' < *sollo-amno~.
PIE *sol(h )-no- 'whole'. IE cognates: W. Bret, holl, Co. oil 'all* < *sol-no-, Olr.
2
sldn 'whole, sound' < *slH-no-.
Nussbaum 1997 has convincingly argued that Pit. *sollo- can reflect PIE *solh uo-;
2
since there is no trace of a form *solauo- with regular vocalization of the laryngeal,
we must conclude that *h was lost due to the de Saussure effect. Alternatively, Pit.
2
*sollo- could reflect PIE *sol(H)-no~, to be compared with BrCl. * sol-no- and Olr.
sldn (Nussbaum 1997: 189, Driessen 2005: 47). This solution would relieve us of the
inconvenience of deriving both salvus and sollus from a Pit. preform in *-uo-.
The adj. solli-citus 'wholly anxious' contains citus 'stirred', see s.v. cieo. In
sollers we find ars 'skill'. For sollemnis, Nussbaum 1997: 188 proposes a new
etymology which seems more likely than the previous one: *soll-epli- 'with all (due)
religious performances, with all due rites'. Dissimilation of the last 1 would have
given *soll-epni-, whence sollemnis. The adj. solox 'coarse' (of wool and sheep)
might represent *soll-ok- 'whole', in the sense of...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: surrounded by meadows, village ', OCS selo [n.] 'field, village', OHG sal [m.]
'habitation, room', OIc. salr 'hall, room, house'.
Since *selo- would regularly yield Lat. solu-, it is uncertain whether solum continues
PIE *sel- or *sol~.
Bibl.: WH II: 554, EM 634, IEW 1046, LIV 2.*sek soled
solvC, -ere 'to loose (an object bound), release, set free' [v. Ill; pf. solvT, ppp.
solutum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: solutid 'payment, unfastening' (Varro+); absolvere 'to pay off, finish,
acquit' (P1.+), dissolvere 'to set free, pay, break into pieces' (P1.+), exsolvere 'to
release, perform, pay' (P1.+), resolvere 'to pay back, loosen, free' (P1.+).
Compound of *se- 'away' and -luo 'to loosen'. For the explanation of so-, see s.v. se-.
The ppp. so-lutus may have long u from PIE *luH-to~, or it may be following the
productive pattern for Latin u-stem verbs.
Bibl.: WH I: 834, II: 557, EM 634, IEW 68If., Schrijver 1991: 246, 469, LIV
*leuH~. —*•luo, se-

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sfllus 'alone' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: solitudd 'loneliness, emptiness' (P1.+), solitas 'loneliness' (Acc.+),
soiitarius 'solitary, single' (Varro+).
The etymological connection with *s(w)e, *sed 'away, apart' which WH, IEW and
EM propose is hardly possible. Only an ins.sg. *s(u)oh would yield so-, but there
r
are no indications that such a form of the reflexive pronoun ever existed. One might
connect Go. sels 'happy' < *sel-, OE selra 'better' < *sdliza-, but the semantics are
uncompelling and the root is not otherwise attested. Semantically, solus could be
connected with the PIE root *slH- found in salvus aid sollus 'whole'; it could reflect
a thematic derivative *solH-o- with vrddhi, or an original root noun *solH- 'the
whole, "the one' with vowel lengthening in a monosyllable.
BibL: WH II: 557, EM 634, IEW 882-884.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: somnus 'sleep' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: sorrmium 'dream, vision' (P1.+), somniare 'to dream, day-dream' (PI+),
somnfculosus 'sleepy' (P1.+), somnurnus 'occurring during sleep' (Varro); msomnis
'sleepless' (Cato+).
Pit. *swe/opno- [m.] 'sleep', *swe/opnjo- 'dream'.
PIE *suepho- / *suopno- 'sleep'. IE cognates: Olr. suan, W. hun 'sleep' < *suopno~;
Hit. supp-('f(ri) 'to sleep' < *sup-(t)o, suppariie/a'to sleep' < *sup-r-ie/o
supparuant-/sleepy?'; Skt. svapna- [m.] 'sleep, dream', svapnya- [n.] 'dream,
vision', dusvapnyam 'nightmare', Av. xvafna- [m.] 'sleep, dream', Gr. wcvoc; [m.]
'sleep', vnap 'reality', Arm. k'owrt, Alb. gjume 'sleep', Lith. sapnas, Latv. sapnis
'dream', OCS stnt, Ru. son 'sleep'; OIc. svefn, ToA spam, ToB spam 'id.'.
Pit. *swepno- > *swopno- would regularly yield Latin somnus. It is uncertain whether
a preform *swopno- or *sopno- would have yielded somnus too: Latin shows a
change *o > u in front of velar and labial nasals (uncus, numerus), but this change is
mostly blocked by a preceding dental consonant: tongeo, domus. Thus, in somnus the
change to u might also have been blocked by s-. In view of the Celtic forms, I have a
slight preference for Pit. *swopno-. Lat. somnium may go back to a PIE /o-derivative
(as proposed by Schindler) or be an inner-Latin formation. PIE *suepno- is probably a
thematized derivation of the r/n-stem *suep-r/n- (see s.v. sopor); the same preform
yielded OIc. svefn, and maybe Ilr. *suapna~. Other languages show *suopno- (Baltic,
Celtic) or *supno- (Greek, Albanian, Slavic).
Bibl.: WH II: 557f, EM 634f, IEW 1048f, Schindler 1966, Schrijver 1991:
467-470, Sihler 1995: 299, Meiser 1398: 83, LIV *suep-. sopor

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sonfl, -are 'to sound' [v. I; pf. sonui, ppp. sonitum] (P1.+; OLat. sonere Enn - Lucr.)
Derivatives: somis 'sound, utterance' (Enn.+), sonor, -oris 'sound' (Lucr.+), sonitus,
-iis 'sound, noise' (Naev.+), sonTvius 'making a rattling sound' (Cato+); consonare
'to resound, sound together' (PL+), obsonare 'to interrupt by speaking' (PI.), resonare
'to resound, make an echo' (P1.+; resonere Enn, Pac, Acc.); sonipes 'making a noise
with the feet' (Lucil.+).
Pit. pr. *swena~, iter. *swon-aje/o- 'to sound, resound', *sweno- [m.] 'sound'. It.
cognates: possibly (but disputed) U. sonitu, simitu [3s.ipv.II]'?'.
PIE *suenH-(ti) 'sounds', *suonh -(e)ie/o- 'to resound'. IE cognates: Olr. seinnid*,
2
seinn 'to play (a musical instrument)' < PCI. *suanna~, MW honni 'to announce' <
PCI. iter. *suonn-T-; Skt. pr. svanaycm [3p.], aor. asvanit, intens. sanisvanat [3s.] 'to
sound, hum', YAv. xvanat.caxra- 'with sounding wheels', OKJhot. hvaha- 'to be
spoken'.
The older class III present sonere is replaced in the course of Latin by sonare.
Schrijver doubts that this could have arisen from athematic *suenH~, and hesitatingly
adopts Steinbauer's suggestion of an original causative; he leaves open the possibility
that sonare is denominal to sonus. Instead of a causative, one may rather expect an
iterative *suonhreie- 'to resound, sound many times', as LIV posits; this is also
reflected in MW honni 'to announce'. The noun sonus could be old, or be de verbal to
sonere.
Bibl.: WH II: 559f, EM 635f, IEW 1046f, Steinbauer 1989: 124f, Schrijver 1991:
103, 395, Meiser 1998: 186, Rix 1999: 518f, Untermann 2000: 717f., Schumacher
2004: 607f, LIV *suenh ~.
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sons, sontis 'guilty' [adj. nt] (PI.+)
Derivatives: sonticus 'genuine, valid' (Lex XI1+); Tnsdns 'innocent' (P1.+).
Pit. *sont- 'being'.
PIE nom. *hies~nt-s (» *h[S-ont-s), acc. *his-ent-m, gen. ^s-nt-os 'existing,
real'. IE cognates: Hit. asant- l?sant-i 'true, real', Skt. sant- 'being, real, good', asat-
'not being', satya- 'true, real', Av. haffliia- 'id.' < *h[Snt-io-, Gr. tbv, 6vr-o<; 'being',
OPr. nom.sg. sins, dat.sg. sentismu, OLith. acc.sg.m. sant}, Lith. sqs, saneio (» esqs,
Ssqs m, esanti f.), Latv. esuots 'being', OCS sy, gen.sg.m. sgsta, OIc. sannr, sadr,
OHG sand, OS sod 'true'.
The original meaning 'being, real' of the pr.ptc. *sont- can still be seen in the
derivative sont-icus 'real, genuine', said of morbus 'disease' and causa 'cause' in the
oldest attestations.
Bibl.: WH II: 560, EM 636, IEW 340-342, Leumann 1977: 523, Cotticelli Kuras
1993, Sihler 1995: 615f, LIV 1 .*h]es-. sum

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sopor, -5ris 'sleepiness, sleep' [m. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: sopire 'to cause to sleep' (Lucr.+), consopire 'to send to sleep, make
unconscious' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *swep-6s [m.] 'sleep' / *swop- 'sleep' [m.].
PIE *suep-/*sup- 'to sleep', *su6p-s, *suop-m, *suep-s [m.] 'sleep'; *suo/ep-r,
*sup-n-os [n.]. IE cognates: OIc. see/a 'to kill' < *suop-ie/o-; further see s.v. sommts.
Since there was a root aor. PIE *su(e)p- (according to LIV), sopor might be regarded
as an original s-stem collective *suep-ds. The fact that somnus 'sleep' goes back to
PIE *sue/opnoa no-stem with different ablaut grades in the daughter languages,
suggests that the original nominal form was a root noun *su(o/e)p- 'sleep' or an
n-stem *su(e)p~n~. In the latter case, this might have had a nom.acc.sg. *suep-f which
would yield Lat. sopor. The consistent -or-inflexion in the rest of the paradigm could
have arisen analogically. Gr. wrap might be quoted in support (but this could be
analogical to ovap 'dream'), as well as Hit. suppariie/a-n 'to sleep* < *sup-r-ie/o-,
supparuant- 'sleepy?'. Yet the fact that all no-derivatives are m. may suggest that the
base noun was also m. Thus, sopor could also be derived from a PIE root noun
'sleep'. Such a root noun could have had a nom.sg. *sudp-s, explaining why we find
-o- in sopire (thus Nussbaum 1976: 250, Bammesberger 1980). The late attestation of
Latin sopire is remarkable; but in view of its factitive meaning, unique for a verb in
-ire, it is still likely to be an old verb. Sopire was explained as a causative PIE
*suop-ie/o- by Klingenschmitt ;1978, but the existence of this verbal type is uncertain.
The same PIE root noun is probably the source of the.ablaut in *su(e/o)pno~.
Bibl.: WH II: 561, EM 635, IEW 1048f, Klingenschmitt 1978, Bammesberger 1980,
Schrijver 1991: 126, LIV *suep-. —» somnus

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sorbeo 'to drink, suck up' [v. II; sorbui, sorbitum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: sorbilo [adv.] 'by sips' (P1.+), sorbildre 'to sip' (Ter.+), sorbitio 'broth,
liquid food' (P1.+); absorbere 'to engulf, swallow up' (P1.+), exsorbere 'to swallow'
(P1.+), obsorbere 'to gulp down' (Naev.+).
Pit. *sorfeie-i
PIE *srobh-eie- [iter.] 'to take various sips, slurp'. IE cognates: Hit. sarap- /sarip-
* ** h h
'to sip' < *srob - / *srb -, Gr. potpeto 'to slurp, gulp', p6(pr]pa 'thick broth, soup',
Arm. arbi 'he drank', Alb. gjerp 'slurp' < *sorb-eie~, Lith. surbiit, surbti 'to suck',
srebti, Is. srebiii 'to eat (liquid food) with a spoon', OCS srbbati, Ru. serbatb 'to
slurp', ORu. serebl'u, Sin. srebljem 'to slurp'.
If one assumes that this root had a full grade *srebh~ in PIE (as Lith. and Gr. seem to
indicate), Latin sorb- must be secondary, since original *srobh- would have given
*frob- (cf. frigus) or maybe *strob~. The PIE form, with the ablaut vowel between the
second and third consonant, can easily be due to onomatopoeic symbolism. It is
assumed that Lat. sorb- must be the reflex of a zero grade *srbh~, or it has
analogically introduced -or- on the model of other iteratives, such as torqued and
mordeo (thus Tichy 1983: 82). A similar process must account for Albanian and
Slavic *sVrbh~.
Bibl.: WH II: 561f„ EM 636, IEW 1001, Klingenschmitt 1982: 272f, LIV *srebh-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sorbus 'service-tree' [f. o] (Veranius+)
Derivatives: sorbum 'service-berry' (Cato+).
No etymology. The connection wity Ru. sorobalina 'rose hip, blackberry', Lith.
serbenta, serbentas 'redcurrant, blackcurrant', sirbti 'to ripen' is possible but does not
point to a PIE root *sVr- 'red', as IEW holds. At most, they show a non-IE word
*sVrb- for 'berry'.
Bibl.: WH II: 562, EM 636, IEW 91 Of.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sordes, -is 'dirt, filth, stain' [f. i/e] (P1.+; abl.sg. sorde PI.)
Derivatives: sordere 'to be dirty, seem unworthy' (P1.+), sorditudo 'dirt' (P1.+),
sordidus 'dirty, squalid' (P1.+), sordidulus 'shabby' (P1.+), sordidatus 'shabbily
dressed' (P1.+).
Pit. *swordi- 'dirt', and/or *swordo- 'dirty'.
PIE *suord-o/i-. IE cognates: Go. swarts, OIc. svart, OE sweart, OHG swarz 'black'.
If an original i-stem, sordes might reflect *sord-i- 'dirt(iness)', according to
Nussbaum 1999a: 403. This might go back to * sword- and be compared with PGm.
* swart- 'black'. No further IE etymology.
Bibl.: WH II: 562, EM 637, IEW 1052, Leumann 1977: 451, Schrijver 1991: 378,
463. —* sudsum

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sorex, -icis 'shrew-mouse' [m. £] (Ter.+)
Derivatives: soricinus 'of a shrew-mouse' (PI.).
Probably related to Gr. tjpa^ 'shrew-mouse'; I regard these words as reflexes of a
common loanword of the structure *sVr-Vk-. It is often assumed that sorex is related
to the onomatopoeia susurrus 'whisper, rustling', but in that case the relationship with
the Greek word becomes less direct.
Bibl.: WH 11: 563, EM 637, IEW 1049f., Schrijver 1991: 124.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: soror, -oris 'sister' [f. r] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: sororcula 'little sister' (P1.+), sororius 'of a sister' (P1.+), sordridre 'to
swell' (of the breasts) (P1.+); sobrinus 'second cousin, son of a person's first cousin'
(P1.+), sobrma 'female second cousin' (P1.+), consobrmus 'mother's sister's son,
cousin' (Ter.+).
Pit. *swes5r- [f.] 'sister', *(kom-)swesr-ino- 'belonging to the sister'.
PIE *sue(-)sr- 'sister' (nom.sg. *suesor, gen.sg. *suesros) . IE cognates: Olr. stur
'sister' [f.], Skt. svasar-, YAv. xvarjhar- [f.], MoP xwahar/xwahar 'sister', Gr. eop
'daughter, nephew' (Hsch.); Arm. k'oyr 'sister'; OPr. swestro, Lith. sesud, OCS
sestra, Ru. sestra, Sin. sestra, Go. swistar 'sister'; OCS sestrirvb 'of the sister', Lith.
seserSnas 'sister's son'.
Latin has generalized the suffix -or- throughout the paradigm; sobrinus shows the
change of intervocalic *-sr- > *-fr- > -br-. Since it has been established that *Cwe >
Co- only occurs in front of a non-front vowel in the next syllable, sobrinus cannot be
phonetic, but has adopted *swo- or so- from soror. The PIE *suesr- may be a
J
compound of *sue 'self and a PIE word *sor, *sr- for 'woman'.
Bibl.: WH I: 265, II: 550, 563, EM 637, IEW 1051, Schrijver 1991: 469, EIEC 392.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sors, -rtis 'lot, lottery ticket, voting tablet' [f. /] (P1.+; nom.sg. sortis PL, abl.sg. sorti)
Derivatives: sortire/i 'to draw lots, cast votes, acquire' (P1.+), sortftid 'lottery'
(Varro+), sortitus, -us 'lottery, lot' (P1.+).
Pit. * sorti- 'joining, joint'.
PIE *sr-ti- 'joining, joint'. IE cognates: see s.v. sero.
Bibl.: WH II: 563f., EM 637f., IEW 911, Leumann 1977: 449, LIV 2.*ser-. sero

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sospes, -itis 'safe and sound' [adj. /] (PL+)
Derivatives: sospitare 'to preserve, defend' (P1.+), sospitalis 'salutary' (P1.+);
Sospita i Sispita 'cult-title of Juno at Lanuvium'; Lanuv.inscr. SeiAspit-.
Pit. *swe-spat- 'self-prospering'?
PIE *-sph[-t- 'prospering'. IE cognates: see s.v. spes.
An earlier connection with Skt. s vasti- 'welfare, happiness' was rejected by WH, but
tl
has been revived by Meier-Briigger 1979, who posits PIE *hsu-ns-ti- 'good return'
f
for both of them. Latin sospes would be from a compound *hisu-ps-ti- + *poti- 'lord'.
Yet his solution is impossible for various reasons. First of all, he compares
*ghosti-poti- (> Lat. hospes, ritis), but this is a noun, not an adj. Secondly, *su-ns-ti-
would normally yield Latin *sunsti-. In Skt., the compound could be syllabified as
*Hsu-ns-ti- because both *Hsu- 'good' and the verb nas-, *ns- were synchronically
existing elements in the language, but neither of them is found in Latin. Thirdly, the
phonetic changes posited by Meier-Briigger are unwarranted in the order in which he
posits them. Especially the alleged paradigm split into *suensti-pit- and *suinsti-pit-
(with pre-syncope vowel assimilation) is fanciful. Steinbauer 1989: 260 suggests that
sospes is for isospes/ from *soispes\ next to Sispita, this would show an alternation
*oi / *ei which would be reminiscent of i/u < *oi. Yet there is no second case of *oi >
Lat. 5, so that this remains speculative.
Since the attempts to see a variant of *poti- in the second element have so far been
unsuccesful, another possibility may be proposed here. In view of locu-plet- and
antistes, praest'es, superstes < *-stat-, Latin inherited from PIE governing compounds
with a Iaryngeal-final second member, to which *-t- was suffixed. In the case of
sospita good possibility seems PIE *speh 'to swell, prosper, ripen' (Lat. spes). A
r
form *-sph t- would have yielded Pit. *-spat- whence Lat. -spit-, as in *-stat- > -stit-
r
'standing'. ...

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sparus 'hunting-spear, javelin' [m. o] (Lucil.+; also Jparum)
Pit. *sparo- 'spear'.
PIE *sprH-o- 'spear, beam'. IE cognates: Alb. shparr, shperdhe 'oak', OIc. spjgrr
'spear', OE spere [n.], OFr. spiri, spere, sper, OS OHG sper < *sperH-u-; OIc. spari,
sparri, OHG sparro 'roof-beam, pole'.
Only found as a nominal form in Germanic, Latin and Albanian. Possibly a loanword,
or an isolated reflex of a lost root.
Bibl.: WH II: 568, EM 638, IEW 990f„ Schrijver 1991: 213.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sperno, -ere 'to dissociate, reject, spum' [v. Ill; pf. sprevT, ppp. spretum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: asperndrT'to scorn, reject' (P1.+), aspernabilis 'negligible' (Acc.+).
Pit. *sperne/o-.
PIE *sp(h)er-n-h ,3- [pr.] 'to beat, kick', *sp(t,)(e)rh2, - [aor.]. IE cognates: Hit.
2 3
isparra- I ispar- 'to trample' < *sp6rh / -; Skt. pr. sphurati, aor. spharlh [2s.inj.act.]
2 3
'to push away with the foot', YAv. spar a- 'to tread', fra-spara- 'to speed on',
vT-spara- 'to trample down'; Gr. aoitaipco 'to sprawl, flounder, lie in convulsions',
Arm. spama- 'to threaten'; Lith. spirit 'to kick out (of horses), defy, sting', Latv.
spert 'to kick out, push'; OIc. sperna, OHG spurnan 'to kick out with the feet'.
Meiser 2003 argues that the full grade of this root is usually *sper(H)~, so that the pf.
sprevi cannot continue a PIE formation, but must have been formed after another
verb, e.g. *pleh 'to fill'. The original ablaut in the aor. may have been *sperH- :
r
*sprH- > *spera-: *spra-, the first member of which was replaced by *spre~. If this is
correct, the quality of the PIE laryngeal cannot be determined. AsperndrT <
*ad-spemafi shows the productive ^-conjugation of derived prepositional verbs. A
nasal present is also found in Armenian and in Germanic. Lubotsky 2006, noticing the
irregular correspondences in the anlaut of the IE verbs, reconstructs the verb as
*TsperH- 'to kick with the heel'; in *tspersn- 'heel' (> Lat. perna), the first s would
have disappeared through dissimilation. The words may be compounds of *pd- 'foot'
and *per(H)~ 'to beat, kick'.
Bibl.: WH II: 572fi, EM 641, IEW 992fi, Schrijver 1991: 22, 140, 184, 407, Meiser
1998: 187,2003: 227, Lubotsky 2006:1007-9, LIV *spherH-. asper, perna

(Naev.+), sperabilis 'that may be hoped for' (PL), Tnsperatus 'unexpected' (P1.+);
exspes 'hopeless' (Acc.+).
Pit. *spes.
PIE *spehs, *spehm, *sphos 'prosperity'. IE cognates: Hit. ispai- / ispi- 'to
r r r
get full, be satiated' < *sph6i- / *sphi-, Skt. sphira- 'fat', sphayatai [3s.sb.med.]
r r
'to become fat, increase', Lith. spSti 'to be in time, be capable', OCS sped 'to
succeed', Ru. spet' 'to ripen' < PIE *speh, OE spowan 'to prosper'.
r
The s-stem forms such as sperem and speres must be secondary; Schrijver considers
the possibility that they stem from a compound adj. in PIE nom.sg. *-es.

de Vaan Latin Etymology: splca 'ear of corn; spike, head (of a plant)' [f. a] (Varro+; also splcum)
Derivatives: spicilegium 'the gathering of ears of corn' (Varro+).
Pit. *speikd-/*spTka-.
PIE *speig-/*spiHg-. IE cognates: Lith. speigliai 'thorns'; OHG speihha, OS speca
[fi], MDu. spike, OE spdca [m.] 'spoke' < *spaikdn-, OIc. spikr, MHG spicher,
MoDu. spijker 'nail' < *spei/ika-.
Can be compared with Germ, and maybe Lith. words in *sp-i-g- which also denote a
sharp object. No further etymology.
Bibl.: WH II: 574, EM 642, IEW 98If.

Pit. *speina-/*spma-. It. cognates: maybe U. spinia, spina [acc.sg.], spiniama,

PIE *spein-/*spiHn-. IE cognates: Latv. spina 'rod', OPo. spina 'spine', Ru. spina

i

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: splro, -are 'to breathe* [v. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: spiramen 'breathing, exhalation' (Enn.+), spfritus, -us 'breath, spirit'
(P1.+); aspfrdre 'to blow, breathe, approach' (Lucil.+), conspirare 'to agree, conspire'
(Acc.+), exspirare 'to breathe out, die' (Acc.+), inspirare 'to breathe deeply, blow in'
(Lucr.+), respfrare 'to recover one's breath' (P1.+), suspTrare 'to sigh' (P1.+),
suspiritus, -iis 'sighing' (P1.+).
Pit. *spfr/s~.
Possibly an onomatopoeic formation imitating the sound of breathing. There are no
direct cognates. In BS1., we find words of a structure *p(e)is-k- 'to squeak' (Lith.
pyskhi 'to click, snap, crack', OCS piskati, Ru. piscat' 'to squeak'); these look
somewhat similar, but the meaning is different, and they may just as well have been
formed within BS1. The same goes for Germ. *JTs- 'to fart', adduced by WH and IEW.
Bibl.: WH II: 575, EM 642, IEW 796.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: spissus 'laborious, slow, dense' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: spissescere 'to thicken' (Lucr.+); spissigradus 'slow-paced' (PI.).
Pit. *spisso~. j ^
PIE *spid-to-? IE cognates: Gr. citi6io<; 'extensive, wide', 'thick,
OTUSVOV
coagulated', omSoev 'black, big' (Hsch.); Lith. spisti (< *spit-), Is. spintu 'to begin to
swarm (of bees), gather'.
The most likely cognate seems to be the Greek word family in omS- 'thick, wide';
spissus might be a derivative in *-to- to this basis. The meaning of Lith. spisti is
further removed. Since spissus is reminiscent of crassus, grossus, it may contain an
expressive geminate - hence a possible preform would also be *spTsus.
Bibl.: WH II: 576, EM 643, IEW 983f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: spolium 'skin, hide; arms, spoils' [n. o] (PI.+)
Derivatives: spolidre (Enn. lx -rT) 'to strip of clothing or arms, rob' (P1.+);
despoliare 'to plunder, strip of (P1.+), despolfijator 'plunderer' (PI.), exspoliare 'to
plunder, despoil' (P1.+).
Pit. *spoli(o)- 'skin, hide'.
PIE *spol(H)-i(o)- 'what is split, stripped off. IE cognates: Gr. OJtoXac;, -a8oq
'leather garment', o7toXia 'wool from sheep's legs' (Hsch.), Lith. spalis 'beard (= part
of the stalk of flax)', pi. spaliai 'parts of the stalk', OCS poh 'sex, half, NHG
spalten 'to cleave', Go. spilda 'writing tablet'.
Probably a derivative *spol-(i-) from a PIE root *(s)pel(H)~ 'to split, cleave', but the
exact form of the root and the number of its derivatives is difficult to establish. LIV
reconstructs a root *(s)pelH-, but Skt.phalati 'bursts' can hardly be connected with it.
We find many o-grade derivatives (Gr, BS1, Germ.), which may point to an iterative
verb (as fits the meaning of 'to cleave, strip of skin') or, in the case of Latin spolium,
a noun *spol-i~.
Bibl.: WH II: 577f„ EM 643, IEW 985-987, Schrijver 1991: 22, LIV 2.*(s)pelH-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sponda 'bed or couch, or its frame' [f. a\ (Varro+)
Pit. * sponda- 'frame'.
PIE *spond-h -. IE cognates: Olr. sond, W. ffon [f.] 'stick' < *spond-\ OCS p§db,
2
Ru. pjad' 'span' < *p(e)nd-i
An Italo-Celtic correspondence *spond-a~, probably derived from the root for 'to
spin'. See s.v. pendo for other cognates. Because of PIE *-d~, the Germanic words for
'bucket' (OIc. spann) are not cognate; their meaning is also deviant.
Bibl.: WH II: 578, EM 643, IEW 989, LIV ?2 *(s)pend-. pendo
spondeC 'to pledge, promise, make a contract' [v. II; pf. spopondT (spe-), ppp.
sponsum] (P1.+; pf.sb. sponsis Fest.)
Derivatives: sponsid 'wager, pledge' (P1.+), sponsus 'affianced husband' (Titin.+),
sponsor 'surety' (Varro+), sponsa 'woman promised in marriage, fiancee' (Naev.+),
sponsalis 'of betrothal' (Varro); conspondere 'to exchange pledges' (Naev.+),
despondere 'to promise in marriage, to despair' (P1.+), respondere 'to reply, react'
(Andr.+), responsio 'answering' (P1.+), responsare 'to give an answer, satisfy' (P1.+),
responsor 'who answers' (PI.).
Pit. *spondeje/o- 'to libate many times' > 'pledge'. It. cognates: U. spefa [acc.sg.f,
abl.sg.f.] 'offered' (attribute of mefa 'sacrificial substance'), O. spentud[3s.ipv.II] '?'.
PIE *spond-eie/o- [pr.] 'to libate'; ?*spend-e/o- [pr.]. IE cognates: Hit. ispant- /
ispant- 'to bring a fluid sacrifice, libate' < *spond~, Gr. CTOVSCO 'to offer a libation,
pour', c7tov5fi 'libation, offering of wine'.
The pf. spopondi may originally have belonged to a pr. *spend~e/o~, which is now lost
from Latin but might be present in O. spentud. Spopondi may be the phonetic reflex
of spepondi, cf. Schrijver 1991: 468; but -o- may also have been analogically
introduced into the reduplication syllable.
Bibl.: WH II: 578f, EM 643f, IEW 989, Untermann 2000: 690f, Meiser 2003:
149f, LIV 1 .*spend-.

de Vaan Latin Etymology: spuma 'foam' [f. d] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: spumare 'to foam, froth' (Enn.+).
Pit. *spoima- 'foam'.
PIE *spoHi-m- / *speh i-m- 'foam'. IE cognates: Skt. phena- 'foam1 [m./n.], OPr.
3
spoayno, Lith. spaine, OCS pena, Ru. pena, SCr. pjena 'foam' < *(s)poHi-nh - or
2
*(s)peh i-nh -; OE jam [n.], OHG feim 'foam' [m.] < PGm. *faima- < PIE
3 3
*poHi-mo-.
The different IE reflexes can be interpreted as derivatives in *-m- and *-«- from a root
*spoHi- or *speh i-; the *-i- may be a present suffix. Alternatively, with Matasovic
3
2004: 126, one may posit a PIE neuter mn-stem: nom.acc.sg. *spoHimn, gen.sg.
*spHimnos. The only disadvantage of the latter solution is that no language retains a
trace of the suffix *-mn- in tact.
Bibl.: WH II: 580, EM 644, IEW 1001, Schrijver 1991: 270. pumex

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: spuo, -ere 'to spit' [v. Ill; pf. spui, ppp. sputum] (Varro+)
Derivatives: sputare 'to spit out, at' (PI.+), sputator 'who habitually spits' (P1.+),
sputum 'spittle' (Lucr.+), spiitdtilicius 'detestable' (Sis.+); despuere 'to spit on the
ground, spurn' (Naev.+), exspuere 'to emit, spit out' (PI.+), Jnsputare 'to spit upon'
(P1.+), respuere 'to reject, repel' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *spu~je/o-, *spiito-.
PIE *sp(i)uH-ie/o- 'to spit', *sp(i)Huto- 'spat'. IE cognates: Skt. sthTvati, aor.
asthavisam 'to spit', Gr. 7rnko 'to spit', Arm. t'owk' 'spittle', t'k'anem 'to spit', Alb.
pshtyj, Lith. spiauti, Latv. sp}aut, CS plbvati, Is. pljujg, Ru. plevat', Go. speiwan,
OIc. spyja, OE OS OHG spiwan 'to spit',
t
Lat. spuo belongs to a PIE onomatopoeic formation meaning 'to spit'. Especially the
anlaut of the word has probably been subject to onomatopoeic reformation; but also
the stem is not identical in all languages. For Latin, both the pr. and ppp. may be old,
but the pr. alone would have sufficed to form the attested paradigm which is that of a
regular verb in -uere.
Bibl.: WH II: 580f., EM 644f„ IEW 999f., Schrijver 1991: 247, Meiser 1998: 194,
2003: 235, LIV *sptieuH-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: squalus 'unidentified sea-fish' [m. o] (Var, Plin.)
Derivatives: squatus 'a fish' (Plin., Fest.).
IE cognates: OPr. kalis 'catfish'; OIc. hvalr [m.], OE hwcel, OS OHG hwal, OHG
hwelira 'whale'; Finn, kala, Northern Lappish guolle 'fish'.
Since the word only occurs in prose, the quantity of the first vowel is unknown.
Therefore, it might as well be /squalusi with the stem *squal- 'scale' which is found
in the previous etymon. The fish would be denominated after its scales. In that case,
the explanation as a loanword from an unknown language, cognate with the Gm.
words for 'whale', OPr. 'catfish' and Uralic 'fish' (for which see Schrijver 2001: 423)
must be dropped; and squalus is not an example of the sound change *-kwo- > -qua-,
Bibl.: WH II: 581f., EM 645, IEW 958, Schrijver 1991: 463,2001: 423.
Ven. sselboisselboi [dat.sg.] 'for oneself.
Pit. *selfo- 'self.
IE cognates: OPr. sups, subs 'own' < *su-bho-; Go. silba, OIc. sjalfr, OE self, OHG
selb< PGm. *selba- 'self < *s(u)elbho~.
The pronoun seems to originate from suffixation of *-bho- to the pronoun *s(u)e
'self, but the origin of *-/- is unclear. IEW suggests a contamination of *sue with the
pronoun *ol(o)~, but this seems implausible.
Bibl.: IEW 882-884, Lejeune 1974: 338, Beekes 1995: 210.
(s)ta- 'to steal' [v. (Duenos inscr. <nemedmalostatod>)
Pit. *(s)ta(je)-tod 'must steal'.
PIE *(s)th -ie/o- 'to steal'. IE cognates: Olr. (aid 'thief [m.], Hit. taie/a-zi 'to steal'
2
< *teh -ie/o-, Skt. stayat [adv.] 'secretly', stayu [m.] 'thief, pickpocket', stena- [m.]
2
'thief, robber', steya- [n.] 'theft', OAv. taiid [adv.] 'secretly', YAv. tdiia-, taiia- [n.]
'theft'; OCS tajiti, Is. tajg 'to conceal', tatb 'thief.
It is disputed whether the final words of the Duenos inscription, which probably mean
'a bad one must not steal me', must be read as Ine med malos tatddl or /ne med malo
statodL The second option is favoured by Rix 1985b: 200-211, the first one by
Eichner 1988-90. Eichner argues ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: stagnura 'standing water, pool' [n. o] (Enn.+)
Pit. *stagno-?
PIE *steh g-. IE cognates: OBret. staer, Bret, ster 'river, brook' (< *stagra); Gr.
2
oxaytbv, -ovoq [f.] 'drop', oia^a) 'to let drip, shed'.
LIV (hesitatingly) posits a PIE root *steh2g- to account for stagnum, Celtic *stagra-
and Gr. crca^co, but we find no matching formations, and the Greek word is
semantically somewhat removed (though the connection is possible). If the etymology
is correct, Latin would have a full grade and Greek and Celtic a zero grade.
Bibl.: WH II: 584f, EM 645, IEW 1010, LIV ?*steh (g)-.
2
Stella 'star' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: stellans 'starry' (Lucr.+), stellumicans 'shining with stars' (Varro),
stel(l)id 'kind of lizard, gecko' (Verg.+).
Pit. *sterla~.
PIE *h ster-s, *h ster- [m.] 'star'. IE cognates: Olr. ser, W. seven (pi. ser) , Co.
2 2
sterenn (pi. steyr) , Bret, sterenn 'star' < PCI. *ster-; Olr. sell [m.] 'iris' < *stillo-,
Mir. sellaid 'to see', MW syllu 'to gaze', Bret, sellout 'to watch' < PCI. *stTrlo- 'star'
< *h ster-Io-; Hit. haster(a)- [c.] 'star' (nom.sg. hasterza IHsterfl) < *h ster-; Skt.
2 2
tarah [nom.pl.], stfbhih [instr.pl.], Av. star- [m.] (YAv. acc.sg. staram, nom.pl. star6,
acc.pl. straus for *stras, gen.pl. strqm) , Khot. stdraa- 'star'; Gr. ciomp, -epoq, Arm.
astl, Go. stairno, ToB scirye 'star'.
i
Schrijver 1995 has established that Olr. sell 'iris' can go back to PCI. *stJrlo-\
together with Lat. stella, this points to an Italo-Celtic formation *ster-lo/a~ 'star'.
Bibl.: WH II: 587f, EM 646, IEW 1027f„ Schrijver 1995: 421-423, Meiser 1998: 123.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: stercus, -oris 'excrement of animals, dung' [n. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: stercoreus 'like dung' (P1.+), sterco/erare 'to supply with dung,
manure' (Cato+), stercordsus 'rich in manure' (Cato+), sterco/erarius 'of dung,
manure' (Cato+), sterco/eratio 'manuring' (Varro), sterquilTnum (or sterculTnum)
'manure-heap, midden' (P1.+).
Pit. *sterkos- [n.].
PIE *sterk-o- 'befoulment, exrements'. IE cognates: Bret, stronk [m. ) 'excrements',
W. trwnc 'urine' < *(s)tronk-, W. troeth, Bret, troazh 'urine' < *trok-ta-; Hit.
istar(k)-zi 'to ail, afflict' < *sterk-ti, istarni(n)k-zi 'id.', Lith. tersti 'to soil'.
According to WH and EM (the latter based on Cato mss. readings), the best reading
of 'manure-heap' is sterculTnum, which invalidates the theory that stereos reflects
*sterkw~. British Celtic contains words of the structure *(s)tro(n)k~, which has the
vowel on the wrong side; also, Lith. tersti 'to soil' has been compared, but there is no
guarantee that s in this verb reflects PIE *k rather than *s. If it does, Lith. has PIE
*terk- without s-mobile; and Celtic may have a new full grade on the basis of the zero
grade PCI. *(s)trik-. Kloekhorst 2008: 416-418 proposes to connect the Hit. forms
mentioned.
Bibl.: WH II: 589, EM 647, IEW 103If, Leumann 1977: 327, Schrijver 1995: 453f,
Stiiber 2002: 153, LIV ?*(s)terk-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sterilis 'barren, sterile, without offspring' [adj. /] (PI.+)
Derivatives: sterilus 'sterile' (Lucr, Gell, Paul, ex F.).
Pit. *ster-eli-l *ster-X-7 ,
PIE *ster- 'lacking, sterile'. IE cognates: Skt. start- [f.] 'a cow that does not give
milk and is not pregnant', MP starwan 'infertile, childless', Gr. oxstpa [f.] 'infertile
(cow, goat, woman)', Arm. sterj 'infertile', Alb. shtjerre 'lamb' < *ster-en-\ Go.
stairo 'infertile', OEstierc 'calf,NHG Sterke 'heifer'.
The IE cognates show a semantic development from 'sterile' via 'which has not
yeaned yet' to 'young animal, Iamb, calf. In Latin, -Hi- may be a dim. suffix *-eli-,
but it is also conceivable that original f. *ster~T~ was suffixed with *-//-, and
analogically shortened the penultimate syllable. According to Eichner 1974, the root
may have been PIE *ster- (Skt. stpioti 'lays down, destroys'). This would require a
semantic shift from 'to lay down' > 'to make stiff > 'make infertile'. Yet the
adjectives in *ster- mean 'infertile', not 'making infertile'. Semantically, the best
candidate for a root etymology seems to be Gr. cxEpopai 'to be robbed, lack' <
*sterh , which is said to have no cognates outside Greek.
r
BibL: WH II: 589, EM 647, IEW 1031, Eichner 1974: 37, LIV *ster-, ?*sterhi-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: spread', stairis- [n.] 'straw' < Ilr. *starH-; Gr. oxopvupi, Eoxopeoa [aor.] 'to extend,
strew out', o-tpcotoc; 'extended'.
The pf. was probably formed after the ppp. *strdto~. The pr. sternere can be explained
from vocalization of interconsonantal laryngeal in e.g. *-n-h -ti. The origin of a in
3
con-sternare is disputed: Schrijver 1991 posits a thematic verb in *-n-k -ie/o-,
3
whereas Meiser 1998 derives -na- from the full grade of the suffix *-nh - (with a
2
replacement of *-no- by *-na- in con-sternare). Meiser's solution does not explain
why -nare is restricted to compounds. The stem strag- seems recent, since strag-es
and strag-ulus are not attested before Varro. They probably have -g- in analogy to
other nouns such as com-pages and amb-dges.
BibL: WH II: 590f, 600, EM 647, IEW 1029-1031, Schrijver 1991: 183, 376,
407-410, Meiser 1998: 187, 2003: 228, Schumacher 2004: 601 f, LIV *sterh -.
3
sternuG, -ere 'to sneeze' [v. Ill; pf. sternui, ppp. sternutum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: stertere (pf. stertuT) 'to snore' (P1.+).
Pit. *(p)ster-nu~.
PIE *(p)st~(e)r-nu- 'to sneeze'. IE cognates: Mir. sreod 'sneezing', W. trew, ystrew
'sneezing', LCo. striwhi, Bret, streviafi 'to sneeze' < *stru-7, Gr. Traxppoq [m.] 'the
sneezing', aor. Jixapeiv, pr. Trcapvupai 'to sneeze', Arm. p'rngam, p'rnem 'to sneeze'.
Latin and Greek (and maybe Armenian) both show a ra/-present, but in Greek, it is
secondary to the thematic aorist. The initial clusters might go back to PIE *pst~, but
this is also uncertain. Onomatopoeic deformation may have affected all cognates in
the IE languages. However, it seems unlikely that all languages would have invented
a form with internal *-r~ to render the sound of sneezing, so Latin may well have
inherited a PIE word of the form *(p)st-r~.
Bibl.: WH II: 591, EM 648, IEW 846fi, Schrijver 1995: 454, Meiser 1998: 113, LIV
*pster-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: stilla ' drop of liquid' [f. a] (Cic.+)
Derivatives: stillicidiitm 'the falling of drops, trickle, drip' (Varro+), stillare 'to fall
in drops, drip' (Varro+), stillatim 'drop by drop' (Varro); destillare 'to drip down,
off (Varro+), exstillare 'to drip away, water' (P1.+), instillare 'to pour in drop by
drop' (Cato+); sfTria 'icicle' (Verg.-t-). 1
Pit. *sti/eir-(la~). > "
IE cognates: Gr. atlXri 'drop'; Lith. styrti 'to become stiff, Norw. stira, OIc. stira
'to be(come) rigid, stiff.
Stilla can be regarded as a diminutive * stir-la- to stiria, even though the latter is only
attested in Imperial Latin. WH and IEW mainly compare Gm. and Baltic words in
*stir- meaning 'stiff, but it is uncertain whether 'stiff was the original meaning on
which stiria was based. In view oi stilla 'drop', stiria may have referred to the form
of drops and icicles, which would leave the connection with 'stiff unfounded. Gr.
OTIXTJ 'drop' cannot be directly compared with stilla; at most, there would be a (IE or
European?) base *sti~.
Bibl.: WH IL.595, EM 648, IEW 101 Of

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: stilus 'pointed piece of metal, stem, stylus' [m. o] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: stimulus 'goad, spike' (P1.+), stimuleus 'inflicted with the goad' (PL),
stimulare 'to urge forward, incite' (P1.+), stimuldtnx [f.J 'who incites' (PL); stiva
'shaft of a plough-handle' (Cato+).
It is uncertain whether Latin stilus, stimulus and stiva all belong together, but one
might see a root sti- 'sharp object' in them. It has been argued that they contain the
core of the root *stig- 'to sting' (see s.v. stinguo), the -g- of which would be a root
enlargement; although this is not completely impossible, there are no positive
indications in this direction. Another possible cognate might be Av. staera-, taera-
[m.] 'mountain-top' < *(s)te/oir/lo-, if to stilus. But since the root cannot be
determined, this etymology remains gratuitous.
Bibl.: WH II: 592, 595, EM 648f., 651, IEW 1015.
stinguG, -ere 'to extinguish' [v. Ill; -stinxi, stinctum] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: distinguere 'to keep separate, mark off, distinguish' (Pac.+), distinctus
'different' (Acc.+), exstinguere 'to extinguish, kill, wipe out' (P1.+), interstinguere 'to
extinguish temporarily; mark off (Lucr.+), restinguere 'to extinguish, suppress' (P1.+).
Pit. *ste-n-gv-e/o~, *in-sti/eig-. It. cognates: U. anstintu, astintu [3s.ipv.II] 'to
connect, attach' < *an + stinge-tod.
PIE *stengw-e/o- 'to push, thrust'. IE cognates: Go. stigqan 'to thrust', caus.
ga-stagqjan.
There is no reason to posit a separate verb *stingere, as EM do. The attestations of
*stingere will have originated e.g. in the 3p. stinguunt > stingunt. The meanings of ex-
and restinguere 'to extinguish' and distinguere seem quite distinct, but can be
understood if the root meant 'to press' or 'push': ex-stinguere 'to put a fire out',
re-stinguere 'to push back, suppress', and dis-stinguere 'to push apart > distinguish,
mark off; cf. Klingenschmitt 1989. Umbrian * ana-sting- 'to connect' is also
unproblematic from 'to push'. The verbal stem ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: stlpo, -are 'to compress, surround' [v. I] (Varro+)
Derivatives: stipator 'bodyguard, attendant' (Varro+); obstipus 'slanted, inclined'
(Enn.+), Tnstipare 'to pack closely' (Cato); stipes, -itis [m.] 'tree trunk, stick' (Cato+).
Pit. *stei/fpo~.
PIE *steip-o- 'stiff, erect'. IE cognates: Lith. stipti, Is. stimpu 'to stiffen, grow
rigid', stipriis 'strong', stiepti 'to stretch'; OE stif, MHG stif 'stiff, erect' < *steifa~,
MLG stivele 'support', OIc. stifla 'to dam'.
Seems to be based on an adj. *stTpo- 'erect, rigid' < *steipo~, on the evidence of the
Gm. accent. The noun stipet- can be interpreted as a deadjectival /-stem *steip-e/ot-
'the stiff one'. There are possible cognate forms in Baltic and Germanic from a root
*stip-.
Bibl.: WH II: 593, EM 650, IEW 1015f., LIV ?*steip-. stips

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: stips, -is 'small offering of money, alms' [f./j] (P1.+)
Derivatives: stipula 'stalk, straw, reed' (Ter.+), stipuldri 'to exact a solemn promise'
(P1.+), stipulatid 'demanding a guarantee' (Varro+), instipuldri 'to stipulate for'
(PI.+); stipendium 'regular cash payment, campaign' (P1.+; stipendium metri causa in
Enn.).
Pit. *stip- 'stalk' > 'payment', *stip-elo- 'straw'. It. cognates: U. steplatu, stiplatu,
anstiplatu [3s.ipv.II], stiplo [2s.ipv.] 'to agree legally'.
PIE *stip- 'stalk'? IE cognates: see s.v. stipo.
The noun stipendium derives by haplology from *stipi-pendium. Stipulari can be
derived from the noun stipula, the original meaning of the verb was 'to draw/cut
straws'. In view of U. ste/ipla-, this must have been an early development. The noun
stip- must have developed from a concrete object that was used for payments, but the
nature of the object is unknown: a certain stalk of a plant? a measure of com? Since
the root meant 'to be stiff, erect', the meaning 'stalk' is attractive.
Bibl.: WH II: 594, EM 650, IEW 1015f, Untermann 2000: 703f, LIV ?*steip-. ^
stipo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: stolo 'shoot, sucker (in plants)' [m. n] (Varro+)
Pit. *stel-on~ or *stol-5n~.
PIE *stel-n- 'shoot, stem'. IE cognates: Gr. oxoloc, 'equipment, armament; ship's
prow' [m.], creXea [f.] 'shaft of an axe, hammer', oteksx0^, tn- (m0] 'the end of the
stem at the root of a tree, stump, log', Arm. stebi 'trunk, branch'; OE stela [m.] 'stalk
of a plant', Norw. stjel 'stalk'.
We find a matching noun in Armenian, and Greek has a few nouns with similar form
and semantics. The meaning of Lat. stold, Gr. oxekeypq and OE stela can be well
understood on the basis of the PIE root *stel- 'to place'.
BibL: WH II: 599f, EM 655, IEW 1019f, LIV *stel-.
strSna 'auspicious sign' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: strenuus 'active, vigorous' (Naev.+), strenuitas 'activity' (Varro+),
strenuosus 'active' (PI.), mstrenuus 'inactive' (P1.+), Strenia 'goddess of health and
luck' (Varro+).
Pit. *stregsno-7
PIE *streg-sno-?
Often compared with Gr. crTprjvf)^ 'rough, harsh', oxprjvoq [n.] 'passion, pride', but
the semantics are unconvincing. If the original adj. was *strenos (as one PI. attestation
seems to suggest), strenuus is due to the influence of the adj. in -uus, in particular
*endostruos > industrius (see s.v. struo). The Romans regarded strena as a Sabine
word. Szemerenyi 1989: 23f. connects Olr. tren 'strong' < *treg-sno-, and (if cognate)
OIc. prek(r), OE pracu 'force' < *treg-, OIc. sterkr < *sterg-. There are no good
verbal cognates for these nominal forms *(s)terg-, hence the PIE character remains
uncertain. \
BibL: WH II: 601, EM 655, IEW 1022-1027, Schrijver 1991: 140.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: struma 'swelling of the lymphatic glands' [f. a\ (Cic.+)
In theory, a connection with Gm. words in PIE *strud- (OIc. strutr 'top', OE strutian
'to stand still', NHG strotz 'stiff, MHG struz 'resistance') is possible, but the
semantic link is not compelling, and the late attestation in Latin makes an IE
etymology less certain.
Bibl.: WH II: 606f., EM 657, IEW 1022-1027.
struO, -ere 'to arrange, construct, compose, build' [v. Ill; pf. struxi, ppp. structum]
(Lex XII+)
Derivatives: strues, -is [f.] 'heap, pile; row of sacrificial cakes' (Cato+), struix, -Tcis
[f.] 'heap, pile' (Andr.+); construere 'to pile up, arrange' (Sis.+), exstruere 'to heap
up, construct' (P1.+), Tnstruere 'to build, arrange, equip' (P1.+), Tnstrumentum
'equipment' (Cato+), industria 'diligent activity' (P1.+), industrius 'active' (PL+),
industriose 'diligently' (Cato+), indostruum 'industrium' (Paul. exF.).
Pit. *strow-e/o~. It. cognates: U. struh^la, struma, strusla, strusla [acc.sg.],
struh^Ias [gen.sg.] f. 'kind of sacrificial cake' < *struwi-k-ela?
PIE *streu-e/o- 'to strew'. IE cognates: Olr. sru'id* 3s. 'to throw', as-sroither 'is
dispersed', OBret. strouis 'strew out' < PIE *strou-eie-, OCS o-strujg, -strujiti 'to
destroy', Go. straujan 'to strew' < *strou-eie-.
Lat. strud can reflect a PIE thematic pr. *streu-e/o~. The velar in the pf. and ppp. must
be analogical to find, where -gw- disappeared regularly between vowels. If industrius
goes back to *endo~struwo~, this was probably directly built to the verb.
Bibl.: WH I: 696, II: 607, EM 316, 658, IEW 1029-1031, Leumann 1977: 591,
Meiser 1998: 208, Untermann 2000: 704f, Schumacher 2004: 602, LIV *streu-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: stupeo 'to be(come) numb, amazed' [v. II] (Ter.+) ;
Derivatives: stupidus 'dazed, stunned, foolish' (P1.+), stupiditas 'stupidity' (Acc.+);
obstu/ipescere 'to be stunned' (P1.+), obstitpidus 'astounded' (P1.+), obstupefacere 'to
strike dumb, stun' (Ter.+); stuprum 'dishonour, illicit sexual intercourse* (Naev.+),
stuprare 'to have illicit sexual intercourse with' (P1.+).
Pit. *stup-e- 'to be stunned', *stup-ro- 'struck, hit'.
PIE *stup-eh 'to hit, push', *stup-ro-. IE cognates: CLuw. dup(a)i-, Lyc. tub(e)i 'to
r
strike', Gr. ruTtTto 'to hit, strike, sting', aor. exum] 'was hit', TU7toc; 'stroke,
impression'; Alb.pertyp 'to chew', shtyp 'to crush'; Gr. cxwtoc, [n.] 'stick, shaft'.
The PIE verbal meaning 'to hit, push' explains the Latin stative 'to be stunned' as
well as the nominal form *stup-ro- 'struck, stunned' > 'dishonoured'.
Bibl.: WH II: 609f, EM 658f, IEW 1032-1034, LIV *(s)teup-.

Pit. *storno- 'starling'?
PIE *(h )st(o)r-no-. 'starling'? IE cognates: Gr. doxpaXoi; 'starling' (Hsch.), OE

Star, MDu. sterre, OIc. stari, OE steer, dim. stcerling 'starling',

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: suasum 'dirty grey colour' [n. o] (PI, Fest.)
Pit. *swarsso-7
PIE *surd-to-? IE cognates: see s.v. Hordes.
The meaning has suggested a connection to sordes < *suord-o/i-, but the exact form is
unclear. A preform *suord-to- would probably yield *sorso-, whereas *suerdto-
would yield *suerso- > *sorso- too. Only if *sufd-to- regularly developed into
*suarsso- could suasum result; this would match the rule *RDT > *RaDT posited by
Schrijver 1991: 485. Without external support for this morphological analysis, this
etymology remains uncompelling.
Bibl.: WH II: 611, EM 659, IEW 1052, Leumann 1977: 211, Schrijver 1991: 463,
494. —> sordes

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: suavis 'sweet,pleasant' [adj. i] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: suavitudo 'sweetness' (P1.+), suavitas 'pleasantness, charm* (P1-+),
sudvisdviatid 'amorous kissing' (PI.), sudvisonus 'sweet-sounding' (Naev.+); sdvium
'kiss' (P1-+), sdvillum 'kind of cheesecake' (Cato); suadere [suasT, suasum] 'to
recommend, advise' (P1.+), consuddere 'to recommend strongly' (P1+), dissuddere
'to advise against' (P1.+), persuadere 'to induce, convince' (P1.+), persuastrix
'persuasive woman' (PI.), suadela 'persuasion' (P1.+), suasor 'who advises,
advocates' (P1.+), suasus, -its 'advising' (Ter.+), suada 'persuasiveness' (Enn.+).
Pit. *swadu- 'sweet', *swadeje- 'to render agreeable'.
PIE *sueh d-u- 'sweet', *su(o)h2d-eie- 'to render tasty'. IE cognates: Skt. svddu-
2
[m.], svadvi- [f.] 'sweet', pr. svadanti [3p.act.], caus. svadaya- 'to make savoury',
svattd- 'made savoury', OAv. huddma- 'sweetness', YAv. xasta- 'cooked, made
done', Bal. wad 'salt' (< PIr. *huada-)\ Gr. TJSUI; 'sweet, tasting good, pleasant', OE
.swof 'sweet', OHG suozi, OE swete; ToA swar 'sweet' < *suad-ro~.
The PIE u-stem adj. yielded Pit. *suadu~, which was then remade into an /-stem as all
other i/-adj. were: *suadwi-. Subsequently, -d- was lost in front of *-w-. The verb
suadere shows by its meaning that it is a causative, but *suoh2d-eie- would have
yielded *sdde-. Hence, the root form of the adj. was probably introduced into the verb
(Schrijver 1991: 148). The semantics 'to recommend' can be understood from earlier
'to make sweet, render agreeable'. In sdvium and sdvillum < *swawio~, the first *-H'-
was dissimilated.
Bibl.: WH II: 483, 61 If, EM 597, 659, IEW 1039f, Schrijver 1991: 145, 148,
Meiser 1998: 120, LIV *sueh d-.
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sub, su(b)s 'under, below; reaching from below, up to' [prep, pref. + acc.
(movement), + abl. (position)] (Elog.Scip, Naev.+); distribution: sus- < *subs~ in
front of p,t,c,qu, su- in front of sp-\ sub- in front of vowels, h-, i-, u-, b-, d-, /-, r-,
s~, {-; sue- in front of c-, suf- in front of/-, sug- in front of g-, sup- in front of p-, often
also sum- in front of m- and sar- in front of r-. The meaning 'movement upwards' can
be seen e.g. in suspicid, sublevo, surgo, sublatus.
Derivatives: subter 'underneath' [adv.]; 'towards/in a position at the base of, under'
[prep.] (Varro+), subtus 'underneath, below' (Cato+); supmus 'lying face upwards'
U. sukatu 595
(P1.+), resupTnare 'to tilt back, lay face upwards' (Ter.+), resupTnus 'lying face
upwards, sloping upwards' (Acc.+).
Pit. *supV (probably *supo) 'down, below; upwards', *supTno- 'upwards'. It. cognates:
O. ou7i (+ loc.), U. su (+ abl.) 'under', as a preverb U. sub- (before vowel), su- (before
vowel or consonant: sumtu < *sup-emtu, sutentu < *sup-tentu). Probably also U.
supu (+ loc.) 'at the foot of; maybe U. subahtu, subotu [3s.ipv.II], subator sent
[3p.pf.ps.] 'to lay aside'.
PIE *(hi)upo 'up, from below'. IE cognates: Gaul, vo-, Olr. fo 'under', OW guo~,
gu-, guaW. go-, gwa- < PCI. *u(po-; Skt. upa 'towards, together with, under, near
to, onto, on', OAv. upa, YAv. upa, OP upa 'towards, with, on, in'; Gr. urco, two
'under, below'; Go. w/'on, under', OHG oba, MHG obe, ob 'over', OIc. q/"'over, on,
in', OE ufe-, OHG wf(ufari) 'on*.
Final -b may have arisen in front of voiced stops. Lat. subtus < *sub-tos (cf. intus).
The theory that supmits contains the root *ei- 'to go' (Leumann 1977: 328) is
unattractive: it will be a simple adjectival derivative in -inus, which shows the old
meaning 'upwards' of *sup(o). Initial *s- in Italic (in sub and super) and in Greek
cannot be explained by comparing sine with *en- as EM do, since sine is now
etymologized differently. Whatever the origin of...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: borrowed a word in *subh- with a common semantic core from a third language.
Bibl.: WH 11:617, EM 661.
U. sukatu [3s.ipv.II], prusikurent [3p.fut.pf.] 'to declare', SPic. persukant [3p.pr.] 'id.'.
Pit. *soiko- 'swearing', *(se-)sik- 'to swear',.
PIE *se-s(o)ik- [pf.] 'to reach', *soik-o- [m./n.]. IE cognates: Gr. IKCO, hcavco,
ixveopai [pr.], iKEcrOai [aor.] 'to come, reach', eveiKai 'to carry off < *en-seik-; Lith.
siekti, 3s. siekia 'to try to reach, strive for', saTkas, siekas, Latv. sieks 'unit of dry
measure'; ToB siknam, sb. saikam 'to set a foot'.
The older etymology with *sekw- 'to say' was dismissed by Meiser, who instead
derives pf. *pro-sik-us- from PIE *seik- 'to reach'. Meiser posits a semantic change
from 'to reach' > 'to reach after, seek' > 'swear'. He explains the stem suka- in U.
and SPic. as denominal *soikaje/o- to a noun *soiko- 'declaration'.
Bibl.: IEW 897fi, Meiser 1986: 87f, Untermann 2000: 589f., 712fi, LIV *seik-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sucus 'juice* [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: siicidus 'fresh, juicy' (Varro+), consucidus 'fresh, juicy' (PI.).
Pit. *souko~. •
PIE *sou&-o- 'juice' (or *seuk-o-?). IE cognates: W. sugno 'to suck', MBret. sunaff
'juice', W. sugnedydd 'pump' (< *seitk-n-l); Latv. siikt, OCS shsati, Is. SKSQ, RU.
sosat' 'to suck' < *suk-; OIc. siiga, OHG OE siigan 'to suck' < *sugan, OE sucan,
MDu. svcen < *sukan < *suk-n-?
We find the root for 'to suck' as *suk- in Celtic, BS1. and in some Germanic forms,
and as *sug- in other Germanic forms and in Latin siigo. It is unlikely that this goes
back to a root *su- with different root enlargements, so we must surmise that the root
originally was *sitk- or *sug-, and changed to *sug- or *suk- in certain phonetic
environments. Both Latin and Germanic conserve both variants. Schrijver 2001: 423
regards the vacillation in root-final stop as a sign that these words for 'to suck' and
'juice' were borrowed from (a) substratum language(s). In its turn, this might have
got the word from Uralic, since Proto-Uralic *soxi or *suwe 'mouth' looks quite
similar to IE *suk/g~.
Bibl.: WH II: 6221, EM 662, IEW 912f., LIV *seuk-. siigo

'to start sweating'; Gr. iSptbc,, -anioq [m.], ep. acc. i5pm 'sweat' < *suid-r-os~; iSico 'to

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sweat', ei5oq (Hsch.), iSoq [n.] 'sweat' < *sueidos-; Arm. k'irtn, Alb. dirse < *suid-r-;
Latv. sviedri [pi.] 'sweat' < *suoidro-; OHG sweiz, OE swat; ToB syelme 'id.'.
Suddre was probably formed on the basis of a m. *swoido- 'sweat', as attested in Ilr.
and Germ. The noun sudor can be inherited from *sweidos if Rix is correct in
assuming a phonetic change of *swei- > *swoi~. Otherwise, the noun can be explained
from the introduction of sud- (or *swoid~) into the j-stem.
Bibl.: WH II: 623, EM 662f., IEW 1043, Rix 1985: 341-343, Stiiber 2002: 156-158,
LIV l.*sueid-.

Pit. *swoido~.

According to Lubotsky 1985, followed by Schrijver 1991: 54, sudus belongs to the IE

< PIE *suid-. The root would thus be homophonous with 'to sweat' (Lat. suddre).
Bibl.: WH II: 624, EM 663, IEW 880f., Sihler 1995: 57, LIV ?2.*sueid-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: suffragium 'vote cast in an assembly, voting' [n. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: refragarefi 'to oppose' (Pompon.+), suffragare/i 'to express public
support' (Sis.+), suffrago, -inis 'joint in the hind leg of a quadruped; sucker shoot'
(Col.+), suffragator 'one who gives support to a candidate' (P1.+).
Pit. *-frdg-.
Clearly, these forms are based on a verb *sub-fragere or a noun *sub-frag-o-
'support'. Since the oldest meanings all refer to the process of voting for or against a
candidate, one might support the view that *sub-Jrag- belongs to frangere 'to break':
* sub-frag- 'who shout in support of & candidate' (but fragor 'noise' is not attested
before Lucr.). This view is advocated in detail by Vaahtera 1993, who explains long
-a- analogically on the model of e.g. tango - contagium. Szemerenyi 1989: 3If.
derives -fragium from a (substratum?) root *bhrag- 'hind part, tail-bone*, which he
connects with Celtic braka and Germanic *brok- 'tail-bone'. He hypothesizes for
suffragium a semantic change from 'support under the hind part' to 'political
support'. This is too fantastic to be credible (cf. Vaahtera 1993: 70).
Bibl.: WH II: 625f., EM 663, IEW 165. -*jrangd
sugO, -ere 'to suck' [v. Ill; pf. siixl, ppp. suctum] (Novius+)
Derivatives: siictus, -us 'sucking' (Varro+), sumen, -inis 'sow's udder' (P1.+);
sugillare (also sugg-) 'to mark with bruises, insult' (Varro+); sanguisuga [f.] 'leech'
(Plin.+); assugere 'to suck towards' (Lucr.+), exsugere 'to suck out' (P1.+).
Pit. *souge/o- 'to suck', *soug(s)mn- 'udder'.
PIE *seug-e/o- 'to suck'. IE cognates: OE sucan 'to suck', socian (< *si/k-) 'to suck up'.
See s.v. sucus.
Bibl.: WH II: 622f., EM 664, IEW 912fi, Schrijver 1991: 234, LIV *seuk-. sucus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sulcus 'furrow' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: sulcare 'to plough, draw furrows' (Varro+); bisulcis / bisulcus 'divided
into two parts, forked' (P1.+).
Pit. *solko-.
PIE *solk-o- 'furrow'. IE cognates: Gr. gjocco 'to draw, drag', OXKOC; [adj., m.]
'attractive, trailing; hauling-engine for ships; furrow', Alb. helq, heq 'to draw' (<
*solkeie/o-), OE sulh [fi] 'plough' < *sjk-, ToB salk- 'to draw out'.
In theory, sulcus can reflect PIE *sjk-o- or *soiko-. In view of Gr. oAxoc;, the latter
seems more likely.
Bibl.: WH II: 627, EM 664, IEW 901, Meiser 1998: 84, LIV *selk-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sulpur, -uris 'sulphur' [n. r] (Cato+)
Derivatives: sulpureus 'containing sulphur' (Enn.+).
Pit. *solpor I *solpur.
PIE *solp-r? 'fat'. IE cognates: Skt. sarpis- [n.] 'melted butter, lard', srpra- [adj.]
'greasy, smooth, sleek', Gr. o^Trq [fi] 'oil-flask', Alb. gjalpe 'butter', OHG salba
'ointment, salve', ToA ?alyp, ToB salype 'butter, oil'.
The best spelling is sulpur. The Seneca mss. mostly have sulphur, those of Pliny have
sulpur and sulphur. Szemerenyi (1995: 410) suggests that sulpur derives from *sulpos
< *solpos, as in sulcus. The preform *solpos would ultimately derive from *selpos
'oily substance', with cognates in Greek, Tocharian and Germanic. The development
of *selpos to *solpos would be irregular, however, and final -ur instead of -or is also
unexpected. Maybe the PIE preform was *solp-r?
Bibl.: WH II: 628, EM 665, IEW 1046.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sum, esse 'to be' [v. Irregular, pr.ind.: sum, es, est, sumus, estis, sunt. pr.sb. fuam etc.
» siem/sim, etc.; pf. fin, fut. futurus sum, inf. fore] (VOLat.+: Forum cippus esed
'erit', Duenos inscr. sied, Garigliano Bowl esom 'sum', Elog.Scip. fuit, fidse, fuet,
Lex Sacra (CIL 401) estod)
Derivatives: escit (Lex XII, Lucr., Paul, ex F.), escunt 'will be' (Cic.), obescet (Paul.
ex F.) 'oberit vel aderit', superescit (Enn, Acc.) 'supererit'; absens, -tis 'absent'
(P1.+), cons ens, -tis [adj./noun] (Var, CIL; only in di consentes 'the twelve major
deities'),praesens 'present, ready' (Lex XII+),praesentarius 'in ready money' (P1.+),
praesentia 'presence' (Ter.+); abesse 'to be absent, different', adesse 'to be present'
(Naev.+), deesse 'to be lacking' (P1.+), inesse 'to be in or on' (Naev.+), interesse 'to
lie between, make a difference; attend' (P1.+), obesse 'to be a hindrance' (P1.+),
praeesse 'to be in charge' (P1.+), prddesse, prosum 'to be of use' (Naev.+), subesse
'to be underneath' (P1.+), superesse 'to be higher, remain'.
Pit. pr.ind. *es-/s~, pr.sb. *es-e/o~, inf. *es-; pf. *fefu-\ fut. fus-. It. cognates: O.

[3s.pr.sb.], O. teremnatust [3s.pf.ps.], with ptc.pr.: U. aterafust, andersafust,
andirsafust [3s.fiit.pf.]; with" preverbs: O. adfust [3s.fiit.pf.], U. amprefu<u>s

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: summits 'highest' [adj. o/a] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: summa 'total number, amount, whole' (P1.+), Summamts 'the god of
high places' (P1.+), summatim 'summarily' (Lucil.+), summatus, -us 'being chief
(Lucr.+), summas, -atis [adj.] 'of the first rank' (P1.+).
Pit. *sup(a)mo- 'highest'. It. cognates: U. sume [loc.sg.], somo [acc.sg.m.], somo
[abl.sg.m.] 'upper' < * sup-mo-.
PIE *(hi)up-m(H)o- 'highest'. IE cognates: Skt. upamd-, YAv. upsma- 'upmost,
highest, closest' < Ilr. *(H)upmHa-, Gr. wratoq 'upmost, highest'; OE ufernest
'highest'.
The Ilr. cognates suggest a superlative formation *supamo > *summo~. Yet a preform
*sup-mo- cannot be excluded, cf. Gr. jrpopoq.
Bibl.: WH II: 629f., EM 666, IEW 1106L, Meiser 1998: 152, Untermann 2000:
716f. —• sub, super

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: suo, -ere 'to sew' [v. Ill; pf. suf, ppp. sutum] (Ter.+)
Derivatives: subula 'shoemaker's awl' (Sen.+), sutor 'shoemaker' (P1.+), sutorius
'of a shoemaker' (Varro+), sutrinus 'of shoemaking' (Varro+), sutela 'cunning
device' (P1.+); pellesuina 'shop where skins are sold' (Varro); consuere 'to sew
together, plan' (P1.+).
Pit. *s(j)uje/o-, *s(J)uto-.
PIE *siuH-ie/o- 'to sew', *siuH-to- 'sewn'. IE cognates: Skt. pr. sfvya- 'to sew',
syuta- 'sewn up', syitman- [n.] 'band, thong, bridle', sutra- [n.] 'wire, line, cord',
Khot. hiya 'sewn stuffs' < *siuHC < PIE *siHuC-\ Lith. siuti, Latv. sut, SCr. siti; Go.
siujan 'to sew'.
Skt. contains traces of the original sequence *siHu- which was metathesized to *siuH-
under certain conditions, probably in front of consonants. The resulting present
*siuH-ie/o- lost its first *-i- (by means of dissimilation?) and subsequently developed
to *suje/o-; the form su- was then also introduced into the ppp. The absence of the
development of *u to / in front of *i (cf. puts, inciens) is explained by Meiser from
'dissimilatory retention' of u between two *f s (2003: 236; until after the fronting of
*u), but maybe u was simply restored on the model of the derivatives in *su-.
Bibl.: WH II: 620, 631, EM 662, 667, IEW 915fi, Schrijver 1991: 247f., Meiser
1998: 227, 2003:235f., LIV *sieuH- moved

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: super 'over, above, beyond' [adv., prep.] (P1.+)
Derivatives: superus [nom.sg.m. super Cato] 'upper, situated above' (Andr.+), supra
[adv, prep.] 'at a higher level; on top of, above' (Andr.+), msuper [adv., prep.] 'on
top; above, over' (P1.+), super are 'to surpass, defeat, climb over' (Naev.+), superior
'higher, stronger' (P1.+), supremus 'highest, last' (Lex XII+), superne [adv.] 'at a
higher level, on top' (Lucr.+), supernus 'situated at a higher level' (Lucr.+).
Pit. * super (i) 'above', *supero- 'upper', *super-jds- 'higher', *superisamo-
'highest'. It. cognates: U. subra, subra [adv.], subra [prep. + acc.] 'above' <
* super dd] U. super [prep. + loc.] 'on top'; U. superne [prep. + acc.] 'over' <
*super-noi [loc.sg.] or adv. * super~ned to *super-no~; O. supruis [dat. pi. m.], U.
supru [abl.sg.] 'upper' < *sup-ero~.
PIE *(h,)uper(i) [adv.] 'over, above', *uper-o- [adj.] 'above'. IE cognates: Celtib.
ueramos 'highest', Gaul, uer- 'high', Olr. for(-) 'up, over, beyond', W. gor-, gwar-
'over', OW guar, MW ar, MCo. ar, OBret. guar, Bret, war 'on' < *uq>er-\ Skt. upari,
YAv. upairi 'above, over', OP upariy 'over, on', Skt. upara- [adj.] 'below, under,
later', YAv. upara- 'upper, higher', Gr. wep, uTtep 'over, above', Gr. (mepoc; [m.]
'pestle', Arm. i ver 'up', OHG ubir 'over', Go. ufar; OE ufera 'higher'.
For the addition of s- in Latin super as opposed to PIE *up-, see s.v. sub. Supra <
abl.sg.f. * super ad. The sup. developed from *supresmos < *suprismos <
*superisamos. The adv. superne probably contains the suffix *-ne (cf. pone <
*postne)\ from it, supernus was backformed.
Bibl.: WH II: 613, 630, EM 660, 667f, IEW 1105f, Leumann 1977: 320, Sihler
1995: 368, 441, Meiser 1998: 152, Untermann 2000: 706f, 720-722. probus (for
super bus), sub, summus, supTnus
supo, -are; -sip5, -are 'to pour, strew' [v. I] (Paul, ex F. supare 'to throw')
Derivatives: dissu/ipare 'to disperse, shatter' (P1.+), msipere, -sipio (Cato+),
Insu/ipare (Paul. exF...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: surdus 'deaf; muted' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: absurdus 'ridiculous, awkward' (P1.+).
Since 'deaf often has two semantic sides, viz. 'who cannot hear' and 'who is not
heard', ab-surdus can be explained as 'which is unheard of, and regularly belongs to
surdus. According to WH and IEW, surdus might belong to the PIE root *suer- 'to
sound', but this leaves the semantics unexplained, since surdus means 'unheard' or
'not hearing'. If ab-surdus was original, it may have meant 'off hearing' (> 'unheard
of), in line with other compounds in ab-. Then surdus would have originally meant
'hearing', but changed its meaning to 'not hearing' under the influence of absurdus.
This is very speculative, of course.
Bibl.: WH I: 5, II: 634, EM 4, 669, IEW I049f., Nussbaum 1999a: 381, LIV 1.
*suer-.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: surus 'post, stake' [m. o] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: surculus 'twig, stick' (Cato+), surculdrius 'living among twigs'
(Varro+); sura 'calf of the leg' (P1.+)?
No certain etymology. The length of the first vowel in surus is uncertain; if it was
long, it may be connected with sura 'calf of the leg': the 'post, stake' would have
been denominated after the body-part, as is often the case. If it was short surus, it
might be cognate with Skt. svaru- 'sacrificial post, stake'.
Bibl.: WH II: 634f., EM 669f., IEW 1050.
